metadata.description: All unique rules from fannie_mae and freddie_mac

business_rules.rule_name: Weighted-average guaranty fee factor rounded 0.01 bp
business_rules.description: To determine the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for an MBS pool (in basis points), the lender multiplies each loan’s issue-date principal balance (or scheduled balance in later months) by the applicable guaranty fee rate (in basis points, adjusted for remittance cycle adjustments and any buyup/buydown), sums the products, divides by the aggregate pool balance (issue-date principal balance or scheduled security balance), and rounds the result to the nearest hundredth of a basis point.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for an MBS pool
business_rules.consequences: Correct factor supports accurate remittance; miscalc may cause remittance shortfall/adjustment
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-3-01, Determining the Annualized Weighted-Average Guaranty Fee Factor for a Pool
source_reference.source_text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for a pool (expressed in basis points) is determined by multiplying the issue date principal balance of each mortgage loan in the pool (or, in subsequent months, the “scheduled” balance of each mortgage loan) by the applicable guaranty fee rate for the mortgage loan expressed in basis points (adjusted for any remittance cycle adjustments, buyup, or buydown, if applicable), then: summing the products of all the mortgage loans in the pool, dividing that sum by the aggregate issue date principal balance for the pool (or, in subsequent months, by the “scheduled” security balance for the pool), and rounding the result to the nearest hundredth of a basis point.

examples: Pool has two loans: $200,000 at 35 bps and $300,000 at 40 bps; factor = ((200,000*35)+(300,000*40))/500,000 = 38 bps (then round to 0.01 bp).
If a loan’s guaranty fee is adjusted due to a buydown, use the adjusted fee rate (in bps) in the multiplication step.
In a subsequent month, use each loan’s scheduled balance and the pool’s scheduled security balance instead of issue-date balances.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Monthly guaranty fee remittance divide by 120,000
business_rules.description: To determine the monthly guaranty fee remittance amount for an MBS pool, the lender multiplies the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor by the pool balance (aggregate issue-date principal balance or scheduled security balance in later months), divides by 120,000, and rounds the result to two decimal places.
business_rules.conditions: When computing the monthly guaranty fee remittance amount for an MBS pool
business_rules.consequences: Correct calculation ensures proper monthly remittance; errors can create drafting discrepancies
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-3-01, Determining the Monthly Guaranty Fee Remittance for the Pool
source_reference.source_text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor is then multiplied by the aggregate issue date principal balance of the pool (or, in subsequent months, the “scheduled” security balance for the pool), divided by 120,000, and rounded to two decimal places. The result is monthly guaranty fee remittance for the pool.

examples: Annualized factor 38.25 bps and pool UPB $50,000,000: remittance = 38.25*50,000,000/120,000 = $15,937.50 (rounded to 2 decimals).
If using scheduled security balance $49,800,000 in month 2, use that balance in the same formula.
If the calculation yields $15,937.505, round to $15,937.51.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_008 instructs the lender to multiply the “annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor” by the pool balance and divide by 120,000. That factor is produced by BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_007; without it, BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_008 cannot be computed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the factor is not calculated (or is wrong), the remittance amount will be wrong, leading to drafting discrepancies, remittance shortfalls, reconciliations, and potential compensatory adjustments.

business_rules.rule_name: Condo full review replacement reserves must be at least 10% of budgeted income
business_rules.description: For condo projects evaluated under the Full Review process, the HOA projected budget must provide for funding replacement reserves for capital expenditures and deferred maintenance of at least 10% of the budget. To determine if the minimum 10% reserve allocation is met, the lender must divide the annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation by the association’s annual budgeted assessment income (including regular common expense fees). Certain income may be excluded from the reserve calculation: incidental income not relied on for ongoing operations/maintenance/capital improvements; utility income typically paid by unit owners; income allocated to reserve accounts; and special assessment income. A reserve study may be used in lieu of the 10% calculation if the stated conditions are met (obtain/retain study and analysis; equivalency to standard requirements; funded reserves meet/exceed study recommendations; and study meets Fannie Mae requirements).
business_rules.conditions: Project_type is condo and lender uses Full Review project eligibility process
business_rules.consequences: If ≥10% (or reserve study acceptable), project may pass; if not, project fails review
business_rules.exceptions: Reserve study may replace 10% calc if all listed reserve study conditions are met

source_reference.source_text: Lenders must review the HOA projected budget to determine that it • is adequate (that is, it includes allocations for line items pertinent to the type of condo project), and • provides for the funding of replacement reserves for capital expenditures and deferred maintenance that is at least 10% of the budget. To determine whether the association has a minimum annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation of 10%, the lender must divide the annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation by the association’s annual budgeted assessment income (which includes regular common expense fees). The following types of income may be excluded from the reserve calculation: • incidental income on which the project does not rely for ongoing operations, maintenance, or capital improvements; • income collected for utilities that would typically be paid by individual unit owners, such as cable TV or Internet access; • income allocated to reserve accounts; and • special assessment income. The lender may use a reserve study in lieu of calculating the replacement reserve of 10% provided the following conditions are met:

examples: HOA reserves $90,000 annually and assessment income is $800,000; ratio is 11.25%, meeting the 10% requirement (after excluding permitted income items).
HOA includes $50,000 incidental rental income in assessment income; lender excludes it if not relied upon for operations, potentially increasing the reserve ratio to meet 10%.

HOA reserve ratio calculates to 7%, but an acceptable reserve study shows funded reserves equivalent to standard requirements and meeting/exceeding study recommendations; lender may use reserve study instead.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery due to project ineligibility

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: In practice, lenders determine condo project classification (new vs established) early to route the file into the correct review workflow. That routing decision often precedes the 10% reserve allocation calculation required under Full Review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If classification is skipped or wrong, the file may be routed to the wrong review process, and the lender may omit or misapply the 10% reserve calculation, creating project eligibility defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008 governs sales/conveyance and phasing constraints; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008 governs HOA reserve funding adequacy. In an automated condo review workflow, both must be executed to reach an overall project eligibility result.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If conveyance/phasing is satisfied but reserves fail (or vice versa), the project still fails Full Review and the loan becomes ineligible for delivery based on project standards.

business_rules.rule_name: Affordable LTV uses appraised value; example 80% and DU entry required
business_rules.description: For shared equity transactions where resale restrictions terminate automatically upon foreclosure (or redemption period expiration) or upon recordation of a deed-in-lieu, the lender must use the Affordable LTV calculation for LTV/CLTV/HCLTV: divide the loan amount by the appraised value (not the lesser of sales price or appraised value). For DU loans, the lender must enter “Affordable LTV” in the Product Description field to ensure DU calculates ratios based solely on the appraised value for purchase transactions. The lender must also use the appraised value (under Affordable LTV) to determine minimum down payment, any required borrower contribution, and required mortgage insurance level.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to shared equity transactions where resale restrictions terminate at foreclosure/deed-in-lieu
business_rules.consequences: If standard LTV is used, LTV/MI/down payment may be wrong and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If resale restrictions survive foreclosure, standard lesser-of calculation applies instead

source_reference.source_text: Accordingly, for these types of transactions, lenders must use the "Aﬀordable LTV" calculation to determine LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios. The Aﬀordable LTV calculation divides the loan amount by the appraised value of the property, rather than the lesser of the sales price or the appraised value. ... Appraised value $200,000 ... First mortgage loan amount $160,000 Aﬀordable LTV Ratio ... 80% When using the Aﬀordable LTV calculation, the lender must for DU loans, enter "Aﬀordable LTV" in the Product Description ﬁeld

examples: Shared equity purchase: appraised value $200,000, subsidized sales price $160,000, loan amount $160,000; Affordable LTV = $160,000 / $200,000 = 80% (not 100%).

DU loan in shared equity program: lender forgets to enter “Affordable LTV” in Product Description; DU calculates LTV using lesser-of sales price/appraised value, causing ratio mismatch and delivery defects.
If resale restrictions survive foreclosure, lender must revert to standard lesser-of calculation; using Affordable LTV would overstate collateral value for foreclosure recovery.
Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: IPCs Above Closing Costs Treated as Sales Concessions
business_rules.description: Financing concessions must be equal to or less than the sum of the borrower’s closing costs. Any IPC financing concessions exceeding (a) the maximum financing concession cap or (b) the borrower’s total closing costs must be treated as sales concessions. Sales concessions must be deducted from the property’s sales price, and the maximum LTV/CLTV ratios must be recalculated using the lower of the reduced sales price or appraised value.
business_rules.conditions: IPCs applied to closing costs/prepaids in a purchase transaction
business_rules.consequences: Deduct excess from sales price; recalc LTV/CLTV; may affect approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text; common/customary fees addressed elsewhere

source_reference.source_text: Financing concessions that exceed the below limits are considered sales concessions and must be deducted from the property's sales price. As a result, the maximum LTV/CLTV ratios must be recalculated using the reduced sales price or appraised value. Additionally, ﬁnancing concessions must be equal to or less than the sum of the borrower's closing costs. Any amount exceeding the borrower's closing costs must be treated as a sales concession.

examples: Primary residence 80% LTV: seller credits of 8% exceed 6% cap; excess is a sales concession deducted from sales price and ratios recalculated.
Seller credit equals 6% cap but exceeds documented borrower closing costs; the overage is treated as a sales concession and deducted from sales price.
After deduction of sales concessions, recalculated LTV exceeds program limit; loan becomes ineligible unless restructured.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit conversion of over-limits and over-costs to sales concessions with required recalculation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility or price/ratio correction required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_006 sets the IPC maximum percentages by occupancy and LTV/CLTV and specifies the basis (lower of sales price or appraised value). BR_009_007 requires comparing IPCs to (a) the maximum cap and (b) closing costs, then treating excess as sales concessions and recalculating ratios. Therefore, BR_009_006 supplies the mandatory cap thresholds that BR_009_007 needs as an input.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_006 is not applied, BR_009_007 may fail to identify excess IPCs, causing incorrect price adjustments and LTV/CLTV recalculations. This can result in delivery defects, incorrect eligibility decisions, or under-collateralization risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 provides the decision rule for whether an HOA payment is an abatement (prohibited) or an IPC (permitted but capped and subject to closing-cost limits). If it is an IPC, BR_009_007 governs treatment of any excess amounts as sales concessions and the resulting recalculation of LTV/CLTV.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_008 classification is wrong, BR_009_007 may not be applied (or may be applied incorrectly), resulting in miscalculated ratios and potential ineligibility at delivery.

dependencies.rationale: BR_021_007 converts certain seller-provided subordinate financing into a 'sales concession' and mandates deduction of that amount from the sales price. BR_009_007 defines the downstream mechanics for sales concessions: deduct from sales price and recalculate ratios using the lower of reduced sales price or appraised value. In an automated workflow, BR_021_007 should feed the 'sales price reduction' input into the BR_009_007 recalculation logic (even though BR_009_007 originates from IPC excess, the recalculation methodology is the same eligibility control point).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_021_007 is applied after ratios are locked, the loan may be delivered with understated LTV/CLTV/HCLTV, causing eligibility defects, MI mis-triggers, and potential repurchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_007 recalculations are sensitive to whether appraised value is valid, because the lower-of comparison determines the denominator. BR_009_005 directly affects the credibility of the appraised value by controlling how concession-inflated comps are adjusted.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_005 fails, BR_009_007 may recalculate ratios using an invalid value, leading to incorrect eligibility outcomes (false pass/fail), downstream underwriting churn, and possible delivery defects if corrected later.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_007’s ratio recalculations rely on a compliant appraised value. BR_010_001 defines acceptability conditions for the appraisal’s site size/parcel valuation. A noncompliant appraisal can’t be relied upon for final LTV/CLTV determination after concession adjustments.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_001 fails, BR_009_007 outcomes are unstable (likely to change after appraisal correction), causing underwriting rework and delaying a reliable eligibility determination.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle LTV Uses As-Completed Value and Purchase Plus Costs
business_rules.description: For HomeStyle Renovation loans, LTV calculation depends on transaction type. Purchase: LTV = original loan amount ÷ lesser of (as-completed appraised value) or (purchase price + total renovation costs). Refinance: LTV = original loan amount ÷ as-completed appraised value.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating LTV for a HomeStyle Renovation purchase or refinance transaction
business_rules.consequences: If calculated incorrectly, LTV/eligibility may be misstated and violate Guide limits
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For a purchase money transaction, the LTV ratio is determined by dividing the original loan amount by the lesser of the “as completed” appraised value of the property or the sum of the purchase price of the property and the total renovation costs. For a reﬁnance transaction, the LTV ratio is determined by dividing the original loan amount by the “as completed” appraised value of the property.

examples: Purchase: loan $300,000; as-completed value $340,000; purchase price $310,000; reno $40,000; denominator lesser of ($340,000, $350,000)=$340,000; LTV=88.24%.
Refinance: loan $280,000; as-completed $350,000; LTV=80.00%.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Solar Debt Not on Title Must Be in DTI but Excluded from LTV and CLTV
business_rules.description: If solar panels are financed and collateralized by separate non-mortgage debt but do not appear on the title report (i.e., treated as personal property collateral), the lender must include the solar debt obligation in the DTI ratio, instruct the appraiser to provide no contributory value for the panels, and exclude the panels and related debt from the LTV and CLTV calculations.
business_rules.conditions: Solar panels financed/collateralized by non-mortgage debt that does not appear on title
business_rules.consequences: If included improperly, ratios and collateral value are misstated; may trigger QC defects/repurchase risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-04 (Solar panels underwriting table: not on title)
source_reference.source_text: Financed and collateralized -- the solar panels are reported to be collateral for separate (non- mortgage) debt used to purchase the panels, but do not appear on the title report• Obtain and review documentation suﬃcient to conﬁrm the terms of the secured loan (such as copies of the credit report, title report, any UCC ﬁnancing statement, related promissory note or related security agreement); • Include the debt obligation in the DTI ratio calculation; • Instruct the appraiser not to provide contributory value of the solar panels towards the appraised value because the panels are collateral for another debt; • Not include the panels in the LTV ratio calculation; and • Not include the debt in the other debt secured by the real estate in the CLTV ratio calculation

examples: Borrower has $150/month solar loan (panels treated as personal property; no title record). Lender counts $150 in DTI; appraiser gives $0 contributory value; LTV uses appraised value excluding solar value → compliant.
Lender omits solar payment from DTI because it is not on title → DTI understated; QC defect likely and delivery risk.
Appraiser adds $12,000 to value for the solar array even though panels are collateral for a separate debt → appraisal value overstated and noncompliant.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple Property Reserves 2%, 4%, or 6% of Aggregate UPB
business_rules.description: When the borrower owns other financed properties, the lender must calculate and document additional reserves for financed properties other than the subject property and the borrower’s principal residence by applying a percentage to the aggregate outstanding UPB of mortgages and HELOCs on those other financed properties: 2% (one to four financed properties), 4% (five to six financed properties), or 6% (seven to ten financed properties; DU only). The aggregate UPB excludes loans/HELOCs on the subject property, the borrower’s principal residence, properties sold or pending sale, and accounts paid by closing (or omitted in DU).
business_rules.conditions: When borrower owns other financed properties and subject is second home/investment
business_rules.consequences: Meets: additional reserve requirement satisfied; Violates: insufficient reserves; underwriting conditions unmet
business_rules.exceptions: 6% tier applies to DU only; exclusions listed must be applied

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.1-01, Calculation of Reserves for Multiple Financed Properties
source_reference.source_text: If the borrower owns other ﬁnanced properties (determined in accordance with B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower ), additional reserves must be calculated and documented for ﬁnanced properties other than the subject property and the borrower’s principal residence. The other ﬁnanced properties reserves amount must be determined by applying a speciﬁc percentage to the aggregate of the outstanding unpaid principal balance (UPB) for mortgages and HELOCs on these other ﬁnanced properties. The percentages are based on the number of ﬁnanced properties: 2% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has one to four ﬁnanced properties, 4% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has ﬁve to six ﬁnanced properties, or 6% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has seven to ten ﬁnanced properties (DU only). The aggregate UPB calculation does not include the mortgages and HELOCs that are on the subject property, the borrower’s principal residence, properties that are sold or pending sale, and accounts that will be paid by closing (or omitted in DU on the online loan application).

examples: Borrower has 3 financed properties; aggregate UPB on other financed properties (excluding subject and principal) is $230,050: additional reserves = 2% × $230,050 = $4,601.
Borrower has 6 financed properties; eligible aggregate UPB is $345,030: additional reserves = 4% × $345,030 = $13,801.
Borrower has 8 financed properties in DU: eligible aggregate UPB $500,000: additional reserves = 6% × $500,000 = $30,000 (DU-only tier).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: To compute additional reserves correctly, the lender must determine financed property count and ensure the transaction type is within allowed limits. BR_021_004 sets the governing tiers/limits by occupancy/product/underwriting method; BR_023_006 then applies tiered reserve percentages based on that count for second home/investment scenarios.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_021_004 is not applied first, the lender can (a) compute the wrong reserve tier, or (b) compute reserves for a scenario that is ineligible due to exceeding financed property limits—wasting processing time and increasing fallout risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_006 produces a required reserve amount; BR_023_007 governs eligibility of assets counted toward meeting that requirement. Therefore, reserve-source exclusion must be applied to the verified assets before concluding that BR_023_006 is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_007 is not enforced, the lender may pass reserves using ineligible funds, causing insufficient reserves findings in QC, underwriting defects, and potential repurchase/remedy exposure.

dependencies.rationale: Reserve validation must first check whether an exception applies. If the transaction is a 1-unit principal residence (no minimum reserves) or a high LTV refinance (exempt), reserve requirement checks should be bypassed to avoid false conditions and unnecessary document requests.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_008 is not applied, the lender may impose unnecessary reserve conditions, delay closing, and generate incorrect underwriting findings; conversely, misapplying the exception could cause under-reserving on transactions where reserves are required.

business_rules.rule_name: Revolving debt payment is 5% of balance when minimum missing
business_rules.description: Revolving charge accounts and unsecured lines of credit must be considered part of the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations. If the credit report does not show a required minimum payment amount and there is no supplemental documentation to support a payment of less than 5%, the lender must use 5% of the outstanding balance as the recurring monthly payment amount for qualifying (DTI).
business_rules.conditions: Revolving tradeline has no stated minimum payment and no supporting docs
business_rules.consequences: If 5% used, DTI is calculated per rule; if not, DTI is understated/defect
business_rules.exceptions: If supplemental documentation supports payment < 5%, use documented amount

source_reference.source_text: Revolving Charge/Lines of Credit Revolving charge accounts and unsecured lines of credit are open-ended and should be treated as long-term debts and must be considered part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations. These tradelines include credit cards, department store charge cards, and personal lines of credit. If the credit report does not show a required minimum payment amount and there is no supplemental documentation to support a payment of less than 5%, the lender must use 5% of the outstanding balance

examples: Credit card shows $10,000 balance with no minimum payment; lender uses $500/month (5%) in DTI.
Personal line of credit balance is $2,400 and no minimum is reported; lender uses $120/month (5%) in DTI.
Borrower provides statement showing a required minimum payment of $75 (which is < 5% of a $2,000 balance); lender uses $75 based on supplemental documentation.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Nontaxable income addback is 25% or actual tax rate if higher
business_rules.description: If income is verified as nontaxable and the income and tax-exempt status are likely to continue, the lender should calculate an adjusted gross income by adding an amount equivalent to 25% of the nontaxable income to the borrower’s income. If the actual amount of federal and state taxes generally paid by a wage earner in a similar bracket is more than 25% of the nontaxable income, the lender may use that higher amount for the adjusted gross income used to calculate qualifying ratios.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower has verified nontaxable income expected to continue
business_rules.consequences: If applied, qualifying income increases; if not verified, no addback allowed
business_rules.exceptions: May use actual tax-equivalent >25% instead of 25% addback

source_reference.source_text: If the income is veriﬁed to be nontaxable, and the income and its tax-exempt status are likely to continue, the lender should develop an “adjusted gross income” for the borrower by adding an amount equivalent to 25% of the nontaxable income to the borrower’s income. If the actual amount of federal and state taxes that would generally be paid by a wage earner in a similar tax bracket is more than 25% of the borrower’s nontaxable income, the lender may use that amount to develop the adjusted gross income, which should be used in calculating the borrower’s qualifying ratio.

examples: Borrower receives $2,000/month tax-free disability income; lender may add $500 (25%) to qualifying income for ratios.
Borrower receives $3,000/month nontaxable income; wage-earner tax equivalent is estimated at 30%; lender may add $900 rather than $750.
Borrower claims income is nontaxable but cannot document tax-exempt status; lender must not apply the addback.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: MCC income added equals mortgage x rate x MCC% ÷ 12
business_rules.description: When calculating the borrower’s DTI ratio for a Mortgage Credit Certificate (MCC), the maximum possible MCC income must be treated as an addition to the borrower’s income (not as a reduction to the mortgage payment). The added monthly income is calculated as: [(Mortgage Amount) × (Note Rate) × (MCC %)] ÷ 12.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has an MCC and lender is calculating DTI for underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If calculated and applied correctly, borrower income for DTI increases; if misapplied, DTI is misstated
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: When calculating the borrower’s DTI ratio, treat the maximum possible MCC income as an addition to the borrower’s income, rather than as a reduction to the amount of the borrower’s mortgage payment. Use the following calculation when determining the available income: [(Mortgage Amount) x (Note Rate) x (MCC %)] ÷ 12 = Amount added to borrower’s monthly income. For example, if a borrower obtains a $100,000 mortgage that has a note rate of 7.5% and they are eligible for a 20% credit... the amount... added... would be $125

examples: Mortgage $250,000 at 6.00% with 25% MCC: added income = 250,000×0.06×0.25 ÷ 12 = $312.50/month.
Mortgage $100,000 at 7.50% with 20% MCC: added income = $125/month (as in Guide example).
If lender incorrectly subtracts MCC benefit from P&I instead of adding to income, DTI calculation is noncompliant with this rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase/indemnification due to incorrect DTI calculation

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Seasonal Income Average Uses YTD Plus Prior 2 Years
business_rules.description: To determine qualifying seasonal income, the lender must calculate an average income amount using year-to-date (YTD) income when present and the previous two years’ earnings.
business_rules.conditions: Seasonal income is eligible and supported and is used for qualification
business_rules.consequences: If averaged per requirement, resulting amount may be used; if not, income calc is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the averaging method requirement

source_reference.source_text: Determination of Qualifying Income The lender must calculate an average income amount using year-to-date income, when present, and previous two years' earnings.

examples: Borrower YTD seasonal income is $12,000 and prior two years are $30,000 and $28,000; lender averages using YTD when present plus prior two years per policy.
Lender uses only last year’s seasonal income to qualify; calculation is not compliant because it omits YTD and the previous two years’ earnings.
Borrower has YTD available but lender ignores it and uses two-year average only; not compliant because YTD must be used when present.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_003 directs the lender to average seasonal income using YTD income (when present) and the previous two years’ earnings. BR_015_001 defines the documentation that provides those data elements (paystub with YTD, two years W-2s, and/or VOE). Therefore the calculation depends on having the required documents.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_015_001 documents are missing/insufficient, BR_015_003 cannot be completed accurately, leading to overstated/understated qualifying income and underwriting defects.

dependencies.rationale: The seasonal income averaging calculation in BR_015_003 is only relevant if seasonal income can be used. BR_015_002 defines that eligibility (two-year history). If BR_015_002 fails, the calculation result cannot be used for qualification.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_015_002 is not met, any seasonal income calculated under BR_015_003 must be excluded and DTI recalculated, potentially changing approval outcome.

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary Leave Income Uses Lesser Amount Until 1st Payment Date
business_rules.description: If the borrower will return to work as of the first loan payment date, the lender can use the borrower’s regular employment income to qualify. If the borrower will not return to work as of the first loan payment date, the lender must use the lesser of the borrower’s temporary leave income (if any) or regular employment income. If temporary leave income is less than regular income, the lender may supplement with available liquid financial reserves by calculating: supplemental income = available liquid reserves ÷ number of months from first payment date to regular income start date (rounded up). Total qualifying income = supplemental income + temporary leave income, and the total may not exceed the borrower’s regular employment income.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower on temporary leave and return timing relative to first payment date differs
business_rules.consequences: If met, qualifying income is capped and supportable; if violated, income overstated and loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Reserve supplement permitted only when temporary leave income is less than regular income

source_reference.source_text: If the borrower will return to work as of the ﬁrst loan payment date, the lender can consider the borrower's regular employment income in qualifying. If the borrower will not return to work as of the ﬁrst loan payment date, the lender must use the lesser of the borrower's temporary leave income (if any) or regular employment income... The following are instructions on how to calculate the "supplemental income": Supplemental income amount = available liquid reserves divided by the number of months of supplemental income... Number of months... rounded up to the next whole number... Total qualifying income = supplemental income + the temporary leave income... may not exceed the borrower's regular employment income.

examples: Borrower returns to work before first payment date; lender uses regular income of $6,500/month for qualification.
Borrower returns after first payment date; regular income $6,000, temporary leave income $2,000—lender must start with lesser amount ($2,000) and may add reserve-based supplement subject to cap.
First payment date to return date is 3.2 months; lender rounds up to 4 months when computing reserve supplement divisor.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_004 specifies the required documentation when the borrower will be on temporary leave at closing and income is needed to qualify, including intent to return and employer confirmation of return date. BR_015_005 then uses the return timing relative to first payment date and amounts (temporary leave income vs regular income) to determine which income amount is allowable. Without BR_015_004, BR_015_005 cannot be applied safely/compliantly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_015_004 is missing, the lender risks using the wrong income basis (overstating qualifying income), leading to ineligible loan delivery/QC findings and possible repurchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_005 (temporary leave) allows supplementing income with liquid reserves via a calculation based on available reserves. In DU-underwritten loans, DU defines required reserves (BR_023_005). The lender typically cannot treat funds needed to meet required reserves as 'available' for supplementation. Thus, reserve requirement determination is a prerequisite input to the temporary-leave supplement calculation for DU casefiles.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If required reserves are not determined correctly, the lender may improperly count reserves twice (once to meet reserve requirement and again as income supplementation), overstating repayment capacity and risking loan ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Capital gains income average uses 1 or 2 years
business_rules.description: Qualifying capital gains income must be averaged based on the historical trend. If stable or increasing, calculate an average using the most recent two years’ personal federal income tax returns; if decreasing, calculate an average using the most recent year’s personal federal income tax returns. Capital losses on Schedule D do not have to be considered when calculating income or liabilities, even if recurring.
business_rules.conditions: Capital gains income is used and tax return trend is evaluated
business_rules.consequences: If averaged per trend, income may qualify; wrong averaging causes QC defects
business_rules.exceptions: Capital losses on Schedule D do not have to be included

source_reference.source_text: Average Income: Depending on the historical trend of the income, the lender must calculate qualifying income as follows: • Stable or Increasing: Calculate an average income amount using the most recent two years personal federal income tax returns. • Decreasing: Calculate an average income amount using the most recent year personal federal income tax returns. Note: Capital losses identiﬁed on IRS Form 1040, Schedule D, do not have to be considered when calculating income or liabilities, even if the losses are recurring.

examples: Schedule D gains: $48,000 (year 1) and $52,000 (year 2) → stable/increasing, average = $50,000/yr ($4,166.67/mo).
Gains drop from $60,000 (year 1) to $30,000 (year 2) → decreasing, use most recent year = $30,000/yr ($2,500/mo).
Borrower has recurring $3,000 capital losses on Schedule D; lender may ignore losses when calculating qualifying income.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_001 requires obtaining signed federal income tax returns for the most recent two years (1040 and Schedule D) when using capital gains income. BR_018_002 then directs how to average capital gains income based on the trend using those returns. Therefore, the documentation is a mandatory prerequisite for the calculation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_018_001 documentation is missing/incomplete, the lender cannot compute qualifying capital gains income per BR_018_002, so the income must be excluded and the loan may no longer qualify.

business_rules.rule_name: Qualifying income from assets equals net assets ÷ term
business_rules.description: For employment-related assets used as qualifying income, the lender must compute monthly qualifying income by dividing Net Documented Assets by the loan amortization term (in months). Net Documented Assets equal the sum of eligible assets minus (i) the penalty for complete distribution at time of calculation and (ii) funds used for down payment, closing costs, and required reserves.
business_rules.conditions: Employment-related assets are used to create a monthly income stream
business_rules.consequences: If computed correctly, income may be used; miscalc can cause defects/QC
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender must divide "Net Documented Assets" by the amortization term of the loan (in months). "Net Documented Assets" are equal to the sum of eligible assets minus • the amount of the penalty that would apply if the account was completely distributed at the time of calculation; and • the amount of funds used for down payment, closing costs, and required reserves.

examples: Eligible assets $450,000 net of penalty; funds to close/reserves $100,000; net documented assets $350,000; 360-month loan → $972.22/month.
Net documented assets $120,000; 180-month amortization → $666.67/month qualifying income.
Penalty of $20,000 applies to full distribution and $30,000 needed for closing: eligible assets $200,000 → net = $150,000; 240 months → $625/month.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_004’s formula requires subtracting "required reserves" from eligible assets to compute Net Documented Assets. In DU workflows, BR_023_005 defines minimum required reserves based on occupancy/property type and DTI conditions. Thus BR_023_005 often supplies a required input to BR_018_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If required reserves are understated or not determined, Net Documented Assets may be overstated, inflating qualifying income derived from assets and creating eligibility/QC defects.

business_rules.rule_name: CLT capitalization rate equals rent divided by price difference
business_rules.description: When market activity includes both fee simple and non-CLT leasehold sales, the capitalization rate must be extracted from the market by dividing the annual ground rent for leasehold properties by the difference between (a) sales prices of fee simple comparable sales and (b) sales prices of leasehold comparable sales.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CLT appraisal can extract cap rate from fee simple vs leasehold sales
business_rules.consequences: If computed per formula, cap rate is market-derived; wrong method is defect
business_rules.exceptions: If no non-CLT leasehold sales exist, use alternative low-risk rates method

source_reference.source_text: To extract the capitalization rate, the appraiser must divide the annual ground rent for the properties subject to leasehold estates by the diﬀerence in the sales prices for the comparable sales of properties owned as fee simple estates and the comparable sales of properties subject to leasehold estates.

examples: Annual ground rent is $1,200; fee simple sale is $300,000; similar leasehold sale is $285,000; cap rate = 1,200 / (300,000−285,000) = 8.00%.
Appraiser uses a cap rate taken from a generic survey despite available fee simple vs leasehold pairs: noncompliant with required direct extraction method.
No non-CLT leasehold sales exist: appraiser documents alternative low-risk rate comparison instead (allowed exception path).
Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_001 establishes the CLT three-step technique and requires fee simple value determination using Sales Comparison (Step 1). BR_014_003 requires fee simple comparable sales prices and leasehold comparable sales prices to extract a cap rate. Those market inputs and their selection occur within the framework established by BR_014_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without completing BR_014_001’s required CLT method (and its sales comparison development), the cap rate extraction in BR_014_003 cannot be supported; appraisal becomes deficient and may not be acceptable for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: CLT leasehold value equals fee simple minus leased fee value
business_rules.description: To determine leasehold value in a CLT appraisal, the appraiser must (1) convert annual ground lease income into leased fee value by dividing that income by the market-derived capitalization rate, and then (2) reduce the estimated fee simple value by the leased fee value to arrive at leasehold value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CLT appraisal is developing leasehold value using 3-step method
business_rules.consequences: Correct computation supports indicated value; incorrect math is appraisal defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: To determine the leasehold value of the subject property, the appraiser must ﬁrst convert the annual income from the community land trust’s ground lease into a leased fee value by dividing the income by the market-derived capitalization rate. The appraiser must then reduce the estimated fee simple value of the subject property by this leased fee value to arrive at their opinion of the leasehold value of the subject property.

examples: Annual rent $300 and cap rate 5.75%: leased fee value = 300/0.0575 = $5,217; fee simple $100,000; leasehold value ≈ $94,800 (as in Guide example).
Appraiser subtracts annual rent ($300) directly from fee simple value: incorrect, because leased fee value must be derived via capitalization.
Appraiser divides fee simple value by cap rate to compute leased fee value: incorrect input; numerator must be annual ground rent income.
Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_004 Step (1) requires dividing annual ground lease income by the market-derived capitalization rate. BR_014_003 defines how that capitalization rate must be extracted in the applicable market condition. Therefore BR_014_003 must be satisfied before BR_014_004 can be executed correctly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_003 is not satisfied (wrong cap rate method or no cap rate), BR_014_004 leased fee and leasehold values are incorrect; appraisal is defective and could lead to ineligible delivery or repurchase risk due to value misstatement.

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_004 requires reducing the estimated fee simple value by the leased fee value to arrive at leasehold value. The fee simple value is produced under BR_014_001 Step 1 (Sales Comparison fee simple value). Without that fee simple value, BR_014_004 cannot be completed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_001 Step 1 is missing/defective, leasehold value cannot be reliably determined; appraisal is deficient and may require revision, delaying underwriting and delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: HCLTV Must Include 100% HELOC Line and UPBs in Numerator
business_rules.description: For first mortgages with subordinate financing under a HELOC, the lender must calculate HCLTV by dividing (i) original loan amount of the first mortgage + (ii) the full amount of any HELOCs (whether or not drawn) + (iii) the UPB of all closed-end subordinate financing by the lesser of the sales price or appraised value of the property.
business_rules.conditions: First mortgage has subordinate financing under a HELOC and HCLTV required
business_rules.consequences: If calculated correctly, eligibility ratios are accurate; if not, eligibility defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (other programs may have different HCLTV details via cross-refs)

source_reference.source_text:
For first mortgages that have subordinate financing under a HELOC, the lender must calculate the HCLTV ratio. This is determined by dividing the sum of the items listed below by the lesser of the sales price or appraised value of the property.
the original loan amount of the first mortgage,
the full amount of any HELOCs (whether or not funds have been drawn), and
the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of all closed-end subordinate financing.

examples: Purchase price $400,000, appraisal $410,000 (use $400,000). First lien $320,000 + HELOC line $50,000 + closed-end 2nd UPB $10,000 => HCLTV = 380,000/400,000 = 95%.
HELOC has $0 drawn but $75,000 line; lender must still include $75,000 in HCLTV numerator.
Refinance: first lien $250,000 and closed-end subordinate UPB $25,000, no HELOC—HCLTV not required under this HELOC-specific rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

dependencies.rationale: When seller-provided subordinate financing has an interest rate more than 2% below current standard rates, it must be treated as a sales concession and deducted from the sales price. Because HCLTV uses the lesser of the sales price or appraised value as denominator, the correct sales price must be finalized (including required deductions) before HCLTV is calculated.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the sales price is not reduced when required, HCLTV can be understated, potentially allowing loans to pass LTV/HCLTV eligibility thresholds incorrectly. This can lead to delivery defects, post-purchase findings, and repurchase/make-whole exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CALCULATION_021_006 uses the appraised value (in the denominator comparison with sales price). BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_009_001 is a core appraisal completeness requirement (minimum three closed comparable sales) supporting a credible value conclusion. If the appraisal does not meet minimum comparable requirements, the appraised value is not acceptable for delivery calculations, requiring cure (revision/new appraisal) before relying on HCLTV.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If appraisal fails the comps requirement, ratio calculations using the appraised value become non-deliverable inputs. The file is blocked pending appraisal cure, delaying underwriting sign-off and delivery timelines.

dependencies.rationale: In new/recently converted projects, BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_009_003 requires a specific mix of inside/outside project settled comps. When HCLTV relies on appraised value as denominator, the appraisal must meet these project-specific comparability standards for the value to be deliverable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the comp mix is missing (e.g., no in-project settled sale), the appraisal must be revised or replaced; HCLTV-based eligibility checks may need to be re-run with the corrected value, delaying approval and delivery.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CALCULATION_021_006 requires dividing by the lesser of sales price or appraised value. For CLT ground lease/leasehold estates, BR_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CALCULATION_014_004 provides the required leasehold value computation that underpins the appraised value conclusion in the CLT appraisal methodology. Without a compliant CLT value conclusion, HCLTV may be computed on an incorrect/unsupported value.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_004 fails, the loan may proceed with an inaccurate appraised value, producing misstated HCLTV and potentially incorrect eligibility determinations (e.g., approving a loan that should fail ratio limits or vice versa). Typically results in underwriting suspense until an updated appraisal is obtained.

dependencies.rationale: BR_021_006 depends on appraised value as an input. BR_023_009 governs a material appraisal component (ANSI Z765-2021 above-/below-grade square footage reporting) that can impact the reported appraised value. Therefore, BR_023_009 serves as a quality/compliance validation layer supporting the appraisal value used in HCLTV.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_009 fails, the appraisal may be deemed defective, leading to appraisal revision/replacement. HCLTV calculations based on the defective appraisal must be recomputed, causing underwriting delays and potential eligibility changes if value changes materially.

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial space percent = nonres sqft ÷ total sqft
business_rules.description: Commercial space allocation is calculated as total non-residential square footage divided by total square footage of the project or building. Lenders are responsible for determining total project square footage, non-residential space square footage, and residential space square footage. The calculation includes commercial space even if residential and commercial owners are represented by separate associations. Rental apartments and hotels located within the project must be classified as commercial space; certain amenities and some parking facilities may be excluded as stated.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating commercial/mixed-use space in condo/co-op project eligibility
business_rules.consequences: Calculated commercial space percentage drives application of project eligibility tests
business_rules.exceptions: Commercial parking facilities can be excluded; certain amenities excluded

source_reference.source_text: Note: Rental apartments and hotels located within the project must be classiﬁed as commercial space even though these may be considered “residential” in nature. Commercial parking facilities can be excluded from the commercial space calculation. Calculation of Commercial Space . Commercial space allocation is calculated by dividing the total non-residential square footage by the total square footage of the project or building. Lenders are responsible for determining the total square footage of the project, the square footage of the non-residential space, and the residential space square footage.

examples: Project totals 200,000 sqft; 30,000 sqft retail/hotel = non-residential; commercial space % = 30,000/200,000 = 15%.
Mixed-use building has 10,000 sqft HOA-owned office leased to a private business; that space is included in non-residential sqft for the percentage calculation.
Project includes a resident-only fitness center owned by HOA; it is excluded from non-residential sqft as an amenity designated for exclusive use of unit owners.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Fully indexed rate equals index plus margin rounded to 1/8 using 90-day index
business_rules.description: The fully indexed rate is calculated as the applicable index value plus the mortgage margin, rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. If the index plus gross margin is exactly equidistant between the higher and lower one-eighth percent, Fannie Mae rounds down. The applicable index value used for the fully indexed rate is any index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating fully indexed rate at origination for initial note rate comparisons
business_rules.consequences: If calculated per rule, compliant fully indexed rate; if not, noncompliant rate calculation
business_rules.exceptions: Unless specific product terms provide otherwise (rounding rule caveat)

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Calculating the Fully Indexed Rate
source_reference.source_text: The fully indexed rate is the sum of the value of the applicable index and the mortgage margin, which is then rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. Note : Unless speciﬁc product terms provide otherwise, if the index plus gross margin equals a number that is equidistant between the higher and lower one-eighth percent, Fannie Mae rounds down to the nearest one-eighth percent. The applicable index value that determines the fully indexed rate is any index value in eﬀect during the 90 days that precede the note date.

examples: Index 4.983% + margin 2.250% = 7.233%; rounded to nearest 1/8% (0.125) gives 7.250% fully indexed rate.
Index 5.000% + margin 2.250% = 7.250% exactly; fully indexed rate remains 7.250%.
If note date is 2026-06-30, the selected index value must be an index value that was in effect during the 90 days preceding 2026-06-30.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until corrected

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

business_rules.rule_name: Closing Costs in Acquisition Cost Limited to 3% of Price
business_rules.description: Total acquisition cost is calculated as purchase price plus permitted closing costs paid to third parties, but only up to an amount no more than 3 percent of the purchase price (and acceptable to Freddie Mac). When determining the “lesser of value or cost” limiter for permitted Mortgage amount, the system must compute total acquisition cost using this capped closing-cost component.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when computing total acquisition cost for permitted Mortgage amount sizing
business_rules.consequences: If closing costs exceed 3%, excess must be excluded from total acquisition cost used for sizing
business_rules.exceptions: Freddie Mac acceptance discretion noted; no numeric exception stated

source_reference.source_text: The total acquisition cost is defined as the sum of : (1) the purchase price and (2) permitted closing costs paid to third parties in connection with the acquisition in an amount no more than three percent of the purchase price and acceptable to Freddie Mac in its sole and absolute discretion.

examples: Purchase price $10,000,000 and third-party closing costs $250,000 (2.5%): total acquisition cost = $10,250,000.
Purchase price $10,000,000 and third-party closing costs $450,000 (4.5%): only $300,000 (3%) may be included; total acquisition cost = $10,300,000 for sizing.
If closing costs are 3% exactly, the full 3% amount is includable (subject to Freddie Mac acceptability).
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Floating-rate interest accrues on 360-day year using actual days
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac calculates interest on a Floating-Rate Mortgage on the basis of a 360-day year and the actual number of days in the interest accrual period (Actual/360 day count convention).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Floating-Rate Mortgages under Guide Chapter 17.3(a).
business_rules.consequences: If interest accrual basis differs, accounting/remittance may be rejected or corrected.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac will calculate interest on a Floating -Rate Mortgage on the basis of a 360 -day year and the actual number of days in the period for which interest is being calculated. As interest on a Floating -Rate Mortgage will accrue at a variable interest rate, there will not be a fixed annual debt service amount for the Mortgage.

examples: For a 31-day month, monthly interest uses 31/360 of the annual rate times principal.
For a 28-day February, monthly interest uses 28/360 of the annual rate times principal.
Servicer using 30/360 instead of Actual/360 must correct interest calculations to match Freddie Mac.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit day-count basis; operational impact is interest and remittance calculations.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Liquidity use fee daily rate uses 365 or 366 days
business_rules.description: For variable-rate Mortgages under the Targeted Affordable Housing Bond Credit Enhancement Forward Contract, the annual liquidity use fee on any Freddie Mac-provided funds (or withdrawals from the principal reserve fund) must be converted to a daily fee by dividing by 365 days (366 in a leap year) and multiplying by the actual number of days elapsed since the payment or withdrawal.
business_rules.conditions: Variable-rate Mortgage where a liquidity use fee applies to a Freddie Mac payment/withdrawal to purchase tendered bonds.
business_rules.consequences: If calculated correctly, correct fee is payable to Freddie Mac; if miscalculated, servicer/seller must correct billing/remittance and may owe underpaid amounts.
business_rules.exceptions: Leap year uses 366 days instead of 365.

source_reference.source_text: The computation of the annual fee on the amount of payment or withdrawal will be converted to a daily fee by dividing by 365  days  (366 days in a leap year) and multiplying by the actual number of days elapsed since the payment or withdrawal.

examples: Annual liquidity fee is 0.50% on a $2,000,000 withdrawal; for 30 days in a non-leap year: daily fee = (0.005/365)*2,000,000*30 = $821.92.
Same withdrawal in a leap year with 10 elapsed days: daily fee = (0.005/366)*2,000,000*10 = $273.22.
Servicer uses 365 in a leap year and overstates the fee; correction is required to match the 366-day basis.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Breakage Fee Triggered Only When Reduction Exceeds 10% of Locked Amount
business_rules.description: When Freddie Mac accepts an early rate lock application with nonmaterial modifications that include a reduced mortgage amount, no Breakage Fee is due if the reduction is 10% or less of the rate locked mortgage amount. If the reduction is more than 10% of the rate locked mortgage amount, Freddie Mac may collect a Breakage Fee calculated on the difference between 90% of the rate locked mortgage amount and the Reduced Mortgage Amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac issues Acceptance Letter with Reduced Mortgage Amount
business_rules.consequences: If reduction ≤10%, no Breakage Fee; if reduction >10%, Breakage Fee may be charged per formula
business_rules.exceptions: Applies "Except as otherwise set forth in this chapter"

source_reference.section_id: 27.19(c)(2) Breakage Fee for Reduced Mortgage Amount (04/15/21)
source_reference.source_text: If the reduction of the Mortgage amount is 10% or less of the rate locked Mortgage amount, then no Breakage Fee will be due. If the reduction of the Mortgage amount is more than 10 percent of the rate locked Mortgage amount, then Freddie Mac, at its option, will collect a Breakage Fee from the Seller calculated on the difference between 90 percent of the rate locked Mortgage amount and the Reduced Mortgage Amount.

examples: Rate locked amount $10,000,000 reduced to $9,200,000 (8% reduction): no Breakage Fee is due.
Rate locked amount $10,000,000 reduced to $8,700,000 (13% reduction): Breakage Fee may be charged on (0.90×$10,000,000) − $8,700,000 = $300,000.
Rate locked amount $5,000,000 reduced to $4,500,000 (10% reduction): no Breakage Fee is due under this section.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBL application fee is 10 bps earned on day 5 at 2 pm
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage, the application fee equals 10 basis points (0.10%) of the Loan Amount and is deemed earned by Freddie Mac at 2:00 pm Eastern time on the fifth Business Day following the day the Seller/Servicer entered the loan status as Under Application in the Pipeline Management Tool (PMT). The fee is nonrefundable except for Top Markets or as otherwise specified in writing by Freddie Mac; for Top Markets, the fee is waived upon written confirmation that the Coupon Rate has been locked unless otherwise specified.
business_rules.conditions: When Seller/Servicer enters SBL loan status as Under Application in PMT
business_rules.consequences: Meets: fee earned/charged per timing; Violated: Seller still owes fee per Guide terms
business_rules.exceptions: Top Markets waiver upon written rate-lock confirmation; other written Freddie Mac terms

source_reference.source_text: 18SBL.5     Standard delivery —application fee ( 04/22/25 ) An application fee equal to 10 basis points of the Loan Amount will be deemed earned by Freddie Mac from Seller/Servicer  at 2:00 pm Eastern time on the fifth Business Day following the day the Seller/Servicer entered the loan status as Under Application in the Pipeline Management Tool (PMT). The application fee will be nonrefundable, except  for Properties located in Top Markets , or unless otherwise specified in writing by Freddie Mac.

examples: Loan Amount $7,500,000; fee = $7,500 (0.10%) and is earned at 2:00 pm ET on the 5th Business Day after PMT status entry.
Property is a Top Market; after written confirmation that the coupon is locked, the 10 bps fee is waived unless Freddie Mac states otherwise.
Seller requests refund after withdrawal on a non-Top Market deal; refund not permitted absent written Freddie Mac exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Application fee charged / due to Freddie Mac

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Report Remediation Cost Estimates Using 3 Source Types
business_rules.description: The Servicer must estimate and report remediation costs for deferred maintenance, life safety hazards, environmental issues, and moisture/mold issues. Acceptable sources for cost estimates include: (1) bids received by the property manager, (2) cost projections by the property manager, and (3) the inspector’s estimate based on experience or industry cost standards.
business_rules.conditions: When identified issues require remediation cost reporting in an assessment
business_rules.consequences: If cost estimates and sources provided, reporting is complete; if absent, reporting is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must estimate and report the cost to remediate any Deferred Maintenance, Life Safety Hazards, environmental issues and moisture or Mold issues. Sources for these cost estimates include: • Bids received by the property manager, • Cost projections by the property manager, and • The inspector’s estimate based on experience or industry cost standards

examples: Servicer reports $48,000 mold remediation based on two vendor bids provided by the property manager—compliant.
Servicer identifies a life safety hazard but provides no cost estimate—noncompliant reporting.
Servicer uses inspector estimate referencing industry unit costs for roof replacement—compliant source type.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'must estimate and report'; sources are enumerated clearly.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Noncompliance finding; require updated cost reporting

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 032_003 defines the required scope of physical condition evaluation and the specific risk/condition items to be assessed (deferred maintenance, life safety hazards, environmental/moisture issues, etc.). 032_004 requires estimating and reporting remediation costs for those same issue categories. The estimation obligation depends on the identification and evaluation outputs from 032_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 032_003 is incomplete, 032_004 outputs will be incomplete/incorrect, leading to deficient inspections, improper risk rating, and missed borrower/Freddie Mac notifications.

business_rules.rule_name: Credit facility fee is percent of UPB excess 03/31/11
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac’s credit facility fee is calculated as a percentage of the excess of the Mortgage UPB over the balance in any principal reserve fund, disregarding any investment earnings accumulated in the principal reserve fund. The Servicer must remit this credit facility fee to Freddie Mac each month from payments collected under the reimbursement and security agreement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to TAH BCE transactions with a credit facility fee due monthly
business_rules.consequences: If calculated and remitted monthly, obligation is met; if not remitted, fee remains due
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac will receive a credit facility fee, which will be expressed as a percentage of the excess of the UPB of the Mortgage over the balance in any principal reserve fund (disregarding any investment earnings accumulated in the principal reserve fund) . The Servicer must remit the credit facility fee to Freddie Mac each month from payments collected under the reimbursement and security agreement.

examples: UPB $10,000,000; principal reserve fund balance $1,500,000 (ignore earnings): fee base = $8,500,000; apply the negotiated percentage and remit monthly.
UPB $10,000,000; principal reserve fund has $1,500,000 plus $20,000 earnings: fee base still uses $1,500,000 (earnings disregarded).
Servicer collects reimbursement-and-security-agreement payments but fails to remit the facility fee in a month; fee remains due for that month.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee collection through remittance; potential noncompliance remedies

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 034_002 requires calculating and remitting a credit facility fee each month 'from payments collected under the reimbursement and security agreement.' 033_005 governs timely deposit of payments and Reserve funds after receipt. While the exact fee source is not limited to custodial accounts here, deposit controls are foundational to ensuring collected funds are available and tracked for monthly fee remittance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 033_005 fails, collected funds may be delayed/misapplied, potentially causing late/incorrect monthly fee remittance and reconciliation issues.

business_rules.rule_name: Variable-rate liquidity fee is percent of UPB 03/31/11
business_rules.description: For a variable-rate Mortgage, Freddie Mac’s liquidity facility fee is calculated as a percentage of the UPB of the Mortgage without reduction by amounts on deposit in any principal reserve fund. The Servicer will remit the liquidity facility fee to Freddie Mac each month from payments collected under the reimbursement and security agreement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the Mortgage is variable-rate and liquidity fee is applicable
business_rules.consequences: If fee is computed on full UPB and remitted monthly, obligation is met; if reduced by reserve deposits, calculation is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: In the case of a variable -rate Mortgage, Freddie Mac will receive a liquidity facility fee, which will be expressed as a percentage of the UPB of the Mortgage (without reduction of the UPB by amounts on deposit in any principal reserve fund). The Servicer will remit the liquidity facility fee to Freddie Mac each month from payments collected under the reimbursement and security agreement.

examples: Variable-rate UPB $12,000,000; principal reserve deposit $2,000,000: fee base remains $12,000,000 (no reduction) and is remitted monthly.
Servicer incorrectly reduces UPB by reserve deposits before applying liquidity fee percentage; calculation violates the rule and must be corrected.
Fixed-rate Mortgage: liquidity facility fee section does not apply because it is limited to variable-rate mortgages.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee collection through remittance; potential noncompliance remedies

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 034_003 requires calculating and remitting a liquidity facility fee monthly from collected payments under the reimbursement and security agreement. 033_005 ensures prompt deposit/handling of received funds, supporting availability and traceability for monthly remittance processing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 033_005 fails, remittance execution and reconciliation of monthly liquidity facility fee may be delayed or incorrect, increasing compliance and operational risk.

business_rules.rule_name: SFHA Flood Coverage Includes 12 Months Business Income
business_rules.description: For each income-generating building fully or partially located in a SFHA, required flood insurance must be at least: (i) IV of first two floors above grade, plus (ii) IV of floors below grade, plus (iii) 12 months of business income/rental value for the building, and (iv) IV of Borrower-owned contents/business personal property within the building.
business_rules.conditions: When an income-generating building is fully/partially in a SFHA (Zone A or V)
business_rules.consequences: Meets minimum formula: acceptable; below minimum: insufficient flood coverage
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For each income -generating building that is fully or partially located in a SFHA, Freddie Mac requires flood insurance equal to at least the following: • The IV of the first two floors of the building above grade, plus • The IV of any floors below grade, plus • 12 months of business income/rental value associated with the building, and • The IV of Borrower -owned contents or business personal property within the building.

examples: Building has IV first two floors $6MM, below-grade IV $1MM, BI 12 months $0.8MM, contents $0.2MM: minimum required = $8.0MM total.
Policy covers $6.5MM total for same building: noncompliant (short of $8.0MM minimum).
Building has no below-grade floors (IV below grade $0): minimum excludes that component but still must include 12 months BI and contents.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: SBL default date uses failure date 46SBL.6(d)(2)
business_rules.description: For SBL Loss calculation purposes, if the Loan Documents provide a grace or cure period for a Borrower failure, the Mortgage Default is deemed to have occurred on the date of the failure (not the end of the grace or cure period). This deemed default date governs SBL Loss timing components (e.g., interest accrual start).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Loan Documents include grace/cure and SBL Loss is calculated
business_rules.consequences: If grace/cure exists, use failure date for default date; using cure-end date is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For purposes of calculation of the amount of the loss for an SBL Mortgage (“SBL Loss”), if the Loan Documents provide a grace or cure period with respect to a failure by the Borrower to comply with one or more of its obligations, the Mortgage Default will be deemed to have occurred on the date of the failure, not the end of the grace or cure period.

examples: Borrower misses a payment due 05/01; note allows 10-day cure → Mortgage Default deemed 05/01 for SBL Loss timing.
Borrower fails to maintain insurance on 03/10; cure period ends 03/25 → default deemed 03/10 for SBL Loss.
Servicer starts default-interest calculation from cure-end date → incorrect; must start from failure date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss obligation recalculation and corrective action

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: SBL loss equals UPB plus costs plus interest minus recoveries
business_rules.description: SBL Loss equals (i) unpaid principal balance of the Defaulted Mortgage as of the Mortgage Default, plus (ii) Default or Modification Resolution Costs as of the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date, plus (iii) interest due from Borrower under the Loan Documents from the Mortgage Default date until the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date not otherwise included in costs, minus any applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac calculates SBL Loss on/after a Mortgage Default
business_rules.consequences: If calculated, forms basis of SBL Loss Obligation (subject to Loss Maximum); errors require correction
business_rules.exceptions: Subtract applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries

source_reference.source_text: The terms “SBL Loss” or “SBL Losses” mean the sum of each of the following, minus any applicable Default Recoveries or Modification Recoveries: • The unpaid principal balance of the Defaulted Mortgage as of the Mortgage Default • Default or Modification Resolution Costs as of the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date • Interest due from Borrower under the Loan Documents from the date of the Mortgage Default until the SBL Loss Obligation Calculation Date not otherwise included in the calculation of Default or Modification Resolution Costs

examples: UPB at default $1,200,000 + costs $80,000 + interest $20,000 − recoveries $150,000 = SBL Loss $1,150,000.
No recoveries yet: UPB $900,000 + costs $25,000 + interest $8,000 = SBL Loss $933,000.
If some interest is included in resolution costs, exclude that portion from the separate interest add-on.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss obligation payment demand and reconciliation

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_003 establishes the deemed default date (date of failure, not cure-end) for SBL loss timing components. BR_002_004 explicitly calculates interest “from the Mortgage Default date until the… Calculation Date,” making BR_002_003 a necessary upstream determiner of inputs to BR_002_004.
