dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_003 is not applied, interest accrual timing in BR_002_004 can be misstated, causing incorrect SBL Loss amounts, billing/settlement disputes, and required recalculations.

business_rules.rule_name: Lease audit unit count may truncate decimals if at least 10 units
business_rules.description: When computing the number of units to be included for lease-related verification, if the calculated unit count results in a decimal, the Seller/Servicer may truncate it down to the nearest whole number, provided the minimum 10-unit requirement is still met (e.g., 10.9 becomes 10).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL lease audit sampling when calculation yields a decimal value
business_rules.consequences: Met: sampling count accepted; Violated: sampling count noncompliant/rework needed
business_rules.exceptions: Truncation allowed only if minimum 10-unit requirement remains satisfied

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8SBL – SBL Property Fundamentals, lease audit units
source_reference.source_text: If the calculation results in a decimal, it can be truncated to the nearest whole number so long as the minimum 10 -unit requirement is met (e.g., 10.9 units becomes 10 units).

examples: Calculated sample size is 10.9 units; Seller truncates to 10 units and meets the 10-unit minimum.
Calculated sample size is 9.4 units; Seller cannot truncate to 9 because it would fall below the 10-unit minimum.
Calculated sample size is 15.2 units; Seller truncates to 15 units (still ≥ 10) for the audit.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: SBL nondelivery breakage fee equals 2% of proposed mortgage amount
business_rules.description: As liquidated damages for the nondelivery of an SBL Mortgage under the standard delivery option, Freddie Mac will charge the Seller/Servicer a Breakage Fee equal to two percent (2%) of the proposed SBL Mortgage amount. Collection of the Breakage Fee does not prevent Freddie Mac from exercising other Guide remedies.
business_rules.conditions: Freddie Mac determines there has been a nondelivery of an SBL Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If nondelivery occurs, Seller/Servicer is charged Breakage Fee = 0.02 × proposed amount
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 18SBL.9(e) Calculation of the Breakage Fee (06/30/16)
source_reference.source_text: e. Calculation of the Breakage Fee (06/30/16) As liquidated damages for the nondelivery of an SBL Mortgage, Freddie Mac will charge the Seller/Servicer a Breakage Fee equal to two percent of the proposed SBL Mortgage amount. Freddie Mac’s collection of the Breakage Fee will not prevent it from exercising any other remedies set forth in the Guide.

examples: Proposed SBL Mortgage amount $8,000,000 and nondelivery determined: Breakage Fee = $160,000.
Proposed SBL Mortgage amount $25,500,000 and nondelivery determined: Breakage Fee = $510,000.
Nondelivery occurs and Breakage Fee is collected: Freddie Mac may still terminate the purchase contract or pursue other remedies.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Charge breakage fee; terminate purchase contract

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_PROCESS_020_006 establishes the mandatory timing and completeness requirements for an SBL Final Delivery Package. BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_CALCULATION_020_007 then defines liquidated damages for nondelivery. In automated workflows, a missed/failed final delivery is a trigger condition that can escalate to a nondelivery determination and fee calculation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If final delivery is not completed on time (or not completed at all), Freddie may determine nondelivery and assess the 2% Breakage Fee, along with other Guide remedies. Operationally this drives financial loss, dispute handling, and potential counterparty performance monitoring.

business_rules.rule_name: Less than 2 years self-employment allowed with 12 months returns
business_rules.description: A borrower with less than a two-year history of self-employment may be considered if the borrower’s most recent signed personal and business federal income tax returns reflect a full year (12 months) of self-employment income from the current business, and the loan file contains documentation supporting prior income receipt at the same or greater level in a same-products/services field or similar-responsibilities occupation.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has <2 years self-employment and income is used to qualify
business_rules.consequences: If 12 months returns + prior income support exist, income may be used; else exclude
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated conditional allowance

source_reference.source_text: However, the income of a person who has less than a two-year history of self-employment may be considered, as long as the borrower’s most recent signed personal and business federal income tax returns reﬂect a full year (12 months) of self-employment income from the current business. The loan ﬁle must also contain documentation to support the history of receipt of prior income at the same (or greater) level

examples: Borrower self-employed 18 months; most recent signed returns show 12 months, and prior W-2 income in same trade at equal/higher level: lender may consider income.
Borrower self-employed 14 months; returns cover only 9 months: lender may not use self-employment income under this exception.
Borrower self-employed 20 months; returns cover 12 months but prior employment was in unrelated field with different duties: exception not met without additional support.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_012_003 sets the general requirement: obtain a two-year history for self-employment income. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_012_004 defines the pathway when the borrower has less than a two-year history—therefore the exception is triggered by failing/being unable to satisfy the general rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 2-year history requirement is not assessed correctly, the lender may incorrectly apply the exception (using ineligible income) or incorrectly deny/underwrite (excluding eligible income).

business_rules.rule_name: One year tax returns allowed only with 5-year business and 25% ownership
business_rules.description: The lender may provide one year of personal and business tax returns for self-employed income if: (1) the business has been in existence for five years as reflected on the Form 1003, and (2) the borrower has had an ownership share of 25% or more for the past five years consecutively. For partnerships/S-corps/corporations, the business return must support the Form 1003; if the business existed before the borrower reached 25% ownership, the lender must demonstrate 25%+ ownership for at least five years consecutively. All businesses must be assessed separately for the five-year benchmark when multiple self-employment income sources exist.
business_rules.conditions: When lender seeks to document self-employed income with 1 year of tax returns
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, 1-year returns permitted; if not, provide 2 years returns/transcripts
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated eligibility to use 1-year returns

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.5-01, Verification of Income (one-year returns option)
source_reference.source_text: The lender may provide one year of personal and business tax returns if the following requirements are met: the business from which the borrower is using self-employed income must have been in existence for ﬁve years as reﬂected on the Form 1003, and the borrower has had an ownership share of 25% or more for the past ﬁve years consecutively, and for partnerships, S corporations and corporations, the federal income tax return for the business must support the information reﬂected on the Form 1003.

examples: Borrower has owned 30% of an S-corp for 6 consecutive years and business exists 6 years: lender may use 1 year of returns (if other requirements met).
Borrower owns 25% now but only 3 years at that level (business exists 10 years): lender may not use 1 year returns; must obtain 2 years.
Borrower has two self-employment sources: one business meets 5-year benchmark, the other does not; lender may apply different return-year requirements per business.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Established condo requires 90% sold and 100% complete
business_rules.description: A condominium project is classified as an established condo project only if all criteria are met: (1) at least 90% of total units conveyed to unit purchasers, (2) project is 100% complete (all units and common elements), (3) not subject to additional phasing or annexation, and (4) HOA control turned over to unit owners. A project may still be treated as established with less than 90% sold if the shortfall is due to developer rental holdback and additional requirements are satisfied, including developer holdback ≤ 20% of total units and no active or pending special assessments.
business_rules.conditions: When subject property is in a condominium project under project standards
business_rules.consequences: If classified as established, established-project eligibility criteria apply; otherwise treat as new condo
business_rules.exceptions: May treat as established if rental holdback causes <90% sold and holdback ≤20%

source_reference.source_text: Established condo projectA project for which all of the following are true: • at least 90% of the total units in the project have been conveyed to unit purchasers; • the project is 100% complete, including all units and common elements; • the project is not subject to additional phasing or annexation; and • control of the HOA has been turned over to the unit owners. A project may also be treated as an established project with less than 90% of the units sold to unit purchasers, provided the deﬁcit is the result of the developer holding back units for rent... the developer’s share of the units held back for rental is no more than 20% of the project’s total units; ... there are no active or pending special assessments in the project.

examples: 92% of units sold; project 100% complete; no phasing; HOA owner-controlled → classify as established condo.
85% sold because developer holds 15% as rentals; HOA fees current on held units; no special assessments → may treat as established condo.
85% sold with developer holding 25% as rentals → cannot use established exception; treat as new condo project.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until correct project type/eligibility applied

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: New condo project when under 90% sold or phased
business_rules.description: A condominium project is classified as a new condo project when any of the following are true: (1) fewer than 90% of total units conveyed to unit purchasers (or fewer than 80% if the project meets the established-project rental holdback exception), (2) project not fully completed (proposed/new construction or proposed/incomplete conversion), (3) newly converted, (4) subject to additional phasing or annexation, or (5) HOA still in developer control.
business_rules.conditions: When determining condo project type for subject property eligibility review
business_rules.consequences: If any condition is true, apply new-condo eligibility criteria and related review requirements
business_rules.exceptions: 80% threshold applies only if established-project exception criteria are met

source_reference.source_text: New condo projectA project for which one or more of the following is true: • fewer than 90% of the total units in the project have been conveyed to unit purchasers (or 80% if it meets the exception noted in the row above); • the project is not fully completed, such as proposed construction, new construction, or the proposed or incomplete conversion of an existing building to a condo; • the project is newly converted; • the project is subject to additional phasing or annexation; or • HOA still in the developer’s control.

examples: Project is 100% complete but only 88% conveyed to purchasers → classify as new condo project.
Project is 82% conveyed and developer holds 18% rentals, but HOA still developer-controlled → new condo (HOA control triggers).
Project is 79% conveyed even with rental holdback scenario → new condo (below 80% exception threshold).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery if wrong project standards applied

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Significant buydown is over 2% or over 2 years
business_rules.description: A temporary interest rate buydown is a “significant interest rate buydown” if it provides for (i) more than a 2% difference between the actual note rate and the bought-down rate, or (ii) a buydown period greater than two years. Such loans require Special Feature Code (SFC) 014 at delivery (and are subject to Fannie Mae MBS pooling composition restrictions for this buydown type).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan has a temporary interest rate buydown feature.
business_rules.consequences: If significant, SFC 014 must be delivered; missing SFC may delay/correct delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: Not significant if ≤2% difference AND buydown period ≤2 years.

source_reference.section_id: E-3-19, Acronyms and Glossary of Defined Terms: S (02/07/2024)
source_reference.source_text:
signiﬁcant interest rate buydown
A temporary reduction in the initial interest rate of a mortgage loan that provides for either more than a 2%
diﬀerence between the actual interest rate as stated in the note and the “bought-down” interest rate, or a
buydown period greater than two years. Fannie Mae restricts the percentage of an MBS pool that can be
comprised of mortgages with this type of buydown. Requires SFC 014 at delivery.

examples: Note rate 7.00%, bought-down rate 4.75% (2.25% difference): significant; deliver with SFC 014.
Note rate 6.50%, bought-down rate 5.00% (1.50% diff) but buydown lasts 36 months: significant; SFC 014 required.
Note rate 6.00%, bought-down rate 4.25% (1.75% diff) for 24 months: not significant under this definition.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct glossary definition with explicit numeric tests and delivery code requirement.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady CLTV May Reach 105% Only with Community Seconds
business_rules.description: For HomeReady transactions in the 95.01% to 97% LTV/CLTV/HCLTV criteria set, CLTV may be increased up to 105% only when the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady loan with a subordinate lien and CLTV above 95%.
business_rules.consequences: If subordinate is Community Seconds, CLTV up to 105% permitted; otherwise not.
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only when the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan.

source_reference.section_id: B5-6-01, Requirements for HomeReady Transactions with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV of 95.01–97%
source_reference.source_text: LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% Note: The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan.

examples: 97% LTV first mortgage plus Community Seconds lien pushes CLTV to 103% → permitted by exception.
96% LTV first mortgage plus non-Community Seconds HELOC pushes CLTV to 101% → not permitted under this exception.
95.5% CLTV with Community Seconds to 105% exactly → permitted; 105.25% would violate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_002 defines the high-ratio HomeReady eligibility band (95.01%–97%) and the required conditions (DU-only, fixed-rate, etc.). BR_020_003 is explicitly an exception 'for HomeReady transactions in the 95.01% to 97%... criteria set' allowing CLTV up to 105% only with a Community Seconds subordinate lien.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_002 is not satisfied, applying BR_020_003 would incorrectly allow excessive CLTV, leading to ineligible delivery or incorrect underwriting approval.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady counseling credit needs 12-month completion, DU data, SFC 184
business_rules.description: For HomeReady loans, borrowers who complete housing counseling within 12 months prior to closing may be eligible for a loan-level price adjustment (LLPA) credit. To apply the LLPA credit, the Housing Counseling data must be submitted to DU and the loan must be delivered with Special Feature Code (SFC) 184.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering a HomeReady loan seeking the housing counseling LLPA credit
business_rules.consequences: If DU data or SFC 184 missing, LLPA credit cannot be applied as described
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (this is an optional credit; base eligibility unaffected if not taken)

source_reference.source_text: HomeReady loans where borrowers who complete housing counseling within 12 months prior to closing may be eligible for a loan-level price adjustment credit. For the LLPA credit to be applied to the loan the Housing Counseling data must be submitted to DU, and the loan must be delivered with SFC 184.

examples: HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 6 months before closing; counseling data submitted to DU; loan delivered with SFC 184: LLPA credit eligible.
HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 10 months before closing; data not submitted to DU: LLPA credit not applied.
HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 14 months before closing: outside 12-month window; LLPA credit not eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pricing adjustment not granted / delivery data correction

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_005 applies 'For HomeReady loans' and requires counseling completion within 12 months and submission of counseling data to DU plus delivery with SFC 184. A loan cannot validly claim a HomeReady LLPA credit unless it is eligible as HomeReady under BR_020_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_001 fails, the lender may incorrectly apply a HomeReady LLPA credit (pricing and delivery defect), leading to post-purchase pricing adjustments or remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_025_006 requires omitting the trust name from delivery data and delivering SFC 168. BR_028_005 requires delivering SFC 184 (and DU submission) to obtain the HomeReady housing counseling LLPA credit. When a HomeReady loan involves a revocable trust mortgagor and seeks the counseling LLPA credit, both sets of delivery coding/data rules must be applied together to avoid delivery defects and pricing issues.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not combined, lender may deliver missing one of the required SFCs (168 or 184) or include prohibited trust name data, causing delivery correction requests and/or loss of LLPA credit.

business_rules.rule_name: DU limited waiver invalid if file contains undisclosed contradictory data
business_rules.description: For loans submitted to DU, the lender must review the entire underwriting file for data or information not submitted to DU or inconsistent with what was submitted. If derogatory or contradictory information is found, the lender must take appropriate action (e.g., further investigate to determine whether it would change the DU recommendation, or set aside the DU recommendation if grounds exist for a different decision). If such information exists and is not appropriately addressed, the limited waiver of representations and warranties is no longer valid even for an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans submitted to DU for evaluation and potential limited waiver
business_rules.consequences: If acted on, lender can re-evaluate; if not, limited waiver becomes invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For loans submitted to DU for evaluation, the lender must review the entire underwriting ﬁle to determine whether it includes any data or other information that was either not submitted to DU, or is inconsistent with any data or information that was in fact submitted to DU. If any such information (especially of a derogatory or contradictory nature) is found, the lender must take appropriate action, such as further investigating the information, to see if it would change the DU recommendation, or setting aside the DU recommendation

examples: DU casefile shows no delinquent housing history, but file later reveals recent 60-day mortgage delinquency not entered into DU: lender must re-evaluate; waiver is invalid if not addressed.
Borrower employment in file conflicts with DU-submitted employer and indicates job loss prior to closing: lender must investigate and potentially set aside DU recommendation.
Lender discovers new undisclosed liability post-submission but before closing: must determine whether DU recommendation changes; waiver depends on proper action.
Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Manual UW collections exempt if under $250 or $1,000 total
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, non-medical collection accounts and charge-offs on non-mortgage accounts do not have to be paid off at or prior to closing when (a) the balance of an individual account is less than $250 OR (b) the total balance of all such accounts is $1,000 or less. Amounts exceeding these limits must be paid off at or prior to closing.
business_rules.conditions: When decision_type indicates manual underwriting and collections/charge-offs are non-medical
business_rules.consequences: If within thresholds, payoff not required; if over thresholds, payoff required or loan is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to manually underwritten loans and non-medical accounts

source_reference.section_id: B3-6-07, Collections, Charge-Offs, Judgments, and Liens
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, non-medical collection accounts and charge-oﬀs on non-mortgage accounts do not have to be paid oﬀ at or prior to closing if the balance of an individual account is less than $250 or the total balance of all accounts is $1,000 or less. Non-medical collection accounts and charge-oﬀs on non-mortgage accounts that exceed these limits must be paid oﬀ at or prior to closing.

examples: Manual UW loan: borrower has one $200 non-medical collection; payoff is not required by this policy.
Manual UW loan: borrower has five collections totaling $950; payoff is not required by this policy.
Manual UW loan: borrower has collections totaling $1,250; payoff is required at or prior to closing.
Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 sets payoff requirements for collections/charge-offs based on property type/occupancy. BR_032_005 states that for manually underwritten loans, certain non-medical collections/charge-offs do not need to be paid off below specified thresholds—altering the baseline requirement. Therefore, payoff decision logic must first determine underwriting method and apply the exception where applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is missed, the lender may impose unnecessary payoffs (delaying closing, harming borrower liquidity) or, if misapplied, fail to require payoff when required (creating underwriting defects and delivery risk).

business_rules.rule_name: Self-employment status triggers at 25% ownership
business_rules.description: A borrower is considered self-employed if they have a 25% or greater ownership interest in a business. If the borrower meets this ownership threshold, self-employment income underwriting and documentation requirements apply to income used for qualifying.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has ownership interest in any business reported for income
business_rules.consequences: If ≥25% owned, apply self-employment analysis; if <25%, not self-employed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.5-01, Factors to Consider for a Self-Employed Borrower
source_reference.source_text: Any individual who has a 25% or greater ownership interest in a business is considered to be self-employed.
source_reference.source_text_note: Verbatim excerpt is a single sentence; shorter than 30 words due to source brevity.

examples: Borrower owns 30% of an LLC: treat as self-employed and underwrite per B3-3.5 requirements.
Borrower owns 24% of an S-corp and receives W-2 wages only: do not classify as self-employed based on ownership threshold.
Borrower owns 50% of a partnership: self-employment income analysis applies to qualifying income from the business.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: No Reserves Required for 1-Unit Primary and High LTV Refi Loans
business_rules.description: There is no minimum reserve requirement for one-unit principal residence transactions. High LTV refinance loans are exempt from the minimum reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When determining minimum required reserves for 1-unit primary or high LTV refi
business_rules.consequences: Meets: reserve minimum not required; Violates: N/A (exception removes minimum reserve test)
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to stated scenarios; DU may still require additional reserves by risk

source_reference.source_text: Note : There is no minimum reserve requirement for one-unit principal residence transactions. High LTV reﬁnance loans are exempt from the minimum reserve requirements.

examples: One-unit primary residence purchase: lender does not need to document a minimum reserve amount (unless DU conditions it).
High LTV refinance: minimum reserve requirement is waived under this policy; lender applies other underwriting conditions normally.
Two-unit primary residence purchase: this exception does not apply; DU may require 6 months reserves.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: IDA Matching Funds Require 80% LTV/CLTV Exception
business_rules.description: If an IDA program’s matching funds require repayment, may be deferred/forgiven under conditions, or involve a lien against the property, the borrower may use matching funds to supplement the down payment only after meeting minimum borrower contribution requirements. The minimum borrower contribution must come from the borrower’s own funds unless (a) the LTV or CLTV is ≤ 80%, or (b) the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or employer assistance for some/all minimum contributions.
business_rules.conditions: When IDA matching funds are repayable/forgivable/secured by lien on the property
business_rules.consequences: If met: matching funds may be counted per rule; if violated: funds-to-close is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Borrower-own-funds minimum waived if LTV/CLTV ≤ 80% or 1-unit PR gift/grant/employer allowed

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.2-03 (02/06/2019), Use of IDA Funds to Meet Borrower Minimum Contribution Requirements
source_reference.source_text: The nonproﬁt agency • requires repayment of the matching funds, • agrees to defer or forgive repayment provided that certain conditions are met, or • ﬁles a lien against the property.The borrower may use the matching funds to supplement the down payment provided they have met the minimum borrower contribution requirements. The minimum borrower contribution must come from the borrower's own funds unless: • the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%; or • the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets the requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or funds received from an employer to pay for some or all of the borrower's minimum contributions.

examples: Purchase of a one-unit principal residence at 95% LTV: IDA matching funds are repayable; borrower must meet minimum contribution from own funds unless gift/grant/employer assistance rules apply.
Rate-term refinance at 75% CLTV: borrower may satisfy minimum contribution requirement exception because CLTV is ≤ 80%.

Purchase at 90% LTV where borrower tries to use only IDA matching funds without any borrower contribution and without qualifying gift/grant/employer assistance: not allowed under this IDA repayment/lien scenario.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / post-purchase remedy exposure

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: No asset documentation required when refinance funds to verify are $500 or less
business_rules.description: For refinance transactions, DU will not require documentation of assets when the total funds to be verified are $500 or less. Lenders must still determine whether additional documentation is warranted based on the borrower and situation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU requires assets to be verified for a refinance and funds-to-verify ≤ $500.
business_rules.consequences: If ≤ $500, DU does not require asset docs; if > $500, asset verification rules apply.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: This level of documentation may not be adequate for every borrower and every situation. The lender must determine whether additional documentation is warranted. DU will not require documentation of assets for reﬁnance transactions when the total funds to be veriﬁed are $500 or less.

examples: Rate/term refinance with required verified funds of $400: DU does not require asset documentation.
Cash-out refinance with required verified funds of $750: DU requires asset documentation per DU messaging.
Limited cash-out refinance with required verified funds of $500: DU does not require asset documentation.
DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster Relief Funds Allow 0% Borrower Contribution
business_rules.description: Borrowers may use lump-sum disaster relief grants or loans to satisfy Fannie Mae’s minimum borrower contribution requirement; no borrower contribution from the borrower’s own funds is required when using this assistance.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower uses a lump-sum disaster relief grant or loan in the transaction
business_rules.consequences: If used, minimum borrower contribution requirement is satisfied; otherwise follow standard rules
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Borrowers may use lump-sum disaster relief grants or loans to satisfy Fannie Mae’s minimum borrower contribution requirement. No borrower contribution is required.

examples: Purchase: Borrower brings $15,000 FEMA grant; lender treats minimum borrower contribution requirement as met (0% required).
Refinance: Borrower receives lump-sum disaster relief loan used for closing costs; no borrower contribution is required under this topic.
If no disaster relief funds are used, lender applies the standard minimum borrower contribution requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase if standard contribution rules misapplied

examples: Disaster relief grant used to replenish savings for reserves
Disaster relief loan with repayment terms impacting DTI
Assistance tied to a declared disaster affecting the subject property area

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op share loan must be first lien with limited assessment subordination
business_rules.description: A co-op share loan must be a first-lien. Fannie Mae permits its lien to be subordinate only where custom dictates, and only to the co-op corporation’s lien against the tenant-stockholder’s shares for unpaid assessments representing the pro rata share of certain project-level expenses (blanket mortgage payments, current year real estate taxes, operating expenses/maintenance fees, and special assessments).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to co-op share loans delivered to Fannie Mae requiring lien position review.
business_rules.consequences: If the share loan is not first-lien (outside permitted subordination), it is ineligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted subordination is limited to unpaid assessment liens as described.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-03, Lien Position for Co-op Share Loans
source_reference.source_text:
The share loan must be a ﬁrst-lien, except that, where custom dictates to the contrary, Fannie Mae will permit
its lien to be subordinate to the co-op corporation’s lien against the tenant-stockholder’s shares for unpaid
assessments that represents the pro rata share of the corporation’s payments for the blanket mortgage, current
year’s real estate taxes, operating expenses or maintenance fees, and special assessments.

examples: Co-op has a standard assessment lien for unpaid maintenance fees that primes the share loan: allowed where customary and limited to unpaid assessments described.
Share loan is junior to a private lender’s UCC lien unrelated to assessments: not permitted; loan ineligible.
Co-op documents grant co-op a broad lien securing unrelated penalties or fines: review required; may exceed permitted subordination scope.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit first-lien rule with narrow exception; limited numerics.

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Single-Entity Ownership Threshold Is Over 2 Units or Over 20%
business_rules.description: A project meets the definition of single-entity ownership when a single entity owns more than 2 units in projects with 5 to 20 units, or owns more than 20% of units in projects with 21 or more units. Units subject to any rental or lease arrangement must be included in the calculation, including lease-purchase and rent-to-own arrangements.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is in a condo/co-op/PUD project undergoing project eligibility review
business_rules.consequences: If threshold exceeded, treat as single-entity ownership concentration for eligibility analysis
business_rules.exceptions: Certain vacant sponsor/developer and affordable housing units may be excluded

source_reference.source_text: A project meets the deﬁnition of single-entity ownership when a single entity (the same individual, investor group, partnership, project sponsor, or corporation) owns more than the following total number of units in the project: projects with 5 to 20 units - 2 units projects with 21 or more units - 20%. Units currently subject to any rental or lease arrangement must be included in the calculation. This includes lease arrangements containing provisions for the future purchase of units such as lease-purchase and rent-to-own arrangements.

examples: 5–20 unit project: One investor owns 3 of 12 units (exceeds 2 units) → single-entity ownership definition met.
21+ unit project: One entity owns 18 of 80 units (22.5%) → exceeds 20% → definition met.
Calculation includes leased units: Entity owns 2 units and controls 1 rent-to-own unit → must include that unit in the count.
Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Single-Entity Ownership Waiver Requires 49% Max and No Special Assessments
business_rules.description: The single-entity ownership requirement may be waived when the transaction is a purchase that will reduce single-entity ownership concentration, provided: units owned by the single entity represent no more than 49% of units; evidence shows the entity is marketing units for sale to reduce concentration to 20% or less; the entity is current on all HOA assessments; and there are no pending or active special assessments in the project.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transaction in a project otherwise meeting single-entity ownership definition
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, waiver may be applied; if not, waiver not permitted
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The single-entity ownership requirement may be waived when the transaction is a purchase transaction that will result in a reduction of the single-entity ownership concentration. In such instances, the following requirements must be met: units owned by the single entity represent no more than 49% of the units; evidence is required that the single entity is marketing units for sale to further reduce single-entity ownership, with the goal of reducing the concentration to 20% or less of the project units; the single entity is current on all HOA assessments; and there are no pending or active special assessments in the project.

examples: Waiver allowed: Entity owns 45% of units, provides listing/marketing evidence, is current on assessments, and there are no special assessments.
Waiver denied: Entity owns 52% of units (exceeds 49%) even if units are marketed for sale.
Waiver denied: Entity owns 40% and markets units, but project has an active special assessment.
Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_EXCEPTION_011_006 is an exception/waiver that applies only when a project otherwise meets the single-entity ownership definition set by BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_DEFINITION_011_005. The waiver’s thresholds (e.g., 49%, marketing evidence to reduce to 20% or less) depend on the same unit-concentration calculations defined in BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_DEFINITION_011_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the single-entity ownership definition is not calculated first, the lender could incorrectly grant a waiver in a project that does not qualify, or miss a permissible waiver, causing unnecessary denials or eligibility violations.

business_rules.rule_name: Bridge Loan Payment Included in DTI Unless Sale Contract Clears Contingencies
business_rules.description: A bridge (or swing) loan creates a contingent liability that must be included in the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations and the DTI ratio. Fannie Mae will waive inclusion in DTI only if the lender documents a fully executed sales contract for the current residence and confirmation that any financing contingencies have been cleared.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower obtains bridge/swing loan before selling current residence
business_rules.consequences: If docs provided, may exclude from DTI; if not, must include in DTI calculation
business_rules.exceptions: Waiver applies only with executed contract and cleared financing contingencies

source_reference.source_text: When a borrower obtains a bridge (or swing) loan, the funds from that loan can be used for closing on a new principal residence before the current residence is sold. This creates a contingent liability that must be considered part of the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations and included in the DTI ratio calculation. Fannie Mae will waive this requirement and not require the debt to be included in the DTI ratio if the following documentation is provided: a fully executed sales contract for the current residence, and conﬁrmation that any ﬁnancing contingencies have been cleared.

examples: Borrower has bridge loan with $800 monthly payment and no signed sales contract: must include $800 in DTI.
Borrower has fully executed contract but contingencies not cleared: still must include bridge loan payment in DTI.
Borrower provides executed sales contract and proof financing contingencies cleared: lender may exclude bridge loan payment from DTI.
Loan uses a bridge loan secured by departing residence until that property sells.
Loan includes swing loan monthly payment in DTI until payoff.

business_rules.rule_name: QC 4506-C Code 10 ends transcript attempts; Codes 1-9 require correction
business_rules.description: During post-closing QC, if the lender attempts to execute Form 4506-C but is unable to obtain a transcript due to IRS rejection Code 10 ('Due to Limitations, the IRS is unable to process this request'), the lender does not need to take further steps to attempt to obtain a transcript. For issues other than rejection Code 10 (including missing/incomplete/illegible/altered information Codes 1-9), the lender should attempt to obtain a corrected and signed Form 4506-C; if the lender chooses not to get the corrected form or is unable to obtain it, the lender must have a process to cite a moderate defect and implement a corrective action plan where appropriate.
business_rules.conditions: When post-closing QC includes tax transcript reverification via Form 4506-C
business_rules.consequences: Code 10 permits stopping; other codes require correction or moderate defect + corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: Code 10 specifically allows no further transcript attempt after rejection

source_reference.source_text: the transcript request is rejected by the IRS with a Code 10 indicating "Due to Limitations, the IRS is unable to process this request"; ... rejected because of missing, incomplete, illegible, or altered information on the Form 4506-C (Codes 1-9)... If the lender attempts to execute Form 4506-C with the IRS but is unable to obtain a transcript due to receiving a rejection Code 10, the lender does not need to take further steps to attempt to obtain a transcript. For issues other than rejection Code 10, the lender should attempt to obtain a corrected and signed Form 4506-C... it must have a process to cite a moderate defect and implement a corrective action plan, where appropriate.

examples: QC requests transcript and IRS returns Code 10 → lender documents result and stops further transcript attempts.
QC request rejected due to borrower name mismatch (Code 2) → lender obtains corrected/signed 4506-C and retries transcript request.
Code 5 rejection and borrower will not re-sign 4506-C → lender cites moderate defect and opens corrective action plan per QC process.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC moderate defect citation and corrective action plan; potential sanctions if systemic

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: C-PACE Program Must Give Lender 30-Day Right to Cure Defaults
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac will approve/consent to C-PACE financing only if the C-PACE program provides prompt notice of any default under the C-PACE financing to the mortgage lender and allows the mortgage lender a right to cure the default of not less than 30 days. This is a minimum condition for C-PACE acceptability to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: When requesting Freddie Mac consent/approval for C-PACE financing
business_rules.consequences: If included: condition satisfied; if absent: Freddie Mac approval denied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The C -PACE program must provide that it will provide prompt notice to the mortgage lender of any default under the C -PACE financing. The C -PACE program must allow the mortgage lender not less than a 30 -day right to cure a default under the C -PACE financing.

examples: C-PACE program notifies lender within 5 days of delinquent tax assessment and provides a 45-day cure period: acceptable on this condition.
C-PACE program provides notice but only a 15-day cure period: fails minimum condition; Freddie Mac consent should be denied.
C-PACE program has no lender-notice requirement for defaults: fails condition; consent should be denied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Consent denied / purchase ineligibility for C-PACE feature

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: SBL repurchase option default is 60-day delinquency or 60-day uncured non-monetary default
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage, a “Repurchase Option Eligible Default” exists if the SBL Borrower is in default due to either (i) a 60 Day Delinquency, or (ii) a Non-Monetary Default that has not been cured within 60 days. This definition controls when the Seller/Servicer may be required/allowed to trigger SBL repurchase processes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Mortgages when Borrower default status is evaluated
business_rules.consequences: If condition exists, repurchase option timeline obligations may be triggered; if not, repurchase option not available on this basis
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 1. SBL Borrower is in default under the applicable SBL Mortgage for either (i) a 60 Day Delinquency, or (ii) a Non- Monetary Default that has not been cured within 60 days (either a “Repurchase Option Eligible Default”).

examples: Borrower misses payments and reaches 60 days delinquent as of 06/01; this is a Repurchase Option Eligible Default.
Borrower violates a reporting covenant on 05/01 and does not cure by 06/30 (60 days); this is a Repurchase Option Eligible Default.
Borrower is 30 days delinquent; repurchase option is not eligible under this definition.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct definitional statement with explicit 60-day thresholds.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBL Repurchase Only for Events After 2020-03-30
business_rules.description: SBL Repurchase Obligations apply only if the underlying event giving rise to the repurchase obligation occurs during the SBL Repurchase Period (as described in Section 46SBL.4(e)). The obligations apply even if the Seller/Servicer endorsed the note or other loan documents to Freddie Mac “without recourse,” and they do not limit Freddie Mac’s other Guide rights against the Seller/Servicer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac asserts SBL Repurchase Obligations under 46SBL.4
business_rules.consequences: If event occurs in Repurchase Period, Freddie Mac may require repurchase; else no repurchase
business_rules.exceptions: None; applies notwithstanding “without recourse” endorsement language

source_reference.source_text: SBL Repurchase Obligations only apply if the underlying event giving rise to the SBL Repurchase Obligation occurs during the SBL Repurchase Period, as described in Section 46SBL.4(e). The SBL Repurchase Obligations apply notwithstanding that the Seller /Servicer may have endorsed any SBL Mortgage Note or other Loan Document to Freddie Mac “without recourse.”

examples: A payment default occurs during the Repurchase Period; Freddie Mac may issue a repurchase notice requiring repurchase.
A defect is discovered after the Repurchase Period ends; this section indicates the repurchase obligation does not apply based on timing.
Seller/Servicer endorsed the note “without recourse,” but an in-period repurchase trigger occurs; repurchase can still be required.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit applicability limitation and ‘without recourse’ carve-out stated in 46SBL.4(a).

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Coinsurance allowed only with written approval before Rate Lock
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac generally will not accept coinsurance (multiple title policies from multiple underwriters for the same transaction). Coinsurance may be allowed only if each title underwriter and policy meets Chapter 29 requirements and Freddie Mac approves the use of coinsurance in writing prior to Rate Lock. Prior to the Origination Date, the Seller/Servicer must submit the coinsured Title Policies to Freddie Mac for review and approval. Freddie Mac also states it does not expect coinsurance for any single SBL Mortgage or pool of SBL Mortgages of $40 million or less; coinsurance for SBL pools in excess of $40 million is subject to Section 29.1(a) and (b).
business_rules.conditions: When a Mortgage transaction proposes coinsurance for title insurance
business_rules.consequences: If not pre-approved in writing, Freddie Mac will not accept coinsured Title Policies
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted only with Freddie Mac written approval prior to Rate Lock

source_reference.source_text: Usually, Freddie Mac will not accept coinsurance (multiple Title Insurance Policies issued by multiple Title Insurance Underwriters for the same transaction). Freddie Mac will consider allowing coinsurance only if the Title Insurance Underwriters and Title Policies each meet the requirements of this chapter and the use of coinsurance is approved in writing by Freddie Mac prior to Rate Lock. Prior to the Origination Date, the Seller/Servicer must submit to Freddie Mac for its review and approval the Title Policies for any Mortgage that will be coinsured. Freddie Mac does not expect that any single SBL Mortgage or pool of SBL Mortgages $40 million or less will require coinsurance. For the financing of a pool of SBL Mortgages in excess of $40 million, coinsurance for such pool is subject to the provisions of Section 29.1(a) and (b).

examples: A Seller proposes two title policies from two underwriters; without written Freddie Mac approval before Rate Lock, the coinsurance structure is rejected for delivery.
An SBL pool of $55,000,000 proposes coinsurance; Seller must obtain Freddie Mac written approval before Rate Lock and comply with Section 29.1(a)-(b).
An SBL pool of $35,000,000 requests coinsurance; Freddie Mac indicates it is not expected and may refuse absent compelling justification and written approval.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_026_001 allows coinsurance only if each underwriter/policy meets requirements and Freddie Mac approves in writing. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_026_003 prohibits using a Title Company on the Multifamily Restricted Vendor List. Therefore, restricted-vendor screening is a prerequisite check before pursuing coinsurance approval and submitting coinsured policies for review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_026_003 fails: (1) coinsurance request/policies will be rejected, (2) closing/title workflows must be restructured with new underwriters, (3) rate lock/closing deadlines may be missed, (4) Freddie Mac may refuse purchase/assumption approval.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_026_001 requires Freddie Mac written approval prior to rate lock. Freddie Mac’s decision will be impacted if the title company/underwriter selection violates BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_026_002 (selection based on improper consideration). Therefore, vendor selection integrity is a prerequisite compliance gate before requesting coinsurance approval.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_026_002 fails: (1) Freddie Mac may refuse the transaction/title structure, (2) potential fraud/anti-kickback concerns, (3) remediation may require vendor replacement and re-issuance of title work, (4) counterparty sanctions risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Equity Conflict Allowed Only Under 5% or Under 25% Limits
business_rules.description: Equity conflicts of interest are restricted; however, limited equity interests are permitted if conditions are met. A non-executive employee engaged in origination/underwriting/servicing may hold up to 5% total direct and indirect equity interest in the Borrower if (i) the employee/family member cannot assume control of the Borrower, (ii) property inspection and lease audit are not delegated to the Seller/Servicer, and (iii) if multiple employee-level owners exist, the 5% threshold applies to the total combined interests per Seller/Servicer. A Seller/Servicer, its affiliate, an executive employee, or an executive employee’s family member may hold less than 25% total direct and indirect interest in the Borrower if (i) it cannot assume control of the Borrower and (ii) property inspection and lease audit are not delegated to the Seller/Servicer.
business_rules.conditions: When Seller/Servicer staff/affiliates have equity interest in a Borrower
business_rules.consequences: If met: equity interest may be acceptable; if violated: escalation/disclosure needed
business_rules.exceptions: Third-party investment vehicles excluded (per section (b))

source_reference.source_text: Such Employee -Level Owner may hold up to five percent of total direct and indirect equity interest in the Borrower so long as: • The Employee -Level Owner does not currently have, or have the ability to assume, control of the Borrower • The property inspection and lease audit is not delegated by Freddie Mac to the Seller/Servicer • If there are multiple Employee -Level Owners with equity interests in the same Borrower, the five percent threshold is applied to total combined interests per Seller/Servicer ... Such Seller/Servicer -Level Owner may hold less than 25 percent of total direct and indirect interest in the Borrower so long as: • The Seller/Servicer -Level Owner does not currently have, or have the ability to assume, control of the Borrower • The property inspection and lease audit is not delegated by Freddie Mac to the Seller/Servicer

examples: Non-executive underwriter owns 3% of Borrower equity and has no control rights; inspection not delegated: permitted within the 5% cap.
Two non-executive employees own 3% and 2.5% in same Borrower; combined 5.5% exceeds 5% cap: must contact Freddie Mac representative.
Seller/Servicer affiliate owns 24.9% in Borrower but cannot assume control and inspection not delegated: permitted under the <25% limit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac refusal to proceed or require remediation/disclosure

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Financial Due Date Adjustment When June 30 Is Impossible
business_rules.description: The Servicer may request an adjustment of the Annual Financial due date by contacting Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Management via PRS if the Borrower’s fiscal year end prevents submission by June 30 (or other Freddie-required date), or if the Mortgage was an acquisition occurring so late in the prior calendar year that available months of data prevent meaningful evaluation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Annual Financial timing/data constraints prevent meaningful/required submission
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, Servicer may request due date adjustment; otherwise due date remains
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer may contact Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Management, Asset Performance and Compliance via PRS to request an adjustment of the Annual Financial due date if: • The Borrower's fiscal year end would prevent the Servicer from submitting the Annual Financial by June 30 or other date required by Freddie Mac. • The Mortgage was for the acquisition of the Property, and the purchase occurred so late in the preceding calendar year that the number of months of data available for review would prevent the Servicer from making a meaningful evaluation.

examples: Fiscal year ends Dec 31; audited financials not available by June 30—Servicer requests adjusted due date via PRS.
Acquisition closed Dec 20; only 11 days of prior-year operations exist—Servicer requests a due date adjustment.
Borrower fiscal year end and acquisition timing do not hinder submission—Servicer should not request adjustment under this rule.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Down units and commercial space inspection is 100% unless sampling allowed
business_rules.description: For property inspections, the inspector must inspect 100% of Down Units and 100% of commercial space. If there is a significant number of Down Units due to a large renovation project or hazard loss, the inspector may choose to inspect a representative sampling of Down Units instead of 100%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when inspection identifies Down Units or any commercial space at the Property.
business_rules.consequences: If met, inspection scope complies; if violated, inspection is incomplete and may require remediation.
business_rules.exceptions: Representative sampling allowed only for significant Down Units due to renovation/hazard loss.

source_reference.source_text: • Down Units: 100 percent (Note: If there is a significant number of Down Units due to large renovation project or hazard loss, inspector may choose to inspect a representative sampling of Down Units) • Commercial space: 100 percent The inspector must conduct additional occupied and vacant unit inspections, as needed, to determine whether any problems detected in units or specific buildings are restricted to the specific units/buildings or pervasive throughout the Property.

examples: Property has 2 Down Units: inspector must inspect both Down Units (100%).
Property has 40 Down Units due to a major renovation: inspector may inspect a representative sampling rather than all 40, documenting rationale.
Property includes 3 retail suites: inspector must inspect all commercial spaces (100%).
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Mortgage default definition under 46SBL.6(d)(1)
business_rules.description: During the Loss Obligation Period, a “Mortgage Default” occurs upon any event that entitles Freddie Mac to accelerate indebtedness or seek other relief against the Borrower under the Loan Documents (subject to any applicable grace or cure period). Upon Mortgage Default, the SBL Mortgage is a “Defaulted Mortgage.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the SBL Loss Obligation Period when default status is assessed
business_rules.consequences: If event qualifies, classify as Mortgage Default/Defaulted Mortgage for loss actions
business_rules.exceptions: Subject to any applicable grace or cure period in Loan Documents

source_reference.source_text: During the Loss Obligation Period for an SBL Mortgage, the occurrence of any event that entitles Freddie Mac to accelerate the indebtedness or seek other relief against the Borrower pursuant to the Loan Documents (subject to any applicable grace or cure period in the Loan Documents) is a “Mortgage Default,” and the SBL Mortgage is a “Defaulted Mortgage.”

examples: Borrower violates a covenant allowing acceleration under the Loan Documents → Mortgage Default; loan becomes a Defaulted Mortgage.
Payment default occurs, but note provides a cure period → still treated as Mortgage Default for status once the event entitles relief (subject to cure terms).
Freddie Mac can seek other relief (not just acceleration) under Loan Documents → still a Mortgage Default.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Default classification enabling loss calculation and remedies

examples: ACTIVE status where borrower payments are current
DELINQUENT status at 30 days delinquent
DEACTIVATED for accounting/reporting after specified servicing action

business_rules.rule_name: SPE Required Unless Loan Under $5 Million Requested
business_rules.description: Each Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE), and if the Borrower is a TIC, each tenant must be an SPE. Exception: for each Mortgage with an initial principal balance of less than $5 million, at the Borrower’s request (except for TICs), Freddie Mac will not require the Borrower to be an SPE. SPEs must be newly formed, but if the Mortgage has an initial principal balance of $100,000,000 or less, Freddie Mac may accept a recycled SPE if the commitment/application recycled SPE requirements are met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to borrower entity structure for Freddie Mac multifamily Mortgages
business_rules.consequences: If not satisfied, Borrower structure may be ineligible or require Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.exceptions: Non-TIC loans < $5M may waive SPE at Borrower request; recycled SPE allowed ≤$100M

source_reference.source_text: Each Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE). If the Borrower is a TIC, each tenant must be an SPE. However, for each Mortgage with an initial principal balance of less than $5 million, at the request of the Borrower (except for TICs), Freddie Mac will not require the Borrower to be an SPE. Each SPE Borrower and each SPE Equity Owner (if applicable) must be newly formed; provided, however, that if a Mortgage has an initial principal balance of $100,000,000 or less, Freddie Mac may accept a recycled SPE if all recycled SPE requirements set forth in the Letter of Commitment or early rate lock application are met.

examples: Loan initial principal balance is $4,750,000 and Borrower requests non-SPE structure (not a TIC): Freddie Mac will not require SPE for the Borrower.
Loan initial principal balance is $6,000,000: Borrower must be an SPE (unless another Guide provision applies).
Loan initial principal balance is $90,000,000 and Borrower proposes a recycled SPE: permitted only if recycled SPE requirements in the commitment/early rate-lock application are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase suspension/decline until compliant borrower structure is provided

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: No Lead Actions If Built 1978+ or Below 0.5%
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac does not require specific Borrower actions for lead-based paint (LBP) if the Property was built in 1978 or later, OR a qualified environmental professional issued a “lead free” certificate (reviewed by the environmental consultant), OR properly specified testing shows acceptable LBP levels (<0.5% by weight by laboratory analysis OR 1.0 mg/cm2 by XRF) AND the Property satisfies State and local LBP laws.
business_rules.conditions: LBP review during environmental due diligence for a multifamily Property
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, no Borrower LBP action required; if not met, O&M/remediation may be required
business_rules.exceptions: None (this rule itself defines the exception cases)

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements; Issue resolution by Borrower (02/16/23)
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac does not require any specific Borrower actions if  • The Property was built in 1978 or later, or  • A “lead free” certificate has been issued by a qualified environmental professional in accordance with EPA, HUD or State requirements, and the environmental consultant has reviewed all supporting documentation, or  • All painted surfaces have been properly tested as specified in Section 61. 11(a) and found to contain acceptable levels of LBP (less than 0.5 percent by weight as measured by laboratory analysis or 1.0 mg/cm2 as measured by XRF), and  • The Property satisfies State and local LBP laws.

examples: A 1995-built property: Borrower provides build year evidence; no LBP O&M or remediation required.
A 1965-built property with lab results showing 0.3% lead by weight and compliance with local law: no additional Borrower action required.
A 1972-built property with a qualified “lead free” certificate reviewed by the consultant: no LBP action required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / underwriting hold until LBP requirements satisfied

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated seismic hazard region defined as PGA at least 0.15g
business_rules.description: An Elevated Seismic Hazard Region is a geographic location with horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) ≥ 0.15g. For continental U.S. properties, the PGA must be calculated using the USGS website. For properties in Hawaii, Alaska (within 100 miles of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g. For areas of Alaska outside the delineated area, Seller/Servicer can assume PGA < 0.15g.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining if a property is in Elevated Seismic Hazard Region
business_rules.consequences: Determines whether seismic risk documentation evaluation is triggered
business_rules.exceptions: Alaska outside delineated area may assume PGA < 0.15g

source_reference.section_id: 64SBL.2(b) Determining whether a Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (12/15/22)
source_reference.source_text: An “Elevated Seismic Hazard Region” is defined as a geographic location with a horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) equal to or greater than 0.15g. For Properties located in the continental United States, the PGA must be calculated using the United States Geological Survey (USGS) website. For any Property located in Hawaii, Alaska (limited to within 100 miles of the city of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or one of the U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume that the PGA is greater than 0.15g

examples: Property in Los Angeles, CA: Seller/Servicer calculates PGA via USGS; if PGA is 0.16g, the property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region.
Property in Honolulu, HI: Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g (no USGS calculation needed for the threshold determination).
Property in Alaska 200 miles from Anchorage and outside the delineated area: Seller/Servicer may assume PGA < 0.15g, so Elevated Seismic Hazard Region definition is not met.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric definition (0.15g) and explicit geographic assumptions.

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated Seismic Hazard Region Defined as PGA at 0.15g
business_rules.description: An Elevated Seismic Hazard Region is a geographic location with horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) ≥ 0.15g. For continental U.S. properties, PGA must be calculated using the USGS website with a 10% in 50 years (475 years) return period. For Hawaii, Alaska (within 100 miles of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), and U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g and an SRA is required; for Alaska outside the delineated area, Seller/Servicer can assume PGA < 0.15g and no SRA is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether seismic risk documentation/SRA is required
business_rules.consequences: If PGA ≥ 0.15g, SRA required; if < 0.15g, no seismic risk documentation required
business_rules.exceptions: Hawaii/covered Alaska/territories assumed >0.15g; Alaska outside area assumed <0.15g

source_reference.source_text: An “Elevated Seismic Hazard Region” is defined as a geographic location with a horizontal Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) equal to or greater than 0.15g. For Properties located in the continental United States, the PGA must be calculated using the United States Geological Survey (USGS) website. For any Property located in Hawaii, Alaska (limited to within 100 miles of the city of Anchorage or the Gulf of Alaska), or one of the U.S. territories, the Seller/Servicer must assume that the PGA is greater than 0.15g, and an SRA is required. For areas of Alaska outside the delineated area, the Seller/Servicer can assume a PGA of less than 0.15g, and therefore an SRA is not required. Time Horizon /Earthquake Return Period 10% in 50 years (475 years)

examples: Property in Los Angeles, CA has USGS PGA = 0.21g at 10% in 50 years: property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and requires an SRA.
Property in Texas has USGS PGA = 0.08g: SRA is not required under Chapter 64.
Property in Hawaii: Seller/Servicer must assume PGA > 0.15g and obtain an SRA without performing the USGS PGA calculation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase condition/hold pending required seismic documentation

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: LBP testing not required for properties built 1978 or later
business_rules.description: For environmental assessments, the environmental consultant is not required to test for lead-based paint (LBP) in a Property built in 1978 or later. A Property built before 1978 is considered to contain LBP unless testing proves otherwise; if the original certificate of occupancy (or legal equivalent) was issued before January 1, 1978, Freddie Mac treats the Property as built before 1978 (pre-1978 Property).
business_rules.conditions: When determining LBP testing scope based on Property build year/COO date
business_rules.consequences: If met: correct LBP scope applied; if violated: report may be deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Pre-1978 may test or presume LBP and implement O&M program

source_reference.source_text: The environmental consultant is not required to test for LBP in a Property built in 1978 or later. A Property built before 1978 is considered to contain LBP unless testing proves otherwise. If the Property's original certificate of occupancy or legal equivalent was issued before January 1, 1978, Freddie Mac considers the Property to be built before 1978 (pre-1978 Property).

examples: Property built in 1985: environmental report may omit LBP testing and still comply with minimum scope.
Property built in 1965: treated as LBP-present unless testing proves otherwise; consultant must address paint condition.
COO issued in 1977 though renovations occurred in 1982: treated as pre-1978 for LBP purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require revised environmental report
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit dates and definitions (1978 cutoff; COO before 01/01/1978).

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Electrical service minimum 60 amps or 40 amps with five conditions
business_rules.description: In the SBL Physical Risk Report inspection, the minimum acceptable electrical service per unit is 60 amperes. Electrical service of 40 amperes is the minimum allowable without a load analysis only if all of the following are met: no washers/dryers in units; no electric heating, stovetops/stovetop appliances, or water heaters; no A/C unless on a separate dedicated circuit; no atypical high-demand equipment (e.g., whirlpool tubs/freezers); and no units over 750 square feet. If service levels below 60 or 40 (as applicable) are encountered or reported, the consultant must perform or review a load analysis based on current NEC guidelines and make corrective recommendations. If inadequate power supply is found, Borrower must correct before Origination Date or list as a Priority Repair; property must meet NEC and all local building codes regardless.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when consultant evaluates electrical capacity in SBL Physical Risk Report
business_rules.consequences: If service meets 60 amps (or 40 with conditions), no load analysis required; if below minimum, load analysis and corrective actions/priority repairs are required
business_rules.exceptions: 40 amps allowed only when all five conditions are satisfied

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 62SBL – c. Problematic materials, equipment and systems, Electrical capacity (12/12/24)
source_reference.source_text: The minimum amperage acceptable to each unit is 60 amperes, except that 40 amperes is the minimum allowable service level acceptable without a load analysis, provided all of the following conditions are met: o No washing machines or clothes dryers are located in the units o No heating systems, stovetops, stovetop appliances, and water heaters are powered by electricity o No air conditioning units of any type are used unless serviced by a separate dedicated circuit o No atypical equipment with high electricity demands ... o No units are over 750 square feet If service levels less than the minimum allowable service levels (60 or 40 amperes, as applicable) are encountered ... the consultant must: • Perform a load analysis

examples: Units have 60-amp service: consultant documents compliance; no load analysis is required solely due to amperage.
Units have 40-amp service, are 700 sq ft, no in-unit laundry, no electric heat/stove/water heater, and A/C on dedicated circuit: 40 amps is acceptable without load analysis.
Units have 40-amp service but include electric water heaters: conditions for 40-amp exception fail; consultant must perform/review NEC-based load analysis and recommend corrective measures.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric thresholds (60/40 amps; 750 sq ft) and explicit conditional exception plus required actions if below minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / priority repair requirement

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Routine repair item threshold is $3000 per item
business_rules.description: For property condition reporting, “Routine Repairs and Maintenance” include minor deficiencies or deferred maintenance items with a cost of $3000 or less per repair item. These routine items are not considered Critical Repairs or Priority Repairs and are expected to be completed in normal operations at nominal cost.
business_rules.conditions: Classifying repair and maintenance items in a property condition report for a multifamily property.
business_rules.consequences: If cost per item is ≤ $3000, classify as routine (not critical/priority); if > $3000 or otherwise severe, classify per applicable repair categories (e.g., priority/critical) and include in report.
business_rules.exceptions: Consultant may categorize as Priority Repair if lack of maintenance warrants.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 62 – Routine Repairs and Maintenance (12/12/24)
source_reference.source_text: • Routine Repairs and Maintenance – Repairs and maintenance that are expected to be completed by the Borrower in the normal course of property operations  and are nominal in cost.  These repairs are not considered to be Critical  Repairs  or Priority Repairs and include work that is:  o Often preventative in nature  o Accomplished within the Property’s normal operating budget  o Typically completed by on -site staff  o Focused on keeping the Property fully functioning and serviceable  o Minor Deficiencies or Deferred Maintenance  with a cost of $3000 or less per repair item

examples: A unit door closer replacement costing $650 is treated as routine repair and not a priority/critical repair.
A parking lot patch repair quoted at $2,950 is within the $3,000 threshold and is classified as routine maintenance.
A roof repair quoted at $12,000 is not routine by this definition and must be evaluated for priority/critical categorization.
Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: MHC allows under 60 amps only if code compliant
business_rules.description: A Manufactured Housing Community (MHC) with electrical power under 60 amperes is permitted only if the property condition consultant confirms electrical power meets the minimum requirements of all State and local building codes.
