If electrical power is below any State or local building code requirements, the property condition consultant must confirm the current power level is sufficient for the MHC (no load calculation required) and recommend corrective measures per Section 22.6(c) or Section 62.5(c), as applicable.
business_rules.conditions: When an MHC property has electrical service under 60 amperes
business_rules.consequences: If met: acceptable for underwriting; if violated: corrective measures required
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted under 60 amps only with code-compliance confirmation

source_reference.source_text: A Manufactured Housing Community with electrical power under 60 amperes is permitted, provided that the property condition consultant confirms electrical power meets the minimum requirement of all State and local building codes. If the electrical power is below the requirements of any State or local building codes, the property condition consultant must: • Confirm the current power level is sufficient for the Manufactured Housing Community (although no load calculation is necessary) • Recommend corrective measures as outlined in Section 22.6(c) or Section 62.5(c), as applicable

examples: MHC has 50-amp service but local code minimum is 50 amps: consultant confirms compliance; property permitted.
MHC has 50-amp service but local code minimum is 60 amps: consultant confirms sufficiency and recommends upgrades.
Consultant does not address code minimums: Freddie requires revised report or repair plan before approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require repair plan or upgrades
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric threshold (60 amperes) and conditional requirements.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Expanded deductible waiver requires 4x liquidity, risk rating ≤6, no 12-mo delinquency
business_rules.description: For existing Mortgages, if the Borrower cannot obtain deductibles that comply with the maximum deductibles in Chapter 31, the Servicer may approve expanded maximum deductibles for specific-limit policies (excluding NFIP, windstorm, and earthquake) only if all conditions are met: Borrower/Borrower Principal liquidity is at least four times the maximum deductible amount; Mortgage risk rating is six or less; Mortgage is not currently delinquent and has not been delinquent within the last 12 months; and Property condition is average or better per most recent inspection. Expanded maximum deductibles are capped at $100,000 (< $10 million IV) and $150,000 (≥ $10 million IV). Waiver is valid for one policy term and may be renewed if continued inability to obtain compliant deductibles.
business_rules.conditions: Existing Mortgage where Borrower cannot obtain Chapter 31 compliant deductibles
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, expanded deductible may be approved for 1 term; else must comply with standard caps
business_rules.exceptions: Not for NFIP, windstorm, or earthquake; waiver limited to one policy term

source_reference.source_text: valid for one policy term. At the end of that period, if the Borrower has been unable to obtain deductibles in compliance with the other applicable sections of this chapter, the Servicer may permit

examples: Existing loan, IV $8,000,000, requested deductible $90,000: allowed if liquidity ≥ $360,000, risk rating 6 or less, no delinquency in 12 months, and property condition average or better.
Existing loan, IV $12,000,000, requested deductible $175,000: not allowed because it exceeds $150,000 cap even if all other conditions are met.
Existing loan meets caps but had a 30-day delinquency 6 months ago: waiver not permitted due to delinquency within last 12 months.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Financial Assessment Waiver Under 6 or 3 Months Data
business_rules.description: If the Borrower cannot provide an Income and Expense Statement with 12 months of data for the immediate preceding fiscal year, the Servicer may accept a partial-year statement. For non-SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than six months of data is available; for SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than three months of data is available.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Annual Financial source financials lack 12 months of prior fiscal data
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, Servicer may waive assessment; otherwise assessment required
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated non-SBL (<6 months) and SBL (<3 months) thresholds

source_reference.source_text: The Property’s Income and Expense Statement must be for the immediate preceding fiscal year. If the Borrower cannot provide an Income and Expense Statement with 12 months of data, the Servicer may accept a partial-year statement from the Borrower. For non-SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than six months of data is available from the Borrower. For SBL Mortgages, the assessment may be waived if less than three months of data is available from the Borrower.

examples: Non-SBL: Borrower provides only 5 months of operations; Servicer may waive the assessment (<6 months).
SBL: Borrower provides 2 months of operations post-acquisition; Servicer may waive the assessment (<3 months).
Non-SBL: Borrower provides 8 months of data; assessment may not be waived under this rule.
Monthly servicing report indicating 30 days delinquent status
Servicing report reflecting partial payment and suspense handling
Corrected servicing report resubmitted after reconciliation

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting cutoff date is close of business on the 15th
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, the accounting cutoff date is defined as the close of business on the 15th of a month. If the 15th is not a Business Day, the cutoff date is the close of business on the Business Day preceding the 15th.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining cutoff date for accounting/reporting under Chapter 50
business_rules.consequences: Correct cutoff date aligns reporting period; incorrect cutoff may misstate transmissions
business_rules.exceptions: If 15th not Business Day, use preceding Business Day close of business

source_reference.source_text: 50.8 Definitions for accounting and reporting ( 02/14/22) As used in this chapter, these terms are defined as follows: 1. Accounting cutoff date . The close of business on the 15th of a month . (If the 15th is not a Business Day, the accounting cutoff date is the close of business on the Business Day preceding the 15th of the month.)

examples: If the 15th falls on a Wednesday Business Day: cutoff is COB on the 15th.
If the 15th falls on a Saturday: cutoff is COB on the preceding Friday Business Day.
A report uses the 16th as cutoff: violates the defined accounting cutoff date.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting cycle runs from the 16th through the 15th
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, the accounting cycle is defined as the 16th of one month through the 15th of the following month.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to defining monthly accounting reporting coverage under Chapter 50
business_rules.consequences: Correct cycle ensures complete loan-level reporting; misaligned cycles create omissions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 2. Accounting cycle . The 16th of one month through the 15th of the following month .

examples: Cycle for May/June: May 16 through June 15.
Loan activity on June 14 belongs to the cycle ending June 15.
Reporting June 1–June 30 as the accounting cycle would not match the defined cycle.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_005 defines the operational cutoff date used for accounting/reporting. BR_039_006 defines the cycle as 16th through 15th. Systems typically must implement the cutoff date logic (including business-day adjustment) to correctly operationalize the cycle close.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If cutoff-date logic is not applied, cycle boundaries can be misapplied (especially when the 15th is not a business day), causing misstatement of monthly accounting transmissions.

business_rules.rule_name: Exception activity means UPB reduced to zero by defined events
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting purposes, exception activity is defined as activity resulting in the Mortgage unpaid principal balance (UPB) being reduced to zero. Transactions that are exception activities include Mortgages paid in full or repurchased, liquidated by third-party foreclosure sale, transferred to REO, and sold into a Securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when classifying and reporting zero-UPB events in accounting transmissions
business_rules.consequences: Correct classification triggers required exception reporting; misclassification may omit reporting
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 3. Exception activity . Activity resulting in the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of a Mortgage being reduced to zero . The following transactions are exception activities: • Mortgages paid in full or repurchased (Sections 54.3 and 54.4) • Mortgages liquidated by third -party foreclosure sale (Section 54.5) • Mortgages transferred to REO (Section 54.6) • Mortgages sold into a Securitization

examples: Mortgage paid in full: UPB becomes $0 and must be treated as exception activity.
Third-party foreclosure sale liquidates the Mortgage: UPB becomes $0 and is exception activity.
Regular scheduled amortization reduces UPB but not to $0: not exception activity under this definition.
Monthly accounting report where accounting net yield differs due to purchase premium
Reconciliation report identifying remittance variance
Exception report for incorrect net yield setup

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_007 defines what qualifies as exception activity (UPB reduced to zero events). BR_039_006 defines the monthly cycle window used for reporting. To report exception activities accurately, the system must place the event into the correct cycle period.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If cycle alignment is missing, exception activity could be reported in the wrong month or omitted from required cycle transmissions.

business_rules.rule_name: Insurance Waiver Must Be Requested Before Origination if Noncompliant
business_rules.description: If the Borrower’s insurance will not meet Freddie Mac insurance requirements, the Seller/Servicer must request a waiver prior to the Origination of the Mortgage. This is an exception-handling requirement to address noncompliant coverage before closing/origination.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitted insurance coverage does not meet Freddie Mac requirements
business_rules.consequences: If waiver requested timely, Freddie may consider exception; if not, insurance noncompliance blocks delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Waiver permitted only if requested prior to Origination

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 55 – Documentation and Deliveries (Evidence of Insurance waiver sentence)
source_reference.source_text: If the Borrower's insurance will not meet the Freddie Mac insurance requirements, the Seller/Servicer must request a waiver prior to the Origination

examples: Borrower’s policy provides insufficient liability limits; Seller/Servicer submits waiver request before origination for Freddie Mac decision.

Seller/Servicer discovers post-origination that deductible exceeds Freddie requirements; waiver was not requested prior to origination, so cure/endorsement (not waiver) may be required and delivery may be delayed.
Borrower cannot obtain required endorsement in time; Seller requests waiver before origination as required to avoid a post-closing insurance defect.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or purchase delay pending waiver decision or insurance cure
business_rules.extraction_notes: Sentence is truncated in the provided chunk; still an explicit 'must request a waiver prior to Origination' requirement but should be verified against the full Guide text.

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: The insurance waiver is an exception-handling branch that only exists after the Seller evaluates the Borrower’s insurance coverage against Freddie’s requirements (using Form 1133 and supporting documents). Therefore, the exception request is conditionally dependent on the insurance documentation and compliance determination step.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If insurance documentation is not submitted/evaluated early enough, noncompliant coverage may be discovered too late to request a waiver prior to origination, potentially blocking closing/origination and delaying purchase/funding.

business_rules.rule_name: Payment and Performance Bonds 100% Contract or 15% LOC Waiver
business_rules.description: Payment and performance bonds are required if (i) the general contractor is unrelated to the Borrower, or (ii) the general contractor is related to the Borrower and will use subcontractors for major elements of rehabilitation (including replacing heating boilers/air chillers, replacing plumbing/electrical systems, site grading, roof replacement, window replacement, or environmental remediation). When required, bonds must: be on a form acceptable to Freddie Mac; cover 100% of the applicable construction contract amount; have a surety licensed through the state in which it operates; have a surety rating of at least A-9 from A.M. Best (or equivalent); and name the Seller/Servicer (and successors/assigns) as a dual obligee. Bonds may not be required if construction monitoring requirements remain in place and any one of the following is met: construction contract amount is less than 15% of UPB; or Mortgage LTV is 65% or less; or cash/letter of credit equal to 15% of the total construction contract is provided (and LOC counterparty acceptable to Freddie Mac).
business_rules.conditions: When a TAH rehab construction contract is part of the transaction/closing file
business_rules.consequences: If bonds meet requirements or waiver applies, closing package is compliant; otherwise deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Waiver allowed with monitoring plus <15% UPB, or ≤65% LTV, or 15% cash/LOC

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 19 – Payment and performance bonds (Page 9-10 context)
source_reference.source_text: Payment and performance bonds are required under either of the following circumstances: • If the general contractor used to complete the construction work is unrelated to the Borrower • If the general contractor is related to the Borrower and will use subcontractors for major elements of the rehabilitation, including: • Replacing heating boilers or air chillers • Replacing plumbing or electrical systems • Site grading • Roof replacement • Window replacement • Environmental remediation Requirements for the payment and performance bonds are as follows: • They must be on a form acceptable to Freddie Mac • They must cover 100 percent of the amount of the applicable construction contract • The surety must be licensed through the State in which it operates • The surety must have a rating of at least A-9 from A.M. Best Company, Inc. or an equivalent rating from a comparable ratings agency • The Seller/Servicer, together with its successors and assigns, must be named a dual obligee Payment and performance bonds may not be required if all construction monitoring requirements remain in place and any of the following conditions are met: • The amount of the construction contract represents less than 15 percent of the UPB • The LTV of the Mortgage is 65 percent or less • Cash or a letter of credit in the amount of 15 percent of the total construction contract is provided, and the letter of credit counterparty is acceptable to Freddie Mac

examples: General contractor is unrelated and contract is $2,000,000; closing package must include bonds covering 100% ($2,000,000) from an A-9+ rated, state-licensed surety naming Seller/Servicer as dual obligee
GC is related and subcontracts roof replacement; bonds are required unless a waiver condition is met (e.g., LTV is 65% or less and monitoring is in place)
Contract is $900,000 and UPB is $7,000,000 (12.86%); with monitoring in place, bonds may be omitted under the <15% UPB waiver, but file must support the waiver basis
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting/closing conditions; loan purchase denial until satisfied

examples: Closing package assembled with executed note and recorded security instrument
Closing package flagged delivery-ready after final title and insurance evidence received
Closing package returned for correction due to missing signature/acknowledgment

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning report not required when appraisal has zoning analysis and loan ≤$20M
business_rules.description: A separate zoning report is not required if the zoning analysis described in Section 60.12(f)(2) is provided in the Appraisal AND all listed conditions are met: (1) initial principal balance is $20 million or less OR the Mortgage is a Supplemental Mortgage and combined initial/prior balances are $25 million or less in the aggregate; (2) Mortgage is not a Moderate Rehabilitation, Lease-up, Value-add, or Forward Commitment Mortgage; and (3) copies of all available Certificates of Occupancy are provided to Freddie Mac in the final underwriting package.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller seeks to omit zoning report and rely on appraisal zoning analysis
business_rules.consequences: If conditions not met, a zoning report is required; omission may halt purchase review
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond listed conditions

source_reference.source_text: Notwithstanding the above, a zoning report is not required if the zoning analysis described in Section 60.12( f)(2) is provided in the Appraisal and all of the following conditions are met: • The Mortgage has an initial principal balance of $20 million or less or the Mortgage is a Supplemental Mortgage and the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage and the unpaid principal balances of any senior Mortgages encumbering th e Property are $25 million or less in the aggregate • The Mortgage is not a Moderate Rehabilitation Mortgage, a Lease -up Mortgage, a Value -add Mortgage or a Forward Commitment Mortgage • Copies of all available Certificates of Occupancy are provided to Freddie Mac in the final underwriting package

examples: New mortgage $18,000,000, not lease-up/value-add, appraisal includes 60.12(f)(2) zoning analysis, COs provided: zoning report can be omitted.
Supplemental mortgage $5,000,000 with senior UPB $22,000,000 (combined $27,000,000): exceeds $25,000,000 threshold, so zoning report is required.
Mortgage is $19,500,000 but is a Value-add Mortgage: condition fails; zoning report is required even if appraisal includes zoning analysis.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit exception to zoning report requirement with dollar thresholds and product-type exclusions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac underwriting suspense / purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

dependencies.rationale: The zoning-report exception is only safe/allowable if the appraisal’s zoning analysis is robust and compliant. BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_VALIDATION_022_010 establishes the baseline appraisal zoning/legal discussion requirements (authoritative source, parking/density/rebuildability). Those elements are prerequisites to using an appraisal as the substitute for a separate zoning report under BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_EXCEPTION_006_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraisal lacks required zoning source and minimum zoning/legal analysis, the Seller cannot rely on the exception and may be required to obtain a separate zoning report—creating delays and potential underwriting package rejection if omitted.

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Independent Property Purchase May Require Modifications or Reserves
business_rules.description: If a property is not an Independent Property, Freddie Mac may impose one or more purchase conditions: (1) if feasible to create/construct essential facilities, recreational facilities, or direct primary access, Freddie Mac may require the Borrower to make necessary modifications so the property meets Independent Property requirements; (2) Freddie Mac may require the Borrower to establish a Reserve of funds necessary to make required modifications later as determined by Freddie Mac; (3) Freddie Mac may require the mortgage to be cross-collateralized and cross-defaulted with mortgages on the property(ies) where the facilities/access are located; and (4) Freddie Mac may require the Borrower to be personally liable for any loss or damages incurred by Freddie Mac because the property is not an Independent Property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivered mortgage is secured by a property that is not an Independent Property
business_rules.consequences: If conditions satisfied, Freddie Mac may purchase; if not, purchase may be declined
business_rules.exceptions: Conditions depend on Freddie Mac feasibility determination and discretion

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 8 – c. Requirements for purchase (06/30/15)
source_reference.source_text: If a Property is not an Independent Property, Freddie Mac may require one or more of the following as a condition to Freddie Mac’s agreeing to purchase the Mortgage: • If Freddie Mac determines that it is feasible to create or construct Essential Facilities, Recreational Facilities or direct primary access, Freddie Mac may require the Borrower to make the necessary modifications... • Freddie Mac may require the Borrower to establish a Reserve... • Freddie Mac may require the Mortgage to be cross -collateralized and cross -defaulted... • Freddie Mac may require that the Borrower be personally liable for any loss or damages incurred by Freddie Mac because the Property is not an Independent Property

examples: Property lacks direct public access but feasible to build; Freddie Mac conditions purchase on Borrower constructing direct access improvements.
Freddie Mac allows purchase but requires a reserve to fund future creation of essential facilities and requires additional personal liability.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Discretionary 'may require' conditions; no numeric thresholds.

examples: Freddie Mac purchases a delivered mortgage and schedules settlement
Purchase conditioned on curative documentation to be provided post-settlement
Freddie Mac declines purchase due to noncompliance findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_003 is an absolute prohibition on purchase/transfer/servicing approvals upon confirmed match to specified lists. BR_019_002 governs conditions Freddie may impose to purchase a loan secured by a non-Independent Property. Even if BR_019_002 conditions could be satisfied, the transaction cannot proceed if BR_007_003 is violated. Therefore sanctions screening is a gating prerequisite before spending effort on BR_019_002 mitigations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails: (1) Freddie will not purchase/approve transaction, (2) underwriting/structuring work under BR_019_002 is wasted, (3) potential legal/operational escalation to resolve false positives or terminate the transaction.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 019_001 requires the Seller to submit specified written information as part of the underwriting package when the property is not an Independent Property. Rule 019_002 describes the potential purchase conditions Freddie Mac may impose in that scenario (modifications, reserves, etc.). Freddie Mac cannot reasonably determine feasibility of modifications, reserve sizing, or access/use compliance without the package content mandated by 019_001, making 019_001 a practical prerequisite to applying 019_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 019_001 is incomplete/missing: (1) Freddie Mac cannot evaluate or tailor purchase conditions under 019_002, (2) underwriting review stalls, (3) delivery is treated as deficient, (4) transaction timelines extend while information is cured.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Insurance Coverage Minimum 100% of Loan Amount
business_rules.description: The lender must obtain title insurance coverage with an amount of coverage that is at least equal to the loan’s original principal amount (i.e., coverage amount must be >= original principal balance at origination).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a title insurance policy is required for the mortgage loan.
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage satisfies minimum amount. If violated: loan not eligible for delivery until corrected.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Amount of Coverage The amount of title insurance coverage must at least equal the original principal amount of the loan. Other Requirements If a mortgage is registered with MERS and is originated naming MERS as original mortgagee of record, solely as nominee for the lender named in the security instrument and the note, and the lender's successors and assigns,

examples: Original loan amount is $350,000; title policy coverage must be at least $350,000.
Original loan amount is $726,200; title policy coverage of $700,000 fails and must be increased before delivery.
Original loan amount is $500,000; coverage of $500,000 passes even if current UPB later declines.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / corrective title policy endorsement required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_026_010 defines when possession rights create an unacceptable title condition that must be cleared. A title policy that meets the dollar-amount requirement (BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001) does not resolve an unacceptable underlying title condition; therefore, curing the title defect is a prerequisite to a deliverable title insurance posture.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If possession-right issues are not resolved, the lender may be unable to deliver an insurable, acceptable title condition to Fannie Mae, blocking delivery or requiring negotiated sale/curative actions.

provenance.reasoning: Both require the title insurance policy amount/coverage to be at least the mortgage’s original principal balance (original principal amount). Same threshold, same compliance intent, and same delivery/acceptance consequence.

g1_rule.rule_name: Title Insurance Coverage Minimum 100% of Loan Amount
g1_rule.description: The lender must obtain title insurance coverage with an amount of coverage that is at least equal to the loan’s original principal amount (i.e., coverage amount must be >= original principal balance at origination).

g2_rule.rule_name: Title Policy Amount Must Be at Least Original Principal Balance
g2_rule.description: The title insurance policy must insure the mortgagee for an amount that is no less than the original principal balance of the insured Mortgage. This establishes the minimum policy amount requirement for Freddie Mac delivery/acceptance purposes.

business_rules.rule_name: SFHA property in non-NFIP community is ineligible
business_rules.description: If a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA and the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan secured by the property is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If the property is in a non-participating community and also in a CBRS/OPA, it is only eligible if it is not located in an SFHA.
business_rules.conditions: Property has principal/residential structure in SFHA; community is non-NFIP
business_rules.consequences: Meets: eligible only if not in SFHA (CBRS/OPA case); Violated: ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: CBRS/OPA non-participating: eligible only when not in SFHA

source_reference.source_text: Community Eligibility If the lender or servicer determines that a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA, but the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If the property is in a non-participating community and a CBRS or an OPA, it is only eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae if it is not located in an SFHA.

examples: SFR in Zone AE; town does not participate in NFIP → loan ineligible for delivery.
Property in CBRS and community is non-NFIP; FEMA zone is X (not SFHA) → eligible if other requirements met.
Property in CBRS and Zone VE (SFHA) in non-NFIP community → ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct ineligibility statement tied to NFIP participation and SFHA status.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

provenance.reasoning: Both make the mortgage ineligible when the property is in an SFHA and the community does not participate in NFIP. Freddie adds an additional ineligibility path (no required flood insurance), so not identical but same core effect on NFIP-nonparticipation.

g1_rule.rule_name: SFHA property in non-NFIP community is ineligible
g1_rule.description: If a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA and the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan secured by the property is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If the property is in a non-participating community and also in a CBRS/OPA, it is only eligible if it is not located in an SFHA.

g2_rule.rule_name: Ineligible in SFHA if NFIP nonparticipation or no flood policy
g2_rule.description: A Mortgage is ineligible for sale to Freddie Mac if the securing Property is in a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA) and either: (i) the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) regardless of private flood insurance availability, or (ii) the Borrower has not obtained the required flood insurance coverage.

business_rules.rule_name: Postponed Improvements: 180 Days and 120% Escrow
business_rules.description: Loans secured by new or proposed construction may be sold before postponed items are complete only if: postponed improvements are completed within 180 days of the note date; the cost of completing improvements is not more than 10% of the “as completed” appraised value; and the lender establishes a completion escrow by withholding from purchase proceeds funds equal to 120% of estimated cost (or full contract price if a guaranteed fixed-price contract is provided). Completion must be confirmed using Form 1004D or an acceptable completion alternative, and documentation must be retained in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: New/proposed construction has postponed items not complete at sale to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If all thresholds/escrow met, loan may be sold; if not, lender must not sell until complete/verified
business_rules.exceptions: Escrow equals contract price (not 120%) if fixed-price contract provided

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.2-05 (Requirements for New or Proposed Construction)
source_reference.source_text: Loans may be sold before postponed items are complete; however, the postponed improvements must be completed within 180 days of the note date. Completion must be conﬁrmed using Form 1004D or an acceptable completion alternative as described above.  All documentation must be retained in the loan ﬁle. The cost of completing improvements must not represent more than 10% of the “as completed” appraised value of the property. Lenders must establish a completion escrow for the postponed improvements, by withholding from the purchase proceeds funds equal to 120% of the estimated cost for completing the improvements. However, if the contractor or builder oﬀers a guaranteed ﬁxed-price contract for completion of the improvements, the funds in the completion escrow only need to equal the full amount of the contract price.

examples: New construction: landscaping postponed due to weather; estimated cost $8,000, as-completed value $300,000 (2.67%); lender escrows $9,600 (120%) and may sell prior to completion.
Proposed construction: postponed improvements estimated at $40,000 on as-completed value $350,000 (11.43%); exceeds 10% cap, so loan may not be sold until items are complete/verified.
Builder provides guaranteed fixed-price contract for $12,000; lender escrows $12,000 (not $14,400) and uses Form 1004D to confirm completion within 180 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_034_010 includes thresholds tied to the “as completed” appraised value (e.g., postponed improvements cost not more than 10% of as-completed value; escrow set to 120% of estimated cost). If the appraisal supporting the as-completed value is deficient under BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_009_001, the as-completed value may not be acceptable for determining whether the 10%/escrow thresholds are met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraisal is not acceptable, the lender may be unable to validate the postponed-items exception and must either cure the appraisal or delay sale until completion, affecting delivery timing and warehouse line utilization.

dependencies.rationale: If the construction/postponed-items loan involves a unit in a new or recently converted project, the appraisal must include the required inside/outside project comp mix. This supports a reliable value conclusion, which is then used in BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_034_010’s 10% cap and escrow analysis.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A missing comp mix can force appraisal revision, delaying the ability to determine whether postponed items can be permitted at sale, potentially pushing delivery beyond internal or investor timelines.

provenance.reasoning: Both address acceptability when property improvements/construction are not fully complete by delivery/sale, and both require a holdback/reserve-type mechanism to ensure completion. Freddie is broader (no 180-day/10%/120% specifics), but the practical control is similar.

g1_rule.description: Loans secured by new or proposed construction may be sold before postponed items are complete only if: postponed improvements are completed within 180 days of the note date; the cost of completing improvements is not more than 10% of the “as completed” appraised value; and the lender establishes a completion escrow by withholding from purchase proceeds funds equal to 120% of estimated cost (or full contract price if a guaranteed fixed-price contract is provided). Completion must be confirmed using Form 1004D or an acceptable completion alternative, and documentation must be retained in the loan file.

g2_rule.rule_name: Property Must Have 5+ Units and Completed Construction
g2_rule.description: To be eligible, the Property must (i) contain five or more dwelling units, (ii) be designed in whole or in part for residential use, (iii) have completed construction (including subsurface utilities and off-site improvements) as of the Delivery Date, and (iv) have each residential unit include kitchen and bathroom facilities. The Property must be served by public water and sanitary sewer systems. If improvements cannot be completed for valid reasons (e.g., inclement weather), an adequate Reserve for incomplete items must be established, and a satisfactory completion certificate with color photographs must be submitted to Freddie Mac.

business_rules.rule_name: Trading margin call threshold $3,000,000 and minimum $50,000
business_rules.description: For MBS trading account margin calls based on price differential, both Fannie Mae’s and the lender’s designated threshold amount are $3,000,000 and both parties’ minimum transfer amount is $50,000, unless otherwise agreed in writing or modified due to a triggering event.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating price differential margin calls for MBS trading account
business_rules.consequences: If below threshold/min transfer, no margin call; if met, margin call allowed
business_rules.exceptions: May differ by written agreement or triggering event modification

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-01 (10/06/2021), Designated Threshold Amount and Minimum Transfer Amount
source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae’s designated threshold amount and a lender’s designated threshold amount shall each be $3,000,000, unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing and/or subject to the occurrence of a triggering event as discussed below. ... Fannie Mae’s minimum transfer amount and a lender’s minimum transfer amount shall each be $50,000, unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing and/or subject to the occurrence of a triggering event as discussed below.

examples: Price differential = $3,100,000 in lender’s favor; lender may call for $100,000 ($3.1M-$3.0M).
Price differential = $3,040,000; amount above threshold is $40,000 (<$50,000), so margin call not allowed.
Parties execute written amendment setting threshold to $1,000,000; margin call eligibility follows amended terms.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_001_001 sets threshold/minimum transfer for margin calls based on price differential. BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 states Fannie will not wire cash even with positive price differential if triggering events occur and may modify/eliminate thresholds without notice. That behavior supersedes normal threshold-based expectations for cash movement in those triggering-event conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If triggering events occur, the lender cannot rely on threshold-based mechanics to receive cash wires; liquidity planning and settlement operations must shift to a restricted-cash posture.

business_rules.rule_name: Pass-through rate ≥8% limits pools to 3, 4, or 5 per $1M
business_rules.description: For MBS trades with pass-through rate ≥ 8%, the maximum number of pools permitted depends on original trade amount: ≤ $500,000 allows 3 pools; > $500,000 and < $1,000,000 allows 4 pools; and ≥ $1,000,000 allows 5 pools per each million dollars of trade amount.
business_rules.conditions: When structuring MBS pools for trades with pass-through rate ≥ 8%
business_rules.consequences: If pool count exceeds limit, delivery may be rejected or require restructuring
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-04 (05/05/2021), MBS Pass-Through Rate / Original Trade Amount
source_reference.source_text:
MBS Pass-Through Rate Original Trade Amount Maximum Number of Pools 
≥ 8%≤ $500,000 3
> $500,000 and < $1,000,000 4
≥ $1,000,0005 pools per each million dollars of
the trade amount

examples: Trade amount $450,000 at 8.125%: maximum 3 pools.
Trade amount $750,000 at 8.000%: maximum 4 pools.
Trade amount $2,400,000 at 8.50%: maximum 10 pools (5 per $1M; apply to each million).
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Seller must sell at least 12 loans in prior calendar year
business_rules.description: To maintain Seller eligibility, the Seller must have sold at least 12 loans to Fannie Mae during the prior calendar year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Fannie Mae-approved sellers during annual eligibility review.
business_rules.consequences: If met, seller remains eligible; if not met, seller approval termination applies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Minimum Requirements Sellers/servicers must comply with the minimum requirements described in the following table. Category Description Loan Sales A seller must sell at least 12 loans to Fannie Mae during the prior calendar year.

examples: Seller delivered 15 loans to Fannie Mae in the prior calendar year: meets the 12-loan minimum.
Seller delivered 11 loans to Fannie Mae in the prior calendar year: fails the minimum and is subject to termination.
Seller delivered 12 loans exactly in the prior calendar year: meets the minimum.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Servicer must service at least 1 Fannie Mae loan as of Dec 31
business_rules.description: To maintain Servicer eligibility, the Servicer must service at least one loan for Fannie Mae as of December 31 of the prior calendar year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Fannie Mae-approved servicers at year-end eligibility assessment.
business_rules.consequences: If met, servicer remains eligible; if not met, servicer approval termination applies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Minimum Requirements Sellers/servicers must comply with the minimum requirements described in the following table. Category Description Loan ServicingA servicer must service at least one loan for Fannie Mae as of December 31 of the prior calendar year.

examples: Servicer has 250 Fannie Mae loans boarded as of Dec 31: meets the minimum.
Servicer has 0 Fannie Mae loans boarded as of Dec 31: fails and is subject to termination.
Servicer has exactly 1 Fannie Mae loan boarded as of Dec 31: meets the minimum.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Adjusted net worth minimum $2.5 million plus UPB factors
business_rules.description: All depository and non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times of at least $2,500,000 plus additional amounts based on servicing unpaid principal balance (UPB): (1) 0.25% of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, plus (2) 0.35% of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus (3) 0.25% of Other Servicing UPB.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times to all approved depository and non-depository Seller/Servicers
business_rules.consequences: If met, Seller/Servicer remains financially eligible; if not met, approval is at risk and Fannie Mae may impose remedies
business_rules.exceptions: Nonprofits may use equivalent financial data per Fannie Mae determination

source_reference.source_text: All depository and non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times an Adjusted Net Worth of at least $2.5 million, plus an amount equal to or greater than the sum of the following: • 0.25% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, plus • 0.35% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus • 0.25% of the Other Servicing UPB

examples: Seller/Servicer has $2.5M base + ($10B FNMA/FHLMC UPB×0.25%=$25M) + ($1B GNMA×0.35%=$3.5M) + ($500M other×0.25%=$1.25M) → minimum ANW $32.25M.
Depository Seller/Servicer with $8M ANW and no servicing UPB beyond $0 must still meet the $2.5M base; $8M passes.
Nonprofit Seller/Servicer cannot calculate Adjusted Net Worth under its reporting; Fannie Mae uses equivalent financial data to test whether it meets the same minimum requirement.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_001 requires submission of annual audited financial statements, which are commonly used to compute/validate adjusted net worth. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001 requires maintaining adjusted net worth at all times; annual AFS is a key validation checkpoint used by Fannie to assess compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If AFS is late or missing, Fannie may treat it as a contract breach and may be unable to validate compliance, increasing oversight and potential eligibility actions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_002 requires quarterly Form 1002 reporting for certain entity types. These financial data submissions are used to monitor financial condition between annual audits, supporting validation of ongoing compliance with BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If quarterly reports are missing or inaccurate, compliance monitoring degrades and can trigger eligibility actions or breach determinations.

business_rules.rule_name: Non-depository capital ratio minimum 6% adjusted net worth
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain at all times a minimum Adjusted Net Worth to total assets ratio of 6% (or equivalent as determined by Fannie Mae). This is a standing capital adequacy requirement separate from absolute net worth and liquidity requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times when Seller/Servicer is non-depository
business_rules.consequences: If met, Seller/Servicer meets minimum capital requirement; if violated, Fannie Mae may require corrective action or restrict eligibility
business_rules.exceptions: Equivalent ratio may be used if determined by Fannie Mae

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times a minimum Adjusted New Worth/total assets ratio of 6%, or equivalent, as determined by Fannie Mae.

examples: Non-depository has Adjusted Net Worth $12M and total assets $150M → ratio 8.0% (passes).
Non-depository has Adjusted Net Worth $9M and total assets $180M → ratio 5.0% (fails; must remediate or risk restrictions).
Seller/Servicer uses an alternative but equivalent ratio metric accepted by Fannie Mae due to reporting conventions; must still demonstrate at least 6% equivalency.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Non-depository minimum liquidity based on UPB and remittance type
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain Allowable Liquidity at all times at or above a calculated minimum equal to: (1) 0.07% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with scheduled/scheduled or scheduled/actual remittance types, plus (2) 0.035% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with actual/actual remittance types, plus (3) 0.10% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus (4) 0.035% of the Other Servicing UPB.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times when Seller/Servicer is non-depository and has servicing UPB
business_rules.consequences: If met, liquidity requirement satisfied; if violated, Seller/Servicer may face eligibility actions and required liquidity remediation
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times an Allowable Liquidity at a level equal to or greater than the sum of the following: • 0.07% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with scheduled/scheduled or scheduled/actual remittance types, plus • 0.035% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with actual/actual remittance types, plus; • 0.10% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus • 0.035% of the Other Servicing UPB.

examples: Non-depository has $5B scheduled/scheduled FNMA/FHLMC UPB and $2B actual/actual FNMA/FHLMC UPB → min liquidity = $5B×0.07% ($3.5M) + $2B×0.035% ($0.7M) plus other components.
Non-depository services $1B GNMA UPB → adds $1B×0.10% = $1.0M to minimum liquidity requirement.
If Seller/Servicer’s computed minimum liquidity is $6.2M but allowable liquidity is $5.8M, the Seller/Servicer fails the “at all times” threshold and must remediate to avoid eligibility actions.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_003 requires monthly Form 1002A for large non-depository seller/servicers. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_003 requires allowable liquidity to be maintained at all times. Monthly reporting is a practical validation tool to monitor liquidity between annual and quarterly checkpoints.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If monthly reports are missing, liquidity compliance may not be detectable timely, increasing the probability of unreported breaches and eligibility actions.

business_rules.rule_name: Origination liquidity 0.5% when 4-quarter originations exceed $1B
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers that originate more than $1,000,000,000 in one- to four-unit residential first lien mortgage loans in the most recent four-quarter period (excluding reverse mortgages, construction-to-permanent loans to home buyers, and lot loans) must maintain origination liquidity equal to 0.5% of the sum of: (1) eligible one- to four-unit first lien loans held for sale at lower of cost or market, plus (2) eligible one- to four-unit first lien loans held for sale at fair value, plus (3) UPB of interest rate lock commitments after fallout adjustments.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when non-depository originations > $1B in most recent 4-quarter period (with stated exclusions)
business_rules.consequences: If met, origination liquidity requirement satisfied; if violated, Seller/Servicer may be required to increase liquidity or face eligibility restrictions
business_rules.exceptions: Reverse, construction-to-permanent to home buyers, and consumer lot loans excluded from threshold/base

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository seller/servicers who originate greater than $1 billion in one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans (excluding reverse mortgages, one- to four-unit residential construction-to- permanent loans to home buyers, and lot loans to consumers) in the most recent four-quarter period must maintain an origination liquidity of 0.5% of the sum of the following: • one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans ... held for sale, at lower of cost or market; plus • one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans ... held for sale, at fair value; plus • UPB of interest rate lock commitments after fallout adjustments.

examples: Non-depository originated $1.2B eligible first liens in last 4 quarters and has $200M HFS at LOCOM + $150M HFS at FV + $300M IRLC UPB → base $650M; min origination liquidity $650M×0.5% = $3.25M.
Non-depository originated $950M in eligible first liens last 4 quarters → origination liquidity rule does not apply (but other liquidity rules still apply).
If originations exceed $1B only after including excluded reverse mortgages, the threshold test must still exclude those loans; do not trigger origination liquidity solely due to excluded products.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Daily mandatory commitment cap is $200 million
business_rules.description: A Seller/Servicer may enter into multiple mandatory whole loan commitments in a day, but the total aggregate commitment volume per day must not exceed $200,000,000 unless Fannie Mae grants permission. If the Seller/Servicer exceeds the limit without Fannie Mae approval, Fannie Mae may require the lender to pair off the commitment(s) at the lender’s expense.
business_rules.conditions: When entering mandatory whole loan commitments in a single day
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded w/o approval, Fannie Mae may require pair-off at lender expense
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed only with prior permission from Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk

source_reference.source_text: Commitment Amounts Lenders may enter into multiple commitments but may not exceed $200 million in aggregate commitment volume per day. Lenders seeking permission to exceed this amount must contact the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk (see E-1-02, List of Contacts ). Lenders exceeding the limit without Fannie Mae approval may be required to pair oﬀ their commitment(s) at the lender's expense.

examples: Lender enters $150M + $40M commitments same day ($190M): compliant; no action needed.
Lender enters $120M + $90M commitments same day ($210M) without approval: may be required to pair off at lender expense.
Lender expects $250M volume; contacts Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk and receives permission: compliant to exceed cap.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Whole loan overdelivery capped at 25% plus $50 tolerance
business_rules.description: For whole loan commitments, the maximum delivery amount generally cannot exceed the original commitment amount by more than the greater of $10,000 or 2.5% of the original commitment amount, unless the lender requests and receives permission to overdeliver. If an overdelivery is requested/approved, the maximum overdelivery amount is 25% of the original commitment amount and the total delivered against the commitment cannot exceed (original commitment + maximum overdelivery + $50). After an overdelivery occurs, the maximum delivery tolerance level decreases to $50 above the new commitment amount. If the 25% overdelivery calculation is less than $10,000, the 'high tolerance level' becomes the maximum amount the lender can deliver.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering whole loans against a Fannie Mae whole loan commitment
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded, delivery may be rejected and/or overdelivery price adjustment may apply
business_rules.exceptions: Overdelivery requires permission; otherwise use greater of $10,000 or 2.5% cap

source_reference.source_text: The maximum delivery amount is an amount that will not exceed the original commitment amount by more than the greater of $10,000 or 2.5% of the original commitment amount, unless a lender requests an overdelivery. If a lender requests an overdelivery, the maximum required delivery amount will be $50 above the revised commitment amount... The maximum overdelivery amount is 25% of the original commitment amount. For example, on an original commitment amount of $150,000, the maximum overdelivery amount is an additional $37,500 (25% of $150,000). In this example, the total delivered against the commitment cannot exceed $187,550 ($150,000 + $37,500 + $50). After an overdelivery occurs, the maximum delivery tolerance level decreases to $50 above the new commitment amount.

examples: Original commitment $400,000: standard max delivery = $400,000 + max($10,000, $10,000) = $410,000 unless overdelivery approved.
Original commitment $150,000 with approved overdelivery: max extra = 25% = $37,500; total cap = $187,550 including $50 tolerance.
Original commitment $30,000: 25% = $7,500 (<$10,000); the high tolerance level becomes the maximum deliverable amount (per text).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Overdelivery price adjustment draft or delivery rejection

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Condo SFHA master flood coverage minimum 80% RCV
business_rules.description: For an attached condo building located in an SFHA, the lender/servicer must verify the HOA maintains an RCBAP (or equivalent private flood insurance). The HOA master policy must cover the entire building and common elements. The building coverage amount must be at least the lesser of 80% of the replacement cost value or the maximum NFIP coverage available per unit. If the master policy meets the 80% replacement-cost requirement but the per-unit amount is insufficient versus one- to four-unit requirements, the unit owner must maintain a supplemental policy for the difference.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by unit in attached condo project in SFHA where flood required
business_rules.consequences: Meets: acceptable project flood coverage; Violated: loan ineligible/delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental unit policy required when per-unit amount is short

source_reference.source_text: The master ﬂood insurance policy maintained by the HOA must cover the subject unit's • entire building; and • all of the common elements and property, including machinery and equipment that are part of the building. The coverage amount for the building must be at least equal to the lesser of • 80% of the replacement cost value(1), or • the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP per unit(2). (1)If the master ﬂood insurance policy meets the minimum coverage requirement of 80% of the replacement cost value, but the per unit coverage amount does not meet the requirement for loans secured by one- to four-unit properties, as described in Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties , the unit owner must maintain a supplemental policy for the diﬀerence.

examples: RCV $1,000,000; 80%=$800,000; NFIP per-unit max totals $900,000 → HOA must carry ≥$800,000 building coverage.
Master policy meets 80% RCV but per-unit allocation is short vs one- to four-unit minimum → borrower must add supplemental flood policy.
HOA policy excludes machinery/equipment common elements → fails coverage requirement; loan has delivery defect risk.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric threshold (80% RCV) and lesser-of test; includes supplemental-policy condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: The condo HOA master flood insurance (RCBAP) rule includes a branch that compares per-unit coverage against the one- to four-unit minimum flood insurance requirement. The minimum required amount benchmark (lesser of replacement cost, NFIP max, or UPB) is the input used to determine whether per-unit coverage is sufficient when applying the condo building rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the one- to four-unit minimum flood insurance requirement is not correctly determined, the lender may incorrectly conclude the HOA master policy is sufficient (or insufficient), leading to delivery defects, loan ineligibility, or post-purchase repurchase exposure for flood insurance noncompliance.

business_rules.rule_name: ADU limited to one and requires stove hookup minimum
business_rules.description: To classify a property as a one-unit dwelling with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU), only one ADU is permitted on the parcel and ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling. The ADU kitchen must, at minimum, have cabinets, a countertop, a sink with running water, and a stove or stove hookup; hotplates, microwaves, or toaster ovens are not acceptable stove substitutes.
business_rules.conditions: Property evaluated as one-unit with ADU; ADU kitchen and unit-count reviewed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: ADU classification allowed; Violated: property not eligible as 1-unit+ADU
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (classification requirements are mandatory)

source_reference.source_text: The following table describes the requirements for classifying an ADU. ✓ Requirements • Only one ADU is permitted on the parcel of the primary one-unit dwelling. • ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling. • The kitchen must, at a minimum, contain the following: ⚬ cabinets; ⚬ a countertop; ⚬ a sink with running water; and ⚬ a stove or stove hookup (hotplates, microwaves, or toaster ovens are not acceptable stove substitutes).

examples: 1-unit SFR with garage apartment having cabinets/sink/stove hookup → eligible as 1-unit with ADU if otherwise compliant.
Duplex with detached studio → ADU not permitted with 2-unit dwelling; cannot classify as ADU under this rule.
Basement unit has microwave only (no stove/hookup) → fails ADU kitchen minimum; cannot be treated as ADU.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 'only one' and explicit minimum kitchen components with prohibited substitutes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Water and sewer utilities must meet 2 community standards
business_rules.description: For a loan to be eligible for purchase or securitization, the subject property’s utilities must meet community standards. If public sewer and/or water (locally supplied and regulated) is not available, the subject property must have community or private well and septic facilities available and in use, and the property owner must have a right to access those facilities on an ongoing basis. Private well/septic must be on the subject site unless the property has rights to access off-site private facilities supported by an adequate, legally binding agreement for access and maintenance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any loan where public water and/or sewer is not available.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: utilities acceptable for eligibility; Violated: property/loan ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Off-site private facilities allowed with legally binding access/maintenance agreement.

source_reference.source_text: For loans to be eligible for purchase or securitization, the utilities of the property must meet community standards. If public sewer and/or water facilities, those that are supplied and regulated by the local government, are not available, community or private well and septic facilities must be available and utilized by the subject property. The owners of the subject property must have the right to access those facilities, which must be viable on an ongoing basis. Private well or septic facilities must be located on the subject site, unless the subject property has the right to access off-site private facilities and there is an adequate, legally binding agreement for access and maintenance.

examples: Rural home lacks public sewer; it uses an on-site septic system and private well—eligible if community-standard and viable.
Property uses a shared off-site community well; eligibility requires a legally binding recorded agreement for access and maintenance.
Property relies on an off-site private septic without enforceable access rights—utility requirement not met; loan ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit eligibility requirement; limited numeric thresholds, but concrete conditional logic.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Improvements must be legal conforming use with 0 illegal use
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize loans if the property improvements constitute a legal conforming use of the land. A property that is a legal nonconforming (grandfathered) use may be eligible only if the appraisal analysis reflects any adverse effect the nonconforming use has on the value and marketability of the property. This applies to all property types.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when appraisal identifies zoning use as conforming or nonconforming.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: property eligible; Violated: loan ineligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.exceptions: Legal nonconforming use permitted if appraisal reflects adverse effect on value/marketability.

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae only purchases or securitizes loans on properties if the improvements constitute a legal conforming use of the land. However, Fannie Mae will purchase or securitizes a loan for a property that constitutes a legal, nonconforming use of the land provided that the appraisal analysis reflects any adverse effect that the nonconforming use has on the value and the marketability of the property. This requirement applies to all property types.

examples: Home is legal conforming use under R-1 zoning—eligible assuming other requirements met.
Home is legal nonconforming due to updated zoning; appraisal analyzes marketability/value impact—eligible if adverse effects are reflected.
Home is an illegal use under zoning—fails legal conforming requirement; loan ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear purchase eligibility standard; numeric precision limited because rule is categorical.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: A property may be a legal conforming or legal nonconforming use (and otherwise eligible under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_010_003), yet still be prohibited if land-use regulations prevent reconstruction/maintenance after damage. In that trigger condition, the prohibition controls the final eligibility decision.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the prohibition is not applied, the lender may deliver a loan on collateral Fannie Mae will not purchase, resulting in a hard eligibility failure and potential repurchase exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Leasehold Property Types Limited to 1–4 Units and Projects
business_rules.description: A property subject to a leasehold estate is eligible collateral for a Fannie Mae loan only in areas where leasehold ownership has market acceptance and only if the property is an eligible property type: one- to four-unit property, condo unit, co-op unit, PUD unit, or a manufactured home located in a condo or PUD project approved by Fannie Mae's Project Review Eligibility Service (PERS).
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is leasehold estate collateral for a Fannie Mae loan
business_rules.consequences: If met, property may be eligible; if not, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that are secured by properties subject to leasehold estates in areas in which this type of property ownership has received market acceptance. Eligible property types are: one- to four-unit properties, condo units, co-op units, PUD units, manufactured homes located in a condo or PUD project approved by Fannie Mae's Project Review Eligibility Service (PERS).

examples: Eligible: A condo unit on a ground lease in Hawaii with documented market acceptance and PERS project approval.
Ineligible: A leasehold 5-unit dwelling (property type not one- to four-unit) offered for delivery to Fannie Mae.
Eligible: A manufactured home in a PUD project that has been approved by PERS and meets all other requirements.
One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_011_001 is a narrower collateral-structure rule that assumes the property is an otherwise eligible residential type for Fannie Mae delivery. Confirming the property type and (where applicable) geographic eligibility (U.S. / territories) prevents wasted processing on a collateral structure that can never be delivered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If base property type/location eligibility is not satisfied, leasehold analysis becomes irrelevant and the loan will be ineligible for purchase/securitization regardless of leasehold terms or market acceptance.

business_rules.rule_name: New Condo Full Review Requires 50% Sold or Under Contract to Owners
business_rules.description: For units in new or newly converted condo projects undergoing Full Review, at least 50% of the total units in the project or subject legal phase must have been conveyed or be under contract for sale to principal residence or second home purchasers. There may not be more than one legal phase per building; and for this review process, a project consisting of one building cannot have more than one legal phase.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Full Review of units in new/newly converted condo projects.
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds/phase limits met, project can be acceptable; if not, fails eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: Florida new/newly converted projects require PERS review (not addressed here).

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.2-03, Additional Requirements for Units in New/Newly Converted Condo Projects
source_reference.source_text: for the unit being ﬁnanced are properly installed prior to closing. At least 50% of the total units in the project or subject legal phase must have been conveyed or be under contract for sale to principal residence or second home purchasers. • For

examples: 100-unit new condo project: 52 units sold/under contract to principal/second home → meets 50% requirement.
40-unit legal phase 2: phase 1 had 10 sold, phase 2 has 8 sold; total prior+subject is 18/80 = 22.5% → fails 50% requirement.
Single-building project structured into two legal phases → prohibited; must restructure or not eligible under this process.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: The Full Review thresholds (50% conveyed/under contract to principal residence/second home, legal phase limits) are only triggered if the project is a new/newly converted condo. Correctly classifying the project determines whether this constraint must be enforced.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the project is misclassified (new vs established), the lender may (a) apply the wrong review standards and incorrectly approve an ineligible project, or (b) over-restrict and unnecessarily decline an eligible project.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_006 states loans in projects with status 'Unavailable' in CPM or on DU findings are ineligible. BR_020_008 applies during Full Review for new/newly converted condo projects. In a workflow, status screening is a gating prerequisite before undertaking deeper Full Review eligibility evaluation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_019_006 triggers: the loan is ineligible regardless of conveyance/phase ratios; proceeding with BR_020_008 wastes time and risks borrower miscommunication until late discovery.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_008 governs Full Review thresholds for new/newly converted condo projects (owner-occupancy/second home conveyance and legal phase limitations). BR_019_007 governs mixed-use limits (commercial space ≤ 35%) for condo/co-op projects. In practice, both must be cleared to establish project eligibility for delivery when applicable, so they operate as complementary checks within the same project review workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure of either rule can make the project ineligible, even if the other is satisfied; underwriting may need to decline the collateral or move to a different financing/investor.

business_rules.rule_name: Waste Site Hazard Acceptable Only if Q1 Yes and Q2 No Otherwise Phase II
business_rules.description: For Phase I environmental hazard assessment (waste sites), the property is acceptable for underwriting purposes from the standpoint of waste site contamination only when question 1 is answered "yes" and question 2 is answered "no". Otherwise, the underwriter must continue evaluation. If the answer to any of questions 2 through 7 is "yes" or "don’t know," then the property either fails or needs a Phase II assessment conducted. If answers to all questions 2 through 7 are "no," the property is acceptable for underwriting purposes from the standpoint of waste site contamination.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when completing Phase I environmental hazard assessment for waste sites.
business_rules.consequences: If acceptable, proceed; if any Q2–Q7 is yes/DK, fail or require Phase II.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answer to question 1 is “yes” and the answer to question 2 is “no,” stop here because, for underwriting purposes, the property will be acceptable from the standpoint of waste site contamination. Otherwise, answer the questions below... Note: If the answer to any of the questions from 2 through 7 is “yes” or “don’t know,” then the property either fails or needs a Phase II assessment conducted. If the answers to all of the questions 2 through 7 are “no,” the property, for underwriting purposes, will be acceptable from the standpoint of waste site contamination.

examples: On-site sampling meets standards (Q1=Yes) and no high-risk adjacent neighbors (Q2=No) → stop; acceptable for waste site contamination.
Q1=Yes but property appears on a state/federal hazardous waste list within one mile (Q6=Yes) → property fails or requires Phase II assessment.
Q2=Don’t Know due to missing neighbor use data → Phase II assessment required or treat as fail per underwriter determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until acceptable/Phase II completed.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: The waste-site Phase I acceptability rule requires evaluating responses to screening questions (especially Q2–Q7). The validation rule operationalizes the accept/fail/Phase II trigger logic and is therefore required input to compute the eligibility outcome.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Q2–Q7 logic is not applied correctly, the lender may proceed without a required Phase II assessment (risking collateral contamination exposure) or incorrectly fail an acceptable property.

business_rules.rule_name: Property Insurance Deductible Maximum 5% Limit
business_rules.description: For required property insurance perils, the maximum allowable deductible is 5% of the property insurance coverage amount. If a policy has multiple deductibles (e.g., separate windstorm deductible and separate roof deductible), the total deductible amount applicable to a single occurrence must not exceed 5% of the property insurance coverage amount.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating required property insurance deductibles for the subject property
business_rules.consequences: Meets: deductible acceptable; Violates: coverage unacceptable; obtain compliant policy or risk ineligibility
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The maximum allowable deductible for all required property insurance perils for one-to four-unit properties is 5% of the property insurance coverage amount. When a property insurance policy includes multiple deductibles, such as a separate deductible that applies to windstorms, or a separate deductible that applies to a speciﬁc property element such as the roof, the total amount for such deductibles applicable to a single occurrence must be no greater than 5% of the property insurance coverage amount.

examples: Coverage amount $400,000: max deductible per occurrence is $20,000 (5%); a $25,000 wind deductible is not acceptable.
Policy has $10,000 wind deductible and $12,000 roof deductible; if both can apply to one storm, total $22,000 must be ≤ 5% of coverage.
Coverage $250,000: a single $12,500 deductible (5%) is acceptable if all required perils are otherwise covered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Security property must be 1 to 4 units and located in US, PR, VI, or Guam
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes first-lien mortgages secured by residential properties when the dwelling consists of one to four units. The security property must be located in the United States (including the District of Columbia), Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, or Guam.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any mortgage loan intended for sale or securitization to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If met, property location/units are eligible; if not, mortgage is ineligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-01, Number of Units; Property Location
source_reference.source_text: of Units Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes ﬁrst-lien mortgages that are secured by residential properties when the dwelling consists of one to four units. Under some circumstances, Fannie Mae limits the number of dwelling units

examples: 3-unit property in California; meets unit count and location eligibility.
5-unit dwelling in Florida; fails because dwelling exceeds four units.
2-unit property in Canada; fails because property is not in the permitted locations.
One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: Unit-count eligibility is foundational for delivery eligibility.
