The ADU rule determines whether the subject is properly treated as 1-unit-with-ADU (still within 1–4) or is misclassified (e.g., multiple ADUs or ADU with 2–4 unit dwelling). That classification directly feeds the 1–4 unit eligibility gate.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ADU classification fails or is misapplied, the lender can deliver a loan with an incorrect unit count (e.g., actually a 2-unit or illegal second unit), creating eligibility defects, appraisal/underwriting errors, and potential purchase/securitization rejection.

business_rules.rule_name: Hawaii Island lava zones 1 and 2 are ineligible; only zones 3 through 9 allowed
business_rules.description: Mortgage loans secured by properties located on the island of Hawaii are eligible for purchase/securitization by Fannie Mae only if the property is located in lava zones 3 through 9. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to increased risk of destruction from lava flows.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is on the island of Hawaii; lava zone classification is known/verified
business_rules.consequences: If lava zone is 1 or 2: property/loan is not eligible for purchase or securitization
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize mortgage loans secured by properties that are located within lava zones 3 through 9 on the island of Hawaii. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to the increased risk of property destruction from lava ﬂows within these areas.

Hawaiian Lava Zones Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize mortgage loans secured by properties that are located within lava zones 3 through 9 on the island of Hawaii. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to the increased risk of property destruction from lava ﬂows within these areas.

examples: Property in lava zone 3 on Hawaii Island: eligible (subject to all other requirements).
Property in lava zone 2 on Hawaii Island: ineligible for Fannie Mae delivery.
Property on another Hawaiian island (not Hawaii Island): this lava-zone rule does not apply (other property eligibility rules still apply).
One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the identical geographic hazard eligibility requirement for the island of Hawaii: properties in lava zones 3–9 are eligible; lava zones 1–2 are ineligible due to heightened lava-flow destruction risk. All numeric zone thresholds and context match; consequences match (loan/property ineligible). The wording differences do not change the business logic.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_039_009 selected as primary because it is slightly clearer/cleaner: it explicitly states the eligibility range (zones 3–9) and the ineligibility trigger (zones 1–2) in a single concise rule statement.

business_rules.rule_name: TBA UMBS nonstandard loans capped 10% and 15%
business_rules.description: In a TBA-eligible UMBS pool, each category of nonstandard loans is limited to 10% of the pool’s issue-date UPB. If loans with more than one nonstandard characteristic are included in the same TBA-eligible UMBS pool, the combined issue-date UPB of two or more nonstandard categories may not exceed 15% of the pool’s total issue-date UPB; however, the 15% cumulative limitation does not apply to high-balance loans.
business_rules.conditions: When pooling nonstandard loans in a TBA-eligible UMBS pool based on issue-date UPB
business_rules.consequences: If limits exceeded, pool is not compliant for TBA-eligible UMBS standard parameters
business_rules.exceptions: 15% cumulative limitation does not apply to high-balance loans

source_reference.source_text: Nonstandard Loans “Nonstandard loans” (or loans with nonstandard characteristics) may be pooled into MBS, but depending on the concentration in the pool, may only be pooled as negotiated rather than standard transactions. Nonstandard loans are: co-op share loans, relocation loans (deﬁned below), loans with signiﬁcant interest rate buydowns, and high-balance loans. In a TBA-eligible UMBS pool, nonstandard loans are each limited to 10% of the issue date UPB of a TBA-eligible UMBS pool. If loans with more than one of the nonstandard characteristics are included in the same TBA-eligible UMBS pool, the sum of the issue date UPB of two or more of the loans with nonstandard characteristics may not exceed 15% of the total issue date UPB of the pool. The 15% cumulative limitation, however, does not apply to high-balance loans.

examples: Pool issue-date UPB is $10,000,000: co-op share loans must be ≤ $1,000,000 (10%).
Pool includes $900,000 co-op loans (9%) and $800,000 significant buydown loans (8%): combined $1,700,000 (17%) violates the 15% cumulative cap.
Pool includes $900,000 co-op loans (9%) and $1,200,000 high-balance loans (12%): combined 21% may be permitted because high-balance loans are excluded from the 15% cumulative limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or negotiated pooling required

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DEFINITION_006_010 explicitly flags that significant buydown loans are subject to pooling composition restrictions. BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_003_010 governs concentration limits for nonstandard categories in TBA UMBS pools. When a lender pools SFC 014 loans as a nonstandard category, the pooling limits rule becomes a required downstream check.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the pooling concentration limits are not applied, a lender may create a noncompliant TBA-eligible UMBS pool, requiring re-pooling, delayed issuance, or moving loans to a different execution channel.

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV Refinance Requires Note Date 10-01-2017 and 15-Month Seasoning
business_rules.description: To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option, the existing loan being refinanced must be a first-lien conventional mortgage loan owned/securitized by Fannie Mae, must have a note date on or after October 1, 2017, and must have seasoning of at least 15 months between the existing note date and the new note date (example provided: existing note date 01/01/2018 → new note date no earlier than 04/01/2019).
business_rules.conditions: Evaluating eligibility for high LTV refinance option based on existing loan data
business_rules.consequences: If met, existing loan meets base eligibility; if not, high LTV option ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited eligibility bullets

source_reference.source_text: ✓ The existing loan must.... be a ﬁrst lien, conventional mortgage loan owned or securitized by Fannie Mae. have a note date on or after October 1, 2017. have seasoning of at least 15 months - meaning at least 15 months have passed from the note date of the existing loan to the note date of the new loan. For example, if the note date on the existing loan is January 1, 2018, the note date of the new loan must be no earlier than April 1, 2019.

examples: Existing note date 2017-09-30: ineligible because note date is before 10/01/2017.
Existing note date 2018-01-01 and new note date 2019-03-01: ineligible because seasoning is <15 months.
Existing note date 2018-01-01 and new note date 2019-04-01: meets 15-month seasoning example.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV Refinance Limits Points to $5,000 and Cash Back to $250
business_rules.description: For high LTV refinance transactions, the standard limited cash-out refinance requirements are modified: the new loan amount is limited to (i) payoff of the UPB of the existing first mortgage being refinanced (including accrued interest), (ii) financing of closing costs, prepaid items, and points up to $5,000 total, and (iii) cash back to the borrower up to $250. Excess proceeds may be applied as a curtailment on the new loan.
business_rules.conditions: High LTV refinance option; determining maximum new loan amount components
business_rules.consequences: If within caps, loan amount eligible; if exceeded, must reduce loan amount/curtail
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (excess proceeds may be applied as curtailment)

source_reference.source_text: The standard limited cash-out reﬁnance requirements are modiﬁed for high LTV loan transactions. The new loan amount is limited to the payoﬀ of the UPB of the existing ﬁrst mortgage loan being reﬁnanced (including accrued interest); the ﬁnancing of closing costs, prepaid items, and points up to $5,000 total for the new loan; and cash back to the borrower up to $250. Excess proceeds may be applied as a curtailment on the new loan.

examples: Closing costs/prepaids/points total $6,200: only $5,000 may be financed; remainder must be paid outside the loan or loan amount reduced.
Borrower receives $600 cash back at closing: reduce to $250 max and apply $350 excess as curtailment or adjust loan amount.
Payoff includes accrued interest: accrued interest may be included in the payoff component of the new loan amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: The max new loan amount components and caps in BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 are specific to high LTV refi option processing. If the transaction is not eligible for the option, standard limited cash-out requirements apply instead.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the base program eligibility is not checked first, the lender may incorrectly apply high-LTV refi loan amount caps to a non-high-LTV refi transaction (causing incorrect denials or mis-disclosures), or fail to apply them to an eligible transaction (causing delivery defects).

dependencies.rationale: To ensure the final new loan amount is compliant, the lender must incorporate mandatory adjustments that reduce the maximum available loan amount (unpaid special assessments), otherwise the calculated loan amount under the high-LTV refi caps may still exceed what is permissible after required reductions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If special assessments are not accounted for, the delivered loan amount can exceed allowable limits, causing eligibility defects, required curtailments, or forced rebalancing of funds at closing.

dependencies.rationale: If the borrower fails the high-LTV payment history gate, there is no need to apply the option-specific loan amount caps and curtailment logic. Sequencing the payment history gate earlier reduces rework and prevents structuring a loan under a program the borrower cannot use.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sequencing is not followed, the lender may spend time calculating compliant loan amount components for a borrower who is ineligible due to payment history, delaying requalification into another product.

business_rules.rule_name: Limited cash-out refi 95.01–97% must be fixed 30-year DU only and no ARM
business_rules.description: For a limited cash-out refinance where the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV exceeds 95% (95.01% to 97%), the loan must be a fixed-rate loan with a term up to 30 years, must be underwritten through DU only, and must be secured by a one-unit principal residence that all borrowers occupy. High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted for this high-LTV limited cash-out refinance band. The CLTV may be up to 105% only if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan (per the note in this section).
business_rules.conditions: Limited cash-out refinance; LTV/CLTV/HCLTV in 95.01–97% band
business_rules.consequences: If any criteria not met, transaction is ineligible for this high-LTV LCOR policy
business_rules.exceptions: CLTV up to 105% allowed only with Community Seconds subordinate lien

source_reference.source_text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a limited cash-out transaction, the following requirements also apply. LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% Note : The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Loan Type Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years. Note : High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted. Property and Occupancy One-unit principal residence. All borrowers must occupy the property. Underwriting Method DU only

examples: LCOR at 96% LTV, fixed-rate 30-year, DU underwritten, 1-unit principal residence: eligible under the 95.01–97% criteria (subject to other requirements).
LCOR at 96% LTV, 5/6 ARM: ineligible because ARM loans are not permitted in this 95.01–97% band.
LCOR at 97% LTV with a Community Seconds subordinate lien and 103% CLTV: allowed because CLTV can be up to 105% with Community Seconds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV limited cash-out refinance band has strict product and collateral constraints and only allows CLTV above typical limits (up to 105%) when Community Seconds rules are met. Therefore, if the file uses the CLTV 105% allowance, it is dependent on satisfying Community Seconds constraints (eligible transaction type, terms, and resubordination recording for LCOR).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Community Seconds constraints are not met, the loan may be ineligible for the 95.01–97% policy band or may require restructuring (reduce CLTV, change subordinate financing, or change transaction type).

dependencies.rationale: If the property is misclassified due to multiple ADUs or an ADU combined with a 2–4 unit dwelling, the loan fails the 'one-unit principal residence' requirement for this specific high-LTV LCOR band. Therefore, unit classification is an input prerequisite for eligibility testing under BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ADU classification fails, the loan cannot be delivered under this high-LTV LCOR policy band and may require program change, restructure, or denial.

dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV LCOR rule is a program overlay that still sits within Fannie Mae’s base collateral eligibility. Base unit/location eligibility must be satisfied before program-specific constraints can be applied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the property fails base eligibility (units/location), the high-LTV LCOR policy band cannot be used and the loan is ineligible for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Community Seconds Maximum 105% CLTV and 5-Year ARM
business_rules.description: When a first mortgage is originated with Community Seconds subordinate financing, the first mortgage must be fixed-rate or an ARM with an initial fixed-rate period of at least 5 years, the maximum CLTV is 105% (unless the first mortgage has an independent CLTV cap), and the transaction must be a purchase or limited cash-out refinance. For limited cash-out refinance, the Community Seconds holder must execute a resubordination agreement and it must be recorded to ensure enforceability.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting a first mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae with Community Seconds financing
business_rules.consequences: If constraints not met, loan is ineligible for delivery/purchase by Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: 105% CLTV may be limited by an independent first-mortgage CLTV cap

source_reference.source_text: First mortgage eligibilityThe ﬁrst mortgage must be ﬁxed-rate or an ARM with an initial ﬁxed-rate period of ﬁve years or more, and otherwise comply with this Guide. Maximum LTV and CLTV ratiosThe maximum LTV is based on the ﬁrst mortgage LTV requirement. The maximum CLTV is 105% with a Community Seconds loan, unless the ﬁrst mortgage has an independent CLTV cap (such as the CLTV cap for manufactured housing that does not meet MH Advantage requirements). Refer to the   Eligibility Matrix  for additional information. Loan purposeThe transaction is limited to a purchase or limited cash-out reﬁnance. For a limited cash-out reﬁnance, the Community Seconds mortgage holder must acknowledge their lien position by executing a resubordination agreement. The agreement must be recorded to ensure enforceability.

examples: Purchase loan with Community Seconds: first mortgage is 7/6 ARM (≥5-year initial fixed) and CLTV is 103%; loan meets this rule.
Purchase loan CLTV is 107%; even if first mortgage LTV meets matrix, CLTV violates the 105% cap and loan is ineligible.
Limited cash-out refinance with Community Seconds: resubordination agreement signed but not recorded; loan violates enforceability requirement until recorded.
Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: Before applying structural constraints on a first mortgage with Community Seconds (rate/CLTV caps/allowed transactions/resubordination requirements), the lender must first confirm the subordinate lien qualifies as Community Seconds (eligible provider and not an interested party). Otherwise, the transaction cannot be treated as Community Seconds and becomes ineligible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If provider eligibility is not validated first, a lender may incorrectly allow CLTV up to 105% or permit a transaction structure based on Community Seconds rules, creating a delivery ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Community Seconds Provider Cannot Be Seller or Interested Party
business_rules.description: A Community Seconds loan must be funded only by an eligible provider type (e.g., government entity, qualifying nonprofit, FHLB affordable housing program, eligible employer assistance, eligible lender in employer-guaranteed structure, or eligible Indian tribe). The Community Seconds provider must not be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction, except when the provider is the provider of a shared equity program or sweat equity program.
business_rules.conditions: When determining whether Community Seconds subordinate financing is eligible
business_rules.consequences: If provider is ineligible or interested party, first mortgage becomes ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed if provider is shared equity or sweat equity program provider

source_reference.source_text: A Community Seconds loan must only be funded by one of the following entities: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state; any city, town, village, or borough of a state that has a local government and that has been created by a special legislative act, has been otherwise individually incorporated or chartered pursuant to state law, or is recognized as such under the constitution or by the laws of the state in which it is located; a housing ﬁnance agency as deﬁned in 24 C.F.R. §266.5; a nonproﬁt organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code; a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its aﬀordable housing programs; an employer where a borrower is an employee (see  B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance ); a lender, only in connection with an employer-guaranteed Community Seconds loan as part of its aﬀordable housing program; or an Indian tribe on the most current list published by the Secretary of the Interior pursuant to 25 U.S.C. §5131. A Community Seconds provider must not be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction, except when they are the provider of a shared equity program or sweat equity program.

examples: Subordinate loan is funded by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit and is not the property seller; provider eligibility is met.
Subordinate loan is funded by the property seller as part of a non-shared-equity arrangement; this violates the interested party prohibition.
Subordinate financing is provided by the seller under a documented shared equity program; exception applies and provider may be eligible.
Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Employment-related assets require max 70% LTV or 80% at 62
business_rules.description: When employment-related assets are used as qualifying income under B3-3.4-06, the maximum LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is 70%. The maximum may be 80% only if the owner of the asset(s) used to qualify is at least 62 years old at closing; if the asset is jointly owned, all owners must be borrowers on the loan and the borrower using the income to qualify must be at least 62 at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Employment-related assets are used to qualify and LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is evaluated
business_rules.consequences: If ratios exceed limits, income method not eligible; loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: 80% cap allowed only with age 62 and joint-ownership borrower conditions

source_reference.source_text: Max LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV Ratio • 70%, or • 80% if the owner of the asset(s) being used to qualify is at least 62 years old at the time of closing. If the asset is jointly owned, all owners must be a borrower on the loan and the borrower using the income to qualify must be at least 62 years old at the time of closing.

examples: Borrower age 45 uses severance assets as income at 75% LTV → not permitted; must reduce LTV to 70% or use another income guideline.
Borrower age 63 uses IRA assets as income at 80% LTV → permitted if all other parameters are met.
Assets jointly owned by borrower (63) and non-borrower spouse → not permitted at 80%; co-owner must be a co-borrower to use jointly owned assets.
Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Delivery requires no 30-day delinquencies in lookback
business_rules.description: To be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae, borrower payment status must meet the applicable “no 30-day delinquency” requirement based on loan age at delivery: (1) If the loan is 12 or fewer months old at the time of delivery, the borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies since origination. (2) If the loan is more than one year old at the time of delivery, the borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies in the 12-month period preceding delivery. (3) For assumed loans, the requirement applies to the current borrower; if seasoned and current borrower has owned the property less than 12 months, the lookback is reduced to the months owned.
business_rules.conditions: When a loan is being delivered to Fannie Mae under a whole loan commitment
business_rules.consequences: If delinquency requirement not met, loan is not eligible for delivery/purchase
business_rules.exceptions: Assumed loan lookback reduced to months owned if <12 months

source_reference.source_text: Borrower Payment Status Requirements The table below provides the requirements for payment status for loans delivered to Fannie Mae. 12 or fewer months old at the time of delivery The borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies since the loan was originated. More than one year old at the time of delivery The borrower must not have any 30-day delinquencies in the 12-month period that precedes the lender’s delivery of the loan to Fannie Mae. Assumed The payment status requirement applies to the current borrower. However, for a seasoned loan, if the current borrower has owned the property less than 12 months, the time period is reduced to the number of months that they have owned the property.

examples: Loan delivered 10 months after origination with a 30-day delinquency in month 6: ineligible for delivery.
Loan delivered 26 months after origination; borrower had a 30-day delinquency 18 months ago but none in last 12 months: eligible under payment-status rule.
Assumed loan: current borrower has owned 8 months and had a 30-day delinquency 7 months ago: ineligible because lookback is 8 months owned.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase remedy if delivered with breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Flow loan age maximum 6 months from first payment date
business_rules.description: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a flow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the first payment date to (i) the Purchase Ready date for whole loans or (ii) the MBS pool issue date for MBS loans. Example: if a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022. Exception: HomeStyle Renovation loans delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date. If the loan exceeds the applicable age limit, it is not flow-eligible and may require a negotiated transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Flow purchase eligibility assessment at delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, eligible for flow purchase; if exceeded, ineligible for flow
business_rules.exceptions: HomeStyle Renovation w/ SFC 279: up to 15 months after note date

source_reference.source_text: Age of Loan To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans). For example, if a whole loan is in "Purchase Ready" status in May 2023 or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool, the ﬁrst payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022. NOTE: HomeStyle Renovation loans that are not delivered until renovation is complete (and delivered with SFC 279) can be sold up to 15 months after the note date.

To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans). ... NOTE: HomeStyle Renovation loans that are not delivered until renovation is complete (and delivered with SFC 279) can be sold up to 15 months after the note date.

examples: If a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022.
HomeStyle Renovation delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same flow purchase eligibility aging constraint: loan age must be ≤ 6 months, measured from first payment date to Purchase Ready date (whole loans) or to MBS pool issue date (MBS loans), with the same explicit HomeStyle Renovation exception permitting sale up to 15 months after note date when delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279. Numeric thresholds (6 months, 15 months) and context (flow purchase eligibility) are identical; consequences align (flow-ineligible if exceeded).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_005 selected as primary because it is the most complete: it includes the same six-month age test, explicitly states the measurement points for whole loans vs MBS, includes an illustrative example, and includes the HomeStyle Renovation exception with SFC 279 and the extended timing.

business_rules.rule_name: Unpaid special assessments reduce max loan amount dollar-for-dollar
business_rules.description: If special assessments have been levied against the property and are not paid before or at closing, the maximum loan amount otherwise available must be reduced by the amount of the unpaid special assessments, unless sufficient deposits to pay them will be collected as part of the loan payment. The lender must document that current installments of taxes and assessments (or future installments of levied special assessments) have been paid or that sufficient deposits are being collected to pay them.
business_rules.conditions: Property has levied special assessments not paid before/at closing
business_rules.consequences: If reduced or deposits collected, compliant; if not, max loan exceeded/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: No reduction if sufficient deposits to pay are collected as part of loan payment

source_reference.source_text: Impact of Special Assessments on Maximum Loan Amount If special assessments have been levied against the property and they are not paid before or at closing, the maximum loan amount otherwise available must be reduced by the amount of the unpaid special assessments (unless suﬃcient deposits to pay them will be collected as part of the loan payment). The lender must provide documentation to show that the current installments of taxes and assessments (or future installments of special assessments that have been levied) - including those which may have been attached as prior liens, but which are not now in arrears - have been paid or that suﬃcient deposits are being collected to pay them.

examples: Unpaid special assessment is $6,000 at closing; max loan amount must be reduced by $6,000 if not escrowed/collected.
Special assessment installments will be collected monthly via escrow deposits; no dollar-for-dollar reduction required.
Lender does not document payment or deposits for levied special assessment; loan is noncompliant and may be ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: If sold > 4 months after note date, current property value cannot be lower
business_rules.description: For loans sold to Fannie Mae more than four months from the note date, the lender must be able to warrant that the current property value is not less than the original value. If the lender cannot provide this warranty, the loan is not eligible for sale except on a negotiated basis.
business_rules.conditions: When note date to sale date exceeds four months for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If current value is lower or unwarranted, loan becomes negotiated-only/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Negotiated basis permitted when lender cannot provide the value warranty

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), Property Value for Loans Sold More than Four Months from Note Date
source_reference.source_text: For loans that are more than four months old from the date of the note to the date the loan is sold to Fannie Mae, the current value of the property cannot be less than the original value. If the lender is unable to warrant that the current value of the property is not less than the original value of the property, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae by the lender except on a negotiated basis.

examples: Loan note date Jan 1; sold May 15 (>4 months); lender obtains evidence current value ≥ original and can warrant—eligible.
Loan note date Jan 10; sold Jun 1; updated data shows value decreased—loan not eligible for flow sale.
Lender cannot obtain adequate support for current value; delivers only via negotiated transaction after Fannie Mae agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for standard sale; negotiated-only delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Flood coverage minimum is lesser of 100% RCV or UPB
business_rules.description: For one- to four-unit properties, the minimum amount of flood insurance required for first mortgages must equal the lesser of (1) 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements, (2) the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or (3) the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan (or loan amount at origination).
business_rules.conditions: First mortgage secured by 1-4 unit property where flood insurance is required
business_rules.consequences: Meets: flood policy satisfies minimum coverage; Violated: ineligible/delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-3-06, Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties
source_reference.source_text: Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties The minimum amount of ﬂood insurance required for ﬁrst mortgages must be equal to the lesser of 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements, the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan (or loan amount at the time of origination).

examples: RCV $350,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $300,000 → minimum required flood coverage is $250,000 (lesser-of).
RCV $200,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $180,000 → minimum required is $180,000.
RCV $500,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $260,000 → minimum required is $250,000.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit lesser-of rule with three quantified coverage bases.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV refi requires 12-month payment history limits
business_rules.description: To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option, the existing loan payment history for the most recent 12 months must show: (1) no delinquencies in the most recent 6 months; and (2) during months 7-12, no more than one 30-day delinquency and no delinquency greater than 30 days.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is evaluated for high LTV refinance option; 12-month payment history reviewed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: eligible for high LTV refi option; Violated: ineligible for the option
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt

source_reference.section_id: B5-7-02, Underwriting and Documentation Requirements (Payment History)
source_reference.source_text: Payment History To be eligible for the high LTV reﬁnance option, the payment history for the existing loan for the most recent 12 months must reﬂect • no delinquencies in the most recent 6 months, and • in months 7-12, no more than one, 30-day delinquency and no delinquency greater than 30 days.

examples: One 30-day late 9 months ago; no lates in last 6 months → passes payment history requirement.
Two separate 30-day lates in months 7-12 → fails (more than one 30-day delinquency).
One 60-day delinquency 10 months ago → fails (delinquency greater than 30 days).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear quantified delinquency limits for eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery under high LTV refinance option

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009 establishes the foundational program eligibility (existing loan owned by Fannie, note date threshold, and seasoning). If those gating criteria are not met, the payment history test is irrelevant to program eligibility and the file should exit the high LTV refi workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If base eligibility is not checked first, lenders may spend time collecting/verifying 12-month payment history for a loan that is categorically ineligible for the high LTV refinance option.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Minimum 5% Own Funds or 3% MH Advantage
business_rules.description: For loans secured by manufactured homes, a minimum down payment of 5% must come from the borrower’s own funds unless (i) LTV or CLTV is ≤ 80%, (ii) the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets requirements to use gifts/grants/employer funds toward minimum contribution, or (iii) the property meets MH Advantage requirements and the loan meets LTV ratio requirements of 95.01–97%, in which case the borrower must contribute a minimum down payment of 3% from own funds unless gift/grant/employer policy applies.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by manufactured home; purchase or applicable scenario with LTV/CLTV
business_rules.consequences: If met, loan satisfies minimum contribution; if violated, restructure funds or ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Own-funds minimum waived when LTV/CLTV ≤80% or gift/grant/employer permitted

source_reference.source_text: Down Payment Requirements A minimum down payment of 5% must come from the borrower’s own funds unless: the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%; the borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets the requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or funds received from an employer to pay for some or all of the borrower's minimum contribution. See B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts ; B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions ; and B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance , for additional information; or the property meets the MH Advantage requirements and the loan meets the requirements for LTV ratios of 95.01-97%. In this case, the borrower must contribute a minimum down payment of 3%, from their own funds unless the loan meets the gift, grant, or funds from an employer policy referenced above.

examples: Purchase manufactured home, LTV 90%: borrower must provide at least 5% down from own funds unless gift/grant/employer assistance is allowed for minimum contribution.
Purchase manufactured home, CLTV 80%: 5% own-funds minimum does not apply because CLTV ≤ 80%.
MH Advantage purchase, LTV 96%: borrower must contribute at least 3% from own funds (unless gift/grant/employer assistance policy permits otherwise).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: If the borrower intends to meet the manufactured home minimum contribution using borrower-owned land equity, the lender must validate and calculate that land equity per timing rules. Without this validation, the lender cannot reliably determine whether the borrower meets the required own-funds down payment threshold.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If land equity is improperly credited, the borrower may appear to meet the minimum contribution when they do not, resulting in an ineligible loan or requiring last-minute additional funds to close.

dependencies.rationale: When trade equity is used as part of the borrower contribution, the lender must apply the equity cap rules (90% of retail value, and additional cap if owned <12 months). The result is used to determine if the borrower’s qualifying own-funds/equity meets the manufactured home minimum contribution requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If trade equity is overstated, the borrower may not actually meet the minimum contribution, creating an eligibility defect or forcing additional funds/cure at closing.

business_rules.rule_name: Primary mortgage insurance required for conventional first mortgage LTV over 80%
business_rules.description: Unless the lender provides another charter-compliant form of credit enhancement, the lender must obtain a primary mortgage insurance policy for a conventional first mortgage loan with an LTV ratio greater than 80% at the time it is purchased for Fannie Mae’s portfolio or securitized. For this purpose, the LTV ratio is calculated based on the unpaid principal balance (UPB) at the time purchased/securitized by Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to conventional first mortgages with LTV > 80% at purchase/securitization
business_rules.consequences: If MI in place, meets coverage requirement; if absent, delivery noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Exception when lender provides other charter-compliant credit enhancement

source_reference.source_text: Unless the lender has provided another charter-compliant form of credit enhancement, the lender must obtain a primary mortgage insurance policy for a conventional first mortgage loan that has an LTV ratio greater than 80% at the time it is purchased for Fannie Mae’s portfolio or securitized. (For this purpose, the LTV ratio is calculated based upon the unpaid principal balance of the mortgage loan at the time it is purchased or securitized by Fannie Mae.)

examples: Loan delivers at 82% LTV based on UPB at acquisition: lender must have primary MI coverage in force.
Loan was originated at 81% LTV but amortized to 79.5% UPB-based LTV at purchase: primary MI is not required under this >80% test.
Lender provides a different charter-compliant credit enhancement: primary MI may not be required if that enhancement satisfies charter compliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; potential repurchase demand if acquired without MI

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_008 explicitly states buydown funds 'cannot be used to reduce the mortgage amount for purposes of determining the LTV ratio.' BR_008_010 relies on LTV>80% to trigger MI. Therefore, correct LTV computation under BR_008_010 depends on honoring BR_028_008’s restriction whenever a temporary buydown exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_028_008 is violated, LTV may be understated (by netting buydown funds), causing the lender to incorrectly omit MI and deliver a noncompliant loan.

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_003 requires excluding the panels and related debt from LTV/CLTV calculations when panels are personal property not appearing on title. BR_008_010 requires MI if LTV>80% at purchase/securitization. Therefore, correct application of BR_033_003 is necessary to ensure the LTV used to trigger MI is computed correctly in these solar scenarios.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_003 is not applied, LTV could be misstated (either overstated by adding value or misstated by treating non-mortgage debt as lien affecting CLTV), leading to incorrect MI decisions and delivery defects.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 005_010 prohibits delivery to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on OFAC lists. Rule 008_010 is a delivery condition for high-LTV conventional loans (MI required unless other credit enhancement). In delivery workflow sequencing, OFAC clearance is a hard gate that must be satisfied before evaluating and acting on other delivery conditions like MI.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails (OFAC match), the loan must not be delivered regardless of MI status; attempting to proceed exposes the lender to sanctions/compliance enforcement and Fannie Mae remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_021_007 reduces the sales price when seller-provided subordinate financing is deemed a sales concession. A lower sales price can raise LTV/CLTV ratios, which can cause the loan to cross above the 80% LTV threshold that triggers the MI requirement in BR_008_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_021_007 is missed, lender may fail to obtain MI when required (delivery noncompliance) or may deliver inaccurate ratios.

business_rules.rule_name: Loans with primary MI and app date on or after Oct 1 2014 must use approved forms
business_rules.description: Any mortgage loan sold to or securitized by Fannie Mae that requires primary mortgage insurance (or is delivered with primary mortgage insurance even though not required) and has a loan application date on or after October 1, 2014, must be insured under one of the Fannie Mae-approved mortgage insurance forms. If the loan is insured under any pre-existing forms or agreements between lenders and mortgage insurers, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae and is subject to repurchase if identified after acquisition.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when primary MI is present/required and application date ≥ 2014-10-01
business_rules.consequences: Approved form makes loan eligible; non-approved form makes ineligible/repurchasable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for application dates on/after 2014-10-01

source_reference.source_text: Any mortgage loan sold to or securitized by Fannie Mae that requires primary mortgage insurance (or is delivered with primary mortgage insurance even though not required) and has a loan application date on or after October 1, 2014, must be insured under one of the Fannie Mae-approved Forms. If such loan is insured under any pre-existing forms or agreements between lenders and mortgage insurers, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, and is subject to repurchase if identified after acquisition by Fannie Mae.

examples: Application date 2018-03-05 with MI: lender uses an approved MI form; loan remains eligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
Application date 2020-09-10 with MI delivered under a lender/MI company legacy agreement: loan is ineligible and subject to repurchase if acquired.
Application date 2014-10-01 with MI: approved MI form must be used because the date is on/after the threshold.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_008_010 establishes when primary MI is required for conventional first mortgages (LTV > 80% at purchase/securitization unless other charter-compliant enhancement is provided). BR_008_011 then constrains *which MI forms* are acceptable for any loan that requires MI (or is delivered with MI). Therefore, MI requirement/presence is the trigger and prerequisite context for the MI-form eligibility rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_008_010 is not satisfied/assessed correctly (e.g., LTV computed incorrectly at purchase), the lender may fail to apply BR_008_011 and deliver a loan with MI that is required but not on an approved form, resulting in ineligibility/repurchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: 005_010 is a universal prohibition on delivery for OFAC matches. 008_011 is an eligibility rule applicable when MI is required/present. A loan that cannot be delivered due to OFAC status should not proceed in the delivery eligibility evaluation tree (including MI form eligibility).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, 008_011 eligibility becomes moot because delivery is prohibited; failure to stop can create severe regulatory and contractual consequences.

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum IPC Financing Concessions 3% 6% 9% 2%
business_rules.description: Interested party financing concessions are capped based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV, and must be calculated using the lower of the sales price or appraised value (not the loan amount). Principal residence or second home: >90% LTV/CLTV max 3%; 75.01%–90% max 6%; 75% or less max 9%. Investment property: all CLTV ratios max 2%.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions with IPC financing concessions from interested parties
business_rules.consequences: Over-limit IPCs become sales concessions; adjust price and recalc ratios
business_rules.exceptions: Common/customary seller fees excluded; HomePath exception referenced

source_reference.source_text: The table below provides maximum ﬁnancing concessions, which are calculated using the lower of the sales price or appraised value (not the loan amount) of the subject transaction. Typical fees and/or closing costs paid by a seller in accordance with local custom, known as common and customary fees or costs, are not subject to Fannie Mae maximum ﬁnancing concessions. Occupancy Type LTV/CLTV Ratio Maximum Financing Concessions Principal residence or second home Greater than 90% 3% 75.01% – 90% 6% 75% or less 9% Investment property All CLTV ratios 2%

examples: Primary residence purchase at 95% LTV: seller-paid closing costs limited to 3% of lower of sales price or appraised value.
Second home at 80% LTV: IPC financing concessions limited to 6% of lower of sales price or appraised value.
Investment property at any CLTV: IPC financing concessions limited to 2% of lower of sales price or appraised value.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Caps and calculation base explicitly stated, including the full % table.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility or price/ratio correction required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 classifies certain HOA fee payments as IPCs when the payment extends 12 months or less. Once classified as an IPC, the IPC cap calculation rule (BR_009_006) applies to limit the concession based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_008 classification is missed, the lender may fail to cap HOA credits as IPCs, causing excessive concessions and potential ratio miscalculations and delivery defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 states: 'Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible.' This creates an eligibility stop that renders IPC cap compliance (BR_009_006) irrelevant for the abatement portion because the structure is prohibited outright.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If override is not implemented, the system may incorrectly clear a loan by capping/converting abatements via IPC logic, when the correct outcome is delivery ineligibility.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_006 caps IPCs based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV thresholds. BR_028_008 prohibits using buydown funds to reduce mortgage amount for LTV determination. When a temporary buydown exists, BR_009_006’s tiering should use the correct LTV/CLTV unaffected by buydown funds.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_028_008 is ignored, a loan could be incorrectly placed into a lower-LTV tier, allowing higher IPC caps than permitted or masking excess concessions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_006 caps IPCs based on LTV/CLTV thresholds. BR_033_003 requires excluding panels and related debt from LTV/CLTV calculations in specific cases. That exclusion can change the LTV/CLTV tier selection used for IPC caps, making BR_033_003 a strong conditional dependency for accurate IPC cap application.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_003 is missed, the lender may apply the wrong IPC cap tier (3/6/9/2%) and mis-handle concessions, leading to purchase ineligibility or required post-closing corrections.

dependencies.rationale: If the appraisal improperly handles concession adjustments (e.g., positive adjustments or non-market-based), the appraised value used in BR_009_006 (lower-of test for IPC cap) may be wrong. Therefore, appraisal concession adjustment compliance should be validated before relying on the appraised value to compute IPC caps.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_005 fails, appraised value may be overstated, causing IPC caps to be calculated on an incorrect (possibly higher) value and masking excess concessions. This can trigger later appraisal corrections, underwriting rework, price renegotiations, and delivery defects.

dependencies.rationale: If the appraisal improperly appraises a hypothetical portion of the site or fails to reflect the entire parcel, the appraised value is not acceptable. IPC caps under BR_009_006 depend on the lower of sales price or appraised value; thus appraisal acceptability should be validated before finalizing IPC cap compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure of BR_010_001 leads to appraisal revision/replacement, potentially changing the appraised value and the IPC cap calculation basis. This can cause re-disclosure/renegotiation, underwriting rework, and delivery delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Converted ARM Must Be 12 Months Old and Current 45 Days
business_rules.description: Converted ARMs delivered (whole loan or MBS) under the “market rate” post-conversion option must meet specific eligibility: the ARM must have been at least 12 months old when the conversion occurred; and the mortgage loan must be current at the time of delivery. For processing purposes, Fannie Mae considers a mortgage current if no more than 45 days have elapsed since the last paid installment date.
business_rules.conditions: Redelivery/delivery of converted ARMs under the “market rate” option
business_rules.consequences: If age/current tests fail, loan is not eligible under this delivery path
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited excerpt

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-03, Eligibility Requirements for Converted ARMs
source_reference.source_text: The following speciﬁc eligibility requirements apply to converted ARMs that are delivered as either whole loans or MBS pool deliveries under the “market rate” post-conversion disposition option that were removed from an ARM MBS pool as the result of the conversion: The ARM must have been at least 12 months old when the conversion occurred. The mortgage loan must be current at the time of delivery. Note : To minimize processing delays, Fannie Mae considers a mortgage current if no more than 45 days have elapsed since the last paid installment date.

examples: ARM originated 14 months ago, converted last month, last paid installment 20 days ago: meets age and current tests.
ARM originated 10 months ago at conversion: fails 12-month minimum; not eligible for this converted-ARM delivery requirement.
Last paid installment was 60 days ago at delivery: not “current” under the 45-day processing definition.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum age and ‘current’ definition with 45-day threshold.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase remedy if delivered defective

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: Converted ARM eligibility conditions (age/current tests) are only actionable if the loan can be delivered at all. OFAC failure blocks delivery regardless of meeting converted-ARM requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, delivery cannot proceed even if the converted ARM is current and seasoned; sanctions actions (notification, blocking funds, ceasing servicing) take priority.

business_rules.rule_name: Converted ARM Post-Origination Caps 2% Rate and 15% Payment
business_rules.description: When the lender qualifies a converted-ARM borrower using the ARM’s origination interest rate and the ARM underwriting guidelines used at that time, the total of all interest rate increases or payment adjustments that occurred after origination (including scheduled ARM changes and buydown-plan increases) must not have exceeded 2% for the interest rate adjustment and must not have exceeded 15% for the payment adjustment.
business_rules.conditions: Converted ARM borrower qualified using origination rate and prior ARM guides
business_rules.consequences: If caps exceeded, borrower cannot be qualified under this option
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited excerpt

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-03, Eligibility Requirements for Converted ARMs
source_reference.source_text: The total of all interest rate increases or payment adjustments (including any combination of scheduled ARM interest rate changes and the increases scheduled under an interest rate buydown plan) that occurred after the ARM was originated must not have exceeded 2% (for the interest rate adjustment) or 15% (for the payment adjustment) if the lender qualiﬁes the borrower on the basis of the mortgage interest rate that was in eﬀect for the ARM when it was originated and the ARM underwriting guidelines Fannie Mae used at that time.

examples: Origination rate 4.00% later rose to 5.75% (1.75% increase) and payment rose 10%: within 2% and 15% caps for this qualification option.
Origination rate 3.50% rose to 5.75% (2.25% increase): exceeds the 2% cap; borrower cannot be qualified under the origination-rate option.
ARM payment increased 18% due to adjustments and buydown step-ups: exceeds 15% cap; borrower cannot be qualified under this option.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric caps with explicit condition describing when they apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or underwriting requalification required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_009 defines the converted ARM delivery scenario ('market rate' post-conversion option) with eligibility requirements (loan age, current status). BR_009_010 adds additional qualification limits when the lender uses the origination rate/prior guidelines qualification method. Thus, BR_009_009 establishes the converted ARM processing path in which BR_009_010 may apply as a branch.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_009 is not identified, BR_009_010 may not be triggered and the lender could qualify the borrower using an impermissible method (or outside caps), creating delivery ineligibility or QC findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Leasehold Loans Must Be First Lien on Improvements and Leasehold
business_rules.description: For a leasehold mortgage, the loan must be secured by a first lien in (i) the property improvements and (ii) the borrower's rights in the leasehold interest in the land. Non-compliance can constitute a breach of life-of-loan representations and warranties if it impacts first-lien enforceability.
business_rules.conditions: Mortgage loan is secured by a leasehold estate (leasehold mortgage)
business_rules.consequences: If met, lien position acceptable; if violated, ineligible and breach if enforceability impacted
business_rules.exceptions: Co-op share loans follow separate co-op legal/eligibility sections

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-03, General Requirements for Leasehold Mortgages
source_reference.source_text: General Requirements for Leasehold Mortgages The lender must comply with all requirements for leasehold mortgages. In addition, the lender agrees that in accordance with A2-2-07, Life-of-Loan Representations and Warranties, any failure to comply at any time with requirements in this topic is a breach of the life-of-loan representations and warranties if it impacts ﬁrst-lien enforceability. The loan must be secured by a ﬁrst lien in the property improvements and the borrower's rights in the leasehold interest in the land.

examples: Compliant: Mortgage is recorded as a first lien on the improvements and assigns the borrower’s leasehold rights as collateral.
Non-compliant: A prior deed of trust encumbers the improvements, making the delivered mortgage a second lien.
Edge case: Co-op share loan is evaluated under B4-2.3-03/B4-2.3-04 rather than this first-lien-on-improvements rule.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_004 requires payoff at or prior to closing for delinquent credit items that could affect Fannie Mae’s lien position (tax liens, mechanic’s liens, etc.). BR_011_002 requires the loan be secured by a first lien in improvements and leasehold rights; delinquent recorded liens can impair that first-lien enforceability. Therefore, lien payoff/clearance is a prerequisite control for satisfying leasehold first-lien conditions when such items exist.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_004 is not satisfied, the loan may close with senior or competing liens that undermine first-lien enforceability, creating delivery ineligibility and breach of R&Ws.

dependencies.rationale: Leasehold lien requirements (011_002) are relevant to delivery eligibility, but only if the loan is deliverable from a sanctions standpoint. OFAC failure overrides all other delivery eligibility determinations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, the loan cannot be delivered regardless of meeting leasehold lien standards; additional actions (block/segregate funds, cease servicing) may be required.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_001 establishes whether a leasehold estate can be eligible collateral (market acceptance + eligible property type/PERS constraints). BR_011_002 then imposes lien-structure requirements for a leasehold mortgage (first lien in improvements and in borrower’s leasehold rights). Operationally, lenders first confirm the leasehold collateral is allowed, then validate lien enforceability structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_011_001 fails and is not caught, the lender may spend time validating lien structure under BR_011_002 for a transaction that is ineligible regardless, causing rework and late-stage fallout. If BR_011_002 is skipped on an eligible leasehold, enforceability issues can create life-of-loan R&W breach risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Lease Term Must Exceed Loan Maturity by at Least 5 Years
business_rules.description: For leasehold mortgage transactions, the lease must have an unexpired term that exceeds the maturity date of the loan by five (5) years or more. This requirement applies to all leases associated with the subject property, regardless of lease form (master lease, sublease, or unit lease).
business_rules.conditions: Mortgage loan is secured by leasehold estate; lease is evaluated for term
business_rules.consequences: If met, lease term is acceptable; if violated, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: Regardless of the lessee, the lender must ensure the terms of the lease address all of the following: The lease must have an unexpired term that exceeds the maturity date of the loan by ﬁve (5) years or more. The lease must not preclude the borrower's membership or voting rights in the HOA or co-op corporation, as applicable.

examples: Compliant: 30-year loan maturity 2056-06-01; lease expires 2062-06-30 (6+ years beyond maturity).
Non-compliant: 30-year loan maturity 2056-06-01; lease expires 2058-05-31 (less than 5 years beyond maturity).
Edge case: 15-year loan maturity 2041-07-01; lease expires 2046-07-01 (exactly 5 years beyond maturity, compliant).
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_002 defines the security requirements for a leasehold mortgage (first lien in improvements and leasehold interest). BR_011_004 imposes an additional leasehold-specific constraint on the lease term (must extend 5 years beyond loan maturity). Therefore, BR_011_002 provides the gating context for applying BR_011_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If leasehold identification/security structure is missed (BR_011_002), lease term sufficiency (BR_011_004) may not be validated, risking delivery ineligibility and life-of-loan R&W breaches.

dependencies.rationale: Lease-term compliance (011_004) is necessary for leasehold delivery, but is only relevant after OFAC screening permits delivery. 005_010 functions as an earlier, absolute block.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, delivery cannot occur even if the lease term exceeds maturity by 5+ years; sanctions response steps apply.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_001 determines whether leasehold collateral can be eligible at all. BR_011_004 then tests a key leasehold attribute (unexpired term must exceed loan maturity by 5+ years). In a compliance workflow, BR_011_001 acts as the initial eligibility gate; BR_011_004 is a subsequent leasehold condition test.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_011_001 is not checked, lenders may waste time on term calculations for ineligible leasehold scenarios. If BR_011_004 is missed, an otherwise eligible leasehold loan may be delivered with an unacceptable lease term, creating an ineligible delivery.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_004 requires the lease term to exceed the loan maturity date by 5+ years. To execute that test, the system must have the loan’s maturity date, which is driven by original term and first payment date conventions addressed in BR_002_005 (term limits and measurement reference). While BR_002_005 is not leasehold-specific, it provides the standardized term boundary and timing convention required for a consistent maturity-date input to the leasehold term test.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If loan term/maturity is not correctly determined per BR_002_005 conventions, the leasehold maturity buffer test may be wrong, leading to improper acceptance/rejection of leasehold collateral.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady manual underwrite maximum LTV 95% not 97%
business_rules.description: For HomeReady mortgage loans, when the underwriting method is manual (not DU), the maximum LTV for one-unit principal residence transactions is 95%. If the same transaction is underwritten in DU, the maximum LTV is 97%. Manually underwritten HomeReady loans also do not receive the DU-related limited waiver of representations and warranties referenced in the Guide.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady loan; 1-unit principal residence; manual underwriting vs DU
business_rules.consequences: If LTV exceeds the manual limit, loan is not eligible as HomeReady/manual
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.source_text: HomeReady mortgage loans can be underwritten with DU or may be manually underwritten. The maximum LTV ratio is lower for manually underwritten transactions versus those underwritten in DU (95% versus 97% for one- unit principal residences). As a reminder, the limited waiver of representations and warranties typically granted for loans underwritten with DU does not apply to manually underwritten loans.

examples: Manual underwrite, 1-unit principal residence at 96% LTV: ineligible for HomeReady/manual; restructure to ≤95% or underwrite in DU if allowed.
DU underwrite, 1-unit principal residence at 97% LTV: meets the stated DU maximum LTV for HomeReady (subject to all other requirements).
Manual underwrite, 1-unit principal residence at 95% LTV: meets the stated manual maximum; note DU limited waiver does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_003 sets HomeReady maximum LTV by underwriting method (manual vs DU). BR_020_001 is the foundational HomeReady eligibility gate (income limit and required AMI source). Therefore, BR_020_001 must be satisfied before BR_013_003 is applied for a HomeReady eligibility decision.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_001 fails, applying BR_013_003 could lead to an incorrect conclusion that the loan is 'HomeReady eligible' based solely on LTV/UW method while it is actually ineligible due to AMI.

dependencies.rationale: HomeReady LTV constraints determine program eligibility, but a loan that fails OFAC screening cannot be delivered irrespective of program compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, HomeReady underwriting/LTV analysis is irrelevant for delivery; delivery must stop and sanctions procedures apply.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady 2–4 unit purchase needs 3% own funds over 80%
business_rules.description: For HomeReady purchase transactions (excluding loans with grants), when the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is greater than 80% and the subject is a two- to four-unit principal residence, the borrower must contribute a minimum of 3% from the borrower’s own funds. The requirement is not applicable when there is any type of grant. No minimum contribution is required for a limited cash-out refinance transaction (as stated in this section).
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady; purchase; 2–4 unit principal residence; ratio > 80%; no grant
business_rules.consequences: If borrower own-funds < 3%, loan fails HomeReady contribution requirement
business_rules.exceptions: Grant/lender-funded grant present; limited cash-out refinance has no minimum

source_reference.section_id: B5-6-02, Minimum Borrower Contribution for Purchase Transactions
source_reference.source_text: Greater than 80% One-unit principal residence A minimum borrower contribution from the borrower's own funds in not required. Two- to four-unit principal residence The borrower must make a 3% minimum contribution from their own funds unless there is any type of grant. Note: If a grant or lender-funded grant is being provided, see B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions for additional borrower contribution requirements. No minimum contribution is required in connection with a limited cash-out reﬁnance transaction.

examples: HomeReady purchase, 3-unit principal residence, 85% LTV, no grant: borrower must contribute at least 3% from own funds.
HomeReady purchase, 4-unit principal residence, 90% LTV with a qualifying grant: the 3% own-funds requirement is not required per this section.
HomeReady limited cash-out refinance: no minimum borrower contribution required (per this section).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_004 is a HomeReady-specific borrower own-funds minimum contribution rule for certain 2–4 unit purchase transactions when ratios exceed 80% and no grant is present. BR_020_001 is the foundational HomeReady eligibility gate; thus it must be satisfied before enforcing HomeReady-specific contribution overlays.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_001 is skipped, lenders may enforce HomeReady contribution rules on non-HomeReady loans (unnecessary friction) or, worse, deliver loans as HomeReady without meeting AMI limits.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_004 states: 'If permissible cash back is provided, the lender must confirm minimum borrower contribution requirements are met.' BR_013_004 defines a specific HomeReady minimum borrower contribution requirement (3% own funds) for certain 2–4 unit, >80% ratio purchase transactions (no grant). Therefore, BR_030_004 acts as a validation checkpoint ensuring BR_013_004 is satisfied in scenarios where cash back might otherwise erode the borrower’s required contribution.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_030_004 validation is not performed, a loan may close with cash back that effectively reduces the borrower’s net contribution below the required 3% own funds, violating BR_013_004 and creating delivery ineligibility.

dependencies.rationale: Even if a HomeReady 2–4 unit purchase meets the 3% own-funds rule, the loan cannot be delivered if OFAC screening fails. Therefore, OFAC is a prerequisite gate in any delivery automation sequence.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, delivery is blocked and sanctions response steps apply; HomeReady contribution compliance does not cure OFAC ineligibility.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_004 applies when 'LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is greater than 80%'. In HELOC-subordinate-financing scenarios, BR_021_006 specifies that HCLTV must include the full HELOC amount (drawn or not) and must use the lesser of sales price or appraised value as the denominator. This calculation is required to decide whether the 3% borrower own-funds contribution requirement is triggered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_021_006 is not applied, HCLTV may be understated (e.g., using drawn amount only), resulting in missed borrower contribution requirements and HomeReady eligibility defects.

business_rules.rule_name: Shared appreciation provider share limited to standard percentage of down payment
business_rules.description: In a Community Seconds shared appreciation transaction, the provider’s share of appreciation must not exceed the “Standard Percentage,” which is derived by dividing the original shared appreciation loan amount by the original sales price. The Guide example indicates that if the provider contributed a 10% down payment, the provider cannot share in appreciation greater than 10% when the home is sold. The provider may exceed the Standard Percentage only if the program meets the stated exception structures (borrower recovery waterfall and/or capped/reducing percentages over time).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when first mortgage includes Community Seconds shared appreciation loan terms
business_rules.consequences: If within cap/exception, subordinate is eligible; if exceeded, ineligible structure
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed with borrower recovery waterfall or 75% then proportional reduction

source_reference.source_text: Except as permitted below, the provider's share of appreciation must not exceed the "Standard Percentage" which is derived by dividing the original shared appreciation loan amount by the original sales price. For example, if the provider contributed a 10% down payment towards the purchase of a home, the provider cannot share in appreciation greater than 10% (the Standard Percentage) when the home is sold.

examples: Provider funds $30,000 on a $300,000 purchase (10%): provider share of appreciation must be ≤ 10% unless an exception structure applies.
Provider funds $20,000 on a $200,000 purchase (10%) but legal docs require provider gets 20% of appreciation with no exception waterfall: violates Standard Percentage cap and is ineligible.
Provider share set to 50% in year 1 and reduces proportionally each year to reach ≤ Standard Percentage by year 5: can be permitted under stated exception approach if all conditions met.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_003 grants additional CLTV only 'when the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan.' BR_014_010 imposes a key Community Seconds shared appreciation constraint (provider appreciation share capped by Standard Percentage unless exception). In practice, using the HomeReady CLTV exception requires confirming the subordinate is an eligible Community Seconds structure, which includes complying with shared appreciation limits when that feature exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_010 is not checked, a lender may allow CLTV up to 105% based on a Community Seconds label even though the subordinate lien terms are ineligible (e.g., excessive shared appreciation), risking delivery rejection or remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum DTI Is 36% Manual and 50% DU Casefiles
business_rules.description: Maximum allowable DTI thresholds are: (1) Manually underwritten loans: maximum total DTI ratio is 36% of stable monthly income, and may be exceeded up to 45% only if the borrower meets credit score and reserve requirements in the Eligibility Matrix; (2) DU-underwritten loan casefiles: maximum allowable DTI ratio is 50%.
business_rules.conditions: DTI ratio is used in underwriting (manual underwriting or DU loan casefile)
business_rules.consequences: If within max, DTI meets Guide limit; if exceeded, loan is not eligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Specific transactions may have different DTI limits per listed cross-references

source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, Fannie Mae’s maximum total DTI ratio is 36% of the borrower’s stable monthly income. The maximum can be exceeded up to 45% if the borrower meets the credit score and reserve requirements reﬂected in the Eligibility Matrix. For loan caseﬁles underwritten through DU, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 50%.

examples: Manual underwrite with DTI 35% meets the 36% maximum—no Eligibility Matrix exception needed.
Manual underwrite with DTI 44% is allowed only if Eligibility Matrix credit score and reserves requirements are met.
DU loan casefile with DTI 51% exceeds 50%—loan is not eligible for delivery.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_006 defines different DTI maxima for manually underwritten loans vs DU casefiles. BR_029_007 mandates manual underwriting for Alternative Qualification Path loans, which determines that the manual-underwriting branch of BR_015_006 is the relevant baseline DTI framework (subject to any path-specific overrides).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_029_007 is not applied, the lender may apply DU DTI limits (50%) instead of manual DTI limits, producing an incorrect eligibility decision.

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_006 provides general DTI maxima (manual 36% with potential up to 45% if additional requirements met; DU up to 50%). BR_029_006 imposes a specific max DTI of 45% for Alternative Qualification Path loans. If an automation engine applies both without prioritization, it could wrongly (a) allow DU 50% on an Alternative Path loan, or (b) require the manual 36% base limit even when Alternative Path intends a hard 45% ceiling. This dependency enforces rule hierarchy: path-specific DTI cap governs Alternative Path loans.
