dependencies.impact_if_fails: If override priority is not implemented, the same loan could be simultaneously marked eligible (under one rule) and ineligible (under the other), breaking automated decisioning and causing compliance defects or unnecessary denials.

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_003 states that when financed solar panels are separate non-mortgage debt and not on title, the lender must include the solar debt obligation in DTI. BR_015_006 sets maximum allowable DTI thresholds. Therefore, BR_033_003 is a direct input rule for DTI computation used by BR_015_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_003 is missed, DTI may be understated, leading to an incorrect approval and potential repurchase/QC findings for miscalculated DTI and ineligible delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle Renovation Costs Cap 75% Purchase 50% MH
business_rules.description: For HomeStyle Renovation mortgages, renovation costs are capped based on transaction type: (1) Purchase: renovation costs must not exceed 75% of the lesser of (a) purchase price plus renovation costs, or (b) the “as completed” appraised value. (2) Refinance: renovation costs must not exceed 75% of the “as completed” appraised value. (3) Manufactured homes: renovation costs must not exceed 50% of the “as completed” appraised value.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting a HomeStyle Renovation mortgage with renovation costs included
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded, loan is not compliant and must be restructured or made ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The maximum cost for renovations for various HomeStyle Renovation scenarios are described in the following table. Transaction Type The cost of renovations must not exceed... Purchase transaction75% of the lesser of • the sum of the purchase price of the property plus renovation costs, or • the “as completed” appraised value of the property. Reﬁnance transaction75% of the “as completed” appraised value of the property. Manufactured homes 50% of the “as completed” appraised value.

examples: Purchase: purchase price $300,000, reno $60,000, as-completed value $355,000. Lesser of ($360,000, $355,000)=$355,000; 75% cap=$266,250; reno $60,000 is within cap.
Refinance: as-completed value $400,000; renovation costs cap is 75%=$300,000; planned renovations $320,000 exceeds cap and must be reduced.
Manufactured home: as-completed value $200,000; renovation costs cap is 50%=$100,000; planned renovations $120,000 exceeds cap.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_017_007 defines the LTV calculation basis for HomeStyle by transaction type (purchase: lesser of as-completed value vs purchase price+reno costs; refi: as-completed value). BR_017_004 caps renovation costs as a percent of a related basis (purchase: lesser of purchase price+reno costs vs as-completed value; refi: as-completed value; manufactured: 50% of as-completed). While neither is a strict mathematical prerequisite of the other, they are tightly linked underwriting tests sharing inputs and should be navigated/executed as a bundle.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_017_007 and BR_017_004 are not evaluated together, a lender might pass one test while failing the other due to inconsistent use of as-completed value or renovation cost totals, causing eligibility errors and post-closing defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_017_005 limits DIY eligibility (one-unit only, not manufactured) and caps DIY renovations to 10% of as-completed value, requiring advance review and inspections. BR_017_004 caps total renovation costs based on transaction type. When DIY is used, the DIY scope becomes part of total renovation costs and must remain within overall HomeStyle renovation cost constraints.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_017_005 is applied without BR_017_004, the DIY portion could be compliant while total renovation costs exceed allowable caps, resulting in overall ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle DIY Limit 10% and Inspect Items Over $5,000
business_rules.description: Under HomeStyle Renovation, the “Do It Yourself” option is permitted only for one-unit properties and is not available for manufactured homes. DIY renovations must not exceed 10% of the “as completed” value of the property. The lender must review and approve the renovations in advance and must inspect completion of all items that cost more than $5,000.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower proposes HomeStyle “Do It Yourself” renovation work for a one-unit property
business_rules.consequences: If limits/inspections not met, DIY scope must be reduced or disallowed to comply
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The “Do It Yourself” option is available for renovations made to one-unit properties by the borrower. This option is not available for manufactured homes. “Do It Yourself” renovations may not represent more than 10% of the “as completed” value of the property. The lender must review and approve the renovations in advance, and must inspect the completion of all items that cost more than $5,000.

examples: One-unit SFR as-completed value $350,000: DIY work maximum is $35,000; borrower proposes $30,000 DIY, compliant if lender approves and inspects >$5,000 items.
Manufactured home as-completed value $250,000: borrower requests DIY work; lender must deny DIY option (not available).
Borrower DIY includes a $7,500 bathroom fixture install; lender must inspect completion of that item.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle recourse repurchase risk triggers after 120-day default before completion
business_rules.description: A HomeStyle Renovation loan may be delivered to Fannie Mae as soon as it is closed even if the renovation is not complete, but only if delivered with recourse (SFC 215). If the borrower defaults before the work is completed and the default continues for at least 120 days, the lender may be required to repurchase the loan. If delivered when renovation is complete, no recourse obligation applies and SFC 279 is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle Renovation delivery timing and recourse based on completion status.
business_rules.consequences: If delivered incomplete with recourse and default lasts ≥120 days, lender may repurchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: A lender may deliver a HomeStyle Renovation loan as soon as it is closed; the renovation does not need to have been completed when the loan is delivered as long as the lender delivers that loan with recourse. If the borrower defaults under the terms of the loan before the work is completed, and that default continues for at least 120 days, the lender may be required to repurchase the loan. One of the following SFCs is required when the loan is delivered... when the renovation is not complete... SFC 215... when the renovation is complete... SFC 279

examples: Loan delivered 5 days after closing with renovation incomplete and SFC 215; borrower becomes delinquent and remains in default 130 days before completion; lender may be required to repurchase.
Loan delivered after renovation complete with SFC 279; no recourse obligation applies under this provision.
Loan delivered incomplete with recourse, but borrower cures delinquency at day 90; the ≥120-day repurchase trigger is not met under this provision.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_011 distinguishes delivery coding and recourse based on whether renovation is complete (SFC 215 vs SFC 279). BR_019_010 governs the renovation completion requirement/timeline and remedy workflow for delays. To truthfully deliver as complete/no-recourse, the lender must confirm completion has occurred under the project controls governed by BR_019_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender delivers with SFC 279 (complete/no recourse) when the work is not actually complete (or completion cannot be supported), the delivery is defective and can create repurchase/remedy exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady Income Maximum 80% of Area Median Income
business_rules.description: For a loan to be eligible as a HomeReady mortgage, the total annual qualifying income (counting income from all borrowers signing the note to the extent used to evaluate creditworthiness) must not exceed 80% of the Area Median Income (AMI) for the property’s location. For loan eligibility determinations, the lender must use AMIs that Fannie Mae uses in Desktop Underwriter (DU) or on Fannie Mae’s website and may not rely on other published AMI versions (e.g., huduser.org).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting a mortgage as a HomeReady loan.
business_rules.consequences: If ≤80% AMI, HomeReady eligible; if >80%, ineligible as HomeReady.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; resale-restriction loans have additional requirements.

source_reference.section_id: B5-6-01, Borrower Income Limits and Calculations
source_reference.source_text: For determining Fannie Mae loan eligibility, lenders must refer to the AMIs that Fannie Mae uses in Desktop Underwriter or on Fannie Mae’s website  and may not rely on other published versions (such as AMIs posted on huduser.org). To be eligible as a HomeReady mortgage, the total annual qualifying income may not exceed 80% of the AMI for the property’s location.

examples: AMI is $100,000; borrower qualifying income is $78,000 (78% AMI) → eligible for HomeReady income test.
AMI is $90,000; borrower qualifying income is $75,000 (83.33% AMI) → not eligible as HomeReady (must be re-underwritten as non-HomeReady or different program).
Lender uses HUDUser AMI table showing different AMI than DU/Fannie site → violates rule even if income is under 80% of HUDUser AMI.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady 95.01–97% LTV Requires 30-Year Fixed and DU Only
business_rules.description: If a HomeReady transaction has an LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeding 95% (95.01% to 97%), it is only eligible if it is a purchase or limited cash-out refinance, is underwritten through DU only, and is a fixed-rate loan with a term up to 30 years. High-balance loans and ARM loans are not permitted in this 95.01%–97% band. Property/occupancy must be a one-unit principal residence; manufactured housing is not permitted unless it meets MH Advantage requirements. All borrowers must occupy the property unless there is a Community Seconds subordinate lien.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady loan where LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV is 95.01%–97%.
business_rules.consequences: If all criteria met, eligible for delivery as HomeReady; otherwise ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Manufactured housing allowed only if MH Advantage; occupancy exception if Community Seconds.

source_reference.section_id: B5-6-01, Requirements for HomeReady Transactions with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV of 95.01–97%
source_reference.source_text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a HomeReady transaction, the following requirements apply. Criteria Requirements LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% ... Loan Purpose Purchase transactions or limited cash-out refinances only. Loan Type Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years. Note: High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted. Property and Occupancy One-unit principal residence. Manufactured housing is not permitted, unless the property meets the MH Advantage requirements. All borrowers must occupy the property unless there is a Community Seconds subordinate lien. Underwriting Method DU only

examples: Purchase, 97% LTV, 30-year fixed, DU Approve/Eligible, one-unit principal residence → eligible under this rule.
Limited cash-out refi, 96% CLTV, 7/1 ARM → ineligible because ARM loans are not permitted for 95.01%–97%.
Purchase, 95.5% LTV, one-unit principal residence, but non-occupant co-borrower (not Community Seconds) → ineligible because all borrowers must occupy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_001 is the core HomeReady income-eligibility rule (≤80% AMI using Fannie Mae/DU AMI sources). BR_020_002 describes additional HomeReady eligibility requirements for high ratios. A loan cannot be evaluated under HomeReady high-LTV criteria if it fails base HomeReady income eligibility.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_001 fails or is skipped, a loan might be incorrectly delivered as HomeReady (including in the 95.01%–97% band), leading to ineligibility and repurchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_002 is ratio-band driven and imposes strict eligibility constraints in the 95.01%–97% range. BR_033_003 instructs that certain solar panels receive no contributory value and that related debt is excluded from LTV/CLTV calculations (while included in DTI). This can affect whether the loan falls into the 95.01%–97% band and thus whether BR_020_002 applies.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_003 is misapplied, a loan may be incorrectly classified into/out of the high-LTV HomeReady band, leading to incorrect underwriting method requirements (DU-only) and potential ineligibility.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_005 sets additional requirements for purchase transactions in the 95.01%–97% band, including a first-time homebuyer requirement. BR_020_002 sets HomeReady-specific requirements for the same ratio band and does not mention first-time homebuyer as a condition. In an automated knowledge graph, the system must route the loan to the correct rule set based on product selection (HomeReady vs non-HomeReady) to avoid false denials or false approvals.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unresolved, the same HomeReady loan could be incorrectly denied for lacking a first-time homebuyer, or a non-HomeReady loan could be incorrectly approved by applying HomeReady criteria not applicable to it.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle 2–4 Unit Contingency Reserve 10% or 15%
business_rules.description: For HomeStyle Renovation mortgages secured by 2–4 unit properties, the lender must establish and fund a contingency reserve equal to 10% of total repair/renovation costs; the lender may increase the reserve to 15% based on scope and scale. A contingency reserve is not required for a one-unit property (but may be established at lender option).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle Renovation loans; unit_count=2–4 triggers reserve
business_rules.consequences: If met: escrow funded correctly; if violated: loan not compliant for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: One-unit property: reserve not required (lender may choose to set one)

source_reference.source_text: A contingency reserve is not required for a mortgage secured by a one-unit property, however the lender may choose to establish one. A contingency reserve equal to 10% of the total costs of the repairs and renovation work must be established and funded for a mortgage that is secured by a two- to four-unit property to cover required unforeseen repairs or deﬁciencies that are discovered during the renovation. The lender may increase the contingency reserve to 15% if it determines the higher reserve is appropriate given the scope and scale of the renovation.

examples: 2-unit HomeStyle with $50,000 renovation cost: contingency reserve must be $5,000 (10%).
4-unit HomeStyle with extensive structural work: lender sets reserve at $12,000 on $80,000 costs (15%).
1-unit HomeStyle: lender elects to set a reserve, but it is not required by the Guide.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_005 requires establishing and funding a contingency reserve equal to 10% (or 15%) of total repair/renovation costs for 2–4 unit HomeStyle loans. BR_017_004 governs the renovation costs and caps them. In practice, the validated renovation cost amount is the input to compute the reserve, making BR_017_004 a prerequisite to reserve calculation and funding.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_017_004 fails or is not finalized, the reserve may be underfunded/overfunded, potentially making the loan noncompliant for delivery and causing escrow/closing errors.

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary buydown limit is 3% down and 1% per year up for 3 years
business_rules.description: Temporary interest rate buydowns are permitted only if the buydown terms stay within Fannie Mae limits: the rate reduction must not exceed 3%, the rate increase must not exceed 1% per year, and the buydown period must not be greater than 3 years. More frequent changes are permitted only if the total annual increase does not exceed 1%.
business_rules.conditions: When a mortgage loan includes a temporary interest rate buydown plan
business_rules.consequences: If limits exceeded, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization as described
business_rules.exceptions: More frequent changes allowed if total annual increase ≤ 1%

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-04 (General Requirements; Terms of the Buydown)
source_reference.source_text: Temporary interest rate buydowns are allowed on ﬁxed-rate mortgages and certain ARM plans for principal residences or second homes provided the rate reduction does not exceed 3%, and the rate increase will not exceed 1% per year. ... An interest rate buydown plan must provide for: a buydown period not greater than 3 years, and increases of not more than 1% in the portion of the interest rate paid by the borrower in each 1-year interval. More frequent changes are permitted as long as the total annual increase does not exceed 1%.

examples: 2-1 buydown: note rate 6.00%, year 1 at 4.00% (2% reduction), year 2 at 5.00% (1% increase), year 3 at 6.00%: compliant.
3-2-1 buydown from 7.00% to 4.00% in year 1 (3% reduction) then 5.00% then 6.00%: compliant if total period ≤ 3 years.
Buydown drops rate 4% below note rate or has a 4-year buydown period: noncompliant/ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase for eligibility breach

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Green MBS requires 10% efficiency and 5-year certification
business_rules.description: To qualify as a Green MBS loan, the mortgage must be secured by a property that received an approved green building certification within the last five years, and the certification must require the home to be at least 10% more efficient than a home built to the applicable state's energy code.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender intends to deliver the loan as Green MBS
business_rules.consequences: If met, loan may be treated as Green MBS; if not, cannot be Green MBS
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae's Green MBS consists of loans secured by properties that have received approved green building certiﬁcations within the last ﬁve years. An eligible green building certiﬁcation requires the home to be at least 10% more eﬃcient than a home built to an individual state's energy code.

examples: Property certified in 2023 and documentation shows ≥10% efficiency vs state code; loan may be delivered as Green MBS.
Property certified in 2018 (more than five years ago); loan cannot be delivered as Green MBS.
Property has an efficiency label but it does not require ≥10% improvement over state code; loan cannot be delivered as Green MBS.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for Green MBS designation

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative Qualification Path minimum credit score is 620
business_rules.description: Loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path for the high LTV refinance option must meet a minimum credit score of 620.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to high LTV refinance loans using the Alternative Qualification Path
business_rules.consequences: If score ≥620, requirement is satisfied; if <620, loan is not eligible under the path
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: In addition to all other requirements associated with the high LTV reﬁnance option loans, loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path must also meet the requirements described in the following table. Minimum credit score of 620. Maximum DTI ratio of 45%.

examples: Borrower representative credit score is 640; passes the Alternative Qualification Path credit score requirement.
Borrower representative credit score is 619; fails the Alternative Qualification Path and cannot be originated under it.
Two borrowers with scores 650 and 625; minimum score requirement is met because each required score is ≥620.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_029_005 applies only to loans 'originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path.' BR_029_004 is the trigger rule establishing when the path is required. Therefore, BR_029_004 must be evaluated first to know whether BR_029_005 applies.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_029_004 is not evaluated, the lender may fail to apply the 620 minimum score requirement and deliver an ineligible loan under the Alternative Qualification Path.

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative Qualification Path maximum debt-to-income ratio is 45%
business_rules.description: Loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path for the high LTV refinance option must meet a maximum DTI ratio of 45%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to high LTV refinance loans using the Alternative Qualification Path
business_rules.consequences: If DTI ≤45%, requirement is satisfied; if >45%, loan is not eligible under the path
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path must also meet the requirements described in the following table. Minimum credit score of 620. Maximum DTI ratio of 45%. Veriﬁed assets needed to close, when applicable.

examples: DTI calculated at 44.9%; passes the Alternative Qualification Path DTI cap.
DTI calculated at 45.1%; fails the Alternative Qualification Path DTI cap and is not eligible under it.
DTI at 45% exactly; passes because requirement is a maximum of 45%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_029_006 applies only when a loan is 'originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path.' BR_029_004 determines when that path must be used based on triggers.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_029_004 is missed, the lender may use a different DTI limit and deliver a loan that violates the Alternative Path 45% cap, creating ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: 95.01–97% purchase requires FRM 30 years and DU
business_rules.description: If a purchase transaction has LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV exceeding 95% (95.01% to 97%), the loan must meet additional eligibility requirements: (a) ratio must be 95.01–97%; (b) loan must be a fixed-rate loan with terms up to 30 years; high-balance and ARM loans are not permitted; (c) property must be a one-unit principal residence (manufactured housing not permitted unless it meets MH Advantage requirements); (d) at least one borrower must be a first-time homebuyer (Form 1003 Declarations—ownership interest in last three years answered “No” by at least one borrower); and (e) underwriting method must be DU only. Note: CLTV can be up to 105% if subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Note: does not apply to HomeReady.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase loans where LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is 95.01%–97% inclusive
business_rules.consequences: If any requirement fails, the loan is ineligible for this high-LTV purchase set
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to HomeReady loans; Community Seconds allows CLTV to 105%

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-01, Requirements for Purchase Transactions with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratios of 95.01–97%
source_reference.source_text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a purchase transaction, the following requirements apply. Criteria Requirements LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% Note: The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Loan Type Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years. Note: High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted. Property and Occupancy One-unit principal residence. Manufactured housing is not permitted, unless the property meets the MH Advantage requirements. Borrower Eligibility At least one borrower must be a first-time homebuyer, as indicated on the Form 1003 in the Declarations section, when at least one borrower responds “No” to the question about having an ownership interest in a property in the last three years. Underwriting Method DU only

examples: Purchase loan at 97% LTV, fixed-rate 30-year, one-unit principal residence, DU Approve/Eligible, with one first-time homebuyer—eligible under this section.
Purchase loan at 96% LTV but ARM product—prohibited; must change product or reduce LTV below threshold.
Purchase loan at 97% LTV with Community Seconds subordinate lien and CLTV 104%—allowed due to Community Seconds exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Highly specific ratio band and product/property/underwriting requirements; includes explicit exceptions/notes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_005 governs eligibility requirements for purchase loans in the 95.01%–97% band. BR_033_003 mandates no contributory value for certain financed solar panels and exclusion from LTV/CLTV calculations. These instructions can materially affect ratio calculations that determine BR_030_005 applicability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_003 is missed, a loan may be incorrectly routed into or out of BR_030_005’s high-LTV purchase eligibility logic, causing incorrect approvals/denials and delivery defects.

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster refi allowed only in FEMA IA counties and PR
business_rules.description: Disaster-related limited cash-out refinance flexibilities may be applied only to properties located in areas designated by FEMA as eligible for Individual Assistance (FEMA Disaster Areas) and are applicable only to loans secured by the borrower’s principal residence (not second homes or investment properties). A borrower may obtain a limited cash-out refinance to consolidate non-purchase money subordinate financing used for disaster-related repairs if the subordinate financing (including draws on an existing HELOC) post-dates the disaster. The borrower may pay off the entire HELOC through the limited cash-out refinance, provided a portion of the amount was used for disaster-related expenses to repair damage to the principal residence.
business_rules.conditions: Limited cash-out refi using disaster flexibilities under B5-4.2-02
business_rules.consequences: If location/occupancy/timing not met, loan must follow standard refi rules
business_rules.exceptions: Entire HELOC payoff allowed if some proceeds used for disaster repairs

source_reference.section_id: B5-4.2-02, Location of Property; Occupancy Status; Transaction Types
source_reference.source_text: These flexibilities may be applied to loans on properties located in any counties, cities, or parishes that are designated by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) as eligible for Individual Assistance as a result of a natural disaster (these areas are referred to as “FEMA Disaster Areas”). These guidelines are applicable only to loans secured by the borrower’s principal residence, and may not be used in connection with second homes or investment properties. A borrower may obtain: a limited cash-out refinance to consolidate non-purchase money subordinate financing used for repair of disaster-related property damage to their principal residence. To be eligible, the subordinate financing, including any draws on an existing HELOC, must post-date the disaster. However, the borrower may pay off the entire HELOC through the limited cash-out refinance, provided that a portion of the amount was used for disaster-related expenses to repair property damage to the principal residence.

examples: Property is in a FEMA Individual Assistance county; borrower’s principal residence; HELOC draw for repairs occurred after the disaster—eligible to apply flexibilities.
Borrower seeks to use flexibilities on a second home in a FEMA IA area—not eligible; must underwrite under standard limited cash-out rules.
Borrower pays off entire HELOC in the refi; documentation shows part of HELOC was for disaster repairs—allowed by exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit FEMA IA geographic constraint, principal-residence-only requirement, and post-date timing test for subordinate financing/HELOC draws.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for flex; potential repurchase demand if delivered incorrectly

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster repair reimbursement cap is 10% or $15,000
business_rules.description: Under disaster-related limited cash-out refinance flexibilities, cash-out for reimbursement of documented out-of-pocket expenses for completed disaster-related repairs is limited to an amount not to exceed the lesser of 10% of the balance of the new refinance loan or $15,000. The lender must document that subordinate financing/HELOC portion or the entire requested cash-out amount represents funds used for completed disaster-related property repairs (e.g., receipts, work orders, canceled checks). The borrower may not receive reimbursement for sweat equity. All documentation must post-date the disaster and be directly related to completed repairs of disaster-caused damage.
business_rules.conditions: Disaster-related limited cash-out refi requesting repair reimbursement cash-out
business_rules.consequences: If cap exceeded or documentation fails, reduce cash-out or treat as ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B5-4.2-02, Transaction Types; Documentation of Eligible Disaster-Related Expenses and Financing
source_reference.source_text: cash-out for reimbursement of documented out-of-pocket expenses for the completed repair of disaster-related property damage to their principal residence in an amount not to exceed the lesser of 10% of the balance of the new refinance loan or $15,000. The lender must document that the subordinate financing (or a portion of the HELOC) or the entire requested cash-out amount represents funds used for completed disaster-related property repairs. Generally, documentation includes copies of receipts, work orders, canceled checks, etc., related to the cost of materials and labor. The borrower may not receive any reimbursement for amounts representing their sweat equity in connection with the repairs. Note: All documentation must post-date the disaster and be directly related to completed repairs of damage to the property resulting from the disaster.

examples: New refi loan balance is $200,000; max reimbursement is lesser of 10% ($20,000) or $15,000 → $15,000 cap applies.
New refi loan balance is $120,000; 10% is $12,000 → max reimbursement is $12,000 with post-disaster receipts/work orders.
Borrower claims $8,000 value of self-performed labor (sweat equity) with no paid invoices; reimbursement is prohibited and must be excluded.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit numeric cap (10% or $15,000) plus explicit documentation/timing and sweat-equity prohibition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_009 defines when disaster-related limited cash-out refinance flexibilities may be applied (FEMA IA area; principal residence only; other conditions). BR_030_010 then limits and documents the reimbursed cash-out amount for completed disaster repairs. Therefore, BR_030_009 is the prerequisite eligibility gate for applying BR_030_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_030_009 is not met but BR_030_010 is used, the lender may incorrectly treat a refinance as eligible under disaster flexibilities, misapplying caps and documentation rules and risking delivery defects.

business_rules.rule_name: PACE Loan for Solar Panels Must Be Paid Off at Closing
business_rules.description: If the subject property has solar panels or other energy efficient items financed with a PACE loan, the mortgage loan is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae unless the PACE loan is paid in full prior to or at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property has solar/energy items financed with a PACE loan
business_rules.consequences: If unpaid, loan is ineligible for delivery; if paid off, standard delivery may proceed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; must be paid in full prior to or at closing

source_reference.source_text: Properties with solar panels and other energy eﬃcient items ﬁnanced with a PACE loan are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if the PACE loan is not paid in full prior to or at closing. For additional information, see B5-3.4-01, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans .

examples: Purchase loan: borrower has a $18,000 PACE balance for solar panels; payoff is not completed at closing → loan is not eligible for delivery.
Purchase loan: PACE payoff is funded and recorded at closing (PACE lien released) → loan may be eligible, subject to standard requirements.
Rate-term refinance: borrower still has an active PACE assessment and will not pay it off → loan is ineligible for delivery.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Financed borrower MI ineligible for 2-4 units and cash-out
business_rules.description: Mortgage loans that include financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if they are secured by a two- to four-unit property, secured by an investment property, or are a cash-out refinance loan.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower-paid MI premium is included in the loan amount (financed MI)
business_rules.consequences: If 2–4 units, investment, or cash-out refinance, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; note clarifies lender-paid MI premiums cannot be financed

source_reference.source_text: The following mortgage loans are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if they include ﬁnanced borrower- purchased mortgage insurance: mortgage loans secured by two- to-four-unit properties, mortgage loans secured by investment properties, and cash-out reﬁnance loans. Note : Lender-paid mortgage insurance premiums cannot be ﬁnanced into the loan amount and are therefore not considered ﬁnanced mortgage insurance transactions.

examples: A 3-unit primary residence purchase includes a financed single-premium MI amount in the loan; it is ineligible for delivery.
A 1-unit investment property limited cash-out refinance includes financed MI; it is ineligible due to investment occupancy.
A 1-unit primary residence cash-out refinance includes financed MI; it is ineligible due to cash-out refinance purpose.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_008_010 drives the decision that MI must be in place (unless other credit enhancement). Once MI is in scope, BR_038_008 must be checked if the MI premium is financed into the loan amount, because that MI structure is prohibited for delivery in specified scenarios (2–4 units, investment property, cash-out refi).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_008 is not evaluated after MI need is established, the lender may deliver an ineligible loan even though MI exists and coverage/form requirements are satisfied.

business_rules.rule_name: MI coverage 12% to 35% for >20-year fixed and ARMs
business_rules.description: For first-lien mortgages on all property types other than standard manufactured homes, fixed-rate loans with terms > 20 years and all ARMs must meet MI coverage requirements by LTV range. Minimum coverage (allowed only with an MI LLPA) is 6% for 80.01–85.00% LTV, 12% for 85.01–90.00% LTV, 16% for 90.01–95.00% LTV, and 18% for 95.01–97.00% LTV. Standard coverage option levels are 12%, 25%, 30%, and 35% respectively. If lender chooses less than standard but no lower than minimum, an MI LLPA is assessed based on LTV and representative credit score (in addition to other LLPAs).
business_rules.conditions: First-lien; fixed-rate term >20 years or any ARM; LTV 80.01–97.00%
business_rules.consequences: If below minimum: ineligible for delivery; if minimum (vs standard): MI LLPA assessed
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (must be ≥ minimum coverage for the LTV range)

source_reference.source_text: For certain transactions, Fannie Mae oﬀers two mortgage insurance coverage level options: standard coverage for the transaction type (noted with ^) and minimum coverage (noted with *) with corresponding LLPAs. Lenders who choose less than standard coverage (but no lower than minimum coverage) will be assessed an LLPA based on the LTV ratio and representative credit score for the mortgage loan... Fixed-rate, term > 20 years All ARMs 6%* + MI LLPA 12%* + MI LLPA 16%* + MI LLPA 18%* + MI LLPA 12%^ 25%^ 30%^ 35%^

examples: Fixed-rate 30-year, 92% LTV: lender may use 16% MI with MI LLPA, or standard 30% MI without the minimum-coverage LLPA.
5/6 ARM, 86% LTV: minimum MI is 12% (with MI LLPA); standard MI is 25%.
30-year fixed, 96.5% LTV: minimum MI is 18% (with MI LLPA); standard MI is 35%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or pricing adjustment (MI LLPA)

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_008_010 establishes the need for primary MI when LTV > 80% unless another charter-compliant enhancement exists. Once MI is required/selected for eligible property types (non-standard manufactured homes), BR_039_001 governs the required coverage percentage by LTV range (and whether an MI LLPA applies).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_008_010 triggers MI but BR_039_001 is not applied, the lender may obtain insufficient MI coverage (delivery ineligibility) or fail to assess required MI LLPA when using minimum (non-standard) coverage.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_001 is driven by LTV ranges (80.01–97.00%). For CLT collateral, BR_014_004 produces the leasehold value conclusion that functions as the appraised value basis; this value is used (directly or via the lesser-of rule outside this excerpt) to compute LTV and therefore determine the applicable MI coverage tier.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_004 fails (incorrect leasehold value), LTV can be miscalculated, resulting in selecting the wrong MI coverage percentage/LLPA or incorrectly treating the loan as eligible/ineligible.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_001 relies on LTV ranges to set MI coverage. BR_023_009 affects the accuracy of reported square footage which can materially influence value conclusion and therefore LTV (especially when the appraisal value is the limiting factor).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_009 fails, appraisal revision/replacement may change value and LTV tier, potentially changing MI coverage requirements or eligibility; delivery can be delayed pending corrected appraisal.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_039_001 requires choosing MI coverage based on specific LTV bands. LTV is sensitive to the appraised value (typically the lesser of sales price and appraised value for many purposes). BR_SUBMITS_TO_UCDP_VALIDATION_013_002 ensures the appraised value reported at sale matches the UCDP-submitted appraisal, preventing last-minute value discrepancies that would alter LTV band and MI coverage requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraised value at sale does not match UCDP, LTV may be mis-banded (e.g., 89.9% vs 90.1%), causing incorrect MI coverage selection, MI LLPA errors, and delivery defects or ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Standard manufactured home MI not allowed above 95.00% LTV
business_rules.description: For first-lien loans secured by standard manufactured homes (not MH Advantage), MI coverage is not available/eligible for the 95.01–97.00% LTV range (shown as 'Not applicable'). For 80.01–85.00%, 85.01–90.00%, and 90.01–95.00% LTV ranges, minimum MI coverage (with MI LLPA) is 6%, 12%, and 16%, and standard coverage is 12%, 25%, and 30% respectively.
business_rules.conditions: First-lien; property is standard manufactured home (not MH Advantage); LTV >80%
business_rules.consequences: If LTV 95.01–97.00%: loan fails MI coverage table and is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (table states 95.01–97.00% is not applicable)

source_reference.source_text: Standard manufactured homes (not MH Advantage) Fixed-rate, ARM, any term 6%* + MI LLPA 12%* + MI LLPA 16%* + MI LLPA Not applicable 12%^ 25%^ 30%^

examples: Standard manufactured home, 94% LTV: minimum MI is 16% (with MI LLPA) or standard 30%.
Standard manufactured home, 96% LTV: 95.01–97.00% is 'Not applicable' so loan cannot meet MI table requirements for delivery.
Standard manufactured home, 83% LTV: minimum MI is 6% (with MI LLPA) or standard 12%.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_001 governs MI coverage by LTV for first-lien loans on property types other than standard manufactured homes. BR_039_002 provides the manufactured-home-specific MI coverage rules and disallows coverage for 95.01–97.00% LTV. When the property is a standard manufactured home, BR_039_002 supersedes the general table in BR_039_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this override is not applied, a lender may incorrectly treat a standard manufactured home at 95–97% LTV as MI-eligible under the general table, resulting in delivery ineligibility and purchase/securitization rejection.

dependencies.rationale: BR_008_010 triggers the need for MI when LTV > 80%. For standard manufactured homes (not MH Advantage), BR_039_002 provides the applicable MI coverage availability/levels and restricts the 95.01–97.00% LTV band entirely. Therefore, once MI need is identified under BR_008_010, property-type routing to BR_039_002 is required for manufactured homes.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_002 is skipped, a lender could incorrectly assume MI is available at 95.01–97.00% LTV on a standard manufactured home and deliver an ineligible loan.

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_009 explicitly includes manufactured homes in ANSI measurement requirements. Because BR_039_002 determines MI eligibility/coverage based on LTV tiers for manufactured homes, appraisal measurement compliance can affect the underlying value and thus LTV tier classification.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Measurement defects can trigger appraisal revision and LTV changes that move the loan into an ineligible LTV band (e.g., 95.01–97.00%) or require different MI coverage.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_039_002 requires applying a manufactured-home-specific MI coverage/availability table (including 'Not applicable' for 95.01–97.00% LTV). BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_DOCUMENTATION_024_010 requires MH-specific reporting (1004C and MH identifiers such as HUD certification numbers). This documentation is a practical prerequisite for reliably classifying the property as a standard manufactured home (not MH Advantage) and applying the correct MI rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If MH appraisal documentation is incomplete/incorrect, the loan may be misclassified and the wrong MI coverage/eligibility table applied, creating delivery ineligibility risk (e.g., erroneously allowing 95–97% LTV MI on standard MH).

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_039_002 makes certain LTV bands ineligible for MI (95.01–97.00% not applicable) and sets minimum/standard coverage in other bands. BR_SUBMITS_TO_UCDP_VALIDATION_013_002 ensures the appraised value at sale matches the UCDP appraisal, preventing shifts into/out of the ineligible LTV band due to mismatched values.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A value mismatch can move a standard manufactured home loan into the ineligible 95.01–97.00% band or change the minimum coverage requirement, creating delivery ineligibility or pricing/MI LLPA errors.

business_rules.rule_name: Credit card early fees capped at 2% of loan amount
business_rules.description: Credit card financing may be allowed for payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing, but only up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount. To permit this, the lender must either (1) confirm the borrower has sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves) to cover these charges in addition to other closing costs and down payment, or (2) recalculate the credit card payment to account for the new charges and include the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower pays common/customary pre-closing fees by credit card
business_rules.consequences: If >2% or requirements unmet, fees must be re-sourced or loan re-underwritten/denied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Lenders may allow credit card ﬁnancing for the payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount if the lender: conﬁrms that the borrower has suﬃcient liquid funds (ﬁnancial reserves) to cover these charges (in addition to funds needed for other closing costs and the down payment that they will be paying); or recalculates the credit card payment, per B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations , to account for the new charges and includes the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.

examples: Loan amount $400,000: max eligible credit-card financed early fees = $8,000; borrower charged $7,500 in origination/lock fees—allowed if reserves cover $7,500 plus other funds-to-close.
Loan amount $250,000: borrower charged $6,000 early fees (2.4%); lender must treat as not permitted and require different payment method or reduce fees.
Borrower charges $3,000 early fees but has limited reserves; lender may still allow if lender recalculates the credit card minimum payment to reflect the new balance and includes it in DTI.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: When borrowers use a credit card for pre-closing fees, underwriting must decide whether to (a) include the increased payment in DTI or (b) exclude it if the balance will be paid off. BR_032_002 provides the payoff-based exclusion logic that conditions how BR_040_007 is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_002 is ignored, the lender may unnecessarily increase DTI using recalculated card payments (causing avoidable declines), or improperly exclude payments without proof of payoff (understating DTI).

dependencies.rationale: BR_040_007’s option (2) explicitly requires updating the credit card payment included in qualifying ratios. BR_035_009 provides the required calculation approach when the credit report lacks a stated minimum payment and no supplemental documentation supports a lower amount.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_035_009 is not applied in those cases, DTI can be understated, potentially generating incorrect approvals and post-close QC findings.

dependencies.rationale: A lender cannot ‘confirm sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves)’ without first excluding ineligible reserve sources (IPCs, lender contributions) as mandated by BR_023_007. Otherwise, the sufficiency determination is unreliable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_007 is not applied, the lender may allow credit card financing of fees based on inflated/invalid reserves, leading to insufficient verified funds and underwriting defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_040_007 allows credit card financing of certain fees only if the lender either confirms sufficient liquid funds (reserves) or recalculates credit card payment into qualifying ratios. When the lender uses the 'sufficient liquid funds' option, it generally needs documented depository assets, which—when DU requires depository asset verification—must meet BR_DESKTOP_UNDERWRITER_DOCUMENTATION_025_005 requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 025_005 documentation is missing/deficient, the lender may be unable to substantiate sufficient liquid funds, forcing the lender to use the alternate branch (recalculate payment) or re-source fees. This can trigger re-underwriting and delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_040_007’s two compliance paths depend on credible reserve verification (assets) or recalculated payments included in qualifying ratios (income/debts). BR_036_007 imposes a recency requirement for those same underwriting documents when the property is in a FEMA-declared disaster area eligible for Individual Assistance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_007 fails, the lender must re-verify assets/income/credit; until updated, BR_040_007 cannot be satisfied reliably, delaying underwriting and potentially requiring re-underwrite/denial.

business_rules.rule_name: De minimis correction limited to $500 and barred if it reduces minimum contribution
business_rules.description: Under the remedies framework, a de minimis correction is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or a higher agreed amount) that can resolve an identified significant defect when remitted/refunded/otherwise provided, but it cannot be used if the correction would cause a specific required minimum borrower contribution to not be met or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When lender proposes a correction to resolve an identified significant defect
business_rules.consequences: If allowed, defect may be treated as corrected; if barred, other remedy applies
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed $500 only if lender and Fannie Mae agree; barred cases apply

source_reference.section_id: D2-1-04 (08/30/2016), Definitions of Remedy and Correction
source_reference.source_text: One example of a correction is a “de minimis correction,” which is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or such higher amount as the lender and Fannie Mae may agree) that, when remitted, refunded, or otherwise provided, corrects or otherwise resolves an identiﬁed signiﬁcant defect. However, a “de minimis correction” cannot be made when the correction would result in a speciﬁc required minimum borrower contribution not being met, or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.

examples: Fee cure of $250 is remitted to resolve a minor disclosed-fee defect; qualifies as de minimis correction.
A $400 correction would reduce borrower’s required minimum contribution below the required level; de minimis correction is not permitted.
Lender and Fannie Mae agree in writing to a higher de minimis cap (e.g., $750) for a specific defect type; allowed only per agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Repurchase demand or repurchase alternative if defect remains significant

examples: Fannie Mae post-purchase QC review identifying an appraisal documentation defect
Lender post-closing QC audit sampling loans and documenting findings
QC review resulting in remediation and updated mortgage file records

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_007 can trigger defect identification (e.g., data inconsistencies, missed derogatory items). Once a significant defect exists, BR_001_009 constrains whether a de minimis correction is allowed. Thus, defect identification/triage precedes remedy selection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_007 is not executed, defects may not be detected early and BR_001_009 may never be considered until post-close QC—raising costs and timelines (e.g., repurchase demands vs minor corrections).

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_009 governs when a de minimis correction may be used to resolve a significant defect. Identifying and substantiating a defect (and whether borrower contribution minimums/Charter requirements are impacted) typically requires the complete file governed by BR_011_009. Thus, robust documentation is a practical prerequisite to applying BR_001_009 correctly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_011_009 fails (incomplete file), the lender may be unable to prove a defect is minor/eligible for de minimis correction, forcing harsher remedies or causing delays while reconstructing documentation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_004 defines a minimum 3% borrower own-funds contribution for certain HomeReady purchase scenarios. BR_001_009 bars using a de minimis correction if it would cause a required minimum borrower contribution to not be met. Thus, QC remedy selection under BR_001_009 depends on identifying whether BR_013_004 applies and whether the correction would reduce the borrower’s qualifying contribution below 3%.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_013_004 is not considered during QC remedy selection, the lender might improperly apply a $500 de minimis correction that effectively reduces the borrower’s own-funds contribution below the minimum, leaving the significant defect unremedied and increasing repurchase/remedy exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_009 states a de minimis correction cannot be used "to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements." BR_008_010 describes the MI/credit enhancement requirement as "charter-compliant" and requires MI absent another charter-compliant enhancement for LTV > 80%. In a QC/remedies workflow, if the significant defect is missing required MI/credit enhancement under BR_008_010, the defect cannot be cured via de minimis correction under BR_001_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this dependency is missed, QC may incorrectly close a charter-level defect via a small refund/correction, leading to unresolved charter noncompliance and heightened remedy exposure (including repurchase).

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_009 noncompliance leads to appraisal defects and potential value/report corrections. When QC identifies that defect as significant, BR_001_009 governs whether a small monetary correction can resolve it or whether other remedies are required.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_009 bars de minimis correction, the lender must pursue alternative remediation (e.g., revised appraisal, repurchase, indemnification) instead of a simple refund/credit approach.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 failures (endorsement chain break, MIN not registered) produce delivery/documentation defects. When those are classified as significant defects, BR_001_009 controls de minimis correction availability and prohibits using de minimis to fix certain minimum contribution or Charter-related issues.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_009 disallows de minimis correction, the lender must cure the document defect substantively (correct endorsements/MERS registration) or face stronger remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_007 sets enforceability-critical endorsement requirements. When QC identifies a breach, BR_001_009 determines whether a de minimis correction is an allowable remedy or whether the lender must pursue a different resolution path.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Incorrectly applying de minimis corrections to endorsement defects can leave the defect unresolved and expose the lender to stronger contractual remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory delivery commitment shortfall limited to $10,000 or 2.5%
business_rules.description: A mandatory delivery commitment generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount by the commitment expiration date. The minimum required delivery amount cannot be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender enters a mandatory delivery commitment for whole loans.
business_rules.consequences: If delivery falls below minimum, lender fails commitment and may need pair-off actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this glossary definition.

source_reference.source_text: mandatory delivery commitment A whole loan commitment that generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount (which is an amount that will not be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount) by the expiration date of the commitment.

examples: Original commitment $2,000,000: minimum required delivery is $1,950,000 (2.5% shortfall) not $1,900,000.
Original commitment $300,000: 2.5% is $7,500, so the tighter $10,000 cap does not apply; minimum is $292,500.
Original commitment $350,000: 2.5% is $8,750; minimum is $341,250 (not $340,000).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pair-off fee or other commitment remedy

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

dependencies.rationale: BR_005_010 prohibits delivery of OFAC-listed borrowers and requires cessation/blocking upon matches. BR_002_007 requires meeting minimum delivery amount by expiration. OFAC events override planned deliveries and can force substitution/pair-off actions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If OFAC screening is not integrated, the lender may plan to satisfy a commitment with loans that later become blocked, leading to last-minute substitutions, operational delays, and increased likelihood of failing minimum required delivery amounts.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_002 defines property utilities eligibility for purchase/securitization. Loans failing collateral eligibility cannot be delivered and therefore cannot be relied upon to satisfy BR_002_007 minimum delivery requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If utilities rights (well/septic access) are not confirmed, the loan may be ineligible and must be replaced in the commitment delivery plan.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_003 is a collateral legality/marketability eligibility requirement. BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages. Therefore, zoning/legal use must be cleared before loans are counted toward mandatory delivery fulfillment forecasts.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_003 fails after commitment planning, a planned delivery cannot be executed, risking pair-off or substitution under time pressure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_004 creates a delivery bar. BR_002_007 measures fulfillment via delivery of eligible mortgages. Therefore, the land-use reconstruction/maintenance screen must be run before using the loan in commitment fulfillment calculations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_004 is discovered late (e.g., during title/legal review), the loan is removed from deliverable inventory, potentially creating a commitment shortfall.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_008 defines acceptability thresholds for new/newly converted condo projects under Full Review. A loan secured by a non-acceptable project is ineligible for delivery; thus it cannot be counted toward BR_002_007’s eligible delivery minimum.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If presale percentage falls below 50% or phase/building rules are violated, the loan must be excluded from delivery allocation and replaced to avoid a commitment shortfall.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_009 defines when a property is acceptable from a waste-site contamination standpoint, and when additional evaluation/Phase II is required. Loans with unresolved or failing environmental conditions are not reliably deliverable; thus this screen should occur before using the loan to meet BR_002_007 minimums.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a Phase II is required and not completed by the commitment expiration, the loan may miss delivery timing, creating a commitment shortfall and pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 renders loans with payment abatements ineligible for sale. Because BR_002_007 measures required delivery amounts based on eligible loans delivered by expiration, BR_009_008 must be applied before commitment allocation and delivery forecasting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If abatement-ineligible loans remain in the commitment set, the lender may fail minimum delivery and incur pair-off or substitution costs.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COOP_UNIT_PROHIBITION_019_006 makes certain condo/co-op project loans ineligible for purchase. BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages. Thus, project status eligibility is a prerequisite determinant for whether a loan can be delivered under a commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If project status is Unavailable, the associated loans cannot be delivered, potentially creating delivery shortfalls and financial consequences under the commitment framework.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COOP_UNIT_CONSTRAINT_019_007 defines an ineligible project characteristic (commercial space >35%). BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivering eligible mortgages. Therefore, passing 019_007 is a prerequisite for condo/co-op loans to be deliverable/countable under the commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 35% threshold is exceeded, loans in that project must be excluded from delivery, potentially impacting commitment minimums and requiring substitution/pair-off handling.

dependencies.rationale: Mandatory delivery commitments rely on acceptance of delivered loans. Allonge defects commonly produce delivery suspense/cure requirements; unresolved defects can prevent acceptance by custodian/Fannie, affecting BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Custodial rejection/suspense delays delivery and may cause commitment expiration shortfalls and pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 governs core custodial deliverables (blank endorsement chain, MIN registered prior to delivery for MERS). If not met, delivery is commonly suspended pending cure, affecting BR_002_007 delivery minimums.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Delivery suspense, cure cycles, or ultimate ineligibility reduces delivered UPB and increases pair-off risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_010 sets transaction eligibility limits (purchase/limited cash-out refi) and POA documentation validity requirements. A POA-signed note/security instrument failing these requirements typically cannot be delivered, directly affecting BR_002_007 outcomes.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: POA defects require cure or render the loan undeliverable; this can cause commitment delivery shortfalls and pair-off.

dependencies.rationale: BR_036_007 imposes documentation dating constraints in FEMA disaster areas. If the loan cannot be cleared for delivery due to outdated verifications, it cannot reliably be delivered within the commitment window governed by BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Outdated docs trigger re-verification and re-underwrite, causing delays that can miss commitment expiration.

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_009 governs acceptable RIN execution standards. If not satisfied, the loan file may be documentation-defective and may not be accepted for delivery in time to satisfy BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Defective RIN documentation can trigger curative actions (re-notarization where possible, additional evidence gathering) causing delivery delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_007 requires long-term retention and the ability to provide audit trail files. In practice, inability to evidence execution/identity can lead to loan file defects and delivery/audit exceptions that can delay or reduce deliverable loans under BR_002_007 timelines.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If retention/audit trail cannot be provided, the lender may face delivery suspense, post-delivery remedies, or exclusion from delivery pools, impacting meeting the commitment minimum.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_007 measures fulfillment as delivery of eligible mortgages by an expiration date. BR_002_006 states HOEPA loans are "not eligible for delivery" and must be blocked/removed. Therefore, HOEPA screening must occur before a loan is allocated to satisfy a mandatory commitment or before counting it toward minimum required delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If HOEPA status is not evaluated first, the lender may allocate prohibited loans to a commitment, later remove them at delivery, and fall below minimum delivery, triggering pair-off actions and potential financial penalties.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_001 makes loans older than six months ineligible for purchase on a flow basis. BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages by a deadline. Therefore, commitment allocation and delivery forecasting depends on BR_002_001 being satisfied for any loan expected to be delivered under the commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_001 fails, the loan cannot be delivered as planned; commitment delivery may fall short, triggering pair-off actions or requiring urgent loan substitution.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_005 states loans outside term bounds are "not eligible for purchase/securitization." BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages by expiration. Therefore, term eligibility must be checked before commitment allocation and before counting loans toward minimum required delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_005 fails for loans in a commitment bucket, the lender must substitute loans or risk failing minimum delivery and incurring pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivery of 'eligible mortgages' by the expiration date. BR_SUBMITS_TO_UCDP_PROCESS_031_005 is a pre-sale requirement ('Successful' prior to sale). A loan that cannot be sold due to UCDP failure cannot be delivered to meet the commitment minimum, creating pair-off/shortfall risk.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If UCDP is not Successful, loans are held in suspense and may miss the commitment expiration date, causing the lender to fail minimum delivery requirements and incur pair-off costs or other contractual remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief requires acquisition on or after July 1, 2014
business_rules.description: To be eligible for representation and warranty enforcement relief, a loan must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either (1) a whole loan purchased on or after July 1, 2014, or (2) a mortgage loan delivered into MBS with a pool issue date on or after July 1, 2014.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether a loan can obtain enforcement relief framework
business_rules.consequences: If date test met, loan may qualify for relief subject to other criteria
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Loans must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either: whole loans purchased on or after July 1, 2014; or mortgage loans delivered into MBS with pool issue dates on or after July 1, 2014.

examples: Whole loan acquired June 15, 2014: fails acquisition-date eligibility and cannot obtain enforcement relief under this framework.
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date July 1, 2014: meets acquisition-date criterion (other criteria still apply).
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date August 2020: meets acquisition-date criterion and can proceed to payment history/QC review tests.
Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief after 36 payments with max two 30-day lates and no 60+
business_rules.description: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV refinance loans, when enforcement relief is based on acceptable payment history, relief occurs after the borrower pays the first 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent on the 36th monthly payment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to non–high LTV refinance loans seeking relief based on payment history
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, lender is relieved from remedies for covered breaches; else not
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans follow separate 12/36-month payment history test

source_reference.source_text: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV reﬁnance loans: To be eligible for relief under the framework, if the relief is based on the borrower’s acceptable payment history, the relief will occur upon payment by the borrower of the ﬁrst 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided that the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent with respect to the 36th monthly payment.

examples: Borrower has one 30-day late in month 10 and one 30-day late in month 22, no 60+ delinquencies, and month 36 is current: meets payment history thresholds for relief.
Borrower has three 30-day delinquencies over 36 months: fails relief threshold (more than two 30-day delinquencies).
Borrower is 30 days delinquent on the 36th payment: fails relief requirement even if prior months were acceptable.
Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_008 defines the eligibility window (whole loan purchased on/after 7/1/2014 or MBS pool issue date on/after 7/1/2014). BR_014_009 then describes when relief occurs after 36 payments for eligible loans (non–high LTV refi). The eligibility gate must be checked first to avoid incorrect relief determinations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_008 fails and BR_014_009 is applied anyway, the lender may incorrectly assume representation & warranty relief has attached, causing improper remedy responses, mispriced risk, and disputes in defect resolution.

business_rules.rule_name: SRP refund 100% if paid off in 120 days and $1,500 EPD fee
business_rules.description: Under the Servicing Marketplace Mortgage Loan Servicing Purchase and Sale Agreement: (a) If a mortgage loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller must refund 100% of the price paid by Servicer for the servicing rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae. (b) If the first, second, or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes 60 or more days delinquent after the Purchase Date (an EPD Mortgage Loan), Seller must reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid for that EPD Mortgage Loan and pay an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan; Seller must wire the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from Servicer’s request date. Failure to pay/refund as required constitutes a breach of the servicing rights purchase/sale agreement.
business_rules.conditions: When servicing rights were purchased and payoff/EPD conditions occur after Purchase Date
business_rules.consequences: Seller must refund SRP and/or pay EPD fee within specified timeframes; nonpayment breaches agreement
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 7.4 Refund of the SRP and Repurchase of Mortgage Loan . (a) Mortgage Loan Paid in Full . If the Mortgage Loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller shall refund one hundred percent (100%) of the price paid by Servicer for such Servicing Rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae... (iii) ...the ﬁrst, second or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes sixty (60) or more days delinquent... Seller shall reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid... along with an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan. Seller shall wire to Servicer the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from the date of Servicer’s request.

(a) Mortgage Loan Paid in Full . If the Mortgage Loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller shall refund one hundred percent (100%) of the price paid by Servicer for such Servicing Rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae.

(iii) In the event that for any Mortgage Loan for which Servicer has purchased the Servicing Rights, the ﬁrst, second or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes sixty (60) or more days delinquent (each such Mortgage Loan, an “ EPD Mortgage Loan ”) after the applicable Purchase Date, Seller shall reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid for said EPD Mortgage Loan, along with an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan. Seller shall wire to Servicer the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from the date of Servicer’s request.

examples: Servicing rights purchased on Jan 1; borrower refinances and pays off Mar 15 (within 120 days) → seller refunds 100% SRP plus applicable interest loss/fees.
First payment due becomes 65 days delinquent after purchase date → seller pays original SRP amount plus $1,500 EPD fee within 7 business days of servicer request.
Third payment becomes 60+ days delinquent on two separate loans → seller owes $1,500 per loan (2 × $1,500) plus reimbursement of both purchase prices.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Contractual reimbursement demand and fees; potential formal dispute/remedies

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

deduplication_info.rationale: BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_005 is a direct restatement of clause (a) within BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007: refund 100% of servicing rights price plus associated interest loss/fees if paid in full within 120 days of Purchase Date. BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_006 is a direct restatement of the EPD clause portion: if the 1st/2nd/3rd payment becomes 60+ days delinquent after Purchase Date, seller must reimburse the servicing-rights purchase price and pay a $1,500 EPD fee, wired within 7 business days of request. All thresholds and consequences align; differences are only that the duplicates were extracted as separate rules rather than combined in one.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007 selected as primary because it is the most comprehensive: it includes both (a) the payoff-within-120-days servicing-rights refund obligation and (b) the early payment default (EPD) reimbursement/fee obligation, whereas the duplicates each capture only one sub-clause.

dependencies.rationale: To detect an EPD mortgage loan (e.g., first three payments becoming 60+ days delinquent), servicer systems rely on the established first payment due date and cadence. BR_038_009 governs compliant setup of that first payment date for delivered whole loans, which downstream supports correct EPD surveillance timelines.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is wrong, EPD monitoring windows can be misaligned, causing late detection, incorrect fee/refund demands, or disputes over whether a loan meets the EPD definition.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Ineligible if 60+ day mortgage delinquency within 12 months
business_rules.description: If any borrower’s credit report contains a mortgage tradeline that is 60 or more days past due when last reported, and that tradeline was reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. This policy applies to all mortgage tradelines, including first liens, second liens, home improvement loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans.
