business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU evaluates a casefile and credit report shows mortgage delinquency.
business_rules.consequences: DU returns Ineligible; loan becomes ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.exceptions: Limited discretion if documented delinquency not borrower’s fault (e.g., servicer error).

source_reference.source_text: If any borrower’s credit report contains a mortgage tradeline that is 60 or more days past due when the account was last reported by the creditor and the account was reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. ... Borrowers may not bring past-due mortgage accounts current prior to closing in order to circumvent Fannie Mae’s policy regarding past-due mortgages. However, the lender may apply some discretion ... if it determines and documents that the past-due account status was not the fault of the borrower

examples: Credit report dated 06/01 shows a first mortgage 60 days past due as of 05/15; tradeline reported within prior 12 months → DU Ineligible.
Borrower has a HELOC with a 90-day delinquency reported 8 months ago → DU Ineligible due to prior mortgage delinquency policy.
Servicer misapplied payments causing a 60-day delinquency; lender documents servicer error and account current → lender may apply discretion per guide text.
Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

business_rules.rule_name: Collections payoff thresholds: $5,000 owner-occ; $250/$1,000 investment
business_rules.description: Non-mortgage accounts reported as past due (not collection accounts) must be brought current. Medical collection accounts are excluded from the stated payoff limits and are not required to be paid in full at or prior to closing. For one-unit principal residence properties, borrowers are not required to pay off outstanding collections or non-mortgage charge-offs regardless of amount. For two- to four-unit owner-occupied and second home properties, collections and non-mortgage charge-offs totaling more than $5,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing. For investment properties, individual collection and non-mortgage charge-off accounts ≥ $250 and accounts that total more than $1,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting evaluates collections/charge-offs for qualifying and closing.
business_rules.consequences: If required payoffs not completed, loan fails DU conditions and may be ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Medical collections excluded; 1-unit primary has no payoff requirement per guide.

source_reference.section_id: B3-5.3-09 (02/05/2025), Past-Due, Collection, and Charge-Off of Non-Mortgage Accounts
source_reference.source_text: Medical collection accounts are excluded from the limits below and are not required to be paid in full at or prior to closing. For one-unit, principal residence properties, borrowers are not required to pay oﬀ outstanding collections or non-mortgage charge-oﬀs—regardless of the amount. ... For two- to four-unit owner-occupied and second home properties, collections and non-mortgage charge-oﬀs totaling more than $5,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing. For investment properties, individual collection and non-mortgage charge-oﬀ accounts equal to or greater than $250 and accounts that total more than $1,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.

examples: 1-unit primary residence: borrower has $18,000 non-medical collections → no payoff required by this guide section.
2-unit owner-occupied: borrower has $5,200 total collections → must pay in full prior to or at closing.
Investment property: three collections of $200, $260, $600 (total $1,060) → must pay $260 and $600 (≥$250) and because total >$1,000, payoff required prior to/at closing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition failure; potential delivery ineligibility

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 governs how delinquent non-mortgage accounts/collections/charge-offs are treated for qualifying/closing. BR_014_007 mandates the lender reconcile the full underwriting file against DU-submitted data; if new derogatory info exists, the lender must investigate/resubmit DU or set aside DU. This creates a validation layer over the application of BR_002_009 in DU workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_007 is not performed, the lender may rely on DU findings that are incomplete or contradicted by the file, invalidating limited waiver protections and increasing defect/remedy exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: ADU rental income limited to 30% of total qualifying income
business_rules.description: For a one-unit principal residence with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU), rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income. When the borrower does not have a current primary housing payment, no rental income can be used in qualifying. When the borrower has a housing payment but no property management experience, rental income used in qualifying remains limited to 30% and cannot exceed the PITIA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower uses ADU rental income to qualify on a 1-unit primary home.
business_rules.consequences: If rental income exceeds cap, qualifying income must be reduced; DTI must be recalculated.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 30% cap in the provided table.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01 (10/08/2025), Subject Property rental income restrictions table
source_reference.source_text: 1 Unit Primary Residence with ADU Yes Yes Rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income No Rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income and cannot exceed PITIA No NA No rental income can be used in qualifying

examples: Borrower total qualifying income (before cap) would be $10,000/month; max ADU rental income usable is $3,000/month (30%).

Borrower has housing payment and ADU rent of $2,500/month, but PITIA is $2,200 and no property management experience → usable ADU rental income is capped at $2,200 and also must be ≤30% of total qualifying income.
Borrower reports no current primary housing payment (e.g., living rent-free) → no ADU rental income can be used for qualifying.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting income adjustment; potential delivery defect if exceeded

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady Max 2 Financed Properties and DU Max 10
business_rules.description: Limits apply to the borrower’s maximum number of financed properties: principal residence transactions other than HomeReady loans have no limit; principal residence HomeReady loans (DU and manually underwritten) are limited to 2 financed properties; and second home or investment property transactions underwritten in DU are limited to 10 financed properties. High LTV refinance loans are exempt from multiple financed property policies.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating loan eligibility based on financed property count and occupancy
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, loan may be eligible; if exceeded, loan is ineligible unless exempt
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans are exempt from this policy

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-03, Limits on the Number of Financed Properties
source_reference.source_text:
Limits on the Number of Financed Properties
The following table describes the limits that apply to the number of financed properties a borrower may have.
Subject Property Occupancy TransactionMaximum Number of
Financed Properties
Principal residenceTransactions other than
HomeReady loansNo limit
Principal residence HomeReady loansDU and manually underwritten -
2
Second home or Investment
propertyAll DU - 10
Exception: High LTV refinance loans are exempt from the multiple financed property policies.

examples: HomeReady purchase of principal residence: borrower has 1 financed investment property now; with subject property becomes 2 (eligible).
DU underwritten second home: borrower already has 10 financed properties; adding another would be 11 (ineligible).
High LTV refinance: borrower has 12 financed properties; policy is exempt, so financed property count limit does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase exposure if delivered

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Contract income must continue 3 years to recur
business_rules.description: When analyzing a self-employed borrower’s personal income, the lender must confirm stability and likelihood of continuance for each income source reported on IRS Form 1040, and must not include income that does not appear stable or likely to continue. Income may be treated as recurring only if the file contains no specific indication of an upcoming change, employment history has no gaps/significant fluctuations, and any contractual (non “at will”) income will continue for at least 3 years.
business_rules.conditions: Self-employed borrower income analysis using IRS Form 1040 sources
business_rules.consequences: If stability/3-year continuance not supported, exclude income from qualifying
business_rules.exceptions: “At will” contractual income is excluded from the 3-year test

source_reference.source_text: In analyzing a self-employed borrower’s personal income, the lender should focus on earnings trends and the actual sources of the income, not just on the total amount of the income. The lender must confirm the stability and likelihood of continuance for each source of income that the borrower reports on their IRS Form 1040. The lender should not include any income that does not appear to be stable or likely to continue. The lender should, however, consider all recurring income that the borrower can expect to continue receiving over time. Income may be considered as recurring if the loan application package does not include any specific indication of an upcoming change in the borrower’s employment or income, the borrower’s employment history has no gaps or other significant fluctuations in income, and any income received under a contractual agreement (other than an “at will” contract) will continue to be received for at least three years.

examples: Borrower has a 5-year consulting contract with 36+ months remaining and steady 2-year history; lender may treat it as recurring income.
Borrower has a contract that expires in 18 months; lender must exclude that contract income from qualifying income.
Borrower’s income is “at will” employment; lender cannot rely on the 3-year contract test to treat it as recurring.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit recurring-income criteria includes a 3-year contractual continuance test.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: BR_012_001 provides the threshold test that turns on self-employment underwriting requirements. BR_030_001 cannot be correctly applied unless the lender first determines the borrower meets the self-employed definition based on ownership interest.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_012_001 is not applied or is misapplied, the lender may skip required self-employment analysis, improperly include unstable income, and materially misstate qualifying income/DTI—creating significant underwriting defects and repurchase risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_001 relies on tax-return-derived income sources and stability determinations. BR_010_009 requires a signed 4506-C (unless DU validation fully validates income) for each borrower whose income is used. This creates a validation dependency: tax-based income analysis is materially weakened/defective without the transcript authorization or DU validation substitute.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_009 fails (missing/expired 4506-C where required), the loan file has an income verification defect; the lender may need to suspend/condition, obtain a new form, re-verify income, or face QC findings and remedies exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Schedule B income needs 2 years and no asset use
business_rules.description: Taxable interest and dividend income reported on IRS Form 1040 Schedule B may be counted as stable income only if it has been received for the past 2 years. Even with a 2-year history, the income cannot be counted if the borrower is using the interest-bearing or dividend-producing asset as the source of the down payment or closing costs. Any nonrecurring taxable interest/dividend income must be deducted from cash flow.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower uses interest/dividend income from IRS Form 1040 Schedule B
business_rules.consequences: If <2 years history or asset used for funds to close, exclude from qualifying
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The taxable interest and dividend income that is reported on IRS Form 1040, Schedule B, may be counted as stable income only if it has been received for the past two years. However, the income cannot be counted if the borrower is using the interest-bearing or dividend-producing asset as the source of the down payment or closing costs. Any taxable interest or dividend income that is not recurring must be deducted from the borrower’s cash flow.

examples: Borrower shows taxable dividends for 2023 and 2024 and will not liquidate the brokerage account; lender may include dividends as stable income.
Borrower has 2-year interest income history but plans to use the same CD account to fund down payment; lender must exclude that interest income.
Borrower received a one-time dividend distribution in 2024 only; lender must deduct it as nonrecurring.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit 2-year history requirement and an explicit prohibition tied to asset use for funds to close.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_001 requires confirming stability/continuance of each income source on Form 1040. BR_030_002 adds explicit, stricter conditions for taxable interest/dividend income (2-year receipt, and asset-not-used-for-funds-to-close restriction). Therefore, when the lender is analyzing Schedule B income as part of the 1040 cash flow, BR_030_001’s general analysis gates into BR_030_002’s specific acceptability test.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_030_001 is performed without BR_030_002, the lender may count interest/dividend income that is unstable, too new, or disallowed due to depletion of the underlying asset for down payment/closing costs—overstating income and understating risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Installment debts with 10 or fewer payments excluded
business_rules.description: Installment loans that will be paid off or paid down to 10 or fewer remaining monthly payments generally do not need to be included in the borrower’s long-term debt for qualification (and therefore may be excluded from DTI).
business_rules.conditions: When an installment loan will have ≤10 payments remaining at/after closing
business_rules.consequences: If met, lender may exclude payment from long-term debt; if violated, DTI may be understated and loan subject to defect/repurchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (Guide uses 'Generally')

source_reference.section_id: B3-6-07, Payoff or Paydown of Debt for Qualification
source_reference.source_text: Installment loans that are being paid oﬀ or paid down to 10 or fewer remaining monthly payments do not need to be included in the borrower’s long-term debt.

examples: Borrower has an auto loan with 9 payments remaining at closing; lender excludes the auto payment from DTI.
Borrower has a personal loan with 11 payments remaining; lender must include the payment in DTI unless it will be paid down to 10 or fewer payments.
Borrower pays down an installment loan so only 10 payments remain; lender documents the paydown and excludes the payment from long-term debt.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op project must meet IRS Section 216 or Fannie Mae will not purchase
business_rules.description: For a co-op share loan to be eligible for sale, the co-op project must qualify as a cooperative housing corporation under IRS Code Section 216, and the lender’s loan or project approval file must contain evidence of the project’s compliance. If the project does not meet Section 216, Fannie Mae will not purchase a co-op share loan from within the project.
business_rules.conditions: Co-op share loan delivery; project eligibility review; Section 216 compliance required
business_rules.consequences: If Section 216 not met/evidenced, loan is not purchasable by Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-02, Eligibility Requirements for Co-op Projects (Full Review)
source_reference.source_text: For a co-op share loan to be eligible for sale, the co-op project in which the secured unit is located must qualify as a cooperative housing corporation under Section 216 of the Internal Revenue Service Code. The lender’s loan or project approval ﬁle must contain evidence regarding the project’s compliance with Section 216. Note : If the co-op project does not meet Section 216 requirements, Fannie Mae will not purchase a co-op share loan from within the project.

examples: Lender file includes legal/organizational evidence showing the co-op qualifies under IRS Section 216: project meets this eligibility element.
Project cannot demonstrate Section 216 qualification: the share loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae from that project.
Project appears to meet Section 216 but lender approval file contains no evidence: fails the documentation/evidence requirement stated here.
Co-op share loan for an owner-occupied unit
Co-op project requiring legal compliance review
Co-op unit with maintenance fee and underlying mortgage considerations

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial or nonresidential space in project may not exceed 35%
business_rules.description: For condo and co-op projects, the total space used for nonresidential or commercial purposes must not exceed 35%. Exceeding 35% is an ineligible project characteristic for purchase/securitization eligibility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating condo/co-op project mixed-use allocation for eligibility.
business_rules.consequences: If nonresidential/commercial space >35%, the project is ineligible for Fannie Mae loans.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: The total space that is used for nonresidential or commercial purposes may not exceed 35%. (See Commercial Space and Mixed-Use Allocation below for additional detail.)

examples: Project has 30% retail space on ground floor; meets the ≤35% requirement.
Project has 36% commercial office space; exceeds 35% and is ineligible for Fannie Mae purchase.
Mixed-use project recalculation shows 34.9% commercial; remains eligible under the 35% cap.
Co-op share loan for an owner-occupied unit
Co-op project requiring legal compliance review
Co-op unit with maintenance fee and underlying mortgage considerations

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_006 is a project eligibility prohibition based on CPM/DU findings status "Unavailable." BR_019_007 is a mixed-use commercial space cap (≤35%) for condo/co-op projects. In workflow automation, both must be checked to determine project eligibility for Fannie Mae purchase/securitization; they complement each other as parallel gating criteria.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, the project is ineligible; if one rule is omitted from checks, lender may incorrectly treat an ineligible project as eligible and face delivery failures/repurchase risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_007 evaluates nonresidential/commercial space percentage for condo/co-op projects. BR_005_007 disqualifies certain condo/co-op project structures outright. Therefore, BR_005_007 acts as a gating screen before assessing other condo/co-op eligibility characteristics like mixed-use limits.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_005_007 fails and is not detected: project might incorrectly appear eligible due to compliant commercial-space percentage, causing delivery defects when the prohibited structure is later discovered.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_001 includes condo/co-op units as eligible leasehold property types (subject to market acceptance and other criteria). BR_019_007 is a condo/co-op project eligibility criterion (commercial space ≤ 35%). Therefore, when BR_011_001 is satisfied for a leasehold condo/co-op unit, BR_019_007 must also be evaluated to confirm project eligibility.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_019_007 fails (commercial space > 35%): the leasehold property type may be permissible in concept, but the specific project is ineligible, preventing delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Reserves 2 Months Second Home and 6 Months for DTI Over 45%
business_rules.description: For DU loan casefiles, DU determines minimum required reserves as follows: two months’ reserves for a second home transaction; six months’ reserves for (i) a two- to four-unit principal residence transaction, (ii) an investment property transaction, and (iii) a cash-out refinance transaction when DTI ratio is greater than 45%.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting through DU and DU determines required minimum reserves
business_rules.consequences: Meets: DU reserve condition satisfied; Violates: underwriting conditions unmet; loan not clear to close/deliver
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans exempt (separate rule)

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.1-01, Determining Required Minimum Reserves (DU loan casefiles)
source_reference.source_text: DU loan caseﬁles : DU will determine the reserve requirements based on the following: Two months' reserves for a second home transaction. Six months' reserves for the following: a two- to four-unit principal residence transaction, an investment property transaction, and a cash-out reﬁnance transaction with a DTI ratio greater than 45%.

examples: Second home purchase with PITIA $3,000: DU requires 2 months = $6,000 reserves verified from eligible assets.
Investment property refinance with PITIA $2,200: DU requires 6 months = $13,200 reserves; borrower has $9,000—fails reserve condition.
Cash-out refi with DTI 46% and PITIA $2,500: DU requires 6 months = $15,000; if DTI is 44%, this specific 6-month trigger does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_005 describes how DU determines required reserves. BR_008_001 requires retaining the final DU Underwriting Analysis Report. In compliance workflows, the DU report is the artifact used to validate that reserve requirements were correctly identified and conditioned. Thus BR_008_001 supports/validates BR_023_005 compliance evidence.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_008_001 fails, the lender may be unable to demonstrate DU-determined reserve requirements were applied correctly, increasing post-close audit/QC findings even if reserves were actually met.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_040_007 requires (in one permitted branch) recalculation of the credit card payment and inclusion of the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation. BR_DESKTOP_UNDERWRITER_CONSTRAINT_023_005 sets certain minimum reserves based on transaction type and DTI (>45% for certain cash-out refis). Therefore, accurate implementation of 040_007 can change the DTI outcome and can trigger higher reserve requirements under 023_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 040_007 is not satisfied (e.g., payment not recalculated), DTI may be understated; DU reserve requirement determination may be incorrect, leading to insufficient reserves documented and potential underwriting defects or post-purchase remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Accessory Dwelling Unit Rental Income Capped at 30%
business_rules.description: Rental income from an existing accessory dwelling unit (ADU) on the subject property may be used for qualifying only when all listed conditions are met, including that the qualifying rental income from the ADU is limited to 30% of the borrower’s total qualifying income.
business_rules.conditions: Subject is 1-unit principal residence with 1 ADU; purchase or LCO refi
business_rules.consequences: If met, ADU rental income may be used; if exceeded, cap at 30% or disallow
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond listed ADU conditions

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01, Rental Income (10/08/2025) - Rental Income from an ADU on the Subject Property
source_reference.source_text: Rental Income from an ADU on the Subject Property Rental income from an existing ADU can be used in qualifying with the following conditions: one-unit, principal residence only, rental income from only one ADU allowed, purchase or limited cash-out reﬁnance transactions, and qualifying rental income amount from the ADU is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income. All other documentation and requirements contained within this topic apply.

examples: Purchase 1-unit primary with ADU: total qualifying income $10,000; ADU rent may contribute up to $3,000.
Limited cash-out refi 1-unit primary with 2 ADUs: rental income is not eligible because only one ADU is allowed.
Purchase primary with ADU: if ADU rent would be 40% of total qualifying income, lender must cap at 30% or not use it to qualify.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_004_005 defines when a property can be classified as a one-unit dwelling with an accessory dwelling unit (only one ADU, not allowed with 2–4 unit dwellings, and specific kitchen requirements). BR_007_001 allows rental income from an existing ADU to qualify only when conditions are met (including a 30% cap). Correct application of BR_007_001 requires confirming the property satisfies BR_004_005’s ADU classification criteria first.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_004_005 fails: the loan cannot treat the property as 1-unit+ADU; any qualifying income based on ADU rent must be removed or recalculated; DTI/qualification may fail; underwriting decision may change from approve to deny or require restructure.

business_rules.rule_name: Seasonal Income Minimum History Must Be 2 Years
business_rules.description: Seasonal income requires a minimum two-year history to be eligible for use as qualifying income.
business_rules.conditions: Seasonal income is being analyzed as borrower qualifying income
business_rules.consequences: If ≥2-year history exists, proceed; if <2 years, exclude seasonal income from qualification
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for seasonal income history requirement

source_reference.source_text: Income History A minimum two-year history is required. Income Continuance The lender is not required to verify continuance unless they have reason to believe the income may not continue.

examples: Borrower has 3 years of seasonal construction income—meets the minimum history requirement.
Borrower has 14 months of seasonal income—does not meet the 2-year minimum; income must be excluded.
Borrower has 2 years of seasonal income but lender has reason to believe it may stop—continuance must be addressed per guidance.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: ULDD data errors in 5 or more loans trigger life-of-loan remedy
business_rules.description: Even if a loan obtained enforcement relief, the lender remains responsible for life-of-loan data accuracy. A remedy may be enforced when delivery data (ULDD) inaccuracies (i) affect five or more loans, (ii) involve the same delivery data element(s), (iii) differ from the information documented in the lender’s mortgage loan files, and (iv) are “significant” such that the loan would have been ineligible for delivery under the contract terms in effect at delivery, or would have been eligible but under different terms (which may result in repricing instead of repurchase).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to delivered loans with a pattern of ULDD inaccuracies across ≥5 loans.
business_rules.consequences: Significant inaccuracies can result in repurchase demand or loan repricing.
business_rules.exceptions: No pattern (fewer than 5 loans) fails this test; fraud has separate standard.

source_reference.source_text:
Even if a mortgage loan has met the requirements for enforcement relief set forth in A2-3.2-02, Enforcement
Relief for Breaches of Certain Representations and Warranties Related to Underwriting and Eligibility , the lender
remains responsible throughout the life of the loan for representations and warranties related to data accuracy
as set forth below. In connection with mortgage loans delivered to Fannie Mae, there must not be delivery data
(Uniform Loan Delivery Dataset) inaccuracies pertaining to the borrower, the property, or the project, if and to
the extent
the data inaccuracies aﬀect ﬁve or more loans and involve the same delivery data element(s);
such delivery data diﬀer from the information documented in the lender's mortgage loan ﬁles; and
the data inaccuracies are “signiﬁcant,” in that, using the information of the loan ﬁle to qualify the
borrower, property, and project,
the loan would not have been eligible for delivery under the terms of the lender’s contracts with
Fannie Mae in eﬀect at the time of delivery of the loan; or
the loan would have been eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, but under diﬀerent terms.

examples: 5 loans delivered with incorrect occupancy type in ULDD vs file; corrected data makes 2 loans ineligible: Fannie may enforce repurchase for those loans.
8 loans delivered with overstated credit scores in ULDD vs file; corrected data changes terms but remains eligible: Fannie may re-price instead of repurchase.
4 loans have the same ULDD data element error: does not meet the ≥5-loan threshold for this pattern-based test.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit multi-part threshold test; consequence references repurchase/repricing framework.

examples: Representation that underwriting complied with eligibility and documentation requirements
Warranty that loan data delivered matches the mortgage file
Limited waiver applied for DU-underwritten loans under specified conditions

business_rules.rule_name: Credit documents must be no more than 4 months old on note date
business_rules.description: For all mortgage loans (existing and new construction), credit documents (including credit reports and employment, income, and asset documentation) must be no more than four months old on the note date. If consecutive documents exist in the file, the most recent document is used to determine compliance with the age requirement (e.g., most recent bank statement date controls when two consecutive monthly statements are used).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all credit reports and income/asset/employment docs for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If within 4 months, age requirement met; if older, documents are stale
business_rules.exceptions: Disaster-impact exceptions referenced elsewhere (B2-3-05)

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents
source_reference.source_text: Credit documents include credit reports and employment, income, and asset documentation. For all mortgage loans (existing and new construction), the credit documents must be no more than four months old on the note date. When consecutive credit documents are in the loan file, the most recent document is used to determine whether it meets the age requirement. For example, when two consecutive monthly bank statements are used to verify a depository asset, the date of the most recent statement must be no more than four months old on the note date.

examples: Note date 2026-06-30: most recent paystub dated 2026-04-15 (76 days old) is acceptable under the 4-month rule.
Two bank statements dated 2026-02-28 and 2026-03-31: lender uses 2026-03-31 to test the 4-month requirement.
Note date 2026-06-30 with latest bank statement dated 2026-01-31: statement is older than 4 months and triggers an update requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential delivery/QC defect if uncorrected

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: B8-5-05 POA Allowed for Purchase and Limited Cash-Out
business_rules.description: Loans with documents executed by an agent under a power of attorney (POA) are eligible for delivery only for the eligible transactions listed (Purchase and Limited cash-out refinance) and only if the lender meets the POA documentation requirements: obtain a copy of the POA, ensure names on the POA match names on the relevant loan document, ensure the POA date shows it was valid when the loan document was executed, ensure the POA is notarized, and ensure the POA references the subject property address.
business_rules.conditions: When note and/or security instrument is signed by an agent under POA
business_rules.consequences: If met: POA-signed docs acceptable; if violated: document defect may block delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None in this excerpt (other guide provisions may apply)

source_reference.section_id: B8-5-05 (10/05/2022), Eligibility Requirements for Using a POA
source_reference.source_text: Underwriting and Documentation Requirements Eligible Transactions• Purchase • Limited cash-out reﬁnance Documentation RequirementsAn agent under a POA may sign the note and/or security instrument on behalf of a borrower (as deﬁned in the Glossary) if all of the following requirements are met: • The lender obtains a copy of the POA. • The name(s) on the POA match the name(s) of the person on the relevant loan document. • The POA is dated such that it was valid at the time the relevant loan document was executed. • The POA is notarized. • The POA must reference the address of the subject property.

examples: Purchase loan: borrower is overseas; attorney-in-fact signs note; file contains notarized POA referencing the subject property address and matching borrower name.
Limited cash-out refinance: POA copy in file but POA does not reference subject property address; lender must cure (new POA) or loan becomes ineligible.
Cash-out refinance: even with a compliant POA, transaction type is not listed as eligible here; lender must not rely on POA under this section.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/custodian certification failure; potential repurchase demand

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: FEMA disaster area underwriting docs must be dated within 180 days of note date
business_rules.description: When the loan is secured by a property located in a FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for Individual Assistance, the underwriting documentation (including credit reports and verifications of income and assets) must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date.
business_rules.conditions: Property is in FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for Individual Assistance
business_rules.consequences: If docs ≤180 days, documentation is acceptable; if >180 days, updated docs required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt

source_reference.source_text: When a loan is secured by a property located in a FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for individual assistance, Fannie Mae provides additional ﬂexibilities. The following requirements apply: The underwriting documentation, including credit reports and veriﬁcations of income and assets, must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date.

examples: Credit report dated 200 days prior to note date for a FEMA IA ZIP; lender must refresh the credit report.
VOE and bank statements dated 150 days prior to note date in FEMA IA ZIP; documentation meets the 180-day requirement.
Income verification dated 181 days before note date; lender must obtain an updated verification dated within 180 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery defect; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental hazard not measurable by comps means loan is not eligible for delivery
business_rules.description: If an environmental hazard has a significant effect but the effect is not measurable because the hazard is so serious or so recently discovered that the appraiser cannot arrive at a reliable opinion of market value due to lack of comparable market data (sales, contract sales, or active listings), the mortgage is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an environmental hazard exists and no comparable data supports a reliable value opinion.
business_rules.consequences: If measurable through comparable data, loan may remain eligible with appraisal reflecting impacts; if not measurable, loan is ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: In rare situations, a particular environmental hazard may have a signiﬁcant eﬀect on the value of the subject property, although the actual eﬀect is not measurable because the hazard is so serious or so recently discovered that an appraiser cannot arrive at a reliable opinion of market value because there is no comparable market data available, such as sales, contract sales, or active listings that are available to reﬂect the eﬀect of the hazard. In such cases, the mortgage will not be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Newly discovered toxic spill adjacent to property with no affected-area sales/listings: appraiser cannot support value; loan is ineligible for delivery.
Known radon issue with ample comparable sales showing no buyer resistance: appraiser analyzes comps; loan may be eligible if impacts addressed.
Serious hazard discovered weeks before appraisal with no market evidence yet: value impact not measurable; delivery ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Special assessment district with no measurable market data is ineligible
business_rules.description: If the financial difficulty of a special assessment district is so severe that its effect on value and marketability is not measurable due to no comparable market data, the mortgage secured by a property in that district is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae until an active market develops that enables the appraiser to demonstrate value and marketability.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property in special assessment district; appraiser lacks comparable market data
business_rules.consequences: If active market data exists, appraisal may support delivery; if not, loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility resumes when an active market develops enabling reliable valuation

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.4-09 (06/04/2025) - Appraisal Requirements for Special Assessment Districts
source_reference.source_text: There may be some instances in which the ﬁnancial diﬃculty of a special assessment district is so severe that its actual eﬀect on the value and marketability of a property is not measurable because there is no comparable market data available to enable the appraiser to arrive at a reliable opinion of market value. When this is the case, a mortgage secured by a property in that district will not be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae until such time that an active market develops that will enable the appraiser to demonstrate the value and marketability of the subject property.

examples: Appraiser documents special assessment district distress and states no comparable sales/listings exist to measure impact; loan cannot be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Comparable closed sales within the district exist showing price impact; appraisal can quantify effect and loan may be eligible.
After market recovers and sufficient sales occur, a new appraisal can demonstrate value/marketability and restore eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Non MH Advantage Manufactured Homes Require LLPA Pricing
business_rules.description: A loan-level price adjustment (LLPA) applies to all mortgages secured by manufactured homes that do not meet MH Advantage requirements, regardless of whether the loan is delivered for whole loan purchase or MBS issuance. The manufactured housing LLPA is in addition to any other price adjustments applicable to the transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Mortgage is secured by a manufactured home and is not MH Advantage eligible
business_rules.consequences: If met, apply manufactured housing LLPA in addition to other LLPAs; if missed, pricing defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: MH Advantage manufactured homes are excluded from this specific LLPA requirement

source_reference.source_text: An LLPA applies to all mortgages secured by manufactured homes that do not meet the MH Advantage requirements, whether delivered to Fannie Mae for whole loan purchase or MBS issuance. These LLPAs are in addition to any other price adjustments that are otherwise applicable to the particular transaction.

examples: Multi-width manufactured home not MH Advantage: lender applies the manufactured housing LLPA plus any standard credit score LLPA.
Single-width manufactured home not MH Advantage delivered into an MBS pool: manufactured housing LLPA still applies.
MH Advantage loan delivered with evidence of MH Advantage eligibility: manufactured housing non-MH Advantage LLPA does not apply.
Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_007 applies an LLPA when the manufactured home does not meet MH Advantage requirements. BR_015_008 requires delivering SFCs that distinguish standard manufactured vs MH Advantage types. While the LLPA decision can be made without SFCs internally, delivery and audit alignment typically depend on consistent classification signals (SFCs) supporting the LLPA choice.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SFCs are wrong/missing, pricing and delivery data may not reconcile (e.g., LLPA applied/not applied inconsistently with reported MH Advantage status), leading to purchase conditions, pricing defects, or post-purchase adjustments.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home must have HUD plate or labels or loan is ineligible for sale
business_rules.description: For manufactured homes, compliance with Federal Manufactured Home Construction and Safety Standards (June 15, 1976, as amended and in force at time of manufacture) and HUD regulations at 24 C.F.R. Part 3280 must be evidenced by HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s). Lenders must obtain photos of the original HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s), or obtain specified alternative documentation (e.g., IBTS label verification letter or duplicate HUD Data Plate) for existing construction. If neither the Data Plate nor Certification Labels can be obtained, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Both are required for new construction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans secured by manufactured homes (existing or new construction).
business_rules.consequences: If HUD evidence cannot be obtained, loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.exceptions: Alternative documentation allowed for existing homes (e.g., IBTS verification).

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-02 (02/04/2026), Manufactured Home Property Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Compliance with these standards will be evidenced by the presence of either a HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certiﬁcation Label(s) for each section of the home for existing construction. Lenders must obtain photos of the original HUD Data Plate or HUD Certiﬁcation Label(s) or provide the alternative documentation described below. If neither of these can be obtained, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. (Both are required for new construction).

examples: Existing doublewide: appraisal includes photo of HUD Certification Labels on each section; eligibility evidence satisfied.
Existing manufactured home: labels removed and Data Plate missing; lender cannot obtain IBTS verification; loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
New construction manufactured home: lender must obtain both HUD Data Plate and Certification Labels documentation; missing either fails eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_003 mandates manufactured home loans be underwritten through DU. BR_019_008 mandates HUD Data Plate/Certification Label evidence (or acceptable alternatives) and states lack of evidence makes the loan ineligible for sale. They are independent but jointly required for a manufactured home loan to be eligible for Fannie Mae delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, the manufactured home loan is ineligible for delivery: (a) not DU-underwritten (BR_007_003 failure) or (b) no HUD compliance evidence (BR_019_008 failure). This blocks sale and creates significant remediation burden.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home minimum size is 12 feet wide and 400 square feet
business_rules.description: A manufactured home securing a Fannie Mae-eligible loan must be at least 12 feet wide and have a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade finished area.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to manufactured home collateral eligibility evaluation.
business_rules.consequences: If width <12 feet or area <400 sq ft, property fails eligibility requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt (MH Advantage noted but not changing these minimums here).

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-02 (02/04/2026), Manufactured Home Property Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: The manufactured home must be at least 12 feet wide and have a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade ﬁnished area.

examples: Singlewide is 14 feet wide with 780 sq ft; meets minimums.
Unit is 11 feet wide (regardless of sq ft); fails the 12-foot minimum width requirement.
Unit is 12 feet wide but only 380 sq ft above-grade finished area; fails the 400 sq ft minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_009 requires a manufactured home to have "a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade finished area." BR_023_009 requires ANSI Z765-2021 when measuring/calculating/reporting above- and below-grade square footage (including manufactured homes). Therefore, correct evaluation of BR_019_009 depends on applying BR_023_009 measurement methodology to produce reliable area figures.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ANSI measurement is not followed, the reported GLA/area may be unreliable, leading to incorrect eligibility determinations (false pass/fail). This can cause delivery ineligibility, repurchase exposure, or require appraisal revision/re-measurement.

business_rules.rule_name: Trade equity capped at 90% of NADA retail value with 12-month lookback
business_rules.description: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used toward the minimum down payment, but the maximum equity contribution is 90% of the traded home’s retail value per the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide. If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the loan application date, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of (i) 90% of NADA retail value or (ii) the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12-month period. If the calculated trade equity exceeds the cap, the excess must be excluded and the transaction must be restructured/adjusted accordingly.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction uses trade equity from borrower’s existing manufactured home
business_rules.consequences: If within cap, equity may count; if over cap, excess must be excluded from funds
business_rules.exceptions: Owned <12 months triggers lesser-of 90% NADA or lowest 12-month sales price

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Trade equity from existing manufactured home
source_reference.source_text: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used as part of the borrower’s minimum down payment requirement. The maximum equity contribution from the traded manufactured home is 90% of the retail value for the traded manufactured home based on the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide except: If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the date of the loan application, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of 90% of the retail value or the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12 month period. Any costs associated with the removal of the traded home or any outstanding indebtedness secured by liens on the home must be deducted from the maximum equity contribution.

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Trade Equity from Existing Manufactured Home
source_reference.source_text: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used as part of the borrower’s minimum down payment requirement. The maximum equity contribution from the traded manufactured home is 90% of the retail value for the traded manufactured home based on the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide except: If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the date of the loan application, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of 90% of the retail value or the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12 month period. Any costs associated with the removal of the traded home or any outstanding indebtedness secured by liens on the home must be deducted from the maximum equity contribution.

examples: NADA retail value is $50,000; borrower owned it 3 years; max equity allowed is $45,000 less removal costs and any lien payoff.
Borrower owned traded home 8 months; NADA retail is $60,000 (90%=$54,000) but lowest sale price in last 12 months was $48,000; max equity is $48,000 minus liens/removal.
Removal cost $5,000 and lien payoff $10,000 on a $45,000 max equity; allowable equity contribution becomes $30,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the same cap on trade equity from an existing manufactured home used toward minimum down payment: maximum equity contribution is 90% of NADA retail value; if owned <12 months, the maximum is the lesser of 90% of retail value or the lowest sales price during that 12-month period. Numeric thresholds (90%, 12 months) and context align; consequences align (cap equity; adjust funds to close). Wording differences only.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_CONSTRAINT_036_004 selected as primary because it is slightly more complete/cleanly scoped as a manufactured home constraint and is better positioned as the canonical manufactured-home trade-equity cap rule for the knowledge graph.

dependencies.rationale: BR_036_004 allows applying trade equity toward down payment subject to caps. BR_036_005 mandates lien searches and title/lien satisfaction proof for traded manufactured homes. Without verifying liens/ownership, the lender cannot determine whether trade equity is valid/available or whether proceeds are encumbered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_005 fails, the trade equity cannot be safely credited; down payment sources become unsupported, potentially making the loan ineligible or requiring restructuring with additional borrower funds.

business_rules.rule_name: 401(k) or IRA qualifies only with unrestricted access to all funds
business_rules.description: A borrower may be considered to have unrestricted access to a 401(k) or IRA/SEP/Keogh retirement account only if, at the time of calculation, the borrower has the unqualified and unlimited right to request a distribution of all funds in the account, regardless of any possible tax withholding or applicable penalty.
business_rules.conditions: Retirement account funds are used to calculate employment-related asset income
business_rules.consequences: If access is unrestricted, account may be used; if restricted, exclude income
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The borrower must only be considered to have unrestricted access to a 401(k) or IRA, SEP, Keogh retirement account if the borrower has, as of the time of calculation, the unqualiﬁed and unlimited right to request a distribution of all funds in the account (regardless of any possible tax withholding or applicable penalty applied to such distribution).

examples: Borrower can request full IRA distribution at any time (penalty applies) → eligible for asset-income calculation (penalty is handled in net assets).
Employer plan restricts distributions until termination of employment → not unrestricted; lender must not use the 401(k) for employment-related asset income.
Account allows only limited hardship withdrawals → not unlimited; exclude the account from the income stream calculation.
401(k) with vested balance used to meet reserve requirements
IRA funds withdrawn for down payment with documented terms
Retirement account with outstanding loan affecting available funds

business_rules.rule_name: Grant donors must be eligible entities; exclude seller or interested party
business_rules.description: Grant funds must be funded by specified acceptable entities and the grant provider cannot be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction. Acceptable entities include governmental bodies, a housing finance agency (24 C.F.R. §266.5), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit, a regional Federal Home Loan Bank program, an employer (for an employee borrower), an Indian tribe (25 U.S.C. §5131 list), or a lender (subject to lender-funded grant rules).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when funds to close/reserves include donated funds treated as a grant
business_rules.consequences: Ineligible donor source makes grant funds unacceptable and may invalidate approval
business_rules.exceptions: Lender-funded grants permitted only under separate lender-funded grant criteria

source_reference.source_text: Grants must be funded by one of the following entities, provided they are not the property seller or other interested party in the transaction: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state; any city, town, village, or borough of a state that has a local government ...; a housing ﬁnance agency as deﬁned in 24 C.F.R. §266.5; a nonproﬁt organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code; a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its aﬀordable housing programs; an employer where the borrower is an employee

examples: A 501(c)(3) nonprofit provides a $10,000 grant; donor is eligible if not the property seller or otherwise an interested party.
The property seller offers a 'grant' through an affiliated entity; it is ineligible because the provider is an interested party.
An employer provides a $3,000 housing grant to its employee borrower; donor is eligible under the employer category.
State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_007 includes "a lender (subject to lender-funded grant rules)." BR_022_009 defines those lender-funded grant rules (HomeReady purchase-only, contribution requirements, documented program). Thus, when the grant source is the lender, BR_022_009 governs and effectively overrides/conditions the general eligibility statement in BR_022_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_009 is not satisfied (e.g., not HomeReady purchase), the lender-funded 'grant' becomes ineligible even though 'lender' is listed generally in BR_022_007; this can invalidate funds-to-close/reserves and require restructuring.

business_rules.rule_name: Grants require 5% borrower funds for 2–4 unit when LTV over 80%
business_rules.description: For transactions that contain grants (excluding lender-funded grants), if the property is a two- to four-unit principal residence and the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV ratio is greater than 80%, the borrower must make a 5% minimum borrower contribution from their own funds. After the minimum contribution is met, grants can supplement down payment, closing costs, reserves, and energy-related improvements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to principal residence 2–4 units with grant funds and LTV/CLTV/HCLTV > 80%
business_rules.consequences: If borrower funds < 5%, loan is not compliant and may be ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to lender-funded grants; other LTV/property cases may have no minimum

source_reference.source_text: Greater than 80% Two- to four-unit principal residence The borrower must make a 5% minimum borrower contribution from their own funds. After the minimum borrower contribution has been met, grants can be used to supplement the down payment, closing costs, reserves, and energy-related improvements.

examples: Two-unit principal residence purchase at 90% LTV with a $20,000 grant: borrower must contribute at least 5% of the value/price from own funds before applying the grant.
Three-unit principal residence at 75% LTV with a grant: no minimum borrower contribution required under the table for ≤80%.
Four-unit principal residence at 85% CLTV: borrower contributes only 3% own funds; loan must be restructured to meet 5% minimum or the grant cannot be used as structured.
State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_008 applies "For transactions that contain grants" and imposes borrower minimum contribution requirements at higher LTVs for 2–4 unit principal residences. BR_022_007 defines whether funds can be treated as grant funds at all (eligible donor; not seller/interested party). Therefore grant eligibility must be established before applying BR_022_008’s contribution logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_007 fails (e.g., seller-funded 'grant'), BR_022_008 calculations become irrelevant because the funds are not eligible. The loan may become ineligible unless restructured with acceptable funds and re-underwritten.

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_008 requires a 5% minimum borrower contribution in certain grant scenarios (2–4 unit PR, >80% LTV). BR_034_004 states disaster relief grants/loans may satisfy minimum borrower contribution and that "no borrower contribution from the borrower’s own funds is required" when using this assistance. This is a direct exception overriding the base constraint.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_034_004 is not applied when it should be, the lender may incorrectly require borrower funds, potentially denying eligible borrowers or delaying closing. If applied incorrectly, the lender may under-collect required contributions, risking ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-funded grants: HomeReady purchase with 3% or 5% borrower contribution
business_rules.description: A lender-funded grant is permitted only if the loan is a HomeReady loan used for a purchase transaction. Minimum borrower contribution requirements apply: (i) one-unit principal residences require 3% contribution from borrower’s own funds/other eligible sources/Community Seconds; (ii) two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV ≤ 80% require 3%; and (iii) two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV > 80% require 5%. Additionally, the lender must have a documented program providing grants for low- to moderate-income borrowers, community development, equitable housing initiatives, or similar initiatives.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the grant source is the lender (lender-funded grant) for a HomeReady purchase
business_rules.consequences: If not HomeReady purchase or contribution shortfall, lender-funded grant is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If only closing cost assistance, Lender Contributions policy may be applied instead

source_reference.source_text: A lender-funded grant is subject to the requirements described in this topic, but must also meet the requirements in the following table. The loan must be a HomeReady loan used for a purchase transaction. ... One-unit principal residences: The borrower(s) must make a 3% contribution ... Two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios 80% or less: The borrower(s) must make a 3% contribution ... Two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios greater than 80%: The borrower(s) must make a 5% minimum contribution from their own funds. The lender must have a documented program

examples: HomeReady 1-unit purchase: lender-funded grant used; borrower contributes 3% from own funds/eligible sources; rule satisfied.
Two-unit HomeReady purchase at 85% LTV: borrower must contribute 5% own funds; if only 3% is documented, the lender-funded grant cannot be used as structured.
Conventional (non-HomeReady) purchase: lender-funded grant is not eligible under this section; lender must remove it or restructure as allowable lender contribution if applicable.
State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_009 specifies minimum borrower contribution requirements for lender-funded grants. BR_034_004 states disaster relief lump sums may satisfy minimum contribution and that no borrower own-funds contribution is required when using this assistance. Therefore, in scenarios where both could apply, BR_034_004 overrides the borrower-funds requirement aspect of BR_022_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not applied, lender may wrongly impose borrower contribution requirements, causing delays/denials. If misapplied, lender may fail to meet HomeReady/grant contribution rules when disaster relief does not qualify, risking ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Desktop Appraisal Allowed Only for 1-Unit Purchase at 90% LTV with DU Approve
business_rules.description: A desktop appraisal (Form 1004 Desktop) is permitted only when the transaction is a one-unit property (including ADU and PUD units), principal residence, purchase (including new construction), the LTV ratio is less than or equal to 90%, and the DU loan casefile receives an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
business_rules.conditions: Lender elects Form 1004 Desktop option offered by DU
business_rules.consequences: If criteria met, desktop appraisal is eligible; if not met, desktop appraisal not permitted and another appraisal type is required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: To be eligible for a desktop appraisal, transactions must meet the following criteria: one-unit property (including those with an ADU and units in a PUD), principal residence, purchase transaction (including new construction), LTV ratio less than or equal to 90%, and DU loan caseﬁle that receives an Approve/Eligible recommendation.

examples: Borrower purchases a one-unit primary residence at 90% LTV and DU returns Approve/Eligible → Form 1004 Desktop is permitted.
Borrower purchases a one-unit primary residence at 92% LTV with DU Approve/Eligible → desktop appraisal is not permitted due to LTV > 90%.
Borrower purchases a PUD unit as a principal residence at 80% LTV but DU returns Ineligible → desktop appraisal is not permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Require different appraisal type; delivery defect if not corrected

examples: Desktop appraisal used for a low-risk refinance where permitted
Desktop appraisal completed with required data sources and exhibits
Desktop appraisal rejected because transaction/property type is ineligible

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_005 permits desktop appraisal using Form 1004 Desktop under certain eligibility criteria. BR_031_004 states Form 1004 Desktop is a required form that must be completed using UAD Appendix D when reporting appraisal results. Therefore, to validly use the desktop appraisal option, the appraisal must meet BR_031_004 form/spec requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_004 fails, the lender may have an otherwise-eligible desktop appraisal transaction but cannot successfully submit/accept the appraisal in required systems, leading to rework and potential loss of the desktop appraisal pathway.

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_005 requires the DU loan casefile receive Approve/Eligible. BR_006_006 governs DU submission timing and ensures the underwriting DU run occurs before closing. Without DU submission/run, the Approve/Eligible condition cannot be satisfied or evidenced.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_006_006 fails (first DU submission after closing), the lender cannot substantiate desktop appraisal eligibility tied to DU outcomes, and the loan may be defective/ineligible for delivery as DU underwritten.

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_010 enforces DU resubmission under defined DTI change triggers after changes (rate increase, new debt, verified income lower). Since BR_033_005 requires DU Approve/Eligible, the valid DU outcome must reflect updated data when triggers occur; otherwise the desktop appraisal decision is based on stale/inaccurate DU findings.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_010 is not followed, the lender may incorrectly proceed with a desktop appraisal based on an outdated Approve/Eligible. This creates underwriting support defects and can lead to appraisal type ineligibility and delivery remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: High-Balance MI Required Over 80% LTV and Max 95%
business_rules.description: Mortgage insurance coverage is required for high-balance mortgage loans when the LTV ratio is greater than 80%. Financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance is permitted, but the maximum gross LTV after including the financed premium cannot exceed 95%.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is a high-balance mortgage loan and LTV ratio > 80%
business_rules.consequences: If MI obtained and gross LTV ≤ 95%, eligible; if no MI or gross LTV > 95%, ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Mortgage Insurance Requirements Mortgage insurance coverage is required for high-balance mortgage loans with LTV ratios greater than 80%. Financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance is permitted; however, the maximum gross LTV (after the inclusion of the ﬁnanced premium) cannot exceed 95%.

examples: High-balance purchase at 85% LTV: borrower must have MI coverage; otherwise the loan is not eligible.
High-balance loan at 94% LTV plus financed MI premium that pushes gross LTV to 96%: not eligible (exceeds 95%).
High-balance loan at 79.9% LTV: MI not required under this specific requirement (other rules may still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: High-balance loan in a designated high-cost county within permitted limits
High-balance loan requiring mortgage insurance due to LTV thresholds
High-balance loan with special delivery limitations and pricing requirements

business_rules.rule_name: DU returns Ineligible for bankruptcy within 2/4 years or foreclosure within 7 years
business_rules.description: DU issues an Ineligible recommendation (loan ineligible for delivery) when bankruptcy/foreclosure seasoning thresholds are not met: Chapter 13 discharged within last 2 years, dismissed within last 4 years, or filed but neither discharged nor dismissed within last 4 years; non–Chapter 13 filed/discharged/dismissed within last 4 years; and foreclosure reported within 7 years prior to credit report date.
business_rules.conditions: When DU identifies bankruptcy or foreclosure in the credit report data for casefile
business_rules.consequences: DU returns Ineligible and loan casefile is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: None in base rule; separate DU override instructions exist for inaccuracies/EC

source_reference.source_text: If a Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged within the last two years, dismissed within the last four years, or ﬁled but neither discharged nor dismissed within the last four years, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. If a non-Chapter 13 bankruptcy was ﬁled, discharged, or dismissed within the last four years, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. ... If a foreclosure was reported within the seven-year period prior to the credit report date, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Chapter 13 discharged 18 months before new loan disbursement: DU returns Ineligible.
Chapter 7 discharged 3 years before disbursement: DU returns Ineligible.
Foreclosure tradeline shows foreclosure reported 6 years before credit report date: DU returns Ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: DU Ineligible; loan ineligible for delivery

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_010 is a DU-issued Ineligible recommendation logic. BR_006_006 governs when DU is first submitted for underwriting. Operationally, you must run DU to receive and act on BR_001_010 outcomes before closing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not submitted early, bankruptcy/foreclosure seasoning issues may be discovered late, causing denial after borrower has incurred costs or causing closing disruption.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Loans Must Be Underwritten Through DU
business_rules.description: Loans secured by manufactured homes (including MH Advantage properties) must be underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU). If DU underwriting is not used, the loan does not meet this requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is a manufactured home, including MH Advantage
business_rules.consequences: If met, underwriting method is acceptable; if not, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Underwriting and DU Requirements Loans secured by manufactured homes (including MH Advantage properties) must be underwritten through DU.

examples: Loan secured by manufactured home underwritten manually (no DU casefile): not permitted; must run through DU.
Loan secured by MH Advantage property: must be underwritten through DU even if lender otherwise uses manual underwriting.
Loan initially entered as site-built but determined to be manufactured: lender must treat as manufactured and ensure DU underwriting is used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: DU value acceptance ineligible when price or value is at least $1,000,000
business_rules.description: DU will not consider a value acceptance offer for transactions where either the purchase price or the estimated value provided to DU is $1,000,000 or more. If the transaction meets this ineligible criterion, an appraisal will be required under DU’s standard appraisal messaging and the lender cannot rely on value acceptance.
business_rules.conditions: DU casefile is evaluated for value acceptance and price/value inputs are provided
business_rules.consequences: If ≥$1,000,000, value acceptance not offered; lender must obtain appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the $1,000,000 threshold

source_reference.source_text: The following transactions are not eligible for a value acceptance oﬀer: two- to four-unit properties; co-op units and manufactured homes; proposed construction; construction-to-permanent loans (single-close and two-close); HomeStyle Renovation and HomeStyle Refresh loans; leasehold properties; Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans; community land trusts or other properties with resale price restrictions, which include loan caseﬁles using the Aﬀordable LTV feature; transactions where either the purchase price or estimated value provided to DU is $1,000,000 or more;

examples: Purchase price $1,250,000 with estimated value $1,260,000 → DU cannot offer value acceptance; appraisal required.
Purchase price $999,999 but estimated value entered as $1,000,000 → ineligible for value acceptance; appraisal required.
Purchase price $850,000 and estimated value $900,000 → this rule does not block value acceptance (other ineligibilities may still apply).
DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_006 describes when DU will not consider value acceptance (≥$1,000,000) and will require an appraisal. That determination is an output of the DU evaluation process, which necessarily follows DU submission governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not run timely, the lender may proceed too far without knowing value acceptance is unavailable, causing late appraisal ordering, closing delays, or last-minute underwriting changes.

business_rules.rule_name: DU non-occupant max LTV 95% (CLTV 105% with CS)
business_rules.description: For DU-underwritten loan casefiles that include a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower who will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios may not exceed 95%. If a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix.
business_rules.conditions: DU loan casefile includes a non-occupant guarantor/co-signer/co-borrower
business_rules.consequences: If ratios within limits, loan may be delivered; if exceeded, ineligible as DU loan
business_rules.exceptions: Community Seconds allows CLTV up to 105% where Eligibility Matrix permits

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, LTV Ratio Requirements for Loan Casefiles Underwritten through DU
source_reference.source_text: For DU loan caseﬁles with a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower who will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratio may not exceed 95% (unless a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, in which case the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix .

examples: DU purchase with non-occupant co-borrower: LTV 97%; fails because maximum is 95% for DU casefiles with non-occupant borrower.
DU purchase with Community Seconds: LTV 95% and CLTV 105%; acceptable if Eligibility Matrix permits CLTV up to 105%.
DU refinance: HCLTV 96% with non-occupant co-borrower; fails because max HCLTV is 95% absent Community Seconds exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 95% cap and explicit Community Seconds CLTV 105% exception are stated for DU casefiles.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae as a DU loan

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_007 begins "For DU-underwritten loan casefiles..." meaning its applicability and enforcement are tied to DU usage and casefile evaluation governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the loan is treated as DU-underwritten without proper DU submission timing/compliance, the lender may incorrectly apply or overlook the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV constraints, leading to ineligible DU delivery.
