business_rules.rule_name: Flow loan age must be 6 months or less from first payment date
business_rules.description: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a flow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old. The loan age is measured from the first payment date to the “Purchase Ready” date for whole loans or to the MBS pool issue date for MBS loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to flow whole loans or MBS loans being delivered for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.consequences: If older than 6 months by the applicable measurement, loan is not eligible for purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this section.

source_reference.source_text: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans).

examples: Whole loan: first payment date 01/01; Purchase Ready date 06/15 → within 6 months → eligible on age.
MBS loan: first payment date 01/01; pool issue date 07/01 → exceeds 6 months → ineligible on age.
Whole loan: first payment date 03/01; Purchase Ready date 08/31 → exceeds 6 months by 1 day → ineligible unless delivery date changes.
Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_001’s age test is defined as a function of first payment date to Purchase Ready date (whole loans) or MBS pool issue date (MBS). Because BR_038_009 constrains the allowed first payment date timing for whole loan delivery and clarifies the start point for CTP loans, BR_038_009 must be satisfied/known before a reliable BR_002_001 age computation can be performed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is wrong or missing, BR_002_001 can incorrectly classify loans as older/newer than six months, causing wrongful delivery blocks or post-delivery repurchase/rejection exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Modified loan limit uses original amount; over current limit is ineligible
business_rules.description: Loan limits for modified loans must be evaluated using the original loan amount, not the unpaid principal balance at the time of modification or acquisition. A modified loan with an original loan amount that exceeds the current applicable loan limit is not eligible for purchase, even if its balance at the time of modification is at or below the current applicable loan limit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a loan delivered to Fannie Mae has been modified prior to acquisition.
business_rules.consequences: If original loan amount exceeds current loan limit, loan is not eligible for purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), Modified Loans / Loan limits for modified loans
source_reference.source_text: Loan limits for modiﬁed loans are based on the original loan amount of the loan and not on the unpaid principal balance of the loan at the time of modiﬁcation or acquisition by Fannie Mae. A modiﬁed loan with an original loan amount exceeding the current loan limit is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae, even though the balance at the time of the modiﬁcation may be at or below the current applicable loan limit.

examples: Original loan amount $800,000 in a baseline area; later modified down to $720,000 UPB → still ineligible if current limit is $766,550 and original exceeded applicable limit at time of evaluation.
Original loan amount exceeds current local high-balance limit; modification reduces UPB below limit → still ineligible due to original amount test.
Original loan amount within current limit; modification changes rate/term only → loan may remain eligible if all other requirements are met.
Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: Total points and fees cap: 3% ATR covered; 5% ATR exempt
business_rules.description: Total points and fees and total loan amount must be calculated in accordance with Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.32). For ATR Covered Loans, total points and fees may not exceed 3% of the total loan amount (or a different amount per QM provisions); a timely cure payment under 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii) satisfies the requirement. For ATR Exempt Loans, total points and fees may not exceed 5% of the total loan amount, with possible adjustments (permitted reduction under 12 CFR § 1026.31(h) or, for loans not subject to TILA, restitution to borrower of at least the portion exceeding 5% at closing).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans with application dates on/after 2014-01-10 under ATR framework.
business_rules.consequences: Exceeding the applicable cap makes loan ineligible unless cured/adjusted as permitted.
business_rules.exceptions: ATR covered: cure payment allowed; ATR exempt: reduction or restitution allowed.

source_reference.source_text: Points and Fees Limitations. For purposes of these requirements, “total points and fees” and “total loan amount” must be calculated in accordance with Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.32). ATR Covered Loans: Total points and fees may not exceed 3% of the total loan amount or such diﬀerent amount in accordance with the qualiﬁed mortgage provisions of Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(i)). If a lender makes a cure payment in the amount and by the time required by 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii), such loan satisﬁes this requirement. ATR Exempt Loans: Total points and fees may not exceed 5% of the total loan amount.

examples: ATR covered purchase, total loan amount $400,000: max points/fees = $12,000 (3%); $12,500 would violate unless cured per Reg Z.
ATR exempt loan, total loan amount $200,000: max points/fees = $10,000 (5%); $10,800 requires permitted reduction/restitution to meet cap.
ATR covered loan initially at 3.2% points/fees: lender makes cure payment per 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii) within required timeframe → satisfies this requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligible; potential repurchase demand for breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: APR cannot exceed APOR by more than 2.25% (ATR covered) or 6.5% (exempt)
business_rules.description: For loans with application dates on and after July 1, 2021, the APR-APOR spread is the maximum difference by which the loan’s APR cannot exceed the APOR. ATR Covered Loans: spread may not exceed 2.25% (or different amount per Revised General QM Rule) calculated per that rule; for ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less, APR must be calculated using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the first five years after the first payment is due. ATR Exempt Loans: spread may not exceed 6.5% (or more calculated per Revised General QM rule).
business_rules.conditions: Applies for application dates on/after 2021-07-01; evaluate APR/APOR per QM rule.
business_rules.consequences: If spread exceeds limit, loan fails ATR/QM eligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition.
business_rules.exceptions: Different amounts may apply if specified by the Revised General QM Rule.

source_reference.source_text: APR-APOR Spread Limitations. Eﬀective for loans with applications dates on and after July 1, 2021, the APR-APOR spread is the maximum diﬀerence by which the loan's APR cannot exceed the APOR. ... ATR Covered Loans: The spread may not exceed 2.25% or a diﬀerent amount as speciﬁed in the Revised General QM Rule, calculated in accordance with the provisions of that rule. Note: For ARMs with initial ﬁxed periods of ﬁve years or less, the APR must be calculated using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the ﬁrst ﬁve years after the ﬁrst payment is due. ATR Exempt Loans: The spread may not exceed 6.5% or more calculated in accordance with the Revised General QM rule.

examples: Fixed-rate ATR covered loan: APR 7.10%, APOR 5.00% → spread 2.10% ≤ 2.25% → passes spread test.
ATR covered ARM with initial fixed period 5 years: APR must be computed using max rate possible in first 5 years; if computed spread becomes 2.40% → fails 2.25% cap.
ATR exempt loan: APR 10.25%, APOR 3.50% → spread 6.75% > 6.5% → fails unless QM rule provides a different allowable amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligible; potential repurchase demand for ATR/QM breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

dependencies.rationale: Both rules are used to establish ATR/QM-related eligibility for delivery/purchase: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_003 constrains points and fees, while BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 constrains pricing via APR/APOR spread. Many compliance workflows run both checks together and treat failure of either as a QM/ATR eligibility failure (and likely delivery ineligibility per investor policy).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If one of the two checks is omitted, the lender may mistakenly treat a loan as ATR/QM-compliant and proceed to delivery. This increases repurchase exposure and post-purchase compliance defect risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_GENERATES_UNDERWRITING_DECISION_PROCESS_035_004 requires qualifying borrowers using an ARM qualifying rate for ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 requires APR calculation for such ARMs using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the first five years (for the APR/APOR spread test). For these products, underwriting qualification and QM pricing compliance must both be satisfied; workflows should navigate users through both checks for the same ARM feature set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender performs only one of the two ARM-specific controls, it may understate risk (qualify too easily) or misclassify QM status (APR/APOR test incorrect), creating eligibility failures and post-purchase compliance findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 states a special APR calculation rule for 'ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less'. Correctly establishing the initial fixed period in months/years (per BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_038_006) is necessary to decide whether that APR rule is triggered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the ARM fixed period is mis-specified, the lender may compute APR using the wrong method for QM/APR-APOR testing, causing false QM compliance conclusions and potential ineligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition.

business_rules.rule_name: Original loan term max 30 years and minimum 85 months (with construction exception)
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans with original terms up to 30 years. The term of a first mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the first payment. The minimum original term is 85 months (subject to applicable committing and delivery requirements). Exception/clarification: for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, the loan term must not exceed 30 years after conversion to permanent financing (per applicable guidance). If the term is outside these limits, the loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to first-lien mortgage original term evaluation for eligibility and delivery.
business_rules.consequences: If term exceeds maximum or is below 85 months, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.exceptions: Single-closing construction-to-permanent: ignore construction period; still ≤30 years post-conversion.

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that have original terms up to 30 years. The term of a ﬁrst mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the ﬁrst payment. The minimum original term is 85 months, subject to applicable committing and delivery requirements for whole loans and loans in MBS. Exception: The only exception to these requirements is for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which must have a loan term not exceeding 30 years after conversion to permanent ﬁnancing (disregarding the construction period).

Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that have original terms up to 30 years. The term of a ﬁrst mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the ﬁrst payment. The minimum original term is 85 months... Exception: The only exception to these requirements is for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which must have a loan term not exceeding 30 years after conversion to permanent ﬁnancing (disregarding the construction period).

examples: Standard purchase: first payment due 06/01; term cannot extend past 05/01 thirty years later → if maturity exceeds that, ineligible.
Loan with original term 60 months → below 85-month minimum → ineligible for delivery.
Single-closing construction-to-permanent: 12-month construction + 30-year permanent; permanent term is 30 years post-conversion → eligible on term under exception.
Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules enforce the same core loan term eligibility boundaries for Fannie Mae acquisition: original terms up to 30 years; the term may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date one month prior to the first payment date; and the minimum original term is 85 months. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 includes an additional explicit construction-to-permanent (single-close) exception clarification. Because the added exception does not change the general rule but clarifies its application to a special case, the rules are treated as duplicates with a completeness variation (semantic not scored at full 40).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same maximum and minimum term constraints and adds the explicit single-closing construction-to-permanent exception details, which increases testability and reduces ambiguity.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_005 repeats the 30-year test relative to a date one month prior to the first payment and adds a construction-to-permanent clarification. BR_038_009 governs permissible first payment timing and defines the conversion start point for the 2‑month period. A compliant first payment date (and correct conversion timing) must exist before evaluating BR_002_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is not satisfied, BR_002_005 eligibility may be determined incorrectly. This can create false eligibility (loan delivered but later rejected) or false ineligibility (loan unnecessarily removed from delivery pipeline).

business_rules.rule_name: Seller Second Mortgage Rate 2% Below Market Counts as Concession
business_rules.description: If the interest rate for subordinate financing provided by the property seller is more than 2% below current standard rates for second mortgages, the subordinate financing must be treated as a sales concession and the subordinate financing amount must be deducted from the sales price.
business_rules.conditions: Subordinate financing is provided by the property seller for the transaction
business_rules.consequences: If met, sales price is reduced for LTV/CLTV; if violated, incorrect ratios/eligibility
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the interest rate for financing provided by the property seller is more than 2% below current standard rates for second mortgages, the subordinate financing must be considered a sales concession and the subordinate financing amount must be deducted from the sales price.

examples: Market second-mortgage rate is 8.00%; seller provides 5.75% (2.25% below). Subordinate amount must be deducted from sales price for ratio calculations.
Market is 7.00%; seller provides 5.10% (1.90% below). This rule does not trigger because it is not more than 2% below.
Seller provides a $20,000 subordinate note at >2% below market; lender reduces the effective sales price by $20,000 when calculating LTV/CLTV.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: Subordinate Balloon Under 5 Years Is Unacceptable Except Employer
business_rules.description: Subordinate financing that does not fully amortize under a level monthly payment plan where the maturity or balloon payment date is less than five years after the note date of the new first mortgage is unacceptable, except for employer subordinate financing that has deferred payments. Fannie Mae notes it may accept these terms when the subordinate debt is minimal relative to borrower financial assets and/or credit profile.
business_rules.conditions: Subordinate financing exists and does not qualify as eligible Community Seconds
business_rules.consequences: If term is unacceptable, loan is not eligible unless exception/acceptance applies
business_rules.exceptions: Employer subordinate financing with deferred payments; possible acceptance if debt minimal

source_reference.source_text:
Subordinate financing that does not fully amortize under a level monthly payment plan where the maturity or balloon payment date is less than five years after the note date of the new first mortgage (with the exception of employer subordinate financing that has deferred payments).
Note: Fannie Mae will accept these subordinate financing terms when the amount of the subordinate debt is minimal relative to the borrower's financial assets and/or credit profile.

examples: Purchase loan note date 2026-06-01; subordinate balloon due 2029-05-01 (<5 years) and not employer deferred—unacceptable.
Employer-provided subordinate loan defers payments and balloons in 3 years—allowed under the stated exception.

Borrower has very high assets and strong credit; subordinate balloon in 4 years may be accepted per the note if debt is minimal relative to assets/credit profile (requires lender judgment and documentation).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: Late fee up to $500 after 3 business days demand
business_rules.description: If Seller fails to transfer all funds required by the agreement within three (3) Business Days of Servicer’s demand, Servicer may (in its sole discretion) charge Seller a late fee of up to $500.00 per mortgage loan.
business_rules.conditions: When Servicer issues a demand and Seller does not transfer required funds within 3 Business Days
business_rules.consequences: If late, Servicer may assess up to $500 per loan; timely transfer avoids the fee exposure
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: (b) Late Fee . If Seller fails to transfer any and all funds required pursuant to this Agreement within three (3) Business Days of demand from Servicer, then Servicer may, in its sole discretion, charge Seller a late fee of up to $500.00 per mortgage loan.

examples: Servicer demands transfer of custodial funds on Monday; if funds not transferred by Thursday (3 Business Days), Servicer may assess up to $500 per affected loan.
If 20 loans are impacted by late transfer, fee exposure can be up to 20 × $500 = $10,000.
If Seller transfers all demanded funds within 2 Business Days, Servicer has no basis under this clause to assess the late fee.
Contract requires record retention and examination of mortgage files for all delivered loans.
Contract defines remedies available to Fannie Mae for material defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_PROCESS_003_003 requires forwarding mortgagor funds within 1 business day after receipt. BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_002 permits a late fee if required funds are not transferred within 3 business days of Servicer’s demand. In practice, complying with the 1-day forwarding rule generally keeps the Seller from reaching the demand-and-late-fee stage, making these rules sequentially aligned in funds-handling workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Seller fails to forward funds promptly, the Servicer may issue a demand; missing the 3-day post-demand transfer window can trigger per-loan fees and escalate disputes.

business_rules.rule_name: Late document fee $100 monthly after 6 months from purchase
business_rules.description: Servicer may charge Seller a Late Document Fee equal to $100.00 per Mortgage Loan per month if all final documents specified in the “Final Documents” section of the Servicing Transfer Instructions are not delivered to Servicer or the designated document custodian within six (6) months of the Purchase Date. The fee begins accruing on the first day of the seventh month after the Purchase Date. Servicer must not impose the fee if the failure to deliver an original loan document is caused by the recording office’s failure to record and return the document, provided Seller has exercised and continues to exercise reasonable good faith efforts.
business_rules.conditions: When final documents are not delivered within 6 months after Purchase Date under servicing transfer
business_rules.consequences: Fee accrues at $100/loan/month starting month 7; timely delivery avoids fee accrual
business_rules.exceptions: No fee if recording office delay and Seller shows good faith efforts

source_reference.source_text: 7.3 Late Fee . Servicer shall be entitled to charge Seller a fee (" Late Document Fee ") equal to $100.00 per Mortgage Loan per month if all ﬁnal documents speciﬁed in the “Final Documents” section of the Servicing Transfer Instructions have not been delivered to Servicer or the designated document custodian within six (6) months of the Purchase Date. Such Late Document Fee shall begin to accrue on the ﬁrst (1st) day of the seventh (7th) month after the Purchase Date. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Servicer shall not impose a Late Document Fee if Seller's failure to deliver an original loan document is caused by the failure of the applicable recording oﬃce to record and return the loan document, provided Seller, in Servicer's reasonable judgment, has exercised and continues to exercise reasonable good faith eﬀorts to fulﬁll its obligation hereunder.

examples: Purchase Date is Jan 15; final documents must be delivered by Jul 15. If not delivered, fee accrues starting Aug 1 at $100 per loan per month.
Seller is missing 12 original notes at month 7; monthly fee exposure is 12 × $100 = $1,200 per month until cured (unless exception applies).
Recording office has not returned a recorded mortgage; Seller provides evidence of repeated follow-up and tracking, so Servicer must not impose the fee.
Contract requires record retention and examination of mortgage files for all delivered loans.
Contract defines remedies available to Fannie Mae for material defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_COMPLIANCE_003_001 requires the Seller to comply with Servicing Transfer Instructions and complete required forms/items. BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_004 imposes a monthly fee if the final documents specified in those instructions are not delivered within six months. Operationally, the fee rule is triggered by failure/non-performance of the document delivery obligations governed by the transfer instructions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Seller does not follow the Servicing Transfer Instructions document package requirements, the Seller faces ongoing fees and escalations, and the Servicer/custodian may not have a complete collateral file, increasing operational and legal risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Flood insurance required for SFHA or CBRS or OPA zones
business_rules.description: Flood insurance coverage is required when the loan is secured by a property located in a Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA) or located in a Coastal Barrier Resources System (CBRS) or Otherwise Protected Area (OPA). The lender/servicer must determine whether the property is in an SFHA/CBRS/OPA using the FEMA Standard Flood Hazard Determination form. Flood zones beginning with "A" or "V" are SFHAs. If the property is within a CBRS or OPA, flood insurance is required regardless of SFHA status.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is secured by property; flood zone/CBRS/OPA determination is performed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: obtain/maintain flood coverage; Violated: delivery defect/ineligibility risk
business_rules.exceptions: CBRS/OPA requires flood regardless of SFHA status

source_reference.section_id: B7-3-06, Determining if a Property Requires Flood Insurance
source_reference.source_text: The lender and servicer must ensure that the subject property is adequately protected by ﬂood insurance when required. Flood insurance coverage is required when a loan is secured by a property located in a Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA), or a Coastal Barrier Resources System (CBRS) or Otherwise Protected Area (OPA). The lender and servicer must determine whether the property is located in an SFHA, a CBRS, or an OPA by using the FEMA Standard Flood Hazard Determination form. All ﬂood zones beginning with the letter "A" or "V" are considered SFHAs. Note: If the subject property is located within a CBRS or an OPA, ﬂood insurance is required regardless of whether the property is located in an SFHA.

examples: Property in Zone AE (SFHA) → flood insurance required.
Property in Zone X but within CBRS → flood insurance still required.
Property in Zone VE (SFHA) → flood insurance required and must meet Guide coverage rules.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must/required language; SFHA defined by A/V zone prefixes; CBRS/OPA override stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

business_rules.rule_name: Properties with condition rating C6 are ineligible until repaired to C5
business_rules.description: If any portion of the dwelling is rated C6, the whole dwelling must be rated C6. Loans secured by properties with a condition rating of C6 are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Deficiencies impacting safety, soundness, or structural integrity must be repaired with a resulting minimum condition rating of C5 prior to sale of the loan.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is secured by a property where appraisal indicates any C6 condition
business_rules.consequences: If repaired and re-rated to at least C5, loan may be eligible; otherwise ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: However, the C6 rating is an exception because it indicates that the property is impacted by one or more deﬁciencies that negatively aﬀect the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property. As a result, if any portion of the dwelling is rated a C6, the whole dwelling must be rated a C6. Loans secured by properties with a condition rating of C6 are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Any deﬁciencies impacting the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property must be repaired with a resulting minimum condition rating of C5 prior to sale of the loan.

examples: Appraisal notes structural instability in one area and rates that portion C6; entire dwelling must be rated C6 and the loan cannot be sold until repairs yield at least C5.
Property has safety deficiency (exposed wiring) resulting in C6; repairs completed and appraisal update supports C5; loan becomes eligible for sale.
Underwriter attempts to treat one-room issue as minor while appraiser assigned C6; loan remains ineligible until repaired and re-rated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster damage must be insured minor or repaired before delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.description: Before delivery when a property may have been damaged by a disaster, the lender is expected to take prudent and reasonable actions to determine whether the property condition has materially changed and determine if an inspection and/or new appraisal is needed. If damage does not affect safety, soundness, or structural integrity and repair items are covered by insurance, the lender may deliver the loan but must obtain professional repair cost estimates and ensure sufficient funds are available for the borrower’s benefit to guarantee completion of repairs. If damage is uninsured or affects safety, soundness, or structural integrity, the property must be repaired before the loan is delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Property may have disaster damage at any time through end of delivery process
business_rules.consequences: If insured minor damage with estimates/funds, delivery allowed; otherwise repairs required
business_rules.exceptions: Requirements do not apply to high LTV refinance loans (per note in section)

source_reference.source_text: If the property has been damaged and the damage does not aﬀect the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property and the repair items are covered by insurance, the lender may deliver the loan to Fannie Mae. In these circumstances, the lender must obtain documentation of the professional estimates of the repair costs and must ensure that suﬃcient funds are available for the borrower's beneﬁt to guarantee the completion of the repairs. If the property was damaged and the damage is uninsured or the damage aﬀects the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of the property, the property must be repaired before the loan is delivered to Fannie Mae.

examples: Hail damage to siding is insured and does not affect structural integrity; lender obtains contractor estimate and holds/ensures funds for repairs, then may deliver the loan.
Flood damage is uninsured and affects safety/soundness; property must be fully repaired before delivery to Fannie Mae.
Condo unit appears undamaged but building is impacted; lender must assess unit and building condition before delivery and may need inspection/appraisal update.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

business_rules.rule_name: Tank leak over 0.05 gallons per hour is unacceptable
business_rules.description: A property is considered to have an unacceptable environmental hazard if it has documented soil or groundwater contamination and/or a documented tank leak leaking at more than 0.05 gallons per hour (National Fire Protection Association standard). Such unacceptable environmental hazards generally result in the project being ineligible, making the loan ineligible for delivery absent permitted remediation under related guidance.
business_rules.conditions: When environmental assessment identifies contamination and tank leak rate >0.05 gph
business_rules.consequences: Loan is ineligible for delivery/purchase unless remedial action qualifies; otherwise deny
business_rules.exceptions: Remedial action may be possible per B4-2.1-06

source_reference.source_text: The existence of one or more unacceptable environmental hazards generally will result in a project being ineligible. In addition, some properties that fail to meet a particular standard may be corrected through remedial action. See B4-2.1-06, Remedial Actions for Environmental Hazard Assessments Below Standards . ... a property that has documented soil or groundwater contamination and/or a documented tank leak that is leaking at more than 0.05 gallons per hour (which is the National Fire Protection Association’s standard)

examples: Phase II identifies tank leak at 0.06 gph and petroleum staining; property is unacceptable and loan must not be delivered unless allowed remedial action is completed and documented.
Environmental report shows tank leak 0.049 gph with no contamination; this specific threshold is not exceeded, but lender must still evaluate for other unacceptable hazards.
Tank leak rate is unknown but contamination is documented; lender must obtain sufficient testing/sampling to determine whether standards are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

business_rules.rule_name: Soil metals over limits (chromium 100 ppm) are unacceptable
business_rules.description: A property is an unacceptable environmental hazard if soil sampling shows metals concentrations above specified parts-per-million (ppm) limits. If exceeded, the project is generally ineligible and the related loan secured by the property is ineligible for delivery unless permitted remedial action is completed and acceptable under related policy.
business_rules.conditions: When soil sampling results report metals concentrations in ppm
business_rules.consequences: If any listed metal exceeds its limit, property is unacceptable; loan generally ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Remedial action may be possible per B4-2.1-06

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-05, Examples of Unacceptable Environmental Hazards
source_reference.source_text: a property with soil sampling that has values for metal in excess of the following concentration limits in parts per million (ppm): • chromium: 100 ppm • arsenic: 20 ppm • zinc: 350 ppm • cadmium: 3 ppm • lead: 100 ppm • nickel: 100 ppm • copper: 170 ppm • selenium: 20 ppm

examples: Soil test shows lead 145 ppm (limit 100 ppm); property is an unacceptable hazard and loan cannot be delivered unless successfully remediated under applicable guidance.
Soil test shows arsenic 19 ppm (limit 20 ppm) and chromium 98 ppm (limit 100 ppm); not an automatic unacceptable hazard under these metal thresholds, but lender must still review other hazards.
Soil test shows cadmium 3.1 ppm (limit 3 ppm); exceeds threshold and triggers unacceptable environmental hazard determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_040_001 addresses contamination/tank leaks above a leak-rate threshold. BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_040_002 addresses metals concentration ppm thresholds from soil sampling. Both govern unacceptable environmental hazards that generally make the project/loan ineligible absent acceptable remediation. As a practical compliance workflow, lenders evaluate these hazards together when environmental risk is identified.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a lender runs only one hazard test, it may miss an independent ineligibility trigger and deliver a loan secured by an unacceptable property condition, increasing enforcement and repurchase risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Radon above 4 pCi/L beyond HOA capacity is unacceptable
business_rules.description: A property with high radon levels (e.g., above 4 picocuries per liter) is an unacceptable environmental hazard when it can be corrected only through large capital improvements or extensive ongoing maintenance programs that are beyond the financial or technical abilities of the HOA or co-op corporation for the project. Unacceptable hazards generally make the project (and thus the secured loan) ineligible.
business_rules.conditions: When radon testing shows >4 pCi/L and remediation exceeds HOA/co-op capability
business_rules.consequences: Property/project generally ineligible; loan cannot be delivered unless corrected acceptably
business_rules.exceptions: If correctable without large capital/extensive maintenance and within HOA capability

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-05, Examples of Unacceptable Environmental Hazards
source_reference.source_text: a property with high radon levels (e.g., above four picocuries per liter) that can be corrected only through large capital improvements or extensive ongoing maintenance programs that are beyond the ﬁnancial or technical abilities of the HOA or co-op corporation for the project

examples: Condo project radon tests average 6.0 pCi/L and engineer report requires multi-year building-wide ventilation retrofits beyond HOA budget; property is unacceptable and loan is ineligible.
Co-op unit radon at 4.2 pCi/L but mitigation is a standard, affordable sub-slab depressurization within HOA ability; may be eligible if remediation is completed and documented per policy.
Radon test indicates elevated levels but no remediation plan exists; lender cannot assume eligibility and must obtain sufficient information on corrective feasibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_040_001 and BR_IS_SECURED_BY_ELIGIBILITY_040_005 both represent unacceptable environmental hazards that can drive ineligibility. They are typically executed as parallel checks in an environmental hazard decision tree, supporting a combined “environmental clearance” outcome.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Omitting either hazard check can allow unacceptable environmental conditions to slip through, leading to ineligible delivery, investor findings, and potential remediation/curtailment costs.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_CONSTRAINT_040_002 defines unacceptable hazard conditions based on metals concentrations. BR_IS_SECURED_BY_ELIGIBILITY_040_005 defines unacceptable hazard conditions based on radon remediation feasibility. Together they contribute to a comprehensive environmental eligibility determination; remediation or denial paths can be triggered by either.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one environmental test is performed, the lender risks delivering loans secured by properties with another untested unacceptable hazard, creating compliance defects and remediation burdens.

business_rules.rule_name: Gifted funds require 5% borrower funds when LTV over 80%
business_rules.description: When a transaction includes gifts and the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is greater than 80% for a two- to four-unit principal residence or a second home, the borrower must make a minimum 5% contribution from the borrower’s own funds. After the 5% minimum contribution has been met, gifts can supplement down payment, closing costs, and reserves.
business_rules.conditions: Gift funds used; LTV/CLTV/HCLTV > 80%; 2-4 unit PR or second home transaction
business_rules.consequences: Meets: gift funds allowed after 5% borrower contribution; Violated: loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: HomeReady may have separate minimum contribution rules (cross-reference)

source_reference.source_text: Greater than 80% Two- to four-unit principal residence Second home The borrower must make a 5% minimum borrower contribution from their own funds. 1 After the minimum borrower contribution has been met, gifts can be used to supplement the down payment, closing costs, and reserves. See B5-6-02, HomeReady Mortgage Underwriting Methods and Requirements , for HomeReady mortgage minimum borrower contribution and down payment requirements.

examples: 85% LTV second home: borrower must contribute at least 5% from own funds; remaining funds to close can be gifted.
90% LTV 3-unit principal residence: borrower contributes 5% from own verified funds; gift covers closing costs/reserves.
75% LTV 2-unit principal residence: minimum borrower contribution not required under this table row; gift may cover all funds needed.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 5% threshold tied to LTV band and property/occupancy types; HomeReady called out as separate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: Loan uses a $15,000 gift from a parent for down payment.
Loan uses documented gift funds transferred prior to closing.

dependencies.rationale: BR_USES_DOWN_PAYMENT_SOURCE_CONSTRAINT_004_008 requires applying a minimum borrower contribution when the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV exceeds 80%. When subordinate financing includes a HELOC, HCLTV must include the full HELOC amount (even if undrawn) and be calculated using the specified numerator/denominator. Without that calculation, the workflow cannot determine whether the 80% trigger is met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If HCLTV is not calculated correctly (e.g., HELOC undrawn amount omitted), the lender may incorrectly allow gifts without the required borrower contribution, resulting in delivery ineligibility and downstream repurchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: If seller-provided subordinate financing must be treated as a sales concession and deducted from sales price, the denominator used to determine LTV/CLTV/HCLTV may decrease, potentially pushing ratios above 80% and triggering the 5% borrower contribution requirement. Therefore, concession adjustment should be applied before evaluating whether BR_USES_DOWN_PAYMENT_SOURCE_CONSTRAINT_004_008 is triggered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to adjust sales price can mis-evaluate the 80% threshold, resulting in (a) allowing gifts without the required minimum borrower contribution, or (b) applying the requirement when not actually triggered after correct denominator selection.

dependencies.rationale: BR_USES_DOWN_PAYMENT_SOURCE_CONSTRAINT_004_008 regulates how gifts may be used once gifts are allowed (and when ratios exceed 80%). BR_USES_DOWN_PAYMENT_SOURCE_PROHIBITION_004_007 determines whether gifts are allowed at all based on occupancy. If gifts are prohibited (investment property), the minimum contribution logic becomes irrelevant because gift funds cannot be used.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If occupancy-based gift eligibility is not evaluated first, the system may mistakenly apply only the 5% contribution rule and allow gifts on an investment property, creating an outright eligibility violation.

business_rules.rule_name: Cash-out refi needs 12-month mortgage age and 6-month title
business_rules.description: For a cash-out refinance, if an existing first mortgage is being paid off, it must be at least 12 months old at the time of refinance (measured note date of existing loan to note date of new loan), with exceptions for paying off subordinate liens and buying out a co-owner under a legal agreement. Additionally, at least one borrower must have been on title for at least six months prior to the disbursement date of the new loan, with stated exceptions (inheritance/legal award, delayed financing exception, and counting time held by borrower-controlled LLC or inter vivos revocable trust under conditions).
business_rules.conditions: Loan purpose is cash-out refinance; payoff of existing first; title history reviewed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: cash-out refinance eligibility satisfied; Violated: cash-out refinance ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: No title wait for inheritance/legal award; delayed financing; LLC/trust time counts

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-03, Eligibility Requirements + Ownership of the Property
source_reference.source_text: If an existing ﬁrst mortgage is being paid oﬀ through the transaction, it must be at least 12 months old at the time of reﬁnance, as measured by the note date of the existing loan to the note date of the new loan. This requirement does not apply to any existing subordinate liens being paid oﬀ through the transaction, or when buying out a co-owner pursuant to a legal agreement. At least one borrower must have been on title for at least for six months prior to the disbursement date of the new loan. See Ownership of the Property below for exceptions. There is no waiting period if the lender documents that the borrower acquired the property through an inheritance or was legally awarded the property (divorce, separation, or dissolution of a domestic partnership).

examples: Cash-out refi paying off first mortgage opened 10 months ago → ineligible (fails 12-month seasoning).
Cash-out refi paying off only a HELOC subordinate lien opened 3 months ago → 12-month rule does not apply to the subordinate payoff.
Borrower inherited property 2 months ago and wants cash-out refi → no title waiting period if inheritance is documented.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 12-month and 6-month timing thresholds with enumerated exceptions (partial excerpt).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: Application requests a 30-year fixed-rate loan amount of $450,000.
Application requests an ARM with a specified initial fixed period.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op pro rata project debt ratio capped at 35% or 40%
business_rules.description: For a co-op share loan, the pro rata share of the project debt related to the share loan cannot exceed 35% of the sum of (a) the related pro rata share of the project debt and (b) the appraised equity interest value of the shares. A higher ratio is permitted only when there are fully documented compensating factors, and the higher ratio cannot exceed 40%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to co-op share loans where blanket/project debt affects unit-level risk.
business_rules.consequences: If ratio exceeds allowed cap, co-op share loan is not eligible for delivery/purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Up to 40% allowed only with fully documented compensating factors.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-03, Lien Position for Co-op Share Loans (Note)
source_reference.source_text:
Note: The pro rata  share of the project debt that is related to the co-op share loan cannot exceed 35%
of the sum of the related pro rata share of the project debt and the appraised equity interest value of
the shares. Lenders may use a higher ratio (not to exceed 40%) when there are fully documented
compensating factors that justify using the higher ratio.

examples: Project debt pro rata = $120,000; appraised equity interest value = $200,000; ratio = 120/(120+200)=37.5%: eligible only if fully documented compensating factors support (≤40%).
Project debt pro rata = $140,000; equity interest value = $220,000; ratio = 38.9%: must document compensating factors; otherwise ineligible.
Project debt pro rata = $170,000; equity interest value = $200,000; ratio = 45.9%: ineligible even with compensating factors (exceeds 40%).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit ratio cap with documented exception up to 40%.

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op flip tax must be ≤5% or exempt/profit-based
business_rules.description: If a co-op project requires a flip tax, the co-op share loan is eligible for delivery if the co-op project legal documents either (i) exempt the lender from paying the flip tax when it acquires the unit through foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, or similar satisfaction transfer, or (ii) make the flip tax profit-based (payable only on appreciation when the sales price exceeds the existing owner’s purchase price). If the flip tax is due regardless of appreciation, the loan may still be eligible only if the flip tax amount is ≤ 5% of the value of the property (lesser of appraised value or sales price) and the flip tax is calculated as a flat fee, fee per share, percentage of appraised value/sales price, or dollar amount per room.
business_rules.conditions: Co-op share loan in a project with flip tax requirement in legal documents
business_rules.consequences: If flip tax meets criteria, eligible; if not, loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility allowed under either exemption/profit-based rule or ≤5%/method rule

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-04, Co-op Share Loans Subject to Flip Tax
source_reference.source_text: If the ﬂip tax does not meet one of these requirements and is due whether or not the sales price exceeds the existing unit owner’s purchase price, then it may still be eligible as long as the amount of the ﬂip tax is less than or equal to 5% of the value of the property (calculated as the lesser of appraised value or sales price) and it is calculated in one of the following ways: a ﬂat fee, a fee per share, a percentage of the appraised value or sales price of the co-op unit, or a dollar amount per room.

examples: Co-op charges flip tax of 2% of sales price regardless of appreciation; eligible if 2% ≤ 5% and calculation method is a percentage of sales price.

Co-op charges a flat $10,000 flip tax; if property value (lesser of sales price/appraised value) is $180,000, then $10,000 is 5.56% and fails the ≤5% requirement (unless lender is exempt or flip tax is profit-based).
Co-op flip tax is payable only on appreciation (profit-based); eligible regardless of the percentage, provided legal documents clearly restrict it to appreciation basis.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit ≤5% threshold, value definition (lesser of appraised value or sales price), and permitted calculation methods are directly stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for purchase or securitization

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Sales Comparison Approach Must Include 3 Closed Comparables
business_rules.description: The appraisal report sales comparison approach must report a minimum of three closed comparable sales. Additional comparable sales may be included for support. Contract offerings and current listings may be included as supporting data when appropriate, but the minimum three must be closed comparables.
business_rules.conditions: Appraisal report uses the sales comparison approach to develop value
business_rules.consequences: If fewer than 3 closed comps, lender must obtain revision/new appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions referenced for new/converted projects; still requires settled sales

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.3-08, Minimum Number of Comparable Sales
source_reference.source_text: A minimum of three closed comparables must be reported in the sales comparison approach. Additional comparable sales may be reported to support the opinion of market value provided by the appraiser. The subject property can be used as a fourth comparable sale or as supporting data if it was previously closed. Contract oﬀerings and current listings can be used as supporting data, if appropriate.

examples: Appraisal includes only two closed sales and one active listing; lender requires a revision to add a third closed comparable.
Appraisal includes three closed sales plus two active listings as support; meets the minimum requirement.
Subject property was a recent resale and is included as a fourth comparable; still must have three other closed comparable sales.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum count and acceptable supporting data are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_ELIGIBILITY_036_010 sets a delivery-stage expectation to assess post-disaster property condition changes and determine if a new appraisal is needed. When that determination results in ordering a new appraisal, the appraisal report must comply with the appraisal content requirements such as BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_009_001 (minimum three closed comps) to be acceptable for delivery decisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender obtains a post-disaster appraisal that does not meet minimum report standards, the disaster-condition clearance cannot be completed, delaying delivery and potentially causing rate lock extension costs and purchase deadline misses.

business_rules.rule_name: New Condo Subdivision PUD Requires 1 Inside and 1 Outside Sale
business_rules.description: For a subject property in a new (or recently converted) condo project, subdivision, or PUD, the appraisal report must include at minimum: (1) at least one settled comparable sale from the subject project/subdivision/PUD, (2) at least one settled comparable sale from outside the subject project/subdivision/PUD, and (3) a third settled comparable sale that may be inside or outside.
business_rules.conditions: Subject is in a new or recently converted condo/subdivision/PUD project
business_rules.consequences: If mix not met, lender must obtain appraisal revision or new appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited bullet list

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.3-08, Additional Requirements for New (or Recently Converted) Condos, Subdivisions, or PUDS
source_reference.source_text: At a minimum, the appraisal report for these properties must include the following: At least one settled comparable sale from the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD. (A resale is preferable if it is veriﬁable and does not involve the subject builder or developer). At least one settled comparable sale from outside the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD. A third settled comparable sale can be from inside or outside of the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD.

examples: New condo: appraisal uses three internal builder sales only; lender requires at least one settled sale outside the project.
New PUD: appraisal includes one resale inside the PUD, one sale in competing neighborhood, and one more inside; meets the minimum mix.
Recently converted condo: appraisal includes one inside settled sale, two outside settled sales; meets the minimum mix.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum composition requirement with counts and inside/outside criteria.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Project Ineligible for Unfunded Repairs Over $10,000 per Unit Within 12 Months
business_rules.description: A project is considered in need of critical repairs (and therefore ineligible) when it has unfunded repairs costing more than $10,000 per unit that should be undertaken within the next 12 months. This definition excludes repairs made by unit owners and repairs funded through a special assessment. If damage or deferred maintenance is isolated to one or a few units and does not affect overall safety/soundness/structural integrity/habitability, these requirements do not apply.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is in a project evaluated under physical condition/critical repair criteria
business_rules.consequences: If threshold met, project is ineligible until remediation; otherwise may proceed
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply if isolated to few units and no overall impact

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-03, Projects in Need of Critical Repairs
source_reference.source_text: Projects in need of critical repairs are those needing repairs or replacements that signiﬁcantly impact the safety, soundness, structural integrity or habitability of the project's building(s), or the ﬁnancial viability or marketability of the project. Critical repairs include conditions such as: ... any unfunded repairs costing more than $10,000 per unit that should be undertaken within the next 12 months (does not include repairs made by the unit owner or repairs funded through a special assessment). If damage or deferred maintenance is isolated to one or a few units and does not aﬀect the overall safety, soundness, structural integrity, or habitability of the project, then these requirements do not apply.

examples: Ineligible: 40-unit project needs roof replacement in 6 months; no reserves/financing; cost equals $12,500 per unit → exceeds $10,000 threshold.
Eligible (on this rule): Repairs are funded via a special assessment already approved and within guidelines, so the ‘unfunded repairs’ trigger is not met.
Exception applies: Water damage isolated to one unit only, with no impact to common structures and no broader habitability concern.
Subject property identified as a detached PUD unit with HOA dues.
Subject property identified as an attached PUD unit in a planned development.

business_rules.rule_name: 3- and 5-year ARM initial rate must be within 3% and margin ≤300 bps
business_rules.description: For a Fannie Mae ARM, the fully indexed rate is calculated as the applicable index value plus the mortgage margin, using any index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date, and rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. To determine acceptability for purchase by Fannie Mae, subtract the initial note rate from the fully indexed rate in effect at origination; for 3-year and 5-year ARMs, the difference must not exceed 3%. Separately, the mortgage margin must be no more than 300 basis points. If the spread exceeds 3% or the margin exceeds 300 bps, the ARM terms are not acceptable for delivery.
business_rules.conditions: When originating a 3- or 5-year ARM intended for Fannie Mae purchase/securitization
business_rules.consequences: If difference ≤3% and margin ≤300 bps, ARM acceptable; otherwise ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Calculating the Fully Indexed Rate; Determining ARM Acceptability; Mortgage Margin
source_reference.source_text: The fully indexed rate is the sum of the value of the applicable index and the mortgage margin, which is then rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. The applicable index value that determines the fully indexed rate is any index value in eﬀect during the 90 days that precede the note date. Lenders must determine whether an ARM loan is acceptable for purchase by Fannie Mae by subtracting the initial note rate of the loan from the fully indexed rate in eﬀect when the loan originated. For 3- and 5-year ARMs, the diﬀerence must not exceed 3%. The maximum mortgage margin may be no more than 300 basis points.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Determining ARM Acceptability; Mortgage Margin
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must determine whether an ARM loan is acceptable for purchase by Fannie Mae by subtracting the initial note rate of the loan from the fully indexed rate in eﬀect when the loan originated. For 3- and 5-year ARMs, the diﬀerence must not exceed 3%. ... The maximum mortgage margin may be no more than 300 basis points.

examples: 5-year ARM: fully indexed 7.125%, initial note 4.500% → difference 2.625% (≤3%): acceptable if margin ≤300 bps.
3-year ARM: fully indexed 6.750%, initial note 3.500% → difference 3.250% (>3%): not acceptable for purchase.
5-year ARM with margin 3.25% (325 bps): fails margin cap even if initial-rate difference is ≤3%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same ARM acceptability constraints for 3- and 5-year ARMs intended for Fannie Mae: (i) subtract initial note rate from fully indexed rate at origination, and the difference must not exceed 3%; and (ii) the mortgage margin must be ≤ 300 bps. BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 additionally specifies how to compute fully indexed rate (index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date and rounding to nearest 1/8%). These are not conflicting requirements; the second rule is a restatement of the same constraint set.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes both the acceptability test (spread and margin caps) and the method details for fully indexed rate calculation (index lookback window and rounding), making it more directly testable.

dependencies.rationale: BR_038_007 defines how to calculate the fully indexed rate (index + margin, rounding rules, index value timing window). BR_012_010 requires that the lender subtract the initial note rate from the fully indexed rate at origination and evaluate the 3% cap for 3/5-year ARMs and the 300 bps margin cap. Accurate and compliant calculation of fully indexed rate is a prerequisite input.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If fully indexed rate is miscalculated, ARM eligibility determination may be wrong, causing ineligible delivery or inappropriate denial/restructure.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM fixed period must match 3-year 36 months and 5-year 60 months
business_rules.description: For standard conventional ARMs, the initial adjustment period in months must align with the initial fixed-rate period in years. Example requirements: a 3-year ARM must have an initial fixed period of 36 months; a 5-year ARM must have an initial fixed period of 60 months.
business_rules.conditions: When setting initial_fixed_period_months for a standard conventional ARM plan
business_rules.consequences: If aligned, ARM characteristic is acceptable; if misaligned, ARM terms are noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The initial adjustment period in months must align with the initial ﬁxed-rate period in years. For example, a "3-year ARM" must have an initial ﬁxed period of 36 months, and a "5-year ARM" must be 60 months.

examples: Loan disclosed as a 3-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 36; it satisfies the alignment requirement.
Loan disclosed as a 5-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 54; it violates the required 60-month fixed period.
Loan disclosed as a 7-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 84; it follows the same alignment principle (7 years × 12 months).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until corrected

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

business_rules.rule_name: Post Purchase LLPA Repricing Limited to 18 Months and $100
business_rules.description: For Seller/Servicer-initiated post-purchase adjustments, data corrections must be submitted within 18 months of the loan’s acquisition date to be considered for repricing of LLPAs. A minimum $100 per-loan threshold (credit or debit) is required. The 18-month lookback period does not apply to drafting or collection of repurchase alternative fees. Fannie Mae may adjust the minimum dollar amount and lookback period in its sole discretion.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer discovers discrepancies in delivered data and seeks LLPA repricing post-acquisition
business_rules.consequences: If within 18 months and ≥$100, repricing may be considered; otherwise, repricing not considered
business_rules.exceptions: 18-month lookback does not apply to repurchase alternative fees drafting/collection

source_reference.source_text: considered for repricing of loan-level price adjustments (LLPAs). A minimum $100 requirement per loan (credit or debit) is also required. The 18-month lookback period does not apply to the drafting or collection of repurchase alternative

examples: Seller discovers wrong occupancy code 9 months after acquisition causing LLPA impact of $250; submits correction—eligible for repricing consideration.
Seller submits a correction 20 months after acquisition for a $400 LLPA issue; beyond 18 months—repricing is not considered.
Seller submits a correction within 18 months but LLPA delta is $75; below $100 minimum—repricing not considered under the stated threshold.
Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Student loan cash-out refinance cash back limit is greater of 1% or $2,000
business_rules.description: For a student loan cash-out refinance, borrower cash back at closing must not exceed the greater of (i) 1% of the new refinance loan amount or (ii) $2,000. The lender may also refund overpayment of fees/charges due to law/regulation or apply a principal curtailment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the refinance is structured as a student loan cash-out refinance.
business_rules.consequences: If cash back exceeds the greater-of limit, the transaction fails program requirements and is not compliant for delivery as such.
business_rules.exceptions: Refunds of overpaid fees/charges due to law or principal curtailment are permitted.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-04 (text preceding), Requirements for Student Loan Cash-out Refinances
source_reference.source_text: The borrower may receive cash back in an amount that does not exceed the greater of 1% of the new reﬁnance loan amount or $2,000. The lender may also refund the borrower for the overpayment of fees and charges due to federal or state laws or regulations, or apply a principal curtailment (see B2-1.3-02, Limited Cash-Out Reﬁnance Transactions for additional information). Unless otherwise stated, all other standard cash-out reﬁnance requirements apply.

examples: New refinance loan amount $300,000: 1% is $3,000, so max cash back is $3,000; $3,100 cash back would violate the rule.
New refinance loan amount $150,000: 1% is $1,500, so max cash back is $2,000; $2,000 cash back is compliant.
Borrower receives $1,800 cash back plus a $250 refund of overpaid recording fee required by state law: refund is permitted per the rule text.
Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Occupying borrower must fund first 5% down payment
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, when income of a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower is used for qualifying, the occupying borrower(s) must make the first 5% of the down payment from their own funds. This 5% own-funds minimum contribution is not required if (i) the LTV or CLTV ratio is ≤ 80%, or (ii) the occupying borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or employer funds for some or all of the borrower’s minimum contribution.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant income used to qualify; principal residence
business_rules.consequences: If met, down payment source compliant; if not, loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Exception if LTV/CLTV ≤80% or eligible 1-unit gift/grant/employer funds

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, Down Payment and Qualifying Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, if the income of a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower is used for qualifying purposes, the occupying borrower(s) must make the ﬁrst 5% of the down payment from their own funds unless: the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%; or the occupying borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets the requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or funds received from an employer to pay for some or all of the borrower’s minimum contribution.

examples: Purchase 95% LTV principal residence: non-occupant co-borrower income used; occupying borrower must contribute first 5% down payment from own verified funds.
Purchase 80% LTV: non-occupant co-borrower income used; 5% own-funds requirement does not apply due to LTV/CLTV ≤80%.

One-unit principal residence at 97% LTV where gifts are permitted and borrower meets gift requirements; gift/grant/employer funds may satisfy minimum contribution instead of occupying borrower own funds.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 5% requirement with explicit exception conditions (≤80% LTV/CLTV, or 1-unit with gift/grant/employer assistance).
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: Both rules apply to manually underwritten loans. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score thresholds for eligibility (620 fixed/640 ARM). BR_026_004 imposes down payment own-funds requirements when using non-occupant income. In practice, lenders must first confirm the loan clears baseline manual underwriting eligibility, including credit score floors, before evaluating conditional overlays like down payment sourcing tied to non-occupant income.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, any work to structure BR_026_004 compliance is wasted; loan is ineligible for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual underwrite DTI capped at 43% using occupier income
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans that use a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower income for qualifying, the lender must calculate the DTI ratio using only the income of the occupying borrower(s). Based on that calculation, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 43%, even if the combined qualifying ratios for all borrowers are below Fannie Mae’s standard qualifying ratio benchmark.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant income present; DTI computed on occupier income only
business_rules.consequences: If DTI ≤43% (occupier-only), compliant; if >43%, loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, Down Payment and Qualifying Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: Using only the income of the occupying borrower(s) to calculate the DTI ratio, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 43%. Note : This policy applies even if the combined qualifying ratios for the borrower and the guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower are well below Fannie Mae’s standard qualifying ratio benchmark.

examples: Occupying borrower income $6,000/month and debts $2,700/month: DTI = 45%; exceeds 43% cap and is not eligible under this manual underwriting policy.
Occupying borrower income $6,000/month and debts $2,400/month: DTI = 40%; compliant even if non-occupant has additional income.
Combined DTI including non-occupant income would be 30%, but occupier-only DTI is 44%; still fails because the rule requires occupier-only DTI ≤43%.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit DTI cap (43%) and explicit instruction to use only occupying borrower income are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_005 applies to manually underwritten loans using non-occupant/guarantor income and caps occupier-only DTI at 43%. Even if the DTI overlay is satisfied, the loan remains ineligible if BR_024_002 credit score floors are not met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, loan is ineligible regardless of BR_026_005 DTI compliance; underwriting resources are misallocated.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_004 governs minimum occupying-borrower own-funds contribution, while BR_026_005 governs DTI calculation and cap using occupier-only income. They are separate requirements triggered by the same fact pattern and jointly determine deliverability for that structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failing either results in ineligibility; checking only one gives a false sense of compliance completeness.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual non-occupant max LTV 90% (CLTV 105% with CS)
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, when income of a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower is used for qualifying and that party will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios may not exceed 90%. If a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant guarantor/co-signer/co-borrower income used
business_rules.consequences: If ratios within limits, eligible; if exceeded, loan not eligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Community Seconds allows CLTV up to 105% where Eligibility Matrix permits

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, LTV Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, if the income of a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower is used for qualifying purposes, and that guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratio may not exceed 90% (unless a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, in which case the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix .

examples: Manual underwrite purchase: LTV 92% with non-occupant co-borrower income used; fails because maximum is 90%.
Manual underwrite with Community Seconds: first mortgage 95% LTV and CLTV 104%; acceptable if Eligibility Matrix permits and CLTV ≤105%.
Manual underwrite refinance: CLTV 106% with Community Seconds; fails because CLTV may not exceed 105% even when Community Seconds is present.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit max 90% and explicit conditional exception to 105% CLTV with Community Seconds are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_006 caps LTV/CLTV/HCLTV for manual underwrite non-occupant income scenarios. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score floors for manual underwriting eligibility. Baseline eligibility is required before ratio overlays can result in a deliverable loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, loan is ineligible no matter how low the LTV is.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_004 addresses minimum own-funds down payment contribution in certain scenarios; BR_026_006 imposes maximum LTV/CLTV/HCLTV caps (with Community Seconds exception). Together they form a combined eligibility test path for that loan structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Loan can fail due to leverage caps even if down payment sourcing passes, or vice versa; workflow should prompt both checks.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual underwrite minimum 620 fixed and 640 ARM credit score
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, the minimum credit score that applies for loan eligibility is 620 for fixed-rate loans and 640 for ARMs. To determine the loan-level credit score for eligibility: use the representative credit score for loans with one borrower; use the average median credit score for loans with more than one borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to manually underwritten loans when determining eligibility credit score
business_rules.consequences: If met: meets minimum score floor; if violated: loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Certain transactions not subject to minimum (see Guide exceptions section)

source_reference.source_text: To determine the credit score that applies for loan eligibility, use the following: • Loans with one borrower - representative credit score, • Loans with more than one borrower - average median credit score. The minimum credit score that applies for loan eligibility is: 620 — ﬁxed-rate loans 640 — ARMs

examples: One borrower, fixed-rate, representative score 621: meets minimum 620.
Two borrowers with median scores 680 and 620: average median is 650; meets ARM minimum 640 if product is ARM.
One borrower fixed-rate with representative score 615: fails minimum; loan ineligible unless a stated exception applies.
Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_002 requires establishing the loan-level credit score (representative or average median) and meeting minimum scores for manual underwriting. BR_024_003 defines when a traditional credit report is acceptable even if 1-2 repositories have no data, provided a tri-merge was requested and at least one repo has data. Without BR_024_003, the credit score inputs could be rejected or misapplied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If credit report acceptability is not established, credit score determination can be invalid, leading to incorrect eligibility outcomes or needing to shift to nontraditional credit requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_004 is an eligibility prohibition tied to credit report availability under freezes. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score thresholds and scoring methodology for manual underwriting. If BR_024_004 renders the loan ineligible (two+ frozen repos), BR_024_002 cannot be meaningfully applied for eligibility. If one repo frozen, BR_024_004 dictates the data sufficiency (two repositories with data) needed to compute a valid loan-level score.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_004 is violated, the loan cannot be sold to Fannie Mae regardless of credit score; proceeding wastes underwriting resources and creates compliance risk if delivered.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle may escrow up to 6 PITIA payments for uninhabitable PR
business_rules.description: At the borrower’s request, up to six mortgage payments (PITIA) that become due during the renovation period may be included as part of total renovation costs for a principal residence when the property cannot be occupied during renovation. The lender/servicer/agent must hold the funds in a renovation escrow account (or separate escrow) and apply them only to payments due during the period the property cannot be occupied.
