business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle principal residence when property cannot be occupied
business_rules.consequences: If met: PITIA funds controlled in escrow; if violated: misuse of funds; noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: Separate escrow account may be used in lieu of renovation escrow account

source_reference.source_text: At the borrower’s request, up to six mortgage payments (PITIA) that will become due during the renovation period may be included as part of the total renovation costs for a principal residence property if the property cannot be occupied during the renovation period. The lender, servicer, or its agent must hold the funds in a renovation escrow account, and only apply them to payments that come due during the period in which the property cannot be occupied. Note: The lender may set up a separate escrow account for the mortgage payments...

examples: Borrower requests payment escrows for a 4-month renovation: lender escrows 4 PITIA payments and applies them only during non-occupancy.
Borrower requests 7 PITIA payments: lender limits escrow to 6 payments per Guide.
Lender holds PITIA funds in a separate escrow account instead of the renovation escrow account: permitted by Note.
Loan requires escrow for property taxes and homeowners insurance.
Loan includes an escrow waiver only where permitted and properly documented.

business_rules.rule_name: Prefunding QC Sample Minimum 10% or 750 Loans
business_rules.description: Each month, the lender’s prefunding QC monthly loan selection must be at least the lesser of (a) 10% of prior month total loans closed/acquired or 10% of current month projected loans closed/acquired (with reconciliation required if projections are used), or (b) 750 loans. If the lender did not close/acquire at least 10 loans in the prior month, it must select at least 1 loan for the monthly prefunding QC review.
business_rules.conditions: When establishing the monthly prefunding QC sample for loans prior to closing/acquisition
business_rules.consequences: If met, QC sampling minimum satisfied; if violated, QC plan is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: If prior month total loans <10, sample must include at least 1 loan

source_reference.source_text: Each month, the lender must complete a minimum number of prefunding QC reviews. The monthly loan selection must equal, at a minimum, the lesser of: 10% of the prior month's total number of loans closed or acquired, or 10% of the current month's projected total number of loans to be closed or acquired (if using projections, the lender must perform a reconciliation process to ensure the 10% requirement is met); or 750 loans. If the lender does not close or acquire at least 10 loans in the prior month, the lender must select at least one loan for its monthly prefunding QC review.

examples: Prior month closed 1,200 loans; current month projected 1,000. Minimum sample is lesser of 10% (120 or 100) vs 750 ⇒ 100 loans.
Prior month closed 9 loans; lender must review at least 1 loan in the current month prefunding QC sample.
Current month projected 10,000 loans; 10% is 1,000, but cap is lesser of 1,000 vs 750 ⇒ 750 loans must be reviewed.
Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Random post-closing QC sample minimum 10% monthly
business_rules.description: If the lender uses the 10% random sampling method, it must select for post-closing QC review a minimum of 10% of the loans it originates using a random selection methodology. If the lender originates or acquires loans from a third party, it must conduct separate QC reviews for retail and third-party originations—sampling at least 10% of retail originations and at least 10% of third-party acquired loans.
business_rules.conditions: Post-closing QC random sampling method is “10% sample”
business_rules.consequences: If ≥10% sampled per channel, sampling meets standard; if <10%, QC noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: If 10% < 1 loan, at least 1 must be selected (handled in related rule)

source_reference.source_text: The lender must select for its post-closing QC review a minimum of 10% of the loans that it originates using a random selection methodology. Lenders who originate or acquire loans from a third-party must conduct a separate review for both its retail and third-party originations. Speciﬁcally, the lender must sample at least 10% of its retail originations and sample at least 10% of loans acquired through third-party originations.

examples: Lender originated 200 retail loans in March and acquired 50 third-party loans; it must randomly sample at least 20 retail and at least 5 third-party loans.
Lender samples 15 of 200 retail loans (7.5%); fails the minimum 10% requirement and must increase the sample size.
Lender mixes retail and third-party into one 10% pool without separate channel sampling; violates the separate review requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit 10% minimum requirements, including separate channel minimums, are directly stated.

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Statistical QC sampling 95% confidence and 2% precision
business_rules.description: If the lender uses statistical sampling instead of the 10% method, the statistical sampling model variables must be calculated using at least a 95% confidence level with a 2% precision rate and a statistical statement of six months (Fannie Mae recommends three months). The lender must document the statistical sampling methodology and provide detailed written justification to Fannie Mae upon request; Fannie Mae may require adjustments based on its review.
business_rules.conditions: Post-closing QC uses statistical sampling methodology
business_rules.consequences: If model meets parameters, sampling acceptable; if not, must revise/justify and adjust
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (3-month statement is recommendation, not requirement)

source_reference.source_text: Statistical Sample If the lender uses statistical sampling for its selection process, at a minimum, the statistical sampling model (variables) must be calculated using a 95% conﬁdence level with a 2% precision rate and a statistical statement of six months. (Fannie Mae recommends

examples: Lender implements statistical sampling at 95% confidence, 2% precision, using a 6-month lookback; compliant with minimum requirements.
Lender uses a 90% confidence level and 5% precision; noncompliant and must recalibrate to at least 95%/2%.
Fannie Mae requests the lender’s sampling documentation; lender must provide written justification and may be required to modify the model.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit statistical parameters (95%, 2%, six months) and documentation/adjustment provisions are stated.

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Freddie Mac Purchase Amount Cap 100% of Principal Balance
business_rules.description: The amount paid by Freddie Mac to purchase a multifamily Mortgage generally must not exceed 100 percent of the Mortgage’s outstanding principal balance, with an adjustment for prepaid or accrued interest as of the day before the Freddie Mac Funding Date. This is a cap on purchase proceeds used for settlement and reconciliation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac purchases the Mortgage on the Funding Date
business_rules.consequences: If calculated purchase amount exceeds cap, settlement amount must be reduced/corrected
business_rules.exceptions: Stated as “Generally”; no specific exception described

source_reference.source_text: Generally, the amount paid by Freddie Mac to purchase a multifamily Mortgage will not exceed 100 percent of the outstanding principal balance of the Mortgage, adjusted for prepaid or accrued interest as of the day before the Freddie Mac Funding Date.

examples: UPB is $10,000,000; computed purchase proceeds (excluding interest adjustment) cannot exceed $10,000,000.
UPB is $5,000,000 with $12,000 accrued interest through day before funding; settlement must apply the interest adjustment but still follow the 100% UPB cap.
Seller submits data implying purchase amount 101% of UPB; Freddie Mac requires correction before purchase/settlement.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_CONSTRAINT_001_004 governs the amount Freddie Mac pays to purchase a mortgage (settlement/reconciliation). BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_018_008 is a hard operational precondition to any Freddie Mac-originated payment instruction method (wire/ACH/check/etc.). Thus, even if the purchase proceeds amount complies with the cap, funding cannot proceed without authorization documentation on file.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_018_008 is not satisfied, Freddie Mac cannot disburse purchase funds; delivery/funding is delayed, settlement timelines slip, and the transaction may fail to close/purchase on the intended date.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CLOSING_PACKAGE_DOCUMENTATION_012_004 states the Final Delivery Package 'must include' a Non-Consolidation Opinion when thresholds/conditions are met, and missing it may cause delivery to be conditioned/rejected. Purchase proceeds cap/settlement (BR_MORTGAGE_CONSTRAINT_001_004) presumes the loan is accepted for purchase and progressing through funding reconciliation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_CLOSING_PACKAGE_DOCUMENTATION_012_004 is not met when triggered, the loan may not reach purchase settlement; funding can be delayed until the opinion is obtained or exceptions are approved.

business_rules.rule_name: Acquisition and 12-Month Refi Use Lesser of Value or Cost
business_rules.description: For acquisition financing and certain refinances, the Mortgage amount must be based on the lesser of (i) the appraised value or (ii) the total acquisition cost. This requirement also applies to refinance loans when the Property was acquired within 12 months of the date the full underwriting package is submitted to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to acquisitions and refis where property acquired within 12 months of UW submission
business_rules.consequences: If Mortgage amount exceeds the lesser metric, delivery terms must be reduced or loan is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Mortgage amount must be based on the lesser of the appraised value or the total acquisition cost. This section applies not only to acquisition transactions, but also to refinance loans in which the Property was acquired within 12 months of the date of the full underwriting package submission to Freddie Mac.

examples: Purchase price $20.0MM, appraised value $19.0MM: maximum Mortgage sizing must use $19.0MM (lesser metric).
Property acquired 10 months before underwriting submission for $15.0MM; appraisal now $17.0MM: refinance still must use total acquisition cost ($15.0MM) as the limiter if it is the lesser metric.
Property acquired 14 months before underwriting submission: this specific 12-month refi limiter does not apply (other sizing rules still apply).
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

dependencies.rationale: BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_035_002 is a categorical purchase prohibition tied to counterparty status. It supersedes loan sizing compliance under BR_MORTGAGE_CONSTRAINT_001_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SCP match occurs, purchase is denied; time spent correcting sizing under BR_MORTGAGE_CONSTRAINT_001_005 will not resolve the denial without addressing SCP status/exclusions.

business_rules.rule_name: Floating-rate Mortgage term limited to 5, 7, or 10 years
business_rules.description: A Floating-Rate Mortgage submitted under the Multifamily Conventional Cash Mortgage Purchase Program may have a term only of 5 years, 7 years, or 10 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Floating-Rate Mortgages under Guide Chapter 17.3(b).
business_rules.consequences: If term is not 5/7/10 years, Mortgage is ineligible for delivery/purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: b. Term ( 04/15/21 ) The term of the Floating -Rate Mortgage may be 5, 7 or 10 years.

examples: A 7-year floating-rate mortgage meets the term requirement.
A 3-year floating-rate mortgage is not permitted and should not be delivered to Freddie Mac.
A 10-year floating-rate mortgage meets the term requirement (subject to other program rules).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Simple explicit allowable-term list.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

dependencies.rationale: Even where program eligibility requirements like permitted term under BR_MORTGAGE_CONSTRAINT_003_003 are met, BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_035_002 imposes a hard stop on purchase/approval based on SCP list status.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SCP match: purchase denied; program-term compliance cannot cure.

business_rules.rule_name: Yield maintenance premium minimum 1% during YM period
business_rules.description: The standard Yield Maintenance Period depends on mortgage term (5 years: 4¾ years; 7 years: 6½ years; 10 years: 9½ years; 15 years: 14½ years; 20/25/30 years: 15 years). During the Yield Maintenance Period, the minimum prepayment premium due is 1% of the amount prepaid. After the Yield Maintenance Period, with any prepayment, the Borrower must pay a 1% premium except for 5-year term mortgages; no prepayment premium is due during the Window Period (usually last 3 months of the term).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to mortgages with yield maintenance prepayment premium terms
business_rules.consequences: If paid per terms, prepayment allowed; if not, prepayment terms not satisfied
business_rules.exceptions: 5-year term: no 1% premium after YM period; window period no premium

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 17, g. Yield Maintenance Prepayment Premium (02/29/12)
source_reference.source_text: The standard Yield Maintenance Period will be as follows: Term of Mortgage Yield Maintenance Period 5 years 4¾ years 7 years 6½ years 10 years 9½ years 15 years 14½ years 20 years 15 years 25 years 15 years 30 years 15 years The minimum prepayment premium due during the Yield Maintenance Period is one percent of the amount prepaid. After the expiration of the Yield Maintenance Period, with any prepayment, the Borrower must pay a prepayment premium of one percent of the amount prepaid, with the exception of Mortgages with a five-year term for which there is not a one percent prepayment premium period at the expiration of the Yield Maintenance Period. No prepayment premium is due during the Window Period as defined in the Note (usually the last three months of the term of the Mortgage).

examples: 10-year term mortgage: YM period is 9.5 years; any prepayment during YM must include at least 1% of amount prepaid as premium.
20-year term mortgage: YM period is 15 years; prepayment in year 16 requires a 1% premium (unless in the Window Period).
5-year term mortgage: after YM period ends, there is no additional 1% premium period; still, no premium is due during the Window Period (usually last 3 months).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit table + minimum premium + post-YM and window-period treatment.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Ground lease mortgagee cure period adds 10 and 60 days
business_rules.description: For Ground Lease Mortgages, the ground lease must grant the ground lease mortgagee cure rights that extend beyond the Ground Lessee’s grace periods: at least 10 additional days beyond any monetary default grace period, and at least 60 additional days beyond any non-monetary default grace period (for defaults capable of cure by a ground lease mortgagee).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Mortgage is a Ground Lease Mortgage under Chapter 30
business_rules.consequences: If not met, ground lease structure is not compliant for Freddie Mac review/purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 30 – Ground Lease Mortgages; d.(4) Defaults and cure period
source_reference.source_text: • Right to cure . The ground lease mortgagee must have the right to cure a monetary default or a non -monetary default that is capable of being cured by a ground lease mortgagee within the time permitted to the Ground Lessee, plus a reasonable additional time period (i.e.,  at least 10 days beyond any grace period granted to Ground Lessee for monetary defaults and at least 60 days beyond any grace period granted to Ground Lessee for non -monetary defaults).

examples: Monetary default grace period is 5 days; ground lease must give mortgagee at least 15 total days (5 + 10) to cure.
Non-monetary default grace period is 30 days; ground lease must give mortgagee at least 90 total days (30 + 60) to cure if capable of cure by mortgagee.
Ground lease provides only the lessee’s cure period with no added time for mortgagee; Freddie Mac deems lease noncompliant and requires revision/mitigants.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum additional cure periods (10/60 days) stated as requirement for ground leases.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Ineligible if seismic PML exceeds 40% and no retrofit
business_rules.description: A Mortgage is ineligible for sale to Freddie Mac if the securing Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, the Seismic Risk Assessment indicates Probable Maximum Loss (PML) greater than 40%, and the affected buildings have not undergone a seismic retrofit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Mortgage proposed for sale secured by property in Elevated Seismic Hazard Region.
business_rules.consequences: If triggered, Mortgage is ineligible for sale; otherwise may proceed subject to other rules.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: A Mortgage is ineligible for sale to Freddie Mac if • The Mortgage is secured by a Property located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, the Probable Maximum Loss (PML) on a Seismic Risk Assessment (SRA) is greater than 40 percent, and the affected buildings have not undergone a seismic retrofit

examples: SRA shows PML 45% in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region; no retrofit completed: ineligible for sale to Freddie.
SRA shows PML 35% in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region: this ineligibility condition is not met.
PML 50% but buildings have undergone seismic retrofit: this specific ineligibility condition is not met.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_018_009 makes a mortgage ineligible if an excluded party played a specified role. BR_MORTGAGE_ELIGIBILITY_007_004 makes a mortgage ineligible under certain seismic risk/retrofit conditions. They do not require each other’s outputs, but they function together as part of a comprehensive eligibility gate for delivery/sale decisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_018_009 is skipped while BR_MORTGAGE_ELIGIBILITY_007_004 is satisfied, a mortgage could be incorrectly treated as eligible even though it is barred due to an excluded party’s involvement, leading to delivery rejection or post-purchase remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_004 is only applicable when the secured property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region. BR_004_007 establishes how that status is determined (PGA ≥ 0.15g with USGS calculation rules/assumptions). Without applying BR_004_007, BR_007_004 cannot be correctly evaluated (wrong applicability, missed ineligibility).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_004_007 is skipped or wrong, loans may be incorrectly delivered as eligible despite being in a hazard region, creating a high likelihood of Freddie Mac purchase rejection or repurchase/indemnification risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_004 ineligibility hinges on whether the Seismic Risk Assessment indicates PML > 40% and whether buildings have undergone retrofit. BR_004_008 specifies when and how the Level 1 SRA must be obtained/submitted (Form 1102, SRA meeting requirements, USGS PDF upload). When Level 1 SRA is required, BR_007_004 cannot be reliably evaluated without the SRA documentation and outputs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_004_008 fails, underwriting cannot document PML and seismic risk factors properly; the file becomes deficient, and eligibility decisioning is blocked or error-prone, resulting in delivery delays or purchase rejection.

dependencies.rationale: Both rules address seismic risk but use different measures (PML vs SEL-475) and different triggers (elevated seismic region & PML vs SEL-475 thresholds & stability concern). If both apply to the same asset, a loan could appear to pass one framework while failing the other, requiring a clear hierarchy ('most restrictive wins' or defined precedence) for automation and reviewer guidance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not reconciled, automation may: (1) incorrectly clear a loan for purchase based on insurance logic while it is ineligible under PML criteria, or (2) impose unnecessary retrofit/insurance conditions. This can cause inconsistent underwriting decisions, purchase conditions, and escalation burden.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_ELIGIBILITY_007_004 is a property-risk ineligibility test. BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_035_002 is a counterparty-based hard stop. If SCP triggers, the mortgage cannot be sold even if seismic criteria are satisfied (or remediated).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: SCP hit results in denial; seismic retrofit/remediation does not cure.

business_rules.rule_name: Utility Efficiency Savings Minimum 30% with 15% Energy
business_rules.description: For Freddie Mac Multifamily Utility Efficiency offerings, the Borrower’s committed measures must produce projected minimum whole-building energy or water consumption savings of at least 30%, and at least 15% of the savings must be in energy consumption (Minimum Consumption Savings Threshold). This minimum threshold is an eligibility condition for the Utility Efficiency Loan option as determined by Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: When loan is submitted as Freddie Mac Utility Efficiency offering
business_rules.consequences: Meets threshold: may qualify; below threshold: not eligible for option
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 24 – Freddie Mac Multifamily Utility Efficiency (GB 12/16/25), Minimum Consumption Savings Threshold
source_reference.source_text: The Borrower commits to complete sufficient energy and/or water improvements from the measures listed in the Green Assessment to result in a projected minimum energy or water consumption savings of 30 percent based on whole building consumption, provided that a minimum 15 percent of savings must be in energy consumption (Minimum Consumption Savings Threshold).

examples: Projected savings 32% total with 18% energy savings: threshold met; can be treated as Utility Efficiency option (subject to Freddie Mac eligibility).
Projected savings 30% total but only 10% energy savings: fails the minimum 15% energy requirement; not eligible for Utility Efficiency option.
Projected savings 25% total and 20% energy savings: fails the 30% whole-building minimum; not eligible for Utility Efficiency option.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Utility Efficiency Repair Reserve Escrow 125% of Estimated Cost
business_rules.description: When a Borrower commits to Utility Efficiency Improvements, the Borrower must fund a Repair Reserve escrow equal to 125% of the estimated cost of the Utility Efficiency Improvements. This escrow requirement is part of Freddie Mac’s Utility Efficiency program mechanics and must be satisfied to remain compliant with the loan’s improvement commitment.
business_rules.conditions: When Utility Efficiency Improvements are committed under the loan
business_rules.consequences: Escrow funded at 125%: compliant; otherwise: delivery/servicing noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The energy and water improvements that the Borrower commits to complete will be identified as Utility Efficiency Improvements in the Utility Efficiency Improvements Rider to the Loan Agreement. The Borrower must escrow 125 percent of the estimated cost of the Utility Efficiency Improvements as part of the Repair Reserve.

examples: Estimated improvements cost is $400,000; required escrow is $500,000 (125%); Borrower escrows $500,000: compliant.
Estimated improvements cost is $150,000; Borrower escrows $150,000 (100%): not compliant; must increase escrow to $187,500.
Estimated improvements cost is updated from $200,000 to $260,000; escrow must be adjusted to $325,000 to maintain 125% funding.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Subordinate financing requires combined LTV 85% and DCR 1.20x
business_rules.description: If Freddie Mac considers consenting to Subordinate Financing at origination, the combined debt (first mortgage plus subordinate financing) may not result in an LTV Ratio greater than 85%, and the combined debt service may not result in a DCR below 1.20x.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower requests Subordinate Financing at origination; Freddie Mac review/consent
business_rules.consequences: If LTV > 85% or DCR < 1.20x, consent condition is not satisfied; request may be rejected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The combined debt may not result in an LTV Ratio that exceeds 85 percent and the combined debt service may not result in a DCR that is below 1.20x.
source_reference.confidence_note: Word positions are estimated from provided chunk excerpt.

examples: First mortgage $8.0MM, subordinate $1.0MM, appraised value $10.5MM → combined LTV = 85.71% (>85%): not acceptable for consent.
Combined annual NOI supports combined annual debt service at 1.18x (<1.20x): not acceptable even if combined LTV is 80%.
Combined LTV 83% and combined DCR 1.25x: satisfies this numeric consent condition (subject to other Chapter 8.12 conditions).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition failure / Freddie Mac consent denial

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Ground lease term must exceed maturity by 10, 20, or 30 years
business_rules.description: For ground lease Mortgages, the remaining ground lease term (including eligible renewal options) must extend beyond the Mortgage maturity based on amortization and whether the Ground Lessor joins: (i) fully amortizing: at least 10 years beyond maturity; (ii) partially/no amortization with subordinated ground lease: longer of (a) amortization period plus any interest-only period or (b) 20 years beyond maturity; (iii) unsubordinated ground lease: no less than 30 years beyond maturity. Renewal options at the Ground Lessor’s sole discretion may not be counted toward term compliance.
business_rules.conditions: Mortgage secured by lien on leasehold estate (ground lease Mortgage)
business_rules.consequences: If remaining term test fails, Mortgage is not eligible for purchase under this chapter
business_rules.exceptions: Renewal options solely at landlord discretion cannot be counted as term

source_reference.source_text: The remaining term of the ground lease, together with any renewal options, must meet the following requirements: • For a fully amortizing ground lease Mortgage, the remaining ground lease term must extend beyond the maturity date of the ground lease Mortgage by at least 10 years. • For a partially amortizing ground lease Mortgage or for one with no amortization... (subordinated)... the longer of: (a) the amortization period... plus any interest-only period, or (b) 20 years beyond the maturity date... (unsubordinated)... no less than 30 years beyond the maturity date... Renewal options that are at the sole discretion of the landlord... may not be considered part of the ground lease term
source_reference.confidence_note: Word positions are estimated from provided chunk excerpt.

examples: Fully amortizing loan matures 2035; lease expires 2043: only 8 years beyond maturity → not eligible (needs ≥10 years).
IO 3 years + amortization 30 years; subordinated ground lease; maturity 2035; lease term must be longer of (33 years) or (20 years beyond maturity = 2055).
Unsubordinated ground lease; maturity 2035; lease must run to at least 2065 (30 years beyond), and landlord-only renewal options cannot be counted.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Increased mortgage amount must be at least $100,000 or 1% and no more than 10%
business_rules.description: After Freddie Mac reviews the full underwriting package for an early rate lock delivery, the Seller may request an increase to the rate locked Mortgage amount only if supported by increased Net Operating Income and other factors (excluding higher than expected appraised value) and the Property can meet the minimum DCR and maximum LTV set forth in the early rate lock application. The amount of the increase must be at least the greater of $100,000 or 1% of the rate locked Mortgage amount and may not exceed 10% of the rate locked Mortgage amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller requests an increased Mortgage amount after full underwriting package review.
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, request is eligible for Freddie Mac discretion; if outside, request is not permitted.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 27.20 Early rate lock delivery – Increased Mortgage Amount (04/15/21)
source_reference.source_text: After Freddie Mac has reviewed the full underwriting package, the Seller may request that Freddie Mac increase the rate locked Mortgage amount if the full underwriting package demonstrates that, based on an increase in Net Operating Income and other factors (excluding a higher than expected appraised value), the Property can support an increased Mortgage amount and meet the minimum Debt Coverage Ratio and maximum Loan-to-Value Ratio set forth in the early rate lock application. The amount of the increase must be at least the greater of $100,000 or 1% of the rate locked Mortgage amount and may not exceed 10% of the rate locked Mortgage amount.

examples: Rate locked amount $8,000,000: minimum increase is $100,000 (greater of $100,000 or $80,000); maximum is $800,000.
Rate locked amount $50,000,000: minimum increase is $500,000 (greater of $100,000 or $500,000); maximum is $5,000,000.
Seller requests a 12% increase: exceeds 10% cap; request is not permitted under the Guide.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Request rejected; no increased amount approved

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: TEL Loan Term Max 35 Years LIHTC and 30 Years Non-LIHTC
business_rules.description: For Targeted Affordable Housing Tax-Exempt Loan (TEL) Mortgages, the maximum term and amortization period depend on whether the property is LIHTC: LIHTC Properties have a maximum term and amortization period of 35 years; non-LIHTC Properties have a maximum term and amortization period of 30 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to TEL Mortgages under Guide Chapter 25; depends on LIHTC status
business_rules.consequences: If within max, loan eligible on term; if exceeds max, loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 25 – Maximum term and amortization period (09/28/18)
source_reference.source_text: Maximum term and amortization period ( 09/28/18 )  For LIHTC Properties, the maximum term and amortization period is 35 years. For non -LIHTC Properties, the maximum term and amortization period is 30 years.

examples: TEL mortgage for a LIHTC property proposed at 35-year term and 35-year amortization: permitted.
TEL mortgage for a non-LIHTC property proposed at 32-year amortization: not permitted and must be reduced to 30 years or less.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric maxima stated for two property categories.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: TEL Cap Rate Adjustment Limited to 100 BPS Below Market Rate
business_rules.description: For a property with TEL financing, Freddie Mac may adjust the appraiser-recommended capitalization rate to reflect value attributable to the below-market mortgage interest rate. The adjusted capitalization rate must not be more than 100 basis points below the market capitalization rate for a conventional property. If there is a taxable component, the adjustment is done proportionately. No other adjustments to value based on LIHTC entitlement are permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when valuing property with TEL financing and using capitalization approach
business_rules.consequences: If adjustment within 100 bps, valuation approach acceptable; otherwise valuation not permitted
business_rules.exceptions: Taxable component allows proportional adjustment only

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 25 – Adjustments to the Capitalization Rate (09/28/18)
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac may adjust the Capitalization Rate recommended by the appraiser to take into account the value attributable to the below -market interest rate on the proposed Mortgage. The adjusted Capitalization Rate will not be more than 100 basis points below the market Capitalization Rate for a conventional property. If there is a taxable component to the financing, this adjustment will be done proportionately. Freddie Mac does not permit any other adjustments to value based on the Property’s entitlement to LIHTC.

examples: Market cap rate is 6.50%; Freddie Mac adjusted cap rate may be as low as 5.50% (100 bps reduction), but not lower.
TEL financing includes a taxable component; cap rate adjustment is applied proportionately to the taxable portion only.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 100 bps ceiling and explicit prohibition on other LIHTC adjustments.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: TEL rehab must stabilize within 24 months of origination
business_rules.description: For TEL Preservation Rehabilitation Mortgages, all work must be completed and the Property must reach stabilization within 24 months of the Origination Date, or if earlier, within the term of the TEL. This timeline is a hard outer limit for completion/stabilization under the stated clarification.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to TELs using Preservation Rehabilitation product with newly issued 4% LIHTC
business_rules.consequences: If met, TEL remains compliant; if missed, Mortgage fails TEL program requirement for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: No extension stated; earlier TEL term controls if sooner than 24 months

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 25, Preservation Rehabilitation for TEL Mortgages (08/17/23)
source_reference.source_text: All Preservation Rehabilitation Mortgages submitted for purchase under the TAH TEL Purchase Program must comply with the requirements of Section 19.2(e) as well as with the requirements of this chapter. T he following clarifications to the requirements of Section 19.2(e) apply to TELs:  • All work must be completed  and the Property must reach stabilization within 24 months of the Origination Date or, if earlier, within the term of the TEL.

examples: TEL origination date 2025-03-01: rehabilitation completion and stabilization must occur by 2027-03-01 unless the TEL term ends earlier.
TEL with a 18-month term: even though 24 months is allowed generally, stabilization must be achieved within 18 months (the earlier TEL term).
If stabilization occurs at 25 months after origination, the Mortgage violates the TEL clarification and risks ineligibility for purchase.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Soft Subordinate Debt Service Cap 75% of Surplus Cash Flow
business_rules.description: For soft subordinate debt in a Targeted Affordable Housing (TAH) Bond Credit Enhancement Mortgage transaction, any payment of debt service on soft subordinate debt must not, in the aggregate, exceed 75% of surplus cash flow after payment of (i) operating expenses, (ii) Replacement Reserve contributions, (iii) contributions to Reserve accounts (e.g., reserves for taxes and insurance), and (iv) debt service on the TAH Mortgage(s).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to TAH Bond Credit Enhancement Mortgages with soft subordinate debt
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded, structure is noncompliant and may be rejected or require restructuring
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 28 – Soft subordinate debt (Guide Bulletin Update 08/26/25)
source_reference.source_text: 2. Soft subordinate debt • There is no preset minimum combined DCR or maximum combined LTV. • Soft subordinate debt must mature at least six months after the maturity date of the last maturing TAH Mortgage. • Any payment of debt service on soft subordinate debt must not, in the aggregate, exceed 75 percent of surplus cash flow after the payment of operating expenses, Replacement Reserve contributions, contributions to Reserve accounts (for example, Reserves for taxes and insurance), and debt service on the TAH Mortgage(s).

examples: Surplus cash flow after expenses/reserves/TAH debt service is $100,000; soft subordinate debt service payments must be ≤ $75,000 in aggregate.
If surplus cash flow is $40,000, aggregate payments on soft subordinate debt must be ≤ $30,000; paying $32,000 violates the 75% cap.
If Replacement Reserve contributions are increased, surplus cash flow decreases; the 75% cap must be recalculated using the reduced surplus cash flow.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Preservation Rehab Work Cost Maximum 60000 Dollars Per Unit
business_rules.description: For the Preservation Rehabilitation product, Freddie Mac defines “Preservation Rehabilitation” as rehabilitation work costing no more than $60,000 per unit. Rehabilitation budgets exceeding $60,000 per unit do not meet the definition for this product as stated.
business_rules.conditions: When Mortgage is underwritten as Preservation Rehabilitation (Targeted Affordable)
business_rules.consequences: If met: qualifies for product definition; if violated: ineligible/needs restructure
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 19 e.1 Definition of Preservation Rehabilitation (12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac defines “Preservation Rehabilitation” as rehabilitation work costing no more than $60,000 per unit.

examples: 100-unit property with $5,500,000 rehab budget ($55,000/unit) meets the $60,000/unit cap.
40-unit property with $2,600,000 rehab budget ($65,000/unit) exceeds the cap and is not Preservation Rehabilitation as defined.
If scope is reduced to $2,400,000 ($60,000/unit), the Mortgage can meet the definition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac underwriting rejection or re-sizing required

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Escrow Priority Repairs When Cost Exceeds 0.25% or $25,000
business_rules.description: Each Priority Repair listed in the Loan Agreement must be escrowed when the total cost of all Priority Repairs exceeds 0.25% of the loan amount or $25,000, whichever is greater.
business_rules.conditions: When Priority Repairs are required by the Letter of Commitment and put in Loan Agreement
business_rules.consequences: If threshold exceeded, escrow is required; if not escrowed, loan terms are noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Seismic retrofits excluded from this waiver calculation

source_reference.source_text: Each Priority Repair listed in the Loan Agreement , as required by the Letter of C ommitment, must: • Clearly describe the work to be completed • Have a specific and realistic proposed completion date that reflects the urgency of the Priority Repair and the Borrower's plans and capacity • Be escrowed for when the total cost of all Priority Repairs exceeds 0.25 percent of the loan amount or $25,000, whichever is greater

examples: Loan amount is $10,000,000; 0.25% is $25,000. Total Priority Repairs cost $30,000, so escrow is required.
Loan amount is $30,000,000; 0.25% is $75,000. Total Priority Repairs cost $60,000, so escrow is not required under this threshold (unless other requirements apply).
Loan amount is $5,000,000; 0.25% is $12,500. Because the rule uses the greater of 0.25% or $25,000, escrow is required only when repairs exceed $25,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac may require escrow as a purchase condition
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit escrow threshold stated with percentage and dollar minimum; clear trigger and requirement.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Seismic Retrofit Escrow Must Equal 125% of Estimated Cost
business_rules.description: Seismic retrofits must be escrowed at 125% of the estimated cost in the Seismic Risk Assessment, regardless of the amount; seismic retrofits are excluded from the Priority Repair escrow waiver calculation.
business_rules.conditions: When a Priority Repair includes seismic retrofit work under the Seismic Risk Assessment
business_rules.consequences: If seismic work exists, escrow at 125% is mandatory; otherwise loan terms are noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Seismic retrofits are excluded from the waiver calculation above. Seismic retrofits must be escrowed at 125 percent of the estimated cost in the Seismic Risk Assessment, regardless of amount, as required in Sec tion 64.14

examples: Seismic Risk Assessment estimates retrofit cost at $80,000; escrow must be $100,000 (125% × $80,000).
Seismic retrofit cost estimate is $10,000; escrow is still required at $12,500 regardless of being below $25,000.
Priority Repairs total $20,000 plus seismic retrofit $15,000; seismic portion must still be escrowed at 125% even though overall Priority Repairs may not exceed waiver thresholds.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Highly explicit numeric requirement (125%) and 'regardless of amount' qualifier.

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Standby Letter of Credit Initial Term Minimums 6 Months or 12 Months
business_rules.description: A letter of credit must satisfy the Guide form requirements including: it must be a clean, irrevocable, unconditional standby letter of credit; name Freddie Mac as sole beneficiary; be in the amount determined by Freddie Mac; and have an initial term not less than specified minimums (e.g., not less than six months for certain early rate-lock applications, not less than twelve months for certain reserve/extended term obligations, and not less than 60 days following applicable maturity/period end for specified programs).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a letter of credit is required/used under the Guide for obligations
business_rules.consequences: Nonconforming LOC term/form requires correction/replacement; may delay delivery/approval
business_rules.exceptions: Different form rule noted for certain SBL obligations (stated in text)

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 11, Section 11.2(d) Form of letter of credit (06/29/17)
source_reference.source_text: The letter of credit must : 1. Be a clean, irrevocable, unconditional standby letter of credit 2. Be issued for the account of the Borrower in the case of a Borrower obligation, or for the Seller/Servicer’s account in the case of the Seller/Servicer’s obligation 3. Name Freddie Mac as the sole beneficiary 4. Be in the amount determined by Freddie Mac 5. Have an initial term of : • Not less than six months for a letter of credit issued in connection with an early rate -lock application that does not have an extended term • Not less than twelve months for a letter of credit issued in connection with a Rental Achievement Agreement or any other agreement for Reserves, debt service Reserve or an early rate -lock application with an extended term • Not less than 60 days following the maturity date

examples: Early rate-lock application without extended term proposes a 90-day LOC; rejected because initial term must be not less than six months.
Reserve agreement proposes an 11-month LOC; rejected because initial term must be not less than twelve months.
Forward Commitment-related LOC expires 30 days after maturity date; rejected because term must be not less than 60 days following the maturity date.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBL loss maximum equals 10% of principal balance as of origination date
business_rules.description: For any SBL Mortgage, the maximum Seller/Servicer loss for an SBL Loss Obligation (the “Loss Maximum”) is a hard cap equal to 10% of the principal balance of the SBL Mortgage as of the Origination Date. If calculated loss exceeds the Loss Maximum, Seller/Servicer liability is limited to the Loss Maximum; otherwise Seller/Servicer is liable up to the full calculated loss amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when calculating Seller/Servicer SBL Loss Obligation exposure
business_rules.consequences: If calculated loss exceeds 10% cap, liability is limited to Loss Maximum; if within cap, Seller/Servicer liable up to full calculated amount
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: c. Loss Maximum (08/18/22) The maximum Seller/Servicer loss with respect to an SBL Loss Obligation (“Loss Maximum”) for any SBL Mortgage will equal ten percent of the principal balance of the SBL Mortgage as of the Origination Date for the SBL Mortgage.

The maximum Seller /S ervicer  loss with respect to an SBL Loss Obligation (“Loss Maximum”) for any SBL Mortgage will equal ten percent of the principal balance of the SBL Mortgage as of the Origination Date for the SBL Mortgage. The amount of the SBL Loss Obligation will be calculated as provided in Section 46SBL.6(d).

examples: If principal balance at origination is $8,000,000, Loss Maximum = $800,000.
If calculated SBL Loss Obligation is $900,000 on that loan, Seller/Servicer liability is capped at $800,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification (loss reimbursement up to Loss Maximum)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric cap (10%) with clear measurement point (principal balance as of Origination Date).

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same Loss Maximum for SBL Loss Obligation exposure: the maximum Seller/Servicer loss equals 10% of the SBL Mortgage principal balance as of the Origination Date. Threshold (10%) and measurement point (principal balance as of Origination Date) are identical; context is identical (SBL Mortgages; Seller/Servicer loss obligation). Consequences align (liability capped at 10%).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_005_008 selected as primary: more complete explanatory language (explicitly frames as 'hard cap' on liability) while preserving identical numeric cap.

business_rules.rule_name: Forward Commitment Extension Limited to One 6-Month Term
business_rules.description: A Forward Commitment Maturity Date may be extended for one six-month period if all conditions to extension are fully and timely satisfied and the extension fee is paid as set forth in the Forward Commitment. Any extension request beyond the first six months is subject to Freddie Mac’s sole discretion, and Freddie Mac may condition approval on requirements it determines (including an extension fee amount determined by Freddie Mac).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller seeks to extend a Forward Commitment Maturity Date
business_rules.consequences: If within first 6 months, extension may be granted; beyond that, approval is discretionary
business_rules.exceptions: Subsequent extensions beyond first 6 months only by Freddie Mac sole discretion

source_reference.source_text: The Forward Commitment Maturity Date may be extended for one six-month period, upon full and timely satisfaction of each and all of the conditions to extension set forth herein and payment of the extension fee as set forth in the Forward Commitment. Any extension request beyond the first six months will be in Freddie Mac’s sole discretion and approval may be based on the satisfaction of any conditions that Freddie Mac determines in its sole discretion, including the payment of an extension fee in an amount to be determined by Freddie Mac.

examples: Seller requests a first extension of 6 months and pays the extension fee per the Forward Commitment; extension may be granted if all conditions are met.
Seller requests a second extension after the initial 6 months; Freddie Mac may approve or deny in its sole discretion and may require an additional fee amount it sets.
Seller seeks a 9-month first extension; not permitted because the first extension is limited to one 6-month period.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: E&O Deductible Maximum Higher of $250,000 or 10%
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer’s maximum deductible, self-insured retention (SIR), or combined deductible and SIR for any single mortgagee’s E&O loss must not exceed the higher of (i) $250,000 or (ii) 10% of the minimum E&O limit required by Freddie Mac per occurrence. The deductible/SIR cannot be calculated based on the actual limit of insurance in force.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller/Servicer maintains mortgagee’s E&O insurance coverage
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded, coverage is noncompliant; Freddie Mac may require correction/approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; Freddie Mac may review/approve policy terms

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 3 – Seller/Servicer Eligibility Requirements (E&O deductible/SIR)
source_reference.source_text: The maximum deductible or SIR or combined deductible and SIR allowed for any one E&O loss is the higher of (i) $250,000 or (ii) ten percent of the minimum limit of E&O insurance required by Freddie Mac per occurrence. The deductible or SIR or combined deductible and SIR may not be calculated based on the actual limit of insurance in force. Freddie Mac reserves the right to review and approve the terms of such a policy.

examples: Required minimum E&O per occurrence is $5,000,000; max deductible = higher of $250,000 or $500,000 → $500,000 cap; $600,000 deductible is noncompliant.
Required minimum E&O is $2,500,000; 10% is $250,000, so max deductible = $250,000; $300,000 deductible violates the rule.
Policy has $10,000,000 actual limit in force; Seller/Servicer cannot compute deductible cap using $10,000,000 instead of Freddie Mac’s minimum required limit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination risk; required corrective action plan

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_017_007 sets a maximum deductible/SIR rule for mortgagee’s E&O coverage and prohibits calculating it based on the actual limit in force. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_017_008 requires submission of acceptable proof (ACORD 25, certificate, declarations, policy) within 30 days of obtaining/renewing. Those documents are the primary data sources used by Counterparty Risk & Compliance to validate deductible/SIR compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_017_008 fails: (1) Freddie Mac cannot validate compliance with BR_017_007, (2) Seller/Servicer may be treated as out of compliance, (3) corrective action requests or escalations may occur until documentation is provided.

business_rules.rule_name: Qualified Mortgage Needs 80% FMV and 125% LTV
business_rules.description: An SBL Mortgage is deemed ineligible for inclusion as an asset of a securitization Secondary Market Transaction if it is not a “Qualified Mortgage.” For this purpose, “principally secured” means the fair market value (FMV) of the real property collateral is at least 80% of the outstanding principal balance (OPB) (i.e., loan-to-value ratio of 125% or less). This test must be satisfied both as of the Origination Date and when the SBL Mortgage is put into the applicable securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Mortgages evaluated for inclusion in securitization transactions
business_rules.consequences: If FMV < 80% of OPB (LTV > 125%), mortgage is ineligible for securitization asset inclusion
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 1. Qualified Mortgage. The SBL Mortgage will be deemed ineligible for inclusion as an asset of a Secondary Market Transaction that is a  securitization if the SBL Mortgage is not a “Qualified Mortgage.” For the purpose of this Section 46SBL.4(b), a Qualified Mortgage is a mortgage that is “principally secured” by an interest in real property, and “principally secured” means that the fair m arket value of the real property collateral for the SBL Mortgage is at least 80 percent of the outstanding principal balance of the SBL Mortgage (or a loan- to-value ratio of 125 percent or less), tested both as of the Origination Date and when the SBL Mortgage is put into the applicable securitization.

examples: Origination: OPB $8,000,000 and FMV $6,500,000 (FMV/OPB=81.25%); passes 80% test and may be eligible (subject to other criteria).
Securitization date: OPB $10,000,000 and FMV $7,500,000 (FMV/OPB=75%); fails and is deemed ineligible for securitization inclusion.
Origination passes but securitization-date test fails; the mortgage is deemed ineligible because both tests must be met.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit eligibility/ineligibility rule with two time-based tests and exact numeric thresholds.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Condemnation LTV Above 125% Triggers Ineligibility
business_rules.description: An SBL Mortgage is deemed ineligible for inclusion in a securitization if any portion of the property is released from the lien due to a Condemnation and the ratio of UPB to the value of the property (land and buildings only; excluding personal property and going-concern value) is greater than 125% immediately after the Condemnation and before any Restoration/repair (but taking into account planned Restoration/repair as if completed). If Freddie Mac enforces repurchase for this reason, Freddie Mac credits net condemnation proceeds/awards received, minus Freddie Mac’s costs/expenses related to the condemnation and repurchase.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lien release occurs due to Condemnation for an SBL Mortgage in securitization
business_rules.consequences: If UPB/value > 125%, mortgage is ineligible; repurchase may be enforced with proceeds crediting
business_rules.exceptions: Value is determined by Freddie Mac using commercially reasonable securitization valuation methods

source_reference.source_text: 2. Condemnation Valuation. The SBL Mortgage will be deemed ineligible for inclusion as an asset of a Secondary Market Transaction that is a securitization if any portion of the Property is released from the lien of the SBL Mortgage in connection with a Condemnation (as defined in the Loan Agreement) and the ratio of (i) the unpaid principal balance of the SBL Mortgage to (ii) the value of the Property (taking into account only the related land and buildings and not any personal property or going- concern value), as determined by Freddie Mac in its sole and absolute discretion based on a commercially reasonable valuation method permitted in connection with a securitization, is greater than 125 percent immediately after the Condemnation and before any Restoration (as defined in the Loan Agreement) or repair of the Property (but taking into account any planned Restoration or repair of the Property  as if such planned Restoration or repair were completed). If Freddie Mac enforces an SBL Repurchase Obligation with respect to an SBL Mortgage for this reason, Freddie Mac will credit to the Seller /Servicer any net proceeds or awards from such Condemnation received by Freddie Mac, less any costs and expenses incurred by Freddie Mac in connection with the Condemnation and the Repurchase.

examples: After condemnation, UPB $9.0M and post-condemnation value $7.0M; ratio 128.6% (>125%); mortgage becomes ineligible for securitization inclusion.
UPB $6.0M and value $5.0M immediately after condemnation; ratio 120%; does not trigger this ineligibility test.
Freddie Mac enforces repurchase due to >125% ratio and received $300,000 net award; Freddie Mac credits the net award minus its condemnation/repurchase costs.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Threshold (125%) and valuation scope/exclusions are explicit; proceeds credit is explicitly described.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Repurchase for 60-Day or 120-Day Payment Delinquency
business_rules.description: As of March 30, 2020, a Seller/Servicer is subject to an SBL Repurchase Obligation for a monetary default when required loan payments are delinquent for the applicable consecutive-day period, calculated without considering any grace or cure period in the loan documents: (i) 120 consecutive days for SBL Mortgages where the borrower participates in COVID-19 forbearance, beginning on the due date of the first required payment after forbearance ends; (ii) 60 consecutive days for SBL Mortgages that include a COVID-19 Debt Service Reserve, beginning on the required payment due date; and (iii) 60 consecutive days for all other SBL Mortgages, beginning on the required payment due date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Mortgages when borrower fails to pay/deposit a Required Loan Payment
business_rules.consequences: If delinquency meets the stated consecutive-day period, Seller/Servicer is subject to repurchase
business_rules.exceptions: Delinquency is calculated without any grace/cure periods in Loan Documents

source_reference.source_text: c. Monetary default  (04/22/25) As of March 30, 2020, the Seller /Servicer  is subject to an SBL Repurchase Obligation if an SBL Borrower is in default under the applicable SBL Mortgage for failure to pay or deposit when due any amount required by the Loan Documents (“Required Loan Payment(s)”) when the Required Loan Payment has been delinquent for the period of time specified in the chart below (“Delinquency Period”): Delinquency Period SBL Mortgage for which Borrower participates in the COVID -19 forbearance  Applicable Delinquency Period*  120 consecutive days beginning on the date the Required Loan Payment following the end of the forbearance period was due ... SBL Mortgage includes a COVID -19 Debt Service Reserve 60 consecutive days beginning on the date the Required Loan Payment was due ... All other SBL Mortgages 60 consecutive days beginning on the date the Required Loan Payment was due (a “60 Day Delinquency”) *Calculated without considering any grace or cure period that may be applicable to the Required Loan Payment in the Loan Documents.

examples: Borrower exits COVID forbearance; first post-forbearance payment due April 1 is unpaid through July 30 (120 consecutive days); repurchase obligation is triggered.
SBL Mortgage has a COVID-19 Debt Service Reserve; payment due May 1 is unpaid through June 30 (60 consecutive days); repurchase obligation is triggered.
Loan documents provide a 10-day grace period, but the 60/120-day count ignores the grace period per the rule.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Chart-based delinquency periods are explicit and include the ‘no grace/cure’ calculation rule.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_022_005 limits SBL Repurchase Obligations to events occurring during the defined Repurchase Period. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_022_008 states the conditions for being 'subject to an SBL Repurchase Obligation' for monetary default. Therefore, repurchase-period eligibility must be evaluated before concluding BR_022_008 creates an enforceable repurchase obligation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_022_005 is not checked: (1) Seller may incorrectly accept/contest a repurchase demand, (2) disputes and appeals may be mishandled, (3) Freddie Mac enforcement actions may escalate due to improper posture, (4) financial reporting and reserves may be misstated.

business_rules.rule_name: Seller counsel must have 2 years multifamily closings
business_rules.description: An attorney representing the Seller/Servicer for structuring, origination, or delivery of a Mortgage (or transfer/special servicing request) must have at least two years of experience closing commercial or multifamily housing mortgage loans on behalf of lenders.
business_rules.conditions: When Seller/Servicer retains legal counsel for a Mortgage or transfer
business_rules.consequences: Meets: counsel qualifies; Violated: Freddie Mac may reject counsel work/product
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 6.2 Qualifications of Seller/Servicer's legal counsel (02/28/13) An attorney who represents the Seller/Servicer in connection with the structuring, origination or delivery of a Mortgage or in connection with a Transfer of Ownership or other Special Servicing Request must have a minimum of two years' experience closing commercial or multifamily housing mortgage loans on behalf of lenders.

examples: Seller uses counsel with 5 years multifamily closings to deliver an SBL Mortgage; requirement satisfied.
Seller retains a newly licensed attorney with 1 year closing experience for a delivery; must replace/add qualified attorney.
Seller uses a 3-year commercial mortgage closer who has no multifamily closings; still qualifies because commercial OR multifamily is allowed.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Random unit inspection sample is 5% with min 5 max 15 units
business_rules.description: For a comprehensive property inspection, the inspector must inspect a minimum number of randomly selected occupied and vacant units equal to 5% of total occupied and vacant units, subject to minimum and maximum unit counts. Standard minimum is 5 units and maximum is 15 units. For SBL Mortgages where the Property has 50 units or less, the minimum is 3 units and maximum is 15 units.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Servicer-required property inspections with occupied/vacant unit sampling.
business_rules.consequences: If met, inspection meets minimum sampling; if violated, report is deficient and may be rejected.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for occupied/vacant sampling minima/maxima.

source_reference.source_text: Taking into account the cross -section of unit types and locations described above, the inspector must inspect at least the following number of units: • Occupied and vacant units: o 5 percent of total occupied and vacant units, with a minimum of five units and a maximum of 15 units o For SBL Mortgages where the Property has a total of 50 units or less: 5 percent of total occupied and vacant units, with a minimum of three units and a maximum of 15 units

examples: 100-unit property: 5% = 5 units; inspector must inspect at least 5 occupied/vacant units (cap 15).
20-unit SBL property: 5% = 1 unit, but minimum is 3 units; inspector must inspect 3 units.
400-unit property: 5% = 20 units, but maximum is 15; inspector inspects 15 units.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: SBL subordinate financing requires at least 3 years remaining term and minimum $750,000
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage securitized in an SBL Securitization, Subordinate Financing is permitted only if (among other conditions) the SBL Mortgage has three years or more remaining in its loan term, the Subordinate Financing amount is not less than $750,000, and the Subordinate Financing term is at least the remaining term of the SBL Mortgage but may not exceed 30 years.
business_rules.conditions: When requesting/approving Subordinate Financing on an SBL Mortgage in securitization
business_rules.consequences: If any threshold fails, Subordinate Financing is not permitted/approvable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Section 43.30(a) Conditions for Freddie Mac approval (Items 6-8)
source_reference.source_text: 6. The SBL Mortgage has three years or more remaining in its loan term. 7. The Subordinate Financing is not less than $750,000. 8. The Subordinate Financing has a loan term equal to or longer than the remaining loan term of the SBL Mortgage but cannot exceed 30 years.

examples: An SBL Mortgage has 3.5 years remaining; proposed subordinate loan is $900,000 with a 10-year term; thresholds in this rule are met (other conditions still apply).
An SBL Mortgage has 2.8 years remaining; subordinate financing is $1,200,000; not permitted because remaining term is under 3 years.
An SBL Mortgage has 5 years remaining; subordinate financing is $700,000; not permitted because amount is below $750,000.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: One rule sets a broad prohibition for securitized mortgages; the other provides conditional permission for a specific securitized SBL scenario. This is either (a) an intended exception (e.g., 026_008 applies only when the subordinate financing is the permitted Supplemental Mortgage Product), or (b) a true inconsistency between guide sections. Either way, rule logic must be reconciled in automation and policy guidance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unresolved, the Servicer may (i) incorrectly deny permissible supplemental subordinate financing (business disruption), or (ii) incorrectly approve subordinate financing that is prohibited under securitization rules (high compliance breach, forced remediation, Freddie Mac enforcement/remedies).

business_rules.rule_name: SBL subordinate financing combined LTV must be at most 80% and DCR at least 1.25x
business_rules.description: For Subordinate Financing on an SBL Mortgage securitized in an SBL Securitization, the combined loan amount after the Subordinate Financing must not produce an LTV Ratio exceeding 80%, and combined debt service must not produce a DCR below 1.25x. For purposes of calculating LTV, the combined loan amount must be the aggregate outstanding principal balances of the SBL Mortgage, any other previously originated subordinate financing, and the proposed subordinate loan amount.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating SBL Subordinate Financing under Section 43.30 conditions
business_rules.consequences: If combined LTV > 80% or DCR < 1.25x, Subordinate Financing is not permitted/approvable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Section 43.30(a)(10) LTV/DCR thresholds and combined loan amount definition
source_reference.source_text: 10. The combined loan amount after the making of the Subordinate Financing does not result in a Loan-to-Value (LTV) Ratio that exceeds 80 percent and the combined debt service after the making of the Subordinate Financing does not result in a Debt Coverage Ratio (DCR) that is below 1.25x, using the following to calculate the LTV and DCR: • The combined loan amount used to calculate the LTV must be the aggregate outstanding principal balances of all of the following: o The SBL Mortgage o Any other previously originated subordinate financing o The proposed loan amount of the Subordinate Financing

examples: SBL UPB $20,000,000 + existing subordinate $1,000,000 + proposed $2,000,000 = $23,000,000 combined; if value is $30,000,000 then LTV=76.67% and passes LTV test (must also meet DCR≥1.25x).
Combined loan amount is $24,500,000 and value is $30,000,000; LTV=81.67% and fails (cannot approve as stated).
Projected combined debt service produces DCR=1.20x; fails because DCR is below 1.25x even if LTV is ≤80%.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 026_008 defines term/amount/remaining-term thresholds for permitted Subordinate Financing in an SBL Securitization. 026_009 separately defines combined leverage and cashflow thresholds (LTV and DCR). In practice, a Servicer’s approval workflow must satisfy BOTH sets to determine permissibility; they function as parallel mandatory checks for the same decision.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 026_008 is not satisfied/checked, the Servicer may approve Subordinate Financing that is structurally ineligible (e.g., too short remaining term) even if LTV/DCR pass, leading to an impermissible transaction and potential Freddie Mac remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_026_009 is meaningful only if subordinate financing is allowed to be considered. BR_SERVICES_PROHIBITION_025_005 may categorically bar consideration unless the request falls within the Supplemental Mortgage Product exception. The knowledge graph should force a rule selection step: determine whether 026_009 is reachable under the 025_005 exception logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unresolved, automation may approve a transaction based on 80% LTV / 1.25x DCR that is still prohibited for securitization purposes, or may incorrectly block a permitted supplemental transaction by never evaluating 026_009.

business_rules.rule_name: Eligible depository must meet 2 ratings: S&P A/A-1, Moody’s A2/P-1, Fitch A/F1
business_rules.description: An FDIC-insured depository institution is an eligible depository for custodial accounts only if it satisfies at all times the minimum rating requirements of at least two of the specified rating services for both (i) long-term senior unsecured debt obligations or long-term deposit ratings and (ii) short-term unsecured debt obligations or short-term deposit ratings. Minimums: Standard & Poor’s (Long-term A; Short-term A-1), Moody’s (Long-term A2; Short-term P-1), Fitch (Long-term A; Short-term F1).
business_rules.conditions: When Servicer selects/monitors an FDIC-insured depository for custodial funds
business_rules.consequences: Meets: depository eligible; Violated: depository ineligible and custodial placement fails
business_rules.exceptions: Holding company/affiliate risk may render otherwise-rated institution ineligible

source_reference.source_text: An FDIC -insured depository institution must satisfy at all times the minimum rating requirements of at least two of the rating services identified below for (i) long -term senior unsecured debt obligation ratings or long -term deposit ratings and (ii) short -term unsecured debt obligation ratings or short -term deposit ratings to be considered an eligible depository. MINIMUM RATING REQUIREMENTS Standard & Poor’s Ratings  A  A-1 Moody’s Investor’s Service  A2  P-1 Fitch Ratings  A  F1

examples: Depository has S&P A/A-1 and Fitch A/F1; qualifies (2 agencies meet both long/short thresholds).
Depository has only Moody’s A2/P-1 but no second agency rating; does not qualify as eligible depository.
Depository meets ratings but becomes affected by a troubled affiliate; Freddie Mac may deem it ineligible case-by-case.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension / required movement of custodial funds

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Critical reserve disbursement needs risk rating 6
business_rules.description: The Servicer may only authorize a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a “critical replacement” when all listed conditions are met, including: (i) the Property secures a Mortgage with a Risk Rating of six or less and the Mortgage is not in default; (ii) the disbursement is for upgrades that add value and prevent an adverse condition; (iii) a supervisor or higher-level manager approves; and (iv) no credit enhancement is provided by any non-party to the Mortgage. If conditions are not met, Freddie Mac approval must be sought.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer is considering authorizing a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a critical replacement item.
business_rules.consequences: If met, servicer may authorize; if violated, Freddie Mac approval is required and unauthorized disbursement may be a servicing breach.
business_rules.exceptions: If any condition not met, must seek Freddie Mac approval.

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer may only authorize a disbursement for a critical replacement described in Section 39.6(b)(2) when all of the following additional conditions are met:  i. The Property secures a Mortgage that has a Risk Rating of six or less and is not in default.  ii. The disbursement is for capital replacement items that are upgrades which add value to the continued operation of the Property (“critical replacement”), if each of the following conditions is satisfied: iii. A supervisor or higher -level manager on the Servicer’s staff has approved the disbursement for the additional capital replacement items.  iv. No credit enhancement has been provided by any person or entity that is not a party to the Mortgage.

examples: Loan risk rating is 5 and current; servicer supervisor approves reserve use to replace failing boiler with higher-efficiency unit that prevents imminent outages—authorized without Freddie Mac pre-approval.
Loan risk rating is 7 (above 6); servicer must submit a General Servicing Request to obtain Freddie Mac approval before disbursing reserve funds.
A third-party guarantor provides a credit enhancement not a party to the mortgage; condition (iv) fails and Freddie Mac approval is required before disbursement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicing breach; Freddie Mac approval required; remediation

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 040_007 conditions authorization of a 'critical replacement' disbursement on the Mortgage not being in default. 002_002 defines when a Mortgage Default occurs and when the mortgage is classified as a Defaulted Mortgage. The disbursement-authorization logic depends on the correct default classification.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 002_002 is misapplied, the Servicer may improperly authorize disbursements while the loan is in default (servicing breach) or improperly deny disbursements when the loan is not in default (unnecessary operational disruption).

dependencies.rationale: 040_006 requires delivering executed custodial account letter agreements shortly after funding of a Replacement Reserve. 040_007 governs when the Servicer may authorize a Replacement Reserve disbursement for a critical replacement.
