In practice, disbursement controls and approvals are typically executed after custodial account setup/documentation is completed, to ensure controlled custody and audit trail.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 040_006 is missed, disbursements may proceed with deficient custodial documentation, increasing audit/compliance findings and potential Freddie Mac-directed remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Floating-rate Note late charge 5% after 5-day grace period
business_rules.description: For a Floating-Rate Mortgage, the Note must provide that if an installment of principal (if applicable) and interest is received more than five days after its Due Date, a late charge accrues equal to 5% of the installment amount. If applicable law requires a longer/shorter minimum time period or a lower maximum late charge, the Seller must use the minimum time period and the maximum late charge permitted by applicable law. Late-charge provisions may not be changed without Freddie Mac’s prior approval.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Floating-Rate Mortgage Notes under Chapter 17.3(f).
business_rules.consequences: If missing/noncompliant, Freddie Mac may require correction or reject purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If state law limits timing/amount, use law-permitted minimum/maximum.

source_reference.source_text: The Floating -Rate Mortgage Note must provide that if an installment of principal (if applicable) and interest is received more than five days after its Due Date, a late charge will accrue equal to 5 percent of the amount of that installment. If applicable law prohibits imposition of a late charge until an installment is more than five days past due or provides for a maximum late charge of less than 5 percent, the Seller must use the minimum time period and the maximum late charge permitted by applicable law. The Seller/Servicer may not change any provisions regarding late charges without Freddie Mac's prior approval.

examples: Payment due 6/1 received 6/8: late charge = 5% of the 6/1 installment, unless state law caps it lower.
State law caps late charge at 4%: Note must use 4% (not 5%) and follow any statutory waiting period.
Seller changes late-charge clause post-closing without Freddie Mac approval: noncompliance requiring remediation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or corrective action demand
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must-language with exact 5-day and 5% thresholds and law-limitation clause.

examples: Executed note with fixed interest rate and 30-year amortization
Modified note executed as part of a loan modification agreement
Note endorsed to Freddie Mac upon delivery

business_rules.rule_name: Default interest minimum Note rate plus 4% after 30 days past due
business_rules.description: For a Floating-Rate Mortgage, the Note must provide that if any installment remains past due for 30 days or more, or if the Borrower is in default under any other provision of the Note or Loan Documents, the Note bears default interest at a rate not less than 4% per annum in excess of the Note rate. If applicable law provides a lower maximum default interest rate, the Seller must use the maximum default interest permitted by applicable law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Floating-Rate Mortgage installment is ≥30 days past due or other default.
business_rules.consequences: If noncompliant, Freddie Mac may require Note correction or reject purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If state law caps default rate below Note+4%, use the law-permitted maximum.

source_reference.source_text: The Floating -Rate Mortgage Note must also provide that if any installment due under the Floating -Rate Mortgage Note remains past due for 30 days or more, or if the Borrower is in default under any other provision of the Floating -Rate Mortgage Note or Loan Documents, the Note will bear default interest at a rate that is not less than 4 percent per annum in excess of the Note rate. If applicable law provides for a maximum rate of default interest that is lower than 4 percent per annum in excess of the Floating-Rate Mortgage Note rate, the Seller must use the maximum rate of default interest permitted by applicable law.

examples: Note rate 6.25% and payment is 35 days past due: default interest must be at least 10.25%, unless capped by law.
Borrower violates a non-monetary covenant (other default): default interest trigger must apply per Note terms.
State law caps default interest at Note+3%: Seller must use the capped rate (not Note+4%).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or corrective action demand
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit trigger (30 days) and minimum default rate (Note+4%) with legal cap exception.

examples: Executed note with fixed interest rate and 30-year amortization
Modified note executed as part of a loan modification agreement
Note endorsed to Freddie Mac upon delivery

business_rules.rule_name: Early rate lock good faith deposit set at 1%–3% with $25k–$50k floors
business_rules.description: Unless the early rate lock application specifies otherwise, the standard early rate lock good faith deposit (GFD) amounts are: (1) Floating-rate loan: cash/letter of credit = greater of 1% of maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000; demand note = greater of 2% or $25,000. (2) Fixed-rate loan: cash/letter of credit with early rate lock 9 months or less = greater of 2% or $50,000; cash/letter of credit with lock >9 months and <12 months = greater of 3% or $50,000; demand note = greater of 3% or $50,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using an early rate lock application and no custom GFD is set.
business_rules.consequences: If GFD is under-collected, Seller is noncompliant and may face remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Different amount applies if specified in the early rate lock application.

source_reference.source_text: Unless otherwise set forth in the early rate lock application, the standard good faith deposit amounts are as follows: Floating -rate loan Cash or letter of credit Greater of 1% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000 Demand note Greater of 2% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $25,000 Fixed -rate Loan Cash or letter of credit (Early Rate Lock 9 months or less) Greater of 2% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000 Cash or letter of credit (Early Rate Lock > 9 months and < 12 months) Greater of 3% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000 Demand note Greater of 3% of the maximum Mortgage amount or $50,000

examples: Floating-rate, max mortgage $3,000,000: cash GFD = greater of $30,000 (1%) or $25,000 → $30,000.
Fixed-rate, 8-month lock, max mortgage $1,500,000: cash GFD = greater of $30,000 (2%) or $50,000 → $50,000.
Fixed-rate, 10-month lock, max mortgage $5,000,000: cash GFD = greater of $150,000 (3%) or $50,000 → $150,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Contract remedies; potential breakage fee recovery
business_rules.extraction_notes: Table provides exact percentages, dollar floors, and lock-term conditions.

examples: New acquisition loan application for a multifamily property
Refinance loan application submitted via a delegated Seller/Servicer
Loan application declined triggering an adverse action notice

business_rules.rule_name: TIC borrower limited to 10 owners and each owner must be an SPE
business_rules.description: A tenancy-in-common (TIC) may be an eligible Borrower only if: (1) there are not more than 10 TIC owners, and (2) each TIC owner is a Single Purpose Entity (SPE) regardless of loan size.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower ownership structure is a tenancy-in-common (TIC).
business_rules.consequences: If >10 owners or any owner not an SPE, TIC Borrower is not eligible.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: 9.12 Tenancy -in-common (TIC) ( 04/15/21 ) A TIC may be an eligible Borrower if it meets all of the following conditions. a. Conditions applicable to TIC owners (06/30/15) • There may not be more than 10 TIC owners. • Each TIC owner must be a Single Purpose Entity (SPE) regardless of the size of the loan.

examples: TIC with 8 owners, each an SPE LLC: eligible (subject to other Guide requirements).
TIC with 11 owners: ineligible due to exceeding the 10-owner maximum.
TIC with 6 owners but one owner is an individual (not an SPE): ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit hard cap (10) and entity-type requirement (SPE).

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Qualifying Manager requires 5 years and up to 10,000 units
business_rules.description: Where a TIC Agreement requires a property manager, the Manager must be a "Qualifying Manager" meeting all stated criteria: reputable management company with at least 5 years’ experience in managing multifamily properties in the relevant geographic area; for at least 5 years prior to engagement has managed at least 5 multifamily properties; at engagement manages same property type units equal to the lesser of 10,000 units or 5× the Property’s unit count; and is not subject to bankruptcy or similar insolvency proceeding.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Borrower structure uses TIC Agreement requiring a Manager
business_rules.consequences: If not met, Freddie Mac may require replacement manager or deem structure noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9 – Borrower/Borrower Principal Fundamentals; TIC Agreement – Management
source_reference.source_text: A “Qualifying Manager” is a property manager acceptable to Lender which meets all of the following requirements: o Is a reputable management company having at least five years’ experience in the management of multifamily properties and in the metropolitan area or other appropriate geographic area in which the Property is located o Has, for at least five years prior to its engagement as property manager, managed at least five multifamily properties o At the time of its engagement as property manager, has units of the same property type as the Property equal to the lesser of (A) 10,000 units and (B) five times the number of units the Property has o Is not the subject of a bankruptcy or similar insolvency proceeding

examples: Property has 200 units; manager must manage same-type units ≥ 1,000 (5×200) at engagement and meet 5-year experience criteria.
Property has 3,000 units; 5× would be 15,000 but cap applies, so manager must manage ≥ 10,000 units of same type.
Proposed manager has 6 years in market but has managed only 3 multifamily properties in prior 5 years; manager is not a Qualifying Manager and must be replaced.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Definition-style eligibility criteria with multiple explicit numeric thresholds (5 years, 5 properties, 10,000 units, 5× units).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

dependencies.rationale: 003_009 establishes when a tenancy-in-common (TIC) borrower structure is eligible (<=10 owners; each owner SPE). 006_004 applies 'where a TIC Agreement requires a property manager' and defines Qualifying Manager criteria. In workflow terms, once a deal is identified as TIC (and proceeds under TIC eligibility), the Servicer/Seller must evaluate the manager qualification gate if the TIC agreement requires a manager.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 003_009 TIC context is not correctly identified/validated, 006_004 may be missed, leading to an unqualified manager under a TIC arrangement and resulting in required replacement or noncompliance.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL guarantor average FICO score minimum 680
business_rules.description: For SBL Borrower Principal/Guarantor that is an individual, the individual must have an average FICO score of 680 or better across all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion, Experian). If fewer than three FICO scores are available, then all available FICO scores must be 680 or better.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBL Borrower Principal/Guarantor is an individual.
business_rules.consequences: Meets threshold: eligible on FICO; fails: Freddie may not approve principal/guarantor.
business_rules.exceptions: If fewer than 3 scores exist, all available scores must be ≥680.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9SBL – SBL Borrower/Borrower Principal Fundamentals (Guide Bulletin Update 12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: • If an individual, must have an average FICO® score of 680 or better with all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion or Experian) . If less than three FICO scores are available, all FICO scores must be 680 or better

examples: Guarantor has Equifax 690, TransUnion 675, Experian 685 (avg 683.3): meets the 680 average requirement.
Guarantor has only two scores: 679 and 702: fails because not all available scores are ≥680.
Guarantor has three scores 720, 710, 700 (avg 710): meets and can proceed on this criterion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Refusal to approve Borrower Principal/Guarantor; purchase ineligibility

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Foreign guarantor U.S. bank liquidity minimum 5% OPB
business_rules.description: When there is no financially compliant U.S. Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor(s) must maintain minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts equal to 5% of the original principal balance of the SBL Mortgage (or such other amount as Freddie Mac may require). The bank(s) holding the account must be acceptable to Freddie Mac, and the requirement may be satisfied in the aggregate by the Foreign Guarantor(s), exclusive of any U.S. Guarantor liquidity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when no financially compliant U.S. Guarantor exists for an SBL Mortgage.
business_rules.consequences: Meets liquidity: condition satisfied; fails: Freddie may deny approval of Foreign Guarantor.
business_rules.exceptions: Freddie may require an amount other than 5%; banks must be acceptable.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9SBL – Foreign Borrower Principal/Foreign Guarantor (02/22/24)
source_reference.source_text: o Maintain a minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts equal to five percent of the original principal balance of the SBL Mortgage, or such other amount as Freddie Mac may require; the bank(s) holding the account must be acceptable to Freddie Mac . This requirement may be satisfied in the aggregate by the Foreign Guarantor (s) exclusive of any U.S. Guarantor Liquidity.

examples: Original principal balance is $8,000,000; required U.S. bank liquidity is at least $400,000 across acceptable U.S. banks.
Two foreign guarantors hold $250,000 and $200,000 in U.S. bank accounts (total $450,000) for a $8,000,000 loan: meets in aggregate.
Foreign guarantor holds sufficient cash in a non-U.S. bank only: fails because liquidity must be in one or more U.S. bank accounts acceptable to Freddie.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Refusal to approve Foreign Guarantor; purchase/commitment denial

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Seniors housing borrower needs 10 years and 500 units
business_rules.description: For Freddie Mac Multifamily Seniors Housing Mortgages, a Seniors Housing Borrower or Key Borrower Principal must demonstrate eligibility by meeting all criteria: (1) own or manage comparable facilities in a similar property type in similar or adjoining markets, (2) have ten or more years' ownership or management experience, and (3) own or manage a minimum of five properties with at least 500 units.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Seniors Housing Mortgages during underwriting review
business_rules.consequences: Meets criteria: borrower eligible; violates: mortgage not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; Freddie Mac discretion may apply to mixed services

source_reference.source_text: c. Eligible Borrower (0 4/13/23 ) A Seniors Housing Borrower or Key Borrower Principal must demonstrate eligibility by meeting all of the following criteria:  • Own or manage comparable facilities in a similar property type (Independent Living Property, Assisted Living Residence, Skilled Nursing Property or CCRC) in similar or adjoining markets  • Have ten or more years' ownership or management experience  • Own or manage a minimum of five properties with at least 500 units

examples: Borrower principal has 12 years managing assisted living and owns 6 properties totaling 650 units: eligible.
Borrower principal has 15 years experience but owns only 3 properties totaling 420 units: ineligible.
Borrower has 10+ years and 5 properties/500+ units but none in similar/adjoining markets: ineligible.
Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: Non-profit Sponsor Needs 3 Years and 3 Properties Experience
business_rules.description: When a non-profit entity is the Borrower or Key Borrower Principal, it must have a minimum of three years’ experience in the same capacity it will have for the proposed transaction and must have experience acquiring, developing, or owning a minimum of three properties.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a non-profit entity is Borrower or Key Borrower Principal
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: non-profit experience requirement satisfied; violated: not eligible without Freddie Mac risk mitigation/approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9, Section 9.2(f) (as shown on Chapter 9 Page 6-7 update 04/21/26)
source_reference.source_text: The following requirements apply when a non-profit entity is the Borrower or the Key Borrower Principal: • The non-profit Borrower/ Key Borrower Principal must have a minimum of three years’ experience o In the same capacity that it will have for the proposed transaction, and o Acquiring, developing or owning a minimum of three properties.

examples: A non-profit property owner with 7 years owning 5 LIHTC properties qualifies on experience.
A non-profit formed 18 months ago with one acquired property fails the 3-year/3-property minimum and is not eligible without Freddie Mac direction.
A non-profit with 10 years as a service provider but only 1 year as an owner fails the “same capacity” requirement for an ownership role.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric minimums (3 years, 3 properties) with clear applicability to non-profit borrower/principal.

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: SBL Physical Risk Report must inspect 10% units and at least 3 total
business_rules.description: For an SBL Physical Risk Report interior review, the consultant must inspect sufficient units to accurately evaluate each building and its systems and may not inspect fewer than three units. The consultant must ensure all buildings and unit types are reviewed and must inspect: (i) 100% of all commercial units; and (ii) at least 10% of all residential units, including at least 50% of vacant units and at least 50% of Down Units. If Down Unit conditions vary considerably, additional units must be inspected to adequately identify rent-ready costs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Physical Risk Report interior inspections of dwelling/commercial units
business_rules.consequences: If inspection sampling meets thresholds, report satisfies minimum scope; if not, report is deficient and must be corrected/replaced before reliance
business_rules.exceptions: Must inspect additional Down Units if conditions vary considerably

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 62SBL, Section 62SBL – b. Dwelling units, commercial units and building interior (06/30/16)
source_reference.source_text: The consultant must inspect sufficient units to accurately evaluate the condition of each building and its systems and in no case may inspect fewer than three units. In addition, the consultant must select the units to be inspected and ensure that all buildings and unit types are reviewed and that all of the following are inspected: • 100 percent of all commercial units • At least 10 percent of all residential units, to include: o At least 50 percent of vacant units, and o At least 50 percent of Down Units

examples: Property has 40 residential units and 2 commercial units: consultant must inspect both commercial units and at least 4 residential units, but also at least 3 total units minimum applies.

Property has 20 residential units with 6 vacant units: at least 2 of the inspected residential units must be vacant (≥50% of vacant units within the inspected set), while still meeting ≥10% residential overall.
Property has 30 residential units with 4 Down Units whose conditions differ widely: consultant must inspect additional Down Units beyond the 50% minimum to estimate rent-ready costs accurately.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric sampling thresholds (3 units, 100%, 10%, 50%).

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Property occupancy must be at least 90% for 3 consecutive months before closing and as of delivery date
business_rules.description: For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90% of the living units (or a higher level if required by the applicable purchase program/product to cover debt service and other required expenses) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property. Failure to satisfy this occupancy condition may render the Mortgage ineligible for delivery/purchase until cured.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to multifamily properties during underwriting prior to closing and delivery
business_rules.consequences: If occupancy meets threshold, property satisfies occupancy condition; if below threshold, mortgage may be ineligible for purchase/delivery until cured
business_rules.exceptions: Higher than 90% may be required by product/purchase program to cover expenses

source_reference.source_text: 8.4 Occupancy (02/07/05) For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90 percent of the living units (or such higher level as may be necessary to cover debt service and pay all other expenses at the level required by the applicable purchase program or product) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property.

8.4 Occupancy (02/07/05) For the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90 percent of the living units (or such higher level as may be necessary to cover debt service and pay all other expenses at the level required by the applicable purchase program or product) must have been occupied at rent levels that support the Freddie Mac Underwriting Value of the Property.

examples: If occupancy is 89% in any of the three months prior to closing, the condition is not met.
If a program requires higher occupancy than 90% to cover required expenses, the higher program threshold governs.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 90% and three-month temporal requirement; includes product-driven higher-threshold clause.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same Minimum Occupancy requirement: for the three consecutive months prior to loan closing and as of the Delivery Date, at least 90% of living units must be occupied at rent levels supporting Freddie Mac Underwriting Value (or higher if required by the applicable program). Same timeframe, same 90% threshold, same delivery/closing context, same consequence (potential ineligibility). Wording differences are purely stylistic and rule_type labeling (constraint vs eligibility) does not change the business logic.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MULTIFAMILY_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_005_009 selected as primary: equal completeness, but retained per priority 5 (earlier rule ID) with no loss of detail.

business_rules.rule_name: MHC rent late grace 5 days and cure 10 days if none
business_rules.description: For MHC tenant protections, an Applicable MHC Resident must have a 5-day grace period for late rent. The resident must have the right to cure a rent-payment default within the lease’s cure period (if any). If the lease has no cure period for rent-payment default, the resident must have the right to cure within 10 days after expiration of the 5-day grace period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to MHC pad site lease terms for Applicable MHC Residents
business_rules.consequences: If not met, tenant protections are noncompliant and may prevent Freddie Mac purchase
business_rules.exceptions: If requirement violates applicable law, it is void per Guide language

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 22 – Originating a Manufactured Housing Community Mortgage; Tenant Protections item 3
source_reference.source_text: 3. Applicable MHC Resident is entitled to a 5 -day grace period for the failure to timely pay rent and has the right to cure any default in the payment of rent within the cure period set forth in its lease, if any.   If no cure period for a default in the payment of rent exists in its lease, then Applicable MHC Resident has the right to cure any default in the payment of rent within 10 days after the expiration of the 5 -day grace period described above.

examples: Rent due on the 1st; lease provides a 7-day cure period. Resident must still receive at least a 5-day grace period, and can cure within the lease cure period.
Lease is silent on cure; resident must be allowed to cure through day 15 (5-day grace + 10 additional days).
Lease provides only a 2-day grace period; this violates the required 5-day minimum and must be amended.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Two explicit timeframes (5-day grace; 10-day cure after grace when no cure period exists).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac purchase ineligibility until cured

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning and Building Code Conformity Required
business_rules.description: For a Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property securing it must conform to all applicable zoning, subdivision and use laws, ordinances or codes and local building and housing codes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Property securing a Mortgage delivered for purchase
business_rules.consequences: If conforming, property is eligible; if not conforming, loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this subsection

source_reference.source_text: 8.5 Zoning and building code conformity ( 04/15/21) a. For a Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property securing it must conform to all applicable zoning, subdivision and use laws, ordinances or codes and local building and housing codes.

examples: Property has a current certificate of occupancy and no zoning/building violations on record; meets conformity requirement.
Municipal records show an open building code violation for illegal unit conversions; property does not conform and loan may be ineligible until cured.
Property use is not permitted under current zoning (no legal nonconforming status documented); fails eligibility.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake SEL-475 Thresholds 20% and 40% Determine Insurance or Retrofit
business_rules.description: For a Property in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, Freddie Mac requires a Seismic Risk Assessment (SRA) at the Borrower’s expense. SEL-475 estimates must be provided for each building construction type when multiple types exist. Purchase eligibility and earthquake mitigation depend on SEL-475 and building stability concern: (i) SEL-475 ≤ 20% with stability concern = ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved; (ii) SEL-475 > 20% and ≤ 40% = insurance required and retrofit optional, but stability concern still makes the building ineligible until retrofit unless otherwise approved; (iii) SEL-475 > 40% = seismic retrofit required prior to submission to Freddie Mac for consideration. Where earthquake insurance is required, coverage must be at least 100% of the building’s Insurable Value (IV).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Properties in Elevated Seismic Hazard Region under Chapter 31.9
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, require insurance/retrofit; if stability concern, property is ineligible until retrofit unless approved
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligibility may be overridden if otherwise approved by Freddie Mac

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac requires an SRA at the Borrower’s expense for a Property located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region... a SEL-475 estimate is required for each building construction type. If any single building has a SEL-475 greater than 20 percent, then earthquake insurance or seismic retrofit is required for that building... SEL-475... ≤ 20%... Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac... > 20% & ≤ 40% Insurance required... Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved... > 40% The affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration... coverage must be at least 100 percent of the IV of the building

examples: Property has SEL-475 of 18% but building stability concern is identified; property is ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed unless Freddie Mac approves otherwise.
Property has two building types; tuck-under parking buildings have SEL-475 25% and garden-style buildings 15%; earthquake insurance or retrofit is required for the 25% building type.
Property has SEL-475 45%; Seller must ensure seismic retrofit is completed before submitting the Mortgage to Freddie Mac for consideration.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: SBL property occupancy must be at least 90% for trailing 3 months and last month
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property must have a minimum stabilized average physical occupancy of 90% for (i) the trailing three-month period prior to underwriting and (ii) the month immediately preceding submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Mortgages evaluated for purchase eligibility by Freddie Mac.
business_rules.consequences: If met, occupancy requirement satisfied; if violated, SBL Mortgage is ineligible for purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: For an SBL Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property securing it must have a minimum stabilized average physical occupancy of 90 percent for the trailing three -month period prior to underwriting and for the month immediately preceding submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac.

examples: Trailing 3 months occupancy averages 92% and last month is 90%: requirement satisfied.
Trailing 3 months average is 89% even though last month is 95%: requirement not satisfied.
Trailing 3 months average is 90% but last month is 88%: requirement not satisfied.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Reserve required when priority repairs total at least $25,000
business_rules.description: When conditions at the Property become Priority Repairs, Freddie Mac requires a Reserve for all identified Priority Repairs (including PR-90 Repairs) if the estimated total cost to complete all such Priority Repairs identified in the SBL Physical Risk Report is $25,000 or greater.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after SBL Physical Risk Report identifies Priority Repairs at property
business_rules.consequences: Met: reserve required/established; Violated: commitment/purchase conditions unmet
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.section_id: 8SBL.17 SBL Physical Risk Report, reserve trigger
source_reference.source_text: 4. If any of the following conditions exist at the Property and become Priority Repairs, Freddie Mac will require a Reserve for all identified Priority Repairs (including PR -90 Repairs): • The estimated cost to complete all Priority Repairs (including PR -90 Repairs) identified in the SBL Physical Risk Report is $25,000 or greater, or

examples: Physical risk report identifies roof and electrical repairs totaling $30,000; a reserve for all identified Priority Repairs is required.
Report identifies Priority Repairs totaling $24,999; this $25,000 trigger is not met (other triggers may still apply if stated elsewhere).
Report identifies PR-90 Repairs totaling $25,000 exactly; reserve requirement applies because cost is “$25,000 or greater.”
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Seniors housing ineligible if skilled nursing NOI exceeds 20%
business_rules.description: A Mortgage secured by a Seniors Housing Property is not eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac if (1) more than 20% of the Net Operating Income is derived from skilled nursing care, acute medical care, or rehabilitative care services, or (2) the Property has a skilled nursing component that does not allocate a minimum of 15% of the total skilled nursing units for assisted living or independent living residents who were residents prior to entering the skilled nursing facility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Seniors Housing Properties during mortgage eligibility review
business_rules.consequences: Meets thresholds: may be eligible; violates: mortgage not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Ineligible Properties (05/11/10)  If a Mortgage is secured by a Property with either of the following conditions, the Mortgage is not eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac:  • A Property in which more than 20 percent of the Net Operating Income is derived from the provision of skilled nursing care, acute medical care or rehabilitative care services, or  • A Property that has a skilled nursing component that does not allocate a minimum of 15 percent of the total number of skilled nursing units for the assisted living or independent living residents of the Property

examples: Property derives 25% of NOI from skilled nursing services: ineligible for purchase.
Property derives 18% of NOI from skilled nursing, but allocates only 10% of skilled nursing units for existing AL/IL residents: ineligible.
Property derives 10% of NOI from skilled nursing and allocates 20% of skilled nursing units for existing residents: passes this eligibility screen.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: SBL mixed-use limits: commercial income ≤ 40% and space ≤ 40%
business_rules.description: For SBL Mortgages on mixed-use properties, purchase eligibility requires that underwritten commercial income is no more than 40% of Gross Potential Rent (GPR) and square footage devoted to commercial space is no more than 40% of the Net Leasable Space; other listed mixed-use conditions (zoning compatibility, market support) must also be satisfied.
business_rules.conditions: When SBL collateral includes any commercial (nonresidential) space or income
business_rules.consequences: Meets: SBL mixed-use eligibility satisfied; Violated: Mortgage ineligible for SBL purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Mortgages on such Properties are eligible for purchase under Freddie Mac's SBL Purchase Product, provided that each of the following conditions is satisfied: ... Underwritten commercial income is no more than 40 percent of Gross Potential Rent (GPR) and is otherwise acceptable to Freddie Mac ... Square footage devoted to commercial space is no more than 40 percent of the Net Leasable Space and is otherwise acceptable to Freddie Mac

examples: GPR $1,000,000; underwritten commercial income $350,000 (35%) and commercial NLS 25%; eligible on these thresholds.
GPR $800,000; underwritten commercial income $360,000 (45%); fails commercial income limit and is ineligible.
Commercial NLS 42% even with commercial income 30%; fails space limit and is ineligible.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Infrastructure Agreement Must Not Impose Obligations of $50,000 or More
business_rules.description: A lease created pursuant to an Infrastructure Agreement is acceptable for an SBL Property only if it contains market-customary terms and does not impose financial obligations equal to or greater than $50,000 on the property owner, does not impose any financial obligations on any mortgagee, and does not impose obligations on a subsequent owner following foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, or other conveyance (including reimbursement of an up-front fee upon termination).
business_rules.conditions: When an SBL Property is subject to (or will be subject to) an Infrastructure Agreement lease
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, lease may be acceptable; if violated, lease terms must be modified or loan may be declined
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: A lease created pursuant to an Infrastructure Agreement (as defined in Section 8.22) is acceptable for an SBL Property , provided, in addition to the requirements specified in Section 8.22, that it (a) contains terms and provisions, including compensation, that are customary for the market in which the Property is located, and (b) does not impose any financial obligations equal to or greater than $50,000 on the Property owner, and does not impose any financial obligations on any mortgagee, and/or subsequent owner by foreclosure, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure or other conveyance (including reimbursement of any up-front fee pai d by the provider to the Property owner upon termination of the agreement).

examples: A cell tower lease requires the property owner to remove equipment and restore the roof at a capped cost of $40,000; it can be acceptable if no mortgagee obligations exist.
A broadband easement agreement obligates the owner to reimburse a $75,000 up-front fee if terminated; it is not acceptable because obligations are ≥ $50,000.
A solar provider agreement requires the lender/foreclosure purchaser to assume maintenance fees; it is not acceptable because it imposes obligations on a mortgagee/subsequent owner.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear acceptance condition with explicit $50,000 threshold and prohibition on mortgagee/successor obligations.

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Flood deductible caps: $50k, $500k, 15 days, $100k
business_rules.description: Flood insurance policies must not exceed the maximum deductible limits stated in the Guide. For first-layer building coverage, the maximum deductible is $50,000 per building when the Property has 10 buildings or less in SFHAs, and $500,000 per occurrence when the Property has more than 10 buildings in SFHAs. For Business Income/Rental Value flood coverage, the maximum deductible is a 15-day waiting period (time-based) or $100,000 per occurrence (monetary). When NFIP policies are used as part of the coverage, the NFIP maximum deductible for the building type is acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: When placing or renewing flood insurance coverage for buildings located in SFHAs
business_rules.consequences: If met: policy is deductible-compliant; if violated: policy must be corrected/replaced to meet limits
business_rules.exceptions: NFIP maximum deductible for insured building type is acceptable when NFIP used

source_reference.source_text: The following are maximum deductibles allowed for flood insurance policies: For first -layer building coverage: • $50,000 per building for a Property with 10 buildings or less located in SFHAs • $500,000 per occurrence for a Property with more than 10 buildings located in SFHAs For Business Income/Rental Value coverage: • 15 day waiting period when expressed as a time -based deductible • $100,000 per occurrence when expressed as a monetary deductible When NFIP policies are used as part of the coverage, the maximum deductible available under the NFIP for the type of building being insured is acceptable.

examples: Property has 8 buildings in SFHA; building flood deductible is $75,000 per building → noncompliant (max $50,000) and must be reduced.
Property has 12 buildings in SFHA; policy uses $600,000 per occurrence deductible → noncompliant (max $500,000) and must be corrected.
Business income flood coverage uses a 30-day waiting period deductible → noncompliant (max 15 days) unless converted to an allowed deductible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Insurance coverage cure requirement and potential force-place

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Deductibles Capped at $50k, $100k, or 7.5% of TIV
business_rules.description: When earthquake insurance is required, the maximum allowable deductible depends on limit type and expression. For Specific Insurance Limits: if the Insurable Value (IV) is less than $10 million, maximum deductible is $50,000; if IV is at least $10 million, maximum deductible is $100,000. If expressed as a percentage, the deductible is 7.5% of the subject property’s Total Insurable Value (TIV), with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, deductible is $250,000; if expressed as a percentage, deductible is 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance under Chapter 31.9
business_rules.consequences: If deductible within caps, policy is compliant; if above caps, insurance is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For Specific Insurance Limits: When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limits: When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000

examples: Specific limit policy on a building with IV of $9,500,000 uses a $60,000 deductible: noncompliant because max is $50,000.
Specific limit policy with IV of $12,000,000 uses a $100,000 deductible: compliant (does not exceed $100,000 cap).
Blanket policy uses 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum of $300,000: noncompliant because the stated minimum may not exceed $250,000.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Insurer must be AM Best A- and size VII or better
business_rules.description: Each insurance carrier providing property damage and/or liability insurance must maintain a minimum AM Best Company rating of A- financial strength and a financial size category of VII or better. Policies issued through State-Sponsored Insurance programs or insurers participating in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) are exempt from these rating requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When a carrier provides property damage and/or liability insurance
business_rules.consequences: If met, carrier is acceptable; if not met, policy/carrier is unacceptable
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to State-Sponsored programs or NFIP insurers

source_reference.source_text: 31.4 Acceptable insurers (0 7/15/25) Each insurance carrier providing property damage and/or liability insurance, whether admitted or non-admitted, must maintain a minimum AM Best Company rating of A - financial strength and a financial size category of VII or better. These rating requirements do not apply to policies issued through State -Sponsored Insurance programs or policies issued by insurers participating in National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

examples: Carrier rated AM Best A (FSR) and size VIII: acceptable for All-Risk coverage.
Carrier rated AM Best B++ (FSR) size VII: unacceptable; policy must be replaced or re-marketed.
NFIP flood policy issued by an NFIP-participating insurer: AM Best rating test not required.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: All-Risk coverage must be 100% IV single or 90% IV multi
business_rules.description: Property damage (All-Risk) insurance is required for all Mortgages and must be written on a replacement cost value basis rather than actual cash value basis (roof coverings may be actual cash value). Coverage amount must be not less than 100% of Insurable Value (IV) for properties with a single residential building and not less than 90% of IV for properties with multiple residential buildings. The policy must either not contain a Coinsurance Clause or must offset it with an Agreed Amount Provision where the agreed amount is no less than the estimated IV.
business_rules.conditions: When placing/renewing All-Risk property damage insurance on a Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met, coverage amount satisfies Freddie Mac minimums; if violated, coverage is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Roof coverings may be written on an actual cash value basis

source_reference.source_text: 31.5 Property damage (All -Risk) insurance ( 12/16/25) Property damage insurance is required for all Mortgages ... Policies must be written on a replacement cost value basis rather than actual cash value basis; however, c overage for roof coverings may be written on an actual cash value basis. Property damage (All -Risk) insurance coverage must : • For properties with a single residential building, be written in an amount not less than 100 percent of the Insurable Value (IV) ... and • For properties with multiple residential buildings, be written in an amount not less than 90 percent of the IV, and • Either not contain a Coinsurance Clause or contain a Coinsurance Clause that is offset by an Agreed Amount Provision. If an Agreed Amount Provision is used, the agreed amount must be no less than the estimated IV.

examples: Single-building property with IV $12,000,000: coverage must be at least $12,000,000.
Campus with 4 residential buildings and IV $20,000,000: coverage must be at least $18,000,000 (90% of IV).
Policy has a coinsurance clause but includes agreed amount equal to estimated IV: acceptable offset.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: All-Risk deductible caps are $50k or $100k; blanket max $250k
business_rules.description: For property damage (All-Risk) insurance policies providing Specific Insurance Limits, the maximum deductible per occurrence is $50,000 when the Property IV is < $10 million and $100,000 when the Property IV is ≥ $10 million. When All-Risk coverage is provided by a Blanket Insurance Limit, the maximum per occurrence deductible is $250,000.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating All-Risk deductible for Specific Limits or Blanket Limits coverage
business_rules.consequences: If met, deductible is within cap; if exceeded, deductible is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Expanded deductible waiver may apply for existing Mortgages (see related rule)

source_reference.source_text: a. Property damage (All -Risk) deductible ( 12/16/25) The maximum deductible per occurrence for property damage (All -Risk) insurance policies providing Specific Insurance Limits is: IV of the Property Maximum Deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 The maximum per occurrence deductible when All -Risk coverage is provided by a Blanket Insurance Limit is $250,000.

examples: Property IV $9,500,000 on specific limits policy: deductible must be ≤ $50,000 per occurrence.
Property IV $10,000,000 on specific limits policy: deductible must be ≤ $100,000 per occurrence.
Blanket All-Risk policy: deductible $300,000 per occurrence is noncompliant because it exceeds $250,000.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Business income coverage must cover 12 months; deductible max 72 hours
business_rules.description: Business Income/Rental Value Insurance is required for all applicable property damage perils within the scope of the Special Causes of Loss/All Risk form. Coverage must be sufficient to cover either (i) Actual Loss Sustained for 12 months, (ii) 12 months of Effective Gross Income based on most recent annual EGI, or (iii) Annual NOI plus continuing expenses with a completed business income worksheet. Maximum deductible is the maximum deductible for the property insurance coverage or a waiting period of three days/72 hours. For a Mortgage Loan with UPB of $35 million or more, coverage must include a 90-day extended period.
business_rules.conditions: When Business Income/Rental Value coverage is required for All-Risk perils
business_rules.consequences: If met, BI/RV coverage meets Freddie Mac minimums; if violated, BI/RV coverage is insufficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 31.6 Business Income/Rental Value Insurance (0 7/15/25) Business Income/Rental Value Insurance is required for all applicable property damage perils within the scope of the “ Special Causes of Loss” or “All Risk” policy form ... The coverage must be sufficient to cover the following: • Income based on Actual Loss Sustained for 12 months ; or • 12 months of Effective Gross Income (EGI) ...; or • Annual NOI plus continuing expenses ... The maximum deductible for business income/rental value coverage is: • The maximum deductible for the property insurance coverage, or • A waiting period ... of three days or 72 hours For a Mortgage Loan with a UPB of $35 million or more, coverage must include a 90 -day extended period

examples: BI coverage written as Actual Loss Sustained with 12-month period and 72-hour waiting period: compliant.
BI coverage limited to 6 months of EGI: noncompliant because it does not meet the 12-month minimum options.
Loan UPB $40,000,000 with BI coverage but no 90-day extended period: noncompliant for ≥$35MM UPB requirement.
Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Insurance and Retrofit Rules for SEL-475 Above 20%
business_rules.description: Earthquake insurance purchase eligibility and retrofit requirements depend on SEL-475 and whether a building stability concern exists. If SEL-475 ≤ 20% and no building stability concern, earthquake insurance is not required. If a building stability concern exists, the property is ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac. If SEL-475 is >20% and ≤40% with no building stability concern, insurance is required (retrofit optional); if retrofit results in SEL-475 ≤20% at completion, insurance will no longer be required. If SEL-475 is >40%, affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration.
business_rules.conditions: When determining earthquake insurance/retrofit requirements for a property
business_rules.consequences: If requirements met, mortgage may proceed; if not, mortgage is ineligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligible-until-retrofit may be overridden if otherwise approved by Freddie Mac

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31 – Insurance Requirements, Required earthquake coverage
source_reference.source_text: Earthquake insurance is required per the table below: SEL-475 Building Stability Concern* No Yes ≤ 20% Insurance not required Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac > 20% & ≤ 40% Insurance required, and seismic retrofit optional; if the retrofit results in a SEL-475 ≤ 20% at completion, then insurance will no longer be required Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed unless otherwise approved by Freddie Mac > 40% The affected building(s) must have a seismic retrofit prior to the Mortgage being submitted to Freddie Mac for consideration

examples: SEL-475 = 18% and no building stability concern: earthquake insurance not required—eligible on this criterion.
SEL-475 = 35% and no stability concern: earthquake insurance required; retrofit optional (if retrofit later reduces SEL-475 to 20%, insurance can cease).
SEL-475 = 25% with building stability concern: mortgage ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit is completed (unless Freddie approves otherwise).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Table provides explicit thresholds and clear consequence (ineligible for purchase).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for purchase until seismic retrofit completed

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_007 governs hazard-related eligibility (earthquake insurance/retrofit). BR_018_009 governs counterparty/role-based eligibility via the Exclusionary List. They do not require each other’s data, but both must pass for purchase. Linking them as complementary improves automated ‘eligibility gate’ navigation (e.g., show all failing eligibility reasons).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not linked: (1) teams may resolve hazard insurance issues but miss an Exclusionary List hit that still blocks sale; (2) systems may incorrectly mark a loan as ‘cleared for delivery’ after hazard fixes; (3) increased cycle time due to sequential rather than parallel remediation planning.

dependencies.rationale: Zoning/CO compliance and hazard insurance eligibility are separate validations, but they converge on the same operational decision: whether the property/loan is eligible for Freddie Mac purchase. Modeling them as complementary supports comprehensive property eligibility checklists and allows parallel task assignment (legal/zoning vs insurance/hazard).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this relationship is not represented: (1) property eligibility may be treated as complete when only one dimension is cleared; (2) remediation plans may be incomplete (e.g., clear zoning but miss retrofit requirement); (3) workflow tools cannot present a consolidated ‘property eligibility’ status.

dependencies.rationale: Property risk and insurance compliance checks are downstream of basic counterparty permissibility. Sanctions is a global prohibition across purchase and servicing-related transactions; it should be evaluated early and gate further underwriting conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sanctions screening fails, requiring retrofit/insurance changes provides no path to purchase; the file should be terminated or escalated per sanctions policy.

dependencies.rationale: BR_HAZARD_INSURANCE_POLICY_ELIGIBILITY_032_007 requires inputs (SEL-475, building stability concern) that are commonly outputs of seismic risk assessment. Operationally, the compliance workflow should obtain/validate the required SRA documentation before attempting to apply the insurance/retrofit decision logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the SRA is missing/deficient, the lender may be unable to justify why earthquake insurance is or is not required, increasing the risk of purchase ineligibility or post-review findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Coverage Must Equal 100% Building IV
business_rules.description: For any property or building where Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance, the earthquake coverage amount must be at least 100% of the building’s insurable value (IV), plus Business Income/Rental Value coverage per the referenced Guide section.
business_rules.conditions: When Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance for a property/building
business_rules.consequences: If coverage meets 100% IV minimum, policy satisfies requirement; if below, policy is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31 – Insurance Requirements, Required earthquake coverage
source_reference.source_text: Any Property or buildings for which Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance, the coverage must be at least 100 percent of the IV of the building Plus Business Income/Rental Value coverage per Section 31.6.

examples: Building IV is $6,000,000; earthquake coverage is $6,000,000 and BI/RV endorsement included—compliant.
Building IV is $12,000,000; earthquake coverage is $10,000,000—noncompliant (must be at least $12,000,000).
Earthquake coverage meets 100% IV but BI/RV coverage is omitted—noncompliant per 'Plus Business Income/Rental Value' requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum percentage of IV; BI/RV is an additional required component.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold or servicing insurance compliance demand

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: BR_HAZARD_INSURANCE_POLICY_ELIGIBILITY_032_007 establishes whether Freddie Mac requires earthquake insurance (based on SEL-475 thresholds and building stability concern). Only after that requirement is triggered can the policy coverage-minimum rule (100% of insurable value plus BI/RV coverage) be evaluated.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If eligibility logic is not applied correctly, the workflow may (a) fail to enforce the 100% IV minimum when earthquake insurance is required (purchase ineligibility/QC findings), or (b) impose unnecessary coverage requirements when insurance is not required (borrower cost, closing delays).

business_rules.rule_name: Earthquake Deductible Caps $50k/$100k and 7.5% TIV
business_rules.description: Earthquake deductible maximums apply based on policy limit structure. For Specific Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, maximum deductible is $50,000 when IV < $10 million and $100,000 when IV ≥ $10 million. If expressed as a percentage, maximum is 7.5% of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: if expressed as a dollar amount, maximum is $250,000; if expressed as a percentage, maximum is 7.5% of TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000. For earthquake business income/rental value coverage, the maximum deductible is 15 days (if in days) or $100,000 (if in dollars).
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating earthquake and BI/RV deductibles on hazard insurance policies
business_rules.consequences: If deductibles within caps, policy is compliant; if above, policy must be amended/replaced
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limits: • When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for earthquake business income/rental value coverage is as follows: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: Specific limits, IV $9.5M, deductible $50,000—compliant; deductible $75,000—noncompliant.
Specific limits, deductible 7.5% of TIV with minimum $125,000—noncompliant (minimum must not exceed $100,000).
Blanket limits with BI/RV deductible of 20 days—noncompliant (max 15 days).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit caps by structure; highly testable thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold or servicing insurance compliance demand

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_009 governs whether a presented earthquake policy meets deductible limits. That evaluation is only actionable when BR_032_007 indicates earthquake coverage is required for purchase eligibility (or when the lender chooses to include it in response to BR_032_007 triggers).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_007 is not applied first, deductible compliance may be checked unnecessarily or missed entirely, causing either avoidable policy rework or purchase ineligibility due to noncompliant deductibles.

dependencies.rationale: Coverage adequacy and deductible permissibility are separate but jointly necessary conditions of an acceptable earthquake policy. In an automated compliance checklist, both checks should be executed as a combined policy-compliance set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_008 is satisfied but BR_032_009 fails (or vice versa), the earthquake insurance requirement is not met, leading to underwriting/closing conditions and potential purchase ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail Deductible Max $50k or $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail coverage under Specific Insurance Limits, the maximum deductible is capped based on Insurable Value (IV): if IV of the Property is < $10 million, the maximum deductible is $50,000; if IV is ≥ $10 million, the maximum deductible is $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail deductible is a dollar amount under Specific Limits
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; exceeds cap: policy noncompliant and must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Wind/ Hail deductibles ( 12/16/25) For Specific Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, five percent of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000

examples: Property IV $9,500,000 and Wind/Hail deductible $50,000: compliant (≤ $50,000).
Property IV $10,000,000 and deductible $100,000: compliant (≤ $100,000).
Property IV $12,000,000 and deductible $150,000: noncompliant; revise policy terms to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail Deductible Cap 5% TIV Max $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail coverage under Specific Insurance Limits where the deductible is expressed as a percentage, the deductible must be five percent (5%) of the subject property's Total Insurable Value (TIV), with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail deductible is expressed as a percentage under Specific Limits
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; stated minimum > $100,000: policy noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, five percent of the subject property’s TIV with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000

examples: TIV $3,000,000; 5% deductible = $150,000 but stated minimum must not exceed $100,000: noncompliant if the policy uses a $150,000 minimum.
TIV $8,000,000; deductible 5% with stated minimum $75,000: compliant (minimum ≤ $100,000).
TIV $20,000,000; deductible 5% with stated minimum $125,000: noncompliant; reduce minimum to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Wind and Hail BI Deductible Max 15 Days or $100k
business_rules.description: For Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage, the maximum deductible is capped at 15 days when expressed as days, or $100,000 when expressed as a dollar amount.
business_rules.conditions: When Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage is included
business_rules.consequences: Meets cap: acceptable; exceeds cap: policy noncompliant and must be revised
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: five percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for Wind/Hail business income/rental value coverage is: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: Policy lists BI waiting period deductible of 10 days: compliant (≤ 15 days).
Policy lists BI deductible of 20 days: noncompliant; must be reduced to 15 days or less.
Policy lists BI deductible $150,000: noncompliant; must be reduced to ≤ $100,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Named Storm Blanket Limit Minimum 90% of Largest TIV
business_rules.description: If Named Storm coverage is provided as part of a Blanket Insurance Limit, the blanket limit may not be less than 90% of the largest individual TIV covered by the policy. The blanket limit must be per-occurrence and must reinstate to the required limit after any losses.
business_rules.conditions: When Named Storm coverage is provided as a Blanket Insurance Limit
business_rules.consequences: Meets limit/reinstatement: acceptable; otherwise: inadequate Named Storm limits
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31, Named Storm blanket limit (Page 14, 12/16/25 update)
source_reference.source_text: The Blanket Insurance Limit for Named Storm may not be less than 90 percent of the largest individual TIV covered by the policy. The blanket limit must be per-occurrence and reinstate to the required limit after any losses.

examples: Largest TIV among covered properties is $20MM; blanket Named Storm limit must be ≥ $18MM (90%): $18MM is compliant.
Largest TIV is $20MM; blanket limit $15MM: noncompliant (75% < 90%).
Blanket is ≥90% but is aggregate (not per-occurrence): noncompliant; must be per-occurrence.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

business_rules.rule_name: Named Storm Deductible 7.5% TIV with $50k $100k Caps
business_rules.description: Named Storm deductible limits apply. For Specific Insurance Limits: dollar deductibles capped at $50,000 when IV < $10 million and $100,000 when IV ≥ $10 million; percent deductibles set at 7.5% of TIV with stated minimum not to exceed $100,000. For Blanket Insurance Limits: dollar deductible $250,000; percent deductible 7.5% of TIV with stated minimum not to exceed $250,000. Named Storm business income/rental value deductible is capped at 15 days or $100,000.
business_rules.conditions: When Named Storm coverage is required and deductibles apply
business_rules.consequences: Meets caps: acceptable; exceeds caps: noncompliant deductible terms
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: d. Named Storm deductibles ( 12/16/25) For Specific Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount: IV of the Property Maximum deductible < $10 million $50,000 ≥ $10 million $100,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $100,000 For Blanket Insurance Limit s: • When expressed as a dollar amount, $250,000 • When expressed as a percentage, 7.5 percent of the subject property’s TIV, with a stated minimum not to exceed $250,000 The maximum deductible for Named Storm business income/rental value coverage is: • When expressed as number of days, 15 days • When expressed as a dollar amount, $100,000

examples: IV $9MM with Named Storm deductible $60,000: noncompliant (must be ≤ $50,000 for IV < $10MM).
Blanket Named Storm deductible $250,000: compliant (dollar deductible equals the allowed max).
Named Storm BI deductible 30 days: noncompliant (must be ≤ 15 days).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: These rules jointly define Named Storm acceptability: BR_037_004 ensures the blanket limit is not under-sized and reinstates after losses, while BR_037_005 ensures deductibles are within Freddie Mac caps. Together they form a cohesive Named Storm compliance set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, Named Storm insurance terms are noncompliant, risking insurance conditions, delayed delivery/purchase, or forced mid-process policy replacement.

business_rules.rule_name: State Windpool Requires 100% TIV and ≤80% Coinsurance
business_rules.description: If windstorm coverage is only available from a state-sponsored insurance program or State Windpool, the policy must be written in an amount no less than 100% of the TIV and meet deductible requirements. If the policy contains a coinsurance clause, it must either include an agreed amount provision equal to no less than 100% of the IV of the improvements, or contain a coinsurance clause less than or equal to 80%.
business_rules.conditions: When windstorm coverage is only available via State Windpool program
business_rules.consequences: Meets limits/coinsurance rules: acceptable; otherwise: policy must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 31, Section 31.7(e) Windstorm insurance through a State Windpool (12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: If windstorm coverage is only available from a state -sponsored insurance program or State Windpool, the policy must: • Be written in an amount no less than 100 percent of the TIV and meet the deductible requirements in this Section 31. 7. • If the policy contains a Coinsurance Clause, it must: o Include an Agreed Amount Provision equal to no less than 100 percent of the IV of the improvements, or o Contain a Coinsurance Clause less than or equal to 80 percent

examples: State Windpool policy limit equals 100% TIV and coinsurance 80%: compliant.
State Windpool policy limit equals 90% TIV: noncompliant; increase limit to ≥100% TIV.
Coinsurance is 90% and no agreed amount provision: noncompliant; add agreed amount ≥100% IV improvements or reduce coinsurance to ≤80%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Property hazard policy with Freddie Mac named as mortgagee/loss payee
Insurance renewal evidence provided prior to policy expiration
Policy change increasing deductible beyond permitted limits

dependencies.rationale: When coverage is only available through a State Windpool, BR_037_006 imposes minimum limit and deductible compliance. BR_037_001 is one of the deductible cap rules needed to determine if the submitted State Windpool policy is acceptable (for dollar-amount deductibles under Specific Limits).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_001 is not applied, a State Windpool policy could be accepted with an excessive deductible, creating a noncompliant insurance file and potential Freddie Mac remedies/conditions.

dependencies.rationale: To confirm a State Windpool policy meets Freddie Mac deductible requirements, the system must evaluate percentage-based deductibles using BR_037_002 when that deductible structure is used.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_002 is skipped, a policy could be accepted with a noncompliant stated minimum (e.g., minimum > $100,000), triggering post-review exceptions and policy rework.

dependencies.rationale: BR_037_006 broadly requires meeting deductible requirements. If the State Windpool policy includes business income/rental value coverage, BR_037_003 supplies the deductible cap for that component and should be evaluated to complete compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not evaluated, the file may pass initial review but fail later audit/QC if BI/RV deductible exceeds 15 days or $100,000, requiring endorsement changes.

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Financial Rent Roll Age Maximum 6 Months
business_rules.description: For Annual Financial submissions, the rent roll must be current: dated no more than six months prior to the Annual Financial submission date and, for purposes of Annual Financial completeness, dated no more than six months prior to the original due date (e.g., due June 1 requires rent roll dated on/after Dec 1 of the prior year).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer submits Annual Financial for a Freddie Mac-held Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met, rent roll satisfies currency; if violated, Annual Financial may be deemed incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: The rent roll must be no older than six months prior to the Annual Financial submission date. ... The Servicer must submit all of the following components of the Annual Financial via PRS in order for Freddie Mac to deem the submission of the Annual Financial to be complete: ... 3. Current rent roll, identifying commercial units where applicable, certified by the Borrower ... For the purposes of the Annual Financial, a rent roll is considered to be current if it is dated no more than six months prior to the original due date. For example, if the Annual Financial due date is June 1, the rent roll is considered current if dated on or after December 1 of the prior year.

examples: Meets rule: Annual Financial due June 1; rent roll dated Dec 15 prior year is acceptable (within 6 months).
Fails rule: Annual Financial due June 1; rent roll dated Nov 30 prior year is stale (older than 6 months).
Meets rule: Submission date Aug 15; rent roll dated Mar 1 is within 6 months of submission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac deems Annual Financial submission incomplete

examples: Monthly servicing report indicating 30 days delinquent status
Servicing report reflecting partial payment and suspense handling
Corrected servicing report resubmitted after reconciliation

business_rules.rule_name: Sales comparison approach requires at least 4 comparable sales plus 4 if subject used
business_rules.description: When supporting value using the sales comparison approach, the appraiser must analyze sales of at least four comparable properties. The appraiser may use the subject property as a comparable sale only if the appraiser provides four additional comparables to the property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Appraisal includes a sales comparison approach value indication
business_rules.consequences: If four+ comparable sales analyzed, approach meets minimum Guide requirement; if fewer, Appraisal is deficient and may be rejected/conditioned
business_rules.exceptions: Subject property may be used only with four additional comparables

source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must support the value indicated by the sales comparison approach by analyzing the sales of at least four comparable properties. The appraiser may use the Property as a comparable sale as long as the appraiser provides four additional comparables to the Property.

examples: Appraiser includes only three comparable sales: Appraisal does not meet the minimum and must be supplemented with at least one more comparable sale.
Appraiser analyzes four comparable sales and does not use the subject as a comparable: requirement is met.
Appraiser uses the subject as a comparable sale: Appraisal must include four additional comparable sales (total at least five sales analyzed with subject included).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum count (four) and conditional rule when subject is used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / appraisal revision required
