examples: MAI appraisal supporting a $35M value conclusion
Update appraisal required due to aging report date at closing
Appraisal with extraordinary assumptions disclosed

dependencies.rationale: Underwriting typically treats appraisal completeness as a bundle of required components. BR_035_005 ensures zoning compliance support is present when a zoning report is absent; BR_038_006 ensures sales comparison methodology meets minimum comparable count. Together they form a practical appraisal QC checklist for acceptance/conditioning.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either requirement fails, the appraisal may be conditioned/rejected, delaying underwriting approval and potentially closing timelines.

business_rules.rule_name: Conversion Restriction Term Must Be No Longer Than 10 Years
business_rules.description: If there is a prohibition against converting the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure (or an indemnification relating to such conversion), the agreement/restriction must meet specified conditions including that the term of the agreement/restriction and any obligations contained in it must be no longer than 10 years.
business_rules.conditions: Recorded/unrecorded agreement restricts condo/co-op conversion or includes conversion indemnity
business_rules.consequences: If term ≤10 years, restriction term condition satisfied; if >10 years, Seller/Servicer must cure/modify or seek Freddie acceptance
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If there is a prohibition against the conversion of the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure or any indemnification by an owner of the Property relating to the conversion of the Property to a condominium or cooperative structure, then the agreement/restriction must meet the following conditions:  a. Other than the restriction prohibiting the conversion of the Property into a condominium or cooperative development, there may be no other restrictions on the use or development of the Property in the agreement/restriction.  b. The term of the agreement/restriction and any obligations contained in it must be no longer than 10 years.

examples: A recorded conversion restriction runs for 8 years and contains no other use restrictions: satisfies the 10-year term condition.
A restriction prohibits conversion for 15 years: noncompliant; must be amended to ≤10 years or otherwise addressed for Freddie acceptance.
A restriction is 5 years but also restricts redevelopment: fails condition (a) even though term is ≤10 years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase eligibility condition / legal documentation cure required

examples: Deed of trust recorded in county land records
Mortgage security instrument with first-lien position
Security instrument amended via recorded modification

business_rules.rule_name: Restoration plan approval required at 10% units or $500k/10% UPB
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac approval of the Borrower’s restoration plans is required if one or more listed conditions exists, including: (1) damaged units are ≥ 10% of total units; (2) estimated loss > $500,000 or > 10% of outstanding UPB; (3) there has been an event of default; (4) death or serious injury; (5) for non-SBL, estimated loss exceeds Borrower Proof of Loss Maximum; (6) if not securitized and Risk Rating > 6 and estimated loss > $50,000; (7) insurance coverage insufficient and estimated loss > $50,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when assessing whether Freddie Mac approval is required for restoration plans
business_rules.consequences: If any trigger met, Freddie Mac approval required and Part II process applies; else Part II not required
business_rules.exceptions: If none of listed conditions exist, Servicer not required to complete Part II

source_reference.section_id: Section 43.8(a)(3) Form 1140 Part II – Restoration Plan for Approval (02/24/26 update)
source_reference.source_text: If one or more of the following conditions exists, Freddie Mac approval of the Borrower’s restoration plans is required: • There has been an event of default under the Loan Documents • Death or serious injury has resulted from the event • The percentage of units that sustained any damage is 10 percent or more of the Property’s total units • The estimated loss amount is greater than $500,000 or 10 percent of the outstanding UPB • For non -SBL Mortgages, the estimated loss exceeds the Borrower Proof of Loss Maximum (as defined in the Loan Agreement) • The Mortgage has not been securitized, has a Risk Rating greater than six and the estimated loss is greater than $50,000

examples: Property has 200 units; 25 units damaged (12.5%) → Freddie Mac approval required (≥10%).
Estimated loss $600,000 on any loan → approval required (> $500,000) even if few units damaged.
Not securitized, Risk Rating 7, estimated loss $60,000 → approval required (RR>6 and loss>$50k).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac approval requirement; disbursement restrictions may apply

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_007 mandates a prompt notification and DMS upload when the servicer learns of loss/damage above thresholds. BR_002_008 then evaluates whether Freddie Mac approval is required based on loss magnitude, unit impact, defaults, and related triggers. Sequencing supports a consistent workflow: notify, then assess approval needs using the same event data.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_007 is delayed or omitted, downstream escalation/approval tracking under BR_002_008 may be delayed, causing restoration to proceed without required approvals or causing Freddie Mac to receive incomplete/late reporting.

business_rules.rule_name: Partial release fee split 50/50 and no extra fees
business_rules.description: For partial releases requiring Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must charge the Borrower a nonrefundable review/processing fee in the amount specified in the Loan Documents (or if not specified, Exhibit 10). The Servicer must remit 50% of the review/processing fee to Freddie Mac (if applicable) and may retain the remaining 50%. The Servicer may not charge the Borrower any additional fees for processing the application. At the time the request is delivered to Freddie Mac, the Servicer must remit Freddie Mac’s portion by wire transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitting a partial release request that requires Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, fee handling is accepted; if not, Freddie Mac may require reimbursement/corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 43 partial release fees and remittance (02/24/26)
source_reference.source_text: For all partial releases that require Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must charge the Borrower a nonrefundable review/processing fee for an application in the amount specified in the Loan Documents, or, if no amount is specified, in the amount set forth in Exhibit 10. The Servicer must remit to Freddie Mac 50 percent of the review/processing fee, if applicable, and may retain the remaining 50 percent. The Servicer may not charge the Borrower any additional fees for processing an application for approval of a partial release. At the time of delivery of the partial release request to Freddie Mac, the Servicer must remit to Freddie Mac by wire transfer the Freddie Mac portion of the review/processing fee.

examples: Loan docs specify $2,000 fee → Servicer collects $2,000, remits $1,000 to Freddie Mac by wire at delivery, retains $1,000; no extra admin fee allowed.
Loan docs silent; Exhibit 10 fee applies → same 50/50 remittance requirement and wire at delivery.
Servicer charges an extra $500 “legal review” fee → prohibited additional fee; must be reversed/refunded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee reimbursement to Freddie Mac; servicing corrective action

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 ensures the package includes a current title update report (≤45 days). BR_002_010 ensures correct fee charging/remittance and prohibits extra fees. They operate in parallel but together determine whether a partial release submission is complete and compliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either fails, Freddie Mac may reject/delay the request: outdated title (risk of undisclosed liens) or incorrect fees (billing/remittance issues and corrective action).

business_rules.rule_name: Late Charge Maximum 5% of Monthly P&I Installment
business_rules.description: For Mortgages (other than TAH bond credit enhancement Mortgages) originated prior to July 8, 1992, the Servicer may not impose a late charge in an amount more than 5% of the monthly principal and interest installment that is late (or any lesser amount specified in the Note).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to pre-1992-07-08 mortgages when calculating late charges
business_rules.consequences: If within cap, late charge may be imposed; if above cap, late fee must be reduced/voided
business_rules.exceptions: Lower cap applies if Note specifies a lesser amount

source_reference.source_text: • May not impose a late charge in an amount more than five percent of the monthly principal and interest installment that is late (or any lesser amount specified in the Note)

examples: Monthly P&I is $18,000; maximum late charge is 5% = $900 unless the Note specifies less.
Note limits late charge to 3%; even though Guide permits up to 5%, Servicer must apply 3%.
Servicer charges $1,200 on a $20,000 monthly P&I (6%); Servicer must reverse or reduce to $1,000 max.
Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_007 limits when a late charge may be imposed (not within 10 days). BR_011_008 limits how much may be imposed (≤5% or lesser note amount). Together they define whether a late charge is permissible and lawful under the Guide constraints for those loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either is missed, servicer may impose or attempt to collect impermissible late charges, requiring reversals/refunds and potentially triggering borrower complaints and audit findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated reserve modification only if risk rating 6 and UPB $30 million
business_rules.description: A Servicer may approve Replacement Reserve requirement modifications without submitting a consent request to Freddie Mac only when a Servicer Delegation Requirement is satisfied and all specified conditions are met, including: Risk Rating of six or less (and if securitized, not on watchlist), UPB less than $30 million, not in default, PCR/PRR completed within six months of the modification request date and required review date, overall property condition 'Good' or better, and no Critical or PR-90 (including Life Safety) repairs identified. The Servicer’s recommendation must align with PCR/PRR findings and escrow analysis.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer seeks to approve Replacement Reserve modification under delegated authority
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, Servicer may approve without Freddie Mac consent; otherwise must request consent
business_rules.exceptions: No delegated authority if securitization has non-Freddie directing party or Retained Authority

source_reference.source_text:
Subject to the foregoing paragraph, i f a Servicer Delegation Requirement has been
satisfied, the Servicer is delegated authority to approve modifications to the
Replacement Reserve requirements without submitting a consent request to
Freddie Mac, provided all of the following conditions are met:
• The Mortgage has a Risk Rating of six or less (and if securitized, is not on the
watchlist), has a UPB of less than $30 million, and is not in default.
• The Property Condition Report (PCR) (or SBL Physical Risk Report (PRR), for
SBL Mortgages)  must have been completed within six (6) months of the
modification request date and within six (6) months of the required review date.
• The PCR (or PRR, as applicable)  must identify the overall Property condition as
“Good” or better.
• No Critical or PR -90 (including Life Safety) repairs may be identified in the PCR
(or PRR, as applicable) .

examples: UPB $25,000,000, risk rating 5, not in default, PCR completed 90 days ago with condition 'Good' and no PR-90 repairs: Servicer may approve reserve modification under delegated authority.
UPB $29,000,000 but PCR completed 8 months ago: delegated authority conditions not met; Servicer must request Freddie Mac consent before approving.
Risk rating 6 but property condition is 'Fair': conditions not met; Servicer must submit formal consent request prior to modification.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac consent required; noncompliance finding if exceeded authority

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Repair Agreement Extension Cap 12 Months After Origination
business_rules.description: Delegated authority limits for extending Repair Agreement completion dates: (1) For non-SBL Mortgages, the Servicer may extend by up to the same amount of time initially granted in the Repair Agreement, but not beyond a date that is 12 months after the Mortgage Origination Date (e.g., original completion 90 days after origination allows ≤90-day extension). (2) For SBL Mortgages, the Servicer may extend up to a date that is 12 months after the Origination Date, provided delegated authority conditions are met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer uses delegated authority to extend Repair Agreement completion date
business_rules.consequences: If met, extension within authority; if violated, Freddie Mac approval is required/extension invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None (outside these limits, Freddie Mac approval is required)

source_reference.source_text: c. For a non -SBL Mortgage, provided that all of the requirements in Section 39.4(b)(2)(a) and (b) are met, the Servicer has delegated authority to extend the completion date of a Repair Agreement by up to the same amount of time initially granted in the Repair Agreement, but not more than a date that is 12 months after the Origination Date of such Mortgage. (For example, if the original completion date was 90 days following the Origination Date of the Mortgage, the Servicer may extend the completion date by no more than 90 days.) For an SBL Mortgage, provided that all of the requirements in Section 39.4(b)(2)(c) are met, the Servicer has delegated authority to extend the completion date of a Repair Agreement up to a date that is 12 months after the Origination Date

examples: Non-SBL: Origination Jan 1; original completion Apr 1 (90 days). Extension cannot exceed 90 days and cannot go past Jan 1 next year.
SBL: Origination Feb 15; Servicer extends completion to Feb 1 next year (within 12 months) if delegation requirements are satisfied.
Non-SBL: Origination Jan 1; Servicer attempts 120-day extension from a 90-day original period—outside delegated authority.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delegated action reversal; Freddie Mac approval required

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated SBL Non-Residential Lease Requires 90% Rent and 20% Cap
business_rules.description: If a Servicer Delegation Requirement has been satisfied, the Servicer is delegated authority to approve a non-residential lease for an SBL Mortgage only if all stated conditions are satisfied, including: tenant is not an Affiliate of the Borrower/Guarantor; rents are not less than 90% of prior rents for that portion of the Property (if any); lease term including extension options is 10 years or less; and aggregate income from the leased space is less than 20% of the Property’s gross income as of the lease entry date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Servicer approval of non-residential leases under SBL Mortgages
business_rules.consequences: If any condition fails, Servicer lacks delegated authority; Freddie Mac approval required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; all listed conditions must be satisfied

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 43 (02/24/26) Non-residential leases for SBL Mortgages
source_reference.source_text: If a Servicer Delegation Requirement has been satisfied, the Servicer is delegated authority to approve a non-residential lease, provided all of the following conditions are satisfied: •The tenant under the non-residential lease is not an Affiliate of the Borrower or an affiliate of any Guarantor •The rents paid to the Borrower pursuant to the non-residential lease are not less than 90 percent of the rents paid to the Borrower pursuant to the non-residential lease, if any, for that portion of the Property that was in effect prior to the non-residential lease •The term of the non-residential lease, including any option to extend, is 10 years or less •The aggregate of the income derived from the space leased pursuant to the Nonresidential Lease accounts for less than 20 percent of the gross income of the Property on the date that the Borrower enters into the Nonresidential Lease

examples: New retail lease would produce 18% of gross property income and has 8-year term with 2-year option (10 total): meets the 20% and 10-year thresholds (subject to all other conditions).
Proposed lease is 12 years including options: fails the 10-year maximum; Servicer cannot approve under delegation.
Non-residential space income would be 22% of gross income: fails the <20% cap; requires Freddie Mac approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicer noncompliance finding; potential reversal/required Freddie Mac approval

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_017_004 governs when the Servicer may approve a non-residential lease under delegated authority. BR_007_003 states Freddie Mac will not 'approve other servicing-related transactions' if it determines a Borrower/Principal/Guarantor is a confirmed match to sanctions/exclusion lists. Therefore, a sanctions screen outcome must be cleared before relying on delegated approval pathways for servicing actions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails (confirmed match) but BR_017_004 is applied anyway, the Servicer may proceed with an action that Freddie Mac will not permit, causing immediate escalation, potential rejection, and heightened regulatory/reputational risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated Easement Approval Requires 5 Conditions
business_rules.description: If a Servicer Delegation Requirement has been satisfied, the Servicer is delegated authority to approve the types of easements described in Section 43.10(a)(3) only if all listed conditions are satisfied: (i) if the Mortgage is in a securitization where Freddie Mac is the named master servicer, Freddie Mac must also be the Directing Certificateholder (or Directing Party); (ii) the Mortgage does not secure tax-exempt debt; (iii) the Servicer determines the easement will not materially affect the operation/value of the property or Freddie Mac’s interest; (iv) the Borrower is not requesting lien subordination to the easement (subordination requests must follow Section 43.10(e)); and (v) there are no credit enhancement investors other than a Seller/Servicer, the Borrower, or a Borrower affiliate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer seeks to approve easement under delegated authority in 43.10(a)
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, Servicer may approve; if any fail, Freddie Mac approval path applies
business_rules.exceptions: Section 43.10 does not apply to Infrastructure Agreement Servicing Requests (43.32)

source_reference.section_id: 43.10(a)(1) Easements the Servicer is permitted to approve (02/24/26)
source_reference.source_text: 1. If a Servicer Delegation Requirement  has been satisfied , the Servicer is delegated authority to approve the types of easements described in Section 43.10(a)(3) , provided  all the following conditions are satisfied: • If the Mortgage is in a Securitization where Freddie Mac is the named  master servicer , Freddie Mac must  also be the Directing Certificateholder (or Directing Party). • The Mortgage does not secure tax -exempt debt. • The Servicer determines, in its discretion, that the easement will not materially affect the operation or value of the Property or Freddie Mac’s interest in the Property. • If the Borrower is not requesting subordinat ion of  the lien of its Mortgage to the easement.  For any such Borrower request, see Section 43.10(e). • There are no investors who have provided credit enhancements for the Mortgage other than a Seller/Servicer, the Borrower or an Affiliate of the Borrower.

examples: Mortgage is in a Freddie Mac securitization and Freddie Mac is also Directing Certificateholder; no tax-exempt debt; no subordination request; Servicer documents no material impact and approves under delegation.
Borrower requests Mortgage lien subordination to an easement; Servicer cannot approve under 43.10(a) and must follow 43.10(e) procedures.
Mortgage has a third-party credit enhancement investor (not Seller/Servicer/Borrower/Affiliate); Servicer cannot use delegated authority.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Eligibility is explicitly conditioned on enumerated requirements in 43.10(a)(1).

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_001 is about delegated authority to approve easements. Even where delegation exists, BR_007_003 provides an overriding prohibition on approving servicing-related transactions involving sanctioned/excluded parties. Thus, sanctions clearance is a prerequisite compliance gate before executing delegated easement authority workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails but BR_022_001 is used, the Servicer may approve an easement in a context Freddie Mac would not permit, creating a high-risk compliance and enforcement issue.

business_rules.rule_name: Third-Party Easement Limits: UPB 30M, Fee 0.5%/$100K
business_rules.description: For third-party easements (other than local residential utility distribution), a Servicer may approve only if additional constraints are satisfied, including: Mortgage UPB must be $30,000,000 or less as of the Borrower’s easement application date; Mortgage Risk Rating must be 6 or less; the Mortgage must be current with no evidence of default; easement compensation must be less than the lesser of 0.5% of UPB or $100,000; and a supervisor or higher-level manager on Servicer staff must approve the easement using the Easement Request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Servicer approval of non-utility third-party easements under 43.10(a)(3)(C)
business_rules.consequences: If any threshold fails, Servicer may not approve under delegation; escalate for Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated within 43.10(a)(3)(C) conditions

source_reference.source_text: C. Easements for the benefit of a third party (other than for local residential distribution of gas, water, electricity or other public utility) if all the following conditions are satisfied: i. The Servicer has determined that there is no material adverse impact on the Property’s value, operation, access, marketability, or on the income production of units or other income -producing improvements. ii. The Property secures a Mortgage having a UPB of $30 million or less as of the date of the Borrower's easement application with the Servicer. iii. The Property secures a Mortgage with a Risk Rating of six or less. iv. The Mortgage is current, with no evidence of default. v. Compensation for the easement is less than the lesser of 0.5 percent of the UPB or $100,000. vi. The proposed easement is compatible with the use of the Property and would be generally acceptable to prudent lenders. vii. A supervisor or higher -level manager on the Servicer’s staff has approved the easement using the Easement Request .

examples: UPB $28,000,000 and compensation $90,000 (<$100,000 and <0.5% of UPB=$140,000); risk rating 5; current; supervisor approves—Servicer may approve.
UPB $31,000,000 (> $30,000,000 threshold); even if risk rating is 4 and loan is current, Servicer cannot approve under this delegated category.
UPB $20,000,000 and compensation $150,000 (> lesser of 0.5%=$100,000 or $100,000); compensation fails, so Servicer cannot approve under delegation.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric constraints (UPB cap, risk rating cap, compensation cap) and approval control are directly stated.

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Subordinate Financing Requires Combined LTV ≤ 85% and DCR ≥ 1.20x
business_rules.description: When Freddie Mac considers consenting to Subordinate Financing (generally for non-securitized Mortgages, and specifically for non-SBL Mortgages as stated), the combined debt (first mortgage plus subordinate financing and any other applicable debt included under the Guide’s combined-debt definition) must not result in an LTV Ratio exceeding 85%, and the combined debt service must not result in a DCR below 1.20x. These thresholds are necessary conditions for consent (and may be accompanied by other Guide conditions). If either threshold is not met (LTV > 85% or DCR < 1.20x), Freddie Mac may reject/deny the request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Servicer requests for Freddie Mac approval of subordinate financing
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds not met, Freddie Mac may reject the subordinate financing request
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac may reject a request for Subordinate Financing at its discretion. Freddie Mac will consider consenting to Subordinate Financing only if the request meets the following conditions (However, the fact that the Subordinate Financing meets these requirements does not mean that Freddie Mac is obligated to consider or approve the Subordinate Financing.): 3. The combined debt may not result in a Loan -to-Value (LTV) Ratio that exceeds 85 percent and the combined debt service may not result in a Debt Coverage Ratio (DCR) that is below 1.20x.
3. The combined debt may not result in a Loan -to-Value (LTV) Ratio that exceeds 85 percent and the combined debt service may not result in a Debt Coverage Ratio (DCR) that is below 1.20x.

examples: Combined LTV = 86% → fails threshold; subordinate financing consent may be denied.
Combined DCR = 1.15x → fails threshold; subordinate financing consent may be denied.
Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same key quantitative consent thresholds for Freddie Mac to consider consenting to Subordinate Financing: combined debt LTV must not exceed 85% and combined debt service DCR must not fall below 1.20x. 025_006 notes 'among other conditions' while 028_005 clarifies non-SBL scope and securitized mortgage exclusion; these differences do not alter the shared core constraint and can be preserved in the merged description as clarifying language.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SERVICES_CONSTRAINT_028_005 selected as primary: more explicit context boundaries (non-SBL Mortgage; expressly excludes securitized Mortgages) with the same LTV/DCR thresholds.

dependencies.rationale: The combined LTV/DCR thresholds apply only when Freddie Mac is considering consent for non-securitized loans. The securitization prohibition is the applicability gate that routes the request to immediate denial/alternate product.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the prohibition gate is missed, Servicer may submit packages for loans Freddie Mac cannot consider under the stated rule, causing process churn and compliance exceptions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_005 is a threshold rule governing Freddie Mac consent to subordinate financing. BR_007_003 is an overarching prohibition preventing Freddie Mac purchase/approval of certain transactions when parties are confirmed sanctions/exclusion matches. Thus, BR_007_003 is an upstream gate to determine if consent consideration under BR_028_005 is even permissible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails but BR_028_005 proceeds, the compliance workflow may reach an apparent 'pass' on thresholds while the transaction remains unapprovable, creating control failures and operational waste.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SERVICES_CONSTRAINT_028_005 provides necessary conditions for Freddie Mac to consider consenting to subordinate financing. BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_035_002 blocks Freddie Mac from approving servicing-related transactions when any borrower/principal/property manager is an SCP Named Party. Thus, SCP match is a decisive override.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_035_002 triggers, Freddie Mac will not approve consent regardless of satisfying LTV/DCR thresholds. Automation should short-circuit the consent workflow at SCP screening to prevent false-positive approvals and reduce processing time.

business_rules.rule_name: Unit inspections at underwriting: 80% or 10% with min/max
business_rules.description: At full underwriting, the Seller must conduct a complete property inspection. Unit inspection sampling must follow unit-count thresholds excluding Down Units and commercial units: (a) properties with 30 units or less: inspect 80% of units with no fewer than 5 and no more than 15; (b) properties with greater than 30 units: inspect 10% of units with no fewer than 10 and no more than 30. The sample must include representative vacant and occupied units with at least 50% occupied, across unit types and floor levels (top/bottom), and include any owner-operated guest suites, corporate lease units, or short-term stay operator units. If calculations produce a decimal, truncate to nearest whole number if minimum is still met. If 80%/10% yields insufficient inspected units to meet lease audit requirements, inspect additional units. Seller must inspect all Down Units and all commercial units.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at full underwriting when complete inspection and lease audit required
business_rules.consequences: If met, inspection sampling complies; if not, underwriting inspection is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Decimal truncation permitted if minimum unit count is met

source_reference.section_id: 8.15(b) Complete property inspection and lease audit (04/21/26)
source_reference.source_text: At full underwriting, in addition to conducting the inspection requirements specified in Section 8.15(a) above, the Seller must conduct a complete property inspection including, but not limited to, the following: 1. The unit inspection must include the following: a. For Properties with 30 units or less: (i) 80 percent of units, with no fewer than 5 units and no more than 15 units. This excludes Down Units and commercial units and must include: • A representative sample of vacant units and occupied units, of which at least 50 percent must be occupied... (ii) All Down Units (iii) All commercial units b. For Properties with greater than 30 units: (i) 10 percent of units, with no fewer than 10 units and no more than 30 units... (ii) All Down Units (iii) All commercial units

examples: Property with 24 units (no Down Units, no commercial): 80% = 19.2 → truncate to 19 units, but apply max 15; inspect 15 units and ensure at least 8 are occupied (≥50%).
Property with 30 units plus 2 Down Units and 1 commercial unit: sample 80% of (30-2-1)=27 units → 21.6 → 21, but cap at 15; inspect 15 regular units plus all 2 Down Units and the 1 commercial unit.
Property with 120 units (no Down Units, 2 commercial): sample 10% of 118 = 11.8 → 11 units (≥10) plus all commercial units; if lease audit needs more, inspect additional units beyond 11.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric thresholds (80%, 10%, min/max, 50% occupied) and explicit inclusion of Down/Commercial units.

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

dependencies.rationale: BR_UNDERWRITES_CONSTRAINT_004_002 sets the unit inspection count thresholds; BR_UNDERWRITES_PROHIBITION_004_004 prevents borrower/property manager influence in which specific units are inspected. Together they form a complete control set for inspection validity.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unit-selection independence fails, the inspection may be considered compromised even if the correct number of units was inspected, leading to re-inspection, delays, and heightened risk of misrepresentation.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL unit inspection minimum is 10% or 5 units with 50% occupied
business_rules.description: At full underwriting for an SBL Mortgage, the Seller must conduct a complete property inspection. The unit inspection must include a minimum number of residential units equal to the greater of (i) 10% of total residential units at the Property or (ii) five units, excluding Down Units and commercial/non-residential units. The inspected sample must include both vacant and occupied units, and at least 50% of the inspected units must be occupied. If the 10% calculation yields a decimal, the Seller may truncate down to a whole number, provided the five-unit minimum is still satisfied. If these requirements are not met, the inspection/lease audit is incomplete and underwriting cannot be finalized per the Guide.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at full underwriting when Seller performs SBL property inspection
business_rules.consequences: If not met, inspection is incomplete and underwriting cannot be finalized per Guide
business_rules.exceptions: May require more than minimum to satisfy lease audit linkage

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 8SBL – SBL Property Fundamentals; 8SBL15(b)(1) Unit inspection
source_reference.source_text: The greater of 10 percent of the total number of units at the Property or five units.   This excludes Down Units and commercial/non -residential units and must  include a representative sample of vacant units  and occupied units  (of which at least 50% must be occupied) , unit types and floor levels, including top floor and bottom floor units , and units subject to  corporate leases. o If the calculation results in a decimal, it can be truncated to the nearest whole number so long as the minimum five-unit requirement is met (e.g., 5.9 units becomes five units).

The unit inspection must include the following: • A minimum number of residential units as follows: • The greater of 10 percent of the total number of units at the Property or five units... must include a representative sample of vacant units and occupied units (of which at least 50% must be occupied)... If the calculation results in a decimal, it can be truncated to the nearest whole number so long as the minimum five-unit requirement is met (e.g., 5.9 units becomes five units).
source_reference.confidence_note: Word positions are estimated from provided chunk excerpt.

examples: Property has 42 units → 10% = 4.2, truncated to 4 but minimum is 5 → must inspect at least 5 units.
If 6 units are inspected, at least 3 must be occupied to meet the 50% occupied requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple numeric thresholds are explicit: 10%, 5-unit minimum, 50% occupied, truncation example.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac underwriting suspense / re-inspection required

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same SBL full-underwriting unit inspection sampling minimums: inspect a minimum of the greater of 10% of total units or five units, excluding Down Units and commercial/non-residential units; include vacant and occupied units; at least 50% of inspected units must be occupied; truncation of decimals is permitted subject to the minimum five-unit floor. Same numeric thresholds and inspection context; same consequence (inspection/lease audit incomplete).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_UNDERWRITES_CONSTRAINT_006_006 selected as primary: marginally more complete language (explicitly frames as 'Seller performs SBL property inspection') while keeping identical sampling rules.

business_rules.rule_name: Lease audit size is 50% (5–15) for properties with 30 units
business_rules.description: At full underwriting, the Seller must complete and submit a lease audit form meeting unit-count-based sampling minimums/maximums: (i) Properties with 30 units or less: audit 50% of total units, with no fewer than 5 and no more than 15 units; (ii) Properties with 31 units or more: audit 10% of total units, with no fewer than 10 and no more than 30 units. At least 50% of audited leases must be units inspected, and the audit must include representative samples such as leases signed within the last 60 days and leases from inspected corporate units. If the calculation yields a decimal, it may be truncated to the nearest whole number as long as the minimum 10-unit requirement is met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller submits property inspection and lease audit at full underwriting
business_rules.consequences: Compliant sample: underwriting package complete; noncompliant: resample/redo lease audit
business_rules.exceptions: Decimal truncation allowed only if minimum 10-unit rule still met

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8 Property Fundamentals, lease audit form requirements (04/21/26)
source_reference.source_text: e. Completed applicable lease audit form,  reflect ing the following:  • For Properties with 30 units or less:  o A lease audit of 50 percent of total units, with no fewer than 5 units and no more than 15 units   • For Properties with 31 units or more:  o A lease audit of 10 percent of total units, with no fewer than 10 units and no more than 30 units   • At least 50 percent of leases audited must be units inspected ; the remainder can be chosen randomly by the ins pector  after includ ing representative samples of:  o Leases from any inspected corporate units  o Leases signed within the last 60 days  If the calculation results in a decimal, it can be truncated to the nearest whole number so long as the minimum 10 -unit requirement is met

examples: Property has 24 units: 50% = 12 leases; audit 12 (within 5–15) and inspect at least 6 of those units.
Property has 8 units: 50% = 4, but minimum is 5; audit 5 leases and inspect at least 3 of the audited units.
Property has 109 units: 10% = 10.9; truncate to 10 (meets minimum 10) and audit 10 leases, inspecting at least 5 of them.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

dependencies.rationale: Lease audit sampling is partially constrained by the inspection population. Without completing inspection sampling per BR_UNDERWRITES_CONSTRAINT_004_002, the lease audit cannot satisfy the '50% audited leases must be units inspected' linkage.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If inspection sampling is incomplete/noncompliant, lease audit composition will also become noncompliant (or unprovable), triggering rework of both inspection and audit and delaying underwriting sign-off.

dependencies.rationale: BR_UNDERWRITES_CONSTRAINT_025_001 imposes sampling percentages and min/max unit counts. When computations produce decimals, BR_UNDERWRITES_CALCULATION_023_001 defines how to truncate while preserving a minimum requirement, preventing sampling math errors and rework.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If truncation rules are not followed, the Seller may under-sample or miscalculate the required number of units, leading to an invalid lease audit and re-performance.

business_rules.rule_name: Conversion requires 85% occupancy for 3 months
business_rules.description: To satisfy the Minimum Occupancy Requirement for Conversion, the Property must have stabilized residential occupancy of 85% for the three consecutive months preceding the submission of the Conversion Underwriting Package. This requirement must be met both at package submission and at Conversion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Forward Commitment Conversion when submitting the Conversion Underwriting Package
business_rules.consequences: If met, occupancy condition satisfied; if not, Freddie Mac purchase condition is not satisfied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 17A, Requirements for Conversion, Minimum Occupancy Requirement (12/16/25)
source_reference.source_text: 3. Minimum Occupancy Requirement .  • The Property must h ave stabilized residential occupancy at 85 percent for the three consecutive months preceding the submission of the  Conversion Underwriting Package

examples: Package submitted 2026-06-15: property occupancy must be ≥85% in March, April, and May 2026.
If occupancy was 84% in one of the prior three months (e.g., 86%, 84%, 88%), the condition is not satisfied.
If occupancy is 90% now but did not meet ≥85% for all three preceding months, the condition is not satisfied for submission.
Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: The minimum occupancy requirement must be satisfied for the three months preceding submission of the Conversion Underwriting Package and again at Conversion. BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_PROCESS_021_002 explicitly states the Seller/Servicer must obtain monthly certified rent rolls 'to support physical occupancy determination for Conversion delivery.' Therefore, the occupancy test depends on the rent roll collection/certification process.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If certified monthly rent rolls are missing/uncertified, the Seller cannot substantiate the 85% stabilized occupancy requirement. Freddie Mac may reject the Conversion Underwriting Package, delay Conversion, or require re-submission once adequate documentation is available.

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_CONSTRAINT_021_001 is a purchase/conversion eligibility condition (minimum occupancy) for Conversion. BR_BORROWER_PROHIBITION_007_003 is a universal ineligibility rule; thus sanctions clearance gates conversion underwriting and eligibility evaluation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sanctions screening fails, conversion cannot proceed even if occupancy is 85% for 3 months; the loan is effectively ineligible and the workflow must stop or replace parties.

business_rules.rule_name: Additional Actual Loan Amount capped at 10% and TEL balance
business_rules.description: At TEL conversion, the Seller may request an increase in the Actual Loan Amount beyond the Rate Locked Maximum Funding Loan Amount, but the increase may not exceed the Additional Actual Loan Amount Percentage listed in the Confirmation Sheet; if not listed and the Forward Commitment revision date is 2024-02-29 or earlier, the percentage is 10%. Additionally, in no event may the Actual Loan Amount at Conversion exceed the TEL outstanding immediately prior to Conversion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller requests Additional Actual Loan Amount at TAH TEL Conversion delivery
business_rules.consequences: If within caps, request is eligible for Freddie Mac discretion; if above caps, request is not allowable
business_rules.exceptions: If Confirmation Sheet lists a percentage, that governs instead of 10%

source_reference.source_text: the Seller may request at the time Seller delivers its TAH TEL Conversion Underwriting Package that the Actual Loan Amount be increased beyond the Rate Locked Maximum Funding Loan Amount as set forth in the Confirmation Sheet by an amount that does not exceed the Additional Actual Loan Amount Percentage listed in the Confirmation Sheet (or, if not listed in the Confirmation Sheet, then for those Forward Commitments with revision dates of 2-29-2024 or earlier , 10%) (the “ Additional Actual Loan Amount Percentage ”) of such Rate Locked Maximum Funding Loan Amount (any such increase referred to as the “ Additional Actual Loan Amount ”); provided, however, in no event will the Actual Loan Amount at Conversion exceed the amount of the TEL outstanding immediately prior to Conversion.

examples: Confirmation Sheet lists 7%: Rate Locked Max Funding $10,000,000; max Additional Actual Loan Amount = $700,000, subject to TEL outstanding cap.
No percentage listed and Forward Commitment revision date 2024-02-15: default cap is 10%; Rate Locked Max Funding $8,000,000; max increase $800,000.
If TEL outstanding immediately prior to Conversion is $9,500,000, Actual Loan Amount at Conversion cannot exceed $9,500,000 even if percentage cap would allow more.
Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

business_rules.rule_name: Rent schedule must be within 7.5% tolerance of actuals
business_rules.description: The rent schedule submitted to Freddie Mac must contain no errors of which the Seller has knowledge and must accurately state both gross potential rents and actual leased rents for the Home Sites and any Borrower-Owned Homes for the Property within a tolerance range of 7.5 percent.
business_rules.conditions: When submitting the rent schedule to Freddie Mac for an MHC Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met: schedule acceptable; if violated: cure/credit or delivery conditions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The rent schedule submitted to Freddie Mac must: • Contain no errors of which the Seller has knowledge, and • Accurately state both the gross potential rents and the actual leased rents for the Home Sites and any Borrower -Owned Homes for the Property within a tolerance range of 7.5 percent

examples: Actual leased rents total $100,000/month; submitted rent schedule states $106,000/month (6% variance): within 7.5% tolerance.
Actual leased rents total $100,000/month; submitted schedule states $110,000/month (10% variance): outside tolerance; must be corrected.
Seller knows a rent roll entry is wrong and submits anyway: violates 'no errors of which the Seller has knowledge'.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / repurchase exposure under R&Ws
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit tolerance threshold (7.5%) and 'must' language.

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

business_rules.rule_name: Delinquency reporting fees: $250, $550, $1,000 then $1,000 monthly; due by 5th business day
business_rules.description: If a Servicer fails to provide timely, complete, and accurate monthly Mortgage Delinquency reporting data (late, omitted, or unusable), Freddie Mac may assess delinquency reporting noncompliance fees: 1st month $250; 2nd month $550; 3rd month $1,000; each additional month $1,000. Freddie Mac will assess only one fee per month regardless of number of violations in that month. Fees are due no later than the fifth Business Day after the first accounting cutoff date following the missed reporting due date, and must be remitted using the myOptigo Servicer Remittance system.
business_rules.conditions: When monthly delinquency reporting is late, omitted, or unusable for a given month
business_rules.consequences: Meets: no fee assessed; Violated: fee assessed and must be remitted by due date
business_rules.exceptions: Only one fee per month regardless of number of violations

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 44, Delinquency reporting noncompliance fees (04/22/25)
source_reference.source_text: Any Servicer that fails to comply with the reporting requirements set forth in this section is subject to the following Delinquency reporting noncompliance fees: 1st month - $250 2nd month - $550 3rd month - $1,000 Each additional month - $1,000 Freddie Mac will assess only one Delinquency reporting noncompliance fee per month regardless of the number of violations that occur in that month... Fees are due to Freddie Mac no later than the fifth Business Day after the first accounting cutoff date following the missed reporting due date

examples: Servicer misses the due date in January for the first time; fee assessed is $250 and must be paid by the 5th Business Day after the first cutoff following the missed due date.
In March, Servicer has both an omitted report and an unusable report condition; only one fee applies for the month.
After three consecutive months of failures, the fourth month failure remains $1,000 (each additional month).
Monthly servicing report submitted for all active loans in portfolio
Servicing report includes 30-day delinquency bucket updates
Corrected servicing report filed after reconciliation

dependencies.rationale: BR_REPORTS_SERVICING_ACTIVITY_PROCESS_029_005 sets the submission requirement (third Business Day by 3:00 p.m. ET; usable, accurate, timely). BR_REPORTS_SERVICING_ACTIVITY_CONSTRAINT_029_006 defines the graduated noncompliance fees when reporting is late/omitted/unusable. This is a direct trigger relationship used for automated fee assessment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If delinquency reporting is late/omitted/unusable, fees are assessed and must be remitted by the stated due date, creating immediate financial and compliance consequences and potential escalation with Freddie Mac.

business_rules.rule_name: Shared Use Agreement must be perpetual and recorded before Funding
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac will consider purchasing an SBL Mortgage on a Property that uses Essential Facilities or Recreational Facilities not located on the Property and not under exclusive Borrower control only if the Shared Use Agreement is recorded in the applicable land records prior to the Freddie Mac Funding Date and is included in the insured legal description, provides perpetual access/use rights that inure to future owners, and does not allow loss of use upon breach (though a subordinate lien for unpaid maintenance costs may be permitted).
business_rules.conditions: When a Property relies on off-site/shared Essential or Recreational Facilities under a Shared Use Agreement
business_rules.consequences: If met: shared-facilities condition satisfied; if violated: mortgage is not eligible for purchase consideration
business_rules.exceptions: Subordinate lien for unpaid maintenance costs may be permitted

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8SBL – SBL Property Fundamentals, e. Shared Essential/Recreational Facilities (06/29/17)
source_reference.source_text: 2. The Shared Use Agreement must be recorded in the applicable land records for the jurisdiction prior to the Freddie Mac Funding Date and must be included in the insured legal description of the Property. 3. The Shared Use Agreement must contain a stipulation that access to and use and enjoyment of the Essential Facilities and/or Recreational Facilities are perpetual and that such rights will inure to the benefit of all future owners of the Property. 5. Each party’s remedies in the event of a breach by any other party must be generally acceptable to a prudent lender. The Shared Use Agreement may not allow for loss of use in the event of a breach. However, the Shared Use Agreement may permit the placement of a lien which is subordinate to the SBL Mortgage for unpaid maintenance costs.

examples: Shared pool is on adjacent parcel under recorded Shared Use Agreement that grants perpetual rights to all future owners → condition satisfied.
Shared parking agreement is unrecorded at Funding Date → noncompliant; mortgage should not be delivered for purchase until recording/legal description requirements are met.
Agreement permits a maintenance lien, but lien is subordinate to the SBL Mortgage → acceptable under the exception.
A 200-unit property secures a first-lien Freddie Mac mortgage
Property secures a refinanced mortgage after payoff of prior lien
Property secures a mortgage that is later securitized into a PC pool

dependencies.rationale: Eligibility for shared/off-site facilities depends on the Shared Use Agreement meeting specific legal/recording conditions. BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_019_001 is the underwriting-package documentation control that collects and presents counsel’s confirmation or gap analysis about Shared Access/Use Agreement compliance and facility descriptions, enabling Freddie to validate that the eligibility requirements in BR_SECURES_ELIGIBILITY_010_003 are actually met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the documentation package does not include the required shared facility descriptions and counsel confirmation/gap analysis, Freddie may be unable to confirm eligibility and may condition or reject purchase consideration until the missing documentation is cured.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL Property tax parcels must be entire parcel(s) with no extra land
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage, the Property must be identified as a single tax parcel, or if identified as multiple tax parcels, the Property must constitute the entirety of those tax parcels. No parcel containing the Property may include any property that is not subject to the SBL Mortgage sold to Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: When determining SBL Property eligibility based on tax parcel configuration
business_rules.consequences: If met: parcel configuration acceptable; if violated: ineligible until collateral/parcel issues resolved
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: The Property must be identified as a single tax parcel or, if identified as multiple tax parcels, the Property must constitute the entirety of those tax parcels. Any tax parcel or parcels in which the Property is located may not include property that is not subject to the SBL Mortgage sold to Freddie Mac.

examples: Property is on two contiguous parcels and the collateral includes both parcels entirely → compliant.
Property is on a parcel that also includes an unrelated outparcel not included in the mortgage lien → noncompliant; must restructure collateral or subdivide.
Property is a single parcel exactly matching the improvements and land → compliant.
A 200-unit property secures a first-lien Freddie Mac mortgage
Property secures a refinanced mortgage after payoff of prior lien
Property secures a mortgage that is later securitized into a PC pool

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Independent Property Purchase Allowed Only if Cross-Tied and Coterminous
business_rules.description: If the property is not and will not be an Independent Property as of the Origination Date and Freddie Mac has not required a reserve of funds or additional recourse under Section 8.6(c), Freddie Mac may agree to purchase the mortgage only if all stated requirements are met: the mortgage must be cross-defaulted and cross-collateralized with any mortgage for property where essential facilities/recreational facilities/direct access are located; the mortgage must be coterminous with those mortgages; the properties must be operated as a single unit with unimpeded ingress/egress to each other and to the facilities/access; and the Cross-Collateralization Agreement must not permit release of any property unless each remaining property can meet Independent Property requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when collateral is not/will not be Independent Property as of origination
business_rules.consequences: If all requirements met, Freddie Mac may purchase; if any fail, purchase may be denied
business_rules.exceptions: Only applicable when Freddie Mac has not required reserve or additional recourse

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 8 – d. Cross-collateralized, cross-defaulted and coterminous (06/30/15)
source_reference.source_text: If the Property is not and will not be an Independent Property as of the Origination Date and Freddie Mac has not required a Reserve of the funds or additional recourse pursuant to Section 8.6(c), Freddie Mac may agree to purchase the Mortgage if each of the following requirements is met: • The Mortgage is cross -defaulted and cross -collateralized... • The Mortgage is coterminous... • The Property and the property... are operated as a single unit with the Property and the other property having unimpeded ingress and egress... • The Cross -Collateralization Agreement does not permit the release of any property unless each of the remaining properties is able to meet the requirements for an Independent Property

examples: Two parcels operate as one community; loans are cross-defaulted, cross-collateralized, and share coterminous maturity dates; Freddie Mac may agree to purchase.
Cross-collateral agreement allows one parcel to be released even though the remaining parcel lacks essential facilities; this fails eligibility and purchase may be denied.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must-meet bullets; conditional applicability is clear.

examples: Freddie Mac purchases a delivered mortgage and schedules settlement
Purchase conditioned on curative documentation to be provided post-settlement
Freddie Mac declines purchase due to noncompliance findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_PURCHASES_ELIGIBILITY_019_003 explicitly conditions its applicability on Freddie Mac not requiring a reserve of funds or additional recourse under Section 8.6(c), which is the same decision space described in BR_PURCHASES_EXCEPTION_019_002 (Freddie may require modifications and/or reserves). Operationally, the workflow must first determine whether Freddie imposed those conditions; only then can the seller know whether they may proceed under the alternate cross-collateralization/cross-default structure path in BR_PURCHASES_ELIGIBILITY_019_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_PURCHASES_EXCEPTION_019_002 is not evaluated/cleared (i.e., it is unknown whether Freddie required reserves/recourse), the seller cannot reliably select the correct purchase-eligibility pathway for a non-Independent Property. This can cause mis-structured delivery packages, purchase conditions, or outright purchase denial.

dependencies.rationale: BR_PURCHASES_ELIGIBILITY_019_003 allows purchase eligibility only if certain cross-default/cross-collateralization/coterminous requirements are met. BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_019_004 prohibits using cross-collateralization/cross-default with any other mortgage not simultaneously purchased by Freddie Mac for meeting the non-Independent Property requirements. Therefore, any attempt to satisfy BR_PURCHASES_ELIGIBILITY_019_003 must first pass the prohibition test in BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_019_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_PURCHASES_PROHIBITION_019_004 fails, the cross-collateralization/cross-default structure is invalid for compliance. The mortgage becomes ineligible under the non-Independent Property eligibility pathway and Freddie Mac may decline purchase.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_003 provides conditions under which Freddie may agree to purchase when the property is not/will not be an Independent Property. BR_007_003 overrides any such eligibility pathway by prohibiting purchase/approvals upon confirmed list match. Therefore BR_007_003 must be cleared before BR_019_003 is meaningfully evaluated.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails: immediate stop—Freddie will not purchase regardless of BR_019_003 satisfaction; transaction must be terminated or restructured with different parties (where permissible).

dependencies.rationale: Rule 019_003 sets a conditional purchase-eligibility pathway when the property is not/will not be Independent Property. Rule 019_001 requires the Seller to provide detailed facility/access descriptions and legal counsel analysis regarding Shared Access/Use Agreements, which are key to determining whether the 019_003 requirements and structure are necessary and correctly documented. In practice, 019_003 cannot be confidently applied without the information package required by 019_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 019_001 fails: (1) eligibility determination under 019_003 may be delayed or inaccurate, (2) Freddie Mac may suspend review pending missing data, (3) higher risk of later discovery that structure fails eligibility conditions, causing purchase denial late in the process.

business_rules.rule_name: Forward Commitment Conversion Requires 85% Occupancy for 3 Consecutive Months
business_rules.description: For a conventional cash mortgage under a Forward Commitment to be eligible for purchase at Conversion, the property must have stabilized residential occupancy at 85% for the three consecutive months preceding submission of the Conversion Underwriting Package. From completion of construction through Conversion, the Borrower must provide the Seller a current monthly rent roll certified as true and correct by the Borrower and the property manager, plus other information reasonably required to determine physical occupancy.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Forward Commitment conversions at package submission and on Conversion date
business_rules.consequences: If 85% for 3 months, condition satisfied; if not, Freddie Mac will not purchase at Conversion
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: underwriting package for the Forward Commitment . 3. Minimum Occupancy Requirement . • The Property must h ave stabilized residential occupancy at 85 percent for the three consecutive months preceding the submission of the Conversion Underwriting Package • For each month from and after completion of construction ,

examples: Occupancy is 86%, 88%, and 85% in the three months before package submission: condition is met and Conversion can proceed.
Occupancy is 84% in one of the three months before package submission: condition is not met and Freddie Mac purchase at Conversion is blocked until stabilized.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 85% and 3-month consecutive measurement window.

examples: Freddie Mac purchases a delivered mortgage and schedules settlement
Purchase conditioned on curative documentation to be provided post-settlement
Freddie Mac declines purchase due to noncompliance findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_007 defines stabilized occupancy and rent-roll delivery conditions for eligibility at Conversion. BR_007_003 prohibits purchase and related approvals upon confirmed sanctions/exclusion match. Thus BR_007_003 must be cleared first; otherwise BR_019_007 cannot lead to purchase even if satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails: (1) Freddie will not purchase at Conversion, (2) borrower may incur carrying costs and lose takeout execution, (3) Seller must resolve sanctions match or terminate.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 021_001 defines the stabilized occupancy threshold and timing for submission and at Conversion. Rule 019_007 states that to be eligible for purchase at Conversion, the property must have stabilized residential occupancy at 85% for the three consecutive months preceding submission of the Conversion Underwriting Package (and also requires monthly certified rent rolls). Thus, the occupancy requirement in 021_001 is a gating prerequisite to the purchase eligibility determination in 019_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 021_001 fails: (1) the Conversion Underwriting Package is noncompliant, (2) Freddie Mac will not purchase at Conversion under 019_007, (3) Borrower/Seller may incur extension costs, re-underwriting, and potential transaction failure.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 019_007 includes an ongoing documentation requirement: from completion of construction through Conversion, the Borrower must provide a current monthly rent roll certified true/correct by Borrower and property manager. Rule 021_002 operationalizes that same requirement and states the Seller/Servicer must obtain them to support occupancy determination. Therefore, implementing 021_002 is mandatory to satisfy 019_007’s conditions for purchase at Conversion.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 021_002 fails: (1) 019_007’s documentation condition is unmet, (2) Freddie Mac may refuse purchase at Conversion, (3) Seller faces delays, cure conditions, and potential non-delivery/purchase fallout.

business_rules.rule_name: Private transfer fee covenant created after 2011-02-08 makes mortgage ineligible
business_rules.description: If the Title Policy contains an exception for a Private Transfer Fee Covenant that was created on or after February 8, 2011, the Mortgage is ineligible for purchase by Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at purchase review when title policy exceptions include private transfer fee covenant
business_rules.consequences: If exception present and covenant date ≥ 2011-02-08: not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 29.2(g) Exception for Private Transfer Fee Covenant (04/22/25)
source_reference.source_text: g. Exception for Private Transfer Fee Covenant (0 4/22/25) If the Title Policy contains an exception for a Private Transfer Fee Covenant that was created on or after February 8, 2011, the Mortgage is ineligible for purchase by Freddie Mac.

examples: Title policy lists an exception for a private transfer fee covenant recorded 2013-09-01: ineligible for purchase.
Title policy exception references a private transfer fee covenant created 2011-02-08: ineligible (on or after).
Title policy has no private transfer fee covenant exception: passes this eligibility check.
Freddie Mac purchases a delivered mortgage and schedules settlement
Purchase conditioned on curative documentation to be provided post-settlement
Freddie Mac declines purchase due to noncompliance findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_009 is an eligibility screen based on participation by excluded parties. BR_025_007 is a title-policy-based ineligibility rule for private transfer fee covenants created on/after Feb 8, 2011. While neither rule is a literal prerequisite to the other, purchase eligibility workflows should treat them as complementary checks that together determine whether a mortgage can be purchased.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_018_009 is executed but BR_025_007 is not: (1) the loan may progress toward purchase despite a title-policy exception that makes it categorically ineligible, (2) late discovery causes purchase denial and pipeline fallout, and (3) corrective actions (endorsements/curative title work) may be impossible if the covenant is disqualifying.

dependencies.rationale: BR_016_005 requires confirmation of zoning/building code compliance and certificates of occupancy (or confirmation that absence is not a violation with enforcement risk). BR_025_007 makes the mortgage ineligible if the title policy contains a disqualifying private transfer fee covenant exception created on/after Feb 8, 2011. Both must be satisfied for a clean property/legal compliance posture; failure of either can stop purchase.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_016_005 is satisfied but BR_025_007 is missed (or vice versa): (1) the loan file may appear ‘property compliant’ while still being ineligible for purchase due to title exception, (2) late-stage purchase denial occurs, and (3) curative actions may be costly or impossible depending on the covenant/exception.

dependencies.rationale: BR_025_007 is a purchase ineligibility test based on the Title Policy exception for a Private Transfer Fee Covenant created on/after 2/8/2011. BR_007_003 independently prohibits purchase upon confirmed sanctions/exclusion match. Therefore sanctions clearance is a gating prerequisite for proceeding with title-based eligibility analysis and purchase decisioning.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_007_003 fails: purchase is denied irrespective of title; compliance workflow should short-circuit to sanctions resolution instead of continuing title review.

business_rules.rule_name: Accelerated Mortgage File Retention 10 Years from Resolution
business_rules.description: If a Mortgage was accelerated and the Property was acquired by Freddie Mac or sold at a third-party auction, the Servicer must retain the Mortgage File for at least 10 years from the resolution date. Even after the 10-year period expires, the Servicer may not destroy the files without Freddie Mac’s written approval.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Mortgage was accelerated and REO/auction disposition occurred
business_rules.consequences: Early destruction violates Guide; Servicer subject to corrective action and damages
business_rules.exceptions: Destruction after 10 years requires Freddie Mac written approval

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 34 – Retention of Mortgage File (item 3)
source_reference.source_text: For a minimum of 10 years from the resolution date if the Mortgage was accelerated and the Property was either acquired by Freddie Mac or sold at a third-party auction (Even after the expiration of the 10-year period, the Servicer may not destroy these files without written approval from Freddie Mac.)

examples: Resolution date is 2020-09-30 after foreclosure and third-party auction sale: file must be retained through at least 2030-09-30.
Servicer wants to destroy file in 2031: must obtain Freddie Mac written approval before destruction.
Property acquired by Freddie Mac (REO) following acceleration: 10-year retention clock starts at the resolution date defined by the workout/disposition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Servicer corrective action; potential servicing transfer; indemnification exposure

examples: Seller/Servicer retains closing package documents for the required retention period
Seller/Servicer retains accounting and servicing reports for audit support
Seller/Servicer applies legal hold to a loan file under investigation

business_rules.rule_name: Builder's Risk Coverage Minimum 100% of Contract
business_rules.description: If a project is not covered by the Borrower’s primary property insurance policies, the Borrower must obtain a separate Builder’s Risk policy. Coverage must equal at least 100% of the sum of the project contract(s) and all materials to complete the work, plus applicable soft costs.
business_rules.conditions: When construction/rehab project not covered by primary property policy
business_rules.consequences: If ≥100% coverage is in place, insurance requirement satisfied; if not, noncompliant coverage
business_rules.exceptions: Builder’s Risk may be discontinued once construction is complete

source_reference.source_text: If insurance for such projects is not provided by the Borrower’s primary property insurance policies, a separate Builder’s Risk policy is required. Coverage must equal at least 100 percent of the sum of the project contract or contracts and all materials to complete the work, as well as applicable soft costs. Once construction is complete, Builder’s Risk coverage may be discontinued.

examples: Rehab contract is $4,000,000, materials are $800,000, soft costs are $200,000; Builder’s Risk coverage must be at least $5,000,000
Primary property policy excludes renovation; Borrower obtains Builder’s Risk with coverage equal to only 90% of costs; policy fails Freddie Mac requirement
Construction completes and certificate of completion is issued; Borrower discontinues Builder’s Risk coverage as permitted
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / purchase denial until cured

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

business_rules.rule_name: Ordinance or Law Coverage A Uses IV Minus 50% Default
business_rules.description: For a legally non-conforming property where Ordinance or Law coverage is required, Coverage “A” (loss to the undamaged portion) must be no less than the Insurable Value (IV) of the property less the damage threshold percentage specified in zoning laws. If the damage threshold percentage is not specified, a 50% threshold is acceptable (i.e., Coverage A must be at least 50% of IV).
business_rules.conditions: When property is non-conforming under current building/zoning/land use laws
business_rules.consequences: If Coverage A meets minimum, requirement satisfied; if below minimum, insurance is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: If zoning damage threshold not specified, 50% threshold may be used

source_reference.source_text: Ordinance or Law coverage is required for any property that is non-conforming under current building, zoning or land use laws or ordinances. Ordinance or Law coverage must include the following: a. Coverage “A” – Loss to the undamaged portion of the Property: Coverage no less than the IV of the Property less the damage threshold percentage specified in the zoning laws. If the damage threshold is not specified, then a 50 percent threshold is acceptable.

examples: IV is $20,000,000 and zoning damage threshold is 60%; Coverage A must be at least $8,000,000 ($20,000,000 × (1 − 0.60))
IV is $12,000,000 and zoning law does not specify a damage threshold; Coverage A must be at least $6,000,000 using the 50% acceptable threshold
Coverage A is set to $4,000,000 on a $12,000,000 IV property with no stated threshold; fails because minimum is $6,000,000
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / purchase denial until corrected

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

business_rules.rule_name: Ordinance or Law Coverage B and C Minimum 10% IV Each
business_rules.description: When Ordinance or Law coverage is required, Coverage “B” (demolition cost) must equal no less than 10% of estimated IV of the property, and Coverage “C” (increased construction cost) must equal no less than 10% of estimated IV of the property. If Ordinance or Law limits are combined, the total combined amount must equal or exceed the sum of required amounts for each type of coverage.
business_rules.conditions: When Ordinance or Law insurance is required for a non-conforming property
business_rules.consequences: If B and C meet ≥10% IV and combined limits meet sum, compliant; otherwise noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Coverage “B” – Demolition cost: The cost to demolish and clear the site of undamaged parts of the Property if such demolition is required. Coverage “B” must equal no less than 10 percent of the estimated IV of the Property. c. Coverage “C” – Construction cost: Increased cost of construction to allow the Borrower to rebuild the Property to meet all applicable zoning laws. Coverage “C” must equal no less than 10 percent of the estimated IV of the Property. When Ordinance or Law limits are combined, the amount must equal or exceed the sum of the required amount of each type of coverage.

examples: Estimated IV is $30,000,000; Coverage B must be ≥ $3,000,000 and Coverage C must be ≥ $3,000,000

Policy combines Ordinance or Law coverages into one limit; IV is $30,000,000 and required B+C totals $6,000,000; combined limit must be at least $6,000,000 (plus any Coverage A/D requirements as applicable)
Coverage C is set at 5% of IV; fails because minimum is 10% of IV
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / purchase denial until corrected

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_002 sets minimum requirements for Coverage A based on Insurable Value and damage threshold; BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_003 sets minimum requirements for Coverage B and Coverage C (each ≥10% of IV) and combined-limits logic. They address separate components of the same Ordinance/Law coverage suite; satisfying one without the other leaves Ordinance/Law coverage incomplete.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, Ordinance or Law coverage is noncompliant, creating coverage gaps for rebuilding, demolition, or increased construction cost, and may violate Freddie Mac insurance requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Primary CGL Limits $1M Occurrence and $2M Aggregate
business_rules.description: The Borrower must maintain primary CGL coverage limits of $1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 in the general aggregate. If the CGL policy covers multiple locations, the aggregate limits must apply per location.
business_rules.conditions: When Borrower maintains primary CGL coverage for the property/locations
business_rules.consequences: If limits meet thresholds, compliant; if below, policy limits must be increased or replaced
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (but aggregate-per-location applies when multiple locations are covered)

source_reference.source_text: in the general aggregate If the CGL policy covers multiple locations, the aggregate limits must apply per location . 2. In addition, the Borrower must maintain,

examples: Single-property policy: $1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 aggregate; compliant
