Policy provides $500,000 per occurrence; noncompliant and must be increased to at least $1,000,000
One CGL policy covers three properties; the $2,000,000 aggregate must apply per location (not shared across all locations)
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / servicing compliance action

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_018_005 sets qualitative requirements (occurrence-based form, no listed exclusions, tail endorsement if switching). BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_006 sets quantitative minimum limits ($1M per occurrence / $2M aggregate, per location). In practice, CGL compliance requires satisfying both; they should be evaluated together in insurance review workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, CGL coverage is noncompliant. Failing 018_005 creates coverage-form/exclusion gaps; failing 018_006 creates insufficient limit protection; both can block acceptance of insurance compliance.

business_rules.rule_name: Umbrella Liability Minimum $1M to $20M by Unit Count
business_rules.description: Borrower must maintain minimum umbrella or excess liability coverage based on the aggregate number of residential units covered: up to 250: $1M; 251-500: $2M; 501-1,000: $3M; 1,001-2,000: $5M; 2,001-5,000: $10M; 5,001-10,000: $15M; more than 10,000: $20M. Minimum limits are evaluated based on total units covered and may be satisfied by any combination of primary CGL, umbrella, and/or excess liability limits, provided aggregate coverage meets the required minimum.
business_rules.conditions: When umbrella/excess liability insurance applies to covered residential units
business_rules.consequences: If total liability coverage meets unit-tier minimum, compliant; if not, limits must be increased
business_rules.exceptions: Combination of primary and umbrella/excess may satisfy, if total meets minimum

source_reference.source_text: In addition, the Borrower must maintain, at a minimum, the following umbrella or excess liability coverage: Up to 250 $1 million 251 to 500 $2 million 501 to 1,000 $3 million 1,001 to 2,000 $5 million 2,001 to 5,000 $10 million 5,001 to 10,000 $15 million More than 10,000 $20 million The minimum coverage limits in this section are to be evaluated based upon the aggregate number of residential units covered by the umbrella and/or excess liability policy, and may be satisfied with any combination of primary CGL, umbrella and/or excess liability limits, provided the aggregate coverage meets the required minimum limits.

examples: A policy covers 800 units across multiple properties; minimum umbrella/excess liability is $3,000,000
A portfolio policy covers 2,200 units; minimum umbrella/excess liability is $10,000,000
Borrower has $1M primary CGL and $1M umbrella covering 400 units; total $2M meets the 251–500 tier minimum
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / servicing compliance action

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_007 allows meeting unit-tier minimum coverage through combinations of primary, umbrella, and/or excess, but it does not eliminate the separate requirement that primary CGL must be at least $1M/$2M (BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_006). Insurance compliance review should therefore verify both base CGL limits and overall stacked coverage for the unit count.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_006 fails, the borrower may have sufficient umbrella limits but still lacks required primary coverage; if BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_007 fails, the borrower may meet base primary limits but total coverage remains insufficient for the portfolio/unit size.

dependencies.rationale: Even if combined limits meet the tier threshold in BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_007, the liability program can still be noncompliant if the primary CGL policy violates BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_018_005 (wrong form or prohibited exclusions). Insurance review workflows should couple limit sufficiency checks with policy form/exclusion checks.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_018_005 fails, the borrower may appear to meet tiered coverage under BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_007 but still be noncompliant due to excluded high-risk claim categories or claims-made gaps.

business_rules.rule_name: Loans Under 10 Million UPB Need Guarantor FICO 680 from Three Bureaus
business_rules.description: For Mortgages with less than $10 million in unpaid principal balance, at least one individual Guarantor must have an average FICO score of 680 or better with all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion, Experian). If fewer than three FICO scores are available, all available FICO scores must be 680 or better. For TIC Borrowers, the requirement applies to the Guarantor(s) for each TIC Borrower. For Supplemental Mortgages, the requirement applies only when the combined UPB of the first Mortgage and the Supplemental Mortgage are less than $10 million.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when mortgage UPB (or combined UPB) is < $10,000,000
business_rules.consequences: Meets rule: credit score requirement satisfied; violated: loan/transfer not eligible without remediation
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental loans: apply only if combined UPB < $10,000,000; TIC applies per TIC borrower

source_reference.source_text: At least one Guarantor, if an individual, must have an average FICO® score of 680 or better with all three national credit bureaus (Equifax, TransUnion or Experian). If less than three FICO scores are available, all FICO scores must be 680 or better. Additionally: • If the Borrower is a Tenancy-In-Common (TIC), this applies to the Guarantor(s) for each TIC Borrower • For Supplemental Mortgages, this applies only when the combined UPB of the first Mortgage and the Supplemental Mortgage are less than $10 million in UPB

examples: UPB is $7.5M; guarantor has FICOs 690/700/710 (avg 700): compliant.
UPB is $9.8M; guarantor has only two scores available (682 and 679): noncompliant because all available scores must be ≥680.
Supplemental Mortgage where first + supplemental combined UPB is $10.2M: this specific FICO rule does not apply based on the stated combined-UPB condition.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Highly specific numeric thresholds ($10M UPB; FICO 680; three bureaus) and clear special cases for TIC and Supplemental.

examples: Mortgage requires credit reports for key principals of the borrowing entity
Mortgage requires updated credit report due to elapsed time before closing
Mortgage requires additional credit documentation after derogatory findings

dependencies.rationale: You cannot calculate or verify the 'average FICO score of 680 or better with all three national credit bureaus' without obtaining a credit report that includes those bureau scores. BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_VALIDATION_033_008 specifies the report must be current (dated within 60 days), include FICO scores, be from an acceptable agency, and be authorized by the subject. This is a hard input dependency for enforcing BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_027_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_VALIDATION_033_008 is not satisfied (no current report, missing FICO, no authorization), the lender cannot evidence compliance with the 680-FICO requirement; delivery/eligibility can fail or require cure, delaying closing/purchase.

dependencies.rationale: In SBL deliveries, BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_VALIDATION_039_010 defines what credit reports must be provided for individual guarantors (and exceptions for entities/foreign sponsors with no SSN). When BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_027_005 applies (e.g., UPB < $10M), the only practical way to confirm the guarantor’s FICO threshold is to obtain/submit the SBL-defined current credit reports.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_VALIDATION_039_010 is not met in SBL delivery (missing current credit report or missing FICO/debt verification elements), the lender cannot substantiate the FICO eligibility threshold; delivery defects/conditions and potential purchase delays occur.

business_rules.rule_name: Title underwriter single risk cap is 25% of surplus
business_rules.description: The maximum single risk amount assumed by one Title Insurance Underwriter for any one Mortgage may not exceed 25 percent of the underwriter’s surplus to policyholders. Policies exceeding the maximum single risk amount may be acceptable if the excess is covered by reinsurance by another Title Insurance Underwriter.
business_rules.conditions: A Title Insurance Policy is being issued/underwritten for a Freddie Mac-purchased Mortgage.
business_rules.consequences: If within 25% cap (or excess reinsured), policy is eligible; if violated without reinsurance, title policy is noncompliant and must be corrected before delivery/purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Excess above 25% may be acceptable if reinsured.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 29.1(a) Maximum single risk amount (08/15/24)
source_reference.source_text: a. Maximum single risk amount ( 08/15/24 ) The maximum single risk amount (the risk in connection with any one Mortgage) assumed by one Title Insurance Underwriter  may not be more than 25 percent of such Title Insurance Underwriters’  surplus to policyholders.  Policies for amounts in excess of the maximum single risk amount may be acceptable if any excess amount is covered by reinsurance by another Title Insurance Underwr

examples: Underwriter surplus is $40,000,000; maximum single risk is 25% ($10,000,000). A $9,500,000 policy is compliant without reinsurance.
Underwriter surplus is $40,000,000; a $14,000,000 policy exceeds the $10,000,000 cap but is acceptable if the $4,000,000 excess is reinsured by another underwriter.
Policy amount is $14,000,000 with no reinsurance evidence; title delivery is rejected until reinsurance or another compliant solution is documented.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection; cure required before purchase

examples: Final title policy insures first-lien priority for delivered mortgage
Policy issued with required endorsements and acceptable exceptions
Policy issuance delayed pending curative action for a recorded lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_PURCHASES_ELIGIBILITY_025_007 declares ineligibility if the title policy contains a private transfer fee covenant exception created on/after Feb 8, 2011. BR_ISSUES_TITLE_POLICY_CONSTRAINT_040_001 requires the underwriter's maximum single risk amount to be within 25% of surplus or covered by reinsurance. These are independent constraints but operationally part of the same title-policy acceptance workflow; completing one without the other leaves title review incomplete.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either fails, the title policy is unacceptable for Freddie purchase, leading to delivery defects, required endorsements/reinsurance, or purchase ineligibility (especially for the private transfer fee covenant rule which is a hard ineligibility).

business_rules.rule_name: Payment Abatements Ineligible and HOA Over 12 Months
business_rules.description: Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, even if disclosed on the settlement statement. This prohibition applies when an interested party funds the abatement directly and/or through another entity. Payment of HOA fees is not considered an abatement unless the HOA fee payment extends for more than 12 months; HOA payments for 12 months or less are treated as IPCs.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase loans where an interested party pays borrower payments/HOA fees
business_rules.consequences: If abatement exists, loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: HOA fee payments for 12 months or less are permitted as IPCs

source_reference.source_text: Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, even if they are disclosed on the settlement statement. This prohibition applies when an interested party funds the abatement directly and/or through another entity, such as a nonproﬁt down payment assistance program. Note : The payment of HOA fees is not considered an abatement unless the payment of the fee extends for more than 12 months. Paying HOA fees for 12 months or less is considered an interested party contribution.

examples: Builder pays 6 months of the borrower’s mortgage payments after closing; the loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
Seller pays HOA dues for 18 months; treated as a payment abatement and the loan is ineligible.
Seller pays HOA dues for 12 months; treated as an IPC (still subject to IPC limits) and not a payment abatement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear ineligibility rule with explicit 12-month HOA boundary for exception.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: Both rules can independently stop delivery, but 005_010 is a sanctions-based prohibition that must be cleared before continuing any delivery eligibility screening such as the payment abatement prohibition in 009_008.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, the lender must not deliver the loan regardless of whether the transaction has an abatement; proceeding risks sanctions violations and Fannie Mae enforcement.

provenance.reasoning: Both aim to prevent loan delivery/purchase when borrower payments are advanced/covered by another party, masking true payment performance. Fannie broadly bans any payment abatement (with an HOA >12 months nuance); Freddie bans outstanding borrower payment advances within 3 months of delivery.

g1_rule.rule_name: Payment Abatements Ineligible and HOA Over 12 Months
g1_rule.description: Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, even if disclosed on the settlement statement. This prohibition applies when an interested party funds the abatement directly and/or through another entity. Payment of HOA fees is not considered an abatement unless the HOA fee payment extends for more than 12 months; HOA payments for 12 months or less are treated as IPCs.

g2_rule.rule_name: No Borrower Payment Advances Within 3 Months of Delivery
g2_rule.description: Within the three-month period before the Delivery Date, there must not have been any outstanding advance of funds made to or on behalf of the Borrower (by the Seller, a prior holder, or another at the Seller’s request) that the Borrower used to pay any monthly installment, principal, interest, or other Mortgage charges. This is a delivery eligibility condition supporting the requirement that the Mortgage is not in default.

business_rules.rule_name: Public MBS Disclosures Limited to Prospectus and Supplement Only
business_rules.description: When selling Fannie Mae securities to third parties, the lender may publicly disclose only the information contained in the MBS Prospectus and the applicable Prospectus Supplement for that security. Any additional pool or loan information disclosure to the public outside those documents is prohibited.
business_rules.conditions: Lender sells or offers Fannie Mae MBS securities to third parties
business_rules.consequences: If met, public disclosure is permitted; if violated, disclosure is non-compliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The information in the MBS Prospectus and the Prospectus Supplement for that security is the only information about a pool (or the mortgage loans included in a pool) that a lender may disclose to the public.

examples: Compliant: Lender posts only the PoolTalk link and references the MBS Prospectus and Prospectus Supplement content.
Non-compliant: Lender publishes a public spreadsheet listing loan-level borrower details beyond what the prospectus materials provide.
Non-compliant: Lender issues a press release describing additional pool loan characteristics not contained in the prospectus/supplement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: No numeric threshold present; explicit prohibition language is clear.

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Employees on GSA HUD or FHFA Lists Cannot Work Fannie Loans
business_rules.description: The seller/servicer must follow a written procedure for checking all employees (including management) involved in origination or servicing of mortgage loans against the GSA Excluded Parties List (EPL), HUD Limited Denial of Participation (LDP) List, and FHFA Suspended Counterparty Program (SCP) list. If at time of hire or any time later an individual is determined to be on any of these lists, the seller/servicer may not permit that employee to manage or perform origination or servicing functions on loans sold to Fannie Mae, regardless of the reason the individual is on the list.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at hire and ongoing for staff supporting Fannie Mae loan lifecycle.
business_rules.consequences: If listed, employee must be removed from covered functions; failure is noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; applies regardless of reason for listing.

source_reference.section_id: A3-4-03, Fraud Prevention Requirements - Hiring Practices
source_reference.source_text: The seller/servicer must follow a written procedure for checking all employees, including management, involved in the origination or servicing of mortgage loans (including application through closing and servicing) against the U.S. General Services Administration (GSA) Excluded Parties List (EPL), the HUD Limited Denial of Participation List (LDP List), and the Federal Housing Finance Agency’s (FHFA) Suspended Counterparty Program (SCP) list. If, at the time of hire or any time later, the seller/servicer has determined an individual is on the GSA, LDP, or SCP list, the seller/servicer may not permit that employee to manage or perform origination or servicing functions on loans sold to Fannie Mae, irrespective of the reason the individual is on such list.

examples: New processor is screened at hire and not on any list → may work Fannie Mae loans.
Underwriter later appears on FHFA SCP list; lender immediately removes underwriter from Fannie Mae loan underwriting → compliant response.
Lender learns a closer is on HUD LDP list but continues assigning closings on Fannie Mae loans → violation and heightened repurchase/termination risk.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: No cash wiring on positive differential after trigger events or net worth drops 20%/30%
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae will not wire cash to a lender even when there is a positive price differential if any specified triggering event has occurred, including (i) event of default under the Lender Contract or other contract, (ii) failure to meet lender eligibility requirements, (iii) adjusted net worth decline >20% over a quarterly period or >30% over two consecutive quarterly periods, (iv) total UPB of outstanding Fannie Mae repurchase requests exceeding 15% of adjusted net worth, (v) adverse information affecting counterparty relationship/financial condition, or (vi) outstanding contractual fees owed to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: When positive price differential would otherwise require Fannie Mae cash wire
business_rules.consequences: Fannie Mae withholds cash wire; may modify/eliminate thresholds without notice
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated trigger event list; thresholds may be changed at discretion

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-01 (10/06/2021), Triggering events / cash wire restriction
source_reference.source_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Fannie Mae will not wire cash to a lender in the event of a positive price diﬀerential if any of the following “triggering events” has occurred with respect to a lender: ... a decline in a lender’s adjusted net worth by more than 20% over a quarterly reporting period or by more than 30% over two consecutive quarterly periods, the total unpaid principal balance of all outstanding Fannie Mae repurchase requests to a lender exceeds 15% of the lender’s adjusted net worth, ... outstanding contractual fees owed by a lender to Fannie Mae.

examples: Lender is in the money by $200,000 but had a 25% adjusted net worth decline last quarter; Fannie Mae does not wire cash.
Outstanding repurchase requests UPB equals 18% of adjusted net worth; positive price differential exists; no cash wire is made.
Lender owes contractual fees to Fannie Mae; even with positive price differential, Fannie Mae withholds wire.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Withhold margin cash; modify thresholds; counterparty action

examples: Fannie Mae Selling Guide (Published March 4, 2026)
Fannie Mae QC process for purchased or securitized loans
Fannie Mae remedies including compensatory fees or loan repurchase

dependencies.rationale: BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 applies when a positive price differential would otherwise require a cash wire, but a triggering event has occurred—explicitly including “failure to meet lender eligibility requirements” and net worth decline triggers. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001 is a core eligibility requirement; violation can place the lender in a triggering event state that activates BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If net worth requirements are not met, even favorable trading economics (positive price differential) may not result in a cash wire to the lender, creating liquidity stress and downstream operational impacts.

dependencies.rationale: BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 includes “failure to meet lender eligibility requirements” as a triggering event. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_003 is an eligibility liquidity requirement; a breach can place the lender in a triggering event posture where Fannie may withhold otherwise payable wires.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If liquidity falls below minimum, the lender may lose access to expected cash wires from positive price differentials, worsening liquidity and potentially accelerating default risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_025_003 describes termination of approval and technology access when minimum activity requirements are not met. BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 triggers when lender eligibility requirements are not met. Approval termination and loss of approved status typically correspond to eligibility failure states relevant to the triggering-event logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If approval is terminated, the lender may also be subject to wire restrictions under triggering-event logic, affecting trading settlement cash flows.

business_rules.rule_name: Multi-dwelling Condo Units Prohibited Unless Two or More Legal Units Combined
business_rules.description: Projects that contain multi-dwelling unit condos or co-ops are not permitted. The restriction applies when a single legal unit is subdivided into multiple residential dwellings under one deed and financed by a single mortgage/share loan. Exception: This restriction does not apply when the borrower buys two or more individual legal units and structurally and legally combines them into a single-unit dwelling, provided all stated requirements (single legal unit under single deed, legal description, amended project legal documents, and completed renovations) are met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to condo/co-op projects where a legal unit is subdivided into multiple dwellings.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: project/unit ineligible for delivery. If exception met: unit may be eligible if all conditions satisfied.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception for combining two or more individual legal units into one, with listed conditions.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-03, Projects that Contain Multi-Dwelling Unit Condos or Co-ops
source_reference.source_text: Projects that Contain Multi-Dwelling Unit Condos or Co-ops Projects that contain multi-dwelling units are not permitted. These projects allow an owner to hold title (or share ownership and the accompanying occupancy rights) to a single legal unit that is sub-divided into multiple residential dwellings within the single legal unit, with ownership of the unit (or shares) evidenced by a single deed and financed by a single mortgage (or share loan). The sub-divided units are not separate legal units. This provision does not apply to condo or co-op projects that allow an individual to buy two or more individual legal units with the intent of structurally and legally combining the units for occupancy as a single-unit dwelling.

examples: A condo unit is a single deed but contains two rentable studios behind separate interior doors; ineligible as a multi-dwelling unit condo.
A co-op share loan finances one legal unit subdivided into two separate dwelling areas with independent entrances; ineligible under this rule.

Borrower buys two adjacent legal condo units, completes permitted renovations, and project documents are amended to reclassify as one unit; may qualify under the stated exception if all requirements are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility (cannot sell to Fannie Mae)

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Setback land-use rules blocking rebuild allow 0 purchase
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize a loan secured by a property subject to specific land-use regulations (e.g., coastal tideland or wetland laws) that create setback lines or other provisions preventing reconstruction or maintenance of the improvements if they are damaged or destroyed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when land-use regulations prevent reconstruction/maintenance after damage.
business_rules.consequences: Meets prohibition trigger: loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize a loan secured by a property that is subject to certain land-use regulations, such as coastal tideland or wetland laws, that create setback lines or other provisions that prevent the reconstruction or maintenance of the property improvements if they are damaged or destroyed. The intent of these types of land-use regulations is to remove existing land uses and to stop land development

examples: Coastal property lies in a tideland setback zone where rebuilding after storm loss is prohibited—loan ineligible.
Wetland regulation prevents maintenance of an existing seawall required to protect improvements—loan ineligible.
Local zoning is restrictive but still allows repair/rebuild after casualty—this prohibition does not apply.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'will not purchase' prohibition; no numeric thresholds but clear triggering condition.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Master Policy Must Settle Claims at Replacement Cost
business_rules.description: When a master property insurance policy is required for a project development, it must settle claims on a replacement cost basis. Policies that settle on an actual cash value basis, or otherwise limit/depreciate/reduce losses to anything other than replacement cost, are not acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: When master property insurance is required for a condo/co-op/PUD project loan
business_rules.consequences: Meets: master policy acceptable; Violates: policy unacceptable; must be replaced to deliver/retain loan
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The master property insurance policy must provide for claims to be settled on a replacement cost basis. Property insurance policies that provide for claims to be settled on an actual cash value basis are not acceptable. Policies that limit, depreciate, reduce or otherwise settle losses at anything other than a replacement cost basis are also not acceptable.

examples: HOA policy states losses are paid at actual cash value for roofs; this is not acceptable—HOA must obtain compliant replacement-cost coverage.
Co-op master policy is replacement cost but excludes certain building components from replacement-cost settlement; lender must treat as noncompliant.
Master policy explicitly provides replacement cost settlement for building; acceptable if other required perils/amounts are satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Parties in possession rights limited to 2 years, no ROFR
business_rules.description: For a conventional loan, title is acceptable with rights of lawful parties in possession only if those rights do not include a right of first refusal to purchase the property, and the duration of any such rights (including a tenant lease term) is not more than two years. If the possession rights include a right of first refusal or exceed two years, the title condition is not acceptable under the listed minor impediments and must be cleared/mitigated to meet Fannie Mae purchase/securitization standards.
business_rules.conditions: Conventional loan; title review finds “rights of parties in possession”
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, title acceptable; if exceeded, loan not eligible until cured
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-2-05, Minor Impediments to Title for Conventional Loans
source_reference.source_text: rights of lawful parties in possession, as long as such rights do not include the right of ﬁrst refusal to purchase the property. (No rights of parties in possession, including the term of a tenant’s lease, may have a duration of more than two years.);

examples: Purchase SFR: existing month-to-month tenant with no purchase option; acceptable if lender ensures no ROFR exists.
Investment property conversion attempt: lease shows 36-month term; not acceptable under minor impediments and must be modified/cleared before delivery.
Title exception shows a right of first refusal in favor of occupant; prohibited—loan ineligible for delivery until removed.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric (2 years) and explicit prohibition (no right of first refusal) stated as condition for acceptable minor title impediment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for purchase or securitization

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Private transfer fee covenants created on/after Feb 8 2011 barred
business_rules.description: Except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after February 8, 2011. Lenders must maintain policies and procedures/controls to ensure delivered loans are not secured by properties with private transfer fee covenants that are unacceptable under the regulation. If an unacceptable covenant exists, the loan is ineligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Property has private transfer fee covenant created on/after 2011-02-08
business_rules.consequences: If unacceptable covenant, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: Excepted transfer fee covenants as defined in 12 CFR Part 1228

source_reference.source_text: Private Transfer Fee Covenants In accordance with a regulation issued by the Federal Housing Finance Agency on March 16, 2012, as amended, and codiﬁed at 12 CFR Part 1228 (the “Private Transfer Fee Regulation”), except as provided below, Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants if those covenants were created on or after February 8, 2011, unless permitted by the Private Transfer Fee Regulation. The lender must establish policies and/or procedures to ensure that the loans it delivers to Fannie Mae... are not secured by properties encumbered with a private transfer fee that is unacceptable under the Private Transfer Fee Regulation.

Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants if those covenants were created on or after February 8, 2011, unless permitted by the Private Transfer Fee Regulation. The lender must establish policies and/or procedures to ensure that the loans it delivers to Fannie Mae, whether or not the loans were originated by the lender, are not secured by properties encumbered with a private transfer fee that is unacceptable under the Private Transfer Fee Regulation.

examples: Property deed includes a private transfer fee covenant recorded in 2013 that is not an excepted covenant; loan is ineligible for Fannie Mae purchase.
Property has an excepted transfer fee covenant under 12 CFR Part 1228 (e.g., shared equity exception); loan may be eligible if all other criteria met.
Lender lacks procedures to screen title for private transfer fee covenants; operational review may cite deficiency and loans may be rejected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules implement the same business logic: except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase/securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after 2011-02-08, and lenders must maintain controls to avoid delivering such loans. Numeric trigger date and the regulatory carve-out are identical; context (property-level eligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition) and consequence (loan ineligible) align. Differences are only phrasing and category label (compliance vs prohibition).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_035_008 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same prohibition plus clarifying that certain covenants may be permitted/excepted under the FHFA regulation, and it frames the requirement directly as a purchase/securitization prohibition (most actionable).

dependencies.rationale: Private transfer fee covenants are discovered through title search, title commitment exceptions, and recorded covenant review. Obtaining and reviewing the title package (including policy/commitment) is a practical prerequisite to reliably determine whether the property is encumbered by a prohibited covenant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If title materials are not obtained early, prohibited private transfer fee covenants may be discovered late, forcing contract/closing delays or making the loan undeliverable.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Policy Cannot Include ALTA 1990 Creditors’ Rights Exclusion
business_rules.description: Title policies for Fannie Mae-delivered loans must not include the creditors’ rights exclusion language adopted by ALTA in 1990.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to title insurance policies obtained for mortgage loans delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: title policy must be revised/endorsed; loan delivery may be blocked until cured.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: For loans originated prior to January 1, 2008, endorsement forms that meet Fannie Mae’s requirements at the time of origination are acceptable. Title policies may not include the creditors’ rights exclusion language that ALTA adopted in 1990.

examples: A title commitment includes ALTA 1990 creditors’ rights exclusion language; lender must obtain removal/acceptable modification before delivery.
A title policy issued without any creditors’ rights exclusion language passes this requirement.
A policy uses alternative language that functions as a creditors’ rights exclusion; it should be treated as noncompliant and cured.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / title policy revision required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: Both rules govern title policy acceptability; the minimum coverage amount check and prohibited language check are typically executed in a single title-policy compliance sequence.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If minimum requirements are not confirmed first, the prohibited language remediation may be duplicated when policy is reissued for amount corrections.

business_rules.rule_name: New Leases After 2025-09-01 Cannot Have Prior Fee-Estate Liens
business_rules.description: For loans sold to Fannie Mae with new leases entered into on or after September 1, 2025, the fee estate must not be subject to any prior secured loans or other liens unless the secured party/lienholder has agreed to recognize and not disturb the lease if it becomes the owner of the fee estate, evidenced by an agreement recorded in the appropriate land records.
business_rules.conditions: Loan sold to Fannie Mae; new lease entered on/after 2025-09-01
business_rules.consequences: If violated, leasehold collateral is unacceptable and loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed if recorded non-disturbance/recognition agreement exists

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-03, General Requirements for Leasehold Mortgages (Note)
source_reference.source_text: Note : For loans sold to Fannie Mae with new leases entered into on or after Sept. 01, 2025: The fee estate must not be subject to any prior secured loans or other liens, unless the secured party or lienholder has agreed to recognize and not disturb the lease if it becomes the owner of the fee estate, as evidenced by an agreement recorded in the appropriate land records.

examples: Non-compliant: New ground lease dated 2025-10-15; fee owner has an existing mortgage with no recorded non-disturbance agreement.
Compliant: New lease dated 2025-09-20; prior lienholder signs and records a recognition/non-disturbance agreement in land records.
Not applicable: Lease executed 2025-08-15 (before 2025-09-01), so this specific prior-lien prohibition does not apply (other leasehold rules still apply).
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_003 is a leasehold-specific prohibition tied to new leases (entered on/after 2025-09-01) and delivery to Fannie Mae, requiring recognition/non-disturbance agreements if the fee estate has prior liens. BR_011_002 establishes the leasehold mortgage collateral context needed to determine applicability and to connect the prohibition to first-lien enforceability concerns.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If leasehold context is missed, a lender may deliver a leasehold loan where the fee estate has a prior lien without a recorded non-disturbance agreement, causing ineligibility for delivery.

dependencies.rationale: 011_003 is a leasehold collateral prohibition/condition for certain new leases. However, OFAC compliance (005_010) must be satisfied before any delivery-based leasehold collateral prohibitions are operationally actionable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 005_010 fails, delivery is prohibited irrespective of recorded non-disturbance agreements or fee-estate lien status.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_001 establishes the loan is in a leasehold collateral context potentially eligible for Fannie Mae. BR_011_003 then adds a date-based prohibition tied to prior liens on the fee estate absent a recorded non-disturbance/recognition agreement. Workflow-wise, BR_011_001 determines that leasehold rules apply; BR_011_003 then screens for the additional prohibition where applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_011_001 is not used to route the file into leasehold checks, BR_011_003 may be missed on post-9/1/2025 leases, resulting in delivery of an ineligible loan due to unacceptable fee-estate lien conditions.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle Limited Cash-Out Prohibits Any Cash Proceeds
business_rules.description: For HomeStyle Renovation limited cash-out refinance transactions, the borrower may not receive any cash proceeds. Any excess funds remaining after renovations are completed may be applied as a curtailment to the loan balance or reimbursed only for actual supplies/additional renovations supported by paid receipts. Sweat equity value may not be reimbursed.
business_rules.conditions: When a HomeStyle Renovation loan is originated as a limited cash-out refinance
business_rules.consequences: If borrower receives cash proceeds, transaction is noncompliant and must be corrected
business_rules.exceptions: Reimbursement allowed only for supplies/additional reno with paid receipts

source_reference.source_text: The borrower may not receive any cash proceeds from the transaction. This includes funds that are generally allowed for a standard limited cash-out reﬁnance transaction. Excess funds, if any, after renovations are completed, may be applied to the loan balance as a curtailment or may be reimbursed to the borrower for the cost of actual supplies or additional renovations for which paid receipts are provided. The value of sweat equity may not be reimbursed.

examples: After renovations, $1,200 remains in escrow; servicer applies $1,200 as a curtailment to principal (compliant).
After renovations, $800 remains; borrower provides paid receipts for additional permitted supplies and is reimbursed $800 (compliant).
Lender disburses $2,000 to borrower as cash back at closing; this violates the prohibition and must be remedied.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady Prohibits Subordinate Financing from Seller-Held Mortgages
business_rules.description: Subordinate financing from a seller-held mortgage is not permitted with HomeReady mortgages.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady loan where any subordinate lien is proposed or present.
business_rules.consequences: If subordinate lien is seller-held, transaction is not permitted for HomeReady.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: . Subordinate ﬁnancing from a seller-held mortgage is not permitted with HomeReady

examples: Purchase with a 3% seller-held second mortgage as down payment assistance → prohibited for HomeReady.
Purchase with Community Seconds second lien administered by eligible agency → permitted (not seller-held).
Limited cash-out refinance with existing seller-held second remaining in place → prohibited for HomeReady unless restructured/paid off.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered.

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_004 prohibits seller-held subordinate financing with HomeReady mortgages. BR_020_001 is the core rule defining HomeReady eligibility and required AMI source. In a navigable workflow, the HomeReady determination must occur before HomeReady-specific subordinate-financing prohibitions are applied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_020_001 is not applied first, a lender may incorrectly block a non-HomeReady loan under BR_020_004, or mistakenly deliver an AMI-ineligible loan as HomeReady.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle Cost Overruns Cannot Increase Loan Amount
business_rules.description: If renovation costs increase during the renovation period, the borrower or the lender must fund the increase; the lender may not increase the loan amount to offset the increase. The lender must obtain additional funds in a way that does not affect the priority of Fannie Mae’s lien.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle Renovation during renovation period when costs increase
business_rules.consequences: If violated: noncompliant lien/loan terms; subject to delivery rejection or remedy
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Should there be an increase in costs during the renovation period, the borrower, or the lender, must fund the amount of the increase. The lender may not increase the loan amount to oﬀset any increase in costs. The lender must ensure that the additional funds are obtained in a manner that will not aﬀect the priority of Fannie Mae’s lien.

examples: Renovation costs rise $7,500 due to discovered plumbing defects: borrower brings $7,500; loan amount remains unchanged.
Lender elects to cover a $3,000 overrun: lender funds separately without modifying note amount and without subordinating lien.
Attempting to increase the renovation loan principal to cover overruns violates the Guide and risks delivery rejection.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_008 applies during the renovation period when renovation costs increase and prohibits increasing the loan amount to offset the increase. To detect and govern an increase, the lender must have an established and compliant initial renovation cost amount, which is addressed by BR_017_004’s renovation cost constraints and definitions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If initial renovation costs are not established/validated, cost overruns may be mishandled (e.g., lender improperly increases loan amount), impairing lien/terms compliance and triggering remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: HUD Section 184 loans with interest rate buydowns are not permitted
business_rules.description: HUD-guaranteed Section 184 mortgages that are subject to an interest rate buydown plan are not permitted for purchase or securitization by Fannie Mae. If a Section 184 loan includes an interest rate buydown plan, it must not be delivered to Fannie Mae under this program eligibility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HUD-guaranteed Section 184 (including 184A) mortgage loans delivered to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If no buydown, loan may proceed (subject to other conditions); if buydown exists, loan is ineligible for Fannie Mae purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will purchase or securitize HUD-guaranteed Section 184 mortgages under the following conditions: The HUD Section 184 mortgage must comply with HUD requirements. The loan must be secured by one- to four-unit properties located on individual lots or in a condo, cooperative, or PUD project. ... The mortgage transaction may be a purchase or a reﬁnance transaction. HUD Section 184 mortgages that are subject to an interest rate buydown plan are not permitted.

examples: Section 184 purchase loan includes a temporary interest rate buydown agreement → not permitted; do not deliver to Fannie Mae as Section 184.
Section 184 refinance has no buydown and meets HUD requirements → passes this rule, continues through other eligibility checks.
Lender attempts to offset a buydown by qualifying at note rate → still prohibited for Section 184; loan remains ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Purchase cash back limited to refunds and tax credits
business_rules.description: For purchase money transactions, proceeds may not be used to give the borrower cash back except (1) reimbursement for overpayment of fees/charges (including federally required refunds) when the settlement statement clearly indicates the refund and the loan file documents the amount and reason; and (2) a legitimate prorated real estate tax credit where taxes are paid in arrears. If permissible cash back is provided, the lender must confirm minimum borrower contribution requirements are met. A prorated tax credit is not an interested party contribution and cannot be considered when determining sufficient borrower assets. Exception: if the Closing Disclosure shows an escrow account established that includes the seller’s portion of taxes, a prorated tax credit may offset escrow funds needed.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions (purchase money) with borrower receiving cash back
business_rules.consequences: If cash back exceeds allowed purposes, loan is ineligible for purchase/delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Escrow setup on Closing Disclosure may allow tax credit to offset escrow funds

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-01, General Purchase Transaction Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Proceeds from the transaction may not be used to give the borrower cash back other than the following: • an amount representing reimbursement for the borrower’s overpayment of fees and charges, including refunds that may be required in accordance with certain federal laws or regulations. The settlement statement must clearly indicate the refund, and the loan file must include documentation to support the amount and reason for the refund; and • a legitimate prorated real estate tax credit in locales where real estate taxes are paid in arrears. Note: If the borrower receives cash back for a permissible purpose as listed above, the lender must confirm the minimum borrower contribution requirements associated with the selected mortgage product, if any, have been met. A prorated real estate tax credit is not an interested party contribution, and it cannot be considered when determining if the borrower has sufficient assets for the transaction. Exception: When the Closing Disclosure indicates an escrow account has been established and includes the portion of real estate taxes owed by the property seller for the period they owned the property, a prorated real estate tax credit from the property seller may offset all or a portion of the funds needed for the escrow account.

examples: Borrower receives $450 back because appraisal fee was collected twice; settlement statement shows refund and file documents the reason—allowed.
Borrower receives $5,000 cash back as an inducement not tied to documented overpayment or tax proration—prohibited; loan ineligible.
Closing Disclosure shows escrow setup includes seller’s tax portion; seller tax credit is applied to escrow funds—permitted by exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition with explicit allowed cash-back categories and documentation requirements; no numeric caps stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_007 defines how to handle IPCs that exceed caps or closing costs (treat as sales concessions, deduct from price, and recalc LTV/CLTV). BR_030_004 prohibits cash back on purchase transactions except narrow exceptions and requires confirming minimum borrower contribution if permissible cash back occurs. Correct application of BR_009_007 is a primary control to prevent excess credits (often a source of cash back) and to ensure final settlement economics are compliant before applying BR_030_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_007 fails, the Closing Disclosure/settlement statement may show excess credits that turn into borrower cash back, triggering BR_030_004 violations and potential purchase ineligibility or post-closing cures/re-disclosures.

business_rules.rule_name: New construction non-arm’s length bars 2nd/investment
business_rules.description: For newly constructed properties, if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation (any ownership interest, or employment) with the builder, developer, or seller, Fannie Mae will only purchase loans secured by a principal residence. Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans on newly constructed homes secured by a second home or investment property in that scenario.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase of newly constructed property with borrower affiliation to builder/seller
business_rules.consequences: If occupancy is second home/investment, loan is not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For the purchase of newly constructed properties, if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation (any ownership interest, or employment) with the builder, developer, or seller of the property, Fannie Mae will only purchase mortgage loans secured by a principal residence. Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans on newly constructed homes secured by a second home or investment property if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation with the builder, developer, or seller of the property.

examples: Borrower is an employee of the builder and buys a newly built home as a principal residence—eligible (subject to other rules).
Borrower is a partial owner of the development entity and intends to occupy as a second home—not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
Borrower has a business affiliation with the seller and the loan is investment occupancy—Fannie Mae will not purchase.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit prohibition tied to occupancy type for a defined non-arm’s length new construction scenario.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: ARM becomes non-assumable after conversion to fixed
business_rules.description: If an ARM converts to a fixed-rate loan per the borrower’s exercise of a conversion option, the mortgage is no longer assumable. Lenders must disclose to borrowers that any ARM plan that includes an option to convert to a fixed-rate mortgage cannot be assumed once the conversion option is exercised.
business_rules.conditions: ARM includes conversion option and conversion option is exercised
business_rules.consequences: If met, assumption is prohibited; if violated, compliance defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Once the ARM plan converts to a ﬁxed-rate loan, the mortgage is no longer assumable. Disclosures Regarding Assumption of ARMs Although Fannie Mae ARMs are usually assumable, some plans do restrict assumability. When assumptions are restricted, the lender must advise the borrower of the exact nature of the restriction(s). Note : Lenders must disclose to borrowers that any ARM plan that includes an option to convert to a ﬁxed-rate mortgage cannot be assumed once the conversion option is exercised.

examples: Borrower converts a 7/1 ARM to fixed; a buyer later seeks to assume it—assumption must be denied as non-assumable post-conversion.
Loan docs disclose that conversion triggers non-assumability; borrower acknowledges at closing (compliant).
Servicer permits assumption after conversion; this violates the prohibition and may trigger remedies.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory arbitration loans after 10/31/2004 are ineligible
business_rules.description: A loan originated on or after October 31, 2004 that is subject to mandatory arbitration is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: When loan originated date is on/after 2004-10-31 and has mandatory arbitration
business_rules.consequences: If arbitration clause applies, loan is ineligible for delivery; must be excluded from sale
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: ArbitrationA loan that was originated on or after October 31, 2004, and is subject to mandatory arbitration is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: A loan closed on 2018-05-10 contains a mandatory arbitration rider; it is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
A loan closed on 2003-09-01 contains a mandatory arbitration clause; this specific ineligibility test does not apply based on date (other restrictions may still apply).
A 2026 loan file has an arbitration provision but it is optional (not mandatory); the mandatory-arbitration ineligibility is not triggered.
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-paid MI prohibited for convertible ARMs except limited MBS case
business_rules.description: Lender-purchased mortgage insurance (LPMI) is accepted for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to fixed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae may consider accepting LPMI for convertible ARMs that are in MBS pools only if the lender uses the 'market rate' post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses lender-purchased MI; evaluate whether ARM is convertible to fixed-rate
business_rules.consequences: If convertible ARM without stated MBS conditions: LPMI not acceptable for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Convertible ARMs in MBS may be considered if market-rate option used and lender assumes IR risk

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae accepts lender-purchased mortgage insurance coverage for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to ﬁxed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae will consider accepting lender-purchased mortgage insurance for convertible adjustable-rate mortgages that are in MBS pools if the lender uses the “market rate” post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.

examples: 7/1 ARM with a conversion option using LPMI: prohibited for delivery as LPMI is not accepted for convertible ARMs.
Convertible ARM delivered into an MBS pool: may be considered only if lender elects the market-rate post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
30-year fixed-rate loan using LPMI: acceptable (subject to LPMI lender requirements).
30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_003 addresses when LPMI is accepted (and when it is not, e.g., certain convertible ARMs). BR_039_004 specifies mandatory LPMI administration and delivery attributes (corporate obligation premiums, servicing comp adjustments, continuous coverage, SFC 019). To satisfy ‘accepted’ LPMI delivery, the lender must comply with these operational requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_004 fails, the loan can be rejected at delivery (missing SFC), become a servicing defect, or trigger contractual remedies due to lapsed/incorrect MI coverage—even if the loan type would otherwise allow LPMI under BR_039_003.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_003 states Fannie Mae "may consider accepting" LPMI for convertible ARMs in MBS pools only if the lender uses the "market rate" post-conversion disposition option. BR_009_009 defines eligibility criteria specifically for converted ARMs delivered under the "market rate" option (12 months seasoned at conversion; current at delivery). Therefore, to rely on the BR_039_003 exception pathway, the loan must meet BR_009_009’s market-rate-option eligibility.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_009 is not satisfied, the lender cannot credibly use the market-rate post-conversion exception referenced in BR_039_003; LPMI acceptance for the convertible ARM should be denied, potentially forcing loan exclusion from MBS delivery or MI restructuring.

business_rules.rule_name: Virtual currency income qualifies for $0 borrower income
business_rules.description: Any income paid to or earned by the borrower in the form of virtual currency (including cryptocurrencies) is not eligible to be used to qualify for the loan; it must be excluded from qualifying income calculations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower income source includes virtual currency (crypto) payments/earnings.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: income excluded and loan recalculated; Violated: loan not compliant/eligible.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.1-02, Income Paid in Virtual Currency
source_reference.source_text: Income Paid in Virtual Currency Any income paid to or earned by the borrower in the form of virtual currency, such as cryptocurrencies, is not eligible to be used to qualify for the loan. Reduced Income Documentation Requirements for High LTV Reﬁnance Loans For certain high LTV reﬁnance loans, the lender is not required to follow the income documentation requirements described in this Chapter.

examples: Borrower receives salary in cryptocurrency; lender must exclude this income and resubmit DU using only eligible income sources.
Borrower reports freelance income paid in virtual currency; lender cannot use it for qualifying even if tax returns show the income.
Borrower has virtual currency holdings as assets (not income); this rule only prohibits using virtual currency as qualifying income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand (if used to qualify and not eligible)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit ineligibility statement; numeric in title expressed as $0 eligible for qualifying.

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Unacceptable Reserves Include IPCs and Lender Contributions at $0
business_rules.description: Certain funds cannot be counted as borrower reserves, including interested party contributions (IPCs) and any amount of a lender contribution. These funds must be excluded from the verified reserves calculation used to satisfy minimum reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When determining and documenting eligible borrower reserve sources
business_rules.consequences: Meets: reserves calculated using eligible assets only; Violates: reserves overstated; underwriting defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this list

source_reference.source_text: The following cannot be counted as part of the borrower’s reserves: funds that have not been vested; funds that cannot be withdrawn under circumstances other than the account owner’s retirement, employment termination, or death; stock held in an unlisted corporation; non-vested stock options and non-vested restricted stock; personal unsecured loans; rent-back credit; interested party contributions (IPCs) (see B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs) ); any amount of a lender contribution (see B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions ); and cash proceeds from a cash-out reﬁnance transaction on the subject property.

examples: Borrower needs $10,000 reserves; lender shows $8,000 in savings plus $2,500 lender credit—credit must be excluded, so borrower only has $8,000 eligible reserves.
Borrower’s cash-out refinance nets $20,000 cash back; those proceeds cannot be counted as reserves for the same subject property cash-out transaction.
Seller pays $5,000 IPC toward closing; IPC cannot be credited as reserves even if funds remain after closing.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: State or local tax refunds are not qualifying income
business_rules.description: Taxable state and local tax refunds, credits, or offsets of state and local income taxes must not be used as qualifying income because the income was accounted for in the previous year’s tax returns. The borrower’s cash flow must be adjusted accordingly (i.e., remove this amount from qualifying cash flow).
business_rules.conditions: Borrower’s IRS Form 1040 includes state/local tax refund income items
business_rules.consequences: If included in qualifying income, cash flow must be adjusted to exclude it
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.6-02, State and Local Tax Refunds
source_reference.source_text: Taxable state and local tax refunds, credits, or offsets of state and local income taxes should not be used as qualifying income since the income was accounted for in the previous year’s tax returns. Therefore, the borrower’s cash flow must be adjusted accordingly.

examples: Borrower reports a $3,000 state tax refund on the 2024 return; lender must not include it as qualifying income.
Borrower has a state tax credit offset shown on the return; lender must adjust cash flow to remove that amount.
Borrower is W-2 and self-employed; regardless of income type, taxable state tax refunds are excluded from qualifying income.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition and required cash-flow adjustment; no numeric thresholds present in source.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_001 requires analyzing Form 1040 income sources for stability and proper inclusion. BR_030_003 prohibits counting state/local tax refunds as qualifying income and requires cash flow adjustment. When tax refunds appear in the 1040 analysis, BR_030_003 becomes a mandatory adjustment step within the broader BR_030_001 workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_030_003 is missed, qualifying income and DTI may be inflated, producing a materially defective underwriting decision and potential enforcement actions/repurchase exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: CPM status Unavailable makes condo and co-op units ineligible for purchase
business_rules.description: Loans secured by units in projects with a status of "Unavailable" in Condo Project Manager (CPM) or on the DU Underwriting Findings report are ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when project status is Unavailable in CPM or shown Unavailable in DU findings.
business_rules.consequences: Loan is ineligible for purchase/securitization by Fannie Mae if condition exists.
business_rules.exceptions: Lender may request an exception for projects with special considerations.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-03 (08/06/2025), List of Ineligible Project Characteristics (Note)
source_reference.source_text: Note : Loans secured by units in projects with a status of "Unavailable" in Condo Project Manager (CPM) or on the DU Underwriting Findings report are ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If a lender determines that a project does not meet all of Fannie Mae's project eligibility requirements but believes that the project has merit and warrants additional consideration, the lender may request an exception

examples: DU findings show project status Unavailable; lender must treat the loan as ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
CPM displays Unavailable for the co-op project; loan cannot be purchased unless an exception is granted under special considerations.
Project is not in CPM but DU findings show Unavailable; lender must not deliver as eligible to Fannie Mae.
Co-op share loan for an owner-occupied unit
Co-op project requiring legal compliance review
Co-op unit with maintenance fee and underlying mortgage considerations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_005_008 requires the lender to (i) notify CPM Management of new adverse project information within five business days and (ii) confirm the project retains approved status as of the note date before selling the loan. BR_COOP_UNIT_PROHIBITION_019_006 prohibits delivery/purchase if the project status is “Unavailable” in CPM or DU findings. The confirmation/notification process is an upstream gate to avoid an “Unavailable” delivery outcome.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender fails to monitor/confirm and the status is (or becomes) Unavailable, the loan becomes ineligible for purchase, creating delivery rejection, repurchase exposure, and pipeline fallout.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_001 allows leasehold estates only for eligible property types (including condo and co-op units). When the leasehold property is in a condo/co-op project, project eligibility controls like CPM/DU project status (BR_019_006) become applicable to determine deliverability. Thus, BR_011_001 conditionally triggers the need to evaluate BR_019_006 for leasehold condo/co-op loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender treats leasehold condo/co-op as eligible without checking project status: loan can be originated but later found ineligible for delivery due to 'Unavailable' status, causing fallout and investor rejection.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Validation Service Optional and Cannot Be Mandatory
business_rules.description: The DU validation service is optional for conventional loans underwritten through DU. Lenders are not required to participate for DU underwriting, and lenders may not require a borrower to participate in DU validation service.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting conventional loans through DU and considering DU validation service
business_rules.consequences: If violated, borrower participation is improperly conditioned; compliance issue
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The DU validation service is an optional service available only for conventional loans underwritten through DU. Lenders are not required to participate in the DU validation service in order for a loan to be underwritten through DU and may not require a borrower to participate in the DU validation service.

examples: Borrower declines electronic verification; lender proceeds with standard documentation and submits loan to DU without DU validation.
Lender tells borrower the loan will be denied unless borrower participates in DU validation service; this violates the prohibition.
Lender opts in to validate income and employment, but also offers a non-validation path with standard documentation.
DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: Principal Residence or Second Home Rental Income Prohibited
business_rules.description: Rental income from the borrower’s principal residence (a one-unit principal residence or the unit occupied in a two- to four-unit property) or a second home cannot be used to qualify the borrower. Fannie Mae allows exceptions only for boarder income and rental income on principal residence properties with accessory units, as referenced.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower seeks to use rent from own principal residence unit or second home
business_rules.consequences: If prohibited use attempted, rental income must be excluded from qualifying income
business_rules.exceptions: Boarder income and ADU/accessory unit rental income exceptions apply

source_reference.source_text: Ineligible Properties Generally, rental income from the borrower’s principal residence (a one-unit principal residence or the unit the borrower occupies in a two- to four-unit property) or a second home cannot be used to qualify the borrower. However, Fannie Mae does allow certain exceptions to this policy for boarder income and rental income on principal residence properties with accessory units. See B3-3.4-04, Boarder Income , for boarder income requirements.

examples: Borrower rents a room in their 1-unit primary residence: rental income cannot be used as ‘rental income’ (may be eligible under boarder income rules).
Borrower owns a second home and wants to count short-term rent to qualify: prohibited; exclude from income.
Borrower has 1-unit primary with ADU: ADU rental income may be considered subject to ADU conditions (including 30% cap).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_002 states rental income from the borrower’s principal residence unit cannot be used to qualify, with exceptions including rental income on principal residence properties with accessory units. BR_007_001 defines the accessory dwelling unit (ADU) exception conditions and imposes the 30% cap on total qualifying income. Therefore, BR_007_001 operates as an override/exception framework to the prohibition when the ADU scenario is present.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the ADU conditions in BR_007_001 are not met, BR_007_002 prohibition applies and the rental income must be excluded, potentially changing qualification/DTI. If lender incorrectly applies the exception, income may be overstated and loan becomes ineligible.

business_rules.rule_name: Authorized User Tradelines Require 12 Months Proof to Use
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, tradelines listing the borrower as an authorized user cannot be considered in the underwriting decision unless another borrower in the transaction is the owner of the tradeline or the borrower provides written documentation showing they were the actual and sole payer of the monthly payment for at least 12 months preceding the application date. If such documentation is provided, late payments must be considered and the monthly payment obligation must be included in the DTI ratio. An authorized user tradeline must be considered if the owner is the borrower’s spouse and the spouse is not a borrower. These requirements do not apply to DU casefiles.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwriting where credit report includes authorized user tradelines
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, tradeline may be used and DTI updated; if not, tradeline excluded
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable to DU; spouse-owned tradeline must be considered even if spouse not borrower

source_reference.section_id: B3-5.3-06, Consideration of Authorized User Accounts
source_reference.source_text:
For manually underwritten loans, credit report tradelines that list a borrower as an authorized user cannot be considered in the underwriting decision, except as outlined below.
An authorized user tradeline may be considered if:
another borrower in the mortgage transaction is the owner of the tradeline; or
the borrower can provide written documentation (e.g., canceled checks, payment receipts, etc.) that they have been the actual and sole payer of the monthly payment on the account for at least 12 months preceding the date of the application.
If written documentation of the borrower’s monthly payments on the authorized user tradeline is provided, then the payment history — particularly any late payments that are indicated — must be considered in the credit analysis and the monthly payment obligation must be included in the debt-to-income ratio.
An authorized user tradeline must be considered if the owner of the tradeline is the borrower's spouse and the spouse is not a borrower in the mortgage transaction.
These requirements do not apply to loan casefiles underwritten through DU.

examples: Manual underwrite: borrower is authorized user on parent’s card; no 12-month proof and parent not a borrower—tradeline cannot be used.
Manual underwrite: borrower provides 12 months of canceled checks showing sole payment—tradeline may be considered, and the monthly payment must be included in DTI.
Manual underwrite: borrower is authorized user on spouse’s card; spouse is not on the loan—tradeline must be considered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible/defect if credit decision improperly considered tradeline

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Automated merged credit report cannot use lender affiliate CRA
business_rules.description: An automated merged credit report is not acceptable if it is provided by a credit reporting agency that is affiliated with the lender in any way.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses an automated merged credit report for underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If violated, lender must replace report with non-affiliated provider report or underwriting file is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The report must meet the following requirements: The report must include all information from three diﬀerent credit repositories, or two repositories, if: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one credit repository. If information from only one credit repository is available, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three diﬀerent credit repositories.
