The report cannot be provided by a credit reporting agency that is aﬃliated with the lender in any way.

examples: Lender-owned subsidiary produces the merged credit report; lender must obtain a report from a non-affiliated credit reporting agency.
AMC-style vendor is independent and not affiliated; merged report is acceptable if other requirements are met.
Lender uses a third-party CRA that has a shared parent company with the lender; report is prohibited under this rule.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Lease Rent Must Be in U.S. Dollars ($) Only
business_rules.description: When a fully executed current lease agreement is used to document rental income, the rental payment stated on the lease must be denominated in U.S. dollars; rental payment in virtual currency is not permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Lease agreement is used to support rental income for qualifying
business_rules.consequences: If USD shown, lease is acceptable; if virtual currency, lease is unacceptable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When the subject property will generate rental income and it is used for qualifying purposes, one of the following Fannie Mae forms must be used to support the income-earning potential of the property: For one-unit properties: Single-Family Comparable Rent Schedule (Form 1007 ) (provided in conjunction with the applicable appraisal report), or For two- to four-unit properties: Small Residential Income Property Appraisal Report  (Form 1025 ). Note : The rental payment on the lease must be reﬂected in U.S. dollars (cannot be in virtual currency).

examples: Purchase: Lease shows rent of $2,400/month; lender may use it (with Form 1007/1025 as applicable).
Refinance: Lease states 0.05 BTC per month; lender must not use this lease for qualifying rental income.
Conversion to investment: Borrower presents USD lease plus Form 1007; acceptable if other requirements met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Credit card financing prohibited for any down payment
business_rules.description: Borrowers may not use credit card financing for the down payment under any circumstances. Credit card financing may only be used for certain early fees (when permitted elsewhere) but never for down payment funds-to-close.
business_rules.conditions: When any portion of down payment is sourced from credit card charges
business_rules.consequences: If used, down payment source is unacceptable; loan must be restructured or denied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae permits certain costs that must be paid early in the application process, such as lock-in fees, origination fees, commitment fees, credit report fees, and appraisal fees, to be charged to the borrower’s credit card because these fees do not represent extraordinary amounts and the credit card debt is considered in the borrower’s total monthly debt-to-income ratio. Borrowers are not required to pay oﬀ these credit card changes before closing. Under no circumstances may credit card ﬁnancing be used for the down payment.

examples: Purchase loan: borrower charges $8,000 down payment to a credit card cash-advance; lender must disallow and require acceptable verified funds.
Borrower charges $600 appraisal fee to a credit card (allowed early fee) but attempts to treat it as down payment; lender must reclassify and ensure down payment is not credit-funded.
Borrower proposes using a new credit card promotional balance transfer to cover down payment gap; lender must treat as prohibited down payment source.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Tax return required Oct 15 to Apr 14; Form 4868 not permitted
business_rules.description: For loans with an application date and disbursement date falling within the October 15 (current year minus 1) through April 14 (current year) window, the most recent year’s tax return is required and the use of a tax extension (IRS Form 4868) is not permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to income types requiring federal tax returns; date window as stated
business_rules.consequences: If return obtained, documentation satisfied; if not, loan file is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for this date window

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Federal Income Tax Returns
source_reference.source_text: October 15, [current year minus 1] to April 14, current year... The most recent year’s tax return is required. The use of a Tax Extension (IRS Form 4868) is not permitted.

examples: Application 2025-12-01 and disbursement 2026-03-15: lender must obtain the most recent year’s tax return; borrower cannot rely on Form 4868.
Application 2025-10-20 and disbursement 2026-04-10: most recent return is required; file is incomplete if only prior-year returns are provided.
Borrower provides Form 4868 instead of the most recent return in this window: lender must obtain the required return to proceed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential post-purchase documentation defect

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: CLT appraisal must not use CLT leasehold sales as comps
business_rules.description: When determining fee simple value for a CLT subject property, the appraiser must use comparable sales of similar fee simple properties. If fee simple comps are not available, the appraiser may use other (non-CLT) leasehold estate sales with appropriate lease-term adjustments to reflect fee simple interest. If the community has sales of CLT leasehold estates, the appraiser must discuss them but must not use them as comparables because CLT restrictions likely limit sales prices and are not comparable to the hypothetical condition required for valuation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to CLT appraisals when selecting comps for fee simple value estimate
business_rules.consequences: Using CLT-restricted sales as comps creates a noncompliant appraisal and defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; non-CLT leasehold sales allowed with adjustments if needed

source_reference.source_text: In determining the fee simple value of the subject property, the appraiser must use comparable sales of similar properties that are owned as fee simple estates. If this is not possible, the appraiser may use sales of properties that are subject to other types of leasehold estates as long as they make appropriate adjustments, based on the terms of their leases, to reﬂect a fee simple interest. When the community or neighborhood has sales activity for other leasehold estates held by a community land trust, the appraiser must discuss them in the appraisal report, but must not use them as comparable sales

examples: Neighborhood has many CLT resales at restricted prices: appraiser lists them in narrative but selects fee simple sales as comps for fee simple value.
No fee simple sales exist but several non-CLT leasehold sales exist: appraiser uses non-CLT leasehold sales and adjusts for lease terms to reflect fee simple interest.
Appraiser uses CLT restricted resale as Comp 1 and 2: prohibited and triggers appraisal revision/QC defect.
Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Chattel Lien Plus Land Real Property Lien Is Unacceptable
business_rules.description: Loans secured by a chattel lien on the manufactured home plus a separate real property lien on the land are unacceptable for Fannie Mae purchase. Acceptable structures described in the Guide require real property lien treatment for the home, not chattel financing combined with land lien.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction uses chattel lien on the home and a real property lien on the land
business_rules.consequences: Loan is unacceptable/ineligible for purchase; lender must restructure liens to meet requirements
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Note : Loans in which there is a chattel lien on the home plus a real property lien on the land are unacceptable.

examples: Borrower finances the manufactured home with a vehicle-style chattel lien and separately records a mortgage on the land → unacceptable for Fannie Mae purchase.
Lender converts/manages titling so the manufactured home is treated as real property and secures both home and land under real property lien → avoids chattel-plus-land structure.
Seller proposes chattel financing to speed closing while land is mortgaged traditionally → lender must not deliver this loan to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility for purchase / delivery rejection

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_010 prohibits chattel lien on the home plus separate real property lien on the land. BR_036_005 requires lien searches in real and personal property records, including "cases with separate liens." This is direct validation evidence used to confirm whether the prohibited structure exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_005 is not performed, the lender may miss a prohibited lien structure and deliver an unacceptable loan, creating immediate ineligibility and repurchase exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Gift of equity must contribute $0 toward financial reserves
business_rules.description: A gift of equity may be used to fund all or part of the down payment and closing costs (including prepaid items), but it cannot be used towards financial reserves. This limits the gift of equity to funds-to-close purposes only, not post-closing reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a purchase transaction includes a seller-provided gift of equity credit
business_rules.consequences: If applied to reserves, reserves are unsupported and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: A gift of equity is permitted for principal residence and second home purchase transactions; can be used to fund all or part of the down payment and closing costs (including prepaid items); and cannot be used towards ﬁnancial reserves.

examples: Purchase of a second home requires 6 months reserves; borrower cannot satisfy reserves using a $15,000 gift of equity credit.
Principal residence purchase: gift of equity covers down payment and prepaid taxes; borrower must still document separate assets for required reserves.
If underwriter credits gift of equity as reserves in DU, the loan must be corrected and reserves re-sourced before delivery.
Seller-parent provides equity gift via below-market sales price
Gift of equity used to satisfy down payment requirement
Equity gift documented in purchase contract and appraisal

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_005 restricts how a gift of equity may be used (not for reserves). BR_022_006 requires retaining a signed gift letter and settlement statement listing the gift. Those documents are the primary evidence to identify the gift-of-equity credit and ensure it was applied only to down payment/closing costs, not reserves.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_006 fails, the lender may be unable to prove compliance with BR_022_005 during post-purchase review (even if the transaction was structured correctly), resulting in file defects and remedy exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Desktop Appraisal Cannot Be Made Subject To Condition Verification
business_rules.description: Desktop appraisals may not use extraordinary assumptions or be made 'subject to' verification of the subject property's condition, quality, or physical characteristics. If such assumptions are present, the appraisal is not permitted/acceptable for desktop appraisal use and must be corrected or replaced.
business_rules.conditions: Desktop appraisal relies on alternative data/photos or third-party information
business_rules.consequences: If extraordinary assumptions used, appraisal is unacceptable; lender must obtain a compliant appraisal report
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Information provided by the homeowner, potential borrower, or a third party can be used to develop the description of the interior and exterior of the improvements. Extraordinary assumption(s) or appraisals made "subject to" veriﬁcation of the subject property's condition, quality, or physical characteristics are not permitted.

examples: Form 1004 Desktop states the value is 'subject to interior inspection to confirm condition' → prohibited; lender must cure or replace appraisal.
Appraiser uses borrower-provided photos but verifies key facts with a disinterested source and makes no 'subject to' condition statement → permitted.
Desktop report includes extraordinary assumption that 'all systems are functional' without verification → prohibited for desktop appraisal.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Require corrected/replacement appraisal; delivery defect if not resolved

examples: Desktop appraisal used for a low-risk refinance where permitted
Desktop appraisal completed with required data sources and exhibits
Desktop appraisal rejected because transaction/property type is ineligible

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_006 governs acceptability constraints unique to desktop appraisals (no extraordinary assumptions, not 'subject to' verification of condition/quality/characteristics). It functions as a validation gate on the use permitted by BR_033_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_006 fails, the desktop appraisal is unacceptable even if BR_033_005 eligibility criteria were met; lender must obtain a corrected/replacement appraisal, causing delays and potential pricing/lock impacts.

business_rules.rule_name: Premium pricing lender credit cannot fund any down payment
business_rules.description: For premium pricing (borrower selects a higher interest rate in exchange for a lender credit), the lender credit cannot be used to fund any portion of the borrower’s down payment, and should not exceed the amount needed to offset the borrower’s closing costs. Any excess lender credit required to be returned to the borrower is considered an overpayment of fees and charges and may be applied as a principal curtailment or returned in cash to the borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses premium pricing to generate a lender credit
business_rules.consequences: If used only for eligible costs, compliant; if used for down payment, noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Excess credit may be principal curtailment or cash back per regulation

source_reference.source_text: Premium Pricing Premium pricing refers to situations when a borrower selects a higher interest rate on a loan in exchange for a lender credit. The lender credit cannot be used to fund any portion of the borrower’s down payment, and should not exceed the amount needed to oﬀset the borrower’s closing costs. Any excess lender credit required to be returned to the borrower in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements is considered an overpayment of fees and charges, and may be applied as a principal curtailment or returned in cash to the borrower.

examples: Borrower receives a $4,000 lender credit from premium pricing and applies it to title, appraisal, and prepaid taxes (compliant).
Borrower applies a premium pricing lender credit to meet minimum down payment funds-to-close (prohibited).
Closing costs total $3,000 but lender credit is $3,800; the $800 excess is treated as overpayment and applied as principal curtailment or returned in cash as allowed.
Lender credit applied to borrower closing costs
Lender contribution structured as pricing credit (rate/points tradeoff)
Contribution limited by transaction caps and documented on closing disclosure

business_rules.rule_name: Disputed tradeline accurate and borrower-responsible makes 0 DU eligibility
business_rules.description: When DU cannot issue an Approve recommendation using disputed tradelines but can issue an Approve recommendation when the disputed tradelines are excluded, the lender must investigate the disputed tradelines to determine borrower responsibility and whether the account information is accurate/complete. If the borrower is responsible and the account/tradeline information is accurate and complete, the loan is not eligible for delivery as a DU loan (though the lender may manually underwrite if the transaction is eligible for manual underwriting). If DU issues an Approve recommendation using the disputed tradelines, no further documentation or action is necessary.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when credit report includes borrower-disputed tradelines and DU reassesses risk.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: lender investigates and routes DU/manual correctly; Violated: DU delivery ineligible risk.
business_rules.exceptions: If DU Approve uses disputed tradelines, no further action is necessary.

source_reference.source_text: When the credit report contains tradelines disputed by the borrower, DU will ﬁrst assess the risk of the loan caseﬁle using all tradelines, including those disputed. If DU issues an Approve recommendation using the disputed tradelines, no further documentation or action is necessary. If DU does not issue an Approve recommendation when including the disputed tradelines, DU will re-assess the risk without using the disputed tradelines. If DU is then able to issue an Approve recommendation, the lender must investigate the tradelines to determine whether the borrower is responsible for the accounts or if the account information is accurate or complete. If the borrower is responsible for the disputed account and the account and tradeline information is accurate and complete, the loan is not eligible for delivery as a DU loan.

examples: DU Approve/Eligible with disputed tradelines included—lender takes no additional dispute action.

DU only approves when disputed tradeline excluded; lender confirms borrower is responsible and tradeline is accurate—loan cannot be delivered as DU; lender may proceed as manual underwrite if permitted.
DU only approves when disputed tradeline excluded; lender obtains documentation showing borrower not responsible—loan may be delivered as DU after documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand (if delivered as DU when not eligible)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit DU logic and ineligibility outcome; numeric precision limited to categorical outcomes.

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_007 applies "when DU cannot issue an Approve recommendation using disputed tradelines but can... when excluded." That state can only occur after a DU submission/run, governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU isn’t run (or isn’t run timely), the lender cannot identify the disputed-tradeline issue and may proceed with incorrect underwriting path, risking DU delivery ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: HOEPA Section 32 loans are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.description: Any mortgage loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. This is an absolute delivery prohibition regardless of other underwriting or eligibility compliance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is determined to be HOEPA/Section 32 under Regulation Z.
business_rules.consequences: Loan is not eligible for delivery; delivery must be blocked or removed from pipeline.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), HOEPA and State Higher-Priced Loans
source_reference.source_text: A loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02, HOEPA and State Higher-Priced Loans
source_reference.source_text: A loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Closed-end refinance triggers HOEPA high-cost coverage under Section 32 → loan must not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Purchase loan later determined to be HOEPA-covered due to points/fees → lender must prevent delivery or remove from delivery batch.
If a delivered loan is later found to be HOEPA-covered, it may be treated as a material eligibility defect subject to remedy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same absolute prohibition: any loan that is a HOEPA/Regulation Z Section 32 covered transaction is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. Context and consequences are identical (do not deliver / ineligible). There are no conflicting thresholds; differences are wording only.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 selected as primary because it is more explicit about the control outcome: it frames HOEPA/Section 32 as an absolute delivery block regardless of other compliance, which is the clearest operational consequence.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 prohibits delivery of any loan that is determined to be HOEPA/Section 32 under Regulation Z. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_003 requires calculating total points and fees and total loan amount in accordance with 12 CFR §1026.32 (Section 32), which is a core input to identifying whether a loan is Section 32/HOEPA.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If §1026.32-aligned points/fees calculations are not performed or are incorrect, the lender may (a) fail to identify a HOEPA loan and inappropriately deliver it (hard delivery defect), or (b) falsely flag a non-HOEPA loan as prohibited, causing avoidable fallout and delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 governs how APR is calculated and compared to APOR for QM/ATR eligibility (including special ARM APR calculation). While BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 references HOEPA/Section 32 status, APR accuracy is typically part of high-cost status determinations and pricing compliance checks. Therefore, APR/APOR computations are a meaningful upstream dependency for robust Section 32 screening workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If APR is calculated incorrectly (especially for short-reset ARMs), the lender may misclassify loans during high-cost screening, leading to prohibited delivery risk or unnecessary delivery blocks pending re-calculation.

business_rules.rule_name: Personal gifts prohibited for investment property transactions
business_rules.description: A borrower of a mortgage loan secured by a principal residence or second home may use funds received as a personal gift from an acceptable donor for down payment, closing costs, or reserves (subject to minimum borrower contribution rules). Gifts are not allowed on an investment property.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses personal gift funds; occupancy is evaluated for eligibility
business_rules.consequences: Meets: gifts allowed for principal/second home; Violated: investment loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (investment property gifts not allowed)

source_reference.source_text: Gift Funds A borrower of a mortgage loan secured by a principal residence or second home may use funds received as a personal gift from an acceptable donor. Gift funds may fund all or part of the down payment, closing costs, or ﬁnancial reserves subject to the minimum borrower contribution requirements below. Gifts are not allowed on an investment property.

examples: Primary residence purchase with $20,000 gift from parent → permitted if donor is acceptable and gift is documented.
Second home purchase using gift funds for closing costs → permitted subject to minimum borrower contribution table.
Investment property purchase using $10,000 gift from sibling → prohibited; restructure funds or loan is ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct prohibition sentence for investment property; permitted use for principal/second home stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: Loan uses a $15,000 gift from a parent for down payment.
Loan uses documented gift funds transferred prior to closing.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op cannot block sublet after lender fails to sell in 60 days
business_rules.description: If a lender assumes the proprietary lease/occupancy agreement after the tenant-stockholder’s default and attempts to sell its interest but cannot effect a satisfactory sale within 60 days (through its own efforts or with co-op assistance), the co-op corporation may not prohibit the lender from subletting the unit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after borrower default when lender acquires/assumes co-op lease rights.
business_rules.consequences: If documents prohibit lender sublet after 60 days, project documents must be corrected or loan may be ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Co-op may approve sublessee if standards/procedures are not unreasonably restrictive.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-03, Assignment of Co-op’s Lease/Occupancy Rights
source_reference.source_text: However, if the lender is unable to eﬀect a satisfactory sale within 60 days—either through its own eﬀorts or with assistance from the co-op corporation—the co-op corporation may not prohibit the lender from subletting the unit.

examples: After default, lender lists the unit for 45 days with no acceptable offers: co-op may still restrict subletting until the 60-day sale attempt period ends.
After default, lender cannot sell within 60 days despite co-op help: co-op documents cannot bar lender from subletting the unit.
Co-op allows subletting only with board approval but processes approvals quickly and lawfully: permitted if not unreasonably restrictive.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition with explicit 60-day timeframe trigger.

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op share loans allow 0 investment properties
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae will purchase co-op share loans only when the borrower occupies the property as a principal residence or second home. Co-op share loans secured by investment properties are prohibited and therefore not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is a co-op share loan secured by a co-op unit
business_rules.consequences: If principal/second home, eligible (subject to other limits); if investment, ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-04, Co-op Share Loan Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will purchase co-op share loans provided borrowers occupy the property as a principal residence or second home. Investment properties are prohibited.

examples: Borrower purchases co-op unit as a primary residence; passes occupancy eligibility for co-op share loans.
Borrower refinances co-op unit used as a second home; passes occupancy eligibility (other co-op requirements still apply).
Borrower intends to rent out the co-op unit as an investment; prohibited—loan is not eligible for delivery.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit prohibition stated; no numeric thresholds besides the ‘0 investment’ implication.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for purchase or securitization

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory third-party dues make project ineligible
business_rules.description: Loans secured by units in condo and co-op projects with mandatory memberships that require HOA or co-op members to pay dues to a third-party organization are ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Projects subject to recreational leases are also not eligible.
business_rules.conditions: When project documents impose mandatory membership dues to a third party or a recreational lease
business_rules.consequences: Project ineligible; loans secured by units in the project are ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Certain exceptions allowed for shared amenities agreements (not detailed here)

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-03, Recreational Leases and Mandatory Memberships
source_reference.source_text: Loans securing units in condo and co-op projects with mandatory memberships that require the HOA or co-op members to pay dues to a third-party organization (such as a golf course or other recreational facility) are ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae. The project must be the sole owner of its amenities, though certain exceptions will be allowed when there is a shared amenities agreement between HOAs or co-op projects. Projects subject to recreational leases are also not eligible.

examples: Condo requires every unit owner to pay $250/month to a privately owned golf club; project is ineligible and lender must not deliver loans secured by units in the project.
Co-op has a long-term recreational lease with a third party for pool access and ongoing payments; project is ineligible for Fannie Mae delivery.
Two HOAs share amenities under a shared amenities agreement (no third-party dues); lender may consider eligibility under the exception framework and must document the agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

business_rules.rule_name: Loan cannot be delivered when DU resubmission returns ineligible
business_rules.description: If a resubmission to DU results in an “ineligible” recommendation, the mortgage loan may not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan casefile is resubmitted to DU after changes and prior to delivery.
business_rules.consequences: Loan delivery to Fannie Mae is prohibited until DU returns an eligible recommendation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text:
If the resubmission to DU results in an “ineligible” recommendation, the mortgage loan may not be delivered to
Fannie Mae.

examples: Loan resubmitted due to updated liabilities; DU returns Refer with Caution/Ineligible: do not deliver to Fannie Mae.
Loan resubmitted due to corrected occupancy; DU returns Approve/Eligible: delivery may proceed if all other requirements met.
Loan resubmitted after closing with changed terms; DU returns ineligible: lender must resolve or keep loan out of Fannie delivery channel.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct prohibition tied to DU ineligible recommendation.

examples: DU generates Approve/Eligible decision with standard documentation conditions.
DU generates Approve/Ineligible decision requiring changes to achieve eligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Comparable Concession Adjustments Cannot Be Positive and Must Be Market Based
business_rules.description: Adjustments for sales or financing concessions on comparable sales must reflect market reaction. The negative dollar adjustment for each comparable should equal the increase in the comparable’s price attributable to the concessions (as determined by the appraiser). Positive adjustments for sales or financing concessions are not acceptable. Dollar-for-dollar adjustments are acceptable only if the appraiser’s analysis concludes the market reaction equals the full amount of the concession.
business_rules.conditions: Comparable sale includes sales/financing concessions requiring adjustment
business_rules.consequences: If positive or non-market-based, appraisal must be corrected or replaced
business_rules.exceptions: Dollar-for-dollar is acceptable only if market reaction is full amount

source_reference.source_text: adjustments for sales or ﬁnancing concessions and the amount of the adjustments to the comparable sales is not based on how typical the concessions might be for a segment of the market area. Large

examples: Comparable sold for $410,000 with a $10,000 seller credit; analysis shows market inflated price by $6,000, so concession adjustment is -$6,000 (not -$10,000 by default).
Appraiser applies a +$5,000 adjustment because the comparable had no concessions; this is prohibited as a positive concession adjustment.
Market analysis supports full pass-through of a $7,500 concession; a -$7,500 dollar-for-dollar adjustment is acceptable.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong prohibitive language; numeric thresholds are not fixed but method is explicit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_005 requires correct identification and treatment of sales/financing concessions on comparable sales. In purchase files, BR_031_002 requires providing the complete ratified sales contract and addenda to the appraiser, which typically contains concession details that the appraiser must analyze. Without the contract, the appraiser may miss or mischaracterize concessions, increasing probability of noncompliant positive or non-market-based adjustments.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_002 fails, the appraisal may omit or mis-handle concessions, triggering appraisal correction/replacement, delaying underwriting and UCDP submission, and increasing delivery defect risk if an incorrect value is delivered.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_003 requires the lender to disclose specific financial information (credits, buydowns, subordinate financing terms, etc.) that can affect value. BR_009_005 governs how concessions must be adjusted (negative, market-reaction based; no positive adjustments). The appraiser cannot reliably apply BR_009_005 if the lender fails to disclose key concession/financing items.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure leads to flawed comparable adjustments, appraisal revisions, potential underwriting rework, and delivery risk due to unsupported value conclusions.

business_rules.rule_name: Loan Delivery Purchase Prohibited Until Fatal Edits Cleared
business_rules.description: Loans may only be submitted and purchased in Loan Delivery if they are clear of all fatal edits. Loans remain subject to fatal edits throughout submission, documentation certification, and acquisition. If a fatal edit is encountered, it must be resolved for Fannie Mae to acquire the loan.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer submits loan data through Loan Delivery for purchase or securitization
business_rules.consequences: If clear, loan can proceed to acquisition; if fatal edits exist, Fannie Mae will not acquire the loan
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C1-2-02, Electronic Submission via Loan Delivery
source_reference.source_text: Loans may only be submitted and purchased in Loan Delivery if they are clear of all fatal edits. Loans are subject to all fatal edits in Loan Delivery throughout the submission, loan documentation certiﬁcation and acquisition purchase process. If a loan encounters a fatal edit, the fatal edit must be resolved for Fannie Mae to acquire the loan.

examples: Seller submits ULDD data and receives a fatal edit for missing required field; loan cannot be purchased until corrected.
A loan passes initial edits but later triggers a fatal edit during documentation certification; seller must resolve before acquisition.
Seller resolves fatal edits and resubmits; loan becomes eligible for purchase in Loan Delivery.
Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_009 requires that loans be clear of all fatal edits to be acquired through Loan Delivery. BR_031_005 requires UCDP Successful prior to sale. In operational delivery workflows, missing/failed UCDP submissions are a frequent source of delivery blocking conditions, meaning BR_031_005 is typically required to satisfy BR_015_009 for appraisal-required loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_005 fails, lender may hit fatal edits or delivery blocks; acquisition cannot proceed until cured.

business_rules.rule_name: Limited cash-out refinance is ineligible if prior cash-out note date is within 30 days
business_rules.description: A transaction is not eligible as a limited cash-out refinance if the borrower completed a cash-out refinance transaction with a note date 30 days or less prior to the application date of a new refinance secured by the same property. This is treated as circumvention of cash-out refinance policies and pricing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to new refinance applications secured by the same property as a recent cash-out refinance.
business_rules.consequences: If prior cash-out note date is ≤ 30 days before new application date, the new refinance cannot be treated as limited cash-out (and is ineligible as such).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: eligible as a limited cash-out reﬁnance if the borrower completed a cash-out reﬁnance transaction with a note date 30 days or less prior to the application date of a new reﬁnance secured by the same property. Published March 4, 2026

examples: Cash-out refinance note date is June 1; new refinance application date is June 25 on same property: 24 days, so limited cash-out is ineligible.
Cash-out refinance note date is June 1; new refinance application date is July 2 on same property: 31 days, so this specific prohibition does not apply.
Cash-out refinance note date is May 15; new refinance application date is June 14 (30 days) on same property: limited cash-out is ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligibility / repurchase risk if mischaracterized

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM delivery prohibited when adjustment litigation exists
business_rules.description: For ARM loans, the loan being delivered must not be subject to any current litigation with respect to how the interest rate and/or payment adjustments were calculated or implemented. Additionally, the lender must not be servicing other ARMs with adjustment provisions similar to the mortgage being sold to Fannie Mae that are the subject of current litigation related to the manner in which adjustments were made.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer delivers an ARM loan to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If no relevant litigation, delivery can proceed; if litigation exists, delivery barred
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Requirements Regarding Interest Rate and Monthly Payment Adjustments The following requirements apply to interest rate and monthly payment adjustments for ARM loans: The loan being delivered must not be subject to any current litigation with respect to the manner in which the interest rate and/or payment adjustments were calculated or implemented. The lender must not be servicing other ARMs that include interest rate and payment adjustment provisions similar to those of the mortgage being sold to Fannie Mae that are the subject of current litigation related to the manner in which adjustments were made.

examples: ARM has an active lawsuit alleging improper index application for past adjustments; loan may not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Lender is being sued on another ARM product with similar adjustment provisions; lender must not deliver a similar ARM to Fannie Mae while litigation is current.
No litigation exists regarding adjustment mechanics; ARM can be delivered if all other eligibility criteria are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_010 is an ARM acceptability constraint for purchase by Fannie Mae. BR_035_003 prohibits delivery of ARM loans subject to relevant litigation (or similar ARM provisions in litigation in the servicer’s portfolio). For ARM delivery workflows, both must be checked; eligibility depends on satisfying both dimensions (terms and litigation status).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Loan may be structurally eligible but still undeliverable due to litigation exposure; delivery must stop or loan must be held/redirected.

business_rules.rule_name: Frozen credit at 2+ bureaus makes loan ineligible
business_rules.description: If a borrower’s credit is frozen at one repository (traditional credit), the credit report is acceptable only if credit data is available from two repositories and the lender requested a three in-file merged report. Loans for borrowers with credit data frozen at two or more repositories are not eligible whether underwritten manually or in DU.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower has a credit freeze at one or more repositories
business_rules.consequences: If violated: loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae (manual or DU)
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one of the credit repositories for borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as credit data is available from two repositories, and the lender requested a three in-ﬁle merged report. Loans for borrowers with credit data frozen at two or more of the credit repositories will not be eligible whether underwritten manually or in DU.

examples: Borrower freezes Experian; Equifax and TransUnion provide data; tri-merge requested: acceptable under this rule.
Borrower freezes Equifax and TransUnion: loan is ineligible whether manual underwrite or DU.
Two borrowers: one has freezes at two bureaus while the other has full credit; loan is still ineligible due to the frozen-credit borrower.
Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

business_rules.rule_name: C-PACE Financing Prohibits Balloon Payment and Mortgage Security
business_rules.description: For C-PACE financing to be acceptable to Freddie Mac, the C-PACE financing terms must (1) be amortized over the term of the C-PACE financing and must not require a balloon payment at maturity, and (2) must not be secured by a mortgage on the property. These are minimum acceptability conditions for Freddie Mac approval/consent.
business_rules.conditions: When C-PACE financing is proposed or already obtained for a Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: may proceed; if balloon or mortgage-secured: consent denied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The C -PACE financing must provide that repayment will be amortized over the term of the C -PACE financing and not require a balloon payment at maturity. The C -PACE financing must not be secured by a mortgage on the Property.

examples: C-PACE assessment is fully amortizing over 20 years with no balloon and is secured only by the assessment lien mechanism: meets this condition.
C-PACE contract requires a balloon payment at year 10: prohibited; Freddie Mac should not approve/consent.
C-PACE lender records a separate mortgage/deed of trust as security: prohibited; Freddie Mac should deny consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Consent denied / purchase ineligibility for C-PACE feature

examples: Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Ground lease base rent cannot reset by property reappraisal
business_rules.description: For ground lease Mortgages, base rent must be a fixed ascertainable sum. The ground lease must not allow increases/escalations/re-sets in base rent except a sum-certain increase at a specified date or time interval. Base rent increases tied to reappraisal of the Property are not acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: Ground lease Mortgage under Guide Chapter 30 requirements
business_rules.consequences: If base rent can reset via reappraisal (or is not fixed/sum-certain), Mortgage is not acceptable
business_rules.exceptions: Sum-certain increase at specified date/time interval is permitted

source_reference.source_text: The base rent payable under the ground lease must be a fixed ascertainable sum and the ground lease must not contain any provisions that permit increases, escalations or re-sets in the base rent, other than a sum certain increase at a specified date or time interval. Increases, escalations or re-sets in the base rent based on a reappraisal of the Property are not acceptable.
source_reference.confidence_note: Word positions are estimated from provided chunk excerpt.

examples: Lease provides base rent increases of $25,000 every 5 years: permitted (sum certain at specified interval).
Lease allows base rent to reset to 6% of appraised land value every 10 years: prohibited; not acceptable.
Lease states base rent increases based on CPI without a sum-certain amount: not permitted by this rule (not a sum-certain increase).
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Combination financing taxable term cannot exceed TEL term
business_rules.description: When using combination financing (TEL plus cash Mortgage or taxable governmental loan) for one Property, the Seller must underwrite the TEL and the other financing as a single financing and must not underwrite the cash Mortgage/taxable loan as secondary debt. Additionally, the taxable portion must have a term no longer than the TEL.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a TEL is combined with a cash Mortgage or taxable governmental loan
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied, structure meets TEL underwriting requirements; if violated, delivery may be rejected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Seller must underwrite the TEL and the cash Mortgage or taxable governmental loan as a single financing, secured (in the case of a taxable governmental loan) by a single Security Instrument or (in the case of a cash Mortgage) by separate Security Instruments that are cross - defaulted. The Seller must neither consider nor underwrite the cash Mortgage or taxable governmental loan as secondary debt. The following conditions also apply:  • The taxable portion must have a term no longer than the TEL.

examples: TEL term 30 years and taxable portion term 30 years: allowed (taxable term not longer than TEL) if structured as single financing.
TEL term 20 years and taxable portion term 25 years: prohibited because taxable term exceeds TEL term.
Seller underwrites cash Mortgage as 'secondary debt' behind the TEL: prohibited even if terms otherwise align.
Fixed-rate multifamily mortgage delivered to Freddie Mac
Floating-rate mortgage held in a warehouse line prior to delivery
Mortgage pooled into a 55-Day Multi PC securitization

business_rules.rule_name: Letters of Credit Cannot Be Issued by Seller Affiliates Without Approval
business_rules.description: Unless the Seller/Servicer has Freddie Mac’s prior written approval, prohibited issuers of letters of credit include (i) the Seller/Servicer or Affiliated Persons of the Seller/Servicer in connection with a Mortgage originated or serviced by that Seller/Servicer, and (ii) Affiliates of the Borrower. This governs acceptable counterparties for letters of credit used in transactions governed by the Guide.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a letter of credit is used for a Mortgage-related obligation
business_rules.consequences: If violated, letter of credit is not permitted; Freddie Mac may reject delivery/require replacement
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted only with Freddie Mac prior written approval

source_reference.source_text: Unless the Seller/Servicer has obtained Freddie Mac’s prior written approval, the following issuers of letters of credit are not permitted: • The Seller/Servicer or Affiliated Persons of the Seller/Servicer in connection with a Mortgage originated or serviced by that Seller/Servicer, or • Affiliates of the Borrower

examples: Seller proposes its own bank affiliate as LOC issuer for a deposit on a loan it originated; prohibited unless prior written approval obtained.
Borrower’s parent company offers to issue the LOC; prohibited because issuer is an Affiliate of the Borrower unless Freddie Mac approves.
A third-party domestic Eligible Institution issues the LOC; permitted (subject to other LOC requirements).
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: When good faith deposit is waived Seller cannot secure Breakage Fee for waived portion
business_rules.description: When Freddie Mac waives all or a portion of the early rate lock good faith deposit, the waiver is intended to benefit the Borrower. The Seller is prohibited from collecting any type of security from the Borrower to secure payment of any portion of the Breakage Fee to the extent the good faith deposit is waived. Seller may still collect funds from Borrower for purposes unrelated to the waived portion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when early rate lock application specifies a partial or full GFD waiver.
business_rules.consequences: If Seller collects prohibited security, Freddie Mac may require refund and impose remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Seller may collect funds unrelated to the waived portion of the good faith deposit.

source_reference.source_text: Under certain circumstances, Freddie Mac may in its sole discretion waive all or a portion of the good faith deposit. The amount of the waiver will be specified in the early rate lock application. Freddie Mac’s waiver of all or a portion of the good faith deposit is intended to benefit the Borrower. The Seller is prohibited from collecting any type of security from the Borrower to secure payment of any portion of the Breakage Fee to the extent that the good faith deposit is waived. Seller may still collect funds from the Borrower for purposes unrelated to the waived portion of the good faith deposit.

examples: Freddie Mac waives $25,000 of GFD; Seller cannot require a pledge or letter of credit to cover Breakage Fee tied to that waived $25,000.
Freddie Mac waives 50% of GFD; Seller may still collect third-party report fees not connected to the waived portion.
Seller takes a deposit labeled “breakage security” equal to waived GFD amount—prohibited and must be reversed.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition; numeric precision moderate because it depends on waiver amount rather than fixed thresholds.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Seller/Servicer Assignment Prohibited Without Written Consent
business_rules.description: A Seller/Servicer must not assign, transfer, or grant a security interest in any obligations, rights, or interests under any Purchase Contract or the unitary master Servicing contract (including rights/obligations under the Guide or Purchase and Servicing Documents) without Freddie Mac's prior written consent. Any attempted assignment/transfer/security interest without such consent is prohibited and is null and void.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any assignment/transfer/security interest in Purchase/Servicing rights
business_rules.consequences: If consent obtained, assignment may proceed; if not, action is null/void
business_rules.exceptions: Only with Freddie Mac prior written consent

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 1 – Introduction, e. Assignments; security interests (05/01/14)
source_reference.source_text: A Seller/Servicer may not, in whole or in part, assign or transfer, or grant a security interest in, any of its obligations, rights or interest under any Purchase Contract or under the unitary, indivisible master Servicing contract, including any of its rights or obligations under the Guide or any of the Purchase and Servicing Documents, without Freddie Mac's prior written consent. Any purported or attempted assignment or transfer of, or grant of a security interest in, any such obligations, rights, or interest is prohibited and will be null and void.

examples: Seller/Servicer attempts to pledge its servicing rights as collateral for a loan without Freddie Mac written consent; the pledge is null and void.
Seller/Servicer seeks Freddie Mac written consent before transferring certain servicing obligations to an affiliate; transfer may proceed only after consent.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit prohibition and null/void consequence; no numeric threshold in text.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Guide Copying Revocable in 60 Days and No Electronic Copies
business_rules.description: Permission to photocopy the Guide is limited to Freddie Mac Seller/Servicers strictly for their own use in originating, selling, and servicing Mortgages for Freddie Mac. Freddie Mac may revoke permission to reproduce the Guide upon 60 days' notice. Seller/Servicers are prohibited from reproducing the Guide by any electronic or mechanical means, including reproduction in or as a component of any information storage and retrieval system.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller/Servicer reproduces or distributes Guide content
business_rules.consequences: If violated, reproduction is prohibited and permission may be revoked in 60 days
business_rules.exceptions: Express written permission from Freddie Mac for other reproduction

source_reference.source_text: Limited permission to photocopy the Guide is granted to Freddie Mac Seller/Servicers strictly for their own use in originating Mortgages and in selling Mortgages to, and servicing Mortgages for, Freddie Mac. No part of the Guide may be reproduced for any other reason (in any form or by any means) without the express written permission of Freddie Mac. Freddie Mac reserves the right to revoke permission to reproduce the Guide upon 60 days' notice to any and all Seller/Servicers. Under no circumstances may a Seller/Servicer reproduce the Guide by any electronic or mechanical means, including reproduction in, or as a component of, any information storage and retrieval system.

examples: Seller/Servicer uploads the Guide PDF into its internal knowledge base search system; this is prohibited electronic reproduction.
Freddie Mac issues a notice revoking photocopy permission; Seller/Servicer must cease permitted reproduction within 60 days.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 60-day notice and categorical prohibition on electronic/mechanical reproduction.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated inspection cannot be re-delegated, exception only
business_rules.description: If Freddie Mac delegates any inspection requirement to the Seller, the Seller may not further delegate that inspection requirement. Such delegation to the Seller must be approved by Freddie Mac and is permitted only on an exception basis.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac has delegated an inspection to Seller
business_rules.consequences: Met: Seller performs inspection; Violated: noncompliance, rework/approval risk
business_rules.exceptions: Delegation to Seller permitted only if Freddie Mac approves as an exception

source_reference.source_text: 9. If Freddie Mac delegates any inspection requirement to the Seller, the Seller may not further delegate the inspection requirement . Delegation to the Seller must be approved by Freddie Mac and is permitted on an exception basis only.

examples: Freddie delegates an inspection to Seller; Seller must use its own staff/approved process and cannot subcontract delegation further.
Seller attempts to delegate the delegated inspection to a third party without Freddie approval; this violates the Guide requirement.
Freddie approves delegation to Seller as an exception; Seller still cannot further delegate beyond the Seller.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase denial or required corrective action

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Title company selection cannot be based on anything of value
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must select or accept any Title Company based solely on prudent institutional lender considerations in the relevant jurisdiction, as permitted by law, and in Freddie Mac’s best interests. The Seller/Servicer must not base the selection on receipt of anything of value or other consideration paid by or on behalf of a Title Company to the Seller/Servicer or its personnel.
business_rules.conditions: When Seller/Servicer selects or accepts a Title Company for a Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If violated, selection is noncompliant and may trigger refusal, remediation, or sanctions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 29.1(c)(1) Selection of the Title Company (04/22/25)
source_reference.source_text: 1. The Seller/Servicer's selection or acceptance of any Title Company must be based solely on considerations typically used by prudent institutional lenders originating or purchasing Mortgages in the jurisdiction where the Property is located, as permitted by applicable law, and acting in the best interests of Freddie Mac. The Seller/Servicer must not base this selection on receipt of anything of value or other consideration by the Seller/Servicer or its employees, officers, or directors paid by or on behalf of a Title Company.

examples: A Title Company offers free ancillary services to win business; Seller/Servicer must reject the inducement and base selection on competence, coverage, and jurisdictional fit.
A Seller/Servicer employee receives a referral fee from a Title Company; the Title Company cannot be selected on that basis and the arrangement must be terminated and escalated.
A Borrower proposes a Title Company; Seller/Servicer still must evaluate selection using prudent lender criteria and cannot accept due to gifts or other consideration.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Restricted Vendor List title company cannot be used until cleared
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac may refuse to accept Mortgages for purchase or approve assumptions involving a specific Title Company on the Multifamily Restricted Vendor List. If a Title Company appears on the Multifamily Restricted Vendor List, the Seller/Servicer may not use that Title Company until Freddie Mac notifies otherwise.
business_rules.conditions: When a Title Company is on Freddie Mac’s Multifamily Restricted Vendor List
business_rules.consequences: Use is prohibited; Mortgage purchase/assumption may be refused by Freddie Mac
business_rules.exceptions: Only permitted after Freddie Mac notifies otherwise

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 29.1(c)(2) Selection of the Title Company (04/22/25)
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac reserves the right to: (i) refuse to accept Mortgages for purchase, or (ii) approve the assumption of a Mortgage, in each case involving any specific Title Company on the Multifamily Restricted Vendor List. If a Title Company appears on the Multifamily Restricted Vendor List, the Seller/Servicer may not use that Title Company until notified otherwise by Freddie Mac.

examples: A Borrower insists on using a Title Company that appears on the Restricted Vendor List; Seller/Servicer must require a different Title Company before proceeding.
A Mortgage is delivered with a title policy issued by a listed Title Company; Freddie Mac may refuse purchase and require corrective action.
A Title Company is removed from the list and Freddie Mac notifies Seller/Servicer; only then may it be used again.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower may not retain or direct seismic consultant
business_rules.description: The Borrower must not retain or direct the seismic risk consultant. The Borrower may be responsible for paying the costs of all SRA services, but the engagement and direction of the consultant cannot be controlled by the Borrower.
business_rules.conditions: When a seismic risk consultant/SRA is required for the Property
business_rules.consequences: If met: SRA independence preserved; if violated: SRA not acceptable
business_rules.exceptions: Borrower may pay costs, but cannot retain/direct

source_reference.source_text: The Borrower must not retain or direct the seismic risk consultant, but the Borrower may be responsible for paying the costs of all SRA services.

examples: Borrower proposes to hire its preferred SRA firm and instruct scope: prohibited; Seller must control retention/direction.
Borrower pays the SRA invoice but Seller executes the engagement letter and manages the consultant: compliant.
Property manager tries to direct the consultant to avoid entering certain buildings: prohibited influence; SRA may be rejected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require new independent SRA
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition; no numeric thresholds in sentence.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Commitment changes require prior written approval 03/03/14
business_rules.description: After executing the Commitment, the Seller may not transfer, assign, or otherwise modify the Commitment unless Freddie Mac provides prior written approval.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after Seller executes the Commitment for the transaction
business_rules.consequences: If approval obtained, change may proceed; if not, change is prohibited
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; only with Freddie Mac prior written approval

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 28, Freddie Mac’s fees (03/03/14) – preceding paragraph
source_reference.source_text: After the Seller executes the Commitment, the Seller may not transfer, assign or otherwise modify the Commitment without Freddie Mac's prior written approval.

examples: Seller executes Commitment then seeks to assign it to an affiliate; prohibited unless Freddie Mac gives prior written approval.
Seller attempts to change a material term in the Commitment post-execution; must obtain Freddie Mac’s prior written approval before doing so.
Seller makes no changes after executing the Commitment; rule is satisfied without additional action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac withholds approval; transaction terms may be rejected

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Terminated Seller Servicer Cannot Reapply for 12 Months
business_rules.description: A Seller/Servicer terminated by Freddie Mac may not apply for the approval that was terminated for at least 12 months after the termination date. If the termination was with cause, the reinstatement application must document appropriate corrective actions. Any reinstatement applicant must meet all approval criteria in effect at the time of application.
business_rules.conditions: When Seller/Servicer approval was terminated and reinstatement is sought
business_rules.consequences: Apply after 12 months with documentation: eligible to be considered; earlier: rejected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 4.9 Reapplication for selling or Servicing approval (07/31/12) For at least 12 months after the date of the termination, a Seller/Servicer (or branch production office, if applicable) that has been terminated by Freddie Mac may not apply for the approval that was terminated. If the termination was with cause, the application for reinstatement must document that the Seller/Servicer has taken all appropriate corrective actions.

examples: Termination date 2026-01-10; Seller/Servicer applies 2026-10-01: prohibited (less than 12 months).
Termination date 2026-01-10; apply 2027-01-11: satisfies 12-month waiting period; may be reviewed if criteria met.
Termination with cause; application omits corrective action documentation: incomplete and subject to denial.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination remains in effect; reapplication rejected

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Material Vendor cannot manage loans with risk rating above 6 without Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.description: A Servicer may not use a Material Vendor to make decisions related to a Mortgage’s performance or credit risk unless approved by Freddie Mac. Specifically, without prior Freddie Mac approval, a Material Vendor may not monitor or manage a Mortgage with a Risk Rating greater than six, a delinquent or maturing Mortgage, or any Mortgage assigned to Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Resolution (and may not perform other enumerated credit-risk decision functions).
business_rules.conditions: When Servicer uses a Material Vendor for servicing functions touching credit/performance risk
business_rules.consequences: If violated, delegation is prohibited and may trigger audit findings or approval restrictions
business_rules.exceptions: Freddie Mac approval allows delegation; then may delegate to a Servicing Agent

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 36(b) Servicing functions prohibited without prior approval (10/20/22)
source_reference.source_text: In general, the Servicer may not use a Material Vendor to make decisions related to the Mortgage's performance or credit risk, unless approved by Freddie Mac. Specifically, Servicers may not use a Material Vendor to: 1. Perform any required Mortgage performance risk assessment or make related recommendations 2. Monitor or manage a Mortgage with a Risk Rating greater than six, a Delinquent or maturing Mortgage, or any Mortgage assigned to Freddie Mac Multifamily Asset Resolution

examples: A Servicer assigns an offshore vendor to manage workouts for a Mortgage with Risk Rating 7; this is prohibited without Freddie Mac approval.
A vendor is asked to monitor and recommend actions for a 30+ day delinquent Mortgage; prohibited absent Freddie Mac approval.
A Servicer obtains Freddie Mac approval to delegate a restricted function; only then may the function be delegated in the approved manner.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Servicer record destruction barred before 3 or 6 years
business_rules.description: The Servicer must not surrender or destroy canceled checks, bank statements, or other Mortgage records/accounts without Freddie Mac’s prior written approval. If permitted by applicable law, the Servicer may destroy original records (including mortgage ledger cards) three years after the Mortgage is fully paid, or if accelerated, six years after it is fully paid.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Mortgage serviced for Freddie Mac, incl. paid off/foreclosed
business_rules.consequences: If violated, records may be deemed improperly retained; audit issues arise
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed by law: destroy originals 3 yrs after payoff; 6 yrs if accelerated

source_reference.source_text: 50.4 Release or destruction of records (12/07/05) Without Freddie Mac's prior written approval, the Servicer must not surrender or destroy any canceled checks, bank statements or other records and accounts for any Mortgage serviced for Freddie Mac, including any Mortgage paid in full, sold, foreclosed or otherwise liquidated. However, when permitted by applicable law, the Servicer may destroy any original records, including mortgage ledger cards : • Three years after the date a Mortgage is fully paid, or • If the Mortgage is accelerated, six years after the date it is fully paid

examples: Mortgage paid in full on 2023-05-01: originals may be destroyed on/after 2026-05-01 if permitted by law.
Mortgage accelerated then paid in full on 2021-09-15: originals may be destroyed on/after 2027-09-15 if permitted by law.
Servicer wants to shred canceled checks 12 months after payoff: prohibited unless Freddie Mac gives prior written approval.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Material vendor cannot access borrower PPI 10/20/22
business_rules.description: For accounting and reporting responsibilities, the Servicer may not use a Material Vendor to perform prohibited functions, including reviewing Borrower/Guarantor financial information or protected personal information, managing the Borrower relationship (including communications), communicating with Freddie Mac on behalf of the Servicer, or performing any function likely to give the Material Vendor access to any Borrower/Guarantor financial or personal information. Delegation of these prohibited functions is only permitted to a Servicing Agent upon Freddie Mac approval.
business_rules.conditions: Servicer is outsourcing accounting/reporting tasks to a Material Vendor or considering delegation to a Servicing Agent.
business_rules.consequences: If complied, outsourcing remains within permitted boundaries; if violated, Freddie Mac may cite noncompliance and require termination/remediation and impose audit findings.
business_rules.exceptions: May delegate to a Servicing Agent only with Freddie Mac approval.

source_reference.source_text: b. Accounting , reporting and communication responsibilities for which use of a Material Vendor  is prohibited (1 0/20/22) Freddie Mac requires that members of a Servicer's staff perform the most high -level accounting and reporting responsibilities.  As a result, the Servicer may not use a Material Vendor  to:  1. Review Borrower or Guarantor financial information or any protected personal information  2. Manage the Borrower relationship, including all communications with the Borrower, unless permitted otherwise by Section 50.2( a)  3. Communicate with Freddie Mac on behalf of the Servicer  4. Perform any services or functions that require or are likely to result in the Material Vendor receiving, using, handling, or otherwise having access to any financial or personal information pertaining to any Borrower, Guarantor, or other individual

examples: Servicer uses a vendor for cash management but prohibits vendor access to borrower statements containing PII; staff retains borrower financial review duties.
Vendor is asked to call borrowers about delinquent payments; this is prohibited borrower relationship management and must be performed by servicer staff or approved servicing agent.
Servicer wants vendor to upload remittance exceptions to Freddie Mac; prohibited because vendor cannot communicate with Freddie Mac on servicer’s behalf.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Seller/Servicer audit finding; required vendor scope change

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: 026_010 prohibits using a Material Vendor for credit/performance risk decisions without Freddie Mac approval. 040_008 prohibits Material Vendors from performing accounting/reporting functions that expose borrower financial/PII or allow them to manage borrower relationships/communicate with Freddie. These rules are complementary: outsourcing governance must satisfy both sets of prohibitions depending on function type, and compliance navigation benefits from treating them as a linked control family.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 026_010 is ignored while applying 040_008, the Servicer might still improperly delegate credit-risk decisions to a vendor. Conversely, focusing only on 026_010 could leave the Servicer exposed to PII/borrower-relationship delegation violations under 040_008.

business_rules.rule_name: No purchase if borrower matches OFAC or FHFA lists
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac will not purchase a Mortgage, allow a Transfer of Ownership under Chapter 41SBL, or approve other servicing-related transactions if it determines the Borrower, Borrower Principal, or Guarantor is a confirmed match to specified sanctions/exclusion lists (OFAC SDN, OFAC Consolidated Sanctions, FHFA Suspended Counterparty List subject to suspension order conditions, or Freddie Mac Exclusionary List).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any borrower/principal/guarantor in SBL purchase, TO, or servicing approvals.
business_rules.consequences: Confirmed match: Freddie will not purchase/approve; no match: may proceed if other criteria met.
business_rules.exceptions: FHFA list applies subject to conditions/exclusions in final suspension order.

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac will not purchase any Mortgage or allow any Transfer of Ownership under Chapter 41SBL, or approve any other Servicing -related transaction if it determines that any of the following is true with regard to a Borrower, Borrower Principal, or Guarantor... 1. Is a confirmed match to a name on any of the following lists: • OFAC Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons List • OFAC Consolidated Sanctions List • FHFA Suspended Counterparty List ... • Freddie Mac Exclusionary List

examples: Borrower principal name is confirmed match to OFAC SDN: Freddie will not purchase the Mortgage.
Guarantor is on Freddie Mac Exclusionary List: transfer of ownership request is denied.
Name resembles an OFAC entry but is not a confirmed match after screening: this rule is not triggered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan purchase denial; refusal to approve servicing-related transaction

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_009 requires the Seller/Servicer to use the most current Exclusionary List and states that if a listed party played certain roles, the mortgage is not eligible. BR_007_003 states Freddie Mac will not purchase/approve if Borrower/Borrower Principal/Guarantor matches sanctions/exclusion lists including the Freddie Mac Exclusionary List. Practically, BR_018_009 is the screening mechanism that provides the factual input (match/no match) necessary to apply BR_007_003’s prohibition decision for borrower/principal/guarantor.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_018_009 fails (outdated list or no screening): loans may be submitted with prohibited parties, leading to purchase denial, TO/servicing approval denial, pipeline fallout, and potential regulatory/sanctions exposure. Conversely, using stale data can cause false clears or false hits that disrupt processing.

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower Prohibited From Home Sharing Rentals Under 1 Month
business_rules.description: The Borrower must not participate in home sharing activities, defined as short-term rentals (typically less than one month) marketed through a peer-to-peer online marketplace or home sharing platform. The Borrower must also not enter into leases (including master leases) of residential, corporate, or commercial units that the Borrower knows or should have known are intended to be used by tenants for full or part-time home sharing activities. Examples of platforms include Airbnb, VRBO, and booking.com.
