Any remittance submitted after 1:00 p.m. Eastern Time will be processed the following Business Day. Remittance can be submitted in advance for up to 30 days, with the exception of non -business days.

examples: Remittance due Thursday: Servicer must submit by 1:00 p.m. ET Wednesday; submitting at 12:45 p.m. ET Wednesday is compliant.
Remittance due Monday: Servicer submits at 2:30 p.m. ET Friday; it will be processed the next Business Day and may be late.
Servicer schedules remittance 25 days in advance for a future due date; allowed (so long as it is not a non-business day).
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_008 restricts when a Servicer may begin using new remittance account/routing instructions and requires continuing the existing account until written authorization. Therefore, if the Servicer attempts to meet remittance timing requirements (BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_007) using a new account, they must first satisfy the account-change prerequisites in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_008.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_008 is violated, remittances may be rejected/misdirected and interest reimbursement fees may apply, even if the Servicer submitted on time under BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_007.

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1064 Must Be Received 15 Business Days Before Account Change
business_rules.description: To change the designated remittance account or modify bank routing instructions, the Servicer must ensure Freddie Mac receives Form 1064 at least 15 Business Days before the Servicer uses the new account or modified routing instructions. The Servicer must continue using the existing account/routing until Freddie Mac provides written authorization to use the new/modified instructions. If the Servicer discontinues the existing account/routing before receiving written authorization, the Servicer will be subject to an interest reimbursement fee.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Servicer requests a remittance account or routing change
business_rules.consequences: If violated, interest reimbursement fee applies and change may be rejected/delayed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 53, Changes in remittance instructions (06/15/23) and timing language
source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac requires its receipt of Form 1064 at least 15 Business Days before the Servicer's use of the new account or modified bank routing instructions for the Freddie Mac cash remittance system. The Servicer must continue to use the existing account or bank routing instructions until Freddie Mac gives the Servicer written authorization that the new account or modified bank routing instructions may be used. If the Servicer discontinues use of the existing account or bank routing instructions before obtaining written authorization from Freddie Mac, the Servicer will be subject to an interest reimbursement fee as set forth in Section 53.11.

examples: Servicer plans to switch custodial accounts on July 1; Form 1064 must be received by Freddie Mac at least 15 Business Days before July 1.
Servicer stops using the old routing on June 20 without written authorization; Servicer becomes subject to an interest reimbursement fee.
Servicer submits Form 1064 20 Business Days ahead and continues using the old routing until written authorization arrives; compliant.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

business_rules.rule_name: Correct rejected myOptigo accounting transmission within 24 hours
business_rules.description: If Freddie Mac Multifamily Loan Accounting notifies a Servicer that a myOptigo transmission is unacceptable, the Servicer must provide a corrected transmission via myOptigo for Investor Reporting within 24 hours. Failure to transmit timely and accurate corrected automated accounting reports may subject the Servicer to accounting reporting noncompliance fees.
business_rules.conditions: When Freddie Mac notifies Servicer a myOptigo accounting transmission is unacceptable
business_rules.consequences: Meets: corrected submission accepted; Violated: subject to accounting reporting noncompliance fees
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 50, c. Back-up reporting requirements (04/15/21)
source_reference.source_text: If Freddie Mac Multifamily Loan Accounting notifies a Servicer that a myOptigo transmission is unacceptable, the Servicer must provide Freddie Mac with a corrected transmission via myOptigo for Investor Reporting  within 24 hours. Failure to transmit timely and accurate corrected automated accounting reports may subject the Servicer to the accounting reporting noncompliance fees set forth in Section 54.9.

examples: Transmission rejected at 11:00 am ET Tuesday; corrected transmission submitted by 10:30 am ET Wednesday; compliant.
Transmission rejected Friday 4:00 pm; corrected submitted Monday 5:00 pm; exceeds 24 hours and may trigger fees.
Servicer resubmits within 24 hours but data is still unusable; still at risk of noncompliance fees.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: The correction rule is activated only after Freddie Mac deems a myOptigo transmission unacceptable. That situation presupposes the Servicer is transmitting via myOptigo as required. In compliance workflow sequencing, the system should (a) enforce mandatory transmissions, (b) handle rejection events, (c) apply the 24-hour correction SLA.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009 is not implemented (or transmissions are not occurring), BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_008 cannot operate as a remediation control; reporting defects may persist uncorrected, increasing fee exposure and investor reporting integrity risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Gold PC accounting reports due 3 business days after cutoff; payoff due in 2 days
business_rules.description: For Gold PC Securitized Mortgages reported via myOptigo for Investor Reporting: (1) For each Mortgage without exception activity, accounting reports are due three Business Days after the accounting cutoff date. (2) For each Mortgage paid in full, reports are due by the earlier of two Business Days after the payoff or the last Business Day of the month in which the payoff occurred. (3) For CME sale, reports are due the same Business Day the securitization occurred. (4) For liquidation via third-party foreclosure sale/short payoff/transfer to REO, reports are due by the earlier of three Business Days after receipt of proceeds/notification or the last Business Day of the month of receipt.
business_rules.conditions: When Servicer reports Gold PC securitized mortgages via myOptigo
business_rules.consequences: Meets: timely accounting reporting; Violated: reporting noncompliance exposure and fees
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For each Mortgage without exception activity, accounting reports are due: Three Business Days after the accounting cutoff date For each Mortgage paid in full, accounting reports are due: By the earlier of: • Two Business Days after the payoff; or • The last Business Day of the month in which the payoff For each Mortgage sold into a securitization (CME sale), accounting reports are due: • The same Business Day that the securitization (CME sale) occurred

examples: Cutoff date is Aug 15; report due Aug 20 (3 Business Days later) for a loan with no exceptions.
Payoff occurs Sept 10; report due Sept 12 (2 Business Days) unless month-end earlier (it is not).
CME sale occurs Oct 3; report due Oct 3 the same Business Day.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_009 only applies in the context of 'Gold PC Securitized Mortgages reported via myOptigo'. That reporting channel and requirement is mandated by BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009. In workflow automation, once a loan is classified as Gold PC and subject to myOptigo, BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009 triggers the need to compute deadlines per BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009 is not enforced as mandatory, the Servicer may not apply/monitor the Gold PC deadlines, increasing missed due dates and noncompliance fee exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: 55-Day Multi PC reports due 1 business day after determination; liquidation due in 5 days
business_rules.description: For 55-Day Multi PC Securitized Mortgages reported via myOptigo: (1) For each Mortgage without exception activity, accounting reports are due one Business Day after the 55-Day Multi PC determination date. (2) For each Mortgage paid in full, reports are due by the earlier of two Business Days after payoff or the last Business Day of the month in which payoff occurred. (3) For CME sale, reports are due the same Business Day as the securitization. (4) For liquidation via third-party foreclosure sale/short payoff/transfer to REO, reports are due by the earlier of five Business Days after receipt/notification or the last Business Day of that month.
business_rules.conditions: When Servicer reports 55-Day Multi PC securitized mortgages via myOptigo
business_rules.consequences: Meets: timely accounting reporting; Violated: reporting noncompliance exposure and fees
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For each Mortgage without exception activity, accounting reports are due: One Business Day after the 55 -Day Multi PC determination date For each Mortgage paid in full, accounting reports are due: By the earlier of: • Two Business Days after the payoff; or • The last Business Day of the month in which the payoff For each Mortgage sold into a securitization (CME sale), accounting reports are due: • The same Business Day that the securitization (CME sale) occurred

examples: Determination date is Nov 6; report due Nov 7 (1 Business Day later) for a loan with no exceptions.
Liquidation notice received Dec 2; report due Dec 9 (5 Business Days later) unless last Business Day occurs earlier.
Payoff occurs on the last Business Day of the month; due date is the earlier of 2 Business Days after payoff or month-end (month-end wins).
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_010 specifies due dates for 55-Day Multi PC securitized mortgages reported via myOptigo. That reporting is governed by the mandatory, timely/accurate transmission obligation of BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009. Automation should first recognize the mandatory reporting requirement, then apply the correct timing rule based on securitization type.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009 is not treated as mandatory, deadline enforcement (BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_010) weakens, increasing late reporting, downstream investor reporting issues, and noncompliance fees.

business_rules.rule_name: Noncompliance fees due by 5th business day after cutoff 06/12/23
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac separately monitors servicing reporting, accounting reporting, and delinquency reporting and separately assesses noncompliance fees. Fees are due no later than the fifth Business Day after the first accounting cutoff date following the missed reporting due date. The Servicer must remit the fee using the myOptigo Servicer Remittance system (Section 53.7).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a reporting due date is missed for required servicing/accounting/delinquency reporting
business_rules.consequences: If remitted by deadline, fee obligation is satisfied; if late, fee is past due and remains owed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; Freddie Mac may change fees and remedies at its discretion

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac separately monitors Servicing reporting, accounting reporting and Delinquency reporting and separately assesses noncompliance fees. Freddie Mac will send the Servicer a written notification of each violation. Fees are due to Freddie Mac no later than the fifth Business Day after the first accounting cutoff date following the missed reporting due date. The Servicer must remit the fee using the myOptigo® Servicer R emittance system described in Section 53.7.

examples: Accounting report due date missed on 2026-06-10; first accounting cutoff date after that is 2026-06-30; fee must be remitted no later than the 5th Business Day after 2026-06-30.
Servicer receives written notification of delinquency reporting violation; Servicer pays assessed fee through myOptigo by the 5th Business Day deadline.
Servicer remits the fee outside myOptigo; payment method violates remittance instruction and may require re-remittance.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: To determine whether a reporting due date was missed (the trigger for BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_034_009), you must first compute the due date. For Gold PC mortgages, those due dates are established by BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_009. Therefore, enforcement of fee-remittance depends on the correct application of the relevant due-date rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If due dates under BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_009 are not calculated/monitored, the Servicer may fail to recognize a missed due date and then miss the subsequent noncompliance fee payment deadline, compounding compliance exposure.

dependencies.rationale: A 'missed reporting due date' must be measured against the correct due date rule. For 55-Day Multi PC loans, that due date logic is in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_010. Without applying it, the Servicer cannot reliably trigger and schedule the fee remittance in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_034_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to apply BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_029_010 can lead to (1) not identifying lateness, (2) missing the noncompliance fee payment window, and (3) increased audit findings and fee escalations.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_039_008 defines that every month the Servicer must report all transactions affecting mortgages serviced, including all loans with UPB and specified entries. Missing these monthly submissions (or omitting required entries) is a primary way a reporting due date is missed, activating the fee payment rule in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_034_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the monthly reporting process is not performed, the Servicer is likely to incur noncompliance fees and then must also execute the fee remittance process timely; failure compounds compliance exposure.

dependencies.rationale: The fee rule requires using the myOptigo Servicer Remittance system. BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_007 states myOptigo remittance requests must be submitted by 1:00 p.m. ET one Business Day before the due date because Freddie Mac does not have use of funds until the next Business Day. Therefore, to satisfy the fee 'due no later than' requirement in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_034_009, the Servicer must follow the earlier submission timing constraint in BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_028_007 is not met, the fee remittance may post late even if initiated on the nominal due date, causing the fee to be past due and increasing noncompliance and reconciliation issues.

business_rules.rule_name: Report monthly transactions for all loans with UPB at cycle start
business_rules.description: Every month, each Servicer must report to Freddie Mac via myOptigo for Investor Reporting all transactions affecting the Mortgages serviced. Monthly automated reports must include all loans with an unpaid principal balance (UPB) as of the beginning of that accounting cycle. The entries transmitted must include monthly principal reductions, net yield interest, and unscheduled principal reductions since the previous month’s accounting cutoff date; exception activity must be reported via myOptigo for Investor Reporting.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to monthly reporting cycle for Mortgages serviced; includes exception activity events
business_rules.consequences: Complete reporting supports investor payments; missing entries risk incorrect investor remits
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 50.9 Monthly reporting and exception activity ( 04/15/21 ) Every month, each Servicer must report to Freddie Mac, via myOptigoSM for Investor Reporting , all transactions affecting the Mortgages serviced (see Section 50.9 (b)). The monthly automated reports must include all loans with an unpaid principal balance as of the beginning of that accounting cycle. The entries that a Servicer must transmit include monthly principal reductions, net yield interest and unscheduled principal reductions since the previous month's accounting cutoff date. If an exception activity occurs with respect to a Mortgage serviced, then the exception activity must be reported via myOptigo for Investor Reporting .

examples: At start of cycle (16th), loan has UPB > $0: include it in the monthly automated report even if no payment occurred.
Loan has a curtailment and an unscheduled principal reduction after prior cutoff: transmit that unscheduled principal reduction entry.
Mortgage paid in full during cycle: report the paid-in-full as exception activity via myOptigo for Investor Reporting.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

dependencies.rationale: The compliance rule (mandatory, usable, accurate, timely) is only operationalizable if the process rule defining what must be reported each month is executed. Conversely, following the monthly process without meeting the 'usable/accurate/timely' compliance standard still results in noncompliance fees. They function as paired controls for myOptigo accounting reporting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_COMPLIANCE_039_009 is not met (e.g., transmission not timely/accurate), then even if BR_REPORTS_ACCOUNTING_NET_YIELD_PROCESS_039_008 content is attempted, the Servicer faces accounting reporting noncompliance fees and potential investor remittance errors.

business_rules.rule_name: Inspection Reports Within 3 Years Must Show No Critical Repairs or Evacuation Orders
business_rules.description: If a structural and/or mechanical inspection was completed within 3 years of the lender's project review date, the lender must obtain and review the inspection report. The report cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed, no evacuation orders are in effect, and no regulatory actions are required. If the report indicates unaddressed critical repairs, the project is ineligible until repairs are completed and documented; the lender must review an engineer’s report (or substantially similar document) to confirm repairs resolved safety/soundness/structural integrity/habitability concerns.
business_rules.conditions: Project review performed and an inspection exists dated within prior 3 years
business_rules.consequences: If report clean, proceed; if critical repairs noted, project ineligible until cured
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: If a structural and/or mechanical inspection was completed within 3 years of the lender's project review date, the lender must obtain and review the inspection report. The report cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed, no evacuation orders are in eﬀect, and no regulatory actions are required. If the inspection report indicates there are unaddressed critical repairs, the project is ineligible until the required repairs have been completed and documented accordingly. The lender must review an engineer's report or substantially similar document to determine if the repairs completed have resolved the safety, soundness, structural integrity, or habitability concerns of the project.

examples: Compliant: Inspection dated 18 months before review shows no critical repairs and no evacuation orders → lender retains and relies on report.
Ineligible: Inspection dated 2 years before review cites structural spalling requiring immediate repair; repairs not done → project ineligible.
Cure: Repairs completed; lender obtains engineer report confirming structural integrity restored → project may become eligible.
Subject property identified as a detached PUD unit with HOA dues.
Subject property identified as an attached PUD unit in a planned development.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_008 requires the lender to review an inspection report and confirm it cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed; if unaddressed critical repairs exist, the project is ineligible until cured. BR_011_007 defines a project in need of critical repairs (unfunded repairs > $10,000 per unit within 12 months with stated exclusions). The lender cannot consistently interpret BR_011_008 outcomes without BR_011_007’s definition.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification can lead to approving an ineligible project (delivery defect) or unnecessarily declining an eligible project (lost production).

provenance.reasoning: Both impose a hard stop when critical repairs are identified: the transaction cannot proceed (project eligibility/underwriting package submission) until repairs are completed and documented/evidenced. Fannie ties trigger to an inspection within 3 years and adds evacuation/regulatory checks; Freddie ties trigger to SBL risk report.

g1_rule.rule_name: Inspection Reports Within 3 Years Must Show No Critical Repairs or Evacuation Orders
g1_rule.description: If a structural and/or mechanical inspection was completed within 3 years of the lender's project review date, the lender must obtain and review the inspection report. The report cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed, no evacuation orders are in effect, and no regulatory actions are required. If the report indicates unaddressed critical repairs, the project is ineligible until repairs are completed and documented; the lender must review an engineer’s report (or substantially similar document) to confirm repairs resolved safety/soundness/structural integrity/habitability concerns.

g2_rule.rule_name: Critical repairs must be completed before full underwriting package submission
g2_rule.description: The Seller must verify that all Critical Repairs have been addressed and must provide evidence of completion to Freddie Mac prior to submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac.

business_rules.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Disclose Buydowns and Subordinate Financing
business_rules.description: On purchase transactions, if applicable, the lender must disclose to the appraiser specific financial information items impacting value, including settlement charges, loan fees/charges, discounts to sales price, interest rate buydowns, below-market-rate financing, terms of subordinate financing provided by interested parties, credits/refunds of borrower expenses, absorption of monthly payments, assignment of rent payments, and any other information that impacts property value.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions with applicable financing terms/concessions/credits affecting value
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser considers value impacts; if violated: value opinion may be flawed
business_rules.exceptions: Only items that are applicable to the transaction must be disclosed

source_reference.source_text: The list below includes items that must be disclosed to the appraiser on purchase transactions, if applicable: settlement charges, loan fees or charges, discounts to the sales price, interest rate buydowns, below-market-rate ﬁnancing, terms of any subordinate ﬁnancing provided by interested parties, credits or refunds of borrower expenses, absorption of monthly payments, assignment of rent payments, and any other information not listed above that impacts property value.

examples: Seller provides a 2-1 temporary buydown and pays $8,000 closing costs; lender discloses to appraiser for value/market reaction.
Interested party provides subordinate financing at below-market terms; lender discloses terms to appraiser.
Rent assignment is part of transaction structure; lender discloses to appraiser because it may affect value opinion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase remedy exposure if value impacted by undisclosed terms

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

provenance.reasoning: Both require the lender/seller/servicer to provide the appraiser with information that could materially affect value so it can be reflected in the appraisal. Rule A focuses on financial concessions; Rule B focuses on property condition report issues.

g1_rule.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Disclose Buydowns and Subordinate Financing
g1_rule.description: On purchase transactions, if applicable, the lender must disclose to the appraiser specific financial information items impacting value, including settlement charges, loan fees/charges, discounts to sales price, interest rate buydowns, below-market-rate financing, terms of subordinate financing provided by interested parties, credits/refunds of borrower expenses, absorption of monthly payments, assignment of rent payments, and any other information that impacts property value.

g2_rule.description: If consultant-identified issues in the property condition report could materially affect value, then before the Seller/Servicer transmits the Appraisal to Freddie Mac, the Seller/Servicer must provide the appraiser with the property condition report ordered by the Seller/Servicer. The report may be provided in draft form if the appraiser notes it was provided with a draft property condition report. If the final version is materially different from the draft, the appraiser must be given the final version and the valuation must be amended to reflect the new information. If Seller/Servicer and Freddie Mac disagree on materiality, Freddie Mac makes the final decision case-by-case.

business_rules.rule_name: Schedule K-1 income allowed only if distributed or liquid
business_rules.description: Income reported on Schedule K-1 may be considered for qualifying only if the lender obtains documentation verifying either (i) the income was actually distributed to the borrower and is consistent with the level of business income used to qualify, or (ii) the business has adequate liquidity to support the withdrawal of earnings. The lender may use discretion in the method used to confirm adequate liquidity.
business_rules.conditions: When qualifying income includes borrower Schedule K-1 income from business
business_rules.consequences: If documentation exists, K-1 income may be used; if not, exclude K-1 income
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Income reported on Schedule K-1 can only be considered if the lender obtains documentation verifying: the income was actually distributed to the borrower and is consistent with the level of business income being used to qualify, or the business has adequate liquidity to support the withdrawal of earnings. The lender may use discretion in the method used to conﬁrm the business has adequate liquidity.

examples: Borrower receives K-1 income but distributions are not evidenced: lender must not use K-1 income for qualifying.
Borrower’s K-1 shows earnings and business bank statements support sufficient liquidity: lender may use K-1 income even if not fully distributed.
K-1 income is distributed but distributions are materially lower than earnings used to qualify: lender must reconcile or reduce qualifying income to supported level.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_002 requires documentation verifying K-1 distributions or business liquidity. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005 provides the standard tax documentation set (returns/transcripts with schedules) that frequently contains K-1s and supporting business financial context used to evaluate distributions/liquidity.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If tax returns/transcripts are missing/incomplete, K-1 income may be improperly included or must be excluded, changing qualifying income and underwriting outcomes.

business_rules.rule_name: Self-employed income verified with 2 years returns or transcripts
business_rules.description: The lender may verify a self-employed borrower’s employment and income by obtaining signed federal income tax returns filed with the IRS for the past two years (individual and, when applicable, business returns) with all schedules attached. Alternatively, the lender may use IRS-issued transcripts for the most recent two years if the transcripts are complete and legible and include information from all applicable schedules.
business_rules.conditions: When verifying self-employed income used for qualifying in underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If acceptable returns/transcripts obtained, proceed; if not, income not properly verified
business_rules.exceptions: One-year returns may be permitted if additional requirements are met

source_reference.source_text: The lender may verify a self-employed borrower’s employment and income by obtaining from the borrower copies of their signed federal income tax returns (both individual returns and in some cases, business returns) that were ﬁled with the IRS for the past two years (with all applicable schedules attached). Alternatively, the lender may use IRS-issued transcripts of the borrower’s individual and business federal income tax returns that were ﬁled with the IRS for the most recent two years—as long as the information provided is complete and legible and the transcripts include the information from all of the applicable schedules.

examples: Lender obtains signed 2024 and 2025 individual returns with Schedule C and all schedules: satisfies 2-year return option.
Lender uses IRS transcripts for 2024 and 2025 but the transcript omits a required schedule: does not meet transcript completeness requirement.
Lender collects only one year of returns without meeting the five-year business/ownership exception: verification requirement not met.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005 describes two-year returns/transcripts as the baseline approach. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_012_006 permits a reduced documentation set (one year) only under defined longevity/ownership conditions; this is an exception/override to the baseline documentation requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the exception is misapplied, the file may be under-documented (QC findings, underwriting defects) or over-documented (unnecessary borrower friction).

business_rules.rule_name: Unit Interior Insurance Required When Master Does Not Cover
business_rules.description: If the master policy does not cover the interior or improvements of the unit in a project development, the borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy. The lender/servicer must verify the coverage amount is sufficient to restore the unit to its pre-loss condition.
business_rules.conditions: When master policy lacks unit interior/improvements coverage for project unit
business_rules.consequences: Meets: adequate unit coverage verified; Violates: insurance deficiency; require borrower policy update before delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-3-04, Determining the Required Coverage Amount
source_reference.source_text: To the extent the master property insurance policy does not cover the interior or improvements of a unit in a project development, the borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy. The lender or servicer must verify the coverage amount is suﬃcient to restore the unit to its condition prior to a loss event.

examples: Condo master policy is ‘bare walls’; borrower must obtain HO-6 coverage for interior finishes and improvements; lender verifies limit supports restoration.
Co-op master policy excludes unit upgrades; borrower provides individual policy/endorsement covering improvements; lender confirms adequate coverage.
PUD master policy covers common elements only; borrower must maintain individual policy for the unit and improvements as needed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004 addresses an interior-coverage gap scenario. If the master policy is unacceptable overall (e.g., ACV settlement), the lender must resolve the master policy defect before relying on borrower-level interior policies for delivery acceptability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the master policy is unacceptable and this is not detected early, the lender may incorrectly focus on borrower HO-6 coverage while the project remains ineligible, causing rework and delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Property Insurer Must Meet AM Best B or Equivalent BBB/A Ratings
business_rules.description: For any first-mortgage property insurance policy (including master policies for projects), the insurer must meet at least one of Fannie Mae’s rating thresholds: AM Best Financial Strength Rating 'B' or better, Demotech 'A' or better, Kroll 'BBB' or better, or S&P Global 'BBB' or better. Meeting one agency’s threshold is sufficient even if the insurer has multiple ratings.
business_rules.conditions: Property secures any first mortgage; lender/servicer reviews insurer acceptability
business_rules.consequences: If insurer does not meet at least one rating threshold, coverage is noncompliant and must be corrected/replaced
business_rules.exceptions: Only one rating agency threshold must be met (per note)

source_reference.source_text: The property insurance policy for the property securing any ﬁrst mortgage, including master policies for project developments, must be written by an insurer that meets one of the rating requirements in the following table. AM Best Company “B” or better Financial Strength Rating. Demotech, Inc. “A” or better Insurance Financial Stability Rating. Kroll Bond Rating Agency “BBB” or better Insurance Financial Strength Rating (IFSR). S&P Global “BBB” or better Insurer Financial Strength Rating. Note: An insurer is only required to meet the rating category requirement for one of the rating agencies

examples: Hazard insurer is rated AM Best 'A-' → meets 'B or better' threshold; policy acceptable.
Insurer has no AM Best rating but has S&P Global IFSR of 'BBB+' → acceptable because it meets one listed threshold.
Insurer is rated below 'B' by AM Best and has no other qualifying rating → lender must replace insurer/policy to comply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension of delivery until corrected / servicing corrective action

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009 requires an identified insurer and policy context. BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002 defines acceptability criteria for the master policy; the rating check is executed against that same master policy’s insurer as part of a complete acceptability decision.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the master policy isn’t properly identified/validated first, the lender might validate the wrong insurer (e.g., borrower HO-6 carrier instead of HOA master carrier) and miss a noncompliant master insurer rating.

dependencies.rationale: When the master policy lacks unit interior coverage, a borrower-level policy becomes required. Once that policy becomes required, it becomes subject to insurer acceptability validation (rating thresholds).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If insurer rating validation is skipped for the required borrower policy, the file may contain a required policy from a non-qualifying carrier, resulting in delivery defects and last-minute re-shopping of insurance.

business_rules.rule_name: Waste site checks: 1 mile, 30 years, Phase II trigger
business_rules.description: For waste site contamination screening in a Phase I environmental hazard assessment format, if any answer to questions 2 through 7 is “yes” or “don’t know,” the property either fails or requires a Phase II assessment. If all answers to questions 2 through 7 are “no,” the property is acceptable for underwriting purposes. The screening questions include whether the site was used for research/industrial/military purposes during the last 30 years and whether the property or any site within one mile appears on state or federal hazardous waste lists.
business_rules.conditions: When completing Phase I waste site contamination screening questions
business_rules.consequences: All “no” answers 2-7 = acceptable; any “yes/DK” 2-7 = fail or Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If Q1=yes and Q2=no, stop: acceptable from waste site contamination standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answer to question 1 is “yes” and the answer to question 2 is “no,” stop here because, for underwriting purposes, the property will be acceptable from the standpoint of waste site contamination. Otherwise, answer the questions below. 3. Was the site ever used for research, industrial, or military purposes during the last 30 years? ... 6. Does the property or any site within one mile of the property appear on any state or federal list of hazardous waste sites?

examples: Underwriter answers Q2-7 all “no”; the property passes waste site contamination screening and is acceptable for underwriting purposes.
Q6 is “yes” because a neighboring site within 0.7 miles is on a federal hazardous list; the property must fail or proceed to Phase II assessment before acceptability can be concluded.
Q3 is “don’t know” regarding industrial use in the last 30 years; the property triggers fail/Phase II per the note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until remediated/cleared

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: PCB concentration 50 ppm or more triggers Phase II or fail
business_rules.description: For PCB contamination screening, if the answer to any question is “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required (stop). Additionally, if PCB concentrations of 50 parts per million or greater have been found in contaminated soils or groundwater, the property either fails or needs a Phase II assessment.
business_rules.conditions: When conducting Phase I PCB screening for subject property
business_rules.consequences: DK answer = Phase II required; PCB ≥50 ppm = fail or Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If initial PCB questions answered “no”, stop: acceptable from PCB standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answers to the above questions are “no,” stop here because, for underwriting purposes, the property will be acceptable from the standpoint of PCB contamination. If the answer to a question is “don’t know,” stop here since a Phase II assessment is required. ... 6. If the answer to question 2 above is “yes,” have PCB concentrations of 50 parts per million or greater been found in contaminated soils or groundwater?

examples: Underwriter selects “don’t know” for whether transformers contain PCBs; the file must obtain a Phase II assessment before acceptability can be concluded.
Environmental report documents PCB in soil at 55 ppm; the property must fail or proceed to Phase II per the screening logic.
All PCB screening questions (including 3-6) are answered “no”; the property is acceptable from the standpoint of PCB contamination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until Phase II completed

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003 is executed in the context of a Phase I environmental hazard screening workflow. When that workflow is initiated (BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009 context), PCB-specific answers can independently force Phase II or failure and thus condition the overall environmental acceptability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PCB screening is omitted when applicable, the lender may fail to obtain a required Phase II assessment even when 'don’t know' responses exist, elevating environmental risk and delivery defensibility concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: Radon test within 6 months must be at or below 4 pCi/L
business_rules.description: For a subject property that is a conversion of an existing building, an EPA-approved short-term radon test performed in the below-grade area of each subject building within the last six months must be at or below four picocuries per liter (4 pCi/L). If the answer is “no” or “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required.
business_rules.conditions: When property is conversion of existing building and radon screening applies
business_rules.consequences: Test ≤4 pCi/L within 6 months = acceptable; otherwise Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If radon questions 2 and 3 are “no,” property acceptable from radon standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answers to questions 2 and 3 are “no,” the property, for underwriting purposes, will be acceptable from the standpoint of radon contamination. If the subject property is a conversion of an existing building, go to question 4. 4. Were the results of an EPA-approved short-term radon test that was performed in the below-grade area of each of the subject buildings within the last six months at or below four picocuries per liter? Note: If the answer to this question is “no” or “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required.

examples: A condo conversion building has a radon test from 5 months ago showing 3.2 pCi/L in the below-grade area; it satisfies the requirement and does not trigger Phase II.
A conversion property has a radon test performed 9 months ago (even if 3.0 pCi/L); it fails the “within the last six months” condition and triggers Phase II.
Underwriter cannot confirm whether an EPA-approved test was performed (“don’t know”); Phase II assessment is required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until Phase II completed

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: Within the Phase I environmental hazard assessment workflow, conversions introduce radon screening requirements. If radon test results are missing/unknown or above threshold, the rules require Phase II, which directly blocks environmental clearance for underwriting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this conditional screening is not executed for conversions, loans may proceed without required testing/Phase II, exposing lender to health/safety risk and potential ineligibility if discovered during QC or post-purchase review.

business_rules.rule_name: Section 184 delivery must report SFC 202 and Assumption Indicator True
business_rules.description: When selling HUD Section 184 (and 184A) loans to Fannie Mae, the lender must report Special Feature Code (SFC) 202 and must set the Assumption Indicator in the Loan Delivery application to "True" to reflect that Section 184 loans are assumable as of the note date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering HUD-guaranteed Section 184/184A loans to Fannie Mae via Loan Delivery
business_rules.consequences: If correctly reported, delivery data meets program delivery requirements; if misreported, delivery defects may require correction and can trigger purchase/delivery issues
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B6-1-04, Delivery of HUD-Guaranteed Section 184 Mortgages
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must report SFC 202 when selling HUD Section 184 (and 184A) loans to Fannie Mae. Section 184 loans are assumable as of the note date. When selling such loans to Fannie Mae, the Assumption Indicator in the Loan Delivery application must be "True" (which means assumable).

examples: Lender delivers a Section 184 loan and reports SFC 202; Assumption Indicator set to True → compliant delivery coding.
Lender forgets SFC 202 even though loan is Section 184 → delivery data defect; must correct prior to/after delivery per Fannie Mae processes.
Assumption Indicator is set to False for a Section 184 loan → misrepresents assumability; must be corrected to True.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery suspense/curative action; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_027_009 is a hard prohibition: Section 184 loans subject to a buydown plan are not permitted for purchase/securitization. BR_027_010 requires reporting SFC 202 and setting Assumption Indicator to True when selling Section 184 loans. Operationally, BR_027_010 only applies once the loan is confirmed eligible for Section 184 delivery (i.e., not prohibited by BR_027_009).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_027_009 is missed, the lender may proceed with BR_027_010 coding and attempt delivery, resulting in delivery rejection, post-delivery findings, and potential corrective actions.

business_rules.rule_name: Buydown loans must be qualified at the note rate not the reduced rate
business_rules.description: When underwriting a loan with a temporary interest rate buydown, the lender must qualify the borrower based on the note rate, without considering the bought-down rate. The underwriting decision must therefore reflect qualifying payment calculations at the permanent note rate rather than the temporary reduced payment.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting a mortgage loan that includes a temporary interest rate buydown
business_rules.consequences: If qualified at reduced rate, underwriting is noncompliant and may be remedied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Qualifying the Borrower When underwriting loans that have a temporary interest rate buydown, the lender must qualify the borrower based on the note rate without consideration of the bought-down rate.

examples: Note rate 6.50% with 2-1 buydown; lender uses 6.50% P&I to qualify: compliant.
Note rate 6.50% with buydown to 4.50% year 1; lender qualifies using 4.50% payment: noncompliant underwriting.
DU approval: underwriter ensures qualifying payment aligns to note rate even though initial payment is lower: compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase underwriting defect remedy / repurchase exposure

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

business_rules.rule_name: IRS Form 4506-C valid 120 days and up to 4 years
business_rules.description: IRS Form 4506-C may be used to obtain tax transcripts for up to four years/tax periods and is valid for 120 days after completion (including signature) by the borrower. The lender must have each borrower whose income is used in qualifying complete and sign a separate IRS Form 4506-C at or before closing. If all of a borrower’s income is validated by the DU validation service, the lender is not required to obtain a signed IRS Form 4506-C for that borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower income is used for qualifying and not fully validated by DU validation.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: transcript authorization compliant; Violated: income verification/QC defect.
business_rules.exceptions: No 4506-C needed when all income validated by DU validation service.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.1-02, Use of IRS Form 4506-C to Verify Borrower Income Documentation
source_reference.source_text: IRS Form 4506-C can be used to obtain tax transcripts for up to four years or tax periods and is valid for 120 days after completion (including signature) by the borrower. The lender must have each borrower whose income is used in qualifying (regardless of income source) complete and sign a separate IRS Form 4506-C at or before closing. An alternative form or process is also acceptable if it authorizes the release of comparable tax information from the IRS. Note: When all of a borrower’s income is validated by the DU validation service, the lender is not required to obtain a signed IRS Form 4506–C for that borrower.

examples: Two W-2 borrowers use paystubs/W-2s (not DU validation); each signs their own 4506-C at closing dated the same day they sign.
Self-employed borrower needs personal and business transcripts; lender obtains separate 4506-Cs per tax form type and within 120-day validity.
Borrower’s income fully validated by DU validation service; lender does not obtain 4506-C for that borrower under the stated exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification or repurchase demand (if income not properly verified)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit must language and numeric limits (120 days; up to four years).

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Boarder income capped at 30% with 12-month history and proof
business_rules.description: When boarder income is used to qualify a HomeReady borrower on a one-unit property, the boarder income may be counted up to 30% of the total gross income used to qualify, only if the boarder is not obligated on the loan and has no ownership interest, has lived with the borrower for the last 12 months, provides documentation of shared residency, and demonstrates rental payments to the borrower for the last 12 months or at least 9 of the most recent 12 months (averaged over 12 months). Rent paid by the boarder directly to a third party is not acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady; 1-unit property; borrower uses boarder income for qualifying
business_rules.consequences: If requirements not met, boarder income must be excluded from qualifying
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.source_text: The rental payments that any borrower receives from one or more individuals who reside with the borrower (who may or may not be related to the borrower) may be considered as acceptable stable income. This applies for a one-unit property in an amount up to 30% of the total gross income that is used to qualify the borrower for the mortgage if the boarder is not obligated on the mortgage loan and does not have an ownership interest in the property; has lived with the borrower for the last 12 months; can provide appropriate documentation to demonstrate a history of shared residency (such as a copy of a driver’s license, bill, or bank statement that shows the boarder’s address as being the same as the borrower’s address); and can demonstrate the payment of rental payments (such as with copies of canceled checks) to the borrower for the last 12 months, or at least 9 of the most recent 12 months provided the rental income is averaged over a 12–month period. Payment of rent by the boarder directly to a third party is not acceptable.

examples: Borrower qualifies with $1,000/month boarder rent; total gross income used to qualify is $3,000/month: only $900 (30%) may be used; remaining $100 must be excluded.
Boarder has lived with borrower 10 months: boarder income is not eligible because 12-month shared residency is required.
Boarder paid rent directly to the landlord/HOA instead of the borrower: payments are not acceptable for boarder income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting denial or condition to remove income

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Nontraditional housing payments must be documented for 12 consecutive months
business_rules.description: When underwriting a borrower using nontraditional credit history, the borrower’s housing payment history must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. Acceptable documentation includes canceled checks or, in lieu of canceled checks, bank statements, copies of money orders, or other reasonable methods that clearly show payee and amount and reflect consistent payment; or direct verification of rent from the landlord (individual or professional management company).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower’s credit history is evaluated using nontraditional credit references
business_rules.consequences: If documented, satisfies housing payment history requirement; if not, nontraditional credit profile is incomplete and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If at least one borrower documents housing payment, others need not; shared lease counts for each

source_reference.source_text: The borrower’s housing payment history must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. The following documentation is acceptable: Canceled checks can be provided. In lieu of canceled checks, the lender may use the borrower’s bank statements, copies of money orders, or other reasonable methods for documenting timely housing payments. The documentation must clearly indicate the payee and amount being paid, and reﬂect that payments were made on a consistent basis. Direct veriﬁcation of the payment of rent from the landlord.

examples: Borrower provides 12 months of bank statements showing monthly rent ACH debits to the landlord with consistent amounts → requirement met.
Borrower provides only 10 months of canceled checks for rent → requirement not met because it is not the most recent consecutive 12-month period.
Two borrowers are on the lease; only one pays rent, but the lease/rent verification covers the household → counts as one nontraditional credit reference for each borrower.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Large deposit is >50% of monthly income and may reduce usable assets
business_rules.description: A large deposit is a single deposit that exceeds 50% of the borrower’s total monthly qualifying income for the loan. When bank statements are used, the lender must evaluate large deposits. For refinance transactions, documentation/explanation for large deposits is not required, but the lender remains responsible to ensure borrowed funds (and any related liability) are considered. For purchase transactions, if large deposit funds are needed for down payment/closing costs/reserves, the lender must document that the funds are from an acceptable source; if not documented, verified funds must be reduced by the undocumented (unsourced) portion and the reduced asset amount must be used for underwriting.
business_rules.conditions: When bank statements are used and a single deposit exceeds 50% of qualifying income
business_rules.consequences: If unsourced purchase deposit used, lender must subtract it or document source
business_rules.exceptions: Refinance: no documentation required, but borrowed funds must be captured as liabilities

source_reference.source_text: Evaluating Large Deposits A large deposit is deﬁned as a single deposit that exceeds 50% of the total monthly qualifying income for the loan. When bank statements (typically covering the most recent two months) are used, the lender must evaluate large deposits. ... Requirements for evaluating large deposits vary based on the transaction type... Reﬁnance transactions Documentation or explanation for large deposits is not required; however, the lender remains responsible for ensuring that any borrowed funds, including any related liability, are considered. Purchase transactions ... If funds from a large deposit are needed... the lender must document that those funds are from an acceptable source... Veriﬁed funds must be reduced by the amount (or portion) of the undocumented large deposit...

examples: Purchase: borrower income $6,000; a $3,500 single deposit (>50%) is unsourced and needed for down payment; lender must document source or subtract $3,500 from usable assets.
Refinance: borrower income $5,000; a $3,000 deposit (>50%) appears; lender does not need source docs but must ensure any borrowing is reflected as a liability in DTI.
Purchase: deposit is $4,000 but $3,500 is sourced (tax refund) and $500 is unsourced; only $500 is tested against the 50% threshold.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting defect remedy / repurchase exposure for asset misstatement

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: Because BR_028_010 uses monthly qualifying income as the denominator/threshold reference, any rule that materially changes qualifying income (like excluding crypto income) must be applied before final large-deposit flagging and documentation decisions are made.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_008 is not applied first, the lender may under-flag large deposits (threshold too high due to improperly included crypto income) and skip required sourcing, creating asset-sourcing/QC defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_010 can cap or eliminate rental income used for qualifying (e.g., no current housing payment → no rental income). Because BR_028_010’s large deposit threshold is based on monthly qualifying income, BR_002_010 must be applied before the final large-deposit threshold is set for documentation decisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_010 is applied after BR_028_010, the lender may fail to request sourcing for deposits that become ‘large’ after income is reduced, increasing asset-sourcing defects and rework late in underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: Nontraditional credit requires 4 references or 30% income exception and DU 2 minimum
business_rules.description: For a borrower without a credit score, the number of nontraditional credit references that must be documented varies by underwriting method and product: (i) Manually underwritten loans other than HomeReady require four credit references for each borrower without a credit score; (ii) Manually underwritten HomeReady loans require three credit references; (iii) If a borrower without a credit score cannot document any nontraditional credit references because the borrower has none, the transaction is still eligible provided no more than 30% of the qualifying income comes from that borrower; (iv) If DU requires nontraditional credit history, at least two credit references for each borrower are required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a borrower has no credit score and nontraditional credit is used/required
business_rules.consequences: If met, credit history documentation is sufficient; if not, loan cannot be approved as structured
business_rules.exceptions: Manual: borrower with none allowed if ≤30% of qualifying income

source_reference.section_id: B3-5.4-02, Number of Nontraditional Credit References Required
source_reference.source_text: Manually underwritten loans• Four credit references for each borrower without a credit score• Three credit references for each borrower without a credit score • If there is a borrower on the loan without a credit score who cannot document any nontraditional credit references (because the borrower has none), the transaction is still eligible, provided no more than 30% of the qualifying income for the loan comes from that borrower. DU messages will specify when a nontraditional credit history must be documented.• If DU requires a nontraditional credit history to be documented, at least two credit references for each borrower are required.

examples: Manual non-HomeReady loan: borrower has no score; lender documents 4 eligible references with 12-month histories; requirement met.
Manual HomeReady loan: borrower has no score and only 2 references; requirement not met (needs 3) unless the borrower has none and qualifies for the ≤30% exception.
DU requires nontraditional credit history; lender documents 2 credit references per borrower; requirement met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: 30-day charge accounts require funds equal to balance
business_rules.description: For open 30-day charge accounts without a stated monthly payment on the credit report (or where the reported monthly payment equals the balance), the lender must verify borrower funds sufficient to cover the account balance. These verified funds must be in addition to funds required for closing costs and reserves. Proof of payoff prior to closing may be used instead of verifying funds to cover the balance.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has open 30-day charge accounts lacking a usable monthly payment
business_rules.consequences: If met, charge account balance is covered via verified funds/proof of payoff; if violated, file is incomplete and loan may be ineligible/defective
business_rules.exceptions: Proof of payoff prior to closing allowed in lieu of verifying funds

source_reference.source_text: For open 30-day charge accounts that do not reﬂect a monthly payment on the credit report, or 30-day accounts that reﬂect a monthly payment that is identical to the account balance, lenders must verify borrower funds to cover the account balance. The veriﬁed funds must be in addition to any funds required for closing costs and reserves. If the borrower paid oﬀ the account balance prior to closing, the lender may provide proof of payoﬀ in lieu of verifying funds to cover the account balance.

examples: Borrower has a charge card with $2,500 balance and no monthly payment shown; lender verifies an additional $2,500 beyond funds to close and required reserves.
Borrower’s 30-day account shows a $1,200 balance and $1,200 payment; lender verifies $1,200 additional funds or obtains proof the balance was paid before closing.
Transaction requires no reserves; lender still verifies separate funds for the 30-day charge account balance (or obtains payoff proof).
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_003 is a funds sufficiency test. It cannot be completed accurately unless the lender knows what reserve requirement applies (including any additional reserves computed under BR_023_006).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_006 isn’t computed first, the lender may falsely conclude the borrower has sufficient additional funds for charge accounts, creating an asset adequacy defect and potential post-close finding.

dependencies.rationale: If no minimum reserves are required due to BR_023_008, the amount of ‘extra’ funds needed beyond closing costs and reserves to cover 30-day charge accounts may be lower, affecting approval/conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_008 is not applied, the lender may demand unnecessary extra liquid funds for charge accounts, or incorrectly condition for payoff.

business_rules.rule_name: Variable Base Income Requires at Least 12 Months of History
business_rules.description: When using variable base income as qualifying income, the lender must document that the borrower has a minimum 12-month history of receiving variable income.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower has variable base income (fluctuating hours or varying hourly rate)
business_rules.consequences: If <12 months history, variable base income cannot be used as qualifying income; may reduce eligibility/approval
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the minimum history requirement

source_reference.source_text: Income History A minimum 12-month history of receiving variable income is required.

examples: Borrower has 18 months of paystubs showing fluctuating hours at a fixed hourly rate → variable base income is eligible to be used (subject to other calculations).
Borrower started variable-hour job 8 months ago → variable base income cannot be used as qualifying income under this rule.
Borrower has 12 months of documented variable hours with consistent earnings trend → meets minimum history requirement.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Fixed Base Income YTD Must Show 30 Days or Match Prior Year
business_rules.description: For fixed base income, the borrower’s qualifying income must be consistent with year-to-date base earnings. If year-to-date income does not reflect at least 30 days of earnings, the lender must confirm that qualifying income is consistent with the prior year’s earnings.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower has fixed base income and YTD earnings are used to support income
business_rules.consequences: If YTD <30 days and lender does not confirm prior year consistency, income is not validated and loan may be noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.3-01 (Fixed Base Income: Determination of Qualifying Income)
source_reference.source_text: The borrower's qualifying income must be consistent with year-to-date base earnings. If year-to-date income does not reﬂect at least 30 days' of earnings, the lender must conﬁrm qualifying income is consistent with the prior year's earnings.

examples: Borrower provides a paystub dated January 5 (only 5 days YTD). Lender reviews prior year W-2 to confirm salary level aligns → compliant validation.
Borrower has only a first-week paystub and lender does not check prior year earnings → income validation fails; must condition/curtail income use.
Borrower provides paystub dated February 15 showing >30 days YTD earnings consistent with stated salary → prior year confirmation not required by this rule.
U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Disability income needs policy and 1 payment by first due date
business_rules.description: To use long-term disability income (excluding Social Security disability), the lender must obtain a copy of the borrower’s disability policy or benefits statement from the benefits payer to determine current eligibility, amount and frequency, and any contractually established termination/modification date. The lender must also obtain evidence the borrower will receive at least one payment on or before the first payment due date. No minimum income history is required; the documented monthly amount may be used as qualifying income. If short-term disability will convert to a lower long-term amount within the next three years, the long-term amount must be used for qualifying.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting uses non-SSA long-term disability income as qualifying income
business_rules.consequences: If documentation/evidence absent, income cannot be used; qualification must be recalculated
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to SSA disability; use Social Security income guidance instead

source_reference.source_text: The lender must obtain • a copy of the borrower's disability policy or beneﬁts statement from the beneﬁts payer (insurance company, employer, or other qualiﬁed disinterested party) to determine: the borrower's current eligibility for the disability beneﬁts, the amount and frequency of the disability payments, and if there is a contractually established termination or modiﬁcation date. Additionally, the lender must obtain evidence that the borrower will receive at least one payment on or before the ﬁrst payment due date. Income History No minimum history is required... if a borrower is currently receiving short-term disability payments that will decrease to a lesser amount within the next three years... the amount of the long-term beneﬁts must be used as income to qualify the borrower.

examples: Borrower provides insurer benefits statement showing $2,000/month and ongoing eligibility; bank proof shows next payment posts before first payment due date → income may be used.
Borrower provides benefits letter but no evidence of any upcoming payment before first due date → do not use disability income until evidence obtained.
Borrower receives $3,000 short-term disability converting to $1,800 long-term within 24 months → qualify using $1,800 (long-term) amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting denial or repurchase risk due to unsupported income

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: BR_037_005’s income acceptability includes a temporal condition tied to the loan’s first payment due date. BR_038_009 defines/limits how the first payment date is established. Therefore, underwriting cannot complete the disability-income validation until the payment schedule is known and compliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is not satisfied (unknown/incorrect first payment date), the lender cannot properly evidence receipt timing for disability income. This can force income exclusion, DTI recalculation, DU resubmission, or a suspense condition.

business_rules.rule_name: Schedule E fair rental days must support partial-year rent
business_rules.description: When rental income is used to qualify and the borrower has only partial (or no) rental history on personal federal tax returns, the lender must determine the period the rental property was in service. If the property was acquired or placed into service during the most recent tax filing year, the lender must confirm the purchase date using the settlement statement (or other documentation) and the Fair Rental Days on Schedule E of the most recently filed tax return must confirm partial-year rental income.
business_rules.conditions: When using rental income to qualify and Schedule E shows partial-year activity
business_rules.consequences: If purchase date or Fair Rental Days do not support rental period, rental income must be excluded or re-derived
business_rules.exceptions: Other situations using lease agreements require file justification

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01, Reconciling Partial or No Rental History (Schedule E Only)
source_reference.source_text: To determine qualifying rental income, in those scenarios where the borrower has only a partial rental history on the personal federal tax returns, the lender must determine the period of time the rental property was in service (that is, rented, with the borrower receiving rental income from that property). If ... the property was acquired or placed into service during the most recent tax ﬁling year, • the lender must conﬁrm the purchase date using the settlement statement or other documentation, and • Fair Rental Days on Schedule E of the most recently ﬁled tax return must conﬁrm partial year rental income.

examples: Borrower bought a rental on 10/15 and Schedule E shows 75 Fair Rental Days; lender confirms purchase with settlement statement and may annualize/derive qualifying rent based on partial-year service.
Schedule E shows rental income but Fair Rental Days indicate only 10 days; lender must reconcile and may be unable to use the stated rent to qualify without acceptable alternative support.
Borrower requests lease-based rental income due to unusual circumstances; lender may use lease option but must document explanation/justification in file.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: DU income validation via assets requires 30–60 day ownership check
business_rules.description: When an asset verification report is used to validate income, the lender must confirm the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reflects the borrower's name as account holder. For this ownership/name confirmation, the lender need only review the report covering the transaction’s required 30- or 60-day asset verification period; if the transaction requires no asset verification, the lender must review account activity covering the most recent 30-day period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU validates income using an Asset Verification Report
business_rules.consequences: If verified, DU validation can be relied upon; if not, validation is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: when an asset veriﬁcation report is used to validate income, the lender must conﬁrm that the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reﬂects the borrower's name as account holder. The lender need only review the report the covering the period of time (30- or 60- days) required for asset veriﬁcation for the transaction type, or if no asset veriﬁcation is required for the transaction, the account activity covering the most recent 30-day period, for these purposes.

examples: Purchase transaction requires 60-day assets: lender reviews 60-day asset verification report and confirms borrower name/ownership before accepting DU income validation.
Rate-term refinance where no asset verification is required: lender reviews most recent 30-day activity to confirm borrower is account holder for the account used in DU validation.
Asset report shows deposits but account holder name is missing: lender cannot rely on DU validation until resolved and documented.
DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

dependencies.rationale: BR_017_002 sets the required standards to use an asset verification report to validate income/employment through DU validation service (must be from authorized vendor and include at least 12 months of data). BR_014_005 then requires the lender to confirm borrower ownership/name within the report over the required period. The ownership/name confirmation step only matters if the report itself is eligible for DU validation use, so BR_017_002 is a prerequisite.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_017_002 is not satisfied (e.g., only 60-day report), the lender cannot rely on DU validation service for income/employment via asset verification; BR_014_005 review becomes insufficient to make the report usable for that purpose, requiring alternative verification methods.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DESKTOP_UNDERWRITER_VALIDATION_014_005 requires review of the asset verification report used to validate income. BR_MORTGAGE_FILE_CONSTRAINT_036_007 sets a 180-day maximum datedness for verifications of income/assets in disaster areas. This creates a scenario where the ownership/name review step must be performed on an updated report if the existing report is too old.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the report is older than 180 days: (1) lender must obtain an updated report, (2) the ownership/name confirmation must be redone, (3) underwriting/closing delays may occur.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Income Validation Requires 12 Months Asset Data
business_rules.description: To use an asset verification report to validate income or employment through DU validation service, the lender must order an asset verification report from an authorized vendor that includes at least 12 months of data. Reports with only 30- or 60-day data are insufficient for income/employment validation via asset verification.
business_rules.conditions: When validating income or employment using an asset verification report via DU
business_rules.consequences: If met, DU may validate component; if not, validation not permitted/eligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated
