source_reference.source_text: Asset veriﬁcation reports provided by third-party vendors may include: a 30- or 60-day report; data covering up to 12 months (or more) of asset account data; a report showing a subset of information typically found on an asset veriﬁcation report (for example, deposit transaction information with no corresponding amount or account balance) intended to be used to verify employment using direct deposit transactions (a "supplemental asset report"). To validate income or employment using an asset veriﬁcation report, the lender must order an asset veriﬁcation report with a minimum of 12 months of data from an authorized vendor.

examples: Lender orders 12-month asset verification report; DU validates employment based on direct deposit history.
Lender orders only a 60-day asset report; DU cannot validate income/employment using the report and lender must meet standard documentation messages.
Vendor provides 15 months of data; lender satisfies the minimum 12-month requirement.
DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady cash-on-hand must be deposited by application or 30 days pre-close
business_rules.description: For HomeReady purchase money mortgages on one-unit properties, cash-on-hand may be used for down payment, closing costs, and prepaid items, but may not be used to meet any reserve requirement. The lender must verify the borrower customarily uses cash and that the saved funds are consistent with prior payment practices, must verify funds exist in a financial institution or acceptable escrow account, and the funds must be on deposit at the time of application or no less than 30 days prior to closing. The lender must also obtain a written borrower statement disclosing the source of funds and stating the funds have not been borrowed.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady; purchase; one-unit property; borrower uses cash-on-hand funds
business_rules.consequences: If not verified/timed, cash-on-hand cannot be counted as eligible funds to close
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.source_text: Lenders may deliver purchase money mortgages for one-unit properties with cash-on-hand as an acceptable source of funds for the borrower’s down payment, funds for closing costs, and prepaid items. Note : Cash-on-hand may not be used to fund the borrower’s reserve requirement, if applicable. The lender must verify and document the following with respect to the cash-on-hand funds: The borrower customarily uses cash for expenses, and the amount of funds saved is consistent with the borrower’s previous payment practices. The lender must verify that funds for the down payment and closing costs exist in a ﬁnancial institution account or an acceptable escrow account. Funds must be on deposit at the time of application, or no less than 30 days prior to closing. The lender must obtain a written statement from the borrower that discloses the source of funds and states that the funds have not been borrowed.

examples: Funds deposited 10 days before closing: fails the “no less than 30 days prior to closing” timing; must use other eligible assets or delay closing.
Funds deposited before application date and verified in a bank statement: meets the deposit timing requirement (subject to all other verification steps).
Borrower attempts to use cash-on-hand for required reserves: not permitted; reserves must come from other eligible verified assets.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting denial or condition to provide eligible funds

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary Leave Requires Return Date Proof and Verbal VOE
business_rules.description: If the lender is aware the borrower will be on temporary leave at closing and that income is needed to qualify, the lender must obtain: (1) documentation supporting qualifying income by income type including amount/duration of temporary leave income and the regular employment income received prior to leave; (2) written confirmation from the borrower of intent to return to work; (3) employer (or employer designee) documentation confirming expected return-to-work date; and (4) a verbal VOE. If employer confirms borrower is currently on temporary leave, the lender must consider the borrower employed.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower on temporary leave at closing and their income is needed to qualify
business_rules.consequences: If met, borrower may be treated employed and income evaluated; if not, income use is unsupported
business_rules.exceptions: Return-date documentation need not meet allowable age of credit documents policy

source_reference.source_text: If a lender is made aware that a borrower will be on temporary leave at the time of the loan closing and that borrower's income is needed to qualify for the loan, the lender must determine allowable income and conﬁrm employment as described below. The lender must obtain documentation to support the borrower's qualifying income... written conﬁrmation from the borrower of their intent to return to work; documentation produced by the employer... that conﬁrms the borrower's expected return to work date... (This documentation does not have to comply with the Allowable Age of Credit Documents policy...) Additionally, a verbal VOE is required... If the employer conﬁrms the borrower is currently on temporary leave, the lender must consider the borrower employed.

examples: Borrower on parental leave at closing; lender gets employer HR system printout showing return date, borrower intent letter, and verbal VOE—borrower may be treated employed.
Lender relies only on borrower’s statement of return date with no employer documentation—temporary leave income cannot be validated for qualification.
Employer confirms borrower is currently on temporary leave during verbal VOE; lender treats borrower as employed for underwriting purposes.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Education provider must meet NIS or HUD standards with certificate
business_rules.description: When homeownership education is required, the lender must ensure the education is administered by a qualified third-party provider that is independent of the lender, and that the provider’s content is aligned with National Industry Standards (NIS) or HUD standards. The lender must confirm the course content alignment and must retain a copy of the certificate of course completion in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: When homeownership education is used to satisfy a required education trigger
business_rules.consequences: If alignment/certificate not retained, education requirement is not evidenced
business_rules.exceptions: Housing counseling may be used in lieu of education

source_reference.source_text: To satisfy the homeownership education requirement, Fannie Mae permits any qualiﬁed third-party provider, independent of the lender, to administer homeownership education. The provider’s content must be aligned with NIS or HUD standards. The education may be delivered in various formats (in-person, Internet, via telephone, or a hybrid format). In lieu of homeownership education, the borrower may receive housing counseling. The lender must conﬁrm the course content is aligned with NIS or HUD standards and must retain a copy of the certiﬁcate of course completion in the loan ﬁle.

examples: Borrower completes an online NIS-aligned course from an independent provider; lender retains completion certificate: compliant.
Lender accepts a course but does not document NIS/HUD alignment or retain certificate: noncompliant evidence of education.
Borrower completes HUD-approved housing counseling instead of education; lender retains counseling completion certificate: compliant via alternative.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase documentation defect remedy / repurchase exposure

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_003 establishes when education must be completed. BR_028_004 specifies how the lender must validate that education (qualified independent provider, NIS/HUD alignment, and certificate retained). Therefore, once BR_028_003 is triggered, BR_028_004 becomes the mandatory validation/evidence requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_028_004 fails, the education completion may not be acceptable for Guide compliance even if the borrower took a course, resulting in a noncompliant loan file and potential post-close findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Most recent paystub must be within 30 days of application and show YTD
business_rules.description: When paystubs are used to document employment income, the most recent paystub must be dated no earlier than 30 days prior to the initial loan application date and must include all year-to-date earnings. If it lacks sufficient information to calculate income, additional documentation must be obtained.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower employment income is documented using paystubs
business_rules.consequences: If met, paystub is acceptable; if violated, updated/extra documentation is required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The most recent paystub must be dated no earlier than 30 days prior to the initial loan application date and it must include all year-to-date earnings. Additionally, the most recent paystub must include suﬃcient information to appropriately calculate income; otherwise, additional documentation must be obtained.

examples: Application date 2026-03-15 and paystub date 2026-03-01 (14 days prior) with YTD earnings; acceptable.
Application date 2026-03-15 and paystub date 2026-02-10 (33 days prior); lender must obtain a more recent paystub or other docs.
Paystub within 30 days but missing YTD totals; lender must obtain additional documentation to calculate income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: VOD Source Verification for Accounts Opened ≤ 90 Days
business_rules.description: If a Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) is used and depository activity is not included, the lender must verify the source of funds for (1) accounts opened within the last 90 days of the application date and (2) account balances that are considerably greater than the average balance reflected on the VOD.
business_rules.conditions: When Form 1006 VOD is used and depository activity is not included
business_rules.consequences: If met: assets acceptable for underwriting; if violated: asset verification is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the VOD requirement sentence

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.2-03 (02/06/2019), Request for Verification of Deposit
source_reference.source_text: When a Veriﬁcation of Deposit (Form 1006) (VOD) is used and depository activity is not included, the lender must verify the source of funds for accounts opened within the last 90 days of the application date, and account balances that are considerably greater than the average balance reﬂected on the VOD.

examples: Borrower opened a new checking account 45 days before application; lender must document where the funds came from.
VOD shows $75,000 balance but average balance is $2,000; lender must verify source for the excess funds.
VOD includes no depository activity; lender obtains bank statements and transfer evidence to confirm funds are not borrowed.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_APPLICATION_VALIDATION_031_007 requires additional sourcing verification for certain VOD scenarios when using Form 1006 without depository activity. BR_MORTGAGE_FILE_VALIDATION_008_003 governs whether the Form 1006 itself is acceptable (direct-source, signed/dated, Blanket Authorization attached). Therefore BR_008_003 must be satisfied before BR_031_007’s VOD-based validation can be relied upon.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Form 1006 is invalid under BR_008_003, the lender cannot rely on the VOD contents to satisfy BR_031_007; the asset verification becomes deficient and may require bank statements or reissued VOD, causing underwriting delays.

business_rules.rule_name: In-file credit report must use 3 repositories or document exception
business_rules.description: An in-file credit report must include all information from three different credit repositories, or two repositories if that is the extent of data available for the borrower or if the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one repository. If only one in-file credit report is available, it is acceptable only if the lender requested information from three repositories.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses an in-file credit report to underwrite a loan application
business_rules.consequences: If met, credit report type is acceptable; if violated, lender must obtain compliant report/verification or file is defective
business_rules.exceptions: Two repositories allowed if data limited or one repository frozen; one repository allowed only if three were requested

source_reference.source_text: The report should include all information from three diﬀerent credit repositories, or two repositories, if: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one credit repository. If only one in-ﬁle credit report is available for a borrower, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three credit repositories.

examples: Borrower has a freeze at one bureau; lender obtains two repositories and documents the freeze as support.
Only one repository returns data; lender shows it requested three repositories, making the single report acceptable.
Lender pulls only one bureau without requesting the other two; report is not acceptable and must be corrected.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_006 addresses repository coverage requirements for in-file credit reports. BR_032_008 separately prohibits certain automated merged credit reports if the CRA is affiliated with the lender. In practice, lenders need both checks in their credit-report intake controls: (1) report content/coverage and (2) provider independence.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_008 is violated, the lender must replace the report even if it otherwise has sufficient repositories, creating re-pull delays and potential fee/consent issues.

business_rules.rule_name: In-file accounts not updated within 90 days must be reverified
business_rules.description: If an in-file credit report lists accounts that were not checked with the creditor within 90 days of the in-file report date, the lender must obtain an updated credit report or a separate written verification for those accounts.
business_rules.conditions: When in-file credit report shows creditor-check age > 90 days for any listed account
business_rules.consequences: If met, lender has current verification; if violated, credit data is stale and underwriting file is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the report lists accounts that were not checked with the creditor within 90 days of the date of the in-ﬁle report, the lender must obtain an updated credit report or a separate written veriﬁcation for those accounts.

examples: In-file report dated 01/15 shows a creditor last checked 09/01; lender orders an updated report before closing.
In-file report includes an account last checked 120 days ago; lender obtains written verification from that creditor for the account terms and status.
All accounts show creditor check dates within 60 days; no additional reverification is required by this rule.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_006 establishes acceptability criteria for an in-file credit report. BR_032_007 then imposes additional actions when that in-file report shows stale creditor-check dates (>90 days). Therefore, using an in-file credit report (BR_032_006 context) conditionally triggers the freshness remediation in BR_032_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender does not satisfy BR_032_007 when triggered, credit data becomes stale and the underwriting file is deficient; the lender must obtain updated reports/verifications, potentially delaying closing.

business_rules.rule_name: RMCR must contact 2 repositories per locality over 2 years
business_rules.description: A residential mortgage credit report (RMCR) must be based on the credit reporting agency contacting at least two national repositories of accumulated credit records for each locality where the borrower lived during the most recent two-year period.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses a residential mortgage credit report (RMCR) to underwrite
business_rules.consequences: If met, RMCR meets repository-contact standard; if violated, RMCR is noncompliant and must be replaced/corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The report must meet the following requirements: The credit reporting agency must contact at least two national repositories of accumulated credit records for each locality in which the borrower has lived during the most recent two-year period.

examples: Borrower lived in two counties over the last 24 months; RMCR vendor contacts at least two national repositories for each locality.
RMCR vendor contacts only one national repository for a locality; RMCR fails this requirement and must be corrected.
Borrower lived in one locality for the last two years; RMCR must still evidence contact with at least two national repositories for that locality.
Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Subject Rental Income Requires Schedule E or 8825
business_rules.description: When the borrower owns rental property other than the subject property, the lender must document monthly gross and net rental income using the borrower’s most recent signed federal income tax return including Schedule 1 and Schedule E, and/or the most recent signed federal business income tax return for a partnership or S corporation including IRS Form 8825 (when only rental income is reported on the K-1); both are required when rental properties are reported on both personal and business returns.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower owns and rents property other than the subject property
business_rules.consequences: If required returns obtained, rental income may be used; if not, rental income not validated
business_rules.exceptions: Lease may substitute for Schedule E only if qualifying exception documented

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01 (Documenting Rental Income from Property Other Than the Subject Property)
source_reference.source_text: When the borrower owns property – other than the subject property – that is rented, the lender must document the monthly gross (and net) rental income with the borrower’s most recent signed federal income tax return that includes Schedule 1 and Schedule E, the most recent signed federal business income tax return for a partnership or S corporation, that includes IRS Form 8825 (when only rental income is reported on the K-1), or both of the above, when rental properties are reported on both personal and business returns.

examples: Borrower owns a duplex rental (not subject): lender obtains signed 1040 with Schedule E; acceptable documentation basis.
Borrower owns rentals in an S-corp: lender obtains business return including Form 8825; acceptable documentation basis.
Borrower has rentals on both 1040 Schedule E and business return Form 8825: lender must obtain both returns.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Income with expiration must continue 3 years from note date
business_rules.description: When a qualifying income source has a defined expiration date or depends on depletion of an asset account/limited benefit, the lender must document that the income is expected to continue for at least three years from the note date. If an asset account is the sole or majority source of qualifying income, the lender must also assess the borrower’s ability to repay after the income ends or asset is depleted prior to loan maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Qualifying income has defined expiration or asset depletion/limited benefit
business_rules.consequences: If documented ≥3 years, income may be used; if not, income cannot qualify loan
business_rules.exceptions: If no defined expiration and required history is documented, no extra docs needed

source_reference.source_text: If the income source does have a deﬁned expiration date or is dependent on the depletion of an asset account or other limited beneﬁt, the lender must document that the income is expected to continue for at least three years from the note date. Additionally, when an asset account is the sole or majority source of qualifying income, the lender must assess the borrower’s ability to continue repaying the loan once the income source expires or the asset is depleted prior to loan maturity.

examples: Borrower receives contract income ending 24 months after note date; lender cannot use it unless documentation supports continuation ≥3 years from note date.
Borrower qualifies using asset depletion as majority income; lender must underwrite post-depletion repayment capacity before approving.
Borrower has ongoing salary with no stated end date and documented history per income type; lender is not expected to obtain extra continuance documentation absent contrary info.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Reward points must convert to cash before closing
business_rules.description: Credit card reward points are acceptable for closing costs, down payment, and financial reserves only if converted to cash prior to closing. If converted cash is deposited into the borrower’s depository account, no additional documentation is required unless the deposit is a large deposit; otherwise the lender must evidence availability and cash value prior to conversion and that conversion occurred prior to closing.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower uses credit card reward points as funds to close or reserves
business_rules.consequences: If not converted before closing or not documented, funds are ineligible and must be excluded
business_rules.exceptions: None (large deposit rules may add documentation)

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae permits credit card reward points as acceptable funds for use towards closing costs , down payment and ﬁnancial reserves, provided the reward points are converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan. The following requirements apply: If the credit card reward points are converted to cash and deposited into the borrower’s depository account (for example, checking or savings), no additional documentation is required unless the deposit is considered a large deposit. In this event, the lender must follow the requirements in Evaluating Large Deposits in B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts . If the credit card reward points are converted to cash, but not deposited into a borrower’s depository account, the lender must provide evidence the reward points were available to the borrower prior to the conversion, including veriﬁcation of the cash value (for example, credit card reward statement prior to conversion); and converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan.

examples: Borrower converts 120,000 points to $1,200 and deposits to checking 40 days before closing; lender accepts without extra documentation unless deposit meets 'large deposit' definition.
Borrower converts points to a prepaid card used at closing; lender must retain reward statement showing cash value and proof conversion occurred before closing.
Borrower plans to convert points at closing table; lender must require conversion prior to closing or exclude funds from assets/funds-to-close.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

dependencies.rationale: BR_040_006 prohibits "credit card financing" for down payment. BR_040_008 allows "credit card reward points" for down payment/closing costs/reserves if converted to cash pre-closing and appropriately evidenced. The apparent conflict must be resolved by classifying converted reward points as an eligible asset source distinct from borrowing/financing via credit card charges.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unresolved, lenders may either wrongly deny acceptable funds (reward points converted to cash) or wrongly allow prohibited financing (charging down payment to a card), both causing compliance and borrower experience issues.

business_rules.rule_name: Verifications must be requested from institution and returned direct
business_rules.description: The lender must attach a copy of the borrower-signed Blanket Authorization form to each Form 1005 or Form 1006 sent to a verifying institution. The information must be requested directly from the institution. The completed verification form(s) must be signed and dated and must be sent directly from the verifying institution.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using Form 1005/1006 to verify employment/income/assets
business_rules.consequences: Direct-source verifications are acceptable; indirect/undated forms are invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender attaches a copy of the Blanket Authorization form to each Form 1005 or Form 1006 sent to a verifying institution. The information must be requested directly from the institution. The completed form(s) must be signed and dated, and must be sent directly from the verifying institution.

examples: VOE: lender emails Form 1005 to employer HR with the blanket authorization attached; HR returns the signed/dated VOE directly to the lender.
Asset verification: bank returns a completed VOD directly to the lender; the lender does not accept a borrower-forwarded copy.
If the verification is missing a signature/date or was routed through the borrower, the lender must re-verify using direct-source methods.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC finding; underwriting condition to re-verify documentation

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_APPLICATION_DOCUMENTATION_008_002 requires obtaining the borrower’s signature on the Blanket Authorization to request creditworthiness documentation. BR_MORTGAGE_FILE_VALIDATION_008_003 then mandates that a copy of that borrower-signed authorization be attached to each Form 1005/1006 sent to verifying institutions. Therefore, the authorization is a hard prerequisite to compliant use of verification forms.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Blanket Authorization is not signed/obtained, any subsequent VOE/VOD requests using Forms 1005/1006 are noncompliant (missing required attachment/authorization), creating audit/QC defects and potentially forcing reverification and processing delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Jul 1 to Oct 14 missing return requires 4868 proof and IRS no-transcript
business_rules.description: For application dates July 1 through October 14 of the current year, the most recent year’s tax return is recommended but previous year(s) are acceptable. If the most recent year’s tax return is not obtained, the lender must: (1) obtain from the borrower a copy of IRS Form 4868, proof of e-filing Form 4868, or confirmation of electronic estimated tax payment(s); (2) review and compare the total tax liability reported/paid against the most recent year obtained to assess income stability; and (3) obtain an IRS response from filing IRS Form 4506-C confirming no transcripts are available for the applicable tax year (or, at lender discretion, borrower-provided evidence obtained directly from the IRS website).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when app date is Jul 1–Oct 14 and most recent return not obtained
business_rules.consequences: If all steps completed, alternative documentation satisfied; else file deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Alternative IRS no-transcript evidence allowed if obtained directly from IRS site

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Federal Income Tax Returns
source_reference.source_text: July 1, current year to October 14, current year... In the event the most recent year's tax return is not obtained, the lender must perform all of the following: Obtain one of the following documents... copy of IRS Form 4868... proof of the e-filing of Form 4868, or confirmation of electronic payment(s)... Review the total tax liability... and compare it with the borrower’s tax liability from the most recent year obtained... Obtain IRS response from the filing of IRS Form 4506–C confirming that no transcripts are available for the applicable tax year.

examples: Application 2026-08-10: borrower has not filed 2025 return; lender obtains 4868 proof, compares estimated tax liability to 2024, and documents IRS 'no transcript available' response via 4506-C.
Application 2026-07-15: lender uses IRS website printout (obtained directly) showing no transcript available instead of separate IRS response, per the allowed alternative.
Application 2026-09-25: lender collects 4868 but does not obtain 4506-C no-transcript confirmation; file remains noncompliant until completed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential post-purchase documentation defect

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Solar Panels Get No Value Unless UCC Search Confirms No Collateral Claim
business_rules.description: If documentation is insufficient and solar panel ownership status is unclear, the lender must ensure no value is attributed to the solar panels in the appraisal unless the lender obtains a UCC personal property search confirming the panels are not claimed as collateral by a non-mortgage lender.
business_rules.conditions: Solar ownership unclear due to insufficient documentation
business_rules.consequences: If UCC search not obtained, appraisal must not include solar value; violation risks value misstatement and delivery defects
business_rules.exceptions: If UCC search confirms no collateral claim, value may be considered

source_reference.source_text: If insuﬃcient documentation is available and the ownership status of the panels is unclear, no value for the panels may be attributed to the property value on the appraisal unless the lender obtains a Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) “personal property” search that conﬁrms the solar panels are not claimed as collateral by any non-mortgage lender.

examples: Borrower cannot provide solar financing/ownership documents; lender orders UCC personal property search showing no lien → appraiser may consider contributory value under standard requirements.
Borrower cannot document ownership and lender does not obtain UCC search → appraisal must show $0 contributory value for the solar array.
UCC search reveals a filed UCC-1 claiming the panels as collateral → appraiser must not include solar value and lender must underwrite debt accordingly.
Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home land equity uses 12-month lesser-of rule
business_rules.description: When borrower-owned land is credited toward the borrower’s minimum down payment/equity for a manufactured home that will be permanently attached, the land value that may be credited depends on the acquisition timing: (i) if the land was purchased more than 12 months before the loan application date, use the current appraised value; (ii) if the land was purchased 12 or fewer months before the application date, use the lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value and document the borrower’s cash investment in the land. If documentation/value requirements are not met, land equity may not be credited toward the minimum contribution/equity requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by manufactured home; borrower’s land equity credited to down payment
business_rules.consequences: If values/docs meet rule, land equity may be credited; if not, credit is disallowed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in table; other funds policies may apply to own-funds requirement

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Land value based on acquisition timing
source_reference.source_text: More than 12 months preceding the loan application.The current appraised value. None. 12 or fewer months preceding the date of the loan application.The lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value.The lender must document the borrower’s cash investment by obtaining: • a copy of the settlement statement, • a copy of the warranty deed that shows there are no outstanding liens against the property, or • a copy of the release of any prior liens(s). The borrower acquired the land at any time as a gift, inheritance, or other non-purchase transaction.The current appraised value.The lender must obtain appropriate documentation to verify the acquisition and transfer of ownership of the land.

More than 12 months preceding the loan application.The current appraised value. None. 12 or fewer months preceding the date of the loan application.The lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value.The lender must document the borrower’s cash investment by obtaining: • a copy of the settlement statement, • a copy of the warranty deed that shows there are no outstanding liens against the property, or • a copy of the release of any prior liens(s). The borrower acquired the land at any time as a gift, inheritance, or other non-purchase transaction.The current appraised value.The lender must obtain appropriate documentation to verify the acquisition and transfer of ownership of the land.

examples: Land bought 18 months before application; lender may credit current appraised land value toward down payment without the table’s additional lien documentation.
Land bought 6 months before application for $80,000 and appraises at $95,000; lender must use $80,000 and retain settlement statement plus deed showing no liens (or lien release).
Land inherited last year; lender may use current appraised value but must document inheritance/transfer into borrower ownership.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules implement the identical manufactured home land-equity credit logic for borrower minimum down payment/equity: if land purchased >12 months prior to application, use current appraised value; if purchased ≤12 months, use lesser of sales price or current appraised value and document cash investment. Threshold (12 months) and context (manufactured home permanently attached; borrower-owned land counted) align; consequences align (land equity can/cannot be credited). Only wording differs.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_VALIDATION_036_003 selected as primary because it is more clearly scoped to manufactured home guidance tables and more explicitly labels the rule as a manufactured home-specific validation requirement (improves graph placement under manufactured home domain).

business_rules.rule_name: Traded manufactured home requires lien search and proof of lien satisfaction
business_rules.description: For traded manufactured homes, a lien search must be completed in the appropriate real property and personal property records to verify ownership and determine whether existing liens are present on the manufactured home and land (including cases with separate liens). The seller of the new manufactured home must provide proof of title transfer and satisfaction of any existing liens on the traded manufactured home.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction includes trade-in (traded manufactured home)
business_rules.consequences: If lien search/proof obtained, trade equity may proceed; if not, trade cannot be relied on
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For traded manufactured homes, Fannie Mae requires a lien search in the appropriate real property and personal property records to verify ownership and to determine whether there are any existing liens on the manufactured home and land, or on the home and the land if they are encumbered by separate liens. The seller of the new manufactured home must provide proof of title transfer and satisfaction of any existing liens on the traded manufactured home.

examples: Borrower trades in a home titled as personal property; lender must obtain UCC/personal property lien search results showing lien status.
Borrower trades in home and separately financed land; lender must verify liens on both the home and the land where separate liens exist.
Dealer cannot provide proof that prior lien was satisfied; lender must not treat the trade as clear equity for down payment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: DU Rent History Requires 12 Months Renting and $300 Minimum Rent
business_rules.description: When rent payment history is used in DU for certain loan casefiles, at least one borrower must have been renting for at least 12 months with monthly rent of $300 or more and must have no mortgage on credit report, limited credit history, or no credit score. If rent history is not on the credit report, the lender must enter monthly rent in the online loan application, obtain a 12-month asset verification report through an authorized DU validation service vendor, and confirm the borrower is an account holder and the account is used to pay rent.
business_rules.conditions: DU casefile uses rent payment history from asset verification or credit report
business_rules.consequences: If met, DU can use rent history; if violated, rent history cannot be credited in DU
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The following requirements apply when using rent payment history in DU: At least one borrower must have been renting for at least 12 months with a monthly rent payment of $300 or more and one of the following: have no mortgage reported on their credit report, have a limited credit history, or have no credit score. When the rent payment history does not appear on the credit report, for DU to be able to identify rent payments using an asset veriﬁcation report, the lender must enter the monthly rent paid by the borrower in the online loan application, obtain an asset veriﬁcation report with 12 months of bank statement data through an authorized DU validation service asset veriﬁcation report vendor, and conﬁrm the borrower is an account holder and that the account provided in the asset veriﬁcation report is the one from which the borrower pays rent.

examples: Compliant: Borrower renting 18 months at $1,500/mo, no mortgage tradeline; lender inputs rent and obtains 12-month asset verification report → DU credits rent history.
Non-compliant: Borrower rent is $250/mo (below $300) → rent history cannot be used under this DU logic requirement.
Non-compliant: Rent not on credit report and lender fails to confirm borrower is account holder for the account used to pay rent → DU cannot identify rent payments from the report.
DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_011 applies "when rent payment history is used in DU" and requires specific borrower/rent conditions and potentially an asset verification report through DU validation vendors. That assumes DU underwriting is in play per BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not run or DU data-entry requirements are not followed, rent history may be improperly credited, changing risk findings and potentially producing unsupported Approve/Eligible decisions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_011 requires obtaining a 12-month asset verification report through an authorized DU validation service vendor when rent history is not on credit report. BR_037_008 requires investigating and resolving conflicting/contradictory information and confirming borrower ownership/name when an asset verification report is used (for employment validation). When the same asset verification report (or vendor ecosystem) supports DU validations, applying BR_037_008-style conflict/ownership checks strengthens and standardizes compliance for BR_011_011 implementations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_008-type conflict/ownership issues are not resolved, DU validation inputs (including rent history via asset verification) may be unreliable, increasing risk of incorrect DU findings and post-purchase validation failures.

business_rules.rule_name: Employment via asset report needs owner match and 30 or 60-day review
business_rules.description: When DU validates employment and an asset verification report is used for that employment validation, the lender must investigate and resolve conflicting or contradictory information. The lender must confirm the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reflects the borrower’s name as account holder. For this purpose, the lender need only review the report covering the 30- or 60-day period required for asset verification for the transaction type; if no asset verification is required for the transaction, the lender must review account activity covering the most recent 30-day period.
business_rules.conditions: When DU employment validation relies on an asset verification report
business_rules.consequences: If owner/name mismatch or unresolved conflicts exist, employment validation is not satisfied
business_rules.exceptions: If no asset verification is required, use most recent 30-day account activity period

source_reference.source_text: The lender must review the veriﬁcation report relied upon by DU... investigate and resolve any conﬂicting or contradictory information, and when an asset veriﬁcation report is used to validate employment, the lender must conﬁrm that the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reﬂects the borrower's name as account holder. The lender need only review the report covering the period of time (30 or 60 days) required for asset veriﬁcation for the transaction type, or if no asset veriﬁcation is required for the transaction, the account activity covering the most recent 30-day period

examples: Asset verification report lists borrower as joint account holder and shows payroll deposits; lender reviews last 60 days (required for transaction) → acceptable if no conflicts.
Report shows payroll deposits but account holder name is spouse only → lender must not treat as validated employment until borrower ownership confirmed/resolved.
Transaction type requires no asset verification; lender reviews most recent 30-day activity to confirm borrower is account holder for employment validation purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of validation relief; underwriting finding cannot be relied upon

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: Property insurance coverage must be 100% replacement cost or 80% test
business_rules.description: For a first mortgage secured by a one- to four-unit property, the lender/servicer must verify property insurance coverage is at least the lesser of: (i) 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the current policy effective date, or (ii) the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan, provided the UPB equals no less than 80% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the policy effective date. If coverage does not meet the minimum required, coverage that does provide the minimum required amount must be obtained.
business_rules.conditions: First mortgage secured by 1- to 4-unit property; policy coverage verified
business_rules.consequences: If minimum met, loan complies; if not met, coverage must be increased
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender or servicer must verify that the property insurance coverage amount for a ﬁrst mortgage secured by a one- to four-unit property is at least equal to the lesser of: 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the current property insurance policy eﬀective date, or the unpaid principal balance of the loan, provided it equals no less than 80% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the current property insurance policy eﬀective date. If the coverage amount does not meet the minimum required, coverage that does provide the minimum required amount must be obtained.

examples: Replacement cost is $500,000 and UPB is $350,000; 80% of replacement cost is $400,000, so required coverage is $400,000 (not $350,000).
Replacement cost is $300,000 and UPB is $260,000; 80% of replacement cost is $240,000, so required coverage is UPB $260,000 (since UPB ≥ 80% test).
Policy shows coverage $200,000 but required minimum is $240,000; lender must obtain increased coverage to at least $240,000.
Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op approval evidence required before purchase when membership is restricted
business_rules.description: If the purchaser’s right to co-op membership or occupancy is subject to the co-op corporation’s approval rights, the lender must furnish evidence clearly showing that such approval has been granted before Fannie Mae will purchase or securitize the co-op share loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when co-op docs require co-op corporation approval for membership/occupancy transfer.
business_rules.consequences: Without evidence of approval, Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize the share loan.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text:
If the purchaser’s
right to membership or occupancy is subject to any right of the co-op corporation to give approval, the lender
must furnish evidence to clearly show that such approval has been given before Fannie Mae will purchase or
securitize the co-op share loan.

examples: Co-op board approval is required by bylaws; lender includes the executed approval letter in the file before delivery: eligible for purchase.
Co-op board approval required but only a pending application is in file: not purchasable/securitizable until approval evidence is obtained.
Co-op documents contain no approval condition for transfer: this approval-evidence requirement does not apply.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit pre-purchase condition; no numeric threshold provided in source text.

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_DOCUMENTATION_006_004 requires assignment of proprietary lease/occupancy evidence, stock power, UCC perfection, and lien-position evidence. BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_VALIDATION_006_005 separately requires evidence that co-op corporation approval has been granted when approval rights exist. In practice, co-op delivery readiness requires assembling a complete co-op documentation set plus any required approval evidence; these controls complement each other as a combined purchase-readiness gate.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If one is satisfied and the other is missing, Fannie Mae may refuse to purchase/securitize or require suspense/cure, causing delivery delays and increased fallout risk (rate lock extensions, contract delays).

business_rules.rule_name: Comparable Distance Must Be Miles with Direction and Straight Line
business_rules.description: When describing the proximity of each comparable sale to the subject, the appraisal report must specify the distance in miles and include a directional indicator (e.g., “1.75 miles NW”). The distance between the subject property and each comparable must be measured using a straight line between the properties.
business_rules.conditions: Appraisal report describes proximity/location of comparable sales
business_rules.consequences: If distance method/format missing, lender must cure via appraisal update
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When describing the proximity of the comparable sale to the subject property, the appraiser must be speciﬁc with respect to the distance in terms of miles and include the applicable directional indicator (for example, “1.75 miles NW”). The distance between the subject property and each comparable property is to be measured using a straight line between the properties.

examples: Comparable described as “nearby” without mileage/direction; lender requests correction to include miles and direction.
Comparable distance shown as “2 miles” without direction; revised to “2.0 miles SE” to comply.
Comparable distance measured as driving miles; must be revised to straight-line measurement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit format and measurement method requirement.

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisal must use actual site size, not 5 of 40 acres
business_rules.description: The appraisal must include the actual size of the site for the subject property and cannot appraise a hypothetical portion of the site. If the property is an unsubdivided parcel, the appraised value must reflect the entire parcel area (e.g., may not appraise only 5 acres of an unsubdivided 40-acre parcel).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when subject property includes an unsubdivided parcel with larger total acreage.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: appraisal acceptable; Violated: appraisal revision/new appraisal required; delivery risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: The appraisal must include the actual size of the site and not a hypothetical portion of the site for the subject property. For example, the appraiser may not appraise only 5 acres of an unsubdivided 40–acre parcel. The appraised value must reﬂect the entire 40–acre parcel.

examples: Subject property is a single residence on an unsubdivided 12-acre parcel; appraisal must value the full 12 acres, not a 1-acre homesite.
Property includes 40 acres with no legal subdivision; appraiser values only 5 acres—lender must require correction because the value must reflect all 40 acres.
Property is legally subdivided and the loan is secured by a specific lot; appraisal uses the actual lot size for that legal parcel—requirement met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery suspended until appraisal corrected; possible repurchase if delivered
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct must language with explicit acreage example; strong validation rule for appraisal review.

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraised value must match 100% between UCDP appraisal and loan sale
business_rules.description: For loans that require an appraisal, the lender must ensure that the appraised value shown on the appraisal submitted to UCDP exactly matches the appraised value reported at sale of the loan. If the underwriting appraisal is a desk or field review of an existing appraisal (and thus cannot be submitted to UCDP), the appraised value at sale must reflect the value in the desk/field review; however, the original appraisal that was reviewed must have been submitted to UCDP.
business_rules.conditions: Loan requires appraisal; appraisal data submitted to UCDP; value reported at sale
business_rules.consequences: If values do not match (or missing required original UCDP submission), delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: Desk/field review appraisals cannot be submitted; sale value reflects review value

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.2-01, Appraised value consistency at UCDP and sale
source_reference.source_text: Additionally, for loans that require an appraisal, lenders must ensure the appraised value as indicated on the appraisal submitted to UCDP matches the appraised value as reported at sale of the loan. An exception is allowed for this requirement when the appraisal used to underwrite the loan is a desk or ﬁeld review of an existing appraisal because those types of reports cannot be submitted through the UCDP. In those instances, the appraised value reported at sale of the loan will reﬂect the value as stated in the desk or ﬁeld review. However, the original appraisal that was the subject of review must have been submitted to UCDP.

examples: UCDP appraisal value is $400,000 but loan delivery data reports $410,000: mismatch violates requirement; must correct delivery or resubmit correct appraisal.
Desk review concludes $395,000; desk review cannot be submitted to UCDP, so loan sale reports $395,000, and the original appraisal being reviewed was submitted to UCDP: meets exception handling.
Desk review used for underwriting but original appraisal was never submitted to UCDP: violates the ‘original appraisal must have been submitted’ requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: Lender submits appraisal file to UCDP and receives hard-stop messages.
Lender resubmits corrected appraisal after resolving UAD formatting errors.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_002 explicitly requires that the appraised value shown on the appraisal submitted to UCDP exactly matches the appraised value reported at sale. That validation is impossible unless the appraisal was submitted to UCDP with a Successful status per BR_031_005 (or required original submission where a desk/field review is used).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_005 fails, BR_013_002 cannot be satisfied; the loan sale/delivery will have a defect or be blocked until UCDP is Successful and values are aligned.

dependencies.rationale: In revised appraisal scenarios, BR_013_001 dictates that the final underwriting version must be submitted and Successful. BR_013_002 then requires the value at sale to match the UCDP-submitted appraisal value. Without BR_013_001, the UCDP record could reflect an earlier value, making BR_013_002 mismatches likely.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Mismatch between UCDP value and delivered value creates delivery defects, potential purchase delays, and required resubmissions/curative actions.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraiser License Must Be Active on Effective Date and Documented
business_rules.description: The lender (or its authorized agent) must use appraisers or supervisory appraisers that are state-licensed or state-certified, must document that the appraisers used are appropriately licensed/certified under state law, and must ensure the license/certification is active as of the effective date of the appraisal report. The appraiser must note their license or certification number on the appraisal report forms in compliance with UAD requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When selecting/using an appraiser for a Fannie Mae-eligible appraisal report
business_rules.consequences: If met, appraisal is compliant; if violated, appraisal is unacceptable for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (trainee rules addressed separately in same section)

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae requires a lender (or its authorized agent) to use appraisers or supervisory appraisers that are state-licensed or state-certified (in accordance with the provisions of Title XI of the Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989 and all applicable state laws). The lender (or its authorized agent) must document that the appraisers it uses are licensed or certified as appropriate under the applicable state law. The lender must ensure that the state license or state certification is active as of the effective date of the appraisal report. The appraiser must note their license or certification number on the individual appraisal report forms

examples: Appraisal effective date is 2026-04-01; appraiser license expired 2026-03-31—lender must not use this appraisal for delivery.
Lender’s file includes evidence of state certification status at engagement and confirms it was active on effective date—compliant.
Appraiser omits license number on the report; lender must obtain corrected report before delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal rejection; potential repurchase exposure if delivered noncompliant

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Requires Full Property Disclosure to Appraiser
business_rules.description: The lender must disclose to the appraiser all information about the subject property that the lender is aware of. The appraiser must then determine whether the disclosed information could affect the marketability of the property or the opinion of market value.
business_rules.conditions: When ordering/engaging an appraisal for a subject property
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser can analyze impacts; if violated: appraisal credibility and delivery risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: All information about the subject property the lender is aware of must be disclosed to the appraiser and the details provided must comply with B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices . The appraiser must determine if the information could aﬀect either the marketability of the property or the opinion of the market value of the property.

examples: Lender learns from agent about an unpermitted addition; lender discloses it to the appraiser for marketability/value consideration.
Borrower reports prior water intrusion; lender discloses to appraiser so condition and stigma can be analyzed.
Lender is aware of nearby environmental hazard (e.g., known contamination) and must disclose to appraiser.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase remedy (make-whole/repurchase) based on defect severity

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: Tri-merge credit report OK with 1 bureau data if requested
business_rules.description: For borrowers who have traditional credit, a credit report is acceptable even if one or two repositories do not contain any credit information, provided credit data is available from one repository and the lender requested a three in-file merged report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower has traditional credit but missing bureau data
business_rules.consequences: If met: credit report acceptable; if violated: follow nontraditional credit rules
business_rules.exceptions: If not met, refer to Nontraditional Credit History requirements

source_reference.source_text: If one or two of the credit repositories do not contain any credit information for the borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as credit data is available from one repository, and the lender requested a three in-ﬁle merged report. If the transaction does not meet the above requirements, refer to Section B3–5.4, Nontraditional Credit History...

examples: Borrower has credit at Experian only; lender requested tri-merge: report acceptable because 1 repository has credit data.
Borrower has credit at Equifax only; lender requested a single-bureau report: not acceptable under this rule; must meet nontraditional credit requirements.
Two borrowers: one has traditional credit at one bureau, other has full tri-merge; lender requested tri-merge: acceptable for the borrower with one bureau data.
Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

business_rules.rule_name: Post-closing QC requires new tri-merge 3-bureau credit report
business_rules.description: Post-closing QC must include reverification of the borrower’s credit history. If the borrower’s credit was evaluated using a traditional credit report, the lender must reverify by obtaining a new tri-merge credit report. The new report does not need to generate a new inquiry that will appear on future credit reports or include trended credit data, even if trended data was reflected on the underwriting report. The lender is not required to analyze trended credit data in the new report.
business_rules.conditions: When post-closing QC reviews a loan underwritten with a traditional credit report
business_rules.consequences: If new tri-merge is not obtained, QC reverification requirement is unmet and defect may be cited
business_rules.exceptions: No new inquiry required; no trended data required; no trended analysis required

source_reference.source_text: Credit History The post-closing QC review must include reveriﬁcation of the borrower's credit history. If a borrower's credit was evaluated by using a traditional credit report, the lender must reverify the borrower's credit history by obtaining a new tri-merge credit report. The new report does not need to generate a new inquiry... or include trended credit data... The lender is not required to analyze trended credit data in the new credit report.

examples: Underwriting used a tri-merge report dated pre-closing; QC obtains a new tri-merge report post-closing without a new inquiry → compliant.
QC file contains only the original underwriting credit report and no new tri-merge → reverification requirement not met; cite defect per QC taxonomy.
Original underwriting report included trended credit; QC’s new tri-merge omits trended data and lender does not analyze trends → compliant per rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC defect citation; corrective action plan; potential additional reporting/sanctions

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_001 establishes that post-closing QC reviews occur monthly and must complete within 90 days. BR_037_010 specifies that post-closing QC must include reverification of borrower credit history (new tri-merge) when traditional credit was used. Thus, performing BR_037_010 depends on running the QC cycle that selects and reviews loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If QC cycle is not operating timely, required credit reverification may be missed, producing QC defects and potential enforcement actions.

business_rules.rule_name: QC must confirm all DU verification messages are resolved and documented
business_rules.description: For loans underwritten through DU, the post-closing QC reviewer must confirm that all DU Verification Messages/Approval Conditions in the DU Underwriting Findings report were satisfactorily resolved and adequately supported by appropriate documentation. If DU returned an Ineligible recommendation, the reviewer must confirm the loan was eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during post-closing QC file review of DU-underwritten loans.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions not supported/resolved, loan may be ineligible and subject to remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligible may be acceptable only under specified DU circumstances (see B3-2).

source_reference.section_id: D1-3-02 (06/04/2025), Review of DU Findings and Conditions
source_reference.source_text: For loans underwritten through DU, the lender must conﬁrm that all DU Veriﬁcation Messages/Approval Conditions that appear in the DU Underwriting Findings report were satisfactorily resolved and adequately supported by appropriate documentation. If DU returned an Ineligible recommendation, the reviewer must conﬁrm that the loan was eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: DU requires verification of employment; QC file contains compliant VOE and condition is marked satisfied—rule met.
DU returns Ineligible due to data issue; QC reviewer confirms corrected eligibility basis and documentation supports delivery eligibility per applicable guidance.
QC finds DU verification message unresolved and no supporting documentation; loan is flagged for reassessment and potential self-report/remedy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase demand or other remedies

examples: QC review examines file for required appraisal exhibits and UCDP acceptance.
QC review checks file for borrower eligibility documentation and DU findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_001 applies during post-closing QC review of DU-underwritten loans (confirm DU conditions resolved/supported). BR_026_001 defines the monthly selection and 90-day completion requirements for the post-closing QC cycle. Without a functioning QC cycle, BR_019_001 cannot be systematically performed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Missing QC file review results in QC findings, potential delivery exposure if DU conditions were unresolved, and possible repurchase/indemnification risk.

business_rules.rule_name: QC review must reassess underwriting when verified data differs from underwriting data
business_rules.description: During post-closing QC, the lender must review the final terms of the loan to ensure they align with the data used to support underwriting decisions. If QC verification identifies discrepancies between underwriting data and QC-verified data, the lender must reassess the underwriting decision using the newly verified information to determine whether the loan remains eligible as delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when QC verifies data and finds discrepancies vs underwriting inputs/terms.
business_rules.consequences: Requires underwriting reassessment; may lead to ineligibility finding and self-report.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: The lender must review the ﬁnal terms of the loan to ensure they align with the data used to support the underwriting decisions. When a lender's review identiﬁes discrepancies between the data used in underwriting and the data veriﬁed through the QC process, the lender must reassess the underwriting decision based on the newly veriﬁed information to determine whether the loan remains eligible as delivered to Fannie Mae.

examples: QC verifies a higher monthly debt than underwritten; lender reassesses DTI and confirms whether loan remains eligible as delivered.
QC verifies occupancy intent documentation differs from application; lender reassesses eligibility and delivery status based on verified facts.
QC finds final loan terms differ from those used in underwriting; lender reassesses the underwriting decision using the final verified terms.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase demand or other remedies

examples: QC review examines file for required appraisal exhibits and UCDP acceptance.
QC review checks file for borrower eligibility documentation and DU findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_002 requires review of final terms vs underwriting data and reassessment if discrepancies are found. This is an activity inside the QC review process, which is organized by BR_026_001 (selection, review, rebuttal, reporting within 90 days).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If QC cycle is not run, discrepancies are not identified and underwriting is not reassessed, increasing undetected ineligible deliveries.

dependencies.rationale: In a DU post-closing QC review, confirming DU conditions are satisfied (BR_019_001) and confirming final terms align with underwriting inputs with reassessment if discrepancies exist (BR_019_002) together create a more complete QC validation. They should be navigated as linked checks within the same QC review workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one is performed, QC may miss either unfulfilled DU conditions or post-closing data drift that changes eligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: SBL property must conform to zoning and codes and Seller must confirm certificates of occupancy
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property must conform to all applicable zoning, subdivision and use laws/ordinances/codes and local building and housing codes (a zoning report is not required). The Seller must confirm that all required certificates of occupancy for the Property’s presently-contemplated use have been issued and are in effect, or if not available, that the absence is not a violation and will not give rise to enforcement action affecting the Property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL Mortgages at underwriting/delivery when zoning/CO compliance is assessed.
business_rules.consequences: If confirmed, eligibility condition satisfied; if not confirmed, Freddie Mac may restrict purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If Seller cannot confirm, Freddie Mac may purchase only if Non-Permitted Unit conditions are met.

source_reference.source_text: For an SBL Mortgage to be eligible for purchase by Freddie Mac, the Property securing it must conform to all applicable zoning, subdivision and use laws, ordinances or codes and local building and housing codes. A zoning report is not required for an SBL Mortgage. The Seller must confirm that all certificates of occupancy required for the use, operation and occupancy of the Property for its presently -contemplated use have been issued and are in effect or, if certificates of occupancy are not available, that the absence of any certificate of occupancy is not a violation of local laws, ordinance, or codes, nor will it give rise to any enforcement action affecting the Property.

examples: Seller obtains evidence that COs are issued for all units and confirms code conformity: requirement satisfied.
CO documentation is missing for certain units; Seller confirms absence is not a violation and no enforcement action risk: requirement satisfied.
Seller cannot confirm CO status for several units and cannot meet Non-Permitted Unit conditions: Freddie Mac may decline purchase.
Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Seismic risk consultant requires 3 lender references
business_rules.description: For Manufactured Housing Community (MHC) seismic risk consultants, the Seller/Servicer must review and verify credentials (including licensing, certifications, memberships, affiliations). For new seismic risk consultants, the Seller/Servicer must check at least three references from lenders that retained/employed the consultant. The Seller/Servicer must maintain a separate seismic risk consultant file for Freddie Mac that includes ongoing performance evaluations, the consultant’s current resume, required references, and current certificate(s) of liability insurance per Section 11.5.
business_rules.conditions: When using a seismic risk consultant for an MHC transaction
business_rules.consequences: If met: consultant eligible; if violated: Freddie may reject/condition file
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Seller/Servicer must review and verify the seismic risk consultant’s credentials, licensing, certifications, memberships and affiliations. For new seismic risk consultants, the Seller must check at least three references from lenders who have retained or employed the seismic risk consultant to sufficiently evaluate the seismic risk consultant’s capabilities and performance. The Seller must maintain a separate seismic risk consultant file for Freddie Mac’s use that includes the Seller’s ongoing evaluations of each seismic risk consultant’s performance, as well as the seismic risk consultant’s current resume, required references and current certificate(s) of liability insurance in accordance with the requirements of Section 11.5.

examples: New consultant proposed for an MHC SRA: Seller verifies licenses/certs and obtains 3 lender references before engagement.
Existing consultant: Seller updates file with current liability insurance certificates and ongoing performance evaluations.
Seller cannot produce the consultant’s references on request: Freddie Mac conditions the underwriting/delivery until provided.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / purchase ineligibility until cured
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'must' language with numeric 'at least three references'.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

business_rules.rule_name: Evidence of Critical Repair Completion Required Before Commitment
business_rules.description: The Seller must verify that all Critical Repairs have been addressed and must provide evidence of completion to Freddie Mac prior to Freddie Mac’s issuance of the Letter of Commitment or Freddie Mac’s acceptance of the early rate-lock application.
business_rules.conditions: When Critical Repairs are identified in the property condition report/Form 1105
business_rules.consequences: If evidence provided, Freddie Mac may proceed; if not, commitment/rate-lock acceptance is blocked
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Verify that all Critical Repairs have been addressed and provide evidence of completion to Freddie Mac prior to Freddie Mac’s issuance of the Letter of Commitment or acceptance of the early rate -lock application

examples: Critical Repair is a failed fire alarm panel; Seller obtains contractor completion certificate and provides it before commitment issuance.
Critical Repair is roof leak affecting units; Seller provides photos and paid invoice as evidence of completion prior to early rate-lock acceptance.
Critical Repair is suspected mold remediation; Seller provides mold remediator clearance documentation before Freddie Mac proceeds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac will not issue Letter of Commitment
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear timing requirement tied to commitment/rate-lock acceptance; no numeric threshold.

examples: Approved multifamily Seller/Servicer #12345 with delegated underwriting authority
Non-delegated Seller/Servicer using Freddie Mac underwriting review
Seller/Servicer under heightened monitoring due to QC findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_036_003 requires disclosure of Critical Repairs after receiving/reviewing Form 1105. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_036_004 requires verifying all Critical Repairs have been addressed and providing evidence prior to Letter of Commitment issuance or early rate-lock acceptance. The verification step cannot be scoped correctly unless the Critical Repairs have been identified and disclosed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_036_003 fails: (1) Freddie Mac may not be informed of repair conditions, (2) BR_036_004 evidence package may be incomplete/misaligned, (3) commitment/rate-lock acceptance can be delayed or later challenged, (4) heightened rep/warranty and reputational exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Transfer UCC search must be dated within 30 days and prior filings approved
business_rules.description: For a proposed Transfer of Ownership, the Servicer must obtain a statement from Servicer counsel confirming it examined the UCC search for the proposed transferee; the search must be dated no earlier than 30 days from the Transfer of Ownership; and the search must not indicate any previous filings, or if previous filings exist, those filings must have been approved by Freddie Mac.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering documentation for a proposed Transfer of Ownership
business_rules.consequences: If met, UCC search recency/filing status is validated; if violated, transfer documentation is deficient
