business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 9. Statement from the Servicer's counsel that it has examined the UCC search for the proposed transferee, that the search is dated no earlier than 30 days from the Transfer of Ownership and that the search does not indicate any previous filings (or, in the ca se of previous filings, that any such filings have been approved by Freddie Mac) (copy)

examples: Transfer closing date June 30: UCC search dated June 10 (20 days prior) is acceptable if no prior filings are shown.
Transfer closing date June 30: UCC search dated May 15 (46 days prior) is noncompliant because it is earlier than 30 days.
UCC search shows prior filings: acceptable only if documentation shows Freddie Mac approved those filings.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

dependencies.rationale: BR_SERVICER_VALIDATION_020_009 mandates counsel involvement (counsel statement examining UCC search). BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_035_003 ensures counsel is available from the commencement of consideration through key milestones. The documentation validation step depends on having counsel engaged/available to prepare and sign the required confirmation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_035_003 fails: (1) UCC search statement is delayed or missing, (2) Transfer of Ownership package is deficient, (3) Freddie Mac approval timeline slips, potentially delaying closing/assumption/transfer effective date.

dependencies.rationale: The transfer package requires a counsel statement (BR_020_009). Counsel eligibility (BR_029_001) is a foundational requirement for Freddie Mac acceptance of counsel work/product. Therefore, counsel must satisfy BR_029_001 for the BR_020_009 deliverable to be reliable and acceptable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_ELIGIBILITY_029_001 fails: (1) Freddie Mac may reject the counsel statement, (2) transfer approval may be withheld pending reissuance by qualified counsel, (3) transfer closing can be delayed or canceled.

business_rules.rule_name: Title update report must be dated no earlier than 45 days before request
business_rules.description: For a Subordinate Financing request package, the Servicer must obtain a title update report dated no earlier than 45 days before the date of the Subordinate Financing request (i.e., report age must be ≤ 45 days as of the request date).
business_rules.conditions: When compiling documentation for a Subordinate Financing request
business_rules.consequences: If older than 45 days, request package is deficient and may be rejected/delayed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 43 – Specific Servicing Responsibilities (Bulletin Update 02/24/26, Page 55, Item 15)
source_reference.source_text: 14. Current rent roll meeting the requirements set forth in Chapter 55 15. Title update report dated no earlier than 45 days before the date of the Subordinate Financing request 16. For all transactions in which the Borrower is a Cooperative (Co-op),

examples: Subordinate Financing request date is 2026-03-15; acceptable title update report date is 2026-01-29 or later (≤45 days old).
Request date is 2026-03-15 and report date is 2026-01-20 (54 days old); Servicer must order an updated report before submitting.
Request date is 2026-03-15 and report date is 2026-03-01 (14 days old); requirement is satisfied.
Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Inspection Must Assess 9 Property Risk Item Types
business_rules.description: When evaluating a property’s physical condition, the Servicer must assess the current condition and trend of all major components and must include specified risk/condition items (repair agreement items, operational repairs, deferred maintenance, life safety hazards, environmental and moisture/mold issues, capital improvements, status of prior deferred maintenance, and property manager concerns).
business_rules.conditions: When completing the physical condition portion of an inspection assessment
business_rules.consequences: If included, assessment meets required scope; if missing items, assessment is incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: In evaluating the Property's physical condition, the Servicer must assess the current condition and trend of all major components of the Property. The Servicer must include in its assessment: • Incomplete items in any Repair Agreement, • Items in any Repair Agreement that were completed subsequent to the previous inspection, • Incomplete Operational Repair items, • Deferred Maintenance items, • Imminent Life Safety Hazards and Potential Life Safety Hazards, as defined in the Glossary, • Environmental issues and any moisture or Mold concerns

examples: Inspection narrative addresses deferred maintenance, prior repair agreement completion status, and current mold concerns—compliant.
Servicer reports only aesthetic issues and omits life safety hazards review—noncompliant due to missing required assessment items.
Property manager reports elevator reliability concerns; servicer includes these concerns in the assessment—compliant.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'must' scope; numeric element is the enumerated item set rather than a threshold.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Noncompliance finding; require revised assessment submission

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Engineer certification required for structural or major system repairs
business_rules.description: The Servicer must cause a qualified engineer to certify repairs were completed in a good and workmanlike manner when: (i) improper work might contribute to material failure of any building component and/or physical deterioration; (ii) the repair is structural; or (iii) the repair is to major building systems (e.g., electrical, mechanical, fire protection). The engineer must meet Section 62.8 or Section 62SBL.17 qualifications (as applicable). If the Servicer has no qualified engineer on staff, the Servicer must retain a qualified third-party engineer; and when specialized knowledge is needed, retain an engineer with appropriate expertise.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when repairs pose material failure risk, are structural, or major systems
business_rules.consequences: If required engineer cert obtained, repair completion is properly documented; if not, Servicer is noncompliant and must remediate per Freddie Mac direction
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must cause a qualified engineer to certify that repairs have been completed in a good and workmanlike manner when any one of the following conditions is present: • The repair, if improperly done, might contribute to material failure of any building component and/or the physical deterioration of the facility • The repair is a structural repair • The repair consists of repair to major building systems (for example, electrical, mechanical, fire protection, etc.) The engineer must meet or exceed the qualification requirements in Section 62.8 or Section 62SBL.17, as applicable.

examples: A fire protection system repair is completed; Servicer must obtain a qualified engineer certification before treating the repair as satisfactorily completed.
A structural beam repair is performed; Servicer must retain a third-party qualified engineer if no qualified engineer is on staff.
Repairs to roofing that, if improperly done, could cause deterioration; Servicer must require an engineer certification due to material failure/deterioration risk.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'must cause' requirement with clear triggering conditions; qualifications referenced by section.

examples: Seller/Servicer acting as Servicer for Freddie Mac-owned loans
Subservicer performing collections and reporting under oversight
Servicer implementing a remediation plan after reporting errors

business_rules.rule_name: Foreign guarantor without U.S. guarantor must hold 5% liquidity and provide opinions at $40M/$50M
business_rules.description: If there is no additional financially compliant United States Guarantor and Freddie Mac approves a Foreign Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor must: (1) maintain minimum liquidity in one or more U.S. bank accounts of 5% of the original principal balance of the Mortgage (may be held in aggregate across multiple Foreign Guarantors) with banks acceptable to Freddie Mac; (2) for any Mortgage > $40 million, provide an additional non-consolidation opinion acceptable to Freddie Mac from the Foreign Guarantor’s country of citizenship/residency (written in English); and (3) for any Mortgage > $50 million, deliver an English opinion of counsel addressing enforceability of process agent appointment and procedures to collect/enforce U.S. judgments in the Foreign Guarantor’s country (counsel/firm acceptable to Freddie Mac).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac-approved Foreign Guarantor lacks an additional compliant U.S. guarantor.
business_rules.consequences: If unmet, Freddie Mac may deem structure noncompliant and require cure or deny purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Liquidity may be held in aggregate if more than one Foreign Guarantor.

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 9 (Foreign Guarantor requirements excerpt on Page 17)
source_reference.source_text: If there is no additional financially compliant United States Guarantor, the Foreign Guarantor will be required to: • Maintain a minimum Liquidity in one or more U.S. bank account(s) of five percent of the original principal balance of the Mortgage... • For any Mortgage greater than $40 million, provide an additional non -consolidation opinion... • For any Mortgage greater than $50 million, deliver an opinion of counsel... covering (i) the validity and enforceability of the form of appointment of the process agent... and (ii) the procedure for the collection and enforcement of any U.S. judgment...

examples: Mortgage OPB $30,000,000 with only foreign guarantor: must evidence ≥$1,500,000 liquidity in acceptable U.S. bank account(s).
Mortgage $45,000,000: must provide the additional non-consolidation opinion (English) from foreign jurisdiction counsel.
Mortgage $60,000,000: must provide both the >$40M non-consolidation opinion and the >$50M process-agent/judgment-enforcement opinion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase refusal or additional conditions imposed
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear numeric thresholds (5%, $40M, $50M); effective date inferred from bulletin header on the same chunk.

examples: Single-asset entity (SAE) borrower owning a 150-unit property
Limited partnership borrower with a managing member
Borrower under a loan modification during servicing

business_rules.rule_name: LBP sampling minimums vary at 100 units and 1.0 mg/cm2
business_rules.description: If LBP testing is performed, the environmental consultant must test a representative sampling of painted surfaces in units and common areas and may use XRF technology indicating lead exceeding 1.0 mg/cm2 or approved lab analysis of scraping samples. Minimum sample counts depend on property size: (1) For 100 or fewer units: 10 common area samples and unit samples from the greater of 10% of living units or 5 living units; in each living unit sampled, at least 3 LBP samples. (2) For more than 100 units: 10 common area samples and unit samples from the greater of 5% of living units or 10 living units; in each living unit sampled, at least 3 LBP samples.
business_rules.conditions: When an environmental consultant conducts LBP testing for a pre-1978 Property
business_rules.consequences: If met: LBP testing meets minimum standard; if violated: retest/revised report
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for minimum sample counts when testing is conducted

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 61 – Pages 20-21 (LBP protocol and minimum samples)
source_reference.source_text: • Use of X -ray fluoroscope (XRF) technology (which indicates a presence of lead exceeding 1.0 mg/cm2) The environmental consultant must test a representative sampling of painted surfaces of both the units and the common areas. The required minimum number of samples depends on the size of the Property: • For a Property with 100 or fewer units, the environmental consultant must take the following samples: o Ten common area samples o Samples from the greater of 10 percent of the living units or five living units (In each living unit sampled, the environmental consultant must take at least three LBP samples.) • For a Property with more than 100 units, the environmental consultant must take the following samples: o Ten common area samples o Samples from the greater of 5 percent of the living units or 10 living units

examples: 80-unit property: 10 common-area samples + units from greater of 10% (8 units) or 5 units → sample 8 units, 3 samples each (24 unit samples).
150-unit property: 10 common-area samples + units from greater of 5% (7.5→8 units) or 10 units → sample 10 units, 3 samples each (30 unit samples).
Consultant tests only 2 samples per unit: does not meet minimum 3 per unit; Freddie requires supplemental testing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery condition / require additional sampling and updated findings
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit numeric thresholds (100 units, 10%, 5%, 5/10 units, 3 per unit, 1.0 mg/cm2).

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Non-municipal Water Tests Must Be ≤180 Days Old
business_rules.description: If the Property is not serviced by a municipal water system, the environmental consultant must determine compliance with applicable federal, State, and local requirements, including reviewing test results that are no more than 180 days old at the time of the site visit. If compliance cannot be determined, the consultant must obtain and analyze drinking water samples including a first draw sample and compare to State standards (or Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards if no State program exists).
business_rules.conditions: Environmental assessment where Property is not served by municipal water
business_rules.consequences: If met, drinking water compliance is supported; if violated, additional testing is required before clearance
business_rules.exceptions: Municipal water system: testing is not required

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 61 – Environmental Requirements; Environmental assessment protocol (06/27/19)
source_reference.source_text: If the Property is not serviced by a municipal water system, the environmental consultant must determine if the drinking water is in compliance with applicable federal, State, and local requirements, which will include reviewing test results that are no mo re than 180 days old at the time of the site visit.   If the consultant cannot determine the compliance of the water system, then the environmental consultant must obtain and analyze drinking water samples for the Property that include a first draw sample.  The environmental consultant must test and compare these samples to State drinking water quality standards to determine whether compliance is currently achieved.  If no State program exists, Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards apply.

examples: A rural property on a private well provides lab tests dated 90 days before the site visit: consultant may use them to assess compliance.
Well-water tests are 220 days old at site visit: consultant must obtain new samples including a first draw sample.
A property in a state without its own drinking water program: consultant compares results to Federal Safe Drinking Water Act standards.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition / Freddie may require additional testing before purchase

examples: Environmental site assessment report used in underwriting
Property condition assessment report required for delivery
Engineering report triggering repair escrow requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Wood-Damaging Insect Report Dated Within 6 Months
business_rules.description: If the Property has wood framing or structural members, the file must include either (i) a wood-damaging insect inspection report stating there is no evidence of infestation, or (ii) a certification from the Property’s current pest control provider stating there is no evidence of infestation and that the Property is regularly inspected and/or treated. The report/certification must be dated within six months prior to submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac and comply with applicable federal, State, and local regulations. Freddie Mac will require a Repair Reserve for costs to repair any areas damaged by wood-damaging insects. Documentation is not required if all three conditions are met: contract in place, contract remains for term of Mortgage, and no evidence of wood damage per the Property Condition Report (if applicable).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Property has wood framing/structural members per PCR/PRR
business_rules.consequences: If missing/outdated, underwriting may be suspended; damage requires Repair Reserve
business_rules.exceptions: No doc required if contract in place + remains term + no wood damage per PCR

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8, Section 8.2(e) Wood-damaging insect inspection reports (12/17/19)
source_reference.source_text: For any Property with wood framing or structural members as described above, the following documentation must be provided: • A wood -damaging insect inspection report stating that there is no evidence of wood -damaging insect infestation, or • Certification from the Property’s current pest control provider stating that there is no evidence of wood -damaging insect infestation and that the Property is regularly inspected and/or treated to prevent wood -damaging insect infestation . The wood -damaging insect inspection report or the certification from the Property’s current pest control provider must be dated within six months prior to the date of the submission of the full underwriting package to Freddie Mac and must be in compliance with all applicable federal, State and local regulations. Freddie Mac will require a Repair Reserve for the costs to repair any areas damaged by wood -damaging insects. Notwithstanding the above, the documentation listed above is not required if the following three conditions are satisfied: • The Borrower provides documentation confirming that there is a wood -damaging insect contract in place for the Property , • A wood -damaging insect contract(s) will remain in place for the term of the Mortgage, and • There is no evidence of wood damage per the Property Condition Report (if applicable) .

examples: A wood-framed property submits a termite inspection report dated 4 months before underwriting package submission; requirement is satisfied.
A wood-framed property submits a pest provider certification dated 9 months before submission; requirement fails due to the 6-month dating limit.
A property has a documented pest contract that will remain for the full term and the PCR shows no wood damage; inspection report may be omitted.
200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Elevated Seismic Regions Require Level 1 SRA If Any Factor Exists
business_rules.description: If the Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (excluding MHC Property), the Seller/Servicer must evaluate the Property for listed seismic risk factors (including: building within 50 feet of a mapped earthquake fault trace or within an Alquist-Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone in California; high-rise buildings 8 stories or greater; reinforced concrete or CMU bearing wall buildings constructed prior to 2000; wood-framed buildings constructed prior to 1960; and other listed factors). If none of the listed factors are present, a Level 0 SRA is required. If any factor is present—or if the Seller/Servicer cannot conclusively determine none are present—a Level 1 SRA is required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region (non-MHC)
business_rules.consequences: If correct SRA level obtained, seismic risk assessment is compliant; if not, underwriting package is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to Mortgage secured by an MHC Property

source_reference.source_text: If the Property is located in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region, the Seller/Servicer must evaluate the Property for the following seismic risk factors: 1. A building located within 50 feet of a mapped earthquake fault trace or located within an Alquist -Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone

examples: Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and includes an 11-story tower (≥8 stories); Seller/Servicer must require a Level 1 SRA.
Property is in an Elevated Seismic Hazard Region and documentation confirms none of the listed risk factors apply; Seller/Servicer obtains a Level 0 SRA.
Seller/Servicer cannot conclusively verify whether the site is within 50 feet of a mapped fault trace; Level 1 SRA is required due to inability to conclude 'none present'.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or purchase delay pending SRA completion
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit decision logic for Level 0 vs Level 1 SRA plus multiple numeric triggers (50 feet, 8 stories, pre-2000, pre-1960).

examples: 200-unit garden-style apartment community in Texas
Mixed-use property with residential units above retail space
Affordable housing property with regulatory restrictions

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning Opinion Required When Loan 20MM or 25MM and No Zoning Report
business_rules.description: When a third-party zoning consulting report is not submitted because the Mortgage is (i) a non-SBL Mortgage with an initial principal balance of $20 million or less, or (ii) a Supplemental Mortgage where the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage plus unpaid principal balances of senior Mortgages is $25 million or less in the aggregate, and the Mortgage is not a Moderate Rehabilitation, Lease-up, Value-add, or Forward Commitment Mortgage, the appraiser must include an opinion as to the legality of the Property’s zoning (Legal, Legally non-conforming, Illegal, or Other with explanation). The preferred method is quoting or referencing an appropriate local zoning office official/employee; without such quote/reference, the appraiser must provide a professional opinion and cannot merely state they are not experts or that the client should consult a legal expert.
business_rules.conditions: When no zoning report is submitted and Mortgage fits $20MM/$25MM criteria
business_rules.consequences: If met: underwriting has zoning legality; if violated: appraisal deficiency/revision
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply if Mortgage is Moderate Rehab/Lease-up/Value-add/Forward Commit

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 60 f. Zoning and other legal issues (06/25/20)
source_reference.source_text: If a Mortgage meets either of the following conditions, a third -party zoning consulting report might not be part of the underwriting package... o Is a non -SBL Mortgage with an initial principal balance of $20 million or less, or is a Supplemental Mortgage and the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage and the unpaid principal balances of any senior Mortgages encumbering the Property are $25 million or less in the aggregate, and o Is not a Moderate Rehabilitation Mortgage, a Lease -up Mortgage, a Value -add Mortgage or a Forward Commitment Mortgage... the appraiser must include an opinion as to the legality of the zoning of the Property... The appraiser cannot merely state that they are not experts in this field or that the client should consult a legal expert.

examples: Non-SBL $18,000,000 refinance with no zoning report: appraisal must state zoning is legal/legally non-conforming/illegal and support it.
Supplemental Mortgage $5,000,000 with $19,000,000 senior UPB (combined $24,000,000): zoning opinion is required if no zoning report is submitted.
Lease-up Mortgage $15,000,000 with no zoning report: this exception applies; the special $20MM condition does not trigger the same instruction.
MAI appraisal supporting a $35M value conclusion
Update appraisal required due to aging report date at closing
Appraisal with extraordinary assumptions disclosed

business_rules.rule_name: Partial release title update must be dated within 45 days of Form 1125
business_rules.description: For partial releases requiring Freddie Mac approval, the Servicer must deliver a title update report dated not earlier than 45 days before the date of Form 1125 (Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitting partial release request package requiring Freddie Mac approval
business_rules.consequences: If title update is older than 45 days, submission is incomplete and may be rejected/delayed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 43 partial release request items (02/24/26), item 5
source_reference.source_text: The Servicer must deliver each of the following documents: 1. A completed Property Partial Release Request , which must include the Servicer's analysis along with the Servicer's recommendation for action 2. Form 1125 , Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement, completed and signed by the Borrower and any Guarantor(s) ... 5. Title update report dated not earlier than 45 days before the date of Form 1125, Borrower Application for Partial Release or Easement

examples: Form 1125 dated 07/15 → title update must be dated 06/01 or later (within 45 days).
Form 1125 dated 11/30 → title update dated 09/30 is too old (>45 days) and must be refreshed.
Servicer submits a 60-day-old title update → Freddie Mac requests re-delivery before review can proceed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Request for additional documentation; processing delay

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

business_rules.rule_name: Full underwriting unit inspections 80% or 10% with caps
business_rules.description: At full underwriting, the Seller must conduct a complete property inspection and unit sampling based on property size. For properties with 30 units or less: inspect 80% of units, with no fewer than 5 and no more than 15 (excluding Down Units and commercial units), plus inspect all Down Units and all commercial units. For properties with greater than 30 units: inspect 10% of units, with no fewer than 10 and no more than 30 (excluding Down Units and commercial units), plus inspect all Down Units and all commercial units. In both cases, at least 50% of inspected units must be occupied, and decimals may be truncated provided the minimum is met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at full underwriting when Seller performs complete property inspection.
business_rules.consequences: Compliant sampling supports underwriting; noncompliance requires re-inspection or underwriting delay.
business_rules.exceptions: If lease audit needs more units, additional units must be inspected.

source_reference.section_id: 8.15(b)(1) Complete property inspection and lease audit (04/21/26)
source_reference.source_text: At full underwriting, in addition to conducting the inspection requirements specified in Section 8.15(a) above, the Seller must conduct a complete property inspection including, but not limited to , the following : 1. The unit inspection must include the following : a. For Properties with 30 units or less: (i) 80 percent of units, with no fewer than 5

examples: 24-unit property: 80% = 19.2 → 19 units (truncated) but capped at 15; Seller inspects 15 regular units + all Down Units + all commercial units; at least 8 of the 15 are occupied (≥50%).
120-unit property: 10% = 12 units; Seller inspects 12 regular units (between 10 and 30) + all Down Units + all commercial units; at least 6 of the 12 are occupied.
31-unit property where 10% = 3.1 → minimum 10 applies; Seller must inspect 10 regular units + all Down Units + all commercial units.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; delivery suspension until corrected

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

dependencies.rationale: Operationally, teams may first apply the basic sampling thresholds (excluding Down/commercial from sample sizing) and must then apply the validation augmentation to ensure Down/commercial units are also inspected. This functions as a 'more complete' rule that supersedes a partial reading of the constraint.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only BR_UNDERWRITES_CONSTRAINT_004_002 is followed, Down Units and commercial units may be omitted from inspection, causing noncompliance and requiring re-inspection or underwriting delay.

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Permitted units must be excluded from income, unit count, and occupancy tests while expenses included
business_rules.description: If the Seller cannot confirm required certificates of occupancy for one or more units (Non-Permitted Units), Freddie Mac may purchase the SBL Mortgage only if underwriting excludes the gross potential rent (GPR) from any Non-Permitted Unit and excludes such units from total unit count; underwriting must include all expenses attributable to all Non-Permitted Units; and Non-Permitted Units must be excluded from determining whether minimum occupancy requirements are satisfied.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CO confirmation cannot be obtained for one or more units on an SBL Property.
business_rules.consequences: If applied, Freddie Mac may purchase subject to conditions; if not, purchase may be declined.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: The underwritten income from the Property must exclude the gross potential rent (GPR) for any Non -Permitted Unit and the total unit count must not include the Non -Permitted Unit. The underwritten expenses for the Property (including management fee and Replacement Reserves) must include all expenses attributable to all Non -Permitted Units. The Non -Permitted Units must be excluded from any determination of whether minimum occupancy requirements are satisfied.

examples: Two basement units lack CO: underwriting removes their GPR and reduces unit count by 2, but still includes their utilities/maintenance expenses.
Occupancy test is calculated using only permitted units; Non-Permitted Units are excluded from the denominator and numerator.
Seller keeps GPR from a Non-Permitted Unit in NOI: violates rule and may render loan ineligible for Freddie Mac purchase.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Leases must be validated to tenant payment docs and discrepancies explained
business_rules.description: Leases must be validated against documentation reflecting actual rental payments received by the respective tenant (e.g., tenant ledger, general ledger, copies of checks, or similar documentation). If discrepancies are identified, the applicable comments field must be used to provide an explanation (e.g., governmental subsidy such as Section 8/Medicaid, or ledgers reflecting additional payments like late fees).
business_rules.conditions: Applies during SBL lease audit when verifying lease terms versus payments
business_rules.consequences: Met: lease audit supports underwriting; Violated: data inconsistency and rework risk
business_rules.exceptions: Discrepancies allowed only if explained in comments (e.g., subsidy/late fees)

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8SBL – SBL Property Fundamentals, lease validation
source_reference.source_text: o Leases must also be validated against documentation reflecting actual rental payments received by the respective tenant, such as a tenant ledger, general ledger, copies of checks, or similar documentation. If discrepancies are identified, the applicable comments field must be used to provide an explanation, such as when a portion of the rental payment is in the form of a governmental subsidy (e.g., Section 8 or Medicaid) or when the ledger reflects additional payments (e.g., late fees or other one -time charges).

examples: Lease shows $1,500 rent; tenant ledger shows $1,500 paid monthly—validation passes with no comments.
Lease shows $1,200 tenant portion but ledger shows $1,800; comments explain $600 Section 8 subsidy—discrepancy is documented.
Ledger shows $1,550 against a $1,500 lease; comments explain $50 late fee—variance is accounted for.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

dependencies.rationale: Validation against tenant payment documentation is part of performing the lease audit. When the audit must occur at the site inspection (on-site files), the practical sequence is: conduct lease audit at inspection → validate leases against payment evidence → document discrepancies/comments.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the audit is not conducted at the time required, validation may be delayed or performed without proper access to on-site files, increasing rework risk and potentially requiring a return site visit.

business_rules.rule_name: TEL Rate Lock Requires Rent Schedule Dated Within 30 Days
business_rules.description: Prior to locking the rate for a TEL Mortgage, the Seller must deliver a rent schedule meeting Guide requirements that is dated no more than 30 days prior to the date of Rate Lock. This item must be in a form acceptable to Freddie Mac as part of the conditions precedent to Rate Lock.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to TEL Mortgages prior to Rate Lock; rent schedule must be Guide-compliant
business_rules.consequences: If dated within 30 days, rate lock condition satisfied; if older, rate lock blocked
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 25 – Conditions Precedent to Rate Lock (08/17/23)
source_reference.source_text: Prior to locking the rate, Seller must deliver each of the following items in form acceptable to Freddie Mac: • Final payment and performance bonds meeting the requirements of Section 19.2(e)(14) • A proforma rehabilitation escrow fund draw schedule • A rent schedule meeting the requirements of this Guide dated no more than 30 days prior to the date of Rate Lock • Form 1133, Certification of Borrower Insurance Compliance and evidence of property insurance for the Property meeting the requirements of this Guide

examples: Rate lock date is 2026-03-15; rent schedule dated 2026-03-01 is acceptable (14 days old).
Rate lock date is 2026-03-15; rent schedule dated 2026-02-01 is not acceptable (42 days old) and must be updated.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 30-day recency limit stated for rent schedule prior to rate lock.

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

business_rules.rule_name: Zoning Minimums plus 20M/25M No-Zoning-Report Thresholds
business_rules.description: For all mortgages, the appraiser must consider, support, and discuss how zoning and other legal issues affect property value, and must reference the authoritative zoning source in the appraisal while addressing at minimum: parking ratio compliance, density compliance, and rebuildability restrictions after substantial damage/casualty loss. Additionally, when the mortgage is a non-SBL mortgage with initial principal balance of $20,000,000 or less, or is a Supplemental Mortgage where combined initial principal balance plus UPBs of any senior mortgages encumbering the property are $25,000,000 or less in the aggregate, and the mortgage is not a Moderate Rehabilitation, Lease-up, Value-add, or Forward Commitment mortgage, a third-party zoning consulting report might not be part of the underwriting package—so the underwriter and appraiser must ensure pertinent zoning compliance information is included and its effect on market value is addressed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to appraisals for all mortgages; includes special threshold scenarios for zoning report omission
business_rules.consequences: If zoning source and minimum items absent, appraisal is deficient; may delay underwriting/delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Special threshold scenario applies only if loan is not Moderate Rehab, Lease-up, Value-add, Forward Commitment

source_reference.source_text: 1. For all Mortgages, the appraiser must consider, support, and discuss how zoning and other legal issues (including shared access agreements, easements, and compliance with local rent control statutes) affect the value of the Property. The appraiser must, reference the authoritative zoning source in the Appraisal, comparing the Property to competing properties, and addressing at a minimum: • Parking ratio compliance • Density compliance • Rebuildability restrictions in the event of substantial damage or casualty loss to the Property. 2. If a Mortgage meets either of the following conditions, a third -party zoning consulting report might not be part of the underwriting package... • The Mortgage o Is a non -SBL Mortgage with an initial principal balance of $20 million or less, or is a Supplemental Mortgage and the combined initial principal balance of the Supplemental Mortgage and the unpaid principal balances of any senior Mortgages encumbering the Property are $25 million or less in the aggregate, and o Is not a Moderate Rehabilitation Mortgage, a Lease -up Mortgage, a Value -add Mortgage or a Forward Commitment Mortgage.

examples: Non-SBL mortgage with initial principal balance $18,000,000; no third-party zoning report is included; appraisal cites zoning code source and addresses parking, density, rebuildability—compliant.

Supplemental mortgage initial principal balance $5,000,000 with seniors totaling $19,000,000 (aggregate $24,000,000); appraisal must still include authoritative zoning source and minimum zoning items—compliant even without zoning consultant report.
Value-add mortgage with initial principal balance $15,000,000; the threshold carve-out does not apply, and underwriting may still require a zoning consulting report per full Guide requirements.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Includes explicit minimum zoning discussion items and exact $20M/$25M threshold scenario language.

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Restricted rent roll source and continuation analysis required
business_rules.description: When restricted rents apply, the appraiser must document the source of the Property’s restricted rent roll and provide adequate support, analysis, and discussion for continuation of restricted rents. If the appraiser concludes restricted rents will expire or not continue, the appraiser must use appropriate valuation methodology considering the likelihood of restricted rents expiring.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when appraisal relies on restricted rent roll and restricted rent continuation
business_rules.consequences: If source and continuation analysis documented, rent inputs are supportable; if not, appraisal is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 60 – Restricted rents documentation requirement (excerpted paragraph)
source_reference.source_text: In the Appraisal, the appraiser must document the source of the Property’s restricted rent roll and provide adequate support, analysis and discussion for the continuation of the restricted rents. If the appraiser concludes that the restricted rents will e xpire or not continue, the appraiser must use the appropriate methodology to value the Property considering the likelihood of the restricted rents expiring.

examples: Appraisal cites the housing agency’s regulatory agreement and current restricted rent roll extract as the source, and explains why restrictions are expected to continue through the loan term.
Appraiser believes restrictions may sunset: appraisal switches to a methodology that weights probability of expiration and reflects transition risk in value.
Appraisal uses restricted rents but fails to identify the rent roll source; lender must obtain a corrected appraisal with source documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/underwriting suspension until compliant appraisal provided

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Guarantor Credit Reports Must Be Current Within 60 Days of Delivery
business_rules.description: For each Guarantor who is an individual, the Seller must provide to Freddie Mac a current credit report that is issued by an independent credit reporting agency acceptable to Freddie Mac, is dated within 60 days before delivery to Freddie Mac, and includes FICO scores. The subject of each report must have authorized the Seller to obtain the report, and the report must be reviewed by the Seller and verify debts listed on the financial statement submitted with the full underwriting package. Credit reports are not required for entities or foreign sponsors with no Social Security number.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to mortgage delivery when any Guarantor is an individual person
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, delivery credit documentation is acceptable; if noncompliant, delivery may be rejected/cured
business_rules.exceptions: No report required for entities or foreign sponsors with no Social Security number

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 55 – Documentation and Deliveries (Credit reports)
source_reference.source_text: Credit reports The Seller must provide to Freddie Mac a current credit report on each Guarantor that is an individual. A credit report is not required for entities or foreign sponsors with no Social Security number. The subject of each report must have authorized the Seller to obtain the report and the report must : 1. Be reviewed by the Seller 2. Be issued by an independent credit reporting agency acceptable to Freddie Mac 3. Be dated within 60 days before delivery to Freddie Mac ... 7. Include FICO® scores

examples: Two individual guarantors sign authorization; Seller orders independent bureau reports dated 45 days before delivery and includes FICO scores—requirement satisfied.
Credit report is dated 75 days before delivery to Freddie Mac; Seller must obtain an updated credit report dated within 60 days.
Guarantor is a foreign sponsor with no Social Security number; credit report is not required under this provision.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or purchase delay pending updated credit report
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit recency threshold (60 days) and party scope (individual guarantors) with a clear exception.

examples: Mortgage requires credit reports for key principals of the borrowing entity
Mortgage requires updated credit report due to elapsed time before closing
Mortgage requires additional credit documentation after derogatory findings

dependencies.rationale: Both rules govern providing current credit reports for individual guarantors with timing and content expectations. BR_REQUIRES_CREDIT_REPORT_VALIDATION_039_010 is explicitly scoped to SBL deliveries and specifies that credit reports are not required for entities or foreign sponsors with no SSN, which can be read as a modification of the general delivery credit report requirement. Workflow engines should select the SBL-specific rule when SBL context is true to avoid over-collecting documents and raising false defects.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is not applied, a lender may incorrectly require credit reports for foreign sponsors with no SSN (contrary to 039_010) or apply mismatched checklists, causing unnecessary conditions, processing delays, or incorrect defect flags.

business_rules.rule_name: Guarantor credit report must be dated within 60 days before delivery
business_rules.description: For SBL deliveries, the Seller must provide a current credit report on each Guarantor that is an individual; credit reports are not required for entities or foreign sponsors with no Social Security number. Each report must be reviewed by the Seller, issued by an independent credit reporting agency acceptable to Freddie Mac, dated within 60 days before delivery to Freddie Mac, verify debts listed on the submitted financial statement (including terms, balances, and ratings), list other debts and legal actions from public records searches, and include FICO scores for Borrowers and Guarantors.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBL underwriting/delivery when a Guarantor is an individual
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, delivery package satisfies credit documentation; if not, delivery condition/defect
business_rules.exceptions: No credit report required for entities or foreign sponsors with no SSN

source_reference.source_text: Credit reports The Seller must provide to Freddie Mac a current credit report on each Guarantor that is an individual. A credit report is not required for entities or foreign sponsors with no Social Security number. The subject of each report must have authorized the Seller to obtain the report and the report must 1. Be reviewed by the Seller 2. Be issued by an independent credit reporting agency acceptable to Freddie Mac 3. Be dated within 60 days before delivery to Freddie Mac 4. Verify debts listed on the financial statement submitted with the full underwriting package, including terms, balances and ratings 5. List any other debts 6. List all legal actions that involve the Borrower or Guarantor and are disclosed by a search of public records 7. Include FICO scores for Borrowers and Guarantors

examples: Individual guarantor report dated 45 days before delivery, includes FICO and public records: compliant.
Individual guarantor report dated 75 days before delivery: not compliant; must refresh to within 60 days.
Foreign sponsor entity with no SSN: credit report is not required under this rule.
Mortgage requires credit reports for key principals of the borrowing entity
Mortgage requires updated credit report due to elapsed time before closing
Mortgage requires additional credit documentation after derogatory findings

business_rules.rule_name: Allonge must be permanently affixed and identify borrower, date, amount, and property address
business_rules.description: If an allonge is used for endorsement, the endorsement must appear on the note and the allonge must comply with law, be permanently affixed to the note, clearly identify the note by referencing at least borrower name(s), note date, note amount, and security property address, and the note must clearly reference the attached allonge. The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses incurred as a result of using an allonge for note endorsement(s).
business_rules.conditions: Allonge used to endorse note delivered to Fannie Mae; enforceability must be preserved
business_rules.consequences: If allonge not compliant/affixed/referenced: endorsement defect; indemnification for losses applies
business_rules.exceptions: Subsequent endorsements may be placed on allonge but are not required

source_reference.section_id: B8-3-04, Using an Allonge for the Endorsement
source_reference.source_text: An allonge may be used for the endorsement as long as the following requirements are met: The allonge must be permanently aﬃxed to the related note and must clearly identify the note by referencing at least the name of the borrower(s), the date of the note, the amount of the note, and the address of the security property. The note must clearly reference the attached allonge... The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae... for any losses incurred by Fannie Mae as a result of the use of an allonge for the note endorsement(s).

examples: Allonge is stapled/glued to the note and references borrower names, note date, note amount, and property address: acceptable.
Allonge is loose in the file and note does not reference it: fails permanent affix/reference requirements and creates enforceability risk.
Allonge used and later causes loss due to improper endorsement: lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses tied to allonge use.
Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_008 is an allonge-specific rule that only matters when the note endorsement(s) required by BR_039_007 are placed on an allonge. Therefore BR_039_007’s endorsement requirement is the trigger/context that makes BR_039_008 applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_007 endorsements are implemented through an allonge but BR_039_008 requirements are not met (not affixed, not referenced, incomplete identification), the endorsement chain is defective and the note may be rejected by custodian/Fannie Mae; indemnification exposure may apply.

provenance.reasoning: Both regulate acceptable Note endorsement to the GSE, allowing an attached allonge and aiming to preserve enforceability/complete endorsement chain. Freddie adds specific endorsement wording, loan number on allonge, and 'must not be dated'; Fannie adds detailed note-identification elements and indemnification.

g1_rule.rule_name: Allonge must be permanently affixed and identify borrower, date, amount, and property address
g1_rule.description: If an allonge is used for endorsement, the endorsement must appear on the note and the allonge must comply with law, be permanently affixed to the note, clearly identify the note by referencing at least borrower name(s), note date, note amount, and security property address, and the note must clearly reference the attached allonge. The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses incurred as a result of using an allonge for note endorsement(s).

g2_rule.rule_name: Note endorsement must use Freddie Mac form and must not be dated
g2_rule.description: The Note must bear an endorsement to Freddie Mac in the specified ‘PAY TO THE ORDER OF THE FEDERAL HOME LOAN MORTGAGE CORPORATION WITHOUT RECOURSE’ form, signed by a duly authorized officer (with typed name and title). The endorsement must not be dated. The endorsement may be on an allonge if the Freddie Mac loan number appears on the allonge and the allonge is attached to the Note. Any chain of endorsements must be complete from the original lender shown on the Note to Freddie Mac.

business_rules.rule_name: Co-op share loan must include lease assignment, stock power, and UCC filings
business_rules.description: For all co-op share loans delivered to Fannie Mae, the lender must receive an assignment of the proprietary lease/occupancy agreement (or similar evidence of occupancy rights), obtain a stock power/assignment authorizing transfer upon default, and execute/file valid UCC financing statements (and assignments, if needed) to perfect the security interest. The lender must obtain information searches/equivalent evidence showing the Fannie Mae co-op share loan is in first-lien position; if co-op units are real property in the state, lien perfection must follow applicable real estate law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to delivery of any co-op share loan secured by shares and leasehold rights.
business_rules.consequences: Missing documents or unperfected/other-than-first-lien status makes loan ineligible or subject to repurchase.
business_rules.exceptions: If state treats co-op units as real property, perfection follows state real estate lien law.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.3-03, Lien Position for Co-op Share Loans
source_reference.source_text:
The lender that is ﬁnancing the share loan must receive an assignment of the proprietary lease, occupancy
agreement, or other similar evidence of the right to occupy the unit for all share loans that it delivers to Fannie
Mae. The lender must also obtain a stock power, assignment, or other similar document that authorizes the
lender to transfer ownership interest in the event of a default. Valid ﬁnancing statements and assignments of
ﬁnancing statements must be executed and ﬁled, if necessary to perfect Fannie Mae’s security interest under
the Uniform Commercial Code of the state in which the property is located. Information searches or equivalent
evidence of ﬁling ﬁnancing statements and assignments of ﬁnancing statements must be obtained and must
show that the Fannie Mae co-op share loan is in ﬁrst-lien position.

examples: Lender delivers co-op share loan with executed proprietary lease assignment and filed UCC-1 showing first-lien position: meets documentation requirements.
UCC filing was never recorded and lien priority cannot be confirmed via search: loan is not deliverable until cured; may be subject to remedies if delivered.
State treats co-op as real property; lender records appropriate lien instrument per state real estate law instead of (or in addition to) UCC filing: acceptable if it perfects first lien.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit 'must' documentation steps; primary eligibility impact though few numerics.

examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_EXCEPTION_006_003 sets the eligibility requirement that a co-op share loan must be first-lien, with limited permitted subordination to the co-op corporation’s lien for specified unpaid assessments. BR_CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT_DOCUMENTATION_006_004 requires UCC filings and information searches/evidence showing the co-op share loan is in first-lien position, operationalizing and evidencing compliance with the lien-position eligibility requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If lien-position evidence and perfection documents are missing, the lender cannot demonstrate compliance with the first-lien requirement; the loan may be deemed ineligible or subject to repurchase for a documentation/perfection defect.

provenance.reasoning: Both address ensuring/perfecting a first-lien security interest in UCC collateral and obtaining search evidence to confirm lien position. Freddie specifies comprehensive UCC searches and certification; Fannie also requires UCC filings plus co-op-specific documents (lease assignment/stock power).

g1_rule.rule_name: Co-op share loan must include lease assignment, stock power, and UCC filings
g1_rule.description: For all co-op share loans delivered to Fannie Mae, the lender must receive an assignment of the proprietary lease/occupancy agreement (or similar evidence of occupancy rights), obtain a stock power/assignment authorizing transfer upon default, and execute/file valid UCC financing statements (and assignments, if needed) to perfect the security interest. The lender must obtain information searches/equivalent evidence showing the Fannie Mae co-op share loan is in first-lien position; if co-op units are real property in the state, lien perfection must follow applicable real estate law.

g2_rule.rule_name: UCC searches must include all filing offices and be certified at final delivery
g2_rule.description: To ensure a first-lien perfected security interest in UCC collateral, the Seller/Servicer must perform public record searches including UCC searches. Each UCC search must include every office where a financing statement would be filed under Revised Article 9. Prior to the Origination Date, any financing statements found (other than those filed by the current lender to be released at origination) must be explained to the applicable Multifamily Attorney (via amended Legal Issues Analysis for non-SBL or written analysis for SBL). Final delivery must include counsel certification that UCC searches reveal only financing statements that have been/will be terminated at origination or have been approved by Freddie Mac.

business_rules.rule_name: QC Reverification Records Must Be Retained for 3 Years
business_rules.description: All post-closing QC reverification documentation must be retained for at least three years in the loan file or lender QC records. If a vendor performs reverification services, the lender must be provided or have access to written results and documentation for at least three years. The lender must provide reverification documentation to Fannie Mae upon request.
business_rules.conditions: Loans subject to post-closing QC reverification and documentation is obtained
business_rules.consequences: If retained, lender can evidence compliance; if not, QC defect and enforcement risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: All reverification documentation must be retained for at least three years in either the loan file or in the lender's QC records. If the lender uses a vendor to perform reverification services, the lender must be provided, or have access to, the written results and documentation, for at least three years. The lender must provide reverification documentation to Fannie Mae upon request.

examples: QC vendor performs VOE and transcript checks; lender stores vendor results in QC system for 3+ years and can retrieve on demand.
Lender deletes QC reverification results after 18 months; upon Fannie request, lender cannot produce them—noncompliant.
QC documentation is stored in the loan file rather than QC records; retention still satisfies the rule if kept 3+ years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase/indemnification exposure; QC finding escalation

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_037_010 requires obtaining a new tri-merge credit report for QC reverification (when traditional credit used). BR_021_010 requires retaining post-closing QC reverification documentation for at least three years and providing it to Fannie Mae upon request. The retention obligation is triggered by the reverification activity and its outputs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If retention fails, lender cannot evidence QC compliance during audits/reviews, increasing enforcement and remedies risk.

provenance.reasoning: Both are record-retention requirements tied to mortgage/loan files and include a 3-year minimum. However, Rule A is limited to QC reverification documentation, while Rule B requires maintaining the entire Mortgage File for the ownership period plus 3 years after payoff (broader/longer scope).

g1_rule.rule_name: QC Reverification Records Must Be Retained for 3 Years
g1_rule.description: All post-closing QC reverification documentation must be retained for at least three years in the loan file or lender QC records. If a vendor performs reverification services, the lender must be provided or have access to written results and documentation for at least three years. The lender must provide reverification documentation to Fannie Mae upon request.

g2_rule.rule_name: Mortgage File Retention During Ownership plus 3 Years
g2_rule.description: The Servicer must maintain the entire Mortgage File for (1) the full period Freddie Mac owns the Mortgage and (2) at least three years from the date the Mortgage is fully paid.

business_rules.rule_name: Self-report Potential Breaches Within 60 Days and 500 Loans or 1% Threshold
business_rules.description: For repeated potential breaches related to compliance with laws, notification is required within 60 days of the later of (i) the end of the calendar quarter in which the loans were delivered, or (ii) discovery of the potential breach, and reporting must be done using Loan Quality Connect self-report functionality. If the calculation results in fewer than five potentially affected loans, notification is not required. For Reporting Category 2, reporting requirements apply when the breach could warrant repurchase, or when the number of loans affected by the same type of potential breach delivered in the same calendar quarter does not exceed the lesser of 500 loans or 1% of prior-year deliveries; if such breach could warrant repurchase and is not remedied (or will not be remedied) within 60 days, the seller/servicer must notify Fannie Mae within 60 days; if remedied (or will be remedied) within 60 days, notification is not required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when seller/servicer identifies potential legal compliance breach affecting delivered loans.
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met: must self-report timely. If violated: increased repurchase/remedy and governance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: No notice required if fewer than 5 affected loans; or if repurchase-warranting breach is remedied within 60 days.

source_reference.section_id: A3-2-01, Reporting Category 2 (partial) and timing rule
source_reference.source_text: The notification is required within 60 days of the later of: the end of the calendar quarter in which such loans were delivered, or discovery of the potential breach. The seller/servicer must report these loans using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect. Note: If the calculation described above results in fewer than five potentially affected loans, then the seller/servicer does not need to notify Fannie Mae. Reporting Category 2 The following reporting requirements apply when: the potential breach could warrant a repurchase demand, or the number of loans affected by the same type of potential breach delivered in the same calendar quarter does not exceed the lesser of 500 loans or 1% of the total number of loans delivered to Fannie Mae in the prior year.

examples: Lender discovers an ECOA valuation notice issue on Aug 15 affecting 200 loans delivered in Q1; because <500 and <1% (if 70,000 prior-year deliveries), reporting may not be required under Category 2 thresholds, but lender must still evaluate whether repurchase could be warranted.
A repurchase-warranting legal breach is identified on May 1 and cannot be remedied by June 30; lender must notify via Loan Quality Connect within 60 days of May 1 (by June 30).
A potential breach impacts 3 loans; because fewer than 5 potentially affected loans, lender does not need to notify Fannie Mae per the note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand / indemnification risk (if breach is material)

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_009 requires reporting using “Loan Quality Connect self-report functionality.” BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_011_009 requires Seller/Servicers to register for Loan Quality Connect and maintain complete loan files; without registration and file completeness, timely self-reporting and supporting evidence submission is severely compromised.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not registered or unable to produce complete supporting files, self-reporting can be missed or incomplete, increasing remedies/repurchase exposure and governance findings.

business_rules.rule_name: OFAC Match Notice Due Within 24 Hours and No Delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.description: Seller/servicer must establish and maintain an effective OFAC compliance program, may not deliver a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on an OFAC sanctions list, and must periodically screen serviced loans against OFAC sanctions lists. If a valid match is identified, the servicer must notify Fannie Mae Ethics via email within 24 hours of identifying the match, block and segregate funds (including escrow), and ensure all servicing activities cease (including remittances and disbursements).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering loans to Fannie Mae or servicing Fannie Mae loans with potential OFAC list matches.
business_rules.consequences: If met: complies with OFAC obligations. If violated: loan cannot be delivered; escalated enforcement and sanctions risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Department of Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) Regulations• must establish and maintain an effective OFAC compliance program; • must report all instances of penalties (civil or criminal) or enforcement actions for compliance failures or violations related to OFAC requirements to Fannie Mae's Ethics division; • may not deliver a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on one of the sanctions lists maintained by OFAC; and • must periodically screen the loans that it services for Fannie Mae against OFAC’s sanctions lists. If the servicer identifies a valid match, the servicer must notify Fannie Mae Ethics via email within 24 hours of identifying a match

examples: Before delivery, lender screens borrower and finds an OFAC SDN list match; loan must not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Servicer runs periodic OFAC screening and identifies a valid match at 2:00 PM Tuesday; it must notify Fannie Mae Ethics by 2:00 PM Wednesday (within 24 hours).
After a valid match, servicer blocks/segregates escrow funds and stops remitting P&I and paying taxes/insurance until directed by Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination risk; refusal to purchase; regulatory enforcement exposure

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_020_010 requires screening employees against exclusion lists (GSA EPL, HUD LDP, FHFA SCP) and removing listed individuals from origination/servicing functions. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_010 requires OFAC program controls including borrower screening and cessation actions. Together they form layered sanctions/exclusion compliance controls across people (staff) and parties (borrowers/loans).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either control is missing, the organization’s sanctions/exclusion compliance posture is incomplete, increasing regulatory risk and contractual noncompliance exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: QC Review Requires Loan Quality Connect Registration and Complete E-Files
business_rules.description: Seller/Servicers must register for Loan Quality Connect (QC selection notifications are sent via email from Loan Quality Connect) and must maintain a complete loan file including all documents used to support the underwriting decision. Upon request, the lender must provide electronic copies of the complete loan file as described in the request. If unable to submit in supported format, the lender must obtain Loan Quality Center approval before using an alternate submission method.
business_rules.conditions: Fannie Mae QC selects a loan and requests the lender’s complete loan file
business_rules.consequences: If met, QC submission is compliant; if violated, QC response may be non-compliant
business_rules.exceptions: Alternate format allowed only with Loan Quality Center approval

source_reference.section_id: D2-1-02, Notification of a QC Review + Document Submission Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Lenders are notiﬁed which loans Fannie Mae has selected for QC review via an email sent by Fannie Mae’s Loan Quality Connect. Therefore, lenders are required to register for Loan Quality Connect to obtain access to it. Lenders must maintain a complete loan ﬁle, including all documents used to support the underwriting decision. Upon Fannie Mae’s request, lenders must provide electronic copies of the complete loan ﬁle, as described in the request.

examples: Compliant: Lender receives a Loan Quality Connect email, logs in, and uploads the complete underwriting file as requested.
Non-compliant: Lender is not registered for Loan Quality Connect and misses the QC request email, delaying file delivery.
Conditional: Lender cannot produce supported upload format and uses an alternate method only after Loan Quality Center approval.
Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_009 is a general obligation to retain and furnish all documents supporting underwriting decisions. BR_010_005 specifies a particular required document for loans on private/community streets. Therefore, satisfying BR_011_009 for these loans depends on capturing the BR_010_005 document in the file (or the file will be incomplete for QC/exam).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_005 fails: (1) underwriting support documentation is missing, (2) QC submission/record exam responses may be deficient, (3) loan eligibility risk increases due to undocumented access/maintenance obligations.

business_rules.rule_name: Private street requires 3 recorded covenant provisions
business_rules.description: If the subject property is on a community-owned or privately-owned and maintained street, the mortgage file must include (and the appraiser/lender must ensure existence of) an adequate, legally enforceable maintenance agreement or covenant recorded in the appropriate land records. The recorded agreement/covenant should include provisions for (1) responsibility for payment of repairs (including each party’s share), (2) default remedies if a party fails to comply, and (3) an effective term that in most cases is perpetual and binding on future owners.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when subject property street is not publicly maintained (private/community).
business_rules.consequences: Meets: access/maintenance obligation documented; Violated: loan eligibility risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; term may be non-perpetual only if justified ("in most cases").

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.3-04, Community-Owned or Privately Maintained Streets
source_reference.source_text: If the property is located on a community-owned or privately-owned and maintained street, an adequate, legally enforceable agreement or covenant for maintenance of the street is required. The agreement or covenant should include the following provisions and be recorded in the land records of the appropriate jurisdiction: responsibility for payment of repairs, including each party’s representative share; default remedies in the event a party to the agreement or covenant fails to comply with their obligations; and the effective term of the agreement or covenant, which in most cases should be perpetual and binding on any future owners.

examples: PUD road is HOA-maintained; file includes recorded covenant allocating repair costs and default remedies—requirement met.
Rural private road shared by three parcels; recorded easement/maintenance agreement specifies each parcel’s 33% repair share—requirement met.
Property fronts on private road but only a verbal neighbor agreement exists—fails requirement; loan subject to eligibility finding/condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery suspended until cured or loan ineligible
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 'required' and enumerated provisions; term guidance includes 'in most cases'.

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005 requires a recorded agreement/covenant in land records. Title/closing workflows that produce title insurance materials are the standard mechanism for confirming recording, enforceability, and obtaining the recorded document for the file.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the recorded maintenance agreement is not identified during title/land record review, the file may be missing required documentation at delivery, risking eligibility defects and post-purchase findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Policy Must Include ALTA 8.1 Environmental Lien Endorsement
business_rules.description: The title insurance coverage must include an environmental protection lien endorsement (ALTA 8.1 Environmental Protection Lien Endorsement or an equivalent state form providing required coverage). As an alternative, the protections may be incorporated into the policy. For loans originated prior to 2008-01-01, endorsement forms that met requirements at origination are acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to mortgage loans requiring title insurance coverage for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage acceptable. If violated: must obtain endorsement/policy revision; delivery risk increases.
business_rules.exceptions: Loans originated before 2008-01-01 may use then-acceptable endorsement forms.

source_reference.source_text: The title insurance coverage must include an environmental protection lien endorsement (ALTA 8.1 Environmental Protection Lien Endorsement or equivalent state form that provides the required coverage). References are to the ALTA form of endorsement, but state forms may be used as described in Terms of Coverage. As an alternative to endorsements, the requisite protections may be incorporated into the policy. For loans originated prior to January 1, 2008, endorsement forms that meet Fannie Mae’s requirements at the time of origination are acceptable.

examples: A 2026 purchase loan includes ALTA 8.1 endorsement attached to the lender’s policy; requirement satisfied.
A state-specific equivalent environmental lien form is used instead of ALTA 8.1; acceptable if it provides the required coverage.
A 2007-originated loan being delivered includes an older endorsement form; acceptable if it met Fannie Mae requirements at origination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / corrective endorsement required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: The environmental protection lien endorsement is a requirement within the title insurance package. In practice, lenders validate baseline title policy terms (including coverage amount) before validating required endorsements/forms, to avoid rework if the base policy is noncompliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If baseline policy requirements are not satisfied first, endorsement work may be wasted, and delivery can be delayed while reissuing the policy to correct coverage amount.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM Loans Require ALTA Endorsement 6 in Title Policy
business_rules.description: For an ARM mortgage loan, the title insurance policy must include ALTA Endorsement 6.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the mortgage loan has adjustable-rate features (ARM).
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage meets ARM endorsement requirement. If violated: obtain endorsement before delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Other Mortgages The table below provides the title insurance coverage requirements or endorsements for other types of loans. Transaction Type Title Insurance Requirements ARM loan The policy must include ALTA Endorsement 6.

examples: A 7/6 ARM includes ALTA Endorsement 6 in the lender’s title policy; compliant.
A 5/1 ARM policy is issued without ALTA Endorsement 6; lender must add endorsement prior to delivery.
A fixed-rate loan is not an ARM; this ALTA 6 requirement does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / endorsement addition required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: The ARM-specific endorsement is part of a compliant title policy package. Establishing that the title policy meets baseline minimum coverage is a practical gating step before checking loan-feature endorsements like ALTA 6.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the baseline title policy is deficient, adding ALTA 6 does not cure the deficiency; delivery remains blocked and rework is required.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Loans Require ALTA Endorsement 7.2 or Equivalent
business_rules.description: For a manufactured home mortgage loan, the title insurance policy must include ALTA Endorsement 7, 7.1, or 7.2.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is secured by a manufactured home property.
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage meets manufactured home endorsement requirement. If violated: endorsement must be added before delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Other Mortgages The table below provides the title insurance coverage requirements or endorsements for other types of loans. Transaction Type Title Insurance Requirements Manufactured home loan The policy must include ALTA Endorsement 7, 7.1, or 7.2.

examples: A manufactured home purchase loan includes ALTA Endorsement 7.1; compliant.
A manufactured home refinance has title policy without 7/7.1/7.2 endorsement; lender must correct prior to delivery.
A site-built SFR loan is not a manufactured home; this endorsement requirement does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / endorsement addition required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks
