dependencies.rationale: BR_001_001 governs the requirement to issue an Adverse Action Notice within 30 days. BR_001_002 requires the Seller/Servicer to upload evidence/copies to DMS within five Business Days following that issuance. The documentation clock and artifact depend on the issuance event.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_001 is missed or date is wrong, BR_001_002 cannot be timed correctly; this creates ECOA timing violations and missing DMS evidence, increasing regulatory exposure and QC findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated Easement File Must Include 4 Document Types
business_rules.description: For a delegated easement approval, the Servicer must maintain a file containing: (i) Borrower’s original request for approval and required information; (ii) evidence of the Servicer’s analysis and approval of the request; (iii) copies of the Servicer’s notification of the approval to the Borrower and Freddie Mac; and (iv) copies of the executed easement agreement and any other document signed on Freddie Mac’s behalf. The file must be maintained in accordance with Section 34.4 requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever Servicer approves an easement under delegated authority (43.10)
business_rules.consequences: If file is complete and retained per 34.4, supports audit; if missing, Servicer is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: d. File retention requirements for an easement not requiring Freddie Mac approval (09/22/17) The Servicer must maintain a file that contains the following information for a delegated easement approval: • Borrower's original request for approval and required information • Evidence of the Servicer's analysis and approval of the request • Copies of the Servicer's notification of the approval to the Borrower and Freddie Mac • Copies of the executed easement agreement and any other document signed on Freddie Mac's behalf The Servicer must maintain the file in accordance with the requirements set forth in Section 34.4.

examples: Servicer retains borrower request, internal approval memo, and copies of emails/letters to borrower and Freddie Mac plus executed easement—compliant.
Servicer approves easement but fails to retain evidence of analysis/approval; file is incomplete—noncompliant on audit.
Servicer retains executed easement but not the document signed on Freddie Mac’s behalf; file is incomplete—noncompliant.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit file contents list and cross-reference to Section 34.4 retention standard.

examples: Servicer collects monthly payments and remits interest and principal per remittance rules
Servicer monitors insurance renewals and enforces coverage requirements
Servicer initiates default management after sustained delinquency

dependencies.rationale: The delegated easement file must contain evidence of notification to Freddie Mac and copies of the executed/recorded easement agreement. BR_SERVICES_PROCESS_022_003 is the operational step that produces those deliverables and timestamps (within 30 days).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SERVICES_PROCESS_022_003 is not performed, the file will be missing mandatory artifacts (notice and delivered documents), causing audit failures, inability to evidence compliance with delegated authority, and potential repurchase/servicing remedies depending on Guide enforcement.

business_rules.rule_name: Easement underwriting package must include survey or tax map and photos
business_rules.description: For an SBL Mortgage where access is via an easement, the Seller must include in the underwriting package either (a) a copy of the existing survey, tax map, or satellite images detailing the easement location and (b) photographs from the public street showing the view of the Property from the public street, the easement location, and any applicable signage.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting an SBL Mortgage on a Property with indirect access via easement
business_rules.consequences: If met: underwriting package is complete for easement evidence; if violated: delivery/underwriting suspense until cured
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 8SBL – SBL Property Fundamentals, d. Indirect access (06/30/16), item 10
source_reference.source_text: 10. Seller must include in the underwriting package either a copy of the existing survey, tax map, or satellite images of the Property detailing the location of the easement as well as photographs taken from the public street showing the view of the Property from the public street and the location of the easement as well as the signage for the Property.

examples: Seller provides a current survey showing the easement and includes street-view photos showing signage → compliant package.
Seller provides only a tax map but no photographs from the public street → noncompliant; must add required photos.
Seller provides satellite images clearly showing easement alignment plus photos showing view/access points → compliant.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial lease ≥5% of GPR requires tenant-signed estoppel
business_rules.description: For SBL underwriting, the Seller must submit a Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL in the full underwriting package for each commercial lease. If income from any one commercial lease represents 5% or more of the Property’s gross potential rent (GPR), then the Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL must be signed by the commercial tenant.
business_rules.conditions: When the Property includes one or more commercial leases used in underwriting income
business_rules.consequences: If met: commercial income is properly supported; if violated: underwriting/delivery condition and potential ineligibility
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: For each commercial lease, the Seller must submit a Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL in the full underwriting package (see Chapter 55SBL for additional details regarding this requirement). If income from any one commercial lease represents five percent or more of gross potential rent (GPR) for the Property, the Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL must be signed by the commercial tenant.

examples: Retail lease contributes 6.2% of GPR; Seller includes analysis and tenant-signed estoppel → compliant.
Office lease contributes 4.9% of GPR; analysis/estoppel still required, but tenant signature is not triggered by the 5% threshold.
Two commercial leases each contribute 5.0%+ of GPR; both must have tenant signatures on their respective estoppels.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated inspections require at least 2 photos per unit inspected
business_rules.description: At full underwriting, the Seller must submit photographs representative of the Property. If Freddie Mac delegates the property inspection to the Seller, at least two photographs of each unit inspected must be provided as part of the property inspection and lease audit documentation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Freddie Mac delegates the property inspection to the Seller
business_rules.consequences: If met: evidence supports inspection; if violated: package deficiency and cure required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: At full underwriting , the Seller must submit the property inspection  and lease audit  documentation  set forth below :  a. Photographs representative of the Property .  If Freddie Mac delegates the property inspection to the Seller, at least two photograph s of each unit inspected  must be provided

examples: Seller inspects 12 units under delegated inspection and provides 24+ photos (2 per unit minimum): compliant.
Seller inspects 10 units but provides only 1 photo for 3 units: noncompliant; must supplement photos or re-inspect.
Seller provides two photos per unit but cannot tie photos to unit identifiers in file: treated as documentation deficiency until corrected.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Deliver Borrower Weather Certification if Inspection Pre-Event
business_rules.description: For specified loans in the quote stage (Conventional, Seniors, TAH, Structured & Facility) and for SBL under Seller Application, when a natural disaster or weather-related adverse condition occurs that could impact Property condition and the property condition consultant’s inspection occurred before the event, the Seller/Servicer must deliver the Borrower Certification of Weather-Related Event as part of the full underwriting package and it must specifically address any impact to the Property sustained from the event. Freddie Mac may require additional information or further inspections in its sole discretion.
business_rules.conditions: In-process loan; inspection occurred before the disaster/weather event
business_rules.consequences: If submitted, underwriting package is complete for disaster item; if missing, Freddie may require it and delay/decline review
business_rules.exceptions: If inspection occurs after the event, nothing further required (per Guide)

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 11; 11.9(b)(1) Types and Status of Mortgage Loans (12/12/24)
source_reference.source_text: If the property condition consultant’s inspection has taken place prior to the occurrence of the natural disaster or weather -related adverse condition:  o The Seller/Servicer must deliver the Borrower Certification of Weather -Related Event  as part of the full underwriting package and must specifically address any impact to the Property sustained from the natural disaster or weather -related adverse condition.  o Freddie Mac, in its sole discretion, may require additional information or further inspections of the Property.

examples: Inspection occurred on June 1; hurricane occurs June 10: Seller includes Borrower Certification addressing damage/no-damage in the underwriting package.
Inspection occurred after the wildfire: no Borrower Certification required under this specific bullet (inspection timing exception).
Certification states only 'no impact' without addressing known roof damage: Freddie may require further inspection/information.
Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Property Condition Report Required Before Commitment or Early Rate Lock
business_rules.description: Freddie Mac requires submission of a property condition report (engineering report) meeting Section 62.3 requirements before Freddie Mac will issue a Letter of Commitment or accept an early rate-lock application to purchase a Mortgage.
business_rules.conditions: Prior to Freddie Mac issuing a Letter of Commitment or accepting early rate-lock
business_rules.consequences: If submitted, Freddie Mac may proceed; if not, commitment/rate-lock acceptance will not occur
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Freddie Mac requires the Seller/Servicer to submit a property condition report (also commonly referred to as an engineering report) meeting the requirements of Section 62.3 before Freddie Mac will issue a Letter of Commitment or accept the early rate -lock application to purchase a Mortgage.

examples: Seller submits early rate-lock request without a Section 62.3-compliant engineering report; Freddie Mac will not accept the early rate-lock application.
Seller provides a complete property condition report that meets Section 62.3; Freddie Mac can issue a Letter of Commitment (subject to other conditions).
Engineering report is missing required elements under Section 62.3; Freddie Mac treats as not meeting requirement and will not proceed to commitment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Freddie Mac will not issue Letter of Commitment
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong 'requires' and 'before' language; no numeric thresholds.

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Remediation Plan Required When Repairs Exceed 1% or $200,000 After 12/31/2024
business_rules.description: For loans taken under Seller Application after December 31, 2024, if Priority Repairs exceed the greater of 100 basis points (1.00%) of the loan amount or $200,000, or include a material repair likely to impact habitability, the Seller must submit a Borrower remediation plan detailing funding source, whether work will be in-house or third-party, permitting needs, and other pertinent details to remediate all repairs within the required time frame.
business_rules.conditions: Seller Application date is after 2024-12-31 and Priority Repair triggers are met
business_rules.consequences: If plan submitted, underwriting can proceed; if missing, Freddie Mac may condition/decline
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the Priority Repairs exceed the greater of 100 b asis points of the loan amount or $200,000 or have a material repair more likely to impact habitability , the Seller must submit a remediation plan from the Borrower indicating the source of funds for completing the work, whether work will be performed in-house or by a third party, if permitting is required and other pertinent details for remediating all repairs within the required time frame.

examples: Loan amount $15,000,000; 1% is $150,000, so the greater threshold is $200,000. If Priority Repairs total $250,000, Seller must submit the remediation plan.
Loan amount $40,000,000; 1% is $400,000, so the threshold is $400,000. If repairs total $350,000 with no habitability-impact repair, remediation plan is not required by this trigger.
Priority Repairs total $120,000 but include foundation structural repair impacting habitability; Seller must submit the remediation plan even though amounts are below $200,000 and 1%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting suspense condition; possible purchase denial
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric triggers (100 bps/1% and $200,000) plus date gating (after 12/31/2024).

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial Lease Analysis Required When One Lease Is at Least 5% of GPR
business_rules.description: If income from any one commercial (non-residential) lease represents 5% or more of the Property’s gross potential rent, the Seller must submit a Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL, signed by the tenant, in the full underwriting package for each such lease.
business_rules.conditions: SBL underwriting where any one commercial lease income ≥ 5% of Property GPR
business_rules.consequences: If provided, Freddie Mac can underwrite lease risk; if missing, underwriting package is incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: Freddie Mac may require it in other circumstances in its discretion

source_reference.source_text: If income from any one commercial lease represents five percent or more of the gross potential rent of the Property, the Seller must submit a Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL, signed by the tenant, in the full underwriting package for each such lease.

examples: Property GPR is $1,000,000; a retail lease contributes $60,000 (6%). Seller must include a tenant-signed Commercial Lease Analysis and Estoppel – SBL.
Property GPR is $2,000,000; a laundry lease contributes $90,000 (4.5%). The 5% trigger is not met, though Freddie Mac could still require analysis in its discretion.
Two commercial leases contribute 3% each; neither individually reaches 5%, so the mandatory submission trigger is not met for either lease.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting package suspense until document provided
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 5% threshold and specific document name; discretionary add-on noted as exception context.

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

dependencies.rationale: Both rules address Commercial Lease Analysis/Estoppel requirements and the 5% of GPR signing trigger. BR_UNDERWRITES_DOCUMENTATION_010_005 is broader (each commercial lease, with signing requirement when ≥5%), while BR_UNDERWRITES_DOCUMENTATION_036_009 repeats the ≥5% signed requirement. In an automated graph, BR_UNDERWRITES_DOCUMENTATION_036_009 can validate that the signing trigger was applied correctly under BR_UNDERWRITES_DOCUMENTATION_010_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the signing trigger is missed, the full underwriting package may be incomplete and underwriting may be suspended until signed tenant estoppels are obtained (often time-consuming).

business_rules.rule_name: Flood Zone Determination Form Effective for Life of Loan
business_rules.description: For each mortgage loan origination, Seller/Servicers must determine whether any buildings at the property are or will be fully or partially located in a SFHA using the FEMA Standard Flood Hazard Determination Form (SFHDF). Seller/Servicers must ensure all structures are evaluated when ordering the SFHDF, provide supplemental legal/parcels to the vendor, ensure the resulting SFHDF (and subsequent versions) is effective for the life of the loan, and include a copy of the SFHDF plus a signed Notice to the Borrower of Special Flood Hazard and Federal Assistance in the Mortgage File.
business_rules.conditions: For each mortgage loan origination where SFHA determination is required
business_rules.consequences: Complete SFHDF + signed notice in file: compliant; missing/outdated: delivery deficiency
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For each Mortgage loan origination, Seller/Servicers must determine whether any buildings located at the Property are or will be fully or partially located in a SFHA, using the FEMA Standard Flood Hazard Determination Form (SFHDF). Seller/Servicers must ensure that all structures at the Property will be evaluated when ordering the SFHDF. In addition to the Property address listed in the Collateral Description of the SFHDF, Seller/Servicers must provide to the vendor supplemental information such as the Property’s legal description or parcel descriptions. The resulting SFHDF and any subsequent versions, must be effective for the life of the related loan. In addition, the SFHDF should include a map image overlay. A copy of the SFHDF along with a signed copy of the Notice to the Borrower of Special Flood Hazard and Federal Assistance must be included in the Mortgage File.

examples: Seller orders SFHDF for parcel A only in a multi-parcel property: noncompliant (must ensure all structures evaluated).
Mortgage file includes SFHDF and signed borrower notice at closing: compliant documentation set.
Vendor receives only property street address without legal description; SFHDF later found incomplete: noncompliant process; must provide legal/parcels.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase hold / delivery deficiency until corrected

examples: Delegated Seller/Servicer underwrites application using required third-party reports
Non-delegated transaction where Seller/Servicer prepares underwriting package for Freddie Mac review
Underwriting identifies conditions such as insurance endorsements and repair escrows

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Independent Property Purchase Request Must Include 4 Written Items
business_rules.description: When requesting Freddie Mac purchase of a mortgage secured by a property that is not an Independent Property, the Seller must submit specified information in writing as part of the applicable underwriting package: (1) description of Essential and Recreational Facilities (including on other properties if cross-collateralized/cross-defaulted/coterminous), (2) legal counsel confirmation of Shared Access/Use Agreement compliance or a detailed gap analysis and recommendation, (3) Seller analysis of feasibility/practicality of creating/constructing required facilities on the property if not all are located on the property (even if Shared Use Agreement exists), and (4) Seller analysis of feasibility/practicality of creating direct access to a publicly dedicated and maintained right of way if primary access is not direct (even if Shared Use Agreement exists). The Seller’s analysis must include financial considerations, zoning issues, and availability of land.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when property collateral is not an Independent Property and purchase is requested
business_rules.consequences: If complete, package is eligible for review; if missing, delivery is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; analyses required even when Shared Use Agreement exists

source_reference.source_text: If the Seller requests that Freddie Mac purchase a Mortgage secured by a Property that is not an Independent Property, the Seller must submit the following information in writing to Freddie Mac as part of the applicable underwriting package: • Description of the Essential Facilities and Recreational Facilities located on the Property... • Either (i) confirmation from the Seller/Servicer’s legal counsel... or (ii) a detailed analysis... • Seller’s analysis of the feasibility and practicality... (required even if the Property has access... through a Shared Use Agreement) • Seller’s analysis of the practicality and feasibility... (required even if the Property has access... through a Shared Use Agreement) The Seller’s analysis must include financial considerations as well as zoning issues and the availability of land.

examples: Property relies on shared amenities on adjacent parcel; Seller submits facilities description, counsel memo on shared use compliance, and feasibility analyses for on-site facilities and direct public access.
Seller provides only a facilities description but omits zoning/land availability analysis; Freddie Mac delivery package is incomplete and must be cured before review.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must-submit list; no numeric thresholds in the list itself.

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_019_001 governs what must be submitted in writing in the underwriting package when requesting Freddie Mac purchase for a property that is not an Independent Property. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_018_009 is an eligibility rule: a prohibited Exclusionary List role match makes the Mortgage ineligible for sale/approval. In a practical compliance workflow, eligibility failures should stop downstream packaging and submission tasks, including specialized underwriting package components for non-Independent Properties.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_018_009 fails, the Seller may be required to halt the purchase request, replace disallowed parties, and re-underwrite/re-document. Even a complete BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_019_001 package will not cure the ineligibility.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_019_001 is triggered when requesting purchase with a non-Independent Property and requires submission of specific underwriting information. BR_BORROWER_PROHIBITION_007_003 creates a hard stop for purchase; therefore sanctions clearance should precede (or gate) assembling/submitting this package for Freddie review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sanctions screening fails, the Seller expends time and legal resources (shared access/use agreement analysis, counsel confirmations) for a loan that cannot be purchased, causing avoidable cost and timeline slippage.

business_rules.rule_name: Additional Actual Loan Amount request needs new appraisal and calculation
business_rules.description: When requesting an Additional Actual Loan Amount at TAH TEL Conversion, the Seller must include in the TAH TEL Conversion Underwriting Package (i) the Seller’s calculation of the Actual Loan Amount supporting the request and (ii) a new Appraisal that complies with Chapter 60 and supports the requested Additional Actual Loan Amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller requests Additional Actual Loan Amount at TEL Conversion underwriting package delivery
business_rules.consequences: If included, request package is complete for Freddie Mac review; if missing, request may be denied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: In connection with any such request, Seller will include in its TAH TEL Conversion  Underwriting Package  each of the following:  • Seller’s calculation of the Actual Loan Amount supporting the requested Additional Actual Loan Amount based on the performance of the Property  • A new Appraisal which complies with the requirements of Chapter 60, and which Appraisal supports the requested Additional Actual Loan Amount

examples: Seller requests a $500,000 increase and provides an updated DCR/NOI-based sizing calculation plus a new appraisal dated within the conversion package timeline, compliant with Chapter 60.
Seller provides only a narrative justification but no calculation worksheet—package is incomplete for the stated requirement.
Seller provides a new appraisal but it does not support the higher value/loan sizing; Freddie Mac can deny the requested increase.
Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_CONSTRAINT_021_003 defines hard limits on any requested Additional Actual Loan Amount. BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_DOCUMENTATION_021_004 requires a Seller calculation and a new Chapter 60-compliant appraisal supporting the requested amount. If the request exceeds the cap, the appraisal/calc package cannot result in an approvable request, so the cap rule is an upstream gating control.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the cap rule is not satisfied, the Seller may waste time/cost ordering a new appraisal and preparing a calculation that Freddie will reject as not allowable. This creates delays at Conversion and may force the Seller to re-size the request and re-underwrite.

dependencies.rationale: If property condition report issues may materially affect value, the appraiser must be provided the report before the appraisal is transmitted to Freddie. Since BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_DOCUMENTATION_021_004 requires an appraisal that will be delivered as part of the Conversion Underwriting Package, this appraisal must satisfy the pre-transmission condition report integration rule where applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the PCR is not provided when required, Freddie may reject the appraisal or condition the underwriting review, forcing appraisal revisions and causing Conversion timeline slippage.

dependencies.rationale: A 'new Appraisal that complies with Chapter 60' must include required zoning/legal analysis. Therefore, any appraisal delivered to support the Additional Actual Loan Amount request must satisfy the zoning source and minimum zoning/legal content rules (parking ratio, density, rebuildability).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If zoning/legal issues are not properly addressed and sourced, Freddie may deem the appraisal deficient and refuse to use it to support the increased loan amount, requiring revisions and potentially blocking the request.

dependencies.rationale: If the underlying transaction is a Lease-Up program, then the 'new Appraisal' required by BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_DOCUMENTATION_021_004 must incorporate the two-value structure required by BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_DOCUMENTATION_034_004, because Freddie Mac Underwriting Value is based on the prospective as-stabilized value.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Lease-Up valuation components are missing, Freddie cannot rely on the appraisal to support underwriting value for the increased loan amount request, triggering appraisal revision and underwriting delay.

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_DELIVERY_DOCUMENTATION_021_004 requires the new appraisal to comply with Chapter 60; for Mod Rehab transactions, Chapter 60 compliance includes dual valuation scenarios per BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_DOCUMENTATION_034_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Mod Rehab dual values are missing, Freddie may reject the appraisal as failing program-specific valuation requirements, delaying the Additional Actual Loan Amount review and potentially Conversion.

dependencies.rationale: If the property includes restricted units affecting rent levels, the appraisal supporting any increase request must capture the required restricted/unrestricted valuation scenarios; otherwise the appraisal is incomplete for underwriting reliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If restricted unit valuation analysis is omitted, Freddie may determine value support is insufficient and suspend/condition the increased amount request pending appraisal revision.

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1104 review requires disclosure of critical repairs including mold
business_rules.description: After receiving and reviewing Form 1104, the Seller must disclose to Freddie Mac any Critical Repairs (as defined in Section 62SBL.3(b)), including actual or suspected structural, mechanical, electrical, or other material physical deficiencies or Mold at the Property, and must inform the Small Balance Loan Team of unusual or questionable conditions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after Seller receives and reviews Form 1104 (SBL Physical Risk Report)
business_rules.consequences: Met: Freddie informed for risk/conditions; Violated: misrepresentation risk and delay
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: 2. Once the Seller has received and reviewed Form 1104 , the Seller must disclose to Freddie Mac any Critical Repairs, as defined in Section 62SBL.3(b), including actual or suspected structural, mechanical, electrical or other material physical deficiencies or Mold at the Property and inform the Small Balance Loan Team of unusual or questionable conditions.

examples: Form 1104 notes suspected electrical panel defects; Seller discloses as a Critical Repair and flags to the Small Balance Loan Team.
Consultant reports visible mold; Seller must disclose mold as part of Critical Repairs to Freddie Mac.
Seller identifies a questionable structural crack during review but does not report it; this violates the disclosure requirement.
Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_PROCESS_023_006 requires the Seller/Servicer to submit the SBL Physical Risk Report (Form 1104) before Freddie Mac will issue a Letter of Commitment. BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_COMPLIANCE_023_007 then requires disclosures 'after receiving and reviewing Form 1104.' Operationally, the output artifact from BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_PROCESS_023_006 (a compliant Form 1104) is the input/trigger for BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_COMPLIANCE_023_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_DELIVERS_LOAN_PROCESS_023_006 fails (no compliant Form 1104 submitted/obtained), the Seller cannot perform the required Form 1104-based Critical Repair disclosures. The SBL commitment process stalls because Freddie will not issue the Letter of Commitment, and downstream underwriting conditions may be incomplete or misinformed.

business_rules.rule_name: Cash Warehouse Delivery Requires Forms 987M and 996M
business_rules.description: For cash program/product warehouse (pledged mortgage) deliveries, the Seller must submit to Freddie Mac Multifamily Cash Management the authorization documentation required by the referenced section, and settlements cannot be made if the authorization documentation is not on file. With each delivery of a pledged Mortgage to be purchased under a cash program/product, the Seller must include a completed Form 987M (Wire Transfer Authorization – Cash Warehouse Delivery) attached to a properly completed and executed Form 996M (Warehouse form).
business_rules.conditions: When delivering a pledged Mortgage under a cash program/product from a warehouse line
business_rules.consequences: If docs on file and forms included, settlement can proceed; if missing, settlement cannot be made
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Seller must submit to Freddie Mac Multifamily Cash Management the authorization documentation required by Section 32.12(b). Settlements cannot be made if such authorization documentation is not on file with Freddie Mac Multifamily Cash Management. With each delivery of a pledged Mortgage to be purchased by Freddie Mac under a cash program or product, the Seller must include a completed Form 987M, Wire Transfer Authorization – Cash Warehouse Delivery, attached to a properly completed and executed Form 996M, Warehouse

examples: Seller submits Section 32.12(b) authorization docs and includes Form 987M attached to executed Form 996M—settlement proceeds.
Seller delivers pledged mortgage but authorization documentation is not on file—settlement cannot be made until provided.
Seller includes Form 987M but Form 996M is not executed—delivery is document-deficient and settlement is blocked.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit settlement gating and required forms; numeric specificity is via form identifiers (987M/996M) not thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Settlement blocked (cannot be made) until documentation is on file

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

business_rules.rule_name: Insurance Evidence Requires Form 1133 via ICT and Section 31.20 Docs
business_rules.description: To verify that the Property has (or will have as of the Freddie Mac Funding Date) adequate property damage and liability insurance, the Seller must submit to Freddie Mac: (i) fully completed Form 1133 (Seller/Servicer Certification of Insurance Coverage) via the Insurance Compliance Tool (ICT), and (ii) the documents listed in Sections 31.20(a) and 31.20(b), as applicable. For refinance of an existing mortgage not owned by Freddie Mac, prior to the Origination Date, the mortgagee/mortgage holders clause and additional insured clause must be changed to reflect Guide requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loan delivery/underwriting package where insurance adequacy must be evidenced
business_rules.consequences: If submitted, insurance verification can be satisfied; if not, delivery conditions remain unmet
business_rules.exceptions: Refinance of non-Freddie loan requires clause changes prior to Origination Date

source_reference.section_id: Guide Chapter 55 – Documentation and Deliveries (Evidence of Insurance)
source_reference.source_text: Evidence of Insurance The Seller must submit the following to Freddie Mac to verify that the Property has, or will have as of the Freddie Mac Funding Date, adequate property damage and liability insurance as required by the Purchase and Servicing Documents: • Fully completed Form 1133, Seller/Servicer Certification of Insurance Coverage , via the Insurance Compliance Tool (ICT) • The documents listed in Sections 31.20(a) and 31.20(b), as applicable For an underwriting package pertaining to the refinance of an existing mortgage not owned by Freddie Mac, prior to the Origination Date of the Mortgage, the mortgagee or mortgage holders clause and additional insured clause must be changed to reflect the requirements of the Guide.

examples: Seller uploads completed Form 1133 through ICT and attaches the required Section 31.20 documents; Freddie Mac can verify coverage effective on the Funding Date.
Refinance of a bank-held loan: Seller fails to update the mortgagee clause before Origination Date; delivery is conditioned until clauses are corrected.
Seller provides an insurance ACORD only but does not submit Form 1133 via ICT; requirement is not met and delivery remains incomplete.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or purchase delay pending insurance evidence
business_rules.extraction_notes: Form and tool requirements are explicit; the timing is tied to Funding Date and refinance clause changes must occur prior to Origination Date.

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

business_rules.rule_name: PLIM Must Be Submitted With Underwriting Package and Transfers
business_rules.description: When the Seller/Servicer submits an underwriting package, an application for approval of a Transfer of Ownership, or certain other Special Servicing Requests (as specified elsewhere in the Guide), the Seller/Servicer or its legal counsel must submit to Freddie Mac a Preliminary Legal Issues Memorandum (PLIM) prepared by the Seller/Servicer’s legal counsel that meets the requirements in Section 6.4(b).
business_rules.conditions: When delivering an underwriting package, Transfer of Ownership, or specified requests
business_rules.consequences: If met: submission is complete; if violated: delivery/approval may be suspended
business_rules.exceptions: Only applies to the listed submission types

source_reference.source_text: 6.4 Preliminary legal issues memorandum (08/26/25) a. When a preliminary legal issues memorandum is required (10/12/17) The Seller/Servicer or its legal counsel must submit to Freddie Mac a memorandum ("preliminary legal issues memorandum" or “PLIM”), prepared by the Seller/Servicer's legal counsel, that meets each requirement set forth in Section 6.4(b) when the Seller/Servicer submits each of the following: • An underwriting package • An application for approval of a Transfer of Ownership • Certain other Special Servicing Requests

examples: When submitting an underwriting package for a Mortgage with nonstandard structure, Seller/Servicer includes a PLIM signed by counsel.
For a Transfer of Ownership approval request, the delivery package includes the PLIM meeting Section 6.4(b) requirements.
If the PLIM is missing from the underwriting package submission, Freddie Mac may place the submission in suspense pending receipt.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery suspense or rejection until PLIM is provided

examples: Seller/Servicer delivers a loan after satisfying title insurance conditions
Seller/Servicer delivers loan data first and documents subsequently per permitted process
Delivery resubmitted after correction of a data mismatch

dependencies.rationale: The UCC search rule creates issue identification and explanation outputs (financing statements and required explanations/amended analyses) that counsel must consider and typically incorporate into legal issue memoranda. Therefore, completing UCC searches and routing exceptions is an upstream prerequisite to producing a complete and accurate PLIM for underwriting package submission.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If UCC searches and exception explanations are not completed, the PLIM may be incomplete/incorrect, creating legal delivery holds, additional counsel review cycles, and potential origination delays.

business_rules.rule_name: All loan document blanks must be completed with Freddie Mac specified info or 'n/a' and unspecified blanks require contact for instructions
business_rules.description: The Seller must complete each blank in a Loan Document or other Freddie Mac-required form with the information specified by Freddie Mac, with “n/a,” or with a cross-reference to the applicable modification Exhibit, as applicable. If the Purchase Contract or the Guide does not specify how a blank is to be completed, the Seller must contact the applicable Freddie Mac Multifamily Regional Office for non-SBL Mortgages or the Multifamily Small Balance Loan Team for SBL Mortgages for instructions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to completion of Freddie Mac required loan documents for closing/delivery
business_rules.consequences: If completed per rule, documents are acceptable for delivery; if blanks are incomplete/incorrect, document defects must be cured and may delay funding/purchase
business_rules.exceptions: Different Freddie Mac contact path for SBL vs non-SBL instructions

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 32 – Completion of documents (10/01/25), Completion of blanks by the Seller
source_reference.source_text: The Seller must complete each blank in a Loan Document or other form required by Freddie Mac with the information specified by Freddie Mac, with "n/a," or with a cross-reference to the applicable modification Exhibit, as applicable. If the Purchase Contract or the Guide does not specify how a blank is to be completed, the Seller must contact : • The Applicable Freddie Mac Multifamily Regional Office for instructions, for non-SBL Mortgages • The Multifamily Small Balance Loan Team, for SBL Mortgages

examples: A Loan Agreement blank for 'Property Name' is filled exactly as specified on Freddie Mac’s form instructions; document is acceptable.
A Rider has a blank that does not apply; Seller enters 'n/a' rather than leaving it empty.
A form has an unclear blank not addressed by the Guide; Seller emails the applicable regional office (non-SBL) for instructions before execution.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong must-language; numeric precision low because the rule is not numeric.

examples: Closing package assembled with executed note and recorded security instrument
Closing package flagged delivery-ready after final title and insurance evidence received
Closing package returned for correction due to missing signature/acknowledgment

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisal must exclude Non-Permitted units and their income from direct cap and final as-is value
business_rules.description: When units lack required certificate-of-occupancy confirmation (Non-Permitted Units), the Appraisal must not include any Non-Permitted Unit or any income derived from any Non-Permitted Unit in either the direct capitalization approach or the final as-is valuation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when there are Non-Permitted Units for an SBL Mortgage property.
business_rules.consequences: If met, appraisal is acceptable for purchase conditions; if violated, appraisal is non-compliant and may block purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: The Appraisal must not include any Non -Permitted Unit or any income derived from any Non-Permitted Unit in either the direct cap or final as -is valuation.

examples: Appraisal removes the two non-permitted units from rent roll inputs and excludes their rent from direct cap NOI: compliant.
Appraisal includes all units but sets rent at $0 for non-permitted units: not compliant if unit itself is included in valuation.
Final as-is value references income that includes non-permitted unit rent: non-compliant; appraisal must be revised.
Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Lease-up appraisals need as-is and as-stabilized values 09/25/15
business_rules.description: For Properties specifically underwritten in Acquisition Lease-Up or Refinance Lease-Up programs, the Appraisal must include (i) the as-is value as of the Appraisal effective date (current date) and (ii) the prospective as-stabilized value as of the prospective future stabilized-operations date. Freddie Mac Underwriting Value is based on the prospective as-stabilized value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting a Property under Acquisition or Refinance Lease-Up
business_rules.consequences: If both values provided, underwriting can use as-stabilized value; if missing, appraisal is incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For Properties to be specifically underwritten in either of these two programs, the Appraisal must provide the as -is value of the Property as of the effective date of the Appraisal report (i.e., the current date) and the prospective as -stabilized value as of the prospective future date of stabilized operations for the Property. The Freddie Mac Underwriting Value is based on the prospective as -stabilized value.

examples: Acquisition Lease-Up: appraisal includes as-is value as of 2026-06-01 and as-stabilized value as of 2027-03-01; underwriting value uses as-stabilized figure.
Refinance Lease-Up: appraisal provides only as-stabilized value; missing as-is value makes the appraisal noncompliant until corrected.
Lease-up property where stabilization is projected 18 months out: appraisal must still state a prospective as-stabilized value as of that future stabilized date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/underwriting suspension until compliant appraisal provided

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Mod Rehab appraisal must include 2 values as-is and as-if renovated 02/28/20
business_rules.description: For a Moderate Rehabilitation (Mod Rehab) Mortgage, the appraiser must provide two values of the Property based on all applicable approaches to value: (1) As-is market value; and (2) Hypothetical as-if renovated and stabilized today market value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the Mortgage is a Moderate Rehabilitation (Mod Rehab) Mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If both values provided, valuation scenarios are supported; if not, appraisal fails program requirement
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: 60.28 Appraisals for Moderate Rehabilitation (Mod Rehab) Mortgages (02/28/20)
source_reference.source_text: For a Mod Rehab Mortgage, the appraiser must provide the following two values of the Property based on all applicable approaches to value: • As-is market value • Hypothetical as -if renovated and stabilized today market value

examples: Appraiser provides as-is market value based on current condition and a separate hypothetical as-if renovated value assuming renovation complete and stabilized today.
Appraiser provides only a single as-completed value; missing as-is market value requires appraisal revision before Freddie Mac underwriting.
Mod Rehab with limited scope: still must include both as-is and as-if renovated and stabilized today values.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/underwriting suspension until compliant appraisal provided

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Restricted units require 2 values and phase-out time analysis
business_rules.description: If a Property has restricted units, the appraiser must provide (i) an estimate of market value with restricted units in place and (ii) a hypothetical market value without restricted units. The appraiser must also opine (with support) on whether Highest and Best Use is to phase out restricted rents in favor of non-restricted rents and, if so, develop an as-is market value considering the time needed to phase in non-restricted rents.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the Property includes restricted units affecting rent levels
business_rules.consequences: If both valuations and HBU/time analysis are included, appraisal supports underwriting; if omitted, appraisal is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 60 – Restricted units requirement (item 5 shown in excerpt)
source_reference.source_text: If the Property has restricted units, the appraiser must include an estimate of market value with the restricted units in place and an estimate of hypothetical market value without the restricted units. In addition to the hypothetical value if leased at n on-restricted rents, the appraiser must opine and support its opinion if the Highest and Best Use of the property is to phase out restricted rents in favor of non -restricted rents, and, if so, develop an as -is estimate of market value considering the time period necessary to phase in non -restricted rents.

examples: LIHTC property: appraisal reports value with restricted rents and a hypothetical value at market rents, and analyzes the HBU and phase-out period to reach market rents.
Property has restricted rents but appraisal provides only one value; underwriting requires revision to include both restricted and hypothetical unrestricted values.
Appraiser concludes HBU is to retain restricted rents; appraisal must still include both valuations and supported HBU conclusion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/underwriting suspension until compliant appraisal provided

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

dependencies.rationale: BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_DOCUMENTATION_034_006 requires restricted and unrestricted valuation outputs. BR_REQUIRES_APPRAISAL_VALIDATION_034_007 then requires documenting the restricted rent roll source and continuation/expiry methodology, which is necessary to make the valuation outputs reliable and acceptable to Freddie.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If restricted rent roll source/continuation analysis is not documented, Freddie may treat the restricted-rent valuation as unsupported, triggering appraisal revision and underwriting delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisal must report total value plus 3 non-real estate allocations
business_rules.description: The Appraisal must clearly and prominently report the total market value of the Property and an allocation for contributory business value, personal property, and/or other non-real estate items. The appraiser must discuss the value segregation process and provide market-derived data supporting allocations (e.g., surveys of market participants, comparable sales data, authoritative sources).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when appraisal includes non-real estate items affecting value conclusion
business_rules.consequences: If segregation is reported and supported, valuation is transparent; if not, appraisal is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: Chapter 60 – Value segregation and allocation requirement (excerpted paragraph)
source_reference.source_text: The Appraisal will clearly and prominently report the total market value of the Property as well as an allocation for contributory business value, personal property and/or other non -real estate items. The appraiser will clearly, adequately and comprehensi vely discuss the value segregation process and provide market -derived data for the value allocations, including, where applicable, surveys of market participants, comparable sales data and authoritative sources for the appraiser’s allocation methodology.

examples: Hotel-like multifamily asset: appraisal reports total real estate value and separately allocates contributory business value and personal property, citing market participant surveys and comparable sales support.
Appraisal includes personal property in the total value but provides no allocation or support; must be revised to clearly allocate and support the segregation.
Appraisal identifies no non-real estate items; it still must clearly report total market value and confirm allocations are not applicable/are zero if appropriate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/underwriting suspension until compliant appraisal provided

examples: Mortgage requires an appraisal dated within the acceptable recency window at closing
Mortgage requires an appraisal update due to market changes or report staleness
Mortgage requires appraisal review when red flags are identified

business_rules.rule_name: Green verification certification must be collected within 30 days and include 3 data points
business_rules.description: For repairs classified as Utility Efficiency Improvements or Green Improvements, the Servicer’s completion notification to Freddie Mac must be made on the applicable Verification Certification (Utility Efficiency Improvement Verification Certification or Green Improvements Verification Certification) submitted via PRS. The Servicer must collect the applicable Verification Certification within 30 days of completion of the improvements, and the certification must provide: (i) the completion date; (ii) specifications of completed improvements; and (iii) confirmation that the Benchmarking Data Consultant uploaded current energy and water usage into ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager (or another Freddie Mac-identified tool). If Freddie Mac no longer owns the Mortgage, the Servicer must submit the Verification Certification to Freddie Mac via DMS.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Utility Efficiency Improvements or Green Improvements completion under a Repair Agreement
business_rules.consequences: If certification is collected and submitted timely, green/utility completion is documented; if not, Servicer must cure missing/late certification and may face compliance findings
business_rules.exceptions: If Portfolio Manager unavailable, use Freddie Mac-identified benchmarking tool; DMS route if no longer owned

source_reference.source_text: For repairs classified as Utility Efficiency Improvements or Green Improvements as applicable, this notification must be on the Utility Efficiency Improvement Verification Certification or the Green Improvements Verification Certification ... submitted to Freddie Mac via the Property Reporting System (PRS). The Servicer must collect the applicable Verification Certification within 30 days of completion of the applicable improvements. The Verification Certification must provide the following: o The date of completion of the improvements o The specifications of the completed improvements o Confirmation that the Benchmarking Data Consultant uploaded current energy and water usage ... into ENERGY STAR® Portfolio Manager®

examples: Green improvements completed May 5; Servicer collects the Green Improvements Verification Certification by June 4 (within 30 days) and submits via PRS including completion date/specs/benchmarking confirmation.
Utility efficiency work completed but certification lacks benchmarking upload confirmation: certification is incomplete and must be corrected and re-submitted.
Freddie Mac has transferred ownership; Servicer submits the Verification Certification via DMS instead of PRS per the instruction.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 30-day timeframe and explicit required certification contents; includes conditional submission method.

examples: Monthly servicing report submitted for all active loans in portfolio
Servicing report includes 30-day delinquency bucket updates
Corrected servicing report filed after reconciliation

business_rules.rule_name: myOptigo automated accounting transmissions mandatory; fees apply
business_rules.description: Automated transmission of accounting transactions via myOptigo for Investor Reporting is mandatory for all Freddie Mac Multifamily Mortgages. Transmissions must be usable, accurate, and timely. Failure to comply will result in assessment of accounting reporting noncompliance fees set forth in Section 54.9.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all Freddie Mac Multifamily Mortgages subject to myOptigo reporting
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, reporting accepted; if not, noncompliance fees are assessed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: b. Reporting requirements for myOptigo for Investor Reporting (04/15/21 ) Automated transmission of accounting transactions via myOptigo for Investor Reporting is mandatory for all Freddie Mac Multifamily Mortgages. Transmissions must be usable, accurate and timely. Failure to comply with these requirements will result in the assessment of the accounting reporting noncompliance fees set forth in Section 54.9.

examples: Servicer submits accounting file with invalid formatting so it is unusable: noncompliance fees may be assessed.
Servicer submits late transmission after due date: noncompliance fees may be assessed.
Servicer submits accurate and timely myOptigo transmission: meets the mandatory requirement.
Accounting report shows accounting net yield equal to required net yield for par purchase loans
Accounting report reflects adjusted accounting net yield due to premium purchase
Accounting report flags exception where net yield setup differs from contract

business_rules.rule_name: Keep screening evidence with date and OFAC/SCP screenshots
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must retain evidence that it has screened (or caused to be screened) Employees, Contractors, and Third-Party Vendors against Restricted Party Lists. Evidence must include the date the search was conducted and, for the SCP List and OFAC Lists, screenshots or copies of the searches. For Third-Party Vendors, the evidence must be retained in the Mortgage File.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when screening is performed for Employees/Contractors/Vendors against Restricted Party Lists
business_rules.consequences: If retained, supports auditability; if not, Seller/Servicer cannot evidence compliance in review
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Seller/Servicer must retain evidence that it has screened, or has caused to be screened, all Freddie Mac Matters Employees , Freddie Mac Matters Contractors , and Third- Party Vendors against the Restricted Party Lists to ensure compliance with the provisions of this chapter .  Such evidence  must include the date the search was conducted and, with respect to the SCP List and the OFAC Lists, screenshots or copies  of the searches ).  With respect to the screening of any Third- Party Vendor, Seller/Servicer must retain such evidence in the Mortgage File.

examples: Mortgage file contains an OFAC screenshot for the appraiser dated 2026-02-03 plus a screening log entry with the search date.
Annual employee screening is documented with a dated report export and SCP list screenshots for flagged names.
Seller screens a surveyor but retains no screenshot/copy for OFAC/SCP—documentation requirement is not met even if screening was performed.
Seller/Servicer retains closing package documents for the required retention period
Seller/Servicer retains accounting and servicing reports for audit support
Seller/Servicer applies legal hold to a loan file under investigation

dependencies.rationale: BR_RETAINS_RECORDS_UNDER_DOCUMENTATION_021_006 explicitly requires evidence retention (including screenshots/copies for SCP and OFAC) and states that for Third-Party Vendors the evidence must be retained in the Mortgage File. BR_RETAINS_RECORDS_UNDER_DOCUMENTATION_017_001 establishes the overarching file retention requirement needed to keep that evidence accessible for the required retention period.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_RETAINS_RECORDS_UNDER_DOCUMENTATION_017_001 is not satisfied, screening evidence placed in the Mortgage File may be lost/destroyed early, leaving the Servicer unable to demonstrate compliance with restricted party screening in audits or reviews.

business_rules.rule_name: Ordinance or Law Coverage D Required for 5+ Stories
business_rules.description: If the property has buildings that are 5 stories or more, an Increased Period of Restoration endorsement (Coverage “D”) is required as part of Ordinance or Law coverage. This endorsement extends business income and extra expense coverage and provides additional time to restore operations when delayed due to enforcement of building or zoning laws.
business_rules.conditions: When a non-conforming property includes any building with 5+ stories
business_rules.consequences: If endorsement is present, requirement satisfied; if missing, Ordinance/Law insurance is incomplete
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable when buildings are fewer than 5 stories

source_reference.source_text: If the Property has buildings 5 stories or more, then an Increased Period of Restoration endorsement is required. The coverage extends business income and extra expense coverage and provides additional time to restore operations when delayed due

examples: A 6-story non-conforming building requires an Increased Period of Restoration endorsement; omission is a documentation deficiency
A 4-story property does not require Coverage D under this requirement (Coverage A-C still apply if Ordinance or Law insurance is required)
A property has a 5-story building and a 2-story building; endorsement is required because at least one building is 5 stories
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery condition / purchase denial until endorsement added

examples: Hazard policy covers the property with coverage amount meeting minimum requirements
Renewal policy replaces expiring policy without coverage lapse
Policy endorsement updates loss payee language after servicing transfer

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_REQUIREMENT_018_004 applies when the property has buildings 5 stories or more and requires Increased Period of Restoration endorsement as part of Ordinance or Law coverage. BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_018_002 establishes the scenario where Ordinance or Law coverage is required for a non-conforming property and defines Coverage A sizing mechanics; once Ordinance/Law coverage is in scope and the 5+ story condition is true, BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_REQUIREMENT_018_004 becomes mandatory.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_COVERS_PROPERTY_REQUIREMENT_018_004 is not satisfied when triggered, Ordinance/Law coverage is incomplete for tall buildings, potentially leaving insufficient time/coverage for restoration delays due to code/zoning enforcement, and creating noncompliance with Freddie Mac insurance requirements.
